title: Man and Medicine: Towards Health for All
content:
OVERVIEW OF THE CHAPTER
This chapter traces the historical evolution of medicine from primitive times to the modern era, highlighting the shift from supernatural beliefs to scientific, evidence-based practices. It covers the major ancient systems of medicine (Indian, Islamic, Chinese, Egyptian, Mesopotamian, Greek, and Roman), the dawn of scientific medicine during the Renaissance, the sanitary awakening, the germ theory, and the emergence of modern medicine. It explores the changing concepts of public health, the development of social and preventive medicine, and the contemporary health care revolution centered around Primary Health Care, the 'Health for All' goal, Millennium Development Goals (MDGs), and Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs).

DEFINITIONS
- Ayurveda: Meaning 'Knowledge of Life' or the knowledge by which life may be prolonged. It is derived from the Atharvaveda (medical knowledge of Atharvaveda gradually developed into the science of Ayurveda).
- Family Medicine: Family-oriented medicine, i.e., health care centered on the family as a unit, from first contact to the ongoing care of chronic problems (from prevention to rehabilitation). It is neither disease nor organ-oriented and is specially designed to deliver Primary Care.
- Community Medicine: A specialty which deals with populations and comprises those doctors who try to measure the needs of the population (both sick and well), who plan and administer services, and who are engaged in research and teaching in the field. It emphasizes the prevention of disease and promotion of health. It is the successor of what was previously known as Public Health, Community Health, or Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM).
- Social Medicine: The study of man as a social being in his total environment, focusing on the health of the community as a whole. It emphasizes the strong relationship between medicine and social sciences.
- Preventive Medicine: A branch of medicine applied to 'healthy' people, customarily by actions affecting large numbers or populations, with the primary objective of prevention of disease and promotion of health.
- Primary Health Care: Essential health care based on practical, scientifically sound, and socially acceptable methods and technology made universally accessible to individuals and families in the community through their full participation and at a cost that the community and country can afford.

CORE CONCEPTS
- The Supernatural Theory of Disease: Primitive man attributed disease and suffering to the wrath of gods, the invasion of the body by 'evil spirits', and the malevolent influence of stars and planets. Treatment involved appeasing gods by prayers, rituals, sacrifices, witchcraft, and the use of charms and amulets.
- Tri-Dosha Theory of Disease (Ayurveda): Disease is explained as a disturbance in the equilibrium of the 3 Humors: Vata (Wind), Pitta (Gall Fluid), and Kapha (Mucus). This theory is strikingly similar to the Theory of 4 Humors in Greek medicine.
- Concept of Yin and Yang (Chinese Medicine): Based on two opposing principlesâ€”Yang (an active, masculine principle) and Yin (a negative, feminine principle). The balance of these two opposing forces meant good health.
- Theory of 4 Humors (Greek Medicine): Greek medicine rejected the supernatural theory and believed matter was made up of four elements (earth, air, fire, water) corresponding to qualities (cold, dry, hot, moist). These were represented in the body by 4 Humors: Phlegm (Mucus), Yellow Bile (Gall), Black Bile (Intestinal Matter), and Blood. Health prevailed when these were in equilibrium.
- Germ Theory of Disease: Pioneered by Louis Pasteur (who demonstrated bacteria in air and disproved spontaneous generation) and Robert Koch (who showed anthrax and tuberculosis were caused by bacteria). This shifted focus from miasma to microbes as the definitive causative agents of diseases.
- Multifactorial Causation: First mooted by Pettenkofer, it recognizes that modern diseases (cancer, diabetes, cardiovascular diseases) cannot be explained by the germ theory alone. It involves social, economic, genetic, environmental, and psychological 'risk factors'.
- Changing Concepts in Public Health: Public health has evolved through four distinct phases: (1) Disease control phase (1880-1920) focusing on sanitary legislation; (2) Health promotional phase (1920-1960) focusing on personal health services and basic health services; (3) Social engineering phase (1960-1980) addressing chronic diseases and risk factors; (4) 'Health for All' phase (1981-2000) aiming to minimize inequalities in health globally.

MEDICINE IN ANTIQUITY
Indian Medicine
- Ayurveda dates back to 400 - 600 BC. The Golden Age of Indian Medicine was between 800 BC - 600 AD.
- Indian Medicine in Asia has played the same role as Greek medicine in the West.
- Celebrated Authorities in Ayurvedic Medicine:
  - Atreya: 1st Indian Physician & Teacher of medicine who lived in the ancient University of Takshashila (about 800 B.C.).
  - Charaka: Father of Indian Medicine. He compiled the 'Charaka Samhita', was a Court Physician to the Buddhist king Kanishka, and mentioned some 500 drugs.
  - Sushruta: Father of Indian Surgery & Father of Plastic Surgery. Compiled his surgical knowledge in the 'Sushruta Samhita'. Known for rhinoplasty and cataract operations.
  - Vagbhata: Medical Writer who compiled all works & gave the Science of Ayurveda.
- Dhanvantri: The Hindu God of Medicine, born from the churning of the oceans.
- Laws of Manu: A code of Personal Hygiene in ancient India. Excavations at Mohenjo-daro and Harappa revealed advanced sanitation.

Islamic Medicine
- Unani Tibb System: A system of the Medieval Age, promoted and compiled by Muslims in the Arab world. It originated in Greece (Graeco-Roman) and was introduced to India by the Muslim Rulers of the 10th Century.
- Key Figures:
  - Rhazes (Abu Bakr al-Razi): Gave the first known book of Child Disease. Noted for keen observation, he worked on Pupillary Reactions, Mercury Purgatives, and Pharmacology.
  - Avicenna (Ibn Sina): An intellectual prodigy who worked extensively on Pharmacology. Compiled a 21-volume encyclopedia, the 'Canon of Medicine'.

Chinese Medicine
- Claims to be the World's 1st Organized Body of Medical Knowledge (dating back to 2700 B.C.).
- Chinese Medicine is a Pioneer in the concept of Specific Protection by Vaccine (early pioneers of immunization using variolation against smallpox).
- Utilized Acupuncture & Acupressure, Hydro Therapy & Massage Therapy, Dietetics & Nutrition. Included the system of 'barefoot doctors'.

Egyptian Medicine
- Medicine was compiled & recorded on Papyrus.
  - Edwin Smith Papyrus: Oldest treatise on surgery (Head & Neck Surgery, accurately describing partial paralysis from skull fractures).
  - Ebers Papyrus: Focused on Prescription Writing & Drugs (contains ~800 prescriptions based on ~700 drugs).
- Concept of Doctor & Priest in combination. Specialization prevailed (eye, head, and tooth doctors).
- Imhotep: The 1st Egyptian Physician, architect of the step pyramid, considered both a doctor and divinity.
- Horus: God of Egyptian medicine/health.

Mesopotamian Medicine
- Known as the Cradle of Civilization. Characterized by Geomancy (interpretation of dreams) & Prescription Writing in cuneiform on tablets.
- Specialties for Diseases included: 
  - Herbs Doctors (Internal Medicine)
  - Knife Doctors (Surgery)
  - Spell Doctors (Psychiatry)
- Code of Hammurabi: Code of Conduct by Doctors & Ethics. It was the 1st codification of Medical Practice, containing drastic laws and penalties for harmful therapy.

Greek Medicine
- Aesculapius (1200 B.C.): God of Medicine. Had two daughters: Hygiea (Goddess of Health / Preventive Medicine) and Panacea (Goddess of Medicine / Curative).
- Hippocrates (460-370 B.C.): Father of Medicine. Regarded as the 1st True Epidemiologist because he distinguished between endemic and epidemic diseases. Author of the book 'Airs, Waters & Places'. He challenged the tradition of magic in medicine and set a high moral standard with the 'Hippocratic Oath'.
- Based on the 4 Humors: Phlegm (Mucus), Yellow Bile (Gall), Black Bile (Intestinal Matter), and Blood.

Roman Medicine
- The Romans were highly practical and gave the concept of Quarantine, Sanitation (Public Sewage System / aqueducts / baths), Public Health, and Health Policy.
- Galen (130-205 A.D.): A 'Medical Dictator' whose teachings in Anatomy & Physiology ruled for 14 centuries. He observed that disease is due to three factors: predisposing, exciting, and environmental.

DAWN OF SCIENTIFIC MEDICINE
- Revival of Medicine (1453-1600 A.D.):
  - Paracelsus: Revived medicine by publicly burning the works of Galen and Avicenna, turning medicine towards rational research.
  - Fracastorius (Fracus Torius): Founder of Epidemiology. Enunciated the 'Theory of Contagion', recognizing the transfer of infection via minute invisible particles (e.g., syphilis).
  - Andreas Vesalius: Father of Modern Anatomy. Considered the '1st Man of Modern Medicine'. Authored the '1st Book on Scientific Medicine' titled 'Fabrica'.
  - Ambroise Pare: Father of Modern Surgery.
  - Thomas Sydenham: The English Hippocrates, set the example for the true clinical method.
  - Morgagni: Founded pathologic anatomy.
- Sanitary Awakening (mid-19th Century England): Sparked by the industrial revolution's ill effects (slums, filth). Edwin Chadwick's 1842 report led to the Public Health Act of 1848. John Snow established the role of polluted water in cholera. William Budd linked typhoid to drinking water. Sir John Simon became the first medical officer of health in London.
- Birth of Preventive Medicine: James Lind advocated fresh fruit for scurvy prevention (1753). Edward Jenner discovered the smallpox vaccination (1796).

MODERN MEDICINE / SCIENTIFIC MEDICINE
- Curative Medicine: Aims at the removal of disease from the patient using refined diagnostic techniques and treatments (e.g., allopathic medicine, antibiotics).
- Preventive Medicine: Focuses on healthy populations. Triumphs include bacterial/viral vaccines, discoveries in nutrition, and synthetic insecticides (DDT). Includes concepts like disease eradication and presymptomatic screening.
- Social Medicine: Envisages medicine as a social science. Initiated by Neumann and Virchow (1848), revived by Alfred Grotjahn (social pathology) in 1911, and established by John Ryle in England. It focuses on epidemiology and the medical needs/care of society.

HEALTH CARE REVOLUTION
- Health for All: The 30th World Health Assembly (1977) set the target to attain a level of health permitting all citizens to lead a socially and economically productive life by the year 2000.
- Primary Health Care: The Alma-Ata Declaration (1978) called for a revolutionary approach to health care based on equity, wider coverage, community involvement, and intersectoral coordination. It involves the 'deprofessionalization of medicine' by utilizing community health workers.
- Millennium Development Goals (MDGs): Adopted in 2000, setting 8 goals for 2015. Three of the eight goals were directly health-related.
- Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs): Adopted in 2015 for 2030, containing 17 goals. SDG 3 is comprehensively dedicated to health: 'Ensure healthy lives and promote well-being for all at all ages'.

KEY POINTS
- First Indian Physician & Teacher: Atreya.
- Father of Indian Medicine: Charaka.
- Father of Indian Surgery & Plastic Surgery: Sushruta.
- First Egyptian Physician: Imhotep.
- 1st codification of Medical Practice: Code of Hammurabi.
- Father of Medicine: Hippocrates.
- 1st True Epidemiologist: Hippocrates.
- Medical Dictator: Galen.
- Founder of Epidemiology / Theory of Contagion: Fracastorius (Fracus Torius).
- Father of Modern Anatomy / 1st Man of Modern Medicine: Andreas Vesalius.
- 1st Book on Scientific Medicine: Fabrica.
- Father of Modern Surgery: Ambroise Pare.
- 1st Organized Body of Medical Knowledge: Chinese Medicine.
- Pioneer in concept of Specific Protection by Vaccine: Chinese Medicine.
- The term 'Community Medicine' has replaced Public Health, Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM).

FORMULAS AND CALCULATIONS
There are no formulas or specific calculations in this chapter.

MNEMONICS
- To remember the 3 Humors of Ayurveda (Tri-Dosha): VPK - Vata (Wind), Pitta (Gall Fluid), Kapha (Mucus).
- To remember the daughters of Aesculapius: H & P (Health & Prevention = Hygiea; Pills & Cures = Panacea).

title: Concept of Health and Disease
content:
OVERVIEW OF THE CHAPTER
The chapter provides a foundational understanding of health, disease, and the multitude of factors that influence them. Moving away from the traditional biomedical model, health is now viewed as a dynamic, multidimensional, and holistic state. The chapter covers the dimensions and determinants of health, indices to measure the quality of life (PQLI, HDI, MPI), the epidemiological triad, natural history of diseases, levels of prevention, and the distinct paradigms of monitoring, surveillance, and community diagnosis.

DEFINITIONS
Health: The WHO defines health (1948) as "a state of complete physical, mental and social well-being and not merely an absence of disease or infirmity." In recent years, this has been amplified to include the ability to lead a "socially and economically productive life."
Operational Definition of Health: A condition or quality of the human organism expressing the adequate functioning of the organism in given conditions, genetic or environmental.
Disease: A physiological/psychological dysfunction; an alteration of the human body interrupting the performance of vital functions. 
Illness: A subjective state of the person who feels aware of not being well.
Sickness: A state of social dysfunction, i.e., a role that the individual assumes when ill ("sickness role").
Public Health: The science and art of preventing disease, prolonging life & promoting health through organized community efforts (C.E.A. Winslow).
Community Medicine: The field concerned with the study of health and disease in the population of a defined community, focusing on community diagnosis and treatment.
Community Diagnosis: The identification and quantification of health problems in a community in terms of mortality and morbidity, and describing the pattern of disease based on age, sex, vital statistics, and incidence/prevalence.

CORE CONCEPTS

Changing Concepts of Health
1. Biomedical / Biological Concept: Health is viewed as the "absence of disease." Based on the germ theory of disease. It views the human body as a machine and disease as a breakdown. Limitation: It minimizes the role of environmental, social, psychological, and cultural determinants of health.
2. Ecological Concept: Views health as a dynamic equilibrium between man and his environment, and disease as a maladjustment of the human organism to the environment.
3. Psychosocial Concept: Health is not only a biomedical phenomenon but is also influenced by social, psychological, cultural, economic, and political factors.
4. Holistic Concept: A synthesis of all the above. It recognizes the strength of social, economic, political, and environmental influences. It implies a sound mind, in a sound body, in a sound family, in a sound environment.

Dimensions of Health
Health is multidimensional. 
- Physical dimension: State in which every cell and organ is functioning at optimum capacity and in perfect harmony with the rest of the body.
- Mental dimension: A state of balance between the individual and the surrounding world. It relates to cognition/knowing and implies being free from internal conflicts.
- Social dimension: Quantity and quality of an individual's interpersonal ties and the extent of involvement with the community.
- Spiritual dimension: Refers to that part of the individual which strives for meaning and purpose in life.
- Emotional dimension: Relates to "feeling" (whereas mental relates to cognition).
- Vocational dimension: Work often plays an important role in promoting both physical and mental health.
- Other dimensions: Cultural, Environmental, Nutritional, Economic, Preventive, Educational, etc.

Determinants of Health
Health is multifactorial. The major determinants include:
- Biological: Diseases known to be of genetic origin (e.g., chromosomal anomalies, diabetes).
- Behavioral and Socio-cultural: Lifestyles and personal habits. In developed countries: Obesity, drug addiction. In developing countries: Lack of sanitation, poor nutrition.
- Environment: Internal environment (tissue, organ, organ system) and External environment (all that which is external to the individual: physical, biological, psychosocial).
- Socio-economic: Economic status (purchasing power), Education (especially female education), Occupation (unemployment leads to psychological and social damage).
- Health System: Health and family welfare services, immunization, provision of safe water.
- Ageing of the population: Increased prevalence of chronic diseases and disabilities.
- Gender: Women's health issues.

Concept of Well-being
Well-being has objective and subjective components:
- Standard of living (Objective): Refers to the scale of our expenditure, goods we consume, and services we enjoy. Includes income, occupation, housing, sanitation, nutrition, and health services. Depends on per capita GNP.
- Level of living (Objective): Parallel term used by the UN, consisting of 9 components including health, food consumption, education, and social security.
- Quality of life (Subjective): Physical, mental, and social well-being as perceived by happiness, satisfaction, and gratification.

Indices of Health and Development
Physical Quality of Life Index (PQLI): 
- Subjective component of quality of life.
- Consolidates 3 indicators: Infant Mortality Rate (IMR), Life expectancy at age one, and Literacy.
- Scaled from 0 (worst) to 100 (best). India's PQLI is ~65.
- Does  measure economic growth (per capita GNP is excluded).

Human Development Index (HDI): 
- Composite index measuring achievements in basic dimensions of human development.
- Dimensions: 
  1. Health (Life expectancy at birth)
  2. Education (Mean years of schooling & Expected years of schooling)
  3. Standard of living (Real GNI per capita in PPP US$)
- Value ranges between 0 to 1. India is in the Medium category (HDI = 0.645).
- Calculated as the geometric mean of the three dimension indices.

Multi-dimensional Poverty Index (MPI): 
- Replaced the Human Poverty Index (HPI).
- Uses 3 dimensions and 10 indicators: 
  1. Health (Nutrition, Child mortality)
  2. Education (Years of schooling, School attendance)
  3. Standard of living (Cooking fuel, Sanitation, Drinking water, Electricity, Housing, Assets).

Kuppuswamy Scale
Scale of Socio-Economic Status (SES) used for Urban families. It has 3 components:
1. Education status of the Head of the family
2. Occupation status of the Head of the family
3. Total per capita family income (per month)

Indicators of Health
An ideal indicator should be Valid, Reliable, Sensitive, Specific, Feasible, and Relevant.
- Mortality Indicators: Life expectancy (positive indicator), Crude death rate, Age-specific death rate, IMR (best indicator for availability, utilization & effectiveness of health services; most universally accepted), Child mortality rate, Under-5 proportionate mortality rate, Maternal mortality rate, Disease-specific mortality rate, Proportional mortality rate (measures burden of a disease), Case Fatality Rate (represents killing power/virulence of the disease).
- Morbidity Indicators: Incidence, Prevalence, Attendance at OPD, Admission at IPD, Duration of stay, Notification rates.
- Disability Indicators: 
  -  Days of restricted activity, Bed disability days, Work loss days.
  -  Limitation of mobility, Limitation of activity.
  -  Disability Adjusted Life Years. Measures the burden of disease. Expresses years lost to premature death & years lived with disability.
  -  Disability Free Life Expectancy (Sullivan's index). Average number of years an individual is expected to live free of disability. 
  -  Health Adjusted Life Expectancy. Replaced DALE. Adjustment for time spent in poor health.
  -  Quality Adjusted Life Years. Number of quality years added by a medical intervention.
- Utilization Rates: E.g., Proportion of fully immunized infants, ANC coverage, Bed occupancy rate (negative value indicates over/under utilization; slight positive value indicates optimum utilization), Bed turnover ratio.

Health Promotion & Ottawa Charter
Health promotion is the process of enabling people to increase control over, and to improve their health. 
Ottawa Charter (1986):
- Logo: Circle with 3 wings.
- 3 Basic Strategies: Advocate, Enable, Mediate.
- 5 Key Action Areas: 
  1. Build healthy public policy 
  2. Create supportive environment for health 
  3. Strengthen community action for health 
  4. Develop personal skills 
  5. Reorient health services

Concept of Disease & Epidemiological Triad
- Epidemiological Triad: Useful in showing the interaction & interdependence of Agent, Host, and Environment. A fourth factor, Time (incubation period/duration), is also considered.
- Agent: Substance living or nonliving whose presence or absence initiates a disease process (Biological, Nutrient, Physical, Chemical [Endogenous/Exogenous], Mechanical, Social).
- Host: Person that harbors the disease (Demographic, Biological, Social, Lifestyle factors).
- Environment: Surroundings that allow disease transmission (Physical, Biological, Psychosocial).
- Web of Causation: Suggested by MacMahon & Pugh. Ideally suited for chronic diseases where the exact agent is unknown. Emphasizes the complex interrelationship of multiple factors.

Iceberg Phenomenon of Disease
Disease in a community is compared to an iceberg.
- Floating tip: Represents what the physician sees (Clinical cases, diagnosed cases).
- Submerged portion: Represents the hidden mass of disease (Latent, inapparent, presymptomatic, undiagnosed cases, carriers). The epidemiologist is concerned with this portion.
- Waterline: Represents the demarcation between apparent & inapparent disease.
-  Iceberg phenomenon is NOT shown by Rabies, Tetanus, and Measles.

Natural History of Disease
Signifies the way a disease evolves over time from the earliest stage to its termination (recovery, disability, or death) in the absence of treatment. Best established by cohort/longitudinal studies.
1. Prepathogenesis phase: The period preliminary to the onset of disease in man. The agent has not yet entered the host, but the interacting factors are present in the environment.
2. Pathogenesis phase: Begins with the entry of the disease agent into the susceptible host. Multiplies and induces physiological changes. Progresses from early (presymptomatic) to late pathogenesis (clinical signs appear).

Levels of Prevention & Modes of Intervention
1. Primordial Prevention: Prevention of the  in populations where they have not yet appeared. It is primary prevention in its purest sense (e.g., educating children to prevent childhood obesity).
   -  Health Education.
2. Primary Prevention: Action taken prior to the onset of disease. Intervention in the prepathogenesis phase. Based on elimination of risk factors.
   -  
     a)  Health education, Environmental modification (safe water, sanitary latrines), Nutritional interventions, Lifestyle/behavioral changes.
     b)  Immunization, chemoprophylaxis, contraceptives, iodization of salt, use of seatbelts/helmets, protective goggles.
3. Secondary Prevention: Action to halt the progress of a disease at its incipient stage & prevent complications. Intervention in early pathogenesis.
   -  Early diagnosis (Screening tests, Pap smear, case finding) and Adequate Treatment (DOTS, MDT).
4. Tertiary Prevention: Action taken to limit impairment & disabilities. Intervention in late pathogenesis.
   -  
     a)  Prevents transition from impairment to handicap (e.g., spectacles for refractory error, resting limb in neutral position in polio).
     b)  Medical, Vocational, Social, Psychological (e.g., reconstructive surgery in leprosy, establishing schools for the blind).

Disease Progression Pathway:
Disease (Abnormal condition) -> Impairment (Loss of anatomical structure/physiological function, e.g., loss of foot) -> Disability (Inability to perform an activity considered normal, e.g., cannot walk) -> Handicap (Disadvantage preventing fulfillment of a role, e.g., unemployed).

Monitoring vs. Surveillance
- Monitoring: Continuous oversight of activities to ensure they are proceeding according to plan. Done by technicians. One-time activity. Stops once disease is eliminated/eradicated.
- Surveillance: Continuous scrutiny of factors that determine occurrence & distribution of disease. Done by professionals. Continuous cycle (continues even after elimination). Includes feedback and in-built action components.
  -  Health worker actively goes to the house to detect cases (e.g., NVBDCP). More expensive.
  -  Data is itself reported to the health system (patients come to PHC). Less expensive.
  -  Identifies missing cases & supplements notified cases. Estimates disease prevalence and trends in larger populations (e.g., HIV surveillance in STD clinics & ANC clinics).

International Classification of Disease (ICD)
Produced by WHO. Used as a base in health fields for epidemiological & health management purposes. Revised every 10 years.
- ICD-10: Came in Jan 1993. Coding system is alphanumeric. Arranged in 22 chapters & 3 volumes.
- ICD-11: Came into full effect in Jan 2022. Numerical (Arabic) coding. 3 volumes, 26 chapters. Includes new chapters on: Sleep wake disorders, Sexual health, Diseases of blood & immune system, Traditional medicine, and Extension codes.

Patient's Care Settings
- Inpatient: Observation care expected to be â‰¥ 24 hours.
- Outpatient: Care received in hospital/clinic/emergency where patient is released within 23 hours.
- Observation status: Active treatment required to determine if patient needs hospitalization or discharge.
- Urgent care: Requires stabilization due to an emergency situation.
- Domiciliary care: Personal services rendered by doctors to patients in their homes or nursing homes.

KEY POINTS
- PQLI focuses purely on health outcomes (IMR, life expectancy at age 1, literacy) without factoring in economic metrics like per capita GNP, making it a subjective measure of the quality of life.
- Case Fatality Rate (CFR) is the ultimate measure of the killing power or virulence of a disease organism.
- Infant Mortality Rate (IMR) is the most universally accepted indicator of the health status of a whole population and their socio-economic conditions.
- Screening tests fall under Secondary Prevention because they aim at early diagnosis to halt disease progression.
- Kuppuswamy Scale relies heavily on the education and occupation of the head of the family, and total family income to classify urban socio-economic status.
- Health care services should be appropriate, comprehensive, adequate, available, accessible, affordable, and feasible.

FORMULAS AND CALCULATIONS
Mortality Rates:
- Crude Death Rate (CDR) = (Total deaths / Midyear population) x 1000
- Age Specific Death Rate (ASDR) = (Total number of deaths in a specific age group / Midyear population of the same age group) x 1000
- Infant Mortality Rate (IMR) = (Total infant deaths / Total live births) x 1000
- Child Mortality Rate (CMR) = (Total deaths of 1-4 years / Midyear population of 1-4 years) x 1000
- Disease Specific Mortality Rate = (Deaths from a specific disease / Midyear population) x 1000
- Proportional Mortality Rate = (Deaths from a specific disease / Total deaths) x 100
- Case Fatality Rate (CFR) = (Total no. of deaths due to specific disease / Total no. of cases due to that specific disease) x 100

Disability Measures:
- DALY = YLL (Years of Lost Life) + YLD (Years Lost to Disability)
- YLL = Number of deaths x Expected remaining years of life
- YLD = Number of cases x Average duration of the disease x Weighting factor
- Sullivan's index (DFLE) = Life expectancy - Duration of disability

Indices:
- HDI Dimension Index = (Actual value - Minimum value) / (Maximum value - Minimum value)
- HDI = âˆ›(Life expectancy index x Education index x Income index)
- Per capita family income = Total income of the family / Total number of family members

MNEMONICS
Dimensions of Health:

- Vocational, Cultural
- Nutritional, Environmental
- Physical, Social, Mental
- Spiritual, Emotional, Economical

Determinants of Health:

- Gender
- Health system
- Socio-economic
- Environmental
- Ageing
- Biological
- Behavioral

Ottawa Charter Key Actions:

- Public's: Public health policy
- Skilled: Personal Skills development
- Action: Strengthen Community Action
- Reorients: Reorient health services
- Environment: Supportive Environment

Web of Causation:

- Web of causation was given by MacMahon & Pugh.

Iceberg Phenomenon Exceptions:

- Measles
- Kutta (Dog bite -> Rabies)
- Tota (Tetanus)

ICD-11 New Chapters:

- Sleep wake disorders
- Sexual health
- Blood & immune system diseases
- Integration of Traditional medicine
- Extension codes

title: Principles of Epidemiology and Epidemiologic Methods

subsections.title: Introduction, Tools of Measurement, and Measurement of Morbidity & Mortality
subsections.content:
OVERVIEW OF EPIDEMIOLOGY

Epidemiology is the basic science of preventive and social medicine. The word is derived from epidemic (epi = among; demos = people; logos = study). It has evolved from studying infectious disease epidemics to chronic diseases, accidents, and health-related states in human populations.

DEFINITIONS

- Old definitions range from Hippocrates to Greenwood (1934), who defined it as the study of disease as a mass phenomenon.
- Modern definition (MacMahon, 1960; modified by Last): The study of the occurrence and distribution of health-related events, states, and processes in specified populations, including the study of the determinants influencing such processes, and the application of this knowledge to control relevant health problems.

CORE COMPONENTS OF EPIDEMIOLOGY

- Disease frequency: Measurement of frequency of disease, disability, or death. The basic measure is a rate or ratio.
- Distribution of disease: Study of disease distribution patterns by time, place, and person. This helps in generating hypotheses (Descriptive epidemiology).
- Determinants of disease: Identifying underlying causes or risk factors. This tests aetiological hypotheses (Analytical epidemiology).

AIMS OF EPIDEMIOLOGY

- To describe the distribution and magnitude of health and disease problems in human populations.
- To identify aetiological factors (risk factors) in the pathogenesis of disease.
- To provide the data essential to the planning, implementation, and evaluation of services for the prevention, control, and treatment of disease, and to setting up priorities among those services.

EPIDEMIOLOGY VERSUS CLINICAL MEDICINE

- Unit of study: In epidemiology, it is a defined population or population at-risk. In clinical medicine, the unit of study is a case or cases.
- Focus: The epidemiologist is concerned with disease patterns in the entire population (both sick and healthy). The clinician is concerned with disease in the individual patient.
- Approach: The epidemiologist goes out into the community to find persons with the disease or exposure. In clinical medicine, the patient comes to the doctor.
- Output: The epidemiologist is interested in the relationship between cases and the population in the form of a rate. The clinician derives a diagnosis, prognosis, and prescribes specific treatment.
- Subject matter: In clinical medicine, the subject matter is easily perceived by clinical and laboratory examinations. In epidemiology, the subject matter is conceptual and can only be symbolized in the form of tables and graphs.

EPIDEMIOLOGICAL APPROACH

- Asking questions: Related to health events (What is the event? What is its magnitude? Where and when did it happen? Who is affected? Why did it happen?) and related to health action (What can be done? How can it be prevented? What resources are required?).
- Making comparisons: The basic approach in epidemiology is to make comparisons and draw inferences (e.g., comparing a group having the disease/exposure to a group not having the disease/exposure) to find crucial differences in host and environmental factors.

MEASUREMENTS IN EPIDEMIOLOGY

The scope of measurements is broad and includes mortality, morbidity, disability, natality, presence or absence of disease characteristics, medical needs, health care facilities utilization, environmental factors, and demographic variables.

TOOLS OF MEASUREMENT

1. Rate
- Measures the occurrence of some particular event (development of disease or the occurrence of death) in a defined population during a given time period.
- It is a statement of the risk of developing a condition.
- Comprises four elements: numerator, denominator, time specification, and multiplier.
- The multiplier is usually 1000, 10000, or 100000 to avoid fractions.
- The numerator is a part of the denominator.
- Types of rates: Crude rates (actual observed rates, unstandardized), Specific rates (actual observed rates due to specific causes or in specific groups), and Standardized rates (obtained by direct or indirect method of standardization).

2. Ratio
- Expresses a relation in size between two random quantities.
- The numerator is NOT a part of the denominator. Both numerator and denominator are unrelated.
- Expressed as x:y or x/y.
- Examples: Sex-ratio, doctor-population ratio, child-woman ratio.

3. Proportion
- A ratio which indicates the relation in magnitude of a part of the whole.
- The numerator is always included in the denominator.
- A proportion is always expressed as a percentage.
- The multiplier is always 100.

CONCEPT OF NUMERATOR AND DENOMINATOR

- Numerator: Refers to the number of times an event (e.g., sickness, birth, death) has occurred in a population during a specified time-period.
- Denominator: Can be related to the population or related to total events.
  - Mid-year population: Because the population size changes daily, the population estimated as on the 1st of July of a year is commonly chosen as a denominator.
  - Population at-risk: Restricted solely to those who are capable of having or acquiring the disease or condition in question. Focuses on groups at risk rather than all individuals.
  - Person-time: Used when persons enter a study at different times and are under observation for varying periods. The denominator is a combination of persons and time (e.g., person-years, person-months).

MEASUREMENT OF MORTALITY

1. Crude Death Rate (CDR)
- Defined as the number of deaths (from all causes) per 1000 estimated mid-year population in one year in a given place.
- Formula: Total number of deaths in an area in a year / Total mid-year population x 1000.
- Mid-year population is the population assessed on the 1st of July.
- Limitation: Lacks comparability for communities with populations that differ by age, sex, race, etc. It summarizes the effect of both population composition and age-specific death rates.

2. Specific Death Rates
- Essential when analysis is planned to throw light on aetiology.
- Can be cause-specific (e.g., tuberculosis), group-specific (e.g., age, sex), or time-specific (e.g., January, weekly).
- Specific death rates help to identify groups at-risk for preventive action and permit comparisons between different causes within the same population.

3. Case Fatality Rate (CFR)
- Represents the killing power of a disease or virulence of the organism.
- Better indicator of the severity of an acute disease.
- Formula: Total number of deaths due to a particular disease / Total number of cases due to the same disease x 100.
- It is essentially a ratio (specifically a proportion) but is conventionally called a rate.
- Limitation: Time interval is not specified (so it is a killing power of a disease with no time interval).
- Compliments the survival rate.

4. Survival Rate (SR)
- The proportion of survivors in a group (e.g., patients) studied and followed over a period (e.g., a 5-year period).
- Formula: Total number of patients alive after 5 years / Total number of patients diagnosed or treated x 100.
- SR = 1 - CFR (here CFR is taken in decimals, i.e., 25% taken as 0.25).
- Describes prognosis and is very useful in cancer studies.
- Assesses standards of therapies.
- Lead time bias: Whenever a screening test is performed, a higher 5-year survival rate is observed. This is a potential bias due to an earlier diagnosis being made, and not because people actually live longer.

5. Proportional Mortality Rate (PMR)
- The simplest measure of estimating the burden of a disease in the community.
- PMR is a proportion.
- Formula: Number of deaths from a specific disease in a year / Total deaths from all causes in that year x 100.
- It does not indicate the risk of members of the population contracting or dying from the disease.

STANDARDIZATION OF DEATH RATES

- While comparing the death rates of two populations, the crude death rate cannot be used for a valid comparison if the age composition is different.
- Standardized death rates are used for the valid comparison of two groups of different health determinants.
- Standardization allows comparison between two different groups by removing the confounding effect of different age structures.
- Standardization is carried out by using a standard population.
- Standard population: A population where the age and sex group distributions are known. The national population need not always be taken as the standard population.

1. Direct Standardization
- Age-specific death rates (ASDR) of the study population are applied to a standard population to calculate expected deaths.
- Feasible only if the actual specific rates in subgroups of the observed population are available.

2. Indirect Standardization
- The Standardized Mortality Ratio (SMR) is the most useful form.
- SMR = Observed deaths / Expected deaths x 100.
- It compares the mortality in a study group (e.g., an occupational group) with the mortality they would have had if they experienced the rates of a standard reference population.
- SMR is best to make a comparison between the health status of 2 populations.
- An SMR greater than 100 indicates a greater mortality risk than the standard population.
- The rate adjusted to allow for the age distribution of the population is known as the age-standardized mortality rate.
- Note: Standardized mortality rate is standardized for age. Specific death rates are specific for age and sex.

MEASUREMENT OF MORBIDITY

Morbidity is defined as any departure, subjective or objective, from a state of physiological well-being. It is measured primarily by incidence and prevalence rates.

INCIDENCE

- Definition: The number of NEW cases occurring in a defined population during a specified period of time.
- Formula: Number of new cases of specific disease during a given time period / Population at risk during that period x 1000.
- Incidence is a rate, expressed per 1000.
- It is NOT influenced by the duration of the disease.
- Use of incidence is generally restricted to acute conditions.
- Incidence can be determined by a Cohort study.
- Incidence is a probability that a healthy individual will develop the disease during a specified period.
- Incidence measures the absolute risk of developing the disease.
- Incidence decreases if a particular programme is efficient.

SPECIAL INCIDENCE RATES

1. Attack Rate
- Used when the population is exposed for a small interval of time (e.g., an epidemic).
- Reflects the extent of the epidemic.
- Formula: Number of new cases of a specified disease during a specified time interval / Total population at risk during the same interval x 100.

2. Secondary Attack Rate (SAR)
- Defined as the number of exposed persons developing the disease within the range of the incubation period following exposure to a primary case.
- The denominator includes ONLY those susceptible in close contact.
- The primary case is ALWAYS excluded from both the numerator and the denominator for SAR calculation.
- Formula: Number of exposed persons developing disease within a range of IP / Total number of exposed susceptible contacts x 100.
- SAR is an index of communicability. If the SAR of a disease is more, it means that the disease is more infectious.
- SAR values for specific diseases:
  - Small pox: 30-45 percent
  - Measles: Greater than 80 percent
  - Chicken pox: 90 percent
  - Mumps: 86 percent
  - Pertussis: 90 percent

USES OF INCIDENCE RATE

- For research into aetiology and pathogenesis, distribution of diseases, and efficiency of preventive or therapeutic measures.
- If the incidence rate is increasing, it indicates the ineffectiveness of current control programmes.
- Better indicator of the efficacy of hospital services and health programs.
- Best measure of disease frequency in etiological studies.

PREVALENCE

- Refers to all current cases (old + new) existing at a given point in time or over a period of time in a given population.
- Prevalence can be determined by a cross-sectional study.
- Prevalence is a proportion and NOT a rate.
- Always expressed in percentage.

TYPES OF PREVALENCE

1. Point Prevalence
- Number of all current cases (old + new) of a specified disease existing at a given point in time / Estimated population at the same point in time x 100.
- When the term "prevalence rate" is used without any further qualification, it is taken to mean point prevalence.

2. Period Prevalence
- Number of existing cases (old + new) of a specified disease during a given period of time interval / Estimated mid-interval population at risk x 100.

USES OF PREVALENCE

- To estimate the magnitude of disease in the community and identify high-risk populations.
- Useful for administrative and planning purposes (e.g., hospital beds, manpower needs).

RELATIONSHIP BETWEEN PREVALENCE AND INCIDENCE

- Formula: P = I x D (Prevalence = Incidence x Mean duration of the disease).
- Assumes that the population is stable, and incidence and duration are unchanging.
- Incidence reflects the causal factor.
- Duration reflects the prognostic factor.
- Prevalence describes the balance between incidence, mortality, and recovery.
- The longer the duration of the disease, the greater is its prevalence (e.g., chronic diseases that are neither curable nor rapidly fatal).
- A decrease in prevalence may result not only due to a decrease in incidence but also due to a decrease in the duration of illness.
- The duration of the disease can be shorter under 2 circumstances:
  - Either the disease is very fatal.
  - Or the disease is easily curable.

SCENARIOS AFFECTING PREVALENCE AND INCIDENCE

- If there is a NEW EFFECTIVE TREATMENT (i.e., NEW DRUG) initiated:
  - Incidence will NOT change as there is no change in the number of new cases.
  - If the new drug cures the disease: The duration of the disease will decrease, and so the Prevalence will also decrease.
  - If the new drug DOES NOT cure the disease but decreases mortality: The duration of the disease will be increased, and so the Prevalence will also be increased.
- If there is an IMPROVED PREVENTIVE MEASURE FOR A DISEASE (i.e., NEW VACCINE):
  - Incidence will decrease as there will be a decrease in the number of new cases (incidence decreased).
  - Duration of the disease will remain the same (as a new vaccine does not change the natural history of disease so rapidly).
  - Prevalence will decrease as the incidence has been decreased.
- If the DISEASE IS MORE FATAL:
  - The duration of the disease will decrease, and hence the Prevalence will also decrease.

subsections.title: Epidemiological Methods, Descriptive Epidemiology, and Disease Fluctuations
subsections.content:
EPIDEMIOLOGICAL METHODS

Epidemiological studies are classified into two main types: Observational studies and Experimental studies. The sequence of studies in epidemiology is typically: Descriptive study -> Analytical study -> Experimental study.

1. Observational Studies
- Allow nature to take its own course; the investigator measures but does not intervene.
- Descriptive studies: Used for hypothesis formulation.
- Analytical studies: Used for hypothesis testing.
  - Ecological / Correlational study: The unit of study is a population.
  - Cross-sectional / Prevalence survey / Snapshot of population survey: The unit of study is an individual.
  - Case control study: The unit of study is an individual.
  - Case series study: The unit of study is an individual.
  - Nested case control study: The unit of study is an individual.
  - Cohort study (Prospective cohort, Retrospective cohort, Mixed cohort / combined prospective-retrospective cohort): The unit of study is an individual.

2. Experimental Studies
- Used for hypothesis confirmation.
- Involve an active attempt to change a disease determinant or the progress of a disease.
  - Randomized Controlled Trial (RCT): The unit of study is patients / cases.
  - Field trials: The unit of study is healthy people.
  - Community trials: The unit of study is a community.
  - Clinical trial: The unit of study is patients / cases.

SPECIAL EPIDEMIOLOGICAL STUDIES

- Migration study: Used to study environmental and genetic factors in a disease in a population.
- KAP (Knowledge, Attitude, Practice) study: Was first used in India to study family planning.

UTILITIES OF EPIDEMIOLOGICAL STUDIES FOR ESTABLISHING CAUSALITY

The strength of evidence for establishing causality follows this hierarchy (from strongest to weakest):
- Meta-analysis (strongest)
- Randomized Controlled Trial (RCT)
- Cohort study (retrospective > prospective)
- Case control study
- Cross-sectional study
- Ecological study (weakest)

EVIDENCE BASED MEDICINE (EBM)

- EBM refers to the use of various research findings for taking decisions about best patient care.
- Aim is to apply the best available evidence gained from the scientific method to clinical decision making.
- It is the gold standard for clinical practice.
- Father of Evidence based medicine: David Sackett.
- Statistical parameters used: Likelihood ratios and receiver operator characteristic curve.
- Highest importance is given to the strongest epidemiological studies (Most important: Meta-analysis, systematic review and blind trials). Highest importance overall is given to meta-analysis.

EVIDENCE BASED PYRAMID

Arranged from highest clinical relevance (gold standard) to lowest clinical relevance:
1. Meta analysis
2. Systematic review
3. Randomized Controlled Trial (RCT)
4. Cohort studies
5. Case control studies
6. Case series
7. Case report
8. Ideas, editorial, opinions
9. Animal research
10. In vitro / test tube research

DESCRIPTIVE EPIDEMIOLOGY

- It is the study of the distribution of disease or health-related characteristics in human populations and identifying the characteristics with which the disease seems to be associated.
- It is the best study for the assessment of an unknown or new disease with no etiological hypothesis.

PROCEDURES IN DESCRIPTIVE STUDIES

1. Defining the population to be studied.
2. Defining the disease under study.
3. Describing the disease by Time, Place, and Person.
4. Comparing with known indices.
5. Formulation of a hypothesis.

DISEASE OCCURRENCE TERMINOLOGY

- Epidemic: Unusual occurrence of a disease in a community, clearly in excess of expected occurrence.
  - Outbreak: A small localized epidemic.
  - The area is declared free of an epidemic when no new case is reported from twice the incubation period of the disease since the last case.
  - Verification of diagnosis is the first step in the investigation of an epidemic.
- Endemic: Constant presence or usual or expected frequency of a disease within a given geographic area.
  - Hyper-endemic: Constant presence of disease at high incidence / prevalence, and affects all age groups.
  - Holo-endemic: High level of infection beginning early in life, affecting mostly children.
  - For the disease to be endemic in a steady state: R0 x S = 1. (R0 = basic reproduction number of an infection, which is the number of secondary cases caused by a single case in a population with no immunity and intervention; S = proportion of susceptibles in the population).
  - Endemic curve: It is not a straight line as the number of cases for the endemic disease in a population will not be fixed throughout a year but it will show seasonal variations. Endemic curve and epidemic curve are different as the baseline of an endemic curve never touches zero.
- Pandemic: An epidemic usually affecting a large proportion of the population over a wide geographic area such as an entire nation, a continent, or the world (country to country spread). Pandemics are typically caused by influenza A.
- Sporadic: Scattered cases, widely separated in space and time, and show no connection with each other. Occurrence of disease in a haphazard or irregular pattern. No recognizable source of infection.
- Ecdemic: A disease that originates outside of the area in which it occurs.
- Exotic disease: A disease imported into a country which was not otherwise present.

TIME DISTRIBUTION OF DISEASE

I. Short Term Fluctuation (Epidemics)
1. Common source epidemics
  - Single exposure or point source epidemics: Explosive in nature, showing a sharp rise and sharp fall in the number of cases. There is a clustering of cases in a narrow interval of time. All cases develop within one incubation period of the disease. The epidemic curve is uniform with no secondary waves. Examples: food poisoning, Bhopal gas tragedy.
  - Continuous or repeated or multiple exposure epidemics: Shows a sharp rise in the number of cases, but the fall in the number of cases is interrupted by secondary waves / peaks. Examples: STD among prostitutes, a contaminated well in a village, Legionnaire's disease outbreak in Philadelphia (1976).
2. Propagated epidemics
  - Shows a gradual rise and gradual fall over a long period of time (tails off over a much longer period of time).
  - Results from person-to-person transmission.
  - Transmission continues till no susceptibles are left or susceptibles are no longer exposed to infected individuals.
  - Speed of spread depends on herd immunity and secondary attack rate.
  - Examples: HIV, TB, Polio, Hepatitis A.

II. Periodic Fluctuation
1. Seasonal trend: It is a seasonal variation of a disease occurrence which may be related to environmental conditions. Examples: URI infections peak in the winter season, GIT infections peak in the summer season.
2. Cyclical trend: Examples: Rubella peaks every 6 to 9 years; Influenza peaks every 7 to 10 years.

III. Long Term or Secular Trends
- Trends that show changes over a long period of time or decades.
- Diseases that have increased in the last few decades: CHD, cancer, diabetes.
- Diseases that have decreased in the last few decades: Polio, diphtheria, typhoid.

PLACE DISTRIBUTION OF DISEASE

1. Local variation: Studied using spot maps or shaded maps. Example: Location of fatal cholera cases around the Broad Street pump mapped by Dr. John Snow.
2. Rural - urban variation: 
  - Rural areas have more zoonotic diseases and soil-transmitted helminths.
  - Urban areas have more accidents and CVD.
3. National variation: 
  - Northern region: Goiter.
  - Southern region: Malaria.
  - Central region: Lathyrism.
4. International variation: 
  - Japan: High rates of stomach cancer.
  - India: High rates of oral cavity cancer.

PERSON DISTRIBUTION OF DISEASE

1. Age:
  - Childhood: Measles.
  - Middle age: Cancer.
  - Old age: Atherosclerosis.
2. Sex:
  - Females: Hyperthyroidism.
  - Males: Lung cancer.
3. Marital status:
  - Married females: Cancer cervix.
  - Mortality rates are generally lower in married males and females.
4. Occupation:
  - Coal miners: Pneumoconiosis.
  - Desk jobs: CHD.
5. Social class:
  - Upper class: Non-communicable diseases like DM, HTN.
  - Lower class: Communicable diseases like TB, AIDS.

INVESTIGATION OF AN EPIDEMIC

1. Verification of diagnosis: Lab investigations are used to confirm the diagnosis. It is the first step in the investigation of an epidemic. It is not necessary to examine all cases; taking a sample is sufficient.
2. Confirmation of epidemic: Done by comparing the disease frequencies during the same period of previous years. Epidemic threshold is an arbitrary limit of 2 standard errors from the endemic occurrence.
3. Defining the population at risk: 
  - Obtaining the map of the area: Should contain information concerning natural landmarks, roads, and location of all dwellings. The area may be divided into segments.
  - Counting the population: Carried out by house-to-house visits.
4. Rapid search for all cases: 
  - Medical survey.
  - Using an epidemiological case sheet / case interview form.
  - Searching for more cases at home, family, or neighborhood.
  - Search for new cases is carried out everyday, till the area is declared free of the epidemic (this period is taken as twice the incubation period of the occurrence of the last case).
5. Data analysis: 
  - Analyzed on the basis of Time (construct an epidemic curve).
  - Analyzed on the basis of Place (prepare a spot map).
  - Analyzed on the basis of Person (analyze data by age, sex, occupation, and other risk factors).
6. Formulation of hypothesis: To explain the possible source, causative agent, modes of spread, and environmental factors.
7. Testing of hypothesis: Done by comparing the attack rates.
8. Evaluation of ecological factors: Includes checking water and milk supply, and relating the disease with environmental factors to find the reservoir of infection and modes of transmission.
9. Further investigation of population at risk: Medical examination, screening test, and examination of suspected sample and biochemical studies.
10. Writing the report: Should be complete and convincing. Conducted under headings like background, historical data, methodology, analysis of data, and control measures.

subsections.title: Analytical Epidemiology: Case Control, Cohort, Cross-Sectional, and Ecological Studies
subsections.content:
ANALYTICAL EPIDEMIOLOGY

Analytical epidemiology is the second major type of epidemiological study, used primarily to test aetiological hypotheses. The subject of interest is the individual within the population. The object is to determine whether or not a statistical association exists between a disease and a suspected factor, and if one exists, the strength of the association.

CASE CONTROL STUDY

- Alternative names: Retrospective study, TROHOC study, Backward looking study, Effect to cause study, Outcome to exposure study, Disease to risk factor study.
- Features: Both exposure and outcome (disease) have occurred before the start of the study. The study proceeds backwards from effect to cause. It uses a control or comparison group to support or refute an inference.
- Steps involved in a case control study:
  1. Selection of cases and controls.
  2. Matching.
  3. Measurement of exposure.
  4. Analysis and interpretation.

SELECTION OF CASES AND CONTROLS

- Definition of a case: Requires two specifications. 
  - Diagnostic criteria: The disease and stage must be specified precisely.
  - Eligibility criteria: Only newly diagnosed (incident) cases within a specified period are usually eligible rather than old cases or cases in advanced stages (prevalent cases).
- Sources of cases: Hospitals (single hospital or network) or General population (through surveys or registries).
- Selection of controls: Controls must be free from the disease under study and must be as similar to the cases as possible, except for the absence of the disease.
  - Ratio: If the study group is small, choose up to 4 controls per case (1:4). In larger studies, a 1:1 ratio of cases and controls should be taken.
- Sources of controls:
  - Hospital: Often a source of selection bias, but convenient.
  - Relatives: Spouses and siblings. Sibling controls are unsuitable in genetic studies.
  - Neighborhood: Persons living in the same locality or working in the same factory.
  - General population: Obtained from defined geographic areas.

MATCHING

- Matching is defined as the process by which we select controls in such a way that they are similar to cases with regard to certain pertinent selected variables (e.g., age, sex, occupation, social class) to ensure comparability.
- Confounding factor: A factor associated with both exposure and outcome, and has an independent effect in the causation of outcome (is itself a risk factor). It is unequally distributed between study and control groups. Matching eliminates confounding as it distributes known confounding factors equally in 2 groups.
- Types of matching:
  - Group matching: Age, occupation, social class.
  - Matching in pairs.
  - Caliper matching: Matching of two groups within a specified distance of a variable (e.g., matching age to within 2 years).
  - Frequency matching: Similar frequency variable are matched between 2 groups.
  - Category matching: Matching study and control groups in broad categories (e.g., similar occupation).
  - Individual matching: Identifying an individual subject for comparison resembling a study subject for a matched variable.
  - Pair matching: Pair of study and comparison subjects are made.
- Note: Randomization is superior to both matching and blinding as it removes selection bias, known confounding, and unknown confounding, whereas matching removes only known confounding and blinding removes bias only.

MEASUREMENT OF EXPOSURE

- Obtained by interviews, by questionnaire, or by studying past records (hospital records, employment records).

ANALYSIS OF CASE CONTROL STUDIES

- Exposure rates:
  - Cases = a / (a + c)
  - Controls = b / (b + d)
- Estimation of risk (Strength of association): 
  - Cannot provide with incidences, so relative risk cannot be calculated directly.
  - An estimate of relative risk known as the Odds Ratio (or cross product ratio) is calculated. It is less accurate than relative risk as it is an estimate.
- Odds Ratio (Cross product ratio):
  - Formula: OR = ad / bc
  - Interpretation: Exposed showed a risk of having the disease [OR value] times that of non-exposed. It is an indicator of increased risk of disease in a predisposed population.
  - OR greater than 1: Positive association. The odds that cases were exposed to a risk factor is more than the odds that the controls were exposed (e.g., OCPs and thromboembolism).
  - OR equal to 1: No association. The odds that cases were exposed to a risk factor is the same as the odds that the controls were exposed (e.g., smoking and HIV/AIDS).
  - OR less than 1: Negative association. The odds that cases were exposed to a risk factor is less than the odds that the control were exposed. Shows a protective effect (e.g., regular physical exercise and CHD).

UTILITIES, ADVANTAGES, AND DISADVANTAGES OF CASE CONTROL STUDIES

- Utilities: Investigation of rare diseases, study of multiple exposures, investigation of long latent periods.
- Advantages: Rapid and inexpensive. Suitable to investigate RARE DISEASES. Risk factors can be identified. Can study multiple potential risk factors of a disease. No ethical problems. No attrition problems as no follow-up is required.
- Disadvantages: Selection of appropriate controls may be difficult. Problem of selection bias. Incidence cannot be measured. Relies on memory or past records (recall bias). Cannot distinguish between causes and associated factors.

BIAS IN EPIDEMIOLOGICAL STUDIES

- Bias is any systematic error in an epidemiological study, occurring during data collection, compilation, analysis, and interpretation. Arises from human errors of assessment of the outcome due to the human element.
- 1. Subject bias: Error introduced by study subjects. Participants may subjectively feel better if they knew they were receiving a new treatment.
  - Attention bias / Hawthorne effect: Study subjects may systematically alter their behaviour when they know they are being watched. Seen in cohort study.
  - Memory / Recall bias: Cases are more likely to remember exposure than controls. It is a systematic distortion of retrospective study that can be eliminated by a prospective design.
- 2. Observer / Investigator bias: Investigator may be influenced if he knows beforehand what therapy is given to which patient. Example: Bias due to wrong interpretation of laboratory test.
  - Selection bias / Susceptibility bias: Groups are more susceptible to desired outcome if biased during selection. Occurs during recruitment.
  - Berksonian bias: A type of selection bias due to different rates of admission to a hospital due to different diseases.
- 3. Analyzer bias: Introduced by the analyzer.
- 4. Interviewer bias: Interviewer devotes more time with cases as compared to controls.
- 5. Lead time bias / Zero time shift bias: Bias of over estimation of survival time due to backward shift of starting point.
- 6. Neyman / Prevalence-incidence bias: Bias due to missing of fatal cases, mild / silent cases, and short duration cases from the study.
- 7. Bias in evaluation: Investigator may subconsciously give a favorable report of the outcome of the trial.
- Note: Blinding, Randomization, and Matching help to reduce bias. Garbage in Garbage out (GIGO) means the result can only be as good as the valid original data.

COHORT STUDY

- Alternative names: Prospective study, Longitudinal study, Incidence study, Forward looking study, Cause to effect study, Exposure to outcome study, Risk factor to disease study, Follow-up study.
- Features: Cohorts are identified prior to the appearance of the disease (i.e., they are free of disease). Study groups are observed over a period of time (follow-up). The study proceeds forward from cause to effect.
- Types of Cohort Studies:
  - 1. Prospective cohort studies (Current/Concurrent cohort studies): Outcome has not yet occurred when the study has begun; only exposure has occurred. We look for the development of some disease in both exposed and non-exposed groups. Example: Framingham heart study.
  - 2. Retrospective cohort studies (Historical/Non-concurrent cohort studies): Both exposure as well as outcome have occurred. First we go back in time and take only exposure into consideration, then look for the development of the same disease in both exposed and non-exposed. Example: Effect of fetal monitoring on neonatal deaths; a cohort of nurses with a control group is studied for use of IUD and abdominal pain as a side effect, in a case control manner.
  - 3. Combined Retrospective-Prospective cohort studies (Mixed cohort study): First we go back in time and take only exposure into consideration, later the cohort is followed prospectively into the future for outcome. Example: Court Brown and Doll study on the effect of radiation therapy.

STEPS IN COHORT STUDY

- 1. Selection of study subjects: Can be from the general population or special groups (e.g., Selected groups like doctors, teachers, lawyers; Exposure groups like industrial workers, radiologists).
- 2. Obtaining data on exposure: Personal interviews, review of records, medical examination/tests, environmental surveys.
- 3. Selection of comparison groups: 
  - Internal comparison: According to degree of exposure (e.g., smoking).
  - External comparison: Cohort of radiologists compared with Cohort of ophthalmologists.
  - Comparison with general population rates: If no comparison group is available.
- 4. Follow up: Periodic medical examination, periodic home visits, mailed questionnaires.
- 5. Analysis.

ANALYSIS OF COHORT STUDIES

- Incidence rates:
  - Among exposed = a / (a + b)
  - Among non-exposed = c / (c + d)
- Estimation of risk (Strength of association):
  - 1. Relative Risk (Risk ratio): 
    - Formula: RR = Incidence of disease among exposed / Incidence of disease among non-exposed.
    - RR measures the strength of association between suspected cause and effect.
    - RR = 1 indicates no association (e.g., smoking and HIV/AIDS).
    - RR greater than 1 indicates a positive association between exposure and disease. Interpretation: RR of 10 indicates that the incidence of disease is 10 times higher in the exposed group as compared with the unexposed.
    - RR less than 1 indicates a negative association between exposure and disease. Interpretation: Chances of disease development is less among exposed as compared to non-exposed. An RR of 0.25 indicates a 75 percent reduction in the incidence of disease in the exposed as compared to the unexposed.
  - 2. Attributable Risk (AR):
    - Formula: AR = (Incidence of disease among exposed - Incidence of disease among non-exposed) / Incidence of disease among exposed x 100.
    - Interpretation: Shows how much of the disease is attributed to exposure. It is a good measure of the extent of a public health problem caused by the exposure. 
    - It is the most appropriate method to know about the contribution of risk factors to a disease and to assess the etiological role or factor in a disease.
    - Also known as absolute risk, excess risk, or risk difference.
  - 3. Population Attributable Risk (PAR):
    - Formula: PAR = (Incidence of disease in total population - Incidence of disease among non-exposed) / Incidence of disease in total population x 100.
    - Interpretation: If the risk factor is modified or eliminated, there will be so much annual reduction in the incidence of disease in the given population.
    - PAR is the estimate of the amount of disease that can be reduced if the risk factor is modified / eliminated. It is an important measure for national health policy.

UTILITIES, ADVANTAGES, AND DISADVANTAGES OF COHORT STUDIES

- Utilities: Investigation of rare causes, testing multiple effects, measurement of time relationship, direct incidence measurement.
- Advantages: Incidence and Relative risk can be calculated. No recall bias. Natural history of disease is best studied by cohort study. Done for RARE CAUSES. Useful for CHRONIC DISEASES.
- Disadvantages: Unsuitable for RARE DISEASES. Expensive and time-consuming. Loss to follow up (attrition). The study itself may alter people's behaviour.

FRAMINGHAM HEART STUDY

- A classic cohort study initiated in 1948 by US public health services at Framingham.
- Objective: To study the relationship of risk factors (serum cholesterol, blood pressure, weight, and smoking) and the development of cardiovascular disease.
- Age group: 30-62 years. Sample size: 5127.
- Multiple exposures were studied using multivariate methods.
- Follow up: The study population was examined every 2 years for 20 years.
- Findings: 
  - Increasing risk of CHD with increasing age, more in males.
  - Hypertensives have a greater risk of CHD.
  - Elevated blood cholesterol associated with CHD.
  - Tobacco smoking and habitual use of alcohol increases the risk of CHD.
  - Increased body weight predisposes to CHD.
  - DM increases the risk of CHD.
  - Increased physical activity decreases CHD development.

DIFFERENCES BETWEEN CASE CONTROL AND COHORT STUDY

- Direction: Case control proceeds from effect to cause; Cohort proceeds from cause to effect.
- Starting point: Case control starts with the disease; Cohort starts with people exposed to risk factor or suspected cause.
- Suspected cause frequency: Case control tests whether the suspected cause occurs more frequently in those with the disease than among those without the disease. Cohort tests whether disease occurs more frequently in those exposed, than in those not similarly exposed.
- Subject numbers: Case control involves a fewer number of subjects. Cohort involves a larger number of subjects.
- Time required: Case control yields relatively quick results. Cohort needs a long follow-up period, involving delayed results.
- Suitability: Case control is suitable for the study of rare diseases. Cohort is inappropriate when the disease or exposure under investigation is rare.
- Risk estimation: Case control generally yields only an estimate of RR (odds ratio). Cohort yields incidence rates, RR, as well as AR.
- Multiplicity: Case control cannot yield information about diseases other than that selected for study. Cohort can yield information about more than one disease outcome.
- Cost: Case control is relatively inexpensive. Cohort is expensive.

CROSS-SECTIONAL STUDY

- Also known as a prevalence survey.
- Exposure as well as outcome may coexist at the same time of study.
- Based on a single examination of a cross-section of a population at one point in time. The results of the sample are projected to the whole population.
- It is the simplest form of observational epidemiological study and the least time-consuming study.
- Advantages: Gives a snapshot of a population. Provides the prevalence of a disease. More useful for chronic diseases. Shows the pattern of disease.
- Disadvantages: Tells about the distribution of disease rather than its ETIOLOGY. Cannot establish causality as it does not establish time sequence.

NESTED CASE CONTROL STUDY

- A hybrid design where a case control study is nested in a cohort study.
- Predominantly a type of cohort study due to its forward direction.
- Limited for studies involving rare diseases and whose diagnostic tests are very expensive.
- Only exposure has occurred when the study begins.
- Study design: A population is identified and base line data is obtained from interviews, blood or urine tests, etc. The population is then followed up for a period of time (cohort study) for the development of diseases. A case control study is then carried out. When the disease develops in a population, 2 groups of cases (diseased) and controls (non-diseased) are formed and their exposure status is compared using the baseline data of history and collected samples.
- Study design used for consanguineous marriage and genetic abnormalities.
- Advantages: 
  - Elimination of recall bias: Baseline information is taken at the start of the study.
  - Maintenance of temporal association: If any disease or abnormality in a biological characteristic is noted, it is more likely that it represents risk factors or other pre-morbid characteristics, rather than the manifestation of early subclinical disease. Compared to a routine case control study, the nested case control study avoids problems related to temporal association.

ECOLOGICAL STUDY

- Also known as a Correlational study.
- Provides the least satisfactory type of evidence on causality. It is the least preferable observational / analytical study design.
- The unit of study is a population.
- Uses data that is already available.
- Advantage: Provides a group's characteristics.
- Disadvantage: Socio-economic confounding.
- Ecological fallacy: An error of interpretation in an ecological study, whereby characteristics are ascribed to a group of people which they may not possess as an individual.
- Examples of ecological study: In the UK it was found that there were more deaths from asthma than the sale of anti-asthma drugs.

subsections.title: Experimental Epidemiology, Causation, and Uses of Epidemiology
subsections.content:
CASE SERIES STUDY

- Both outcome as well as exposure have occurred when the study has begun.
- First we take outcome into consideration and then go back in time taking exposure into consideration.
- There is no comparison with non-diseased or controls.
- Example: Patients of glaucoma are identified and surveyed by patient interviews regarding family history of glaucoma.

EXPERIMENTAL EPIDEMIOLOGY

- Experimental studies are used for hypothesis testing and confirmation.
- Experimental trials are longitudinal and prospective.
- Double blinding can be performed on an animal trial.
- Ethical issues on animal is debatable.
- Interim analysis can be done in an experimental trial.
- Types of Experimental Studies:
  - 1. RCT (unit of study is patients).
  - 2. Field trials (unit of study is healthy people).
  - 3. Community trials (unit of study is community).
  - 4. Clinical trial (unit of study is patients/cases).

NATURAL EXPERIMENTS

- If experimental studies are not possible, then natural circumstances can be used in a human population that mimic an experimental study.
- John Snow cholera study is an experiment of natural experiment study.
- Researcher has no control over allocation of subjects.
- Population that can be used: Migrant population, religious group, population affected by nuclear disaster / natural disaster.
- Used to study effects of intervention.

RANDOMIZED CONTROL TRIAL (RCT)

- Gold standard for clinical research is RCT.
- For new programmes and therapies RCT is the best method of evaluation.
- Baseline characteristics of intervention are similar in both arms.
- Baseline characteristics are comparable.
- Investigator bias is minimized by double blinding.
- Sample size required depends on the hypothesis and type of study.
- The dropouts from the trial are not excluded from the study.
- Intention to treat analysis is done in RCT.

STEPS OF RCT

1. Drawing up a protocol
- Specifies the aims and objectives of the study.
- Size of the sample.
- Treatment to be applied: when, where and to whom.

2. Selecting reference and experimental population
- Reference or target population: It is the population to which the findings of the trial, if found successful, are expected to be applicable. May be geographically limited or limited to persons in specific age, sex, occupational or social groups.
- Experimental or study population: Derived from the reference population. Actual population that participates in the study. It should be randomly chosen from the reference population.
- Purpose of control group in an experimental study is that it helps to eliminate alternative explanations for the results of the study.

3. Randomization
- Statistical procedure by which the participants are allocated into groups called study and control groups to receive or not to receive the intervention.
- Randomization is done while dividing patients into experimental group and reference group.
- Randomization is the heart of trial.
- Best done by using a table of random numbers.
- Purpose of randomization:
  - To equalize the effects of extraneous variables, thus guarding against bias.
  - Participants have equal and known chance of falling into either of the 2 groups.
  - To eliminate selection bias.
  - To ensure comparability of 2 groups. To ensure that study groups are comparable on baseline characteristics.
  - To have similar prognostic factor among 2 groups.
  - Facilitates blinding of treatment.
  - Increase internal validity of study.
- Random in randomization means equal and known chance.
- Randomization is better than matching as it removes both confounding and bias. Both known and unknown confounding factors are distributed equally among 2 groups thus nullifying their effect, whereas matching is only useful for known confounding factors.
- Stratified randomization: Ideal to ensure similarity between experimental and control groups. Study population is first stratified by each important variable and than randomization is done to treatment groups within each stratum.

4. Manipulation or intervention
- Deliberate application or removal of causal factor (e.g. drug, vaccine, habit etc.).

5. Follow-up
- Examination of the experimental and control group subjects at defined intervals of time with equal intensity, under the same given circumstances.
- Attrition: Some losses to follow up are inevitable due to factors, such as death, migration and loss of interest.

6. Assessment of outcome
- Positive results: Reduced incidence or severity of disease.
- Negative results: Frequency of side effects and complications.

STUDY DESIGNS IN RCT

1. Concurrent parallel study designs
- Comparison is made between 2 random groups.
- One group exposed to specific treatment (experiment group) and the other group not exposed (reference group).
- Patient remains in the same group throughout the study duration.

2. Crossover type of study designs
- Each patient serves as his own control.
- Study group receives the treatment and control group receives the placebo.
- Patient in each group are taken off from their treatment or placebo to allow elimination of carry over effects (Wash out period).
- The two groups are now switched.
- Those who received treatment will receive placebo and those received placebo will now receive the treatment.
- Both groups acts as exposed as well as non exposed groups.
- Cases acts as their own controls.
- Helps in removing ethical concerns.
- Intention to treat trial: Result of RCT are unaffected by loss to follow up or changeover of study subjects from one group to another.

TYPES OF RCT

- Clinical trials: Concerned with evaluating therapeutic agents, mainly drugs. E.g., trial of aspirin on cardiovascular mortality. Should be carried out before any new therapy is introduced.
- Preventive trials: Trials of primary preventive measures. E.g., trials of vaccines or chemoprophylactic drugs.
- Risk factor trials: Investigator interrupts the usual sequence in the development of disease for those individuals who have risk factors of disease. E.g., multiple risk factor intervention trial (MRFIT) in USA.
- Cessation experiments: Attempt is made to evaluate the effect of termination of habit or removal of agent and reduction of disease.
- Trial of aetiological agents: Aims to confirm or refuse an aetiological hypothesis. E.g., trial for retrolental fibroplasia as a cause of blindness.
- Evaluation of health services: To assess the effectiveness and efficiency of health services. Also called health services research studies.

CLINICAL TRIALS (PHASES)

- Does not have true control group: patients acts as their own control.
- Each patient has pre-test score followed by a post-test score.
- Preclinical phase: Lab experiments on animals. Purpose is pretesting.
- Phase 0: Healthy human volunteers. Purpose is Micro-dosing.
- Phase I: Healthy human volunteers. Purpose is Safety and non toxicity profile. Maximum tolerated dose of a drug is given and so evaluated in this phase.
- Phase II: Patients. Purpose is Efficacy. Maximum drug failure reported.
- Phase III: Patients. Purpose is Comparison with existing drug. It is an RCT as comparison of new drug is done with old drug. New drug is launched in market after phase III.
- Phase IV: Patients. Purpose is Long term and rare side effects. Longest phase of a trial. Post marketing surveillance.
- Note: When an intervention is applied to a community to evaluate its usefulness, it is termed as a trial for effectiveness.

META-ANALYSIS

- An objective and systematic review to combine and summarize the results of several studies.
- Meant for summarizing large volume of data, determining the magnitude of an effect and to increase precision of studies.
- Statistical technique for combining the finding from several independent studies on topic.
- Its purpose is not to identify risk factors but to increase statistical power by increasing sample size.
- Validity depends on the quality of systemic review.
- Meta-analysis can be embedded in systematic review but not always done in systematic review.
- Result: 
  - Forest plot: To describe distribution of effect size. The position of the square is a point estimate of the odds ratio (OR). The size of the square represents the weight of the study according to the weighing rules of the meta-analysis, likely representing the sample size and statistical power.
