The diamond represents the combined results of the trial. The vertical line represents an OR of 1, i.e. line of no effect.
  - Funnel plot: To check publication bias.
- Strength: Provide point estimate of effect size. Report confidence interval around effect size. Identify gaps in a particular field.
- Limitation: 
  - Garbage in Garbage out (GIGO): Result can only be as good as the valid original data.
  - Apple and oranges effect: Tendency to mash together different effects.
  - File drawer effect: Publication bias.

ASSOCIATION AND CAUSATION

- Hills (Surgeon General's) criteria of causal association:
- 1. Temporal association: Cause preceded effect or effect follows cause. Suspected cause preceding the observed effect is an example for temporality. Considers both order of appearance and length of interval between exposure and disease. Most Important / essential criterion of causal association. Best established by a cohort study (especially concurrent cohort study).
- 2. Strength of association: Relative risk (cohort study). Cohort study is associated with antecedent causation. Odds ratio (case control study).
- 3. Specificity of association: Implies that Disease is caused by risk factor. Most difficult criterion to establish. Weakest criterion.
- 4. Consistency of association: Results are replicable in different settings.
- 5. Biological plausibility: Credibility of association by anatomical / physiological justification.
- 6. Coherence of association: Association is supported with relevant facts and studies.
- 7. Dose response relationship: Increase in dose cause increase in incidence of effect.
- 8. Reversibility: Removal of cause reduces risk of disease. Example: current smokers are at higher risk of developing lung cancer as compared to ex smokers.
- Note: RCT are best studies for establishing causation. Ecological study is the weakest study to test the association between risk factor and disease. Indirect association: Example is association of high altitude areas with goitre.

USES OF EPIDEMIOLOGY

1. To study rise and fall of the disease: Study of disease profiles and time trends in human population.
2. Community diagnosis: Identification and quantification of health problems in a community in terms of mortality and morbidity rates and ratios.
3. Planning and evaluation of health services: Epidemiological information about the distribution of health problems over time and place provides the fundamental basis for planning and developing the health services and for assessing the impact of these services.
4. Evaluation of Individual risks: Epidemiologists calculate relative risk and attributable risk for a factor related to be a cause of the disease.
5. Syndrome identification: Medical syndromes are identified by observing frequently associated findings in individual patients.
6. Completing the natural history of disease: Epidemiologists by studying disease patterns in the community in relation to agent, host and environmental factors is in a better position to fill up the gaps in the natural history of disease.
7. Searching for causes and risk factors: Epidemiology, by relating disease to interpopulation differences and other attributes, tries to identify the causes of disease.

INTERNATIONAL DEATH CERTIFICATE (IDC)

- Consists of 5 lines.
- Line I.a: Condition directly leading to death.
- Line I.b: Under lying cause.
- Line I.c: Main underlying cause (if I.d is filled than that becomes main underlying cause).
- Line I.d: Main underlying cause.
- Line II: Other conditions contributing to deaths but not related to condition causing it.
- Line I.c or I.d is the most important line in death certificate thus also known as essence of death certificate.
- Example 1:
  - Line I.a: Renal failure
  - Line I.b: Diabetic nephropathy
  - Line I.c: Diabetes Mellitus
  - Line I.d: [Blank]
  - Line II: Hypertension
- Example 2:
  - Line I.a: Intraperitonial hemorrhage
  - Line I.b: Ruptured metastatic deposits in liver
  - Line I.c: Metastatic deposits in liver
  - Line I.d: Primary adenocarcinoma of ascending colon
  - Line II: Hypertension

subsections.title: Infectious Disease Epidemiology, Transmission Dynamics, and Disease Control
subsections.content:
INFECTIOUS DISEASE EPIDEMIOLOGY DEFINITIONS

- Infection: The entry and development or multiplication of an infectious agent in an organism, including the body of man or animals. It implies that the body responds to defend itself (immune response). An infection does not always cause illness.
- Contamination: The presence of an infectious agent on a body surface, also on or in clothes, beddings, toys, surgical instruments or dressings, or other inanimate articles or substances. Pollution is distinct from contamination.
- Infestation: For persons or animals, the lodgement, development, and reproduction of arthropods on the surface of the body or in the clothing (e.g., lice, itch mite). Also used to describe invasion of the gut by parasitic worms (e.g., ascariasis).
- Host: A person or other living animal that affords subsistence or lodgement to an infectious agent under natural conditions. 
  - Obligate host: The only host (e.g., man in measles and typhoid fever).
  - Definitive / Primary host: Host in which the parasite attains maturity or passes its sexual stage.
  - Intermediate / Secondary host: Host in which the parasite is in a larval or asexual state.
  - Transport host: A carrier in which the organism remains alive but does not undergo development.
- Infectious disease: A disease due to an infectious agent. All infectious diseases and infestations are communicable diseases.
- Contagious disease: A disease that is transmitted through contact (e.g., scabies, trachoma, STD, and leprosy).
- Communicable disease: An illness due to a specific infectious agent or its toxic products that arises through transmission of that agent or its products from an infected person, animal, or reservoir to a susceptible host.
- Zoonoses: An infection or infectious disease transmissible under natural conditions from vertebrate animals to man. 
  - Anthropozoonoses: Infections transmitted from vertebrate animals to man (e.g., rabies, plague, anthrax, hydatid disease, trichinosis).
  - Zooanthroponoses: Infections transmitted from man to vertebrate animals (e.g., human TB in cattle).
  - Amphixenoses: Infections maintained in both man and lower vertebrate animals that may be transmitted in either direction (e.g., T. cruzi, Schistosoma japonicum).
- Epizootic: An outbreak (epidemic) of disease in an animal population.
- Epornitic: An outbreak (epidemic) of disease in a bird population.
- Enzootic: An endemic occurring in animals (e.g., anthrax, rabies, brucellosis).
- Nosocomial infection: Hospital acquired infection originating in a patient while in a hospital or other health care facility, which was not present or incubating at the time of admission.
- Opportunistic infection: Infection with organisms that are normally innocuous but become pathogenic when the body's immunological defenses are compromised (e.g., in AIDS).
- Iatrogenic disease: Doctor-generated adverse effects of preventive, diagnostic, therapeutic, surgical, and other medical procedures.

DISEASE CONTROL, ELIMINATION, AND ERADICATION

1. Disease Control
- The disease agent is permitted to persist in the community at a level where it ceases to be a public health problem.
- Aims in reducing: Incidence of disease (new cases), duration of the disease, effects of infection, and financial burden of the community.

2. Disease Elimination
- Interruption of transmission of disease. 
- The causative organism may still be persisting in the environment.
- India has eliminated 4 diseases till date (Maternal and neonatal tetanus, Yaws, Guinea worm / dracunculiasis, Leprosy).
- Next diseases likely to be eliminated from India: Kala-azar and Lymphatic filariasis.

3. Disease Eradication
- Termination of all transmission of infection by extermination of the infectious agent.
- Tearing out by roots of a disease.
- Exhibits all or none phenomenon.
- It is a global term, meaning it can only be used for the whole planet.
- Small pox is the only disease that has been globally eradicated.
- Next candidates for global eradication: Dracunculiasis, Polio, and Measles.
- Polio virus 2 was declared eradicated on 20 Sep 2015 (This led to the Big Switch from trivalent OPV to Bivalent OPV in April 2016).

SOURCE AND RESERVOIR

- Source and Reservoir of infection are NOT SYNONYMOUS.
- Source: Person, animal, object, or substance from which an infectious agent passes to the host. It is the immediate source of infection which may or may not be a part of the reservoir.
- Reservoir: Any person, animal, arthropod, plant, soil, or substance in which an infectious agent lives and multiplies and on which it depends for survival and reproduction in such a manner that it can be transmitted to a susceptible host.

TYPES OF RESERVOIRS

1. Human reservoir
- Cases: A person having a particular disease or health disorder.
- Carriers: An infected person or animal that harbors a specific agent in the absence of clinical disease and serves as a potential source of infection.
- Carriers are less infectious than cases but are more dangerous epidemiologically.
- Examples where human is the reservoir: Polio, Salmonella typhi, and Chicken Pox.

2. Animal reservoir
- Seen in rabies, influenza, yellow fever, etc.

3. Reservoir in non-living things
- Soil acts as a reservoir for tetanus, anthrax, coccidiomycosis, etc.

EXAMPLES OF SOURCES AND RESERVOIRS

- Hookworm: Source is Soil; Reservoir is Man.
- Tetanus: Source is Soil; Reservoir is Soil.
- Typhoid: Source is Feces / urine / water; Reservoir is Cases / carriers.
- Note: Man is a dead end for tetanus and rabies.

CASES

- Clinical illness: Mild cases may be more important sources of infection than severe cases because they are abundant and ambulant.
- Subclinical illness: Referred to as Inapparent, Covert, Missed, or Abortive Cases. They play a dominant role in maintaining Endemicity in the community. Subclinical infection occurs mostly in infectious diseases (e.g., polio, influenza). Note: Subclinical infection is NOT seen in Rabies.
- Latent infections: The host does not shed the infectious agent which lies dormant within the host without symptoms (e.g., herpes simplex).
- Primary case: The first case of a disease introduced into a population. Always excluded from both the numerator and denominator for secondary attack rate calculation.
- Index case: The first case to come to the attention of the investigator (can be the primary case, but not always).
- Secondary case: Those developing from contact with the primary case.
- Suspect case: Individuals who have all signs and symptoms of a disease but have not yet been diagnosed as having the disease.

CARRIERS

- An infected person that harbors an infectious agent in the absence of clinical disease and serves as a source of infection.
- Carriers are less infectious than cases but are more dangerous epidemiologically.
- Typhoid Mary is a classic example of a carrier.

CLASSIFICATION OF CARRIERS

I. By Type
1. Incubatory carriers: Those who shed the infectious agent during the incubation period of the disease. Examples: measles, mumps, polio, pertussis, influenza, hepatitis B, diphtheria.
2. Convalescent carriers: Continue to shed the disease agent during the period of convalescence (gradual healing after illness). Clinical recovery does not coincide with Bacteriological Recovery. Examples: Typhoid fever, bacillary / amoebic dysentery, cholera, pertussis, diphtheria.
3. Healthy carriers: Victims of subclinical infection who have developed the carrier state without suffering from overt disease. Examples: polio, Salmonellosis, meningococcal meningitis, diphtheria, cholera, typhoid.

II. By Duration
1. Temporary carriers: Shed the infectious agent for short periods of time. Includes incubatory, convalescent, and healthy carriers.
2. Chronic carriers: Excretes infectious agent for indefinite periods. Examples: typhoid, gonorrhoeae, hepatitis B, malaria. Chronic carriers are known to reintroduce disease into areas which are otherwise free of infection.

III. By Portal of Exit
1. Urinary carriers: typhoid.
2. Intestinal carriers: typhoid, cholera, amoebiasis. Note: In typhoid fever, a Urinary carrier is more dangerous than an intestinal carrier.
3. Respiratory carriers.
4. Nasopharyngeal carriers: meningococcus.
5. Nasal carriers: diphtheria.

- Paradoxical carrier: A carrier who gets infected from another carrier.
- Pseudo-carriers: Carriers of avirulent organisms (not important epidemiologically).

DISEASE TRANSMISSION CONCEPTS

- Communicability: Ability of a disease to spread from infective to susceptible hosts.
- Period of Communicability: Time during which an infectious agent may be transferred directly or indirectly from an infected person to another. Important measure of communicability is the Secondary Attack Rate.
- Infectivity: Ability of an infectious agent to invade and multiply in a host. Formula: number of infected / number of exposed.
- Pathogenicity: Formula: number of diseased / number of infected.

CLASSIFICATION OF DISEASE TRANSMISSION

1. Direct Transmission
- Direct contact: Skin to skin contact. Examples: STD, AIDS, Leprosy. Note: The most common route of Nosocomial / hospital acquired infection is through direct contact (Hospital acquired infection of surgical wound is mostly by instruments; Nosocomial infection develops after 48 hours of hospitalization).
- Droplet infection: Direct projection of spray of droplets of saliva and nasopharyngeal secretions during coughing / sneezing. Examples: Pertussis (Whooping cough), tuberculosis.
- Contact with soil: Hookworm, tetanus.
- Inoculation into skin or mucosa: Rabies virus by dog bite, Hepatitis B.
- Transplacental (vertical): TORCH agents (Toxoplasma gondii, Rubella virus, Cytomegalovirus, and Herpes virus).

2. Indirect Transmission
- Traditional 5 F's: Flies, Fingers, Fomites, Food, and Fluid.
- Vehicle borne: Transmission of infectious agent through the agency of water, food, blood, tissue, and organs.
  - Disease transmitted by water and food: typhoid fever, cholera, and hepatitis A.
  - Transmitted by blood include: hepatitis B, malaria, and syphilis.
  - Organ transplantation: cytomegalovirus in kidney transplants.
- Vector borne: By Vector (Invertebrate type or Vertebrate type).
  - Invertebrate Arthropod vectors: Diptera (flies and mosquitoes), Siphonaptera (fleas), Orthoptera (cockroaches), Anoplura (sucking lice), Hemiptera (bugs), Acarina (ticks and mites), Copepoda (cyclops).
  - Vertebrate type: Mice, rodents, bats.
  - By transmission chain: Man and non-vertebrate host (Man-arthropod-man, e.g., malaria); Man, vertebrate host, and non-vertebrate host (Mammal-arthropod-man, e.g., plague); Man and 2 intermediate hosts (Man-snail-fish-man, e.g., clonorchis sinensis).
  - By methods in which vectors transmit agents: Biting, Regurgitation, Scratching in of infective faeces, Contamination of host with body fluids of vectors.
  - Methods of transmission in Arthropod Borne Diseases:
    - Direct contact: Transferred from man to man close contact (e.g., Scabies).
    - Mechanical transmission: Agent is transferred mechanically by the arthropod (e.g., typhoid / trachoma by housefly).
    - Biological transmission: Agent multiplies or undergoes developmental change. Types include:
      - Cyclo-developmental: Disease agent undergoes cyclical changes but does not multiply in the body of the arthropod (e.g., guinea worm embryo in Cyclops, filarial parasite in Culex mosquito).
      - Propagative: Disease agent multiplies in the body of the vector but does not undergo any cyclical changes (e.g., plague bacilli in rat fleas, yellow fever virus in Aedes mosquito).
      - Cyclo-Propagative: Disease agent undergoes cyclical changes and multiplies in the body of arthropod (e.g., malaria parasite in anopheles mosquito).
    - Transovarial transmission: Female vector passes the infectious agent through her eggs to the next generation (e.g., rickettsia rickettsii in ticks).
    - Transstadial transmission: Agent transmitted from nymph to adult vector (e.g., borrelia burgdorferi in ticks).
- Air-borne transmission:
  - Droplet nuclei: Dried residue of droplet. Examples: TB, influenza, measles, diphtheria, pertussis.
  - Dust: Droplets expelled during talking or coughing settle down on the floor and become part of dust. During sweeping, dust is released into the air. Examples: coccidiomycosis, streptococcal, and staphylococcal infections.
- Fomite-borne transmission: Articles contaminated by infectious discharges from a patient (e.g., soiled clothes, linen handkerchiefs, surgical dressings). Examples: Typhoid, diphtheria, hepatitis A, bacillary dysentery, eye and skin infections. Note: Contamination is the presence of an infectious organism on the body, clothing, beds, dressings, instruments, etc.
- Unclean hands and fingers: Intestinal parasites, hepatitis A, dysentery, etc.

INCUBATION PERIOD

- Definition: Time interval between invasion by an infectious agent and appearance of the first sign or symptom of the disease.
- During the incubation period, the agent undergoes multiplication in the host. When sufficient density of the agent is built up, host equilibrium is disturbed and the disease becomes overt.
- Uses of incubation period:
  1. Tracing the source of infection and contacts (Follow the trail of spread of infections).
  2. Determining the Period of surveillance (Usually equal to the maximum incubation period).
  3. Applying Immunization principles (Knowledge of IP helps to prevent clinical illness by active or passive immunizations).
  4. Identification of point source or propagated epidemics (Point source: all cases occur within one incubation period; Propagated: cases occur later than the known length of incubation period).
  5. Estimating prognosis (Prognosis is poor in diseases with a shorter incubation period, e.g., tetanus, rabies).
- Incubation period depends upon: Infective dose, portal of entry, individual susceptibility, and Generation time of the pathogen.
- Generation time: Interval of time between the receipt of infection by a host and the maximal infectivity of that host. Roughly equal to the incubation period.
- Application of incubation period: To differentiate primary case from secondary case, To find out time for quarantine, To prevent infection to the contacts of the infected person.
- Median incubation period: Time required for 50% of the cases to occur following exposure.
- Latent period: Used in Non-infectious Diseases as the equivalent of incubation period in infectious diseases. Defined as the period from disease initiation to disease detection.
- Window period: Time required between Exposure to laboratory detection of disease.
- Proxy measures: Latent period, Serial interval, and Generation time are all used as a proxy measure for incubation period in disease.

MNEMONICS AND RULES FOR INCUBATION / COMMUNICABILITY

- Diseases communicable or highly transmitted during the later part of the incubation period: Measles, Pertussis (whooping cough), Hepatitis A. (Mnemonic: Later He Who Me).
- Diseases with incubation period less than 10 days: Cholera, Influenza, Plague.
- Disease infectious before and after onset of symptoms: Measles, Mumps, Hep B, and Polio. (Mnemonic: Before & after Hip-Po Mum-Me).

ISOLATION

- Separation of Infected Persons from others for the Period of Communicability, to prevent transmission of the infectious agent from the infected to the susceptible.
- Hospital isolation is better than home isolation.
- Today isolation is recommended only when the risk of infection is exceptionally serious.
- Types of isolation: 1. Standard isolation. 2. Strict isolation. 3. Protective isolation. 4. High security isolation.
- Isolation is needed in: Diphtheria, TB, herpes zoster, cholera, staphylococcal pharyngitis, polio, leprosy, pneumonic plague.
- Periods of isolation:
  - Measles: From onset of catarrhal stage through 3rd day of rash.
  - Cholera and Diphtheria: 3 days after tetracycline started, until culture is negative.
  - Influenza: 3 days after onset.
  - Polio: 2 weeks (adults) and 6 weeks (children).
  - TB: Until 3 weeks of chemotherapy.
  - Chicken pox and Herpes zoster: Upto 6 days of onset of rash.
- Ring Immunization: Isolation may also be achieved by encircling the infected persons with a barrier of immune persons through whom infection is unable to spread. Method was applied to eradicate small pox and for the control of measles in North America.
- Isolation has failed in control of disease such as: TB, leprosy, and STD. In these diseases, physical isolation is replaced by Chemical Isolation (i.e., rapid treatment of cases in their own homes and rendering them non-infectious as quickly as possible).

QUARANTINE

- Limitation of freedom of movement of such Well Persons Exposed to communicable disease for a period of time not longer than the Longest Incubation Period of the disease.
- Applied to: Ships, aircrafts, train, road vehicle, etc.
- Comprise of:
  1. Absolute quarantine: As defined above.
  2. Modified quarantine: Selective partial limitation of freedom of movement. Example: exclusion of children from school.
  3. Segregation: Separation of a group of susceptible persons from others to facilitate the control of communicable diseases. Example: removal of susceptible children to homes of immune persons.
- Quarantine as a method of disease control has become outdated and has been replaced by Active Surveillance.

EMPORIATRICS

- Term coined to describe the science of health of travelers.
- International travelers may be subject to resistance to disease due to crowding, disruption of eating habits, change in climate, etc.
- Risk can be minimized by Immunization and Chemoprophylaxis.
- Recommendations for travelers:
  1. Avoid bath in polluted water.
  2. Prevention of insect bites: Mosquito repellents / bed nets.
  3. Prevention of Diarrhoeal diseases: Use boiled water or bottled mineral water, Oral rehydration fluids.
  4. Prevention of Malaria: Prophylaxis begins at the day of arrival in the malarious areas and continued for 4 weeks after leaving the area.
  5. Prevention of Hepatitis A: Immunoglobulin 0.05 mg/kg every 4 months.
  6. Prevention of Hepatitis E: Avoidance of contaminated food and water.
  7. Prevention of Hepatitis B: 3 doses of vaccines (First two doses are given 1 month apart and the third dose about 6 months later).
  8. Prevention of STD and HIV: Avoidance of sex or limit it to a single uninfected partner, Use of condom.
  9. Prevention of Yellow fever: Vaccination certificate for yellow fever is required for international travel, especially in endemic zones.
  10. Prevention of Tetanus: Booster dose of TT if 10 years or more have elapsed since the last injection.
- Medical kit for travelers should have: Disinfectant, sun screen, mosquito repellent, ORS, first aid articles, a card noting blood group, drug sensitivity, and chronic disease suffering from.
- WHO publishes every year: International travel and health, vaccination requirements, and health advice for travelers.

DISINFECTION

- Physical (thermal) or chemical agents that destroy disease-causing pathogens or microorganisms (Except bacterial spores).
- Ideal disinfectant: Broad spectrum, Fast acting, Nontoxic, Odourless, Economical, and Have Residual effects.
- Types of disinfection:
  1. Concurrent disinfection: Application of disinfective measure as soon as possible after soiling of articles with infectious discharge. Example: disinfection of urine.
  2. Terminal disinfection: Application of disinfective measures after the patient has been removed from the hospital due to death or is discharged.
  3. Precurrent disinfection / prophylactic disinfection: Disinfection of water (chlorination), pasteurization of milk, hand washing, etc.
- Sterile: State of being free from all living organisms including spores (e.g., application of sanitizer on hand).
- Asepsis: Prevention of contact with microorganisms (e.g., wearing gloves on hand).

AGENTS USED FOR DISINFECTION

- Physical agents: 
  - Sunlight and Air.
  - Burning / Incineration.
  - Hot air (160-180 deg C for at least one hour to kill spores).
  - Boiling (Rolling boil for 5-10 minutes kills bacteria, but not spores or viruses).
  - Autoclaving (Steam under pressure, 122 deg C under 15 lbs/sq. inch; most effective method for sterilization).
  - Radiation (Ionizing radiation, UV radiation).
- Chemical agents:
  - Phenol and related compounds: Crude phenol, Cresol, Chlorhexidine (hibitane), Hexachlorphane, Dettol.
  - Quaternary ammonia compounds: Cetrimide (Cetavlon), Savlon.
  - Halogens: Bleaching powder (contains about 33 percent of available chlorine), Iodine, Iodophores (Betadine).
  - Alcohols: Ethyl and isopropyl alcohols (60-90 percent).
  - Formaldehyde: Highly toxic and irritant gas.
  - Oxidizing agents: Potassium permanganate, Hydrogen peroxide.
  - Metals: Silver, iron, copper.
  - Lime: Cheapest of all disinfectants.

HOST DEFENCES (IMMUNITY)

- Active immunity: The immunity which an individual develops as a result of infection or by specific immunization. It takes time to develop but is frequently long-lasting. It involves both humoral and cellular responses.
  - Primary response: First exposure to an antigen. Has a latent period of 3-10 days. IgM antibody is elicited first, followed by IgG.
  - Secondary (Booster) response: Response to a booster dose. Has a shorter latent period, more rapid and abundant antibody production (mainly IgG), and is maintained at higher levels for a longer period. Forms the basis of vaccination.
- Passive immunity: Antibodies produced in one body (human or animal) are transferred to another to induce protection. It is rapidly established but is only temporary (days to months). Used when immediate protection is required (e.g., normal human Ig, specific human Ig, antisera or antitoxins).

subsections.title: Immunization, Cold Chain, National Immunization Schedule, and AEFI
subsections.content:
IMMUNIZATION AND VACCINES

- Vaccine: An immuno-biological substance designed to produce specific protection against a given disease. It stimulates the production of protective antibodies and other immune mechanisms.
- Types of vaccines: Live attenuated vaccines, Inactivated / killed vaccines, Toxoids, Cellular fraction vaccines, and Recombinant / mixed vaccines.

1. Live Attenuated Vaccines
- Prepared from live or wild (generally attenuated) organisms. They lose their capacity to induce full-blown disease but retain their immunogenicity.
- More potent immunizing agents than killed vaccines because they multiply in the host, have all major and minor antigenic components, and engage certain tissues (e.g., intestinal mucosa by OPV).
- Examples: BCG, OPV(Sabin), Measles, Mumps, Rubella, Yellow fever, Varicella, Rotavirus, JE, Cold-adapted influenza.
- Contraindications: Should not be administered to persons with immune deficiency diseases, leukemia, lymphoma, malignancy, or those on immunosuppressive therapy (corticosteroids, radiation). Pregnancy is another absolute contraindication (unless the risk of infection exceeds the risk to the fetus, such as in high-risk Yellow Fever exposure).
- 2 live vaccines can be administered simultaneously at different sites or at an interval of 3 to 4 weeks. Exception: Cholera vaccine and yellow fever vaccine CANNOT be given together.
- Live vaccines should not be given till 12 weeks of immunoglobulin administration. Immunoglobulins should not be given till 2 weeks from live vaccine.

2. Inactivated / Killed Vaccines
- Organisms are killed by heat or chemicals. They stimulate active immunity when introduced in the body.
- Less efficacious than live vaccines. Usually require a primary series of 2 or 3 doses to produce an adequate antibody response, and booster injections are required.
- Usually administered by I/M or S/C route.
- Absolute contraindication: A severe local or general reaction to a previous dose.
- Examples: IPV (Salk), Pertussis, Cholera, Typhoid (injectable), Rabies, Hepatitis A.

3. Toxoids, Cellular Fractions, and Recombinant Vaccines
- Toxoid: Toxins produced by organisms are detoxicated and used for vaccines. Examples: Diphtheria toxoid, Tetanus toxoid.
- Cellular fraction: Vaccines prepared from extracted cellular fractions. Examples: Pneumococcal, Meningococcal.
- Recombinant / mixed vaccines: Synthesized from cloned genes using DNA technology. Examples: Hepatitis B (inserted into a yeast cell), HPV. Mixed vaccines: Pentavalent, DPT, DT, MMR.

SPECIFIC VACCINE STRAINS

- BCG: Danish 1331 strain (Derived from Mycobacterium bovis).
- OPV / IPV: P1, P2, P3.
- Measles: Edmonston Zagreb strain.
- Mumps: Jeryll Lynn strain.
- Rubella: RA 27/3.
- JE (Japanese Encephalitis): Nakayama strain, SA-14-14-2 (cell culture derived live attenuated).
- Swine flu (killed): A7 / California / 2009. (Trivalent influenza vaccine contains 2 influenza A strains and 1 influenza B strain. Administered as nose drops).
- Typhoid: Ty 21 A (For typhoid, the best vaccine is typhoid oral).
- Malaria: SPF 66 (lytic cocktail) and RTS,S / AS01 (Mosquirix).
- Chickenpox: OKA.
- HPV vaccine: Bivalent (Cervarix - HPV16,18), Quadrivalent (Gardasil - HPV 6,11,16,18), and Nanovalent (Gardasil 9 - HPV 6, 11, 16, 18, 31, 33, 45, 52, 58).
- Cholera vaccine: Dukoral, Sanchol, mORCVAX, Orochol.
- Dengue: CYD-TDV.
- Meningococcal: A, C, W135, Y.
- Leprosy: Mycobacterium Indicus Pranii (MIP).
- Yellow fever: 17D.
- Vaccine strain which changes every year: Influenza.
- Hib conjugate vaccine: PRP (Polyribosyl Ribitol Phosphate) with carrier.

VACCINE EFFICACY AND IMMUNITY FACTS

- Efficacy of vaccines (single dose): Rubella (highest, 95 percent), Measles (90 percent), OPV (65-80 percent), BCG (50 percent), TT (70 percent).
- Colostrum is known as the 1st immunization of the baby.
- Congenital passive immunity is found in: Polio, mumps, measles, etc.
- Most common cause of vaccine failure: Maternally derived antibodies.
- Secondary booster response (compared to primary response): Shorter latent period, antibody level is maximum, antibody response is maintained at higher levels for a longer period of time.
- Antisera is obtained from horses.
- Premunition: State of active immunity. Protects an individual. Immunity depends on the presence of an inactive infection with the same species in the host. Species specificity is present.

THE COLD CHAIN

- A system of storage and transport of vaccine at low temperature from the manufacturer to the actual vaccination site.
- 6 Rights of supply chain: Right Vaccine in the Right Quantity at the Right Place at Right Time in the Right Condition and at the Right Cost.
- Reverse Cold Chain: Seen in poliomyelitis, when a stool specimen is sent for testing from the field to the laboratory under low temperature.

TEMPERATURE SENSITIVITY OF VACCINES

- Heat and Freeze sensitive vaccines are grouped into categories based on sensitivity.
- Most heat sensitive vaccines: OPV (Group A). Then Measles, BCG, Rota. Least heat sensitive (Group F): Hepatitis B, TT, DPT.
- Freeze sensitive vaccines (DO NOT FREEZE): DPT, TT, DT, Td, Hepatitis B, IPV, Pentavalent, Liquid PCV.
- Light sensitive vaccines: BCG, Measles, MR, Rubella. (Supplied in amber color vials to protect from UV light).

STORAGE TEMPERATURES

- The Cold Chain Temperature of vaccines in India is +2 to +8 degree C.
- Most vaccines can be stored up to 5 weeks at +2 to +8 degree C.
- OPV: Routine storage at +2 to +8 degree C (in ILR). Long term storage at -15 to -25 degree C (in Deep Freezer).
- Diluents: Kept between +2 to +8 degree C or outside the cold chain at room temperature, but must be cooled in the ILR for 24 hours prior to reconstitution to prevent thermal shock between diluent and vaccine.
- Yellow fever vaccine: Stored at -30 to +5 degree C.
- Vitamin A: Stored outside cold chain at room temperature.
- Reconstituted vaccines: Must be kept at +2 to +8 degree C. Can be kept for 4 hours (BCG and Measles/MR) and for 2 hours (JE).

COLD CHAIN EQUIPMENT (CCE)

1. Walk in freezer (WIF)
- Temperature: -15 to -25 degree C.
- Used for preparation of frozen ice packs and bulk storage of OPV at national, state, and regional levels.

2. Walk in cold rooms (WIC)
- Temperature: +2 to +8 degree C.
- Storage of large quantities of vaccines at government medical store depots, state, and regional vaccine stores.

3. Deep freezers (DF)
- Temperature: -15 to -25 degree C.
- Available at district and sub-district level.
- Used for storing OPV vaccine for 3 months (at district level) and for freezing of ice packs (at sub-district level).

4. Ice Lined Refrigerator (ILR)
- Most important component of the cold chain in India. Top opening to prevent loss of cold air.
- Temperature: +2 to +8 degree C. Can maintain temperature for long hours of uninterrupted electricity failure due to ice lining.
- Placement of vaccines inside ILR:
  - Freeze sensitive vaccines (DPT, TT, Hepatitis B, IPV, Pentavalent, Diluents) are kept in the UPPER PART / basket because they cannot be frozen.
  - Heat sensitive vaccines (OPV, BCG, Measles, JE) are kept in the LOWER PART / bottom, as cold air settles down.

5. Domestic refrigerators (front load)
- Used in private clinics.
- Top shelf (freezer): Ice packs.
- Middle shelf: Pentavalent, Td, Hepatitis B, IPV, JE.
- Below freezer shelf: OPV, Rota, Measles, BCG.
- Bottom shelf / Door: Ice packs filled with water (never store vaccines in the door).

6. Cold boxes
- Used for vaccine transportation and storage during defrosting or power failure.

7. Vaccine carriers
- Capacity: 16-20 vials. Uses 4 fully frozen ice packs.

8. Day carriers
- Capacity: 6-8 vials. Uses 2 ice packs.

9. Ice packs
- Prepared by keeping in a deep freezer.
- Contains only plain water (no salt added).
- Conditioning of ice packs: Before use, fully frozen ice packs must be kept at room temperature until they sweat (water droplets form) and water sloshes inside when shaken. This prevents freeze-sensitive vaccines from freezing when placed next to them in a carrier.

COLD CHAIN LEVELS IN INDIA

- State level: WIC and WIF. 3 months of storage.
- District level: Large ILRs and Large DFs. 1 month of storage.
- CHC / PHC level: Small ILRs and Small DFs. 1 month of storage. PHC is the focal point of the cold chain.
- Sub-centre level: Vaccine carrier and Day carrier. 48-72 hours of storage. Maximum chance of cold chain failure in India is at the sub-centre level and village level (Anganwadi centre).

MONITORING HEAT EXPOSURE

1. Dial thermometer
- Used to monitor the temperature of the cold chain at the PHC.
- Kept inside the ILR. Twice daily temperature recording is done.

2. Vaccine Vial Monitor (VVM)
- Heat sensitive material (Para-toluene sulphonate) placed on the vaccine vial to register cumulative heat exposure over time.
- Indicates potency and efficiency of cold chain maintenance.
- Consists of an Outer Circle and an Inner Square. Only the inner square is made of heat-sensitive material, which changes color. The outer circle always remains the same.
- Stages of VVM:
  - Stage 1: Inner square is lighter (white) than the outer circle. USE THE VACCINE.
  - Stage 2: Inner square is still lighter (light blue) than the outer circle. USE THE VACCINE.
  - Stage 3 (Discard point): Color of the inner square matches (blue) that of the outer circle. DO NOT USE.
  - Stage 4: Color of the inner square is darker (purple/black) than the outer circle. DO NOT USE.
- 4 types of VVM based on heat sensitivity: VVM2, VVM7, VVM14, VVM30 (the number indicates days it takes for the inner square to reach the discard point if exposed to a constant 37 degree C).

SHAKE TEST

- Conducted to identify a frozen vaccine (freeze-sensitive vaccines like DPT, Hep B, Pentavalent).
- Procedure: Take a suspect vial. Take a control vial from the same batch and deliberately freeze it solid overnight, then let it thaw. Shake both vials vigorously and place them side-by-side to observe sedimentation for 30 minutes.
- Result: If the suspect vial sediments SLOWER than the control vial, it has not been damaged -> USE IT. If the suspect vial sediments at the SAME RATE or FASTER than the control vial, it is damaged -> DO NOT USE IT.

OPEN VIAL POLICY (2015)

- Allows reuse of partially used multidose vials of applicable vaccines in subsequent sessions for up to 4 weeks (28 days).
- Applicable to: bOPV, IPV, Pentavalent, Hepatitis B, DPT, Td, TT.
- NOT applicable to: Live lyophilized vaccines (Measles, BCG, JE) and Rotavirus vaccine. These must be destroyed after 48 hours or before the next session, whichever is earlier (JE is discarded after 2 hours; BCG/Measles after 4 hours).
- Pre-requisites for Open Vial Policy:
  - Expiry date has not passed.
  - Vaccines are stored strictly under appropriate temperature in the cold chain.
  - Vaccine vial septum has not submerged in water or been contaminated.
  - Aseptic technique used to withdraw doses.
  - VVM has not reached the discard point.

NATIONAL IMMUNIZATION SCHEDULE (NIS), INDIA (2020)

For Pregnant Women:
- Tetanus Toxoid (TT) / Td-1: Early in pregnancy. (0.5 ml, I/M, Upper Arm).
- TT / Td-2: 4 weeks after TT-1. (0.5 ml, I/M, Upper Arm).
- TT / Td-Booster: If received 2 TT doses in a pregnancy within the last 3 years. (0.5 ml, I/M, Upper Arm).

For Infants:
- At birth:
  - BCG: At birth or as early as possible till 1 year. (0.1 ml ID; but 0.05 ml if less than 1 month old). Left Upper Arm.
  - Hepatitis B (Birth dose): Within 24 hours. (0.5 ml, I/M). Antero-lateral side of mid-thigh.
  - OPV-0: Within the first 15 days. (2 drops, Oral).
- At 6 weeks:
  - OPV-1 (2 drops, Oral).
  - Pentavalent-1 (0.5 ml, I/M, Antero-lateral side of mid-thigh).
  - Rotavirus-1 (5 drops, Oral).
  - fIPV-1 (0.1 ml, I/D, Right upper arm).
  - PCV-1 (0.5 ml, I/M, Antero-lateral side of mid-thigh).
- At 10 weeks:
  - OPV-2 (2 drops, Oral).
  - Pentavalent-2 (0.5 ml, I/M).
  - Rotavirus-2 (5 drops, Oral).
- At 14 weeks:
  - OPV-3 (2 drops, Oral).
  - Pentavalent-3 (0.5 ml, I/M).
  - Rotavirus-3 (5 drops, Oral).
  - fIPV-2 (0.1 ml, I/D, Right upper arm).
  - PCV-2 (0.5 ml, I/M).
- At 9-12 months:
  - MR 1st dose (0.5 ml, S/C, Right upper arm).
  - JE-1 (0.5 ml, S/C, Left upper arm).
  - Vitamin A 1st dose (1 ml = 1 lakh IU, Oral).
  - PCV Booster (0.5 ml, I/M, Antero-lateral side of mid-thigh).

For Children:
- At 16-24 months:
  - MR 2nd dose (0.5 ml, S/C, Right upper arm).
  - JE-2 (0.5 ml, S/C, Left upper arm).
  - DPT Booster-1 (0.5 ml, I/M, Antero-lateral side of mid-thigh).
  - OPV Booster (2 drops, Oral).
- 16-18 months up to 5 years:
  - Vitamin A 2nd to 9th dose. Given every 6 months. (2 ml = 2 lakh IU, Oral).
- At 5-6 years:
  - DPT Booster-2 (0.5 ml, I/M, Upper arm).
- At 10 years and 16 years:
  - TT / Td (0.5 ml, I/M, Upper arm).

AGE LIMITS FOR DELAYED IMMUNIZATION IN NIS

- Hepatitis B birth dose: Upto 24 hours.
- bOPV 0 (zero) dose: Upto 15 days.
- Rota, fIPV, PCV, Pentavalent, BCG (direct BCG): Upto 1 year.
- Hib: Upto 2 years.
- bOPV 1/2/3, Vitamin A, MR: Upto 5 years.
- DPT booster 1 and 2: Upto 7 years.
- JE: Upto 15 years.
- TT / Td: Upto 16 years.

CASES OF DELAYED IMMUNIZATION EXAMPLES

- Completely unimmunized child of 9 months: Receives BCG (direct), OPV-1, Pentavalent-1, Rota-1, fIPV-1, PCV-1, MR-1, JE-1, and Vitamin A (1 lakh IU).
- Completely unimmunized child of 18 months: Receives BCG (indirect, after Mantoux test), OPV-1, DPT-1, Hib-1, MR-1, JE-1, and Vitamin A (2 lakh IU).
- Fully immunized child: Has taken all vaccines till 1 year of age.
- Completely immunized child: Has taken all vaccines till 2 years of age.

MISSION INDRADHANUSH

- Started on 25th December 2014 by Ministry of Health and Family Welfare.
- Aim: To fully immunize unvaccinated or partially vaccinated children and achieve >90 percent coverage.
- Depicts 7 colors of the rainbow for prevention against 7 vaccine-preventable diseases initially: Childhood TB (BCG), Polio, Hepatitis B, Diphtheria, Pertussis, Tetanus, Measles.
- Later included 5 more: H. influenzae type b, Rotavirus, Pneumonia, Rubella, Japanese Encephalitis. (Total 12 diseases covered).
- Intensified Mission Indradhanush (IMI) launched in October 2017 to cover left-outs and drop-outs. Target is to achieve >90 percent coverage. 4 rounds of 7 days per month.
- High-risk areas identified for IMI: Vacant subcentres, unserved/low coverage pockets, urban slums with migratory populations, nomadic sites, brick kilns.
- New mobile app for IMI: Kilkari app.

SPECIFIC VACCINES: DETAILED PROFILES

1. BCG Vaccine (Bacille Calmette Guerin)
- Live attenuated vaccine. Liquid fresh type or Freeze dried type (more stable and used currently).
- Danish 1331 strain (Derived from Mycobacterium bovis).
- Reconstituted with Normal Saline (NaCl) and must be used within 4 hours. Diluent is also kept cold.
- Route: Intradermally (I/D) over the left deltoid using a Tuberculin syringe (26-gauge needle).
- Dose: 0.1 ml. For infants birth to 28 days: 0.05 ml.
- Site of vaccine must NOT be cleaned with spirit as it kills the live component.
- Scar phenomenon: Papule formation (2-3 weeks), Ulcer covered with crust (6-8 weeks), Scar formation (6-12 weeks), Mantoux test becomes positive (8-14 weeks).
- Protective efficacy: Severe form of TB (50 percent), Leprosy (30 percent). Not effective for pulmonary TB (0 percent).
- Complications: Suppurative lymphadenitis.

2. Measles Vaccine
- Live attenuated, lyophilized, freeze-dried. Amber colored vial.
- Edmonston Zagreb strain.
- Route: Subcutaneous (S/C). Dose: 0.5 ml. Site: Right upper arm.
- Diluent: Distilled water or Sterile water. Use within 4 hours after reconstitution.
- Efficacy: >90 percent with one dose, >99 percent with 2 doses.
- Side effects: Toxic Shock Syndrome (TSS) if used past 4 hours. Fever with rash, SSPE (rare).
- Vaccine induced measles has an incubation period of 7 days.

3. Oral Polio Vaccine (OPV)
- Sabin (Live attenuated). Bivalent (Type 1 and 3) used since the "Big Switch" on 25 April 2016.
- Primary multiplication occurs in intestinal epithelial cells. Secondary multiplication in Peyer's patches (leads to viremia).
- Includes both systemic and local (gut) immunity.
- Dose: 2 drops (equivalent to 0.1 ml) orally.
- Stabilizer: MgCl2.
- Thermolabile. Kept at +2 to +8 degree C in ILR, or -15 to -25 degree C in Deep Freezer for long term.
- Advantages: Easy to administer, includes humoral and systemic immunity, spreads to others by excretion (contact immunity) helping herd immunity.
- Complications: 
  - Vaccine Associated Paralytic Polio (VAPP): Most commonly caused by type 3. Occurs in the recipient or close non-immune contact.
  - Vaccine Derived Polio Virus (VDPV): Most commonly caused by type 2. Genetically changed from the strain in the vaccine.

4. Inactivated Polio Vaccine (IPV)
- Salk (Killed vaccine). Induces humoral immunity only. No contact immunity.
- Safe in immunodeficiency disorders and pregnancy. No risk of VAPP and VDPV.
- Fractional IPV (fIPV): 0.1 ml Intradermal (I/D) on the right upper arm. Two fractional doses of fIPV given I/D produce higher seroconversion and better immunogenicity than a single standard IM dose.

5. Rotavirus Vaccine
- Live attenuated vaccine.
- Open vial policy does NOT apply. Should be used within 4 hours of opening.
- Route: Oral. Dose: 5 drops.
- Rotarix: Monovalent, human strain, 2 doses.
- RotaTeq: Pentavalent, bovine & human reassortant strain, 3 doses.
- Should not be initiated (first dose) for age > 12 weeks. When the first dose is given after 12 weeks it may cause intussusception. Therefore, avoided in catch-up campaigns where exact age is not certain.

6. DPT Vaccine
- Diphtheria, Tetanus (Toxoids) and Pertussis (Killed whole cell bacilli).
- Adjuvant: Aluminum phosphate or hydroxide (increases immunogenicity).
- Preservative: Thiomersal.
- Stored at +2 to +8 degree C. Freeze sensitive.
- Contraindication: Progressive neurologic disease, active epilepsy, cerebral palsy. In such cases, DT is given.
- Side effects: Inconsolable cry, fever.

7. Pneumococcal Conjugate Vaccine (PCV)
- Killed and Conjugated vaccine. Polyvalent (PCV13 in India).
- Open vial policy is applicable.
- Route: I/M. Dose: 0.5 ml. Site: Antero-lateral aspect of mid-thigh.
- Stored at +2 to +8 degree C. DO NOT FREEZE.

8. Pentavalent Vaccine
- Diphtheria, Pertussis, Tetanus, Hepatitis B, and Haemophilus Influenzae type b (Hib).
- Route: I/M. Dose: 0.5 ml.
- Stored at +2 to +8 degree C. DO NOT FREEZE.
- Contraindications: Severe allergic reactions to an earlier dose. Moderate or severe acute illness. (However, minor illnesses like URI are NOT a contraindication).

9. Mycobacterium Indicus Pranii (MIP) Vaccine
- Leprosy vaccine developed by Dr. Pran Talwar in India.
- Given to close contacts of leprosy patients.
- Dose: 2 doses (6 months apart).
- Given along with MDT (Rifampicin). Decreases bacterial load and shortens treatment.

ADVERSE EVENTS FOLLOWING IMMUNIZATION (AEFI)

- Definition: Any untoward medical occurrence which follows immunization and which does not necessarily have a causal relationship with the usage of the vaccine.
- Classifications of AEFI:
  1. Vaccine product-related reaction: Caused by inherent properties of the vaccine (e.g., extensive limb swelling following DTP).
  2. Vaccine quality defect-related reaction: Caused by a manufacturing defect (e.g., failure to inactivate polio vaccine leading to paralytic polio).
  3. Immunization error-related reaction: Caused by inappropriate handling, prescribing, or administration. Preventable. (e.g., transmission of infection by a contaminated multidose vial, TSS due to unsterile reconstitution).
  4. Immunization anxiety-related reaction: Arising from anxiety (e.g., vasovagal syncope).
  5. Coincidental event: Caused by something other than the vaccine product (e.g., fever occurring at the time of vaccination but caused by malaria).

INVESTIGATION OF AEFI

- Step 1: Confirm the report (obtain medical file, check details).
- Step 2: Investigate and collect data (immunization history, medical history, shipment, cold chain tracking, observe service in action).
- Step 3: Assess the service (vaccine storage, reconstitution, sterilization, training).
- Step 4: Formulate a hypothesis.
- Step 5: Test the hypothesis (laboratory tests for sterility of vial/diluent/syringe).
- Step 6: Conclude investigation and take corrective action.
- AEFI Cluster: Defined as two or more cases of the same adverse event related in time, place, or vaccine administration.

SPECIFIC AEFI REACTIONS AND TREATMENTS

- Toxic Shock Syndrome (TSS): Abrupt onset of fever, vomiting, and watery diarrhoea within a few hours of immunization. Often leads to death in 24-48 hours. Usually an indicator of programme error (Staph aureus contamination). Treat urgently with parenteral antibiotics.
- Anaphylaxis: Severe, immediate (within 1 hour) allergic reaction with circulatory failure or bronchospasm. Treat immediately with Adrenaline 1:1000 IM (0.5 ml for adults).
- Fainting (Syncope) vs Anaphylaxis: 
  - Fainting: Occurs before, during, or a few mins after injection. Pallor, cold clammy skin, bradycardia (slow pulse), normal breathing, relieved by supine posture.
  - Anaphylaxis: Occurs up to a few hours after. Urticaria, erythema, tachycardia (weak fast pulse), tachypnea (wheezing/stridor), NOT relieved by supine posture.
- Suppurative lymphadenitis: Often caused by BCG (within 2-6 months). Best not to treat unless sticking to the skin. Surgical drainage or anti-tuberculous drugs if needed.

title: Screening for Disease
content:
OVERVIEW OF THE CHAPTER
The chapter focuses on the principles and methodologies of screening for unrecognized diseases in apparently healthy populations. It covers the iceberg phenomenon of disease, the crucial differences between screening and diagnostic tests, the concept of lead time, types of screening, criteria for evaluating diseases and screening tests, and methods for assessing the effectiveness of screening programs.

DEFINITIONS
Screening: Defined as the active search for unrecognized disease or defect by means of rapidly applied tests, examinations, or other procedures in apparently healthy individuals. It is a presumptive identification of disease.
Lead Time: The period between diagnosis by early detection (via screening) and the usual time of diagnosis. It is the advantage gained by screening.
Validity (Accuracy): The ability of a test to accurately measure what it purports to measure, distinguishing those who have the disease from those who do not.
Repeatability (Reliability/Precision): The ability of a test to give consistent results when repeated more than once on the same individual or material under the same conditions.
Yield: The amount of previously unrecognized disease that is diagnosed as a result of the screening effort.

CORE CONCEPTS

Iceberg Phenomenon of Disease
- Epidemiologists observe that the pattern of disease in hospitals is quite different from that in the community. A far larger proportion of disease (e.g., diabetes, hypertension) is hidden from view.
- Submerged portion: Represents the hidden mass of disease (sub-clinical cases, carriers, undiagnosed cases).
- Floating tip: Represents what the physician sees in clinical practice (overt or apparent disease).
- The hidden part constitutes the mass of unrecognized disease in the community, and its detection is a primary challenge in preventive medicine.

Concept of Screening
- Screening is a fundamental aspect of prevention. 
- It differs from periodic health examinations in three major respects: 
  1. Capable of wide application.
  2. Relatively inexpensive.
  3. Requires little physician-time (the physician is not required to administer the test, only to interpret it).

Difference between Screening Test & Diagnostic Test
- Done on: Screening (Apparently Healthy individuals) vs. Diagnostic (Sick individuals or those with indications).
- Applied to: Screening (Groups) vs. Diagnostic (Single Patient).
- Based on: Screening (Cut-off Criteria / One criterion) vs. Diagnostic (Evaluation of Signs/Symptoms/Lab Findings).
- Test Results: Screening (Arbitrary & Final) vs. Diagnostic (Not Final / Modifiable).
- Time taken: Screening (Rapid) vs. Diagnostic (Time Consuming).
- Accuracy: Screening (Less Accurate) vs. Diagnostic (More Accurate).
- Expense: Screening (Less Expensive) vs. Diagnostic (More Expensive).
- Basis of Treatment: Screening (No) vs. Diagnostic (Yes).
- Done by/Initiative: Screening (Health Care providing Agencies / Investigator) vs. Diagnostic (Doctor's advice / Patient with a complaint).

Lead Time and Lead Time Bias
- 1st Possible Point of Diagnosis: The earliest point a disease can be detected. A screening test CANNOT detect a disease before it.
- Final Critical Point: The point after which treatment of a disease may NOT yield the Desirable Beneficial Effects.
- A screening test is most useful if applied between the 1st Possible Point of Diagnosis and the Final Critical Point.
- Lead Time Bias: Occurs when the survival time appears longer simply because the diagnosis was done earlier (by screening), irrespective of whether the patient actually lived longer. In cases of long Lead Time without significant advancement in the treatment of the disease, there is a VIRTUAL increase in Survival Rate or a decrease in Mortality.

Types of Screening
1. Mass Screening: Screening of a Whole Population. It is employed for a disease with high prevalence (e.g., Chest X-Ray for TB on a large population). 
2. High Risk / Selective Screening: The Most Economical & Best screening method. Applied selectively to High-Risk Groups (e.g., Cervical Cancer screening on Low SES Females; DM screening on people having a Family History of DM; HIV screening on High-Risk Groups like Prostitutes, IV Drug Users, Truck Drivers).
3. Multiphasic Screening: Application of 2 or more Screening Tests in combination to a large number of people at one time (e.g., Annual Health Checkups).

Uses / Objectives of Screening
1. Case Detection (Prescriptive Screening): People are screened for their Own Benefit (e.g., Breast Cancer, Cervical Cancer, Anemia, Diabetes). Represents Secondary Prevention (Early Diagnosis).
2. Control of Disease (Prospective Screening): People are examined for the Benefit of Others (e.g., Tuberculosis, Screening of Immigrants, HIV screening among Sex Workers). Represents Primary Prevention (Specific Protection).
3. Research Purposes: For diseases whose Natural History is not fully known.
4. Educational Opportunities: For creating Public Awareness.

Screening for Diseases at Different Age Groups
- Pregnancy: Anemia, Hypertension, Diabetes.
- Infancy: Congenital Heart Disease, Haemoglobinopathies, Sickle Cell Anemia.
- Middle Aged Person: Obesity, Hypertension, Diabetes.
- Elderly: Cancer, Cataract, Glaucoma.

Examples of Screening Tests
- Cervical Cancer: Pap Smear, VIA (Visual Inspection with Acetic acid at PHC).
- Endometrial Cancer: USG, Office Endometrial Washing.
- Breast Cancer: BSE (Breast Self-Examination; best time is 1 week after menses), Mammography.
- Prostate Cancer: DRE (Digital Rectal Examination), PSA (Prostate Specific Antigen).
- Colorectal Cancer: Fecal Occult Blood Test.
- Oral Cancer: Bimanual Oral Examination.
- HIV: ELISA, RAPID Test.
- DM: Urine for Sugar, Random Blood Sugar, Fasting Blood Sugar (most reliable).
- Developmental Anomalies in Fetus: AFP (Alpha-Feto-Protein).
- TB: Chest X-Ray for TB on large population.
- Vitamin D Deficiency: 25 Hydroxy Vitamin D.

Criteria for Diseases to be Screened & Screening Test

- It should be an Important Health Problem.
- A recognizable latent or early asymptomatic stage must exist.
- Natural history of disease can be adequately understood.
- Effective Treatment should be available.
- Diagnostic Test or Examination should be available.
- 


1. Acceptability: Discomforting screening tests are not likely to be acceptable (e.g., PR or PV examination).
2. Repeatability / Consistency / Reproducibility / Reliability (Precision): Gives consistent results when repeated more than once on the same individual under same conditions. It gives the extent of Variation & measurement of Contained Behavior. Evaluated using a Range Chart (R chart). Influenced by:
   -  Intra-observer (within the same observer) and Inter-observer (between observers).
   -  E.g., fluctuations in blood pressure, regression to the mean.
   -  Defective instruments, faulty reagents.
3. Validity (Accuracy): Means close to True or Actual Value. Ability of the test to separate those who have the disease from those who don't. Measured using Mean Chart, Levy Jennings (LJ) Chart, Shewhart Control Chart. It has two components:
   - Sensitivity
   - Specificity

Components of Validity: Sensitivity & Specificity
- Sensitivity: Ability of a test to identify all those who have the Disease (True Positives). 
  - It measures the ability to correctly diagnose the percentage of Sick People who are having the condition.
  - 
  - Interpretation: If Sensitivity is 90%, it means 90% of diseased people screened by the test will give a True Positive result, and the remaining 10% will give a False Negative.
  - A screening test with High Sensitivity suggests that those testing Positive are likely to have the Disease (especially if Disease Prevalence is high).
  - If True Positive (TP) is High, it means Prevalence is High. If False Positive (FP) is High, it means Prevalence is Low.
  -  It is the most important factor for being a good screening test.
- Specificity: Ability of a test to identify correctly all those who do NOT have the disease (True Negatives).
  - 
  - Interpretation: If specificity is 90%, 90% of non-diseased persons will give a True Negative result, and 10% will give a False Positive.

Predictivity of Screening Test
- Reflects the Diagnostic Power of the Test. Depends on Sensitivity, Specificity, and Prevalence of a Disease.
- Positive Predictive Value (PPV): Ability of a screening test to identify correctly all those who have the disease, out of all those who are Tested Positive. 
  - PPV is directly proportional to Prevalence (PPV Î± Prevalence).
  - 
- Negative Predictive Value (NPV): Ability of a test to identify correctly all those who do NOT have the disease, out of all those who are Tested Negative. 
  - Reflects the diagnostic power of a test to correctly Exclude the Disease.
  - NPV is inversely proportional to Prevalence (NPV Î± 1/Prevalence).
- Errors in Predictivity:
  - False Positive Error Rate = Type I Error.
  - False Negative Error Rate = Type II Error.
  -  (as it burdens diagnostic facilities and causes anxiety among healthy individuals).

Ideal Screening Test & Screening Programs
- An Ideal Screening Test should be Acceptable, Repeatable, Valid, Safe, Rapid, Simple, Cheap & Easily administrable. It should be 100% Sensitive & 100% Specific (but practically, NO test achieves this).
- A screening test with High Sensitivity has few False Negatives. High Specificity has few False Positives.
- Screening in General Population: Done in cancers of Colon, Pancreas, Breast, Cervix. It is NOT routinely recommended for Testicular Cancer, Lung Cancer, or Ovarian Cancer.
- Among Cancers, Colorectal Cancer is ideal for screening as it is common in both Men & Women and is curable if detected in early stages. Maximum Survival Rate is seen in screening for Colon Cancer.
- Blood Screening is routinely done for HIV, HBV, HCV.
- National Screening Programs include: DM under NPCDCS, Carcinoma Cervix under NPCDCS, Refractory Error under NPCBVI.

Screening Tests in Series & Parallel
- Tests in Series: 1st Screening Test is followed by 2nd Screening Test. The 2nd test is applied only on those individuals whose 1st Screening Test is Positive.
  - Combined Sensitivity: Decreases
  - Combined Specificity: Increases
  - Combined PPV: Increases
  - Combined NPV: Decreases
- Tests in Parallel: 2 or more Screening Tests applied at the Same Time. Each individual is subjected to Both (All) Tests.
  - Combined Sensitivity: Increases
  - Combined Specificity: Decreases
  - Combined PPV: Decreases
  - Combined NPV: Increases

Interpretation of Distribution Curve (Shifting Cut-offs)
- For unimodal distributions (e.g., blood sugar, blood pressure), there is no sharp dividing line between normal and diseased.
- If Cut-off value is shifted to the LEFT:
  - False Positive (FP) is increased, so Specificity is decreased.
  - False Negative (FN) is decreased, so Sensitivity is increased.
- If Cut-off value is shifted to the RIGHT:
  - False Negative (FN) is increased, so Sensitivity is decreased.
  - False Positive (FP) is decreased, so Specificity is increased.

Likelihood Ratio
- Provides a direct estimate of how much a Test result will change the Odds of having a Disease.
- LR of Positive result: Tells how much the Odds of Disease Increase when a Test is Positive.
- LR of Negative result: Tells how much the Odds of Disease Decrease when a Test is Negative.

Receiver Operator Characteristic (ROC) Curve
- A plot between Sensitivity (True Positive Rate) on the Y-axis & 1 - Specificity (False Positive Rate) on the X-axis.
- Used for Comparison of 2 or more Screening Tests.
- Best ROC: Maximum Sensitivity & Minimum 1-Specificity (located Up & Left on the graph).
- It determines the Threshold of screening for a Positive Test.
- Area Under Curve (AUC): Represents the Overall Performance of the test.
- Youden Index (J): Enables selection of the Optimal Threshold Value (Cutoff). Tells about the Utilization of investigation.

Evaluation of Screening Programmes
- Randomized Controlled Trials (RCT): Ideal method of evaluation, comparing screened group with unscreened group. Often prohibitive due to cost and length.
- Uncontrolled Trials: Seeing if people with disease detected through screening appear to live longer. Susceptible to lead time bias.
- Other methods: Case control studies and comparison in trends between areas with different degrees of screening coverage.

KEY POINTS
- Sensitivity denotes the usefulness of a screening test and is the most important factor for being a good screening test.
- PPV is the most affected parameter by changes in disease prevalence.
- Type 1 error (False Positives) amounts to giving healthy people unnecessary anxiety and diagnostic procedures, which can discredit a screening program.
- Specificity and Sensitivity are inversely related; increasing one will automatically decrease the other depending on the cut-off point chosen.

FORMULAS AND CALCULATIONS
Validity Measures:
- Sensitivity = [True Positive / Total Diseased (i.e., TP + FN)] Ã— 100
- Specificity = [True Negative / Total Non-Diseased (i.e., FP + TN)] Ã— 100

Predictivity Measures:
- Positive Predictive Value (PPV) = [True Positive / Total Positive (i.e., TP + FP)] Ã— 100
- Negative Predictive Value (NPV) = [True Negative / Total Negative (i.e., TN + FN)] Ã— 100

Error Rates:
- False Positive Error Rate (Type I Error) = 1 - Specificity
- False Negative Error Rate (Type II Error) = 1 - Sensitivity

Tests in Series & Parallel:
- Series Combined Sensitivity = Sensitivity A Ã— Sensitivity B
- Series Combined Specificity = (Specificity A + Specificity B) - (Specificity A Ã— Specificity B)
- Parallel Combined Sensitivity = (Sensitivity A + Sensitivity B) - (Sensitivity A Ã— Sensitivity B)
- Parallel Combined Specificity = Specificity A Ã— Specificity B

Likelihood Ratio (LR):
- LR+ = Sensitivity / (1 - Specificity)
- LR- = (1 - Sensitivity) / Specificity

ROC Curve Parameters:
- Youden Index (J) = Sensitivity + Specificity - 1

Bayes' Theorem:
- PPV =[(Sensitivity Ã— Prevalence) / {(Sensitivity Ã— Prevalence) + (1 - Specificity) Ã— (1 - Prevalence)}] Ã— 100
- NPV =[(Specificity Ã— (1 - Prevalence)) / {(Specificity Ã— (1 - Prevalence)) + (1 - Sensitivity) Ã— (Prevalence)}] Ã— 100

Actual Bayes' Theorem:
- Post Test Probability (PTP) is the same as PPV. Pre Test Probability (pTP) is the same as Prevalence.
- PTP =[(Sensitivity Ã— pTP) / {(Sensitivity Ã— pTP) + (1 - Specificity) Ã— (1 - pTP)}] Ã— 100

subsections.title: Respiratory Infections - Part A (Smallpox, Mpox, Chickenpox, Measles, Rubella, Mumps, Diphtheria, Pertussis)
subsections.content:
SMALLPOX (VARIOLA)

OVERVIEW OF THE DISEASE
- An acute infectious disease caused by the variola virus.
- Clinically characterized by a sudden onset of fever, headache, backache, vomiting, and sometimes convulsions, especially in children.
- On the third day of fever, a typical rash appears which is centrifugal in distribution and passes through successive stages of macule, papule, vesicle, pustule, and scab, with subsequent scarring.
- Previously, it was one of the greatest killer diseases.

ERADICATION TIMELINE
- In 1967, WHO began an intensified worldwide campaign to eradicate smallpox based on surveillance and containment.
- The last known case of smallpox in India occurred on 24th May 1975.
- India was declared smallpox-free on 5th July 1975.
- The eradication of smallpox was confirmed in April 1977 by an international commission.
- The World Health Assembly confirmed the global eradication of smallpox in May 1980.
- All countries have discontinued routine vaccination against smallpox.
- WHO maintains a reserve stock of smallpox vaccine and vaccination needles sufficient to protect more than 200 million people should an emergency arise.

CASE DEFINITION FOR NOTIFICATION (IHR 2005)
- Member states are required to notify WHO immediately of any confirmed case of smallpox.
- An individual of any age presenting with acute onset of fever (>= 38.3 C / 101 F), malaise, and severe prostration with headache and backache occurring 2-4 days before onset of rash.
- Subsequent development of a maculopapular rash starting on the face and forearms, then spreading to the trunk and legs.
- Evolves within 48 hours to deep-seated, firm or hard and round well-circumscribed vesicles, and later pustules, which may become umbilicated or confluent.
- Lesions appear at the same stage of development (i.e., all are vesicles or all are pustules) on any given part of the body.
- No alternative diagnosis explaining the illness.
- Laboratory confirmation is required.

MPOX (MONKEYPOX)

OVERVIEW OF THE DISEASE
- Mpox (previously known as monkeypox) is a viral zoonosis (a virus transmitted to humans from animals).
- Caused by the monkeypox virus (MPXV) of the Orthopoxvirus genus (a DNA virus).
- Symptoms are similar to those seen in the past in smallpox patients, although it is clinically less severe.
- Emerged as the most important orthopoxvirus for public health following the eradication of smallpox and cessation of smallpox vaccination.

AGENT FACTORS
- MPXV comprises 2 clades:
  - Clade I (formerly known as Congo Basin clade), further divided into subclades Clade Ia and Ib.
  - Clade II (formerly known as West African clade), further divided into IIa and IIb.

NATURAL HOST AND RESERVOIR
- Various animal species are susceptible, including rope squirrels, tree squirrels, Gambian pouched rats, dormice, and non-human primates.
- Uncertainty remains on the natural history and exact reservoirs in nature.

TRANSMISSION OF DISEASE
- Animal-to-human (zoonotic): Direct contact with blood, bodily fluids, or cutaneous/mucosal lesions of infected animals. Eating inadequately cooked meat of infected animals is a possible risk factor.
- Human-to-human: Mainly through close contact, including skin-to-skin (touching, sex), mouth-to-mouth, or mouth-to-skin (kissing).
- Face-to-face contact can generate infectious respiratory particles.
- Fomite transmission: Contracting mpox from contaminated objects such as clothing, linen, or needle injuries in healthcare or tattoo parlours.
- Mother-to-child: Virus may be passed during pregnancy or birth. Can lead to pregnancy loss, stillbirth, or neonatal death.

HIGH-RISK GROUPS
- Health and care workers at risk of exposure.
- People in the same household or close community as an infected person (including children).
- People who have multiple sex partners, including men who have sex with men (MSM).
- Sex workers of any gender and their clients.

INCUBATION PERIOD
- Usually 6 to 13 days, but can range from 2 to 21 days.

CLINICAL PICTURE
- The infection is divided into two periods:
- Invasion period (lasts 1-5 days): Characterized by fever, intense headache, lymphadenopathy, back pain, myalgia, and intense asthenia. Lymphadenopathy is a distinctive feature compared to chickenpox, measles, and smallpox.
- Skin eruption: Usually begins within 1-3 days of fever appearance. The rash is more concentrated on the face and extremities (centrifugal) rather than the trunk. Affects the face (95%), palms and soles (75%), oral mucous membranes (70%), genitalia (30%), and conjunctiva/cornea (20%).
- Rash evolves from macules to papules, vesicles, pustules, and crusts. Lesions vary from a few to several thousand. Severe cases can cause coalescence and skin sloughing.
- Usually a self-limited disease lasting 2 to 4 weeks.
- Severe cases are more common among children, those with immune deficiencies, or those with untreated HIV.
- Complications: Secondary infections, bronchopneumonia, sepsis, encephalitis, and corneal infection with vision loss.
- Case fatality ratio (CFR) has been around 3-6%.

DIAGNOSIS
- Polymerase chain reaction (PCR) is the preferred laboratory test due to accuracy and sensitivity.
- Optimal diagnostic samples are from skin lesions (swabs of fluid, roofs, or crusts).
- In the absence of skin lesions, testing can be done using swabs of the throat or anus.
- Blood testing is not recommended. Antibody detection methods may not distinguish between different orthopoxviruses.
- HIV testing should be offered to adults with mpox.

PREVENTION AND VACCINATION
- A newer vaccine based on a modified attenuated vaccinia virus (Ankara strain) was approved for mpox prevention in 2019 (two-dose vaccine, limited availability).
- Prior smallpox vaccination was about 85% effective in preventing mpox and may result in milder illness.
- Surveillance, rapid identification, and isolation of new cases are critical for outbreak containment.

GLOBAL SCENARIO AND INDIA
- WHO declared a Public Health Emergency of International Concern (PHEIC) on 23 July 2022 due to global spread (driven by Clade IIb MPXV among MSM).
- The PHEIC was lifted on 10 May 2023 after cases subsided.
- Due to the emergence of a new Clade Ib and a resurgence of cases in Africa, a PHEIC was declared again on 14 August 2024.
- India reported its first case of mpox on 9th September 2024 in a returning traveler (Clade II strain).

CHICKENPOX (VARICELLA)

OVERVIEW OF THE DISEASE
- An acute, highly infectious disease caused by the varicella-zoster (V-Z) virus.
- Characterized by a vesicular rash that may be accompanied by fever and malaise.
- Worldwide in distribution, occurs in epidemic and endemic forms.
- Chickenpox and herpes zoster are different host responses to the same aetiological agent.

PROBLEM STATEMENT
- Global annual burden includes 4.2 million severe complications leading to hospitalization and 4,200 deaths.
- Case fatality rate in the pre-vaccine era in high-income countries was about 3 per lakh cases (compared to 1-3 per 1000 for measles).
- In India (2022), 27,279 cases were reported with 15 deaths (CFR ~ 0.0549%).

AGENT FACTORS
- Agent: Varicella-Zoster (V-Z) virus, also called Human (alpha) herpes virus 3.
- Primary infection causes chickenpox. Reactivation of latent infection in cranial nerves, sensory ganglia, and spinal dorsal root ganglia causes herpes zoster (in 10-30% of persons, marked increase after 50 years of age).
- Source of infection: Usually a case of chickenpox. Virus occurs in oropharyngeal secretions and skin/mucosal lesions. Scabs are not infective.
- Infectivity: 1 to 2 days before the rash appears and 4 to 5 days thereafter. Patient is non-infectious once lesions have crusted.
- Secondary attack rate: Highly contagious, approximately 85% (range 61-100%) in susceptible household contacts.
- Herpes zoster is about 20% as infectious as chickenpox; contact with zoster causes varicella (chickenpox) in susceptible people, not zoster.

HOST FACTORS
- Age: Occurs primarily among children under 10 years of age.
- Immunity: One attack gives durable immunity; second attacks are rare. Maternal antibodies protect infants for the first few months.
- Pregnancy: Infection during pregnancy presents a risk of congenital varicella syndrome (0.4-2.0% in children born to mothers infected in the first 20 weeks). High risk of developing herpes zoster in early life.

ENVIRONMENTAL FACTORS
- Seasonal trend in temperate settings and most tropical settings with peak incidence during winter and spring.
- VZV is heat labile and survives in the external environment for only a few hours. Inactivated by lipid solvents, detergents, and proteases.

TRANSMISSION
- Droplet infection and droplet nuclei.
- Face-to-face personal contact.
- Portal of entry: Upper respiratory tract or conjunctiva.
- Vertical transmission (placental barrier) causing congenital varicella.

INCUBATION PERIOD
- Usually 14 to 16 days (extremes of 10 to 21 days reported).

CLINICAL FEATURES
- Pre-eruptive stage: Sudden onset of mild/moderate fever, back pain, shivering, malaise. Lasts about 24 hours (adults: more severe, 2-3 days).
- Eruptive stage: Rash comes on the day fever starts in children.
- Distribution: Symmetrical. First on the trunk (abundant), then face, arms, and legs (less abundant). Axilla affected. Centripetal distribution. Palms and soles usually unaffected.
- Rapid evolution: Macule -> papule -> vesicle -> scab. Vesicles are superficial, dew-drop like, easily ruptured, not umbilicated. Scabbing begins 4-7 days after rash.
- Pleomorphism: All stages of the rash are seen simultaneously in the same area because the rash appears in successive crops for 4-5 days.
- Fever: Shows exacerbations with each fresh crop of eruption.

DIFFERENCES BETWEEN SMALLPOX AND CHICKENPOX
- Incubation: Smallpox (12 days), Chickenpox (15 days).
- Prodrome: Smallpox (Severe), Chickenpox (Usually mild).
- Distribution: Smallpox (Centrifugal, axilla free, extensor surfaces), Chickenpox (Centripetal, axilla affected, flexor surfaces).
- Rash characteristics: Smallpox (Deep-seated, multilocular, umbilicated, single stage at a time, inflammation around vesicles), Chickenpox (Superficial, unilocular/dew-drop, pleomorphic, no inflammation around vesicles).
- Evolution: Smallpox (Slow, majestic), Chickenpox (Very rapid).
- Scabs: Smallpox (10-14 days), Chickenpox (4-7 days).

COMPLICATIONS
- Mild, self-limiting in most cases. Mortality <1% in uncomplicated cases.
- Severe complications: Haemorrhages (varicella haemorrhagical), pneumonia, encephalitis, acute cerebellar ataxia, Reye's syndrome.
- Secondary bacterial infections (Group A strep, S. aureus) cause cellulitis, erysipelas, osteomyelitis, sepsis.
- Maternal varicella -> foetal wastage, cutaneous scars, atrophied limbs, microcephaly, cataracts, deafness.

LABORATORY DIAGNOSIS
- Detecting VZV DNA using PCR or isolating VZV in cell culture from vesicular fluid, crusts, saliva, CSF.
- Direct immunofluorescence.
- IgM/IgG antibodies.

PREVENTION AND VACCINATION
- Varicella-Zoster Immunoglobulin (VZIG): Given within 72 hours of exposure for susceptible immunosuppressed persons, pregnant women, newborns, and premature infants. Dose: 12.5 units/kg up to 625 units IM. Repeat in 3 weeks if exposure continues.
- Live attenuated vaccine: Safe and recommended for children 12-18 months. Administered as 0.5 ml SC. 2-dose schedule recommended for persons >13 years (4-8 weeks apart). Combination MMRV vaccine available.
- Efficacy is high, duration probably 10 years to lifetime. Breakthrough infections are milder.
- Contraindicated in pregnancy and individuals allergic to neomycin or severely immunocompromised.

MEASLES (RUBEOLA)

OVERVIEW OF THE DISEASE
- An acute highly infectious disease of childhood caused by myxoviruses (RNA paramyxovirus).
- Characterized by fever and catarrhal symptoms of the upper respiratory tract (coryza, sore throat, cough), followed by a typical rash.
- Endemic virtually in all parts of the world. Epidemics occur when the proportion of susceptible children reaches about 40%.
- Responsible for about 2% of under-5 mortality in India.

AGENT FACTORS
- Agent: RNA paramyxovirus (1 serotype).
- Source of infection: Case of measles. No known carriers.
- Infective material: Secretions of nose, throat, and respiratory tract.
- Communicability: Highly infectious during the prodromal period. 4 days before and 4 days after the appearance of the rash.
- Secondary attack rate: 90% (only one antigenic type, confers life-long immunity).

HOST FACTORS
- Age: Affects virtually everyone in infancy/childhood. 6 months to 3 years in developing countries. Following vaccine introduction, seen in somewhat older age groups.
- Sex: Incidence equal.
- Immunity: One attack gives lifelong immunity. Infants protected by maternal antibodies up to 6 months (may persist up to 9 months).
- Nutrition: Measles tends to be very severe in malnourished children, carrying a mortality up to 400 times higher than in well-nourished children. Associated with poor cell-mediated immune response.

ENVIRONMENTAL FACTORS
- Occurs in winter and early spring (January to April in India). Promoted by overcrowding.

TRANSMISSION
- Directly from person to person mainly by droplet infection and droplet nuclei.
- Portal of entry: Respiratory tract or conjunctiva.

INCUBATION PERIOD
- 7 days (as per notes).
- 10 days from exposure to onset of fever, and 14 days to appearance of rash (as per textbook).

CLINICAL FEATURES
- Prodromal stage (starts 10 days after infection, lasts till day 14): Fever, coryza, sneezing, nasal discharge, cough, redness of the eyes, lacrimation, photophobia. Koplik's spots appear 1-2 days before rash (small, bluish-white spots on a red base on the buccal mucosa opposite the first and second lower molars). Pathognomonic for measles.
- Eruptive phase: Dusky-red, macular or maculo-papular rash begins behind the ears and spreads rapidly in a few hours over the face, neck, and lower extremities (takes 2-3 days to progress down). Rash fades in the same order, leaving brownish discoloration.
- Post-measles stage: Weight loss, weakness, growth retardation, and diarrhoea.

COMPLICATIONS
- Otitis media (7-9%).
- Diarrhoea (8%).
- Pneumonia (1-6%).
- Encephalitis (1-4 per 1000-2000 cases).
- Sub-acute sclerosing panencephalitis (SSPE) (1 per 10,000-100,000 cases, develops years later).
- High CFR in immunocompromised (up to 50% in HIV) and malnourished.

PREVENTION AND CONTROL
- Measles Vaccine: Live attenuated, freeze-dried. 
- Reconstituted vaccine must be stored in the dark at 2-8 C and used within 4 hours. 
- Given at 9 months (can be lowered to 6 months in an outbreak).
- Route: Subcutaneous (0.5 ml dose containing >1000 viral infective units).
- Immunity develops within 12 days after vaccination.
- Contraindications: History of anaphylactic reaction to neomycin/gelatin, severe immunocompromise (HIV, leukemia, malignancy), pregnancy.
- Adverse effects: Toxic Shock Syndrome (TSS) if contaminated vial is used, mild measles illness (fever/rash in 15-20%).
- Immunoglobulin: 0.25 ml per kg body weight given within 3-4 days of exposure for passive protection. Vaccine can be given 8-12 weeks later.

ERADICATION OF MEASLES
- Requires achieving an immunization coverage of at least 96% of children under 1 year of age.

RUBELLA (GERMAN MEASLES)

OVERVIEW OF THE DISEASE
- An acute childhood infection, usually mild, of short duration (approximately 3 days).
- Accompanied by low-grade fever, lymphadenopathy, and a maculopapular rash.
- Highly teratogenic. Infection in early pregnancy causes serious congenital defects (Congenital Rubella Syndrome).

PROBLEM STATEMENT
- Worldwide over 100,000 babies are born with congenital rubella syndrome every year.

AGENT FACTORS
- Agent: RNA virus of the Togavirus family (only 1 serotype).
- Source of infection: Clinical or subclinical cases.
- Communicability: Weeks before symptoms to about a week after rash appears (most infective 1-5 days after rash).

HOST FACTORS
- Age: Mainly 3 to 10 years.
- Immunity: One attack results in lifelong immunity. Infants of immune mothers are protected for 4 to 6 months.

ENVIRONMENTAL FACTORS
- Occurs in a seasonal pattern during late winter and spring.

TRANSMISSION
- Directly from person to person by droplets from nose/throat and droplet nuclei (aerosols).
- Vertical transmission (placenta to fetus) causing congenital rubella.

INCUBATION PERIOD
- 2 to 3 weeks (average 18 days).

CLINICAL FEATURES
- Prodromal: Mild coryza, sore throat, low-grade fever.
- Lymphadenopathy: Enlargement of post-auricular, occipital, and posterior cervical lymph nodes (appears 5-10 days before rash, or 7 days as per notes).
- Rash: Minute, discrete, pinkish, macular rash. Appears first on the face (within 24 hours of prodrome), spreads rapidly to trunk/extremities, disappears by the third day. Absent in up to 25% of cases.
- Complications (rare): Arthralgia in adult women, encephalitis, thrombocytopenic purpura.

DIAGNOSIS
- Throat swabs, Haemagglutination Inhibition (HI) test, ELISA (IgM/IgG).

CONGENITAL RUBELLA SYNDROME (CRS)
- Infants born with defects secondary to intrauterine infection.
- Risk of transmission is 20-25% overall, but up to 90% if infected in the first 8-10 weeks of gestation.
- Classical triad: Deafness, cardiac malformations (PDA, VSD), and cataracts.
