- Other defects: Glaucoma, retinopathy, microcephaly, cerebral palsy, IUGR, hepatosplenomegaly, mental and motor retardation, radiolucency in long bones.
- These occurring singly or in combination are known as Congenital Rubella Syndrome.

PREVENTION AND VACCINATION
- Rubella Vaccine: Live attenuated RA 27/3 strain.
- Single dose of 0.5 ml, subcutaneous.
- Immunity persists for 16 years to a lifetime.
- Infants under 1 year should not be vaccinated due to possible interference from persisting maternal antibodies.
- Contraindicated in pregnancy. Women advised not to become pregnant for 1 month after vaccination.
- Vaccination Strategy: 1st - protect women of childbearing age (15-34 or 39 years). 2nd - vaccinate all children 1-14 years of age. 3rd - children of less than 1 year.

MUMPS

OVERVIEW OF THE DISEASE
- An acute infectious disease caused by an RNA virus (genus Rubulavirus, family Paramyxoviridae).
- Clinically recognized by non-suppurative enlargement and tenderness of one or both parotid glands.
- Morbidity rate tends to be high, mortality rate is negligible.

EPIDEMIOLOGICAL DETERMINANTS
- Agent: Myxovirus parotiditis (RNA virus). 1 serotype.
- Source: Clinical and subclinical cases (subclinical accounts for 30-40%).
- Communicability: 4-6 days before onset of symptoms and a week or more thereafter.
- Secondary attack rate: 86%.
- Age: Most frequent in children aged 5-9 years.
- Immunity: One attack induces lifelong immunity.
- Environmental: Endemic disease. Peak incidence in winter and spring. Associated with overcrowding.
- Transmission: Droplet infection and direct contact.
- Incubation period: 2 to 4 weeks (usually 14-18 days).

CLINICAL FEATURES & COMPLICATIONS
- Pain and swelling in parotid glands, "ear ache", trismus (stiffness on opening mouth).
- Fever, headache, malaise for 3-5 days.
- Complications: Orchitis (unilateral in 75% of cases, occurs in 25-40% of post-pubertal men, sterility is rare), Ovaritis (5%), Pancreatitis (4%, leading cause in children), Meningoencephalitis, Sensorineural deafness.
- Infection in the first trimester of pregnancy is associated with a 25% incidence of spontaneous abortion.

PREVENTION
- Live attenuated mumps vaccine: Strains include Jeryl-Lynn, RIT 4385, Leningrad-3, L-Zagreb, and Urabe.
- WHO recommends that the Rubini strain should not be used due to low effectiveness.
- Dose: 0.5 ml intramuscularly.
- Given routinely over 1 year of age (often as MMR/MMRV). Second dose at 4-6 years.

DIPHTHERIA

OVERVIEW OF THE DISEASE
- An acute bacterial infectious disease caused by toxigenic strains of Corynebacterium diphtheriae.
- Major clinical types: Anterior nasal, faucial, and laryngeal. Skin, conjunctiva, and vulva may also be affected.
- Characterized by the formation of a greyish/yellowish false membrane and signs of toxaemia.
- Fatality rate: 5-10%.

EPIDEMIOLOGICAL DETERMINANTS
- Agent: C. diphtheriae (gram-positive, non-motile organism). Four types: gravis, mitis, belfanti, intermedius. Produces a powerful exotoxin (beta phage dependent).
- Source of infection: Cases and carriers (temporary or chronic; nasal carriers are particularly dangerous due to frequent shedding).
- Period of infectivity: 14 to 28 days (longer in carriers).
- Age: Affects children aged 1 to 5 years predominantly.
- Immunity: Infants immune for first few months if mother is immune. 75% of children in developing countries have protective antibodies by age 6-8 years due to inapparent infections.
- Transmission: Droplet infection, direct contact with cutaneous lesions, or infected objects (cups, toys).
- Incubation period: 2 to 6 days.

CLINICAL FEATURES
- Sore throat, difficulty swallowing, low-grade fever.
- False membrane: Greyish/yellowish patch over tonsils/pharynx. Extends to become thick and adherent. Bleeds on attempt to remove.
- "Bull-neck" appearance due to marked oedema of the submandibular area and lymphadenopathy.
- Complications: Myocarditis, nerve paralysis.

CONTROL AND PREVENTION
- Cases & Carriers: Isolation for at least 14 days (or until 2 consecutive negative swabs). Diphtheria antitoxin (20,000-100,000 units IV/IM) + Penicillin or Erythromycin (5-6 days). Carriers receive 10 days of oral erythromycin.
- Contacts: Throat swabbing. Booster dose of toxoid or prophylactic penicillin/erythromycin + 1000-2000 units of antitoxin.
- Active Immunization: DPT vaccine or Pentavalent vaccine. Contains 25 Lf of diphtheria toxoid per dose. Adsorbed on aluminium phosphate/hydroxide. Adult type (dT) contains <= 2 Lf per dose.
- Efficacy: Failures may occur if the toxin dose is extremely large, despite high antitoxin titres.

WHOOPING COUGH (PERTUSSIS)

OVERVIEW OF THE DISEASE
- Acute infectious disease usually of young children, caused by B. pertussis.
- Characterized by insidious onset with mild fever, irritating cough becoming paroxysmal with a characteristic "whoop" (loud crowing inspiration), cyanosis, and vomiting.
- Chinese call it a "Hundred Day Cough".

EPIDEMIOLOGICAL DETERMINANTS
- Agent: Bordetella pertussis. B. parapertussis causes <5% of cases.
- Source of infection: Case of pertussis. No chronic carrier state exists.
- Infective material: Nasopharyngeal and bronchial secretions.
- Period of infectivity: Most infectious during catarrhal stage. Extends from 1 week after exposure to ~3 weeks after onset of paroxysmal stage.
- Secondary attack rate: 90% in unimmunized household contacts.
- Age: Infants and pre-school children. Highest incidence below 5 years. Median age of infection is 20-30 months in developing countries.
- Sex: Incidence and fatality are higher in females.
- Transmission: Droplet infection and direct contact.
- Incubation period: 7 to 14 days (not more than 3 weeks).

CLINICAL COURSE
- Catarrhal stage (10 days): Insidious onset, lacrimation, sneezing, coryza, hacking night cough.
- Paroxysmal stage (2-4 weeks): Bursts of rapid consecutive coughs followed by a deep, high-pitched inspiration (whoop). Followed by vomiting.
- Convalescent stage (1-2 weeks): Recovery phase. Total illness lasts 6-8 weeks.
- Complications: Bronchopneumonia, bronchiectasis, subconjunctival haemorrhages, epistaxis. Encephalopathy is rare.

CONTROL AND PREVENTION
- Treatment: Erythromycin (drug of choice, 30-50 mg/kg/day in 4 divided doses for 10 days). Azithromycin, Clarithromycin.
- Immunization: DPT, DTwP, or DTaP vaccine. Administered at 6, 10, and 14 weeks with boosters. Both whole-cell and acellular vaccines provide ~85% protection. Immunity lasts 6-12 years.

subsections.title: Respiratory Infections - Part B (Influenza, Acute Respiratory Infections, Pneumonia, Tuberculosis)
subsections.content:
INFLUENZA

OVERVIEW OF THE DISEASE
- An acute respiratory tract infection caused by the influenza virus.
- Characterized by a sudden onset of chills, malaise, fever, muscular pains, and cough.
- Occurs in all countries and affects millions of people every year. Causes 3-5 million cases of severe illness and about 290,000 to 650,000 deaths globally per year.
- 3 types of influenza viruses: A, B, and C.
- Epidemics are caused by influenza A and B strains. Pandemics are caused by influenza A strains.
- At present, 3 types of influenza viruses are circulating: A(H1N1), A(H3N2), and B viruses.
- Influenza A predominates in most countries, accounting for 59% of all influenza viruses detected.

AGENT FACTORS
- Agent: Influenza virus, family Orthomyxoviridae.
- 4 viral subtypes: Type A, Type B, Type C, and Type D (antigenically distinct, no cross-immunity).
- Influenza A has 2 distinct surface antigens: Haemagglutinin (H) and Neuraminidase (N).
- The H antigen initiates infection; the N antigen is responsible for the release of the virus from the infected cell.
- There are 18 H subtypes and 11 N subtypes, but humans are mainly infected with H1, H2, or H3 and N1 or N2.
- Antigenic Shift: Sudden complete or major change in the virus resulting from genetic recombination. Causes major epidemics or pandemics. Occurred in 1918 (Spanish), 1957 (Asian), 1968 (Hong Kong).
- Antigenic Drift: Gradual change over a period of time due to point mutation. Causes minor epidemics.

HOST AND ENVIRONMENTAL FACTORS
- Reservoir of infection: Animals and birds (e.g., Swine, domestic poultry, wild birds, horses, dogs, cats).
- Source of infection: A case or subclinical case.
- Period of infectivity: 1 to 2 days before and 1 to 2 days after the onset of symptoms.
- Age: Affects all ages. Highest mortality in old people over 65 years of age and children under 18 months.
- Immunity: Antibodies appear 7 days after an attack and reach maximum in 2 weeks. After 1-2 months, drops to pre-infection level. Immunity is subtype-specific.
- Environmental factors: Epidemics usually occur in winter months in the Northern Hemisphere and winter or rainy season in the Southern Hemisphere. Overcrowding enhances transmission.

MODE OF TRANSMISSION AND INCUBATION
- Mode of transmission: Droplet infection or droplet nuclei created by sneezing, coughing, or talking.
- Portal of entry: Respiratory tract.
- Incubation period: 1 to 4 days (Notes mention 72 hours specifically).

CLINICAL FEATURES AND COMPLICATIONS
- Fever (lasts 1-5 days, averaging 3 days in adults), chills, aches, coughing, and generalized weakness.
- Complications: Acute sinusitis, otitis media, purulent bronchitis, and pneumonia (most dreaded).
- Reye syndrome: Fatty liver with encephalopathy. Rare but severe complication of influenza A and B in children (2-16 years), associated with aspirin use. Mortality rate 10-40%.

LABORATORY DIAGNOSIS
- Virus isolation from nasopharyngeal secretions.
- Serology: ELISA.

PREVENTION AND CONTROL
- Good ventilation of public buildings.
- Avoidance of crowded places during epidemics.
- Encouraging sufferers to cover their faces with a handkerchief when coughing and sneezing and to stay home.
- Hygienic practices during handling of poultry products.

INFLUENZA VACCINES
- Vaccines currently in use include egg-based, cell-cultured, and recombinant technologies.
- 1. Killed / Inactivated Vaccines (IIVs): Trivalent or Quadrivalent. Given via subcutaneous or intramuscular route as a single dose. Dose: 0.5 ml for adults and children over 3 years; 0.25 ml for children from 6 months to 36 months. Immunity lasts for 6-12 months (50-60% effective).
- 2. Live Attenuated Vaccines (LAIV): Trivalent. Single dose via intranasal spray. Given to individuals between 2 years and 49 years.
- High-dose IIV (HD-IIV) and adjuvanted IIV (aIIV) are designed for persons aged 65 years and older.
- Contraindications to inactivated vaccines: Severe allergy to chicken eggs, history of anaphylactic reactions, history of Guillain-Barre syndrome within 6 weeks of previous vaccine, children less than 6 months of age.

ANTIVIRAL DRUGS (CHEMOPROPHYLAXIS & TREATMENT)
- Oseltamivir: 75 mg per day for prophylaxis; 75 mg twice daily for 5 days for therapy.
- Zanamivir: By inhaler (10gm) twice daily for therapy and once daily for prophylaxis.
- Dosing schedule for children is based on body weight (e.g., <15 kg: 30 mg twice a day for 5 days; 15-23 kg: 45 mg; 24-40 kg: 60 mg; >40 kg: 75 mg).

AVIAN INFLUENZA (BIRD FLU)
- Primarily affects birds. Vast majority do not infect humans.
- H5N1 is a strain with pandemic potential, since it might ultimately adapt into a strain that is contagious among humans.

PANDEMIC INFLUENZA A (H1N1) 2009 (SWINE FLU)
- Emerged in March 2009. New nomenclature in 2011: influenza A(H1N1) pdm09.
- Incubation period: 1-4 days, could range up to 7 days.
- Clinical Features are divided into:
  - 1. Uncomplicated influenza: Fever, cough, sore throat, rhinorrhoea, muscle pain, malaise, diarrhoea, vomiting.
  - 2. Complicated or severe influenza: Shortness of breath, dyspnoea, tachypnea, hypoxia, pneumonia, CNS involvement (encephalopathy, encephalitis), severe dehydration, renal failure, septic shock. Exacerbation of underlying chronic diseases (asthma, COPD, diabetes).
  - 3. Signs & symptoms suggesting progressive disease: Cardiopulmonary insufficiency (turning blue, bloody sputum, low BP), CNS complications (altered mental status, unconsciousness, convulsions, paralysis), severe dehydration (decreased urinary output, dizziness, lethargy).
- Lab diagnosis: RT-PCR, nasopharyngeal samples, throat swab. Rapid influenza diagnostic tests (point-of-care).
- Prevention & Control: Hand hygiene, cover mouth and nose with tissue, ill persons should wear a face mask. Isolation of patients for 7 days after onset of illness or 24 hours after the resolution of fever & respiratory symptoms.
- Pandemic Influenza A (H1N1) 2009 Vaccine:
  - Inactivated vaccine: Monovalent vaccine containing antigen A/California/7/2009 (H1N1). Single dose, Intramuscular. Effective 14 days after vaccination.
  - Live attenuated vaccine: Nasal spray.
- Treatment: Symptomatic care, antiviral drugs, oxygen therapy, fluid replacement, supportive care.
- Antiviral Treatment: Oseltamivir (Oral, 75 mg twice daily for 5 days), Zanamivir (Indicated for adults & children >5 years; 2 Inhalations / 2 x 5 mg twice daily for 5 days).

ACUTE RESPIRATORY INFECTIONS (ARI)

OVERVIEW OF THE DISEASE
- Cause inflammation of the respiratory tract anywhere from nose to alveoli, with a wide range of symptom combinations.
- Clinical features include running nose, cough, sore throat, difficult breathing, ear problem, and fever.
- Upper respiratory tract infection (AURI): Common cold, pharyngitis, and otitis media.
- Lower respiratory tract infection (ALRI): Epiglottitis, laryngitis, laryngotracheitis, bronchitis, bronchiolitis, and pneumonia.

PROBLEM STATEMENT
- About 90% of ARI deaths are due to pneumonia.
- Pneumonia killed an estimated 0.80 million children in 2018 (>80% of deaths in children <2 years).
- Incidence in developed countries: 20-30%.
- Responsible for 17% of under-five deaths worldwide; 18% of all under-5 deaths in India.
- India incidence: 59 cases per lakh. In 2022, India reported 1,93,92,926 ARI cases (5,132 deaths) and 4,25,555 pneumonia cases (2,692 deaths).
- Up to 13% of inpatient deaths in pediatric wards are due to ARI.

EPIDEMIOLOGICAL DETERMINANTS
- Agent factors:
  - Bacteria: Klebsiella pneumoniae, Legionella pneumophila, Streptococcus pneumoniae (causes 78% of lobar pneumonia cases), Staphylococcus aureus, Haemophilus influenzae type b (2nd most common cause), Group B streptococcus.
  - Virus: Influenza A, B, C; Parainfluenza 1, 2, 3; Respiratory syncytial virus (most common viral cause); Adenoviruses, Rhinoviruses, Coronaviruses.
  - Other agents: Mycoplasma pneumoniae, Chlamydia type B, Coxiella burnetii. Pneumocystis jirovecii is the most common cause in HIV-infected infants.
- Host factors: Highest incidence in young children (developing countries) and the elderly (developed countries). Adult women experience more illness than men. Under 3 years of age, boys are affected more often and more severely.
- Environmental factors: Overcrowding, poor nutrition, low birth weight, maternal cigarette smoking, low socio-economic status, indoor smoke pollution.
- Mode of transmission: Air-borne route (inhalation of droplets) and direct person-to-person contact.

CLINICAL ASSESSMENT (IMCI GUIDELINES)
- Physical Examination (Look and Listen):
  - Fast breathing cut-offs:
    - Child less than 2 months: 60 breaths/min or more.
    - 2 to 12 months: 50 breaths/min or more.
    - 12 months to 5 years: 40 breaths/min or more.
  - Chest indrawing: Lower chest wall goes in when the child breathes IN.
  - Stridor: Harsh noise when breathing IN (narrowing of larynx, trachea, epiglottis).
  - Wheeze: Soft whistling sound on breathing OUT (narrowing of air passage in lungs).
  - Check for severe malnutrition, abnormally sleepy child, fever/low body temp, and cyanosis.

MANAGEMENT GUIDELINES (2 MONTHS TO 5 YEARS)
- 1. No Pneumonia (Cough and Cold): Signs: No chest indrawing, no fast breathing. Treatment: Advise mother to give home care, treat fever, treat wheezing if present.
- 2. Pneumonia: Signs: Fast breathing and/or chest indrawing. Treatment: Advise home care, oral amoxicillin, treat fever, treat wheezing. Reassess after 2 days (Worse: refer urgently to hospital; Same: change antibiotics; Improving: finish 5 days of antibiotics).
- 3. Severe Pneumonia / Very Severe Disease: Signs: Any general danger sign (not able to drink, persistent vomiting, convulsions, lethargic/unconscious, stridor in calm child, severe malnutrition). Treatment: Refer urgently to hospital, give first dose of injectable antibiotic, supportive therapy.

MANAGEMENT GUIDELINES (UNDER 2 MONTHS)
- 1. No Pneumonia: Signs: No chest indrawing, no fast breathing. Treatment: Home care, keep young infant warm, frequent breastfeeding.
- 2. Severe / Very Severe Pneumonia: Signs: Chest indrawing, fast breathing, stopped feeding, convulsions, abnormally sleepy, stridor, wheezing. Treatment: Refer urgently to hospital, give first dose of an antibiotic.

TREATMENT REGIMENS (ANTIBIOTICS)
- Pneumonia (Cotrimoxazole Daily Dose Schedule):
  - Sulphamethoxazole (100mg) & Trimethoprim (20mg).
  - < 2 months: 1 tablet twice a day.
  - 2-12 months: 2 tablets twice a day.
  - 1-5 years: 3 tablets twice a day.
- Pneumonia (Amoxicillin Dispersible Tablets - 250 mg):
  - 2 months to <12 months (4-<10 kg): 1 tab twice a day x 5 days.
  - 12 months to <3 years (10-<14 kg): 2 tabs twice a day x 5 days.
  - 3 years to 5 years (14-19 kg): 3 tabs twice a day x 5 days.
- Severe Pneumonia (2 months - 5 years):
  - First 48 hours: Benzyl penicillin (50,000 IU/kg/dose, 6 hourly, I/M) OR Ampicillin (50mg/kg/dose, 6 hourly, I/M) OR Chloramphenicol (25mg/kg/dose, 6 hourly, I/M).
  - If condition improves (next 3 days): Procaine penicillin (50,000 IU/kg, once, I/M) OR Ampicillin (oral) OR Chloramphenicol (oral).
  - If no improvement (next 48 hours): Change antibiotic. (Ampicillin -> Chloramphenicol I/M. Chloramphenicol -> Cloxacillin + Gentamycin).
- Severe Pneumonia (< 2 months): 
  - Benzyl penicillin (50,000 IU/kg/dose, 12 hourly if <7 days or 6 hourly if 7 days-2 months, I/M) OR Ampicillin.
  - AND Gentamycin (2.5mg/kg/dose, 12 hourly if <7 days or 6 hourly if 7 days-2 months, I/M).
  - Chloramphenicol is NOT recommended as first line in young infants.

PREVENTION & CONTROL
- Improving living conditions, better nutrition, reduction in indoor smoke pollution, better MCH care.
- Immunization:
  - Measles vaccine.
  - HIB vaccine: Given in combination with Hep B & DPT & polio. 0.5 ml, I/M, at 6, 10 & 14 weeks. Not generally offered >24 months.
  - Pneumococcal pneumonia vaccine:
    - PPV23: Polysaccharide non-conjugate vaccine. Routinely advised for everyone >65 years in USA. 0.5 ml, I/M, deltoid muscle, single dose.
    - PCV (Conjugate vaccine): PCV10 & PCV13. 3 doses at 6, 10 & 14 weeks.

INTEGRATED GLOBAL ACTION PLAN FOR PNEUMONIA & DIARRHOEA (GAPPD)
- Brings together critical services & interventions to create healthy environments, promote protective practices, and ensure access to treatment.
- Specific goals for 2025: Reduce pneumonia mortality in children <5 years to <3 per 1000 live births; 90% full-dose coverage of relevant vaccines; 90% access to appropriate pneumonia management.

TUBERCULOSIS

OVERVIEW OF THE DISEASE
- A specific infectious disease caused by Mycobacterium tuberculosis.
- Primarily affects lungs (pulmonary tuberculosis) but can affect intestine, meninges, bones and joints, lymph glands, skin, and other tissues (extrapulmonary).
- Affects animals like cattle (bovine tuberculosis), which can be communicated to man.

PROBLEM STATEMENT
- India is the highest TB burden country in the world (accounts for 26% of global incident cases in 2023).
- Global TB incidence in 2023: 10.8 million people.
- Global TB deaths in 2023: 1.25 million.
- India Incidence rate of all forms of TB: 167 per lakh (Notes) / 195 per lakh (Textbook 2023 estimate).
- India Prevalence rate of all forms of TB: 195 per lakh (Notes) / 315 per lakh (Textbook 2023 estimate).

EPIDEMIOLOGICAL INDICES
- 1. Incidence: Number of new and recurrent (relapse) episodes of TB occurring in a given year.
- 2. Prevalence: Number of TB cases (all forms) at a given point in time.
- 3. Mortality from TB: Number of deaths caused by TB in HIV-negative people.
- 4. Case fatality rate: Risk of death from TB among people with active TB disease.
- 5. Case notification rate: New and recurrent episodes notified to WHO per 100,000 population.
- 6. Case detection rate: Number of notifications divided by estimated incidence.
- 7. Prevalence of drug-resistant cases: Patient excreting tubercle bacilli resistant to anti-TB drugs.

AGENT FACTORS
- Agent: M. tuberculosis. Human and bovine strains. Facultative intracellular parasite.
- Atypical mycobacteria (4 groups): Photochromogens (M. kansasii), Scotochromogens (M. scrofulaceum), Non-photochromogens (M. intracellulare), Rapid growers (M. fortuitum).
- Source of infection: Human source (case whose sputum is positive). Bovine source (infected milk).
- Communicability: Patients are infective as long as they remain untreated. Effective anti-microbial treatment reduces infectivity by 90% within 48 hours.

HOST FACTORS
- Age: Affects all ages. In India, sharp rise in 15-24 years age group.
- Sex: More prevalent in males.
- Nutrition: Malnutrition heavily predisposes to TB.
- Immunity: No inherited immunity. Acquired via natural infection or BCG vaccine.

ENVIRONMENTAL FACTORS
- Poor housing, overcrowding, undernutrition, smoking, alcohol abuse, indoor air pollution, diabetes.

MODE OF TRANSMISSION & INCUBATION
- Mode of transmission: Droplet infection and droplet nuclei generated by sputum-positive patients.
- Incubation period: 3 to 6 weeks from receipt of infection to positive tuberculin test. Disease development may take months or years.

LABORATORY DIAGNOSIS
- 1. Sputum Smear Microscopy: Method of choice. 2 samples (Day 1 on the spot, Day 2 early morning sample). Zeihl-Neelsen (ZN) acid-fast stain or Diode-based fluorescent microscopy (LED FM).
- 2. Sputum Culture: Solid medium (Lowenstein-Jensen). Liquid media (Middlebrook 7H9 broth, BACTEC MGIT 960 automated system, BacT Alert).
- 3. Rapid Molecular Diagnostic Tests (NAAT): 
  - CBNAAT (Cartridge Based Nucleic Acid Amplification Test / GeneXpert MTB/RIF).
  - Truenat.
  - Line Probe Assay (LPA): FL LPA for RIF & INH resistance, SL LPA for fluoroquinolone & injectable resistance.
- 4. Insta test TB: MycoDot (Dot-blot), Detect-TB (ELISA).
- 5. Tuberculin Skin Test (Mantoux Test): 1 TU of PPD-RT 23 with Tween 80 injected intradermally. Read at 48-96 hours. >10 mm induration is positive; <6 mm is negative; 6-9 mm is doubtful.
- 6. TB Interferon Gamma Release Assays (IGRAs): QuantiFERON TB Gold, T-SPOT. Blood tests measuring immune response.

TREATMENT: ANTI-TUBERCULOSIS DRUGS
- First-line drugs:
  - Bactericidal: Rifampicin (RMP/R), Isoniazid (INH/H), Streptomycin (S), Pyrazinamide (Z).
  - Bacteriostatic: Ethambutol (E).
- Second-line drugs:
  - Fluoroquinolones (Levofloxacin Lfx, Moxifloxacin Mfx).
  - Ethionamide (Eto), Protionamide (Pto).
  - Injectables: Capreomycin, Kanamycin, Amikacin.
  - Cycloserine (Cs), Thioacetazone, Macrolides, PAS.
  - Newer drugs: Bedaquiline (Bdq), Delamanid (Dlm), Linezolid (Lzd), Clofazimine (Cfz).

CHEMOTHERAPY PHASES (DOTS)
- 1. Intensive Phase (IP): 1-3 months. Health worker watches as patient swallows drugs. Rapidly kills bacilli.
- 2. Continuation Phase (CP): 6-9 months. Patient issued medicine in multiblister combipacks. Aimed at sterilizing dormant bacilli.

TREATMENT CATEGORIES & REGIMENS (NOTES & OLDER DOTS)
- Category I (Red Box): New cases (sputum positive, negative, extra-pulmonary). Regimen: 2(HRZE)3 + 4(HR)3.
- Category II (Blue Box): Previously treated (Relapse, Failure, Default). Regimen: 2(HRZES)3 + 1(HRZE)3 + 5(HRE)3.

NTEP REVISED DAILY DOSE REGIMEN (CURRENT)
- All patients treated with daily fixed-dose combinations (FDCs) based on weight bands.
- New Cases: IP - 2 HRZE; CP - 4 HRE.
- Previously Treated Cases: Treated exactly as new cases under recent guidelines unless drug resistance is detected.

DRUG-RESISTANT TB (DEFINITIONS)
- Mono-resistance: Resistant to one first-line drug.
- Poly-drug resistance: Resistant to >1 first-line drug (other than both H & R).
- Multidrug-resistant (MDR-TB): Resistant to at least Isoniazid (H) and Rifampicin (R).
- Rifampicin-resistant (RR-TB): Resistant to Rifampicin, with or without other resistance.
- Extensively drug-resistant (XDR-TB): MDR/RR-TB + resistance to any fluoroquinolone AND at least one of the second-line injectable drugs (amikacin, capreomycin, kanamycin) or newer drugs (bedaquiline/linezolid).

DRUG-RESISTANT TB TREATMENT REGIMENS
- 1. Shorter Oral Bedaquiline-containing MDR/RR-TB regimen: 9-11 months. 
  - IP (4-6 months): Bdq (6 months), Lfx, Cfz, Z, E, H(high dose), Eto.
  - CP (5 months): Lfx, Cfz, Z, E.
- 2. Longer Oral M/XDR-TB regimen: 18-20 months.
  - Lfx, Bdq (6m or longer), Lzd, Cfz, Cs.
- 3. BPaLM Regimen (New 6-month regimen): Bedaquiline, Pretomanid, Linezolid, Moxifloxacin. For MDR/RR-TB.
- 4. H mono/poly DR-TB regimen: 6-9 months of Lfx, R, E, Z.

BCG VACCINATION
- Bacillus Calmette-Guerin. Live attenuated vaccine (Danish 1331 strain used in India).
- Produced at BCG Laboratory at Guindy, Chennai.
- Types: Liquid (fresh) and Freeze-dried (stable for 1 year if kept below 10 C and away from sunlight).
- Diluent: Normal saline. Must be used within 3-4 hours after reconstitution.
- Age: At birth or at 6 weeks.
- Dose: <4 weeks = 0.05 ml; >4 weeks = 0.1 ml.
- Route: Intradermal using Tuberculin (Omega microstat) syringe. Site: Left deltoid muscle.
- Phenomenon after vaccination: 2-3 weeks (papule) -> 5 weeks (shallow ulcer) -> 6-12 weeks (heals with a permanent 4-8 mm scar).
- Complications: Severe ulceration, lymphadenitis, osteomyelitis, disseminated BCG infection.
- Protective value: Duration is 15-20 years. 80% effective in some trials, but highly effective in preventing severe childhood TB (meningitis, miliary).
- Contraindications: Infective dermatosis, immunodeficiency (HIV, leukemia, malignancy), immunosuppressive treatment, pregnancy.

CHEMOPROPHYLAXIS (TB PREVENTIVE TREATMENT - TPT)
- Recommended for PLHIV, household contacts <5 years, and other clinical risk groups.
- Regimens: 
  - 6H: Isoniazid daily for 6 months (or 1 year for PLHIV in notes).
  - 3HP: Isoniazid + Rifapentine weekly for 3 months.

TB AND COMORBIDITIES
- HIV: Co-infected patients must receive CPT (Cotrimoxazole) and ART. Rifampicin interacts with ART, requiring careful management.
- Diabetes: Bidirectional relationship. Worsens TB outcomes.
- Pregnancy: First-line drugs are safe. Streptomycin is contraindicated (ototoxic to fetus).

GLOBAL TB STRATEGIES
- DOTS Strategy (1994): 5 components (Government commitment, case detection via microscopy, standard short-course chemo, drug supply, monitoring).
- Stop TB Strategy (2006): Expanded DOTS, addressed TB/HIV and MDR-TB.
- End TB Strategy (2015-2035): 
  - Vision: World free of TB (0 deaths, 0 disease).
  - Indicators & Targets (2020 / 2025 / 2030 / 2035):
    - Reduction in TB deaths: 35% / 75% / 90% / 95%.
    - Reduction in TB incidence rate: 20% / 50% / 80% / 90%.
    - TB-affected families facing catastrophic costs: 0% at all milestones.
  - Pillars: 1. Integrated patient-centered care and prevention; 2. Bold policies and supportive systems; 3. Intensified research and innovation.
- Global Plan 90-90-90 targets: Reach at least 90% of all people with TB, Reach at least 90% of key populations, Achieve at least 90% treatment success.

subsections.title: Intestinal Infections - Part A (Poliomyelitis, Viral Hepatitis A, B, C, D, E)
subsections.content:
POLIOMYELITIS

OVERVIEW OF THE DISEASE
- An acute viral infection caused by an RNA virus.
- Primarily an infection of the human alimentary tract, but the virus may infect the central nervous system in a very small percentage (about 1%) of cases, resulting in varying degrees of flaccid paralysis and possibly death.

PROBLEM STATEMENT
- India was certified as polio-free on 27th March 2014.
- Polio remains endemic in Afghanistan and Pakistan. (Nigeria was previously endemic but no longer sustains WPV1).
- Wild Poliovirus (WPV) type 2 has not been detected since 1999 and was declared eradicated in September 2015.
- WPV type 3 has not been detected since November 2012 and is declared eradicated.
- WPV type 1 is the only wild poliovirus likely remaining in circulation.

AGENT FACTORS
- Agent: Poliovirus (RNA virus). Has 3 serotypes: 1, 2, and 3. Most outbreaks of paralytic polio are due to type-1 virus.
- Resistance: Survives for long periods in the external environment. In cold environments, it can live in water for 4 months and in faeces for 6 months. Rapidly inactivated by pasteurization and chemicals.
- Reservoir of infection: Man is the only known reservoir. Most infections are subclinical (1000 subclinical cases for every clinical case in children, 75 in adults). No chronic carriers.
- Infective material: Faeces and oropharyngeal secretions of an infected person.
- Period of communicability: 10 days before and 10 days after the onset of symptoms. Virus is excreted in the faeces for 2 to 3 weeks (sometimes up to 3 to 4 months), but is most infectious 7 to 10 days before and after onset.

HOST FACTORS
- Age: Affects all ages, but in India, it is essentially a disease of infancy and childhood. Between 6 months and 3 years is the most vulnerable age.
- Sex: Males are more affected. Sex ratio of 3 males to 1 female.
- Immunity: Maternal antibodies protect during the first 6 months. One attack confers solid, type-specific immunity.
- Risk factors (provocative factors): Intramuscular injections, tonsillectomy undertaken especially during an epidemic, administration of alum-containing DPT, and fatigue can precipitate an attack of paralytic polio.

ENVIRONMENTAL FACTORS
- Rainy season: More likely to occur during the rainy season (June to September in India).
- Overcrowding and poor sanitation provide opportunities for exposure.

MODE OF TRANSMISSION
- Faecal-oral route: Main route in developing countries (via contaminated water, food, flies, fingers).
- Droplet infection: May occur in the acute phase of the disease when the virus is in the throat.

INCUBATION PERIOD
- 7 to 14 days (range 3 to 35 days). Notes highlight 14 days.

CLINICAL SPECTRUM
- 1. Inapparent (Subclinical) infection: 91-96% of infections. No presenting symptoms.
- 2. Abortive polio or Minor illness: 4-8% of infections. Mild or self-limiting illness (viraemia) causing fever, malaise, sore throat. Patient recovers quickly.
- 3. Non-paralytic polio: About 1% of infections. Features stiffness and pain in the neck and back (aseptic meningitis).
- 4. Paralytic polio: Occurs in less than 1% of infections. Virus invades CNS. Asymmetrical flaccid paralysis. Deep tendon reflexes (DTRs) diminished. No sensory loss. 
- Tripod sign: Child sits by supporting hands at the back and partially flexing the hips and knees.
- Cause of death: Respiratory insufficiency.

PREVENTION AND CONTROL
- 1. Inactivated (Salk) Polio Vaccine (IPV):
  - Killed vaccine containing all 3 types (formulated to 40 D-antigen units of type 1, 8 of type 2, 32 of type 3).
  - Induces circulating (humoral) antibodies but no local intestinal immunity.
  - Prevents paralysis but does not prevent reinfection of the gut by wild viruses.
  - Dose: 0.5 ml Intramuscular (IM) in the anterolateral thigh, or as a fractional dose.
  - Fractional dose of IPV (fIPV): 0.1 ml given intradermally (ID) at 6th and 14th weeks provides higher seroconversion rates than a single full dose of IPV given IM at 14 weeks.
- 2. Oral (Sabin) Polio Vaccine (OPV):
  - Live attenuated virus. Given orally (2 drops).
  - Induces both humoral and intestinal (local) immunity.
  - Excreted virus infects others, immunizing them (herd immunity).
  - Contains 10^6.0 CCID50 of type 1 and 10^5.8 CCID50 of type 3 (in bOPV).
  - Highly effective in controlling epidemics.
  - Contraindications: Immunocompromised individuals, leukemia, malignancy. Diarrhoea is not a contraindication, but an extra dose should be given later.
  - Vaccine Associated Paralytic Poliomyelitis (VAPP): Rare adverse event. Frequently associated with Sabin 3 (60% of cases), followed by Sabin 2 and Sabin 1.
  - Vaccine Derived Poliovirus (VDPV): Mutated vaccine strains. Divided into cVDPVs (circulating), iVDPVs (immunodeficiency-associated), and aVDPVs (ambiguous).

POLIO ERADICATION AND ENDGAME STRATEGIC PLAN (2013-2018)
- Objectives: 1. Virus detection and interruption; 2. Routine Immunization (RI) strengthening and OPV withdrawal; 3. Containment and certification; 4. Legacy planning.
- The Switch: From trivalent OPV (tOPV) to bivalent OPV (bOPV) globally in April 2016. 
- Reason for Switch: WPV type 2 has been eradicated since 1999. The risk of VAPP and cVDPV to the type 2 component of OPV outweighs its benefits.
- IPV Introduction: At least 1 dose of IPV introduced before the switch to maintain immunity against type 2 poliovirus.

PULSE POLIO IMMUNIZATION (PPI)
- Simultaneous, mass administration of OPV on a single day to all children 0-5 years of age.
- First round was held on 9th December 1995 and 20th January 1996 (initially targeted under 3 years, later WHO recommended under 5 years).
- Conducted as 2 rounds 6 weeks apart during the low transmission season of polio (November to February).
- Doses during PPI are extra doses supplementing, and not replacing, routine immunization services.

ACUTE FLACCID PARALYSIS (AFP) SURVEILLANCE
- Finding and reporting children with AFP under 15 years of age.
- Conducted through Surveillance Medical Officers (SMOs) stationed at state headquarters.
- Requires transporting 2 stool samples collected 24-48 hours apart, within 14 days of paralysis onset.
- Stool specimens are shipped in a cold box (4-8 C) via reverse cold chain and must reach the lab within 72 hours.
- Isolating and mapping the virus by a WHO accredited laboratory.
- Surveillance Indicator: At least 2 cases of non-polio AFP per 100,000 population aged <15 years must be detected annually.

VIRAL HEPATITIS

HEPATITIS A

OVERVIEW OF THE DISEASE
- Formerly known as infectious hepatitis or epidemic jaundice.
- Caused by Hepatitis A virus (HAV).
- Endemic in most developing countries.

EPIDEMIOLOGICAL DETERMINANTS
- Agent: HAV, an Enterovirus (type 72) of the Picornaviridae family (RNA virus).
- Reservoir of infection: Human cases. No chronic carrier state.
- Infective material: Man's faeces.
- Period of infectivity: 2 weeks before to 1 week after onset of jaundice.
- Virus excretion: Excreted in faeces for about 2 weeks before jaundice and up to 2 weeks thereafter.
- Host factors: Children. The ratio of anicteric to icteric cases is 1:3 in adults, and up to 12:1 in children. One attack confers lifelong immunity.
- Environmental factors: Heavy rainfall, poor sanitation, overcrowding.
- Mode of transmission: Faecal-oral route is the major route (contaminated water, food). Parenteral and sexual routes are rare.
- Incubation period: 10 to 50 days (usually 14-28 days; Notes state 28 days).

CLINICAL SPECTRUM
- Nausea, vomiting, anorexia, mild fever, followed by jaundice.

PREVENTION AND CONTROL
- Control of reservoir: Disinfection of faeces & fomites using sodium hypochlorite (0.5%).
- Control of transmission: Personal & community hygiene (hand washing), sanitary disposal of excreta, purification of water.
- Vaccines: 
  - Formaldehyde inactivated vaccine (>12 months of age, 2 doses with 6-12 months interval, I/M in deltoid).
  - Live attenuated vaccine (single dose, subcutaneous).

HEPATITIS B

OVERVIEW OF THE DISEASE
- Formerly known as Serum Hepatitis.
- Accounts for 45% of Hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC) and 30% of cirrhosis of liver.
- Hepatitis B prevalence is highest in sub-Saharan Africa & East Asia.

EPIDEMIOLOGICAL DETERMINANTS
- Agent: Hepatitis B virus (HBV), a double-shelled DNA virus (Hepadnavirus family), originally known as the "Dane particle" (42-nm).
- Antigens: Surface antigen (HBsAg / Australia antigen), Core antigen (HBcAg), e antigen (HBeAg).
- Reservoir of infection: Man is the only reservoir. Carrier state is defined as the presence of HBsAg for more than 6 months.
- Infective material: Contaminated blood, body secretions (saliva, vaginal secretions, semen of infected persons).
- Period of communicability: Present in blood during the incubation period (month before jaundice).
- Resistance: Survives for at least 7 days on environmental surfaces. Destroyed by sodium hypochlorite or autoclaving.
- Host factors (Age & Chronicity): Chronic Hep B develops in 90% of perinatal infections, 30% in children <6 years, and 5% in >6 years (adults).
- High-risk groups: Surgeons, recipients of blood transfusion / organ transplants, laboratory personnel, homosexuals, prostitutes, percutaneous drug abusers, infants of HBV carrier mothers.
- Mode of transmission: 
  - Parenteral route (Blood-borne infection).
  - Perinatal transmission (vertical): Leak of maternal blood into baby's circulation.
  - Sexual transmission.
  - Other routes: Horizontal transmission (child to child through physical contact with skin cuts/grazes).
- Incubation period: 30 to 180 days (average 75 days).

CLINICAL PICTURE
- Insidious onset. Can lead to progressive liver fibrosis, cirrhosis, and markedly increased risk of hepatocellular carcinoma.
- Diagnosis: HBsAg is the primary marker. IgM anti-HBc confirms acute infection.

PREVENTION AND CONTROL
- 1. Hepatitis B Vaccine:
  - Recombinant Hep B vaccine has replaced the plasma-derived vaccine. Active substance is HBsAg.
  - Available as monovalent or fixed combination (Pentavalent vaccine: DPT, Hep B & Hib).
  - Dose: 0.5 ml, I/M.
  - Site: Anterolateral aspect of mid-thigh.
  - Schedule: 6, 10 & 14 weeks (after Hep B birth dose at 0 week).
- 2. Hepatitis B Immunoglobulin (HBIG):
  - Used for those acutely exposed to HBsAg positive blood (surgeons, lab workers, newborn infants of carrier mothers, sexual contacts).
  - Given as soon as possible (ideally within 6 hours, preferably not later than 48 hours).
  - Dose: 0.05 ml/kg body weight. 2 doses 30 days apart.

TREATMENT
- Tenofovir (TDF) or Entecavir (ETV).

HEPATITIS C

OVERVIEW OF THE DISEASE
- Agent: Hepatitis C virus (HCV), a RNA virus of the Flavivirus family.
- Major cause of post-transfusion hepatitis.
- High rate of chronicity: 55-85% of persons will develop chronic HCV infection. Risk of cirrhosis is 15-30% within 20 years.

TRANSMISSION AND INCUBATION
- Transmission: Exposure to infectious blood (blood transfusions, contaminated syringes, injecting drug use). Sexual and perinatal transmission are less common.
- Incubation period: 2 weeks to 6 months (avg 40-120 days).

DIAGNOSIS AND TREATMENT
- Diagnosis: Anti-HCV antibody testing, followed by HCV RNA (viral load) testing for confirmation.
- Treatment: Direct Antiviral Agents (DAAs) such as Sofosbuvir/velpatasvir, Glecaprevir/pibrentasvir, Sofosbuvir/daclatasvir. Highly effective (cure rate high), short duration (8-24 weeks).
- Prevention: Blood safety, safe injections, harm reduction for IDUs. No vaccine is currently available.

HEPATITIS D

OVERVIEW OF THE DISEASE
- Agent: Hepatitis D virus (HDV), a defective RNA virus that requires HBV for its replication.
- Transmission: Co-infection or super-infection with HBV. Transmitted percutaneously or sexually.
- Clinical: Causes a more severe disease than HBV monoinfection. High prevalence in Mediterranean, Middle East, Pakistan.
- Prevention: Vaccination against HBV prevents HDV co-infection.

HEPATITIS E

OVERVIEW OF THE DISEASE
- Agent: Hepatitis E virus (HEV), an RNA virus (Calicivirus/Hepevirus).
- Enterically transmitted non-A, non-B hepatitis. Essentially a water-borne disease causing large epidemics in Asia and Africa.

TRANSMISSION AND INCUBATION
- Transmission: Faecal-oral route (contaminated drinking water). Food-borne and vertical transmission also possible.
- Incubation period: 2 to 9 weeks (average 40 days).

CLINICAL PICTURE
- Usually mild and self-limiting in young adults.
- Can result in fulminant hepatitis (acute liver failure) and death, particularly during pregnancy. Pregnant women are at greater risk of obstetrical complications and mortality.
- Prevention: Provision of safe drinking water and proper disposal of sanitary waste. No vaccine available globally (one registered only in China).

subsections.title: Intestinal Infections - Part B (Acute Diarrhoeal Diseases, Cholera, Typhoid Fever, Food Poisoning, Amoebiasis, Helminthiasis)
subsections.content:
ACUTE DIARRHOEAL DISEASES

OVERVIEW OF THE DISEASE
- Diarrhoea is defined as the passage of loose, liquid or watery stools, more than 3 times a day.
- The recent change in consistency and character of stools is more important than the number of stools.
- Diarrhoeal diseases should be considered as a group of diseases in which the predominant symptom is diarrhoea.

CLINICAL TYPES OF DIARRHOEAL DISEASE
- 1. Acute watery diarrhoea: Lasts several hours to days. Main danger is dehydration, weight loss also occurs if feeding is not continued. Pathogens: V. cholerae, E. coli, and Rotavirus.
- 2. Acute bloody diarrhoea: Also called Dysentery. Main dangers are damage to the intestinal mucosa, sepsis, malnutrition, and dehydration. Pathogen: Shigella.
- 3. Persistent diarrhoea: Lasts 14 days or longer. Main dangers are malnutrition, non-intestinal infections, and dehydration. Persons with other illnesses such as AIDS are more likely to develop persistent diarrhoea.
- 4. Diarrhoea with severe malnutrition: Marasmus and kwashiorkor. Main dangers are severe systemic infection, dehydration, heart failure, and vitamin/mineral deficiency.

PROBLEM STATEMENT
- Accounts for 9% (India 8%) of all under-5 deaths, mostly in children less than 2 years of age.

EPIDEMIOLOGICAL DETERMINANTS

AGENT FACTORS
- 1. Viruses:
  - Rotavirus: Leading cause of severe, dehydrating diarrhoea in children under 5 years globally. Peaks during the winter season. Shed in very high concentration (>10^12 particles / gram).
  - Other viruses: Astroviruses, Adenoviruses, Calciviruses, Coronaviruses, Norwalk group viruses, Enteroviruses, Cytomegalovirus.
- 2. Bacteria:
  - Enterotoxigenic Escherichia coli (ETEC): Important cause of acute watery diarrhoea in adults and children. Most common cause of traveler's diarrhoea. 2 toxins: heat labile (LT) and heat stable (ST).
  - Shigella: Accounts for a high percentage of mortality due to diarrhoeal disease. Major cause of diarrhoea in India.
  - Campylobacter jejuni: Commonest cause of enteritis.
  - Vibrio cholerae 01: In cholera endemic areas, it accounts for 5-10% of all acute diarrhoeas yearly.
  - Salmonella: Causes inflammation of bowel epithelium.
  - Other bacteria: V. parahaemolyticus, Bacillus cereus, Staphylococcus aureus, Clostridium perfringens, Clostridium difficile, Enteroinvasive E. coli, Aeromonas, Yersinia enterocolitica, Chlamydia, Neisseria gonorrhoeae.
- 3. Others (Parasites):
  - Cryptosporidium: Coccidian parasite causing diarrhoea in infants, immunodeficient patients (e.g., AIDS), and domestic animals.
  - Giardia intestinalis: Epithelial surface is smothered with them.
  - Entamoeba histolytica, Trichuriasis, Intestinal worms, Cyclospora.

BESIDES ABOVE CAUSES
- 1. Malnutrition: May lead to kwashiorkor, sprue, coeliac disease, and pellagra which are all associated with diarrhoea.
- 2. Inborn error of metabolism: Such as congenital enzyme deficiencies may lead to diarrhoea in the newborn.
- 3. AIDS: Persistent diarrhoea is one of the main clinical signs.

RESERVOIR OF INFECTION
- Man: E. coli, Shigella, V. cholerae, G. lamblia, and E. histolytica.
- Animal: C. jejuni and Salmonella.

HOST FACTORS
- Highest incidence in 6-11 months when weaning occurs.
- Due to declining levels of maternal antibodies.
- Contaminated foods and direct contact with infections as infants start to crawl.
- Due to incorrect feeding practices.

ENVIRONMENTAL FACTORS
- Bacterial diarrhoea: Occurs more frequently during the warm and rainy season.
- Viral diarrhoea: Peaks during the winter season.

MODE OF TRANSMISSION
- Faecal-oral route is the primary mode of transmission.
- Water-borne.
- Food-borne.

DIARRHOEAL DISEASE CONTROL PROGRAMME IN INDIA
- 1978: Started with objectives of reducing mortality due to diarrhoeal diseases.
- 1985-86: Inception of National Oral Rehydration Therapy programme.
- 1992-93: Became a part of the CSSM (Child Survival and Safe Motherhood) programme.
- At present: Part of NRHM (National Rural Health Mission).

SHORT TERM PROGRAMME (APPROPRIATE CLINICAL MANAGEMENT)
- 1. Oral Rehydration Therapy (ORT)
- 2. Intravenous rehydration
- 3. Maintenance therapy
- 4. Appropriate feeding
- 5. Chemotherapy
- 6. Zinc supplementation

LONG TERM PROGRAMME
- 1. Better MCH care practices: Maternal nutrition and Child nutrition.
- 2. Preventing strategies: Sanitation, Health education, Immunization, Fly control.
- 3. Control and preventing diarrhoeal epidemics.
- 4. Integrated global action plan for the prevention and control of pneumonia and diarrhoea (GAPPD).

APPROPRIATE CLINICAL MANAGEMENT DETAILS

ORAL REHYDRATION THERAPY (ORT)
- Composition of reduced osmolarity ORS in grams/litre:
  - Sodium chloride: 2.6
  - Glucose, anhydrous: 13.5
  - Potassium chloride: 1.5
  - Trisodium citrate, dihydrate: 2.9
  - Total weight: 20.5
- Composition of reduced osmolarity ORS in mmol/litre:
  - Sodium: 75
  - Chloride: 65
  - Glucose, anhydrous: 75
  - Potassium: 20
  - Citrate: 10
  - Total osmolarity: 245 mmol/L

RULES FOR ORS DOSAGE
- Children under 2 years: 50-100ml after each loose stool.
- 2-10 years: 100-200ml after each loose stool.
- Older children and adults: Give as much fluid as they want.
- If the child vomits, wait for 10 minutes then try again slowly (a spoonful every 2 to 3 minutes).
- Breastfeeding should be continued during treatment with ORS.
- Contents of the packet are to be dissolved in 1 litre of drinking water and should be used within 24 hours.
- If ORS is not available: 1 teaspoon table salt and 6 teaspoons of sugar dissolved in 1 litre of drinking water is used until ORS is available.

INTRAVENOUS REHYDRATION
- Ringer lactate solution (RL) / Hartmann's solution is the best commercially available solution.
- Diarrhoea treatment solution (DTS): 1 litre water, sodium chloride 4g, sodium acetate 6.5g, potassium chloride 1g, and glucose 10g.
- If nothing is available, normal saline can be given.
- Dose for Infants: 30ml/kg in 1 hour, then 70ml/kg in 5 hours.
- Dose for Older Patients: 30ml/kg in 30 min, then 70ml/kg in 2.5 hours.
- Rehydration must continue until all signs of dehydration have disappeared.

MAINTENANCE THERAPY
- Mild diarrhoea: Not more than 1 stool every 2 hours or less than 5ml stool/kg/hr. Give 100ml of oral fluid / kg body weight / day until diarrhoea stops.
- Severe diarrhoea: More than 1 stool every 2 hours or more than 5ml stool/kg/hr. Give 15ml of oral fluid / kg body weight / hour.

APPROPRIATE FEEDING
- During episodes of diarrhoea, normal food intake should be promoted as soon as the child is able to eat.
- Newborn infants with diarrhoea who show little or no signs of dehydration can be treated by breastfeeding alone.
- Moderate or severe dehydration receives oral rehydration solution.

CHEMOTHERAPY
- Diarrhoea due to V. Cholerae: Doxycycline, tetracycline, TMP-SMX, and erythromycin.
- Diarrhoea due to Shigella: Ciprofloxacin.

ZINC SUPPLEMENTATION
- Reduces the episode's duration and severity.
- Lower the incidence of diarrhoea in the following 2 to 3 months.
- Infants under 6 months: 10mg / day for 14 days.
- Children older than 6 months: 20mg / day for 14 days.

LONG TERM PROGRAMME DETAILS
- Better MCH care practices: Improving prenatal nutrition will reduce LBW. Improving postnatal nutrition will improve quality of breast milk. Promotion of breastfeeding (limit commercial formulas). Appropriate weaning practices (not earlier than 6 months). Supplementary feeding (improve nutritional status aged 6-59 months). Vitamin A supplementation (reduces duration, severity, and complications).
- Preventing strategies: Sanitation (improve water supply, excreta disposal, food hygiene). Health education (breastfeeding, weaning, clean drinking water, proper excreta disposal).
- Immunization:
  - Measles vaccine: Can prevent up to 25% of diarrhoeal deaths in under-5 children.
  - Rotavirus vaccine: Rotarix (Monovalent human rotavirus vaccine, orally, 2 doses at 6 and 12 weeks). Rota Teq (Pentavalent bovine-human reassortant vaccine, orally, 3 doses at 2, 4, and 6 months).
- Fly control: Flies breeding in association with faeces should be controlled.
- Control and preventing diarrhoeal epidemics: Requires strengthening of epidemiological surveillance systems.
- Integrated global action plan for the prevention and control of pneumonia and diarrhoea (GAPPD): Brings together critical services and interventions to create healthy environments, promotes protective practices, and ensures access to proven preventive and treatment measures.

CHOLERA

OVERVIEW OF THE DISEASE
- An acute diarrhoeal disease caused by V. Cholerae O1 (Classical or El Tor) and O139.
- Characterized by sudden onset of profuse, effortless, watery diarrhoea followed by vomiting, rapid dehydration, muscular cramps, and suppression of urine.

PROBLEM STATEMENT
- El Tor biotype of V. Cholerae O1 has rapidly replaced the classical biotype in all parts of the country.
- Most of the El Tor biotype isolated today belongs to the serotype Ogawa.
- During 2014, 969 cholera cases were reported in India with 4 deaths.
- Majority of cases were reported from Maharashtra (253) followed by West Bengal (173).

EPIDEMIOLOGICAL DETERMINANTS

AGENT FACTORS
- Agent: V. Cholerae O group 1 or V. Cholerae O1 & O139 (epidemic strain). Non-O group 1V / O139 Cholerae (non-epidemic strain) includes Vibrio parahaemolyticus.
- O group 1 has 2 biotypes: Classical and El Tor.
- Classical and El Tor vibrios are further divided into 3 serological types: Inaba, Ogawa, and Hikojima.
- Toxin production: Vibrios multiply in the lumen of the small intestine and produce enterotoxins which produce diarrhoea through its effect on the adenylate cyclase-cyclic AMP system of mucosal cells of the small intestine.
- Reservoir of infection: Human (Cases and Carriers).
- Infective material: Stools and vomit of cases and carriers.

CARRIERS IN CHOLERA
- 1. Incubatory carriers: 1 to 5 days.
- 2. Convalescent carriers: 2 to 3 weeks (Notes mention 21 days).
- 3. Healthy carriers: 10 days.
- 4. Chronic carriers: 10 years. Affects the gall bladder.

HOST FACTORS
- Age: All ages and both sexes.
- Population mobility: Pilgrimages and fairs.
- Economic status: Low socio-economic status.

ENVIRONMENTAL FACTORS
- Poor environmental sanitation.
- Contaminated water and food.
- Low standards of personal hygiene.

MODE OF TRANSMISSION
- Faecally contaminated water.
- Contaminated food.
- Direct contact with excreta and vomit of patients, and contaminated linen and fomites.

INCUBATION PERIOD
- 1 to 5 days (Notes mention 2 days specifically).

CLINICAL FEATURES
- 1. Stage of evacuation: Profuse, painless, watery diarrhoea followed by vomiting. Up to 40 stools in a day.
- 2. Stage of collapse: Sunken eyes, hollow cheeks, absent pulse, unrecordable blood pressure, loss of skin elasticity, and decreased urine output.
- 3. Stage of recovery: Clinical improvement, blood pressure and temperature returns to normal, and urine output is reestablished.

DIAGNOSIS
- Collection: Venkatraman-Ramakrishnan (VR) medium or alkaline peptone water.
- Transportation: Cary-Blair Medium.
- Direct examination: Dark field illumination showing shooting stars in a dark sky.
- Culture: Bile Salt Agar medium.
- Characterization: Scintillating type of movement in hanging drop preparations.

PREVENTION AND CONTROL
- 1. Rehydration therapy: ORS and I/V rehydration.
- 2. Sanitation measures: Provision of piped water supplies, drinking water containing free residual chlorine, provision of sanitary latrines, sale of food under hygienic conditions, proper disinfection with cresol or bleaching powder.
- 3. Chemoprophylaxis: Tetracycline for 3 days, twice daily, 500mg.
- 4. Vaccination:
  - Dukoral: Monovalent, Oral, 2 doses given 7 days apart. For adults and children >2 years, 1 booster dose after 2 years.
  - Sanchol: Bivalent, Oral, 2 doses given 14 days apart. For individuals > 1 year.
- 5. Health education: Effectiveness of ORT, food hygiene practices, proper hand washing practices after defecation and before eating.

DIARRHOEAL DISEASE CONTROL PROGRAMME
- Objective is to prevent diarrhoea-associated deaths in children due to dehydration.
- Health education highlighting the rational management of diarrhoea in children.
- ORS is promoted as the first line of treatment.

TYPHOID FEVER

OVERVIEW OF THE DISEASE
- Systemic infection mainly caused by S. typhi, found only in man.
- Characterized by continuous fever, bradycardia with involvement of lymphoid tissues.
- The term enteric fever includes both typhoid and paratyphoid fevers.

PROBLEM STATEMENT
- Endemic in India.
- Maximum cases were reported from Bihar followed by UP.

EPIDEMIOLOGICAL DETERMINANTS

AGENT FACTORS
- Agent: S. typhi. Contains 3 antigens: O, H, and Vi.
- Reservoir of infection: Man (Cases and Carriers).
- Carriers: Convalescent carriers (6 to 8 weeks), Chronic carriers (> 1 year). Faecal carriers are more frequent than urinary carriers. "Typhoid Mary" is a famous example.
- Source of infection: Faeces and urine of cases and carriers.

HOST FACTORS
- Age: 5 to 19 years.
- Sex: Males (cases), Females (carriers).

ENVIRONMENTAL FACTORS
- Rainy season (July-September coincides with the peak incidence).

INCUBATION PERIOD
- 10 to 14 days.

MODES OF TRANSMISSION
- Faecal-oral route.
- Urine-oral routes.

CLINICAL FEATURES
- Malaise, headache, cough, and sore throat.
- Abdominal pain and constipation.
- Fever ascends in a step-ladder fashion.
- Pea soup diarrhoea.
- Rash: Rose spots.

COMPLICATIONS
- Intestinal hemorrhage.
- Intestinal perforation.

DIAGNOSIS
- Blood culture (mainstay of diagnosis).
- Widal test.
- IDL Tubex test.
- Typhidot test (detects specific IgM and IgG antibodies).

PREVENTION AND CONTROL
- 1. Control of reservoir:
  - Cases: Fluoroquinolones. Disinfection of stool and urine with 5% cresol and soiled clothes/linen with chlorine.
  - Carriers: Amoxicillin with probenecid for 6 weeks. Cholecystectomy is the most successful approach for carriers.
- 2. Control of sanitation:
  - Purification of drinking water supplies and improvement of basic sanitation.
- 3. Immunization:
  - Vi Polysaccharide Vaccine: Ty2 S. typhi strain. S/C or I/M route. For > 2 years of age, 1 dose. Protection starts after 7 days of injection. Revaccination every 3 years.
  - Ty 21 A Vaccine: Live attenuated Ty2 S. typhi strain. Orally (enteric coated capsules). For > 5 years of age, 3 doses on 1st, 3rd, and 5th day. Immunity is achieved in 7 days after the last dose. Revaccination every 3 years in endemic areas and every year for individuals travelling to endemic areas from non-endemic areas.

FOOD POISONING

OVERVIEW OF THE DISEASE
- An acute gastroenteritis caused by ingestion of food or drink contaminated with either living bacteria or their toxins or inorganic chemical substances.
- Characterized by: History of ingestion of common food, attack of many persons at the same time, and similarity of signs and symptoms in the majority of cases.

TYPES OF FOOD POISONING
- 1. Non-bacterial: Caused by chemicals like arsenic, mercury, cadmium, etc. Also caused by sea food, fertilizers, pesticides, etc.
- 2. Bacterial: Foods contaminated with bacteria or their toxins.
  - Salmonella food poisoning: Agents are S. typhimurium, S. enteritidis. Source: farm animals, poultry, contaminated meat. Incubation period: 12-24 hours.
  - Staphylococcal food poisoning: Agents are enterotoxins of Staphylococcus aureus. Source: milk or milk products from cows suffering from mastitis, or from human food handlers. Incubation period: 1-6 hours (short because of preformed toxin).
  - Botulism: Agent is the exotoxin of Clostridium botulinum. Source: canned food or pickled foods. Incubation period: 12-36 hours.
  - C. perfringens food poisoning: Agent is Clostridium perfringens (welchii). Source: food consumed after 24 hours of preparation (meat dishes, poultry). Incubation period: 6-24 hours.
  - B. cereus food poisoning: Agent is Bacillus cereus. Source: dried and processed food (e.g., fried rice). Incubation period: Emetic form (1-6 hours), Diarrheal form (12-24 hours).

INVESTIGATION OF FOOD POISONING
- 1. Secure a complete list of people involved.
- 2. Laboratory investigations.
- 3. Animal experiments.
- 4. Blood for antibodies.
- 5. Environmental study.
- 6. Data analysis.

PREVENTION AND CONTROL
- 1. Food sanitation: Meat inspection, personal hygiene, food handlers, food handling techniques, sanitary improvements, health education.
- 2. Refrigeration: Below 4 deg. C (40 deg. F).

AMOEBIASIS

OVERVIEW OF THE DISEASE
- The condition of harbouring the protozoan parasite Entamoeba histolytica with or without clinical manifestations.
- A common infection of the human gastrointestinal tract. Worldwide distribution, major health problem in developing countries.

EPIDEMIOLOGICAL DETERMINANTS
- Agent: E. histolytica (exists in vegetative trophozoite and cystic forms).
- Reservoir of Infection: Man is the only reservoir (cases and carriers).
- Infective material: Faeces containing the cysts of E. histolytica.
- Period of communicability: As long as cysts are excreted (may be several years).
- Host factors: Affects all ages, no sex or racial difference. Often a household infection.
- Environmental factors: Closely related to poor sanitation and low socio-economic status. Use of nightsoil for agriculture favours spread.
- Mode of transmission: Faecal-oral route (contaminated water or food), sexual transmission (oral-rectal contact), and vectors (flies, cockroaches, rodents).
- Incubation period: About 2 to 4 weeks or longer.

CLINICAL FEATURES
- Intestinal amoebiasis (mild abdominal discomfort, diarrhoea to acute fulminating dysentery).
- Extraintestinal amoebiasis (involvement of liver leading to liver abscess, lungs, brain, spleen, skin).

PREVENTION AND CONTROL
- Primary prevention: Sanitation (safe disposal of human excreta, hand washing), Water supply (protection, filtration, boiling), Food hygiene.
- Secondary prevention: Early diagnosis (demonstration of trophozoites/cysts in stool) and treatment (Metronidazole, Tinidazole for symptomatic cases; Diiodohyroxyquin or Diloxanide furoate for asymptomatic carriers).

SOIL-TRANSMITTED HELMINTHIASIS

OVERVIEW OF THE DISEASE
- Includes intestinal roundworms (Ascariasis), hookworms (Necator americanus, Ancylostoma duodenale), and whipworm (Trichuris trichiura).
- Widely distributed in tropical and subtropical areas.

ASCARIASIS
- Agent: Ascaris lumbricoides (adult worm lives in small intestine).
- Reservoir: Man is the only reservoir.
- Transmission: Faecal-oral (ingestion of embryonated eggs via contaminated soil, vegetables, water).
- Host factors: Highest incidence in children aged 3-8 years.
- Incubation period: About 2 months for the life cycle to complete.

HOOKWORM INFECTION
- Agent: Ancylostoma duodenale and Necator americanus.
- Reservoir: Man.
- Transmission: Larvae penetrate the skin (usually walking barefoot on contaminated soil). Direct transmission can occur via oral route for A. duodenale.
- Incubation period (prepatent period): 7 weeks for N. americanus, unpredictable for A. duodenale (up to 9 months).
- Clinical: Chronic blood loss leading to iron-deficiency anaemia, hypoalbuminaemia.

PREVENTION AND CONTROL
- Primary prevention: Sanitary disposal of human excreta, safe drinking water, food hygiene, health education, wearing protective footwear.
- Secondary prevention: Deworming with Albendazole (400 mg) or Mebendazole (100 mg twice daily for 3 days). Levamisole and Pyrantel are also used.

subsections.title: Arthropod-Borne Infections (Dengue Syndrome, Malaria, Lymphatic Filariasis, Zika Virus)
subsections.content:
DENGUE SYNDROME

OVERVIEW OF THE DISEASE
- Dengue viruses are arboviruses capable of infecting humans and causing disease.
- Infections may be asymptomatic or may lead to classical dengue fever (DF), dengue haemorrhagic fever (DHF) without shock, or dengue haemorrhagic fever with shock (Dengue Shock Syndrome - DSS).
- It is a self-limiting disease. Aedes aegypti and Aedes albopictus are the main vectors.
- At present, DENV-1 and DENV-2 serotypes are widespread in India.

EPIDEMIOLOGICAL DETERMINANTS

AGENT FACTORS
- Agent: Dengue virus (genus Flavivirus) is an RNA virus.
- 4 virus serotypes exist: DENV-1, DENV-2, DENV-3, and DENV-4.
- Infection with any one serotype confers lifelong immunity to that virus serotype only. They elicit cross-protection for only a few months against other types.
- Secondary infection with dengue serotype 2 or multiple infections with different serotypes lead to severe form (DHF/DSS).
- The first infection probably sensitizes the patient, while the second infection with a different serotype appears to produce immunological catastrophy (Antibody-dependent enhancement).
- Vector: Aedes aegypti and Aedes albopictus carry high vectorial competency.
  - Aedes aegypti is highly domesticated, strongly anthropophilic, and a nervous feeder (bites more than one host to complete one blood meal). It is a discordant species (needs more than one feed for gonotropic cycle completion). This results in multiple cases and clustering of dengue cases in cities.
  - Aedes albopictus partly invades peripheral areas, is an aggressive feeder, and is a concordant species (completes blood meal in one go on one person).

TRANSMISSION OF DISEASE
- The Aedes mosquito becomes infective by feeding on a patient from the day before onset to the 5th day (viraemia stage) of illness.
- Extrinsic incubation period: 8 to 10 days. Once infective, the mosquito remains so for life.
- Transovarian transmission of dengue virus occurs when the virus enters fully developed eggs at the time of oviposition.

ENVIRONMENTAL FACTORS
- Being a domestic breeder, it breeds in the containers in and around the houses (e.g., roof tops, stored water).
- It is an endophagic and endophilic vector.
- With a 2 C increase in temperature, the extrinsic incubation period is shortened.

INCUBATION PERIOD
- 3 to 10 days (commonly 5-6 days).

CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS

1. UNDIFFERENTIATED FEVER
- Infants, children, and adults infected for the first time may develop a simple fever indistinguishable from other viral infections.
- Maculopapular rashes may accompany the fever or appear during defervescence.

2. CLASSICAL DENGUE FEVER (DF)
- Sudden onset with chills and high fever, intense headache, muscle and joint pain.
- Within 24 hours, retroorbital pain (on eye movements) & photophobia develops.
- Extreme weakness, anorexia, constipation, altered taste, colicky pain, dragging pain in inguinal region.
- Biphasic fever curve (remission of a few hours to 2 days).
- Skin eruptions (maculopapular rash) appear in 80% of cases during remission or second febrile phase (lasts 1-2 days), followed by desquamation.

3. DENGUE HAEMORRHAGIC FEVER (DHF)
- A severe form characterized by plasma leakage and abnormal haemostasis.
- Divided into three phases:
  - 1. Febrile phase: Lasts 2-7 days. Sudden high fever, facial flushing, headache, vomiting. Positive tourniquet test or petechiae, ecchymoses, purpura. Bleeding from mucosa, GI tract, injection sites. Mild hepatomegaly. 
  - 2. Critical phase: Occurs around defervescence (days 3-7). Progressive leukopenia followed by rapid decrease in platelet count (<100,000 cells/cu.mm). Plasma leakage leads to haemoconcentration (Haematocrit rises >= 20%), pleural effusion, ascites, hypoproteinaemia. 
  - 3. Recovery phase: 24-48 hours after critical phase. Gradual reabsorption of extravascular fluid. Rash of "isles of white in the sea of red". Haematocrit stabilizes. White blood cell count rises first, followed by platelet recovery.

4. DENGUE SHOCK SYNDROME (DSS)
- Criteria for DHF plus signs of shock: Tachycardia, cool extremities, delayed capillary refill, weak pulse, lethargy or restlessness.
- Pulse pressure <= 20 mm Hg.
- Systolic pressure < 80 mm Hg for < 5 years, or < 90 mm Hg for older children and adults.

LABORATORY DIAGNOSIS
- Virus isolation: From blood during the first 5 days (viraemic stage).
- RT-PCR: Detects viral RNA.
- NS1 Antigen Test: Highly conserved glycoprotein, abundant in serum during early stages. Simple test, high sensitivity, enables early detection.
- MAC-ELISA (IgM-Capture ELISA): Detects IgM antibodies by day 5 of illness. Wanes off by 60 days (up to 90 days).
- IgG ELISA: Used for past infections. 4-fold rise in paired sera confirms infection.

GRADING OF SEVERITY AND MANAGEMENT
- Mild Dengue (Group A): Managed on OPD basis. Patients can tolerate adequate oral fluids, pass urine every 6 hours, no warning signs. Treatment: ORS, paracetamol (avoid aspirin/NSAIDs), tepid sponging.
- Moderate Dengue (Group B): Observation/in-hospital management. Dengue with warning signs (persistent vomiting, abdominal pain, lethargy, mucosal bleeding, liver >2cm, rising Hct with falling platelets) or co-morbidities (pregnancy, infancy, old age, diabetes). Treatment: IV fluid (0.9% NS or Ringer Lactate) if oral not tolerated.
- Severe Dengue (Group C): Emergency treatment. Severe plasma leakage leading to shock (DSS), severe bleeding, or severe organ impairment (hepatic damage, cardiomyopathy, encephalitis). Treatment: Isotonic crystalloid solutions (10-20 ml/kg/hr over 1 hour), Colloids (Dextran 40) if massive leakage, Blood transfusion (10 ml/kg whole blood or 5 ml/kg packed RBC) if suspected severe overt bleed.

PREVENTION AND CONTROL
- Mosquito control: Anti-adult and anti-larval measures, source reduction (elimination of breeding places), covering stored water, weekly dry day.
- Personal prophylaxis: Bed nets, full sleeves, mosquito repellents.
- Dengue Vaccines:
  - CYD-TDV (Dengvaxia, Sanofi): Tetravalent live-attenuated recombinant vaccine. 3 doses at 0, 6, and 12 months. Indicated for ages 9-45 years living in endemic areas. Safe ONLY in persons who have had a prior dengue virus infection (seropositive). Increases risk of severe dengue in seronegative individuals.
  - TAK-003 (Qdenga, Takeda): Tetravalent live-attenuated vaccine.

MALARIA

OVERVIEW OF THE DISEASE
- A protozoal disease caused by Plasmodium parasites and transmitted by the bite of certain species of infected female Anopheline mosquitoes.
- Global burden: 249 million cases in 2022. Mostly in sub-Saharan Africa. 
- In India: 91% of malaria cases & 99% of deaths reported from North Eastern states.
- Substantial progress in India: 80.53% reduction in morbidity and 78.38% reduction in mortality between 2015 and 2023. API was 0.16 per 1000 population in 2023.

MAJOR EPIDEMIOLOGICAL TYPES OF MALARIA IN INDIA
- 1. Tribal malaria: Accounts for ~50% of P. falciparum cases.
- 2. Rural malaria: Irrigated areas of arid and semiarid plains. An. culicifacies is the main vector. P. vivax predominant during lean periods, P. falciparum during periodic exacerbations.
- 3. Urban malaria: An. stephensi is the vector. Influenced by poor sanitary conditions, unplanned settlements, overhead tanks, fountains.
- 4. Malaria in project areas: Tropical aggregation of labourers brings different strains of parasites. Ecological disturbance creates prolific vector breeding.
- 5. Border malaria: High transmission belts along international/state borders.
- 6. Forest malaria: An. dirus vector. Deforested areas harbour efficient vectors that bite humans and return to the forest, avoiding indoor residual sprays.
- 7. Floods and malaria: Initial flush out of breeding, followed by pools of water creating mosquitogenic conditions. Lag time of 6-8 weeks.

DEFINITIONS
- Malaria control: Reducing disease burden to a level at which it is no longer a public health problem.
- Malaria elimination: Interruption of local mosquito-borne transmission (reduction to zero incidence in a defined area).
- Certification of elimination: Granted by WHO after proven interruption of local transmission for at least 3 consecutive years.
- Malaria eradication: Permanent reduction to zero worldwide incidence. Intervention measures no longer needed.

EPIDEMIOLOGICAL DETERMINANTS

AGENT FACTORS
- Agent: Four species of Plasmodium infect humans: P. vivax, P. falciparum, P. malariae, and P. ovale. 
- In India, ~50% cases are P. falciparum, 4-8% mixed, and the rest P. vivax. P. malariae is <1% (Tumkur and Hassan districts in Karnataka). P. ovale is very rare (mostly tropical Africa).
- Reservoir of infection: Human reservoir (cases and carriers). A person must harbour both sexes of the gametocyte in the blood. Gametocytes must be mature and present in sufficient density (at least 12 per cu.mm).
- Period of communicability: As long as mature, viable gametocytes exist in circulating blood. In vivax, they appear in 4-5 days; in falciparum, 10-12 days after asexual parasites.
- Relapses: P. vivax and P. ovale can relapse >3 years after the first attack due to original sporozoite-induced liver schizonts (hypnozoites) lying dormant. P. falciparum and P. malariae do not have a true relapse (no latent liver phase); recrudescences are due to chronic blood infection.

HOST FACTORS
- Age: Affects all ages. Newborns have considerable resistance to P. falciparum due to high foetal haemoglobin.
- Sex: Males mostly exposed due to outdoor habits.
- Race/Genetic: Sickle cell trait (AS) causes milder illness with falciparum. "Duffy negative" RBCs are resistant to P. vivax.
- Pregnancy: Increases risk. Primigravid women are at greatest risk. May cause intrauterine death, premature labour, abortion.
- Human Habits: Sleeping outdoors, nomadism.
- Immunity: Acquired only after repeated exposure over years. Wanes when people migrate out of endemic regions.

ENVIRONMENTAL FACTORS
- Season: July to November (peak in India).
- Temperature: Optimum for parasite development in vector is 20 to 30 C. Below 16 C, development ceases. Above 30 C is lethal.
- Humidity: Relative humidity of 60% is necessary for mosquitoes to live their normal span. High humidity = more active, voracious feeding.
- Rainfall: Creates breeding opportunities. 
- Altitude: Anophelines are not found above 2000-2500 metres.
- Man-made malaria: Burrow pits, garden pools, irrigation channels, dams, bridges.

VECTORS OF MALARIA (IN INDIA)
- 6 primary vectors: An. culicifacies (rural/peri-urban), An. stephensi (urban/industrial), An. fluviatilis (hilly/forest fringes), An. minimus (foothills of NE states), An. dirus (forest vector in NE), An. epiroticus (Andaman & Nicobar Islands).
- Life span of vector must be at least 10-12 days for parasite to complete sporogony and vector to become infective.

MODE OF TRANSMISSION
- Vector transmission: Bite of infected female anopheline mosquito.
- Direct transmission: Blood transfusion, hypodermic intramuscular/intravenous injections (drug addicts), transplacental (congenital).

INCUBATION PERIOD
- P. falciparum: 12 days (range 9-14)
- P. vivax: 14 days (range 8-17)
- P. ovale: 17 days (range 16-18)
- P. malariae: 28 days (range 18-40)

LIFE CYCLE
- Asexual cycle (Schizogony in Human):
  - 1. Hepatic phase: Sporozoites enter liver cells. P. vivax and P. ovale have dormant forms (hypnozoites). P. falciparum bursts almost simultaneously (no exo-erythrocytic phase).
  - 2. Erythrocytic phase: Merozoites infect RBCs. Pass through trophozoite and schizont stages. Cycle is 48 hrs for Pf, Pv, Po; and 72 hrs for Pm.
  - 3. Gametogeny: Some forms become male/female gametocytes (infective to mosquito).
- Sexual cycle (Sporogony in Mosquito):
  - Gametocytes ingested -> Exflagellation -> Microgametes fertilize macrogamete -> Zygote -> Ookinete -> Oocyst -> Sporozoites migrate to salivary glands.

CLINICAL FEATURES
- Primary fever corresponds to the release of merozoites into the blood.
- 1. Cold stage (15-60 mins): Lassitude, headache, nausea, chilly sensation, rigors. Temp rises to 39-41 C. Pulse rapid.
- 2. Hot stage (2-6 hours): Patient feels burning hot, casts off clothes. Skin hot and dry. Intense headache.
- 3. Sweating stage (2-4 hours): Fever comes down with profuse sweating. Temp drops to normal, skin cool/moist.
- Complications (P. falciparum): Cerebral malaria, acute renal failure, liver damage, blackwater fever, dehydration, collapse.

LABORATORY DIAGNOSIS
- 1. Microscopy: Thick smear (sensitive, 20x more rapidly detects parasites) and Thin smear (for species identification).
- 2. Rapid Diagnostic Test (RDT): Dipstick format detecting circulating parasite antigens.
- 3. Serology: Fluorescent antibody test.

MEASUREMENT OF MALARIA (PARAMETERS)
- Annual Parasite Incidence (API) = (Confirmed cases during one year / Population under surveillance) x 1000.
- Annual Blood Examination Rate (ABER) = (Number of slides examined / Population) x 100.
- Annual Falciparum Incidence (AFI).
- Slide Positivity Rate (SPR) = Percentage of slides positive for any malarial parasite.
- Slide Falciparum Rate (SFR) = Percentage of slides positive for P. falciparum.

VECTOR INDICES
- 1. Human Blood Index: Proportion of freshly-fed female mosquitoes containing human blood.
- 2. Sporozoite Rate: Percentage of female anophelines with sporozoites in salivary glands.
- 3. Mosquito Density: Number of mosquitoes per man-hour-catch.
- 4. Man-Biting Rate: Average incidence of bites per day per person.
- 5. Inoculation Rate: Man-biting rate multiplied by the infective sporozoite rate.

TREATMENT OF UNCOMPLICATED MALARIA
- P. vivax:
  - Chloroquine (CQ): 25 mg/kg body weight divided over 3 days (10mg/kg day 1; 10mg/kg day 2; 5mg/kg day 3).
  - Primaquine (PQ): 0.25 mg/kg daily for 14 days (to prevent relapse/hypnozoites). Contraindicated in G6PD deficiency, infants, and pregnant women.
- P. falciparum:
  - Artemisinin Based Combination Therapy (ACT).
  - North-Eastern States: Artemether (20mg) + Lumefantrine (120mg) for 3 days + Primaquine single dose (0.75 mg/kg) on day 2.
  - Other States: Artesunate (4mg/kg) for 3 days + Sulfadoxine-Pyrimethamine (25mg/1.25mg/kg) on day 1 + Primaquine single dose (0.75 mg/kg) on day 2.
- P. ovale and P. malariae: Treated same as P. vivax and P. falciparum respectively.
- Mixed Infections (Pv + Pf): Full course of ACT + Primaquine 0.25 mg/kg/day for 14 days.
- Pregnancy:
  - 1st Trimester: Quinine salt 10 mg/kg 3 times daily for 7 days (ACT is contraindicated).
  - 2nd and 3rd Trimester: Area-specific ACT.
  - Primaquine is contraindicated in all trimesters.

TREATMENT OF SEVERE MALARIA
- Emergency management.
- Initial parenteral treatment for at least 48 hours with:
  - Quinine (20 mg/kg loading, 10 mg/kg maintenance)
  - OR Artesunate (2.4 mg/kg IV/IM at 0, 12, 24 hrs, then daily)
  - OR Artemether (3.2 mg/kg IM then 1.6 mg/kg daily)
  - OR Arteether (150 mg daily IM for 3 days, adults only).
- Followed by full oral course of area-specific ACT + PQ (for Pf) OR Quinine + Doxycycline/Clindamycin.
- Corticosteroids, IV mannitol, heparin are contraindicated.

CHEMOPROPHYLAXIS
- Recommended for travellers to endemic areas, soldiers, police, labour forces.
- Short-term (<6 weeks): Doxycycline (started day before arrival) or Mefloquine (started 2-3 weeks before departure).
- Prophylactic drugs taken continuously and for 4 weeks after leaving the risk area.

VECTOR CONTROL STRATEGIES
- Anti-adult measures: Indoor Residual Spraying (IRS) using DDT, malathion. Space application (fog/mist), bed nets.
- Anti-larval measures: Oiling, Paris green, Temephos (chemical); Source reduction (drainage, filling); Integrated control.

MALARIA VACCINES
- RTS,S/AS01 and R21/Matrix-M: Pre-erythrocytic vaccines. Prevent P. falciparum infection in children.
- Does not interrupt transmission. No cross-protection for other species.
- Schedule: 4 doses (at 5-17 months age, 3 doses at 1-month intervals, 4th dose 18 months later).

LYMPHATIC FILARIASIS

OVERVIEW OF THE DISEASE
- Caused by nematode worms: Wuchereria bancrofti (90% of cases globally), Brugia malayi, and Brugia timori.
- Transmitted by the bites of infective mosquitoes.
- Chronic manifestations include lymphangitis, lymphadenitis, elephantiasis of genitals, legs and arms, and hydrocele.

PROBLEM STATEMENT
- More than 657 million people live in at-risk areas. 120 million infected. 15 million with lymphoedema, 25 million men with scrotal hydrocele.
- India: Heavy endemic areas in UP, Bihar, Jharkhand, AP, Odisha, TN, Kerala, Gujarat.

EPIDEMIOLOGICAL DETERMINANTS

AGENT FACTORS
- Agent: W. bancrofti, B. malayi, B. timori.
- Microfilariae (Mf) periodicity: Nocturnal periodicity in India (appear in large numbers at night, 10 PM to 2 AM). Biological adaptation to the vector's biting habits.
- Life span: Adult worms live for up to 15 years. Mf live for up to a year or more.

HOST FACTORS
- Man is the definitive host. 
- Age: Infection rates rise up to 20-30 years, then level off. Disease usually appears over 10 years of age.
- Sex: Mf rate higher in men.
- Social factors: Urbanization, industrialization, migration, poor sanitation.

ENVIRONMENTAL FACTORS
- Climate: Max prevalence of Culex quinquefasciatus at 22 to 38 C and 70% humidity.
- Bad drainage, polluted water, cesspools, open ditches favour breeding.

VECTORS
- Mosquito is the intermediate host.
- Bancroftian filariasis: Culex quinquefasciatus (C. fatigans).
- Brugian filariasis: Mansonia annulifers and Mansonia uniformis.

MODE OF TRANSMISSION
- Bite of infected vector mosquito. Parasite is deposited near the puncture site and penetrates the skin to reach the lymphatic system.

INCUBATION PERIOD
- Pre-patent period: Time from inoculation to first appearance of detectable Mf.
- Clinical incubation period: Time from invasion to clinical manifestations (8 to 16 months, or longer).

CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS
- 1. Lymphatic Filariasis:
  - (i) Asymptomatic amicrofilaraemia.
  - (ii) Asymptomatic microfilaraemia: Mf in blood, no symptoms. Important source of infection.
  - (iii) Acute manifestations: Filarial fever, lymphangitis, lymphadenitis, lymphoedema, epididymo-orchitis.
  - (iv) Chronic obstructive lesions: 10-15 years from onset. Fibrosis and obstruction. Hydrocele, elephantiasis (legs, scrotum, arms, penis, vulva, breasts), chyluria.
- 2. Occult / Cryptic Filariasis:
  - Classical manifestations absent, Mf not found in blood. 
  - Result of hypersensitivity reaction to filarial antigens. 
  - Example: Tropical pulmonary eosinophilia.

FILARIA SURVEY
- 1. Mass Blood Survey: Thick film (20 cu.mm blood) collected by deep finger prick between 8:30 PM and 12 midnight. 
- 2. Membrane filter concentration (MFC) method: For detecting low-density microfilaraemia. Venepuncture required.
- 3. DEC Provocation test: 100 mg of Diethylcarbamazine (DEC) given orally. Mf peak in the blood within 15 mins. Blood examined after 1 hour in daytime.
- 4. Clinical survey: For manifestations.
- 5. Entomological survey.

PARAMETERS ASSESSED
- Microfilaria rate: % showing Mf in peripheral blood.
- Filarial endemicity rate: % showing Mf or disease manifestation.
- Microfilarial density: Number of Mf per unit volume.
- Average infestation rate.

PREVENTION AND CONTROL

1. CHEMOTHERAPY
- Diethylcarbamazine (DEC): Safe and effective. Kills Mf, uncertain effect on adults. 
  - Dose for W. bancrofti: 6 mg/kg daily for 12 days (72 mg/kg total).
  - Dose for B. malayi: 3 to 6 mg/kg daily, up to total 36-72 mg/kg.
  - Mass therapy / Selective treatment.
  - DEC-medicated salt: 1-4 g of DEC per kg of common salt for 6 to 9 months.
