- Ivermectin: 150-200 mcg/kg body weight single dose. Macrolide antibiotic.
- Albendazole: 400 mg single dose for adults and children >2 years.
- Global Strategy (MDA): Annual mass drug administration of a 2-drug regimen (Ivermectin or DEC + Albendazole) for 4-6 years.

2. VECTOR CONTROL
- Anti-larval measures: 
  - Chemical: Mosquito larvicidal oil (MLO), Pyrethrum-based emulsifiable larvicide (Pyrosene oil-E), Temephos, Fenthion.
  - Removal of Pistia plant (for Mansonia mosquitoes).
  - Minor environmental measures: filling ditches, drainage.
- Anti-adult measures: Pyrethrum space spray.
- Personal prophylaxis: Mosquito nets.

ZIKA VIRUS DISEASE

OVERVIEW OF THE DISEASE
- Mosquito-borne flavivirus identified in Uganda (1947). 
- Outbreaks in Yap Island (2007) and Brazil (2015).
- Associated with Guillain-Barre Syndrome and Microcephaly in newborns.

EPIDEMIOLOGICAL DETERMINANTS
- Agent: Zika virus (Flavivirus).
- Vector: Aedes mosquitoes (mainly Aedes aegypti).
- Mode of transmission: Bite of infected mosquito, sexual contact, transfusion of blood, and mother-to-child (transplacental or during birth).

INCUBATION PERIOD
- A few days (usually 2 to 14 days).

CLINICAL PICTURE
- Often asymptomatic (3 out of 4 cases).
- Mild symptoms similar to dengue: Fever, skin rashes, conjunctivitis, muscle and joint pain, malaise, headache. Lasts 2-7 days.
- Severe forms/hospitalization are uncommon, fatalities are rare.
- Major complications: Microcephaly (congenital) and Guillain-Barre syndrome.

DIAGNOSIS
- RT-PCR (Polymerase Chain Reaction) and virus isolation from blood samples.
- Serology is not recommended due to cross-reactivity.

PREVENTION AND TREATMENT
- No specific treatment; symptomatic care (rest, fluids, paracetamol).
- No vaccine currently available.
- Prevention relies on vector control (source reduction of Aedes breeding sites) and reducing mosquito-human contact.

subsections.title: Zoonoses (Rabies, Yellow Fever, Japanese Encephalitis, Rickettsial Zoonoses)
subsections.content:
ZOONOSES

DEFINITIONS AND CLASSIFICATION
- Zoonoses are defined as diseases and infections which are naturally transmitted between vertebrate animals and man.
- Classified in terms of their reservoir hosts, whether these are man or lower vertebrate animals:
  - Anthropo-zoo-nosis: Infections transmitted from lower vertebrate to man (e.g., Rabies).
  - Zoo-anthropo-nosis: Infections transmitted from man to lower vertebrate.
  - Amphi-xe-nosis: Infection that may be transmitted in both directions.
- Classification based upon the type of life cycle of the infecting organism:
  - Direct-zoonosis: Transmitted from an infected vertebrate host to a susceptible vertebrate host by direct contact, by contact with a fomite, or by a mechanical vector. The agent undergoes no propagative and developmental changes during transmission. Examples: Rabies, trichinosis, brucellosis.
  - Cyclo-zoonosis: Requires more than one vertebrate host, but no invertebrate host, in order to complete the developmental cycle of the agent. Examples: Taeniasis, echinococcosis.
  - Meta-zoonosis: Transmitted biologically by invertebrate vectors. In the invertebrate, the agent multiplies or develops, and there is always an extrinsic incubation period before transmission to another vertebrate host is possible. Examples: Arbovirus infection, plague, schistosomiasis.
  - Sapro-zoonosis: Have both a vertebrate host and a non-animal developmental site or reservoir (food, soil, plants). Examples: various forms of larva migrans, mycoses.

RABIES

OVERVIEW OF THE DISEASE
- Also known as Hydrophobia.
- An acute, highly fatal viral disease of the central nervous system.
- Caused by Lyssavirus type 1.
- Transmitted to man usually by bites or licks of rabid animals.
- Always fatal.

PROBLEM STATEMENT
- Rabies is an enzootic and epizootic disease of worldwide importance.
- Water appears to be the most effective natural barrier to rabies.
- Australia, Ireland, New Zealand, Japan, Taiwan, etc., are free of the disease.
- In India, Lakshadweep and Andaman & Nicobar islands are free of the disease.
- Although a number of carnivorous and bat species serve as natural reservoirs, rabid dogs are the source of 99% of human infection.
- India reports 20,000 deaths annually.

AGENT FACTORS
- Agent: Lyssavirus type 1. A bullet-shaped neurotropic RNA containing virus belonging to the family Rhabdoviridae.
- Serotypes 2, 3, and 4 are rabies-related but antigenically distinct viruses.
- Contains two distinct major antigens: a glycoprotein (G protein) from the virus membrane (induces virus-neutralizing antibodies) and an internal nucleoprotein antigen.
- Street virus: Virus recovered from naturally occurring cases of rabies. Incubation period is long and variable (20-60 days in dogs).
- Fixed virus: Serial brain-to-brain passage of the street virus in rabbits modifies the virus such that its incubation period is progressively reduced until it becomes constant between 4-6 days. It does not form Negri bodies. Used in the preparation of antirabies vaccine.
- Source of infection: Saliva of rabid animals. Present in the saliva for 3-4 days (occasionally 5-6 days) before the onset of clinical symptoms and during the course of illness till death.

HOST FACTORS
- Age/Sex: Highest incidence in age group 1-24 years. Males are more frequently affected.
- Rabies in man is a dead-end infection.
- Veterinarians, dog handlers, and laboratory staff are at special risk of rabies.

MODES OF TRANSMISSION
- Deep bite or scratch by an infected animal.
- Saliva comes into direct contact with human mucosa or fresh skin wounds.
- Human-to-human transmission by bite is theoretically possible but never confirmed.
- Rarely contracted by inhalation of virus-containing aerosol or via transplantation of an infected organ.

INCUBATION PERIOD
- Highly variable. Commonly 1-3 months following exposure, but may vary from 7 days to many years.
- Depends on the site of the bite, severity of the bite, number of wounds, amount of virus injected, species of the biting animal, clothing, and treatment undertaken.
- Incubation period tends to be shorter in severe exposures and bites on face, head, neck, and upper extremities.

PATHOGENESIS
- Virus replicates in muscle or connective tissue cells at or near the site of introduction before it attaches to nerve endings and enters peripheral nerves.
- Spreads centripetally via the peripheral nerves towards the CNS.
- Following infection of the CNS, the virus spreads centrifugally in peripheral nerves to many tissues, including skeletal and myocardial muscle, adrenal glands, and skin.
- Salivary gland invasion is crucial for transmission to another animal or human.

CLINICAL PICTURE (RABIES IN MAN)
- Prodromal symptoms: Headache, malaise, sore throat, and slight fever lasting for 3-4 days.
- 80% of patients complain of pain or tingling at the site of the bite.
- Aerophobia (fear of air): Fanning a current of air across the face causes violent spasms of the pharyngeal and neck muscles.
- Spasms of the muscles of deglutition on the mere sight or sound of water (Hydrophobia - fear of water) is pathognomonic of rabies.
- Mental changes include fear of death, anger, irritability, and depression.
- Duration of illness is 2 to 3 days, may be prolonged to 5-6 days. Patient dies abruptly during a convulsion or passes into paralysis and coma.

DIAGNOSIS
- Clinical diagnosis based on history of bite and characteristic signs.
- Confirmed early in illness by antigen detection using immunofluorescence of skin biopsy, and by virus isolation from saliva.
- Neutralizing antibodies are not usually detectable in serum or CSF before the eighth day.

TREATMENT
- No specific treatment. Case management is supportive.
- Isolate patient in a quiet room, protected from bright light, noise, or cold draughts.
- Relieve anxiety and pain (Morphia 30-45 mg).
- Muscle relaxants, hydration, diuresis, and respiratory/cardiac support.

PREVENTION: POST-EXPOSURE PROPHYLAXIS (PEP)
- Local treatment of wound:
  - Cleansing: Immediate flushing and washing the wound(s), scratches, and adjoining areas with plenty of soap and water under a running tap for at least 15 minutes.
  - Chemical treatment: Inactivate residual virus with virucidal agents - alcohol (400-700 ml/litre), tincture, or 0.01% aqueous iodine or povidone iodine.
  - Suturing: Bite wounds should not be immediately sutured. If necessary, done 24-48 hours later, applying minimum stitches, under the cover of rabies immunoglobulin locally.
  - Antibiotics and anti-tetanus measures when indicated.

CATEGORIES OF CONTACT AND PEP MEASURES
- Category I: Touching or feeding animals, licks on intact skin.
  - Measures: None.
- Category II: Nibbling of uncovered skin, minor scratches or abrasions without bleeding.
  - Measures: Immediate vaccination and local treatment of the wound.
- Category III: Single or multiple transdermal bites or scratches, licks on broken skin; contamination of mucous membrane with saliva; contacts with bats.
  - Measures: Immediate vaccination and administration of rabies immunoglobulin; local treatment of the wound.

IMMUNIZATION (VACCINES)
- Cell-culture and embryonated egg-based vaccines (CCEEVs) such as human diploid cells, Vero cells, chick embryo cells.
- Must comply with WHO recommended potency of >= 2.5 IU per single IM dose (0.5 ml or 1.0 ml).
- Intramuscular administration:
  - Essen regimen: 5-dose regimen on days 0, 3, 7, 14, and 28 into deltoid (or thigh in children <2 years).
  - Zareb regimen: 4-dose abbreviated multisite regimen (2-1-1). 2 doses on day 0 (one in each deltoid), 1 dose on day 7, 1 dose on day 21.
- Intradermal administration:
  - 2-site intradermal regimen: 0.1 ml at 2 sites (deltoid or thigh) on days 0, 3, 7, and 28. (2+2+2+0+2 schedule).
- Rabies Immunoglobulin (RIG): 
  - Administered only once, preferably at or as soon as possible after initiation of PEP (not beyond the 7th day of vaccine).
  - Human rabies immunoglobulin (HRIG): 20 IU/kg body weight.
  - Equine rabies immunoglobulin (ERIG): 40 IU/kg body weight.
  - Administered into or around the wound site. Remaining volume injected IM at a distant site.
- Pre-exposure prophylaxis (PrEP): For high-risk groups. IM or ID doses on days 0, 7, and 21 or 28.
- Previously vaccinated individuals: 1 dose IM or ID on days 0 and 3. RIG is not indicated.

RABIES IN DOGS
- Incubation period: Ranges from 3-8 weeks (may be 10 days to a year).
- Clinical picture manifests in two forms:
  - Furious rabies (mad-dog syndrome): Change in behaviour, bites without provocation, runs amuck, hoarse voice, excessive salivation and foaming at the mouth, leads to paralytic stage and death.
  - Dumb rabies: Excitative stage is lacking. Predominantly paralytic. Dog withdraws and dies in about 3 days.
- Laboratory Diagnosis:
  - Fluorescent Antibody Test (FRA): Highly reliable, best single test for rapid diagnosis.
  - Microscopic examination: Negri bodies identified in 75-90% of rabid dogs.
  - Mouse inoculation test: 10% emulsion of brain tissue injected intracerebrally into 4 mice. Mice die in 6-8 days.
  - Corneal test: Rabies virus antigen detected in live animals via corneal impressions.
- Control of urban rabies:
  - Prophylactic vaccination: Mass immunization covering 80-90% of dog population. Primary immunization at 3-4 months, boosters regularly.
  - BPL inactivated nervous tissue vaccine (20% suspension of sheep brain). Dose 5 ml for dogs, 3 ml for cats.
  - Modified live virus vaccine (33% chick embryo). Dose 3 ml single injection.
  - Elimination of stray dogs.

YELLOW FEVER

OVERVIEW OF THE DISEASE
- A zoonotic disease caused by an arbovirus.
- Affects principally monkeys and other vertebrates in tropical America and Africa. Transmitted to man by culicine mosquitoes.
- Shares clinical features with other viral haemorrhagic fevers (dengue, Lassa fever) but characterized by more severe hepatic and renal involvement.

PROBLEM STATEMENT
- 47 countries in Africa and Latin America are at risk.
- Disease has never been reported in India, but India is a "receptive" area because the conditions required for transmission (Aedes aegypti, susceptible monkeys) are present.

EPIDEMIOLOGICAL DETERMINANTS
- Agent: Flavivirus fibricus (Group B arbovirus / togavirus family). Shares group-specific antigens with West Nile, dengue. Virus is pantropic. In chick embryo cultures, it loses pathogenic properties but retains antigenicity.
- Reservoir of infection: Forest areas (monkeys and forest mosquitoes). Urban areas (man and Aedes aegypti).
- Period of communicability: Blood of patients is infective during the first 3 to 4 days of illness. Mosquitoes become infective after an extrinsic incubation period of 8 to 12 days and remain infective for life. Transovarian transmission occurs in mosquitoes.
- Host factors: All ages and sexes. Wood cutters and hunters at risk. One attack gives lifelong immunity. Infants of immune mothers have antibodies up to 6 months.
- Environmental factors: Temperature of 24 deg C or over and relative humidity >60% required for virus multiplication in mosquitoes. Urbanization leads to extension.

MODES OF TRANSMISSION
- Sylvatic (jungle) yellow fever: Monkeys -> wild mosquitoes -> man (mainly young men logging in forests).
- Intermediate yellow fever: Semi-domestic mosquitoes infect both monkeys and humans. Small-scale epidemics in Africa.
- Urban yellow fever: Infected people introduce virus into densely populated areas with Aedes mosquitoes -> man to man transmission.

INCUBATION PERIOD
- 3 to 6 days.

CLINICAL FEATURES & TREATMENT
- Severe cases develop jaundice with haemorrhagic manifestations (black vomit, epistaxis, melena), albuminuria, anuria, shock, coma.
- Death occurs between the 5th and 10th day. CFR may reach 80% in severe cases.
- No specific treatment. Supportive care for dehydration and fever.

PREVENTION AND CONTROL
- 1. Vaccination: 17D vaccine (live attenuated, grown in chick embryo, freeze-dried).
  - Stored between +5 and -30 deg C, preferably below zero.
  - Reconstituted with cold physiological saline. Discarded if not used within half an hour.
  - Dose: 0.5 ml subcutaneously.
  - Immunity appears on 7th day and lasts for life.
  - Contraindications: Children under 9 months (or 6 months during epidemic), pregnant women (unless outbreak risk is high), severe egg allergy, immunodeficiency/HIV.
- 2. Vector Control: Anti-adult and anti-larval measures. Aedes aegypti index should not be more than 1% in towns and seaports in endemic areas.
- 3. International Measures (India):
  - All travellers exposed to risk must possess a valid international certificate of vaccination.
  - If no certificate, traveller is placed in quarantine in a mosquito-proof ward for 6 days.
  - Validity of certificate begins 10 days after vaccination. (Notes state up to 10 years; textbook update mentions lifetime validity under IHR amendments of 2016).
  - Aircraft/ships aerosol sprayed on arrival. Airports kept free from breeding within 400 metres.

JAPANESE ENCEPHALITIS (JE)

OVERVIEW OF THE DISEASE
- A mosquito-borne encephalitis caused by a Group B arbovirus (Flavivirus).
- Zoonotic disease infecting mainly animals and incidentally man.
- Leading cause of viral encephalitis in Asia.

PROBLEM STATEMENT
- Endemic in 21 states of India. Assam, Bihar, UP, Karnataka, West Bengal, and Tamil Nadu report outbreaks every year (contributing ~55% of cases and deaths).

EPIDEMIOLOGICAL DETERMINANTS
- Agent: Group B arbovirus (Flavivirus).
- Transmission cycle:
  - Pig -> Mosquito -> Pig
  - Ardeid bird -> Mosquito -> Ardeid bird
  - Man is an incidental "dead-end" host.
- Animal hosts: Pigs are the major vertebrate hosts and "amplifiers" of the virus. Cattle and buffaloes act as "mosquito attractants". Birds (pond herons, cattle egrets) are involved in the natural history.
- Mosquito Vectors: Culicine mosquitoes, notably Culex tritaeniorhynchus, C. vishnui, and C. gelidus. Breed in irrigated rice fields, shallow ditches. Zoophilic. Extrinsic incubation is 9-12 days.

INCUBATION PERIOD
- 5 to 15 days in man.

CLINICAL FEATURES
- Ratio of overt disease to inapparent infection is about 1:250.
- 1. Prodromal stage (1-6 days): Fever, headache, GI disturbances, lethargy.
- 2. Acute encephalitic stage: High fever (38-40.7 C), nuchal rigidity, focal CNS signs, convulsions, speech difficulty, ocular palsies, hemiplegia, altered sensorium -> coma.
- 3. Late stage & sequelae: Temperature normalizes. Residual neurological deficits common. CFR varies between 20-40%.

DIAGNOSIS
- IgM-capture ELISA detecting specific IgM in CSF or blood within 7 days of onset.

PREVENTION AND CONTROL
- 1. Vaccination:
  - Mouse brain-derived (Nakayama or Beijing strain, inactivated).
  - Cell culture-derived inactivated (Beijing P-3 strain).
  - Cell culture-derived live attenuated (SA-14-14-2 strain). Integral part of Universal Immunization Programme in 83 endemic districts in India. Single dose + booster at 1 year interval.
- 2. Vector control: Fogging with malathion or fenitrothion (ULV) within 2-3 km radius of infected villages. Use of mosquito nets.

RICKETTSIAL ZOONOSES

OVERVIEW OF THE DISEASE
- Rickettsial diseases are caused by rickettsial organisms and transmitted to man by arthropod vectors (Q fever excepted).

CLASSIFICATION
- Typhus group:
  - Epidemic typhus (louse-borne): R. prowazekii. Vector: Louse. Reservoir: Humans.
  - Murine typhus (endemic/flea-borne): R. typhi. Vector: Flea. Reservoir: Rodents.
  - Scrub typhus: Orientia tsutsugamushi. Vector: Mite. Reservoir: Rodents.
- Spotted fever group:
  - Rocky Mountain spotted fever: R. rickettsii. Vector: Tick. Reservoir: Rodents, dogs.
  - Fievre boutonneuse, Indian tick typhus: R. conorii. Vector: Tick. Reservoir: Rodents, dogs.
  - Siberian tick typhus: R. sibirica. Vector: Tick. Reservoir: Rodents.
- Transitional group:
  - Rickettsial pox: R. akari. Vector: Mite. Reservoir: Mice.
  - Queensland tick typhus: R. australis. Vector: Tick. Reservoir: Rodents, marsupials.
- Q fever:
  - Coxiella burnetii. Vector: Airborne fomites/tick. Reservoir: Sheep, cattle, goats.

AGENT FACTORS
- Rickettsiae are small, obligate intracellular bacteria. Pleomorphic (short rods/cocci). Stain blue with Giemsa.
- Grow readily in the yolk sac of the embryonated egg.

CLINICAL FEATURES & DIAGNOSIS
- Characterized by fever, headache, malaise, prostration, skin rash, and enlargement of spleen and liver.
- Diagnosis: Isolation, IFA test, complement fixation, Weil Felix reaction, ELISA.
- Treatment: Tetracycline is the drug of choice.

SCRUB TYPHUS
- Agent: Orientia tsutsugamushi.
- Reservoir: Trombiculid mite (transovarial transmission). Larva (chigger) feeds on vertebrate hosts.
- Transmission: Bite of infected larval mites.
- Incubation period: 10 to 12 days (range 6-21 days).
- Clinical: Acute onset chills, fever, headache. Macular rash on 5th day. Punched-out ulcer covered with a blackened scab (eschar) at mite bite site. Weil Felix reaction is strongly positive with Proteus strain OXK.
- Control: Tetracycline. Clearing vegetation, lindane/chlordane to ground. Mite repellents (benzyl benzoate, DEET).

MURINE TYPHUS (ENDEMIC TYPHUS)
- Agent: Rickettsia typhi (R. mooseri).
- Reservoir: Rats (R. rattus, R. norvegicus).
- Transmission: Spread from rat to rat by rat flea (X. cheopis). Transmitted to man by inoculation of infected faeces into skin or inhalation of dried faeces.
- Incubation period: 1 to 2 weeks (commonly 12 days).
- Clinical: Resembles louse-borne typhus but milder. Weil Felix reaction with Proteus OX-19 becomes positive in 2nd week.
- Control: Tetracycline. Rat flea control (residual insecticides like BHC, malathion).

INDIAN TICK TYPHUS
- Agent: Rickettsia conorii.
- Reservoir: Tick (Ixodidae family). Transovarial and trans-stadial passage.
- Transmission: Bite of an infected tick or contamination of skin with crushed tissues/faeces.
- Incubation period: 3 to 7 days.
- Clinical: Lesion or eschar at bite site. Maculopapular rash on 3rd day, appearing first on extremities (ankles, wrists) and moving centripetally.
- Control: Broad-spectrum antibiotics. Daily inspection of body for ticks, disinfection of dogs.

Q FEVER
- Agent: Coxiella burnetii.
- Reservoir: Cattle, sheep, goats, ticks.
- Transmission: Inhalation of infected dust from contaminated soil/urine/faeces, aerosols during parturition, or ingestion of infected milk. No arthropod vector required for man.
- Incubation period: 2 to 3 weeks.
- Clinical: Acute fever, chills, headache. No rash or local lesion. Clinical picture is atypical pneumonia or hepatitis.
- Control: Doxycycline. Pasteurization or boiling of milk.

subsections.title: Surface Infections - Part A (Tetanus, Leprosy)
subsections.content:
TETANUS

OVERVIEW OF THE DISEASE
- An acute disease induced by the exotoxin of Clostridium tetani.
- Clinically characterized by muscular rigidity which persists throughout illness, punctuated by painful paroxysmal spasms of the voluntary muscles.
- Specially affects the masseters (trismus or "lock-jaw"), facial muscles (risus sardonicus), muscles of the back and neck (opisthotonos), and those of the lower limbs and abdomen.
- Mortality tends to be very high, varying from 40 to 80 per cent.

PROBLEM STATEMENT
- Comparatively rare in developed countries but a major problem in developing countries.
- Neonatal tetanus (NT) is a killer disease, second only to measles among the nine target diseases of the EPI. 
- Without high quality treatment, the case-fatality rate of NT can be as high as 80-90%.
- Maternal and neonatal tetanus (MNT) is easily preventable by maternal immunization with tetanus toxoid containing vaccines (TTCV) and aseptic obstetric and postnatal umbilical cord care practices.
- WHO defined elimination of NT as <1 case per 1000 live births in every district.
- India was officially certified as achieving maternal and neonatal tetanus elimination (MNTE) in May 2015.
- Success in India was aided by the National Rural Health Mission (NRHM) and the Janani Shishu Suraksha Karyakram (JSY) conditional cash transfer scheme to encourage institutional deliveries.

EPIDEMIOLOGICAL DETERMINANTS

AGENT FACTORS
- Agent: C. tetani. It is a gram-positive, anaerobic, spore-bearing organism.
- The spores are terminal and give the organism a "drum-stick" appearance.
- Resistance: Spores are highly resistant to boiling, phenol, cresol, and autoclaving for 15 minutes at 120 C. They are best destroyed by steam under pressure at 120 C for 20 minutes or by gamma irradiation.
- Reservoir of infection: The natural habitat is soil and dust. Bacilli are found in the intestine of herbivorous animals (cattle, horses, goats, sheep) and excreted in their faeces. Spores survive for years in nature.
- Exotoxin: Tetanus bacilli produce a soluble exotoxin called tetanospasmin.
- Toxicity: It has astounding lethal toxicity, exceeded only by botulinum toxin. The lethal dose for a 70 kg man is about 0.1 mg.
- Mechanism of action: The toxin acts on 4 areas of the nervous system: 
  - 1. Motor end plates in the skeletal system.
  - 2. Spinal cord.
  - 3. Brain.
  - 4. Sympathetic nervous system.
- Its principal action is to block the inhibition of spinal reflexes.
- Period of communicability: None. It is not transmitted from person to person.

HOST FACTORS
- Age: Commonly 5 to 40 years, which predisposes to all kinds of trauma. Tetanus in the newborn is "neonatal tetanus" (NT).
- NT occurs when the umbilical cord is cut with unclean instruments or the stump is dressed with ashes, soil, or cowdung.
- Sex: Males have a higher incidence due to exposure, but females are exposed to the risk of "puerperal tetanus" during delivery or abortion.
- Occupation: Agricultural workers are at special risk due to contact with soil.
- Rural-urban differences: Incidence is much lower in urban than in rural areas.
- Immunity: No age is immune unless protected by previous immunization. Herd immunity does not protect the individual. The disease does not stimulate protective immunity because the lethal dose of the toxin is too small to be immunogenic. Patients who recover must be actively immunized.

ENVIRONMENTAL FACTORS
- Tetanus is a positive environmental hazard. Depends on man's physical and ecological surroundings (soil, agriculture, animal husbandry) and unhygienic customs.

MODE OF TRANSMISSION
- Infection is acquired by contamination of wounds with tetanus spores.
- Injuries include: trivial pin prick, skin abrasion, puncture wounds, burns, human/animal bites, unsterile surgery, intra-uterine death, bowel surgery, dental extractions, injections, unsterile division of umbilical cord, compound fractures, otitis media, chronic skin ulcers, eye infections, and gangrenous limbs.

INCUBATION PERIOD
- Usually 6 to 10 days.
- May be as short as one day or as long as several months.
- Long incubation is explained by spores lying dormant in the wounds. Incubation is also prolonged by prophylaxis.

TYPES OF TETANUS
- 1. Traumatic: Major and important cause. May result from trivial/unnoticed wounds.
- 2. Puerperal: Follows abortion more frequently than normal labour. Post-abortal uterus is a favourable site.
- 3. Otogenic: Ear may be a rare portal of entry (foreign bodies like infected pencils/matches). Occurs in children and adults.
- 4. Idiopathic: No definite history of injury. Believed to be from microscopic trauma or absorption of toxin from the intestinal tract or inhaled spores.
- 5. Tetanus Neonatorum: Infection of the umbilical stump after birth. First symptom seen around the 7th day. Known as "8th day disease" in Punjab.

PREVENTION AND CONTROL

1. ACTIVE IMMUNIZATION
- Best prevented by active immunization with tetanus toxoid (stimulates antitoxin production).
- Aim is a protective level of antitoxin (approximately 0.01 IU/ml serum) throughout life.
- Combined Vaccines: DPT, Pentavalent (DPT, Hep B, Hib), DT, dT (adult type).
- Monovalent Vaccines: Plain or fluid (formal) toxoid, and Tetanus vaccine adsorbed (PTAP, APT).
- Schedule for Infants: 3 doses of Pentavalent/DPT at 6, 10, 14 weeks. Boosters at 16-24 months, 5-6 years, and a TT/dT booster at 10 years and 16 years.

2. PASSIVE IMMUNIZATION
- Human Tetanus Hyperimmunoglobulin (TIG): Best prophylactic. Dose for all ages is 250 IU. Gives passive protection up to 30 days. No serum sickness.
- Anti-Tetanus Serum (ATS) - Equine: Used if TIG is unavailable. Standard dose is 1500 IU, injected subcutaneously after sensitivity testing. Protection lasts about 7-10 days. High risk of anaphylaxis and serum sickness.

3. ACTIVE AND PASSIVE IMMUNIZATION (SIMULTANEOUS)
- Patient is given 1500 IU of ATS or 250 IU of TIG in one arm, and 0.5 ml of adsorbed tetanus toxoid into the other arm or gluteal region.
- Followed 6 weeks later by another 0.5 ml of toxoid, and a third dose one year later.

4. ANTIBIOTICS
- Intramuscular injection of 1.2 mega units of long-acting benzathine penicillin to kill vegetative forms.
- Penicillin has no effect on tetanus spores.
- If allergic to penicillin: Erythromycin estolate 500 mg 6-hourly by mouth for 7 days.

PREVENTION OF NEONATAL TETANUS
- 1. Clean delivery practices: The "Five Cleans" (Clean hands, clean delivery surface, clean blade for cutting cord, clean tie for the cord, and no application on the cord stump).
- 2. Maternal Immunization: Two doses of tetanus toxoid to unimmunized pregnant women. First as early as possible, second at least a month later and at least 3 weeks before delivery. Previously immunized women require only one booster dose.

PREVENTION OF TETANUS AFTER INJURY
- All wounds must be thoroughly cleaned soon after injury (surgical toilet) to remove foreign bodies, soil, and necrotic tissue to abolish anaerobic conditions.
- Categorization of Immunity:
  - Category A: Has had a complete course of toxoid or a booster dose within the past 5 years.
  - Category B: Has had a complete course of toxoid or a booster dose more than 5 years ago and less than 10 years ago.
  - Category C: Has had a complete course of toxoid or a booster dose more than 10 years ago.
  - Category D: Has not had a complete course of toxoid or immunity status is unknown.
- Treatment based on wound type:
  - Wounds less than 6 hours old, clean, non-penetrating, and with negligible tissue damage:
    - Category A: Nothing more required.
    - Category B: Toxoid 1 dose.
    - Category C: Toxoid 1 dose.
    - Category D: Toxoid complete course.
  - Other wounds:
    - Category A: Nothing more required.
    - Category B: Toxoid 1 dose.
    - Category C: Toxoid 1 dose + Human Tet. Ig.
    - Category D: Toxoid complete course + Human Tet. Ig.

LEPROSY (HANSEN'S DISEASE)

OVERVIEW OF THE DISEASE
- A chronic infectious disease caused by Mycobacterium leprae.
- Affects mainly the peripheral nerves, but also affects the skin, muscles, eyes, bones, testes, and internal organs.
- Spectrum ranges from two polar forms: Lepromatous leprosy (LL) and Tuberculoid leprosy (TT), with borderline and indeterminate forms in between depending on host immune response.

CARDINAL FEATURES
- Clinically characterized by one or more of the following:
  - 1. Hypopigmented or reddish skin patch(es) with definite loss of sensation (earliest sensation lost is usually light touch).
  - 2. Thickened peripheral nerves with loss of sensation and weakness/paralysis of corresponding muscles.
  - 3. Presence of acid-fast bacilli (M. leprae) in the skin or nasal smears.
- Advanced signs: Nodules/lumps (face and ears), plantar ulcers, loss of fingers/toes, nasal depression, foot-drop, claw toes, facies leonina, madarosis (loss of eyebrows/eyelashes), gynaecomastia, and perforated palate.

PROBLEM STATEMENT
- India achieved the goal of leprosy elimination (<1 case per 10,000 population) at the national level in December 2005.
- Global burden: Dropped from 5.2 million cases in 1985 to 165,459 cases at the end of 2020.
- In 2022, 174,087 new cases were detected globally. SEAR accounted for 71.4% of the global burden (India, Brazil, and Indonesia contributed 78.1% of global new cases).
- Multibacillary cases account for ~68.9% of new cases.
- Female cases: 38.9%. Children <15 years: 5.1%.

EPIDEMIOLOGICAL DETERMINANTS

AGENT FACTORS
- Agent: M. leprae. Acid-fast, occurs both intracellularly and extracellularly in clumps or bundles called "globi".
- Affinity for Schwann cells and cells of the reticulo-endothelial system.
- Generation time is very long: 12-14 days for one bacterium to divide into two.
- Cannot be grown in artificial media. Replicates in the 9-banded armadillo and nude mouse foot-pads.
- Phenolic glycolipid (PGL) is a highly specific M. leprae antigen.
- Source of infection: Multibacillary cases (lepromatous and borderline lepromatous). The current view is that all patients with "active leprosy" must be considered infectious.
- Portal of exit: The nose is a major portal of exit. Lepromatous cases harbour millions of bacilli in their nasal mucosa, discharged when sneezing or blowing the nose. Can also exit through ulcerated/broken skin.
- Infectivity: Highly infectious but of low pathogenicity. An infectious patient can be rendered non-infectious by treatment with dapsone for 90 days or rifampicin for 3 weeks (or local rifampicin drops/spray in 8 days).
- Attack rates: Among household contacts of lepromatous cases, 4.4% to 12% are expected to show signs within 5 years.

HOST FACTORS
- Age: Peaks between 20 and 30 years of age.
- Sex: Incidence and prevalence are higher in males. More marked among adults and lepromatous cases.
- Migration: Movement from rural to urban areas makes it an urban problem too.
- Immunity: Cell-mediated immunity (CMI) is responsible for resistance. 
  - In lepromatous leprosy, there is a complete breakdown of CMI (Lepromin test is negative).
  - Anergy in lepromatous leprosy is due to suppression of T cell production of interleukin-2 (IL-2).
  - Strong CMI restricts disease to Pure Neural or Paucibacillary (PB) types.
  - Deficient CMI leads to uncontrolled spread and Multibacillary (MB) type.
- Genetic factors: HLA-linked genes influence the immune response.

ENVIRONMENTAL FACTORS
- Humidity favours survival of M. leprae. Viable in dried nasal secretions for at least 9 days, and in moist soil at room temperature for 46 days.
- Overcrowding and lack of ventilation.

MODE OF TRANSMISSION
- Droplet infection: Aerosols containing M. leprae from the respiratory tract (nasal discharges).
- Contact transmission: Close, direct person-to-person contact, or indirect contact (soil, fomites, contaminated linen).
- Other routes: Insect vectors or tattooing needles cannot be completely ruled out.

INCUBATION PERIOD
- Long incubation period. Average is 3 to 5 years, but can take up to 20 years. Often referred to as the "latent period".

PATHOGENESIS
- Bacilli enter the body (usually respiratory tract) -> migrate to neural tissue -> enter Schwann cells (prefer cooler places like cutaneous nerves & peripheral nerve trunks of limbs and face).
- M. leprae multiplies slowly. Escapes to skin causing lesions.
- Outcome is dictated by the patient's CMI.

CLASSIFICATION OF LEPROSY

1. INDIAN VS MADRID CLASSIFICATION
- Indian: Indeterminate type, Tuberculoid type, Borderline type, Lepromatous type, Pure neuritic type (no skin lesions).
- Madrid: Indeterminate, Tuberculoid (flat/raised), Borderline, Lepromatous.

2. RIDLEY-JOPLING CLASSIFICATION
- Based on an immuno-histological scale: Tuberculoid (TT), Borderline tuberculoid (BT), Mid-borderline (BB), Borderline lepromatous (BL), Lepromatous (LL).

3. CLINICAL CLASSIFICATION FOR CONTROL PROGRAMMES (WHO)
- Paucibacillary (PB) leprosy: 1 to 5 skin lesions, and/or only one nerve involvement. Skin smear negative at all sites.
- Multibacillary (MB) leprosy: 6 and above skin lesions, and/or more than one nerve involvement. Skin smear positive at any site.

DIAGNOSTIC PROCEDURES
- Clinical diagnosis: Based on the 3 cardinal signs (Hypopigmented patch with sensory loss, thickened nerves, AFB positive smear).
- Sensation testing: Use a light ballpoint pen to test for touch sensation on the skin patch. Do not use a pin or cotton wool.
- Nerve examination: Palpate for thickening, tenderness. Assess motor/sensory function. Commonly affected nerves: Ulnar, Median, Radial, Facial, Posterior tibial, Lateral popliteal (common peroneal).
- Bacteriological examination:
  - Slit and scrape method (Skin smears): Taken from active lesions and ear lobes.
  - Bacterial Index (BI): Measures density of living and dead bacilli on Ridley's logarithmic scale (0 to 6+ based on 100 oil-immersion fields). BI = 1+ means 1-10 bacilli in 100 fields; 6+ means >1000 bacilli per field.
  - Morphological Index (MI): Percentage of solid-staining (viable) bacilli among 200 pink-stained bacilli. Indicates drug response and resistance.
  - Solid-fragmented-granular (SFG) percentage.
  - Foot-pad culture in mice: Highly sensitive. Used for detecting drug resistance and evaluating drug potency.
- Histamine test: Detects early peripheral nerve damage (loss of flare response due to nerve destruction).
- Immunological tests:
  - Lepromin test: Injects 0.1 ml lepromin intradermally. 
    - Early (Fernandez) reaction: 48 hours. Indicates cell-mediated immunity to soluble antigens.
    - Late (Mitsuda) reaction: 21 days. Nodule >5mm indicates CMI to bacillary component.
    - Used for prognosis and classification (positive in TT, negative in LL), NOT for diagnosis.
  - Fluorescent Leprosy Antibody Absorption Test (FLA-ABS).
  - Monoclonal antibodies and ELISA (based on PGL antigen).

TREATMENT: MULTIDRUG THERAPY (MDT)
- Rifampicin (RMP): Highly bactericidal. 600 mg monthly. Side effects: red urine, hepatotoxic, shock.
- Dapsone (DDS): Bacteriostatic. 1-2 mg/kg/day. Side effects: haemolytic anaemia, methaemoglobinaemia, DDS syndrome (fever, rash, hepatitis).
- Clofazimine (CLF): Anti-leprosy and anti-inflammatory. Weak bactericidal. Side effects: darkish red coloration of skin and body fluids, severe GI symptoms. (Ethionamide/Protionamide used if unacceptable).

MDT REGIMENS (REVISED WHO)
- Adult Multibacillary (MB) Leprosy (12 months within 18 months):
  - Rifampicin: 600 mg once a month, supervised.
  - Dapsone: 100 mg daily, self-administered.
  - Clofazimine: 300 mg once a month supervised, and 50 mg daily self-administered.
- Adult Paucibacillary (PB) Leprosy (6 months within 9 months):
  - Rifampicin: 600 mg once a month, supervised.
  - Dapsone: 100 mg daily, self-administered.
- Children 10-14 years MB Leprosy:
  - Rifampicin: 450 mg/month supervised.
  - Dapsone: 50 mg/day self.
  - Clofazimine: 150 mg/month supervised + 50 mg every other day self.
- Children 10-14 years PB Leprosy:
  - Rifampicin: 450 mg/month supervised.
  - Dapsone: 50 mg/day self.

TREATMENT DEFINITIONS
- Defaulter: Has not collected treatment for 12 consecutive months.
- Relapse: Appearance of new signs/symptoms after successfully completing an adequate course of MDT.

LEPRA REACTIONS
- Immunologically mediated episodes of acute/subacute inflammation. If untreated, leads to permanent deformities.
- Type 1 (Reversal Reaction):
  - Delayed hypersensitivity.
  - Occurs in borderline cases (BT, BB, BL) due to shift in CMI.
  - Existing lesions become reddish, swollen, warm, painful, tender. Nerves enlarged and painful (neuritis).
  - Treatment: Prednisolone (corticosteroids).
- Type 2 (Erythema Nodosum Leprosum - ENL):
  - Antigen-antibody (Arthus-type) reaction.
  - Occurs in MB cases only (BL and LL).
  - Red, painful, tender subcutaneous nodules appearing in crops. Evanescent (subside in a few days).
  - Systemic symptoms: Fever, joint pain, iridocyclitis, orchitis, proteinuria, lymphadenitis.
  - Treatment: Clofazimine (less potent but effective), analgesics, Prednisolone (for severe cases).

DISABILITY IN LEPROSY
- Primary: Direct result of nerve damage (loss of sensation, paralysis, dryness).
- Secondary: Result of neglected primary disability (ulcers, contractures).
- Disability Grading (WHO):
  - Hands and Feet:
    - Grade 0: Sensation present, muscle power normal.
    - Grade 1: Sensation absent, muscle power normal.
    - Grade 2: Sensation absent, muscle power weak/paralyzed. Visible deformity (ulcer, contracture, claw hand, foot drop).
  - Eyes:
    - Grade 0: Vision normal, no lid gap.
    - Grade 1: N/A for eyes in this scale structure, or grouped with 0.
    - Grade 2: Cannot count fingers at 6 metres (vision loss), gap present (lagophthalmos), red eye, corneal ulcer/opacity.

PREVENTION AND CONTROL STRATEGIES
- Case finding: Contact survey, Group surveys (school children, slums), Mass surveys (in hyperendemic areas, >1/1000 prevalence).
- Chemoprophylaxis: Single Dose Rifampicin (SDR) given as Post-Exposure Prophylaxis (PEP) to contacts.
- Immunoprophylaxis: BCG vaccine provides protective efficacy ranging from 28% to 60%. 
- Rehabilitation: Physical and mental restoration to normal activity. Prevention of dehabilitation.
- Global Leprosy Strategy 2021-2030 ("Towards Zero Leprosy"):
  - Targets for 2030: 120 countries with zero new autochthonous cases; 70% reduction in annual new cases; 90% reduction in rate per million of new cases with Grade-2 disability.
- Key Indicators: Annual New Case Detection Rate (ANCDR), Treatment Completion Rate (TCR >90%), Prevalence Rate (PR <1 per 10,000), Proportion of Grade II disabilities among new cases (<2%).

subsections.title: Surface Infections - Part B (STDs, HIV/AIDS) & 5.6 Emerging/Re-emerging Infections & 5.7 Hospital Acquired Infections
subsections.content:
SEXUALLY TRANSMITTED DISEASES (STDS)

OVERVIEW OF THE DISEASE
- A group of communicable diseases that are transmitted predominantly by sexual contact.
- Caused by a wide range of bacterial, viral, protozoal, and fungal agents, as well as ectoparasites.
- Have undergone a dramatic transformation; the change in name from venereal diseases (VD) to sexually transmitted diseases (STD) indicates this transformation.
- The list of pathogens has expanded from the 5 "classical" venereal diseases (syphilis, gonorrhoea, chancroid, lymphogranuloma venereum, and donovanosis) to include more than 20 agents.
- Attention is now given to clinical syndromes associated with STDs (second generation STDs).

PROBLEM STATEMENT
- STDs have a profound impact on sexual and reproductive health worldwide and rank among the top 5 disease categories for which adults seek health care.
- More than 1 million STIs are acquired every day in the age group 15-49 years worldwide, majority of which are asymptomatic.
- Each year, there are an estimated 374 million new infections with 1 of 4 STIs. During 2022: chlamydia (129 million), gonorrhoea (82 million), syphilis (8 million), and trichomoniasis (156 million).
- More than 500 million people are living with genital HSV (herpes) infections.
- More than 300 million women have an HPV (Human Papilloma Virus) infection.
- An estimated 254 million people are living with chronic hepatitis B globally.
- Consequences: STIs like herpes and syphilis can increase the risk of HIV acquisition three-fold or more. Mother-to-child transmission can result in stillbirth, neonatal death, low-birth-weight, prematurity, sepsis, pneumonia, neonatal conjunctivitis, and congenital deformities. HPV infection causes about 528,000 cases of cervical cancer and 311,000 cervical cancer deaths each year. STIs such as gonorrhoea and Chlamydia are major causes of pelvic inflammatory disease (PID) and infertility in women.
- In India: STDs are becoming a major public health problem. During 2022, about 38,587 cases of syphilis (14,747 males, 23,840 females) and 49 deaths were reported. Gonorrhoea is notoriously lacking in reporting but widely prevalent (30,250 cases reported, 9,989 males and 20,261 females, 10 deaths). 
- Chancroid is fairly widely prevalent in India.
- Lymphogranuloma venereum (LGV) is more prevalent in the southern states (Tamil Nadu, Andhra Pradesh, Maharashtra, Karnataka) than in northern states.
- Donovanosis (granuloma inguinale) is endemic in Tamil Nadu, Andhra Pradesh, Orissa, Karnataka, and Maharashtra, with greater prevalence along coastal areas.

EPIDEMIOLOGICAL DETERMINANTS

AGENT FACTORS
- Bacterial agents: Neisseria gonorrhoeae, Chlamydia trachomatis, Treponema pallidum, Haemophilus ducreyi, Mycoplasma hominis, Ureaplasma urealyticum, Calymmatobacterium granulomatis, Shigella spp., Campylobacter spp., Group B streptococcus, Bacterial vaginosis-associated organisms.
- Viral agents: Human (alpha) herpesvirus 1 or 2 (herpes simplex virus), Human (beta) herpesvirus 5 (cytomegalovirus), Hepatitis virus B, Human papilloma viruses, Molluscum contagiosum virus, Human immunodeficiency virus (HIV).
- Protozoal agents: Entamoeba histolytica, Giardia lamblia, Trichomonas vaginalis.
- Fungal agents: Candida albicans.
- Ectoparasites: Phthirus pubis (Pediculosis), Sarcoptes scabiei.

HOST FACTORS
- Age: Highest rates of incidence are observed in 20-24 year-olds, followed by 25-29 and 15-19 years age groups. 
- Sex: Overall morbidity rate is higher for men than for women, but morbidity caused by infection is generally much more severe in women (e.g., PID).
- Marital status: Frequency of STD infection is higher among single, divorced, and separated persons than among married couples.
- Socio-economic status: Individuals from the lowest socio-economic groups have the highest morbidity rate.

DEMOGRAPHIC FACTORS
- Population explosion, marked increase in the number of young people, and rural-to-urban migration.

SOCIAL AND BEHAVIOURAL FACTORS
- Prostitution: A major factor. The professional prostitute has largely been replaced by the "good-time girl" in developed countries. The male component (prostituant) is equally important.
- Broken homes: Promiscuous women are usually drawn from homes broken either due to death of one/both parents or separation.
- Sexual disharmony: Married people with strained relations, divorced, and separated persons.
- Easy money: Fostered by lack of female employment.
- Emotional immaturity: Often stressed as a social factor.
- Urbanization and industrialization: Long working hours, isolation from family, and mobility foster casual sexual relationships.
- Social disruption: Caused by disasters, wars, and civil unrest.
- International travel: Import/export of infection (e.g., resistant N. gonorrhoeae and HIV).
- Changing behavioural patterns: Values traditionally set on chastity are in conflict with ideas of independence and freedom from supervision.
- Social stigma: Accounts for non-detection, not disclosing contacts, dropping out of treatment, and going to quacks.
- Alcoholism: Indirect effect, may encourage prostitution or boost alcohol sales.

CLINICAL SPECTRUM
- Gonococcal infection: Inflammation of the genital tract involving the urethra in men and women, cervix and rectum in women, and rectum in men who have sex with men. Other sites: throat (pharyngitis) and eyes. Complications: PID, ectopic pregnancy, infertility, chronic pelvic pain, epididymitis, infant eye infection (blindness). Antibiotics: Ciprofloxacin, ceftriaxone, cefixime, spectinomycin.
- Syphilis: Ulceration of the uro-genital tract, mouth, or rectum. Later stages range from skin eruptions to cardiovascular and nervous system complications. Important cause of stillbirth. Antibiotics: Penicillin, doxycycline, erythromycin.
- Chlamydial infection: High percentage of women and men are asymptomatic. Complications similar to gonococcal infection. Antibiotics: Doxycycline, azithromycin, erythromycin, tetracycline, ofloxacin.
- Trichomoniasis: Vaginitis and vaginal discharge. Treatment: Metronidazole or tinidazole.
- Chancroid: Small papule develops at inoculation site (2-3 days), erodes into a deep, extremely painful ulcer. Painful swelling of inguinal lymph nodes (bubo). Antibiotics: Ciprofloxacin, erythromycin, ceftriaxone, azithromycin.
- Lymphogranuloma venereum (LGV): Swelling of lymph nodes in the groin. Small, painless ulcer (3-30 days) that may pass unrecognized. Extensive lymphatic damage causes elephantiasis of genitalia. Antibiotics: Doxycycline, erythromycin, tetracycline.
- Donovanosis: Granuloma inguinale. Small papule (3-40 days incubation) ruptures to form a granulomatous lesion that is characteristically pain-free and bleeds readily. Antibiotics: Azithromycin, doxycycline, erythromycin, tetracycline, TMP-SMX.
- Genital herpes: HSV-2. Papular lesions progressing to multiple blisters and ulcers. Lifelong infection with recurrences. Antivirals: Acyclovir, valacyclovir, famciclovir.
- Human papilloma virus: Ano-genital warts (soft flesh-coloured cauliflowers to flat warts). Cause of cervical cancer. Routine Papanicolaou staining recommended.

SYNDROMIC MANAGEMENT OF STDS
- A scientifically derived approach using flowcharts based on signs and symptoms, without waiting for lab tests. 
- Urethral discharge in males (Gonorrhoea + Chlamydia): 
  - Tab Cefixime 400 mg orally, single dose PLUS Tab Azithromycin 1 gram orally, single dose.
  - If discharge persists after 7 days: Tab Secnidazole 2 gm orally, single dose (to treat T. vaginalis).
  - Treat all recent partners. Advise sexual abstinence, provide condoms. 
  - Pregnant partner: Cefixime 400 mg or Ceftriaxone 125 mg IM PLUS Erythromycin 500 mg QID for 7 days (or Amoxicillin 500 mg TDS for 7 days). Quinolones and doxycycline are contraindicated.
- Vaginal discharge (Vaginitis: T. vaginalis, Candida, Bacterial vaginosis / Cervicitis: N. gonorrhoeae, C. trachomatis):
  - Vaginitis (Trichomonas - greenish frothy, Candida - curdy white, BV - adherent): Tab Secnidazole 2 gm single dose OR Tinidazole 500 mg twice daily for 5 days PLUS Fluconazole 150 mg single dose OR local Clotrimazole 500 mg vaginal pessaries once.
  - Cervicitis (Cervical erosion/ulcer): Tab Cefixime 400 mg single dose PLUS Azithromycin 1 gram single dose.
  - Pregnant women: 1st trimester - Clotrimazole vaginal pessary only (Oral Fluconazole contraindicated). 2nd/3rd trimester - Oral Metronidazole/Secnidazole can be given.
- Lower abdominal pain in females (PID: N. gonorrhoeae, C. trachomatis, Anaerobes):
  - Tab Cefixime 400 mg twice daily for 7 days PLUS Tab Metronidazole 400 mg twice daily for 14 days PLUS Doxycycline 100 mg twice daily for 14 days.
  - Pregnant women: Refer to district hospital for hospitalization and parenteral regimen. Doxycycline is contraindicated.
- Genital ulcers (Syphilis, Chancroid, Granuloma inguinale, LGV, Herpes):
  - Treat for syphilis and chancroid: Inj Benzathine penicillin 2.4 million IU IM after test dose PLUS Azithromycin 1g orally single dose OR Ciprofloxacin 500 mg twice a day for 3 days.
  - If vesicles present: Treat for herpes with Tab Acyclovir 400 mg orally, 3 times a day for 7 days.
  - Pregnant women: Benzathine penicillin. If allergic to penicillin, treat with Erythromycin 500 mg QID for 15 days. Quinolones and doxycycline are contraindicated.
- Scrotal swelling (Gonococcal and chlamydial):
  - Tab Cefixime 400 mg twice daily for 7 days PLUS Cap Doxycycline 100 mg twice daily for 14 days. Supportive therapy (bed rest, scrotal elevation, analgesics).
- Inguinal bubo (LGV and Chancroid):
  - Cap Doxycycline 100 mg twice daily for 21 days PLUS Tab Azithromycin 1 gram single dose (or Ciprofloxacin 500 mg BID for 3 days). A bubo should never be incised and drained; aspirate if fluctuant.
  - Pregnant women: Erythromycin base 500 mg 4 times daily for 21 days.

CONTROL OF STDS
- 1. Initial planning: Problem definition, establishing priorities, setting objectives, considering strategies.
- 2. Intervention strategies:
  - Case detection: Screening (high priority for pregnant women, blood donors, sex workers), Contact tracing, Cluster testing (testing people in the same socio-sexual environment).
  - Case holding and treatment: Ensure complete and adequate treatment.
  - Epidemiological treatment: Administration of full therapeutic dose to persons recently exposed to STD while awaiting lab results.
  - Personal prophylaxis: Mechanical barriers (condoms, diaphragms), Vaccines (Hep B).
  - Health education: Help individuals alter their behaviour.
- 3. Support components:
  - STD clinics: Consultation, investigation, treatment, contact tracing.
  - Laboratory services.
  - Primary health care: Inclusion of PHC workers in the STD "health team".
  - Information system: Clinical notification, lab notification, sentinel/adhoc surveillance.
  - Legislation: To encourage early treatment and control commercial exploitation (e.g., The Immoral Traffic Prevention Act, 1986).
  - Social welfare measures: Rehabilitation of prostitutes, marriage counseling, prohibiting sexually stimulating literature.
- 4. Monitoring and evaluation: Determine appropriateness and effectiveness of strategies.

ACQUIRED IMMUNO DEFICIENCY SYNDROME (AIDS)

OVERVIEW OF THE DISEASE
- Sometimes called "slim disease" in Africa.
- Caused by the human immunodeficiency virus (HIV), a retrovirus.
- Breaks down the body's immune system, leaving the victim vulnerable to life-threatening opportunistic infections, neurological disorders, and unusual malignancies.
- AIDS refers only to the last stage of the HIV infection.

PROBLEM STATEMENT
- Worldwide: 39.9 million people living with HIV in 2023. 1.3 million new infections (reduced by 60% since 1995 peak). 630,000 AIDS-related deaths (reduced by 69% since 2004 peak).
- Median HIV prevalence among adults (15-49) is 0.8% globally.
- Globally, 44% of new infections are among women and girls.
- Key populations are highly affected: MSM (7.7x higher risk), sex workers (3x), injecting drug users (5x), transgender people (9.2x), prisoners (1.3x).
- India: Estimated adult HIV prevalence is 0.27% (as per notes) and 0.20% (as per latest textbook estimates). Male: 0.22%, Female: 0.19%.
- Number of PLHIV in India: 25.44 lakh (2.54 million).
- New HIV infections in India: 68,450 in 2023. AIDS-related deaths: 35,870 in 2023.
- Final Mother-to-Child Transmission (MTCT) rate: 11.75%.
- Epidemic in India is concentrated. High prevalence states include Mizoram, Nagaland, and Southern states.
- India high-risk groups HIV prevalence: IDU (9.9%), Transgender (8.8%), MSM (4.3%), Truckers (2.5%), FSW (2.2%), Migrants (0.99%), ANC attendees (0.29%).

EPIDEMIOLOGICAL DETERMINANTS

AGENT FACTORS
- Agent: First called lymphadenopathy associated virus (LAV). Two types: HIV-1 and HIV-2. HIV-1 is the most common.
- Targets T4 helper lymphocytes. Virus mutates rapidly.
- Readily inactivated by heat, ether, acetone, 20% ethanol, and beta-propiolactone (1:400 dilution). Relatively resistant to ionizing radiation and UV light.
- Reservoir of infection: Cases and carriers. Lifelong infection.
- Source of infection: Greatest concentration in blood, semen, and CSF. Lower concentrations in tears, saliva, breast milk, urine, cervical and vaginal secretions.

HOST FACTORS
- Age: Most cases in sexually active persons aged 20-49 years.
- Sex: High proportion of homosexual/bisexual men globally, but heterosexual transmission dominates in developing regions.
- High-risk groups: Male homosexuals/bisexuals, intravenous drug abusers, transfusion recipients, haemophiliacs, clients of STDs.

MODE OF TRANSMISSION (INDIA)
- Heterosexual: 88.2%
- Parent to child (vertical): 5%
- Injectable drug users: 1.7%
- Homosexual: 1.5%
- Blood and blood products: 1%
- (Not transmitted by mosquitoes, casual contact, sharing food/water).

INCUBATION PERIOD
- From a few months to 10 years or more from infection to development of AIDS.

CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS
- 1. Initial infection: 70% experience mild illness (fever, sore throat, rash). Window period: 2 to 12 weeks before antibodies are produced. Highly infectious but tests negative on standard antibody tests.
- 2. Asymptomatic carrier state: Antibodies present but no overt signs of disease (except persistent generalized lymphadenopathy).
- 3. AIDS-related complex (ARC): Illness without opportunistic infections. Unexplained diarrhoea lasting >1 month, loss of >10% body weight, generalized lymphadenopathy/enlarged spleen, decreased T-helper lymphocytes.
- 4. AIDS: End stage. Appearance of opportunistic infections and cancers.

RELATIONSHIP OF CD4 COUNT TO OPPORTUNISTIC INFECTIONS
- Normal CD4 count: 500-1200 cells/mm3.
- CD4 count 200-500: Tuberculosis, Herpes simplex/zoster, Vaginal candidiasis, Hairy leukoplakia, Kaposi's sarcoma.
- CD4 count 50-200: Pneumocystis pneumonia, Toxoplasmosis, Cryptococcosis, Coccidioidomycosis, Cryptosporidiosis.
- CD4 count Upto 50: Histoplasmosis, CMV retinitis, CNS lymphoma.

WHO CLINICAL STAGING OF HIV DISEASE
- Stage 1: Asymptomatic, Persistent generalized lymphadenopathy.
- Stage 2: Moderate unexplained weight loss (<10%), recurrent respiratory tract infections, herpes zoster, angular cheilitis, recurrent oral ulcerations, papular pruritic eruptions, seborrhoeic dermatitis, fungal nail infection.
- Stage 3: Unexplained severe weight loss (>10%), unexplained chronic diarrhoea (>1 month), unexplained persistent fever (>1 month), unexplained anaemia (<8 g/dl)/neutropenia/thrombocytopenia, persistent oral candidiasis, oral hairy leukoplakia, pulmonary tuberculosis, severe bacterial infections.
- Stage 4: HIV wasting syndrome, Pneumocystis pneumonia, recurrent severe bacterial pneumonia, chronic herpes simplex, oesophageal candidiasis, extrapulmonary tuberculosis, Kaposi sarcoma, CMV disease (retinitis), CNS toxoplasmosis, HIV encephalopathy, disseminated mycosis.

CLINICAL DIAGNOSIS (SURVEILLANCE CASE DEFINITION)
- Major signs: Weight loss >10% body weight, chronic diarrhoea for >1 month, prolonged fever for >1 month.
- Minor signs: Cough for >1 month, generalized pruritic dermatitis, oropharyngeal candidiasis, history of herpes zoster, progressive/disseminated herpes, generalized lymphadenopathy.
- Person has AIDS if at least 2 major signs and 1 minor sign are present, in the absence of other known causes of immunosuppression (or presence of Kaposi sarcoma / cryptococcal meningitis alone is sufficient).

LABORATORY DIAGNOSIS
- Screening tests: ELISA (sensitivity test), Rapid diagnostic tests (point-of-care).
- Confirmatory tests: Western Blot (detects specific antibodies to viral core p24 and envelope gp41).
- Other markers: Absolute CD4 lymphocyte count, CD4 lymphocyte percentage, HIV viral load tests, p24 antigen, beta-2 microglobulin.

PREVENTION
- Education: Avoiding indiscriminate sex, not sharing razors/toothbrushes, avoiding pregnancy if infected, mass media campaigns.
- Combination HIV prevention: 
  - Biomedical/behavioral/structural interventions.
  - ARV drugs for pre-exposure (PrEP) and post-exposure (PEP).
  - Condoms.
  - Needle and syringe programmes.
  - Opioid substitution therapy (methadone/buprenorphine).
  - Voluntary medical male circumcision (VMMC) - reduces risk by approx 60%.
- Prevention of blood-borne transmission: Screening blood for HIV 1 & 2, strict sterilization practices.

TREATMENT (ANTIRETROVIRAL THERAPY - ART)
- ART consists of combining at least three ARV drugs from different classes.
- NRTIs: Abacavir, Didanosine, Emtricitabine, Lamivudine (3TC), Stavudine, Zidovudine (AZT).
- NtRTIs: Tenofovir (TDF).
- NNRTIs: Efavirenz, Etravirine, Nevirapine.
- Protease Inhibitors (PIs): Atazanavir/r, Darunavir/r, Indinavir/r, Lopinavir/r.
- Integrase Inhibitors (INSTIs): Raltegravir, Dolutegravir (DTG).
- Fusion Inhibitors: Enfuvirtide.
- NACO Guidelines (Treat All): ART should be initiated in all adults living with HIV regardless of WHO clinical stage or CD4 count. Priority for stage 3 or 4 and CD4 <350.
- First-line regimen (Adults >10 yrs, >30kg): Tenofovir (300 mg) + Lamivudine (300 mg) + Dolutegravir (50 mg) [TLD regimen] as fixed-dose combination (FDC) once daily.
- Paediatric regimens: Abacavir + Lamivudine + Lopinavir/ritonavir (if <6 yrs and <20kg) or Dolutegravir (if >6 yrs and 20-30kg).

POST-EXPOSURE PROPHYLAXIS (PEP)
- Initiated as soon as possible (preferably within hours) for occupational exposure.
- Adults & adolescents: TDF + 3TC (or FTC) + third drug (like Dolutegravir or Raltegravir) for 28 days.
- <10 years: AZT + 3TC + third drug.

CO-TRIMOXAZOLE PROPHYLAXIS
- Fixed dose combination (Sulphamethoxazole + Trimethoprim).
- Recommended for persons with WHO stage 3 or 4 and/or CD4 count <350 to prevent bacterial, fungal, and protozoan infections.

GLOBAL HEALTH SECTOR STRATEGY ON HIV (95-95-95 TARGETS)
- Reach 95% of all people with TB, 95% of key populations, and achieve 95% treatment success.
- Fast-Track Targets by 2025: 95% know their status, 95% on treatment, 95% virally suppressed.

EMERGING AND RE-EMERGING INFECTIONS

OVERVIEW AND FACTORS RESPONSIBLE
- Emerging diseases: Infectious diseases whose incidence in humans has increased during the last 2 decades or threaten to increase in the near future (e.g., SARS, COVID-19, Nipah, Ebola).
- Re-emerging diseases: Diseases previously easily controlled but now developing antimicrobial resistance or appearing in epidemic form (e.g., MDR-TB, malaria, dengue, diphtheria in Russia).
- Factors responsible: Unplanned urbanization, overcrowding, poor sanitation, inadequate public health structure, increased exposure to disease vectors/reservoirs, microbial genetic mutation, antimicrobial resistance, international travel.

EBOLA VIRUS DISEASE
- Appeared for the first time in Zaire & Sudan in 1976.
- Family: Filoviridae.
- 5 species: Zaire ebolavirus, Reston ebolavirus, Sudan ebolavirus, Tai ebolavirus, Bundibugyo ebolavirus.
- Incubation period: 2 to 21 days.
- Transmission: Direct contact with blood, organs, body secretions of infected animals (chimpanzees, gorillas, fruit bats) or infected symptomatic persons/contaminated needles.
- Clinical features: Sudden onset of fever, intense weakness, muscle pain, headache, sore throat, vomiting, diarrhoea, rash, impaired kidney & liver function, internal and external bleeding.
- CFR: Up to 70%.
- Treatment: No specific treatment, no vaccine widely available initially (supportive care is primary).

HANTA PULMONARY SYNDROME
- First recognized in 1993.
- Carried by rodents, particularly deer mice.
- Characterized by respiratory failure and hemorrhagic fever with renal involvement. CFR >50%.

CORONAVIRUS DISEASE 2019 (COVID-19)
- Agent: SARS-CoV-2 (betacoronavirus, single-stranded RNA virus with a crown-like appearance under electron microscope). Identified in Wuhan, China in Dec 2019. Declared pandemic by WHO in Mar 2020.
- Variants of concern: Omicron (B.1.1.529), BA.1, BA.2, BA.3, BA.4, BA.5. Spreads more easily and quickly.
- Transmission: Direct/indirect contact with mucosal membranes, respiratory droplets, aerosols (airborne), and fomites.
- Incubation period: 2-14 days.
- Clinical features: 
  - Common: Fever, cough, fatigue, shortness of breath, loss of smell/taste, body aches.
  - Progressive disease signs: Oxygen impairment (hypoxia, cyanosis), CNS complications (altered mental status), severe dehydration.
  - Complications: ARDS, sepsis, septic shock, acute kidney injury, multi-organ dysfunction, acute pulmonary embolism.
- Diagnosis: RT-PCR is the gold standard. 
  - CT (Cycle Threshold) score: Number of cycles taken to detect viral load. Lower CT score = higher viral load and higher transmissibility. Range <25 = high viral load; 25-35 = moderate; >35 = negative or very low.
  - Rapid Antigen Test (RDT): High specificity, less sensitive.
- Clinical Management:
  - Mild Disease: SpO2 >94%. Home isolation. Hydration, antipyretics. Do NOT use Lopinavir-ritonavir, Hydroxychloroquine, Ivermectin, Molnupiravir, Favipiravir, Azithromycin, Doxycycline.
  - Moderate Disease: SpO2 90-93%, Respiratory rate >=24/min. Admit to ward. Oxygen support, awake proning. Anti-inflammatory: Dexamethasone 6mg/day or Methylprednisolone. Prophylactic anticoagulation (Enoxaparin). Remdesivir (within 10 days of onset for high-risk progression).
  - Severe Disease: SpO2 <90%, Respiratory rate >30/min. Admit to HDU/ICU. Respiratory support (NIV, HFNC, Intubation). Dexamethasone, Anticoagulation. Consider Tocilizumab if rapidly progressing and CRP/IL-6 is raised.
- Post-COVID conditions: Fatigue, brain fog, chest pain, palpitations, joint pain lasting weeks/months after initial infection.

BLACK FUNGUS INFECTION (MUCORMYCOSIS)
- A rare fungal infection caused by mucormycetes. 
- Risk factors: Uncontrolled pre-existing diabetes, rampant overuse of steroids in COVID-19 management, prolonged ICU stay, broad-spectrum antibiotics.
- Signs & Symptoms: Facial pain, pain over sinuses, decreased sensation over half face, blackish discoloration of skin over nasolabial groove, nasal crusting, periorbital swelling, loosening of teeth.
- Diagnosis: KOH staining and microscopy, histopathology, CT scan of paranasal sinuses/lungs.
- Treatment: Liposomal Amphotericin B (initial dose 5 mg/kg body weight, up to 10 mg/kg if CNS involvement). Step down to oral Posaconazole or Isavuconazole. Surgery required to remove dead/infected tissues.

HOSPITAL ACQUIRED INFECTIONS (NOSOCOMIAL INFECTIONS)

OVERVIEW AND DEFINITION
- Infections acquired during hospital care which were not present or incubating at admission.
- Infections occurring more than 48 hours after admission are usually considered nosocomial.

SITES OF MOST COMMON NOSOCOMIAL INFECTIONS
- 1. Urinary tract (most common, often associated with catheterization)
- 2. Lower respiratory tract (ventilator-associated pneumonia)
- 3. Surgical sites
- 4. Skin & soft tissue
- 5. Bacteraemia/Septicaemia (central line associated)
- 6. ENT / Eye

EPIDEMIOLOGICAL DETERMINANTS
- Sources:
  - Patients (suffering from infectious disease, e.g., B. coli).
  - Staff (doctors, nurses, ward boys - S. aureus, Streptococcus, Salmonella).
  - Environment (hospital dust, linen, bed clothes, basins, door handles, air).
- Routes of spread:
  - Direct contact (hands of nurse or doctor to patient).
  - Droplet infection (coughing or sneezing).
  - Airborne (hospital dust).
  - Fomites.
  - Various hospital procedures (catheterization, intravenous procedures, infected cat gut, dressings).
- Recipients: Severely ill patients, those on corticosteroid therapy, immunocompromised, those in intensive care units, urological/geriatric wards, and special baby care units.

PREVENTIVE MEASURES
- Isolation: Of infectious patients.
- Hospital staff: Those suffering from skin diseases or sore throats should be kept away from patient care.
- Hand washing: Must be thorough. "Clean Care is Safer Care" guidelines. Most important step.
- Dust control: Wet dusting & vacuum cleaning.
- Disinfection: Patient's urine, faeces, sputum must be properly disinfected.
- Control of droplet infection: Face masks, proper bed spacing, preventing overcrowding, adequate ventilation.
- Nursing techniques: Barrier nursing & task nursing.
- Precautions for clothing: Gown (washable), Gloves (inexpensive plastic), Mask (cloth or paper).
- For all patients (Standard Precautions):
  - Use no-touch technique.
  - Wear gloves when in contact with blood, body fluids, mucous membranes, & contaminated items.
  - All sharps should be handled with extreme care.
  - Ensure patient care equipment & linen are either discarded, disinfected, or sterilized.
  - Ensure appropriate waste handling.

SPECIFIC PREVENTION
- Urinary tract infections: Aseptic catheter technique at insertion, limit duration, closed system.
- Surgical site infections: Local skin preparation of patients, clean operating environment, limiting preoperative hospital stay, optimal antibiotic prophylaxis.
- Pneumonia: Influenza vaccination for staff, aseptic intubation/suctioning, elevate head of bed.
- Vascular device infections: Local skin preparation, aseptic technique at insertion, limitation of dressing changes.

title: Non Communicable Diseases
content:
OVERVIEW OF THE CHAPTER
The chapter provides a comprehensive understanding of Non-Communicable Diseases (NCDs), which are currently the leading cause of mortality and morbidity globally and in India. It covers the epidemiological determinants, risk factors, prevention strategies, and national programs for major NCDs including Coronary Heart Disease, Rheumatic Heart Disease, Hypertension, Obesity, Diabetes Mellitus, Cancers, Stroke, Accidents & Injuries, and Blindness. It also details the NPCDCS (now NP-NCD) and the WHO STEPwise approach to surveillance.

DEFINITIONS
Non-Communicable Diseases (NCDs): Impairment of bodily structure and/or function that necessitates a modification of the patient's normal life, and has persisted over an extended period of time. Caused by non-reversible pathological alterations.
Epidemiological Transition Ratio: Defined as the ratio of DALYs caused by CMNNDs (Communicable, Maternal, Neonatal, and Nutritional Diseases) to those caused by NCDs and injuries. A ratio less than one indicates a higher burden of NCDs and injuries.
Coronary Heart Disease (CHD): Impairment of heart function due to inadequate blood flow to the heart compared to its needs, caused by obstructive changes in the coronary circulation.
Hypertension (HTN): A chronic condition distributed continuously as a bell-shaped curve in a population with no real separation between normotension and hypertension, posing a major risk for cardiovascular mortality.
Obesity: Abnormal growth of adipose tissue due to an increase in cell number (Hyperplastic Obesity) or an enlargement of fat cell size (Hypertrophic Obesity).
Diabetes Mellitus (DM): A group of metabolic disorders characterized by hyperglycemia in the absence of treatment, due to defective production or action of insulin.
Cancer: A group of diseases characterized by an abnormal growth of cells and the ability to invade adjacent tissues and distant organs.
Stroke (Apoplexy): Rapidly developed clinical signs of focal disturbance in cerebral function, lasting more than 24 hours or leading to death with no apparent cause other than vascular origin.
Accident: An unexpected, unplanned occurrence which may involve injury.
Blindness (WHO & NPCBVI): Visual acuity of less than 3/60 in the better eye with best possible correction (i.e., unable to count fingers from a distance of 3 meters).

CORE CONCEPTS

1. CORONARY HEART DISEASE (CHD)
- Manifestations: Irregularities of Heart, Angina Pectoris, Myocardial Infarction (MI), Cardiac Failure, Sudden Death (death within 24 hours of appearance of symptoms).
- Pattern in India: CHD in India occurs a decade earlier than in developed countries (peak in 51-60 years). Males are more affected. HTN & DM account for >40% cases of CHD. A large number of cases are seen with heavy smokers.
- Measuring the Burden: Proportional Mortality Ratio, Incidence Rate, Age Specific Death Rates, Prevalence Rate, Case Fatality Rate.
- Risk Factors:
  -  Age, Sex, Family History (most important & most common independent risk factor), Genetic factors, Type A Personality, Ethnicity, Race.
  -  High Blood Pressure, High Salt Intake, High Blood Sugar (Diabetes), High BMI (Obesity) & Sedentary Habits, High Serum Cholesterol & Stress, Alcohol & Smoking, Oral Contraceptive Pills (OCP), Socio-Economic Status (SES).
- Specific Risk Factors for CVD:
  -  Causes 25% of CHD deaths under 65 years. Carbon Monoxide induces atherogenesis. Nicotine stimulates adrenergic drive (raising BP & myocardial O2 demand). Degree of risk is directly related to the number of cigarettes smoked per day.  Risk of death decreases with cessation (after 10 years, risk is similar to a non-smoker). Shows synergistic action with HTN & Hypercholesterolemia.
  -  Accelerates the atherosclerotic process.
  -  >200 mg/dl is associated with high risk of CHD. LDL is most directly associated with CHD. VLDL is associated with premature atherosclerosis & peripheral vascular disease. HDL is protective (>30mg/dl).
  -  >75 gm/dl increases the risk of CHD.
- Prevention & Treatment:
  -  Beta Blockers, Revascularization Procedures like CABG (Coronary Artery Bypass Grafting) and PTCA (Percutaneous Transluminal Coronary Angioplasty).
  -  Prudent Diet (Fat <20-30% of total energy, Saturated Fats <7%, Dietary Cholesterol <200 mg/day, Salt intake <5 gm/day, avoid alcohol, increase complex carbohydrates). Regular physical activity, weight control, smoking cessation.
  -  Blood Pressure is the single most useful test for identifying at-risk individuals. Systolic BP is a better predictor than diastolic BP. Apolipoprotein A-1 (for HDL) & B (for LDL) are better predictors of CHD than serum cholesterol.
- NYHA Classification for Congestive Heart Failure:
  - Class I: Comfortable at rest, Ordinary Exertion is Comfortable, NO limitation in Physical Activity (PA).
  - Class II: Comfortable at rest, Ordinary Exertion causes symptoms, Slight limitation in PA.
  - Class III: Comfortable at rest, Less than ordinary exertion causes symptoms, Marked limitation in PA. (IIIa: NO Dyspnea at rest, IIIb: Dyspnea at rest).
  - Class IV: Symptoms at rest, Unable to carry out PA.
- Intervention Trials for CHD:
  -  Large Sample Study Program for CHD.
  -  Evaluate Risk Factors of CHD (Mnemonic: RRR = R = Risk factor).
  -  To see if Health Education works to prevent CAD (Mnemonic: 3 = E = Education).
  -  To access role of Smoking, Diet & Hypertension on CHD (in US).
  -  To decrease Lipid & Smoking (Mnemonic: oSLo = S & L = Smoking & Lipid).
  -  Multinational monitoring of trends & determinants in CVD.

2. RHEUMATIC HEART DISEASE (RHD)
- Overview: Rheumatic Fever (RF) affects connective tissues in Heart & Joints, initiated by throat infection (Group A Beta Hemolytic Streptococci). RF is NOT a communicable disease, but it results from one (Streptococcal Pharyngitis). Occurs in 1-3% of streptococcal infections and leads to RHD, a crippling disease.
- Problem Statement: India prevalence is 5-7/1000 in 5-15 years age group. Globally, it is the most common cause of heart disease in 5-30 years of age.
- Epidemiological Triad:
  -  Causative factor is Coxsackie Virus B4. Conditioning agent is Group A B Hemolytic Streptococci (Serotype M Type 5 has highest rheumatogenic potential). Carriers can be convalescent, transient, or chronic.
  -  Age 5-15 years. India has a high incidence of Juvenile Mitral Stenosis. Affects both sexes equally (M=F). Streptococcal toxic products & host tissues react antigenically. High-risk groups: School-age children, slum dwellers, closed communities (barracks).
  -  Poverty, overcrowding, poor housing, inadequate health services/expertise, low awareness.
- Clinical Features: Fever >= 38 C. Poly/Monoarthritis. Carditis (most common lesion in children is Mitral Regurgitation; in adults is Mitral Stenosis. Most common ECG finding is 1st Degree AV block). Brain involvement (Sydenham's Chorea). Skin (Erythema Marginatum - seen in conjunction with Carditis, Subcutaneous nodules).
- Diagnosis (JONES CRITERIA):
  -  2 Major OR 1 Major + 2 Minor (plus evidence of preceding GAS infection).
  -  2 Major OR 1 Major + 2 Minor OR 3 Minor (plus evidence of preceding GAS infection).
  -  Polyarthritis (Low risk) / Monoarthritis or Polyarthralgia (High risk); Carditis; Subcutaneous Nodules; Erythema Marginatum; Sydenham's Chorea.
  -  Polyarthralgia (Low risk) / Monoarthralgia (High risk); Fever >= 38.5 C (Low) / >= 38 C (High); ESR >= 60 mm/h or CRP >= 3mg/dl (Low) / ESR >= 30 or CRP >= 3 (High); Prolonged PR Interval.
  -  Low Risk Population = ARF Incidence <= 2/1 Lakh school-aged children or Prevalence <= 1/1000.
- Prevention & Treatment:
  -  To prevent 1st attack. Intramuscular Benzathine Benzyl Penicillin (Single dose: Adults 12 lakh units, Children 6 lakh units). RHD can be prevented by screening school-going children.
  -  To prevent recurrence. 1 IM injection at 3 weeks interval for: 5 years or until 18 yrs (if no carditis); 10 years or until 25 yrs (with carditis but no valvular disease); 10 years or until 40 yrs/lifelong (with carditis & valvular disease).
  -  Oral Penicillin V & G for 10 days (least expensive). For allergy, use Cephalosporins.
- Jai Vigyan Mission: ICMR initiated Jai Vigyan National Mission Mode Science & Technology Project on control of RF/RHD in year 2000.

3. HYPERTENSION (HTN)
- Overview: Accounts for over 200 million DALYs worldwide. Classified as Primary/Essential (90%, causes unknown) and Secondary (10%, due to Tumors of Adrenal Gland, Toxemia of Pregnancy, OCPs).
- Classification (WHO/JNC):
  - Optimal: <120 and <80
  - Normal: <130 and/or <85
  - High Normal: 130-139 and/or 85-89
  - Grade 1: 140-159 and/or 90-99
  - Grade 2: >160 and/or >100
  - HTN Crises: >=180 and/or >110
  - Isolated Systolic: >=140 and <90
  -  
- Rule of Halves: HTN is an iceberg disease. Out of total population, half are normotensive, half are hypertensive. Out of hypertensives, half are undiagnosed. Out of diagnosed, half are untreated. Out of treated, half are inadequately treated, and only half get adequate treatment.
- Risk Factors:
  -  Age (rises with age), Sex (Men higher at adolescence, Women later due to post-menopausal changes), Genetic factors (Children of 2 hypertensive parents have 45% risk), Ethnicity (Higher in Black communities).
  -  Central Obesity, Salt intake >8 gm/day (High incidence in Japan), Saturated Fat, Dietary Fiber (inversely related), Alcohol (raises systolic BP), Physical inactivity, Stress, OCP.
- Prevention:
  -  Goal is to shift the population curve of risk factors towards lower levels (biological normality). Nutrition: Reduce salt <5gm/d, moderate fat, limit alcohol. DASH Diet (Dietary Approach to Stop Hypertension - reduces SBP by 6mm, DBP by 3mm; rich in fruits/veg/low-fat dairy, low in saturated fat). Maintain BMI <25, aerobic PA >30 min/day, behavioral changes.
  -  Detect at-risk subjects. Tracking of Blood Pressure (Screen school-going children 6-18 years; BP tracks from childhood to adult - those having low BP tend to remain low, those with high BP tend to become higher).
  -  Early case detection and treatment (Beta blockers, etc.).

4. OBESITY
- Overview: Abnormal growth of adipose tissue due to an increase in fat cell number (Hyperplastic Obesity) or enlargement of fat cell size (Hypertrophic Obesity). Android Obesity (abdominal fat distribution) is more dangerous than Gynoid Obesity (even and peripheral).
- Problem Statement: 5th leading cause of global death. High prevalence in all age groups except 15-24 years. Higher among females and in urban areas.
- Assessment of Obesity:
  -  Weight (kg) / Height^2 (m^2). Categories: Underweight <18.5, Normal 18.5-24.99, Overweight >=25, Pre-Obese 25-29.99, Obese Class 1 30-34.99, Obese Class 2 35-39.99, Obese Class 3 >=40.
  -  Males <13, Females <11.
  -  Height (cm) / Cube root of Body Weight (kg).
  -  Height (cm) - 100.
  -  Height (cm) - 100 -[(Height(cm) - 150) / 2 (for Women) or 4 (for Men)].
  -  Actual Weight / Desirable Weight (Normal <=1.2, does NOT include height).
  -  Harpenden Skinfold Calipers. 4 sites (Mid-Triceps, Biceps, Subscapular, Suprailiac). Obese when sum of 4 sites is >=40mm in boys, >=50mm in girls. Single best measurement site is Mid Triceps (Boys >=18mm, Girls >=32mm).
  -  Obese if waist >102 cm for Men (India 90), >88 cm for Women (India 80). WHR assesses abdominal fat accumulation (Obese if Men >1, Women >0.85).
  -  Best indicator for CVD. Cutoff is 0.5.
  -  Total Body Water, Potassium, Body Density.
- Determinants: Age (low in 15-24), Sex (Women 45-49), Physical Inactivity, High SES, Energy Dense Foods (Extra 100 Kcal/d = gain 5 kg/year), Psychosocial factors (overeating as symptom of depression), Endocrine (Cushing's), Drugs (OCP, Insulin, B blockers), Familial, Alcohol.
- Hazards: Increased Morbidity (HTN, DM, CHD, OA). Increased Mortality (mainly by CHD). 
- Prevention: Weight control (1 kg fat = 7700 Kcal of energy). Dietary changes (reduce carbs/fats, increase fiber). Appetite suppressing drugs (Orlistat). Surgical (Gastric Bypass).

5. DIABETES MELLITUS (DM)
- Overview: Characterized by Hyperglycemia. Underlying cause is defective production or action of insulin. World Diabetes Day is 14 November.
- Classification:
  -  Seen in <30 years. Circulating insulin is ABSENT. HLA DR-3 & DR-4 associated.
  -  Middle aged & elderly. Resistance to insulin. Often discovered by chance. More common. NOT associated with HLA. Strong genetic components.
  -  Hyperglycemia during pregnancy, increases risk for future Type 2 DM.
  -  Intermediate state. At-risk group.
  -  In obese patients with DM, association of Hyperglycemia, Hyperinsulinemia, Dyslipidemia & HTN which may result in CAD & Stroke. Central obesity is an important determinant.
- Agent Factors (causes of insulin deficiency): Pancreatic disorders, defects in insulin synthesis, viral infections (Rubella, Mumps, Coxsackie Virus B4 triggers B cell destruction), Chemical agents (Alloxan, Streptozotocin, VALCOR, Cyanide from cassava), Autoimmunity.
- Diagnostic Criteria (WHO): Fasting Plasma Glucose >= 126 mg/dL (7.0 mmol/L). 2 Hours Post Load (Venous/Capillary) >= 200 mg/dL (11.1 mmol/L). Random Plasma Glucose >= 200 mg/dL. HbA1c >= 6.5%. 
  - 
- Screening: Urine examination (often absent in milder forms). High-risk screening (Age >40, Family Hx, Obesity, Woman with a newborn > 4.5 kg).
- Prevention: Primary (Normal weight, healthy diet, PA, avoid alcohol/smoking. Lifestyle modification decreases incidence by 35-50%). Secondary (Diet, Metformin, Insulin, Self-Care - patient should take major responsibility).

6. CANCERS
- Overview: Characterized by abnormal growth of cells & ability to invade adjacent tissues. 
  -  Arise from epithelial cells (Mouth, Skin).
  -  Arise from mesodermal cells (Fibrous tissue, Bone).
  -  Arise from immune system cells.
- Prevalence: India has 1 million cancer patients/year. 
  - Most common globally: Males (Lung Ca), Females (Breast Ca). Overall: Breast Ca. Mortality: Lung Ca.
