  - Most common in India: Males (Lip/Oral Cavity), Females (Breast Ca). Overall: Breast Ca. 
- Causes/Risk Factors: Tobacco (Lung, Oral - chewing), Alcohol (Rectal, Liver), Dietary (Smoked fish for Stomach Ca, High Fat for Breast Ca), Occupational (Benzidine for Bladder, Arsenic for Lung), Viruses (Hep B/C for HCC, HPV for Cervix), Parasites (Schistosomiasis for Bladder), Genetics (Mongols - Leukemia).
  -  Due to the use of Hot-Pot in winter.
  -  Young women, early marriage, early coitus, early childbearing, OCP, low SES, multiple sexual partners.
- Prevention:
  -  Control tobacco/alcohol, HPV Vaccines (Cervarix 16/18, Gardasil). 3 doses recommended for 9-25 years. Protects against HPV 16 & 18 (causes 70% of cases). Must be refrigerated, NOT frozen. Control air pollution, treat precancerous lesions.
  -  Change in bowel habits, A sore that doesn't heal, Unusual bleeding, Swelling/Lump, Indigestion, Obvious change in wart/mole, Nagging cough.
  -  Screening. Cervix (Pap smear at CHC, VIA/VILI at PHC). Breast (BSE, Mammography - most sensitive). Lung (Low dose CT, Sputum cytology). 
  -  Freedom from cancer pain is a right of patients.
- Registries: Hospital Based (useful for diagnostic evaluation) vs Population Based (evaluates etiology, incidence, surveillance).
- National Tobacco Control Programme: COTPA 2003 prohibits smoking in public, ads, sale to minors, and mandates pictorial warnings and Tar/Nicotine depiction.

7. STROKE
- Overview: Termed Apoplexy. Rapidly developed clinical signs of focal disturbance in cerebral function, >24 hours or leading to death. (The 24-hour threshold excludes Transient Ischemic Attacks - TIA, which last less than 24 hours & are warning signs).
- Causes: Stenosis, Occlusion, Rupture of arteries. 
  -  Lacunar infarct, Carotid obstruction, Vertebrobasilar obstruction. 
  -  Spontaneous intracerebral, Subarachnoid, Intracranial aneurysm, AVM.
- Symptoms: Sudden weakness/numbness of face/arm/leg (mostly one side), confusion, severe headache, difficulty speaking/seeing/walking.
- Risk Factors: Age (47% globally in >70 yrs, but in India 20% occurs below 40 years - 'Stroke in the Young'). Males > Females. Hx of TIA/Family Hx. Cardiac abnormalities (LVH, AF). DM, Obesity, Smoking, OCP.
- Prevention: Primary (Tobacco cessation, salt reduction). Secondary (Early diagnosis via CT/MRI/Ultrasound; Ischemic treated with IV tPA within 3 hours; Hemorrhagic treated via surgery). Tertiary (Physical/Speech/Occupational therapy).

8. ACCIDENTS AND INJURIES
- Overview: Unexpected & unplanned occurrences. Measurement via Proportional Mortality Rate, Death rate per 1000 vehicles, Abbreviated Injury Scale.
- Types: Road Traffic Accidents (RTA) > Burns > Violence > Suicide > Poisoning > Drowning.
- Road Traffic Accidents (RTA): A person is said to be killed by RTA if he/she dies within 30 days of the accident. 
  -  Human (Age, Sex, Risk taking, Impaired vision), Environmental (Roads, Vehicle, Weather), Precipitating (Emotional tension, Alcohol > 0.05 gm/dl, Speed, Distracted driving).
  -  Haddon Matrix (Most successful theoretical approach to Injury Prevention). Enforcement of laws (Seat Belts, Helmets, Roadside breath testing).
- Burns: Thermal burns (Scalds - liquid, Contact, Flame). 
  -  Extinguish flames (roll on ground/blanket), irrigate with cool running water, wrap in cloth.
  -  DON'T apply paste/oil/turmeric, DON'T apply ice, avoid prolonged cooling (hypothermia), DON'T open blisters.
  -  Burn units should be present at all District Hospitals.
- Snake Bite: Features include Nausea, Ptosis, Swelling, Shock. 
  -  Reassure (70% are non-poisonous), Immobilize, DON'T apply tight ligature, DON'T incise or apply ice, Transport to facility.
  -  Reassure, Immobilize, Go to Hospital, Treat.
  -  Polyvalent IV route (first developed by Albert Calmette).

9. BLINDNESS & VISUAL IMPAIRMENT
- WHO & NPCBVI Definition: Visual Acuity of less than 3/60 in Better Eye with best possible correction (Unable to count fingers from 3 meters).
- Categories: Low Vision (1: <6/18-6/60, 2: <6/60-3/60); Blindness (3: <3/60-1/60, 4: <1/60-Light Perception, 5: No Light Perception).
- Causes: 
  - World: Cataract (48%), Refractive Error, Glaucoma.
  - India: Cataract (66.2%), Refractive Error (19.7%).
  - Cataract is the most common cause. Vitamin A deficiency is the most common easily preventable cause of blindness in children.
- Prevention Programs:
  -  Trachoma & Xerophthalmia mgmt (Tetracycline, Vit A). Done by Village Health Guide/MPW.
  -  Cataract/Glaucoma mgmt (PHC, Dist Hosp, Eye Camps).
  -  Retinal detachment surgery, Corneal grafting (Eye Banks).
- Vision 2020 (Right to Sight): Global initiative to reduce blindness from 75M to 25M by 2020. 
  -  Childhood Blindness, Cataract, Refractive Errors, Trachoma, Onchocerciasis.
  -  Childhood Blindness, Cataract, Refractive Errors, Trachoma, Diabetic Retinopathy, Glaucoma, Corneal Blindness.
- National Programme for Control of Blindness & Visual Impairment (NPCBVI): Started 1976. 100% centrally sponsored. Upgraded Regional Institutes of Ophthalmology (RIOs). PMOA deployed. Target is to reduce blindness prevalence to 0.3%. Includes School Eye Screening and Hospital Retrieval Programme for Donated Eyes.

10. NPCDCS / NP-NCD
- Milestones: National Program for Prevention & Control of Cancer, Diabetes, CVD & Stroke (NPCDCS) launched in 2010. Renamed in May 2023 to National Program for Prevention & Control of Non-Communicable Diseases (NP-NCD).
- Recent Additions: NAFLD (Non-Alcoholic Fatty Liver Disease), CKD (Chronic Kidney Disease), STEMI.
- Management Structure: Nodal Agency is the National Institute of Health & Family Welfare. Setup ranges from State NCD Division down to Subcenters (for Health Promotion & Opportunistic Screening).
- WHO Global Monitoring Targets (2025):
  - 25% relative reduction in premature mortality from NCDs.
  - Halt the rise in DM & Obesity.
  - 10% reduction in insufficient PA.
  - 25% reduction in raised BP.
  - 30% reduction in salt intake.
  - 30% reduction in tobacco use.
  - 10% reduction in alcohol use.
  - 50% reduction in household use of solid fuel.
  - 80% availability of essential medicines.
  - 50% eligible people receiving drug therapy & counseling.

11. STEP's APPROACH
- Purpose: Simple, Standardized & STEPwise method for Surveillance of NCDs (WHO). Focuses on core data of risk factors.
- 3 STEPS:
  - STEP 1: Behavioral measurements (Questionnaire) - Tobacco, Alcohol, Diet, PA, Hx of HTN/DM.
  - STEP 2: Physical measurements - BP, Height, Weight, Waist, Hip.
  - STEP 3: Biochemical measurements - Blood Glucose, Blood Lipids, Urinary Sodium & Creatinine.

KEY POINTS
- Epidemiological Transition Ratio measures the shift in disease burden from communicable to non-communicable diseases. A lower ratio means higher NCD burden.
- Case Fatality Rate (CFR) is the ultimate measure of the killing power or virulence of a disease organism.
- Filter Cigarettes are NOT protective against CHD risk; risk correlates directly to the number of cigarettes smoked per day.
- Kuppuswamy Scale relies heavily on education, occupation of the head of the family, and total per capita income for urban socio-economic status assessment.
- Rule of Halves highlights the massive under-diagnosis and under-treatment of hypertension in communities.
- Obesity's maximum relative risk is attributed to the development of Diabetes Mellitus.
- Cerebral Thrombosis is the most common cause of stroke.
- Haddon Matrix is considered the most successful theoretical approach to Injury Prevention.
- Vision 2020 (Right to Sight) targets 5 diseases globally (5G) but 7 diseases in India (7 Hindustani) due to specific local burdens like Diabetic Retinopathy and Corneal Blindness.
- Screening of Carcinoma Colon yields the highest survival rate improvement among cancer screenings.

FORMULAS AND CALCULATIONS
Obesity Indicators:
- BMI = Weight (kg) / Height^2 (m^2)
- Ponderal Index = Height (cm) / âˆ›(Weight in kg)
- Broca Index = Height (cm) - 100
- Lorentz's Formula = Height (cm) - 100 -[(Height in cm - 150) / 2 (Women) or 4 (Men)]
- Corpulence Index = Actual Weight / Desirable Weight

Mortality Metrics:
- Proportional Mortality Rate = (Deaths from a specific disease / Total deaths) x 100
- Case Fatality Rate = (Total no. of deaths due to specific disease / Total no. of cases due to that specific disease) x 100

MNEMONICS
Intervention Trials for CHD:
- Stanford 3 Community Study: 3 = E = Education
- North Karelia Project: RRR = R = Risk factor evaluation
- Oslo Diet Study: oSLo = Smoking & Lipid

Lethal BMI Thresholds:
- Males < 13 (Testosterone - Males - Thirteen - 13)
- Females < 11 (Estrogen - Females - Eleven - 11)

Vision 2020 Target Diseases:
- Global: 5G (Childhood, Cataract, Refractive Errors, Trachoma, Onchocerciasis)
- India: 7 Hindustani (Childhood, Cataract, Refractive Errors, Trachoma, Diabetic Retinopathy, Glaucoma, Corneal Blindness)

Cancer Early Warning Signs (CAUTION):
- Change in bowel/digestive habits
- A sore that does not get better
- Unexplained weight loss / Unusual bleeding
- Thickening/Lump in breast
- Indigestion
- Obvious change in wart/mole
- Nagging cough/hoarseness

title: Health Programmes in India
description: Comprehensive strategies and interventions launched by the Government of India to control and eradicate major diseases.

subsections.title: National Vector Borne Disease Control Programme (NVBDCP)
subsections.content:
INTRODUCTION

The National Vector Borne Disease Control Programme (NVBDCP) is implemented in the States and Union Territories for the prevention and control of vector-borne diseases. The Directorate of NVBDCP is the nodal agency for planning, policy-making, technical guidance, monitoring, and evaluation under the overall umbrella of the National Health Mission (NHM).

The programme covers the following six vector-borne diseases:
1. Malaria (mosquito)
2. Filariasis (mosquito)
3. Kala-azar (sand flies)
4. Japanese Encephalitis (mosquito)
5. Dengue (mosquito)
6. Chikungunya (mosquito)

STRATEGIES AND INTERVENTIONS

The three-pronged strategy for the prevention and control of vector-borne diseases is as follows:
1. Disease Management
- Early case detection and complete treatment.
- Strengthening of referral services.
- Epidemic preparedness and rapid response.
2. Integrated Vector Management (IVM)
- Measures to control adult mosquitoes using Indoor Residual Sprays (IRS).
- Personal protection using Long-Lasting Insecticidal Nets (LLINs) or Insecticide Treated bed Nets (ITNs).
- Anti-larval measures using chemical, biological, and environmental methods.
- Use of larvivorous fish in water bodies.
3. Supportive Interventions
- Behaviour Change Communication (BCC).
- Public-private partnership.
- Inter-sectoral convergence.
- Capacity building and operational research.

MALARIA CONTROL AND ELIMINATION

KEY MILESTONES
- 1953: Launching of National Malaria Control Programme (NMCP).
- 1958: NMCP changed to National Malaria Eradication Programme (NMEP).
- 1965: Cases reduced to 0.1 million.
- 1970s: Resurgence of malaria (6.46 million cases in 1976).
- 1977: Modified Plan of Operations implemented.
- 1997: World Bank assisted Enhanced Malaria Control Project (EMCP) launched.
- 1999: Renamed to National Anti Malaria Programme (NAMP).
- 2002: Renamed to National Vector Borne Disease Control Programme (NVBDCP).
- 2005: Global Fund assisted Intensified Malaria Control Project (IMCP) launched. Introduction of Rapid Diagnostic Tests (RDT) for P. falciparum cases in 2004-05.
- 2006: Artemisinin-based Combination Therapy (ACT) introduced in chloroquine resistance areas.
- 2009: Introduction of LLINs.
- 2010: New drug policy.
- 2012: Introduction of bivalent RDT.
- 2013: New drug policy.
- 2016: National Framework for Malaria Elimination in India 2016-2030 launched.
- 2017: National Strategic Plan for Malaria Elimination 2017-2022 launched.
- 2023: National Strategic Plan for Malaria Elimination 2023-2027 launched.

ORGANIZATIONAL STRUCTURE
- State level: State Programme Officer (SPO).
- District level: Chief Medical Officer (CMO) or District Health Officer (DHO). A District Vector Borne Disease Control (DVBDC) / DMO assists the CMO/DHO. An Assistant Malaria Officer (AMO) and Malaria Inspector assist him.
- Block level: Medical Officer (MO).
- Village level: Multipurpose Worker (MPW) and Accredited Social Health Activist (ASHA).

DRUG DISTRIBUTION CENTERS AND FEVER TREATMENT DEPOTS
- Located at sub-centres.
- Drug distribution centers dispense anti-malarial tablets as per schedules.
- Fever treatment depots collect the blood slide in addition to the distribution of anti-malarial tablets.

URBAN MALARIA SCHEME
- Launched in 1971 to reduce or interrupt malaria transmission in towns and cities.
- Strategy focuses on vector control by intensive anti-larval measures and drug treatment.
- Uses larvivorous fish and larvicides in water bodies.
- Enacts civic bye-laws to prevent mosquito breeding in domestic and peridomestic areas.
- Presently protecting 131 towns in 19 states and union territories.

NATIONAL FRAMEWORK FOR MALARIA ELIMINATION IN INDIA (2016-2030)
- Launched in February 2016.
- Goals: Eliminate malaria (0 indigenous cases) throughout the entire country by 2030, and maintain malaria-free status.
- Objectives:
  - By 2022: Transmission of malaria interrupted and 0 indigenous cases attained in all States/UTs under Category 1 and 2.
  - By 2024: Incidence of malaria to be reduced to less than 1 case per 1000 population in all States and UTs.
  - By 2027: Transmission of malaria interrupted in all States and UTs of India.
  - By 2030: Malaria to be eliminated throughout the entire country.

PROGRAMME PHASING AND CATEGORY STATUS
- Category 0 (Prevention of re-establishment phase): States/UTs with 0 indigenous cases of malaria.
- Category 1 (Elimination phase): States/UTs with API < 1 and all their districts reporting API < 1 (Initial baseline: 15 States/UTs).
- Category 2 (Pre-elimination phase): States/UTs with API < 1 but some of their districts reporting API >= 1 (Initial baseline: 11 States/UTs).
- Category 3 (Intensified control phase): States/UTs with API >= 1 (Initial baseline: 10 States/UTs).

MILESTONES AND TARGETS
- By the end of 2016: All states and UTs to include malaria elimination in their health policies and planning framework.
- By the end of 2020:
  - All Category 1 states to completely interrupt malaria transmission and achieve zero indigenous cases and deaths due to malaria.
  - All Category 2 states to enter Category 1.
  - 5 Category 3 states to enter Category 2.
  - Remaining Category 3 states to reduce disease burden but continue to remain in Category 3.
  - Reduce malaria burden at the national level by 20 percent.
- By the end of 2022:
  - Category 1 and 2 states to interrupt transmission and achieve zero indigenous cases and deaths.
  - 5 states under Category 3 to enter Category 1.
  - 5 states under Category 3 to enter Category 2.
  - Reduce malaria burden at the national level by 35 percent.
- By the end of 2024:
  - Reduce API to less than 1 case per 1000 population at risk, sustain zero deaths, and establish fully functional surveillance to track.
  - 31 states and UTs to interrupt transmission of malaria and achieve zero indigenous cases and deaths.
  - 5 states under Category 3 to enter into elimination phase.
- By the end of 2027: Transmission of malaria interrupted with no indigenous cases and no deaths due to malaria.
- By the end of 2030: Entire country to sustain status of zero indigenous cases and deaths due to malaria for 3 consecutive years.

SURVEILLANCE AND MONITORING
- Annual Parasite Incidence (API) = (Confirmed cases during 1 year / Population under surveillance) x 1000
- Annual Blood Examination Rate (ABER)
- Slide Positivity Rate (SPR)
- Active Case Detection (ACD): Carried out in rural areas with blood smears collected by MPWs during fortnightly house visits.
- Passive Case Detection (PCD): Done in fever cases reporting by RDTs and blood smear.

SPECIAL STRATEGY FOR P. VIVAX ELIMINATION
- Expanding bivalent RDTs and quality microscopy services.
- Ensuring compliance with the 14 days radical treatment to kill hypnozoites.
- Tackling urban malaria by targeting Anopheles stephensi by anti-larval measures.

DISTRICT AS THE UNIT OF PLANNING AND IMPLEMENTATION
Each district should stratify its PHCs and sub-centres into the following five strata:
1. Zero cases
2. API > 0 to < 1
3. API 1 to < 2
4. API 2 to < 5
5. API >= 5

CATEGORY-SPECIFIC STRATEGIES
- Category 3: Scaling up of existing disease management, screening of all fever cases, strengthening of intersectoral collaboration, one-stop centre or mobile clinics on fixed days in tribal areas, timely referral and treatment of severe malaria cases, maintenance of an optimum level of surveillance, equipping all health institutions with microscopy facilities, RDTs for emergency use, and injectable artemisinin derivatives.
- Category 2: Malaria elimination interventions with focus on elimination surveillance system and initiating elimination phase activities. Elimination investigation and classification of each malaria case and focus.
- Category 1: Interrupting local transmission, mandatory notification of each case, case-based surveillance and complete case management, investigation and classification of all foci of malaria, early detection and treatment of all cases, state and national level malaria elimination database established, effective screening, management and prevention of malaria among migrant populations, effective epidemic forecasting and response system. National level reference laboratory established for confirmation of all positive and a fixed percentage of negative slides, cross-checking, training of trainers, and certification of microscopists.
- Category 0: Detect any re-introduced case, notify immediately all detected cases, determine the underlying causes, apply rapid curative and preventive measures, prevent reintroduction and re-establishment of malaria transmission, and maintain malaria-free status.

MALARIA ECOTYPES AND TRIBAL MALARIA ACTION PLAN
- Presently, malaria burden in the country is highly concentrated in a few forest tribal states and areas.
- North east has specific difficulties in implementation and monitoring due to difficulty of terrain and exophily of vector Anopheles dirus.
- Tribal Malaria Action Plan includes:
  - Strengthening surveillance.
  - Provision of hamlet-wise ASHA.
  - Wherever ASHA is not available, Anganwadi workers, local health care providers, village headmen, and school teachers are trained to diagnose and treat malaria cases.
  - Areas with civic disturbances are provided mobile health services.
  - Involvement of locally available NGOs.
  - Epidemiological tracking of all positive cases.
  - Mass screening of migrants.
  - Social marketing of bed nets.
  - Minor environmental engineering (cleaning of drainage, filling pits, solid waste management) through Village Health Sanitation and Nutrition Committee (VHS&NC).

BEHAVIOUR CHANGE COMMUNICATION (BCC)
- Directed to: Early recognition of signs and symptoms of malaria, early treatment seeking, adherence to treatment, use of ITNs/LLINs, and acceptance of IRS.
- Anti-malaria month campaign: Observed every year in the month of June throughout the country to enhance the level of awareness and encourage community participation.

INTEGRATION WITH OTHER HEALTH PROGRAMMES
- IDSP (Integrated Disease Surveillance Programme): Providing early warning signals on malaria outbreaks.
- Other vector-borne diseases programme: Dengue and malaria control activities overlap in many urban areas.
- RCH (Reproductive and Child Health): ANC services utilized for the distribution of LLINs. JSY (Janani Suraksha Yojna) makes provision of bed net distribution to pregnant women.

MAJOR EXTERNALLY SUPPORTED PROJECTS
- The Global Fund supported Intensified Malaria Control Project (IMCP).
- The World Bank supported project on malaria control and kala-azar elimination.

FILARIASIS CONTROL AND ELIMINATION

KEY MILESTONES
- 1955: National Filaria Control Programme launched.
- 1978: Merged with the urban malaria scheme.

ELIMINATION CRITERIA
- Filaria Elimination is defined as when the number of Filaria carriers is less than 1 percent and children born are free from circulating antigenaemia.

IMPLEMENTATION STRUCTURE
- Implemented through Filaria Control Units, Filaria Clinics, and the Primary Health Care system.

STRATEGIES
1. Annual Mass Drug Administration (MDA):
  - Single dose for >= 5 years (except pregnant women and children < 2 years).
  - MDA starts in the month of November.
  - 2004: Single dose of DEC tablets along with home-based care and hydrocele operation.
  - 2007: DEC + Albendazole combination.
  - Recently, Ivermectin + DEC + Albendazole (IDA) is administered.
  - Microfilaria survey is done through night blood survey before MDA.
2. Morbidity Management:
  - Management of lymphoedema cases and hydrocele operations at CHC, District hospitals, and Medical colleges.
  - 2004: Line listing of lymphoedema and hydrocele cases by door-to-door survey in Filaria endemic districts.

KALA-AZAR ELIMINATION

CURRENT STATUS
- Endemic in districts of Bihar, Jharkhand, West Bengal, and UP.
- Incidence of 7277 cases reported in 2015.

STRATEGIES
- Case detection and complete treatment through rK39 rapid diagnostic test kits and oral drug Miltefosine.
- Vector control: Replacement of DDT with synthetic pyrethroid.
- Behaviour Change Communication (BCC).
- Intersectoral convergence.
- Capacity building.
- Monitoring and evaluation.

ACTIVE CASE SEARCH
- During Kala-azar fortnight, peripheral health workers make door-to-door searches and refer cases.
- Incentive amount of Rs 300 is provided to ASHA for identifying a case.
- Rs 100 for ensuring 1 round and Rs 200 for 2 rounds of insecticide spraying.
- Rs 500 is given to the patient treated.
- Rs 2000 is given for Post Kala-azar Dermal Leishmaniasis (PKDL) treatment.

NEW STRATEGY
- Rapid diagnostic kit.
- Single dose of liposomal amphotericin B given I/V at 10 mg/kg body weight.

JAPANESE ENCEPHALITIS (JE)

STRATEGIES
- Surveillance through sentinel sites.
- Integrated vector control.
- As JE vectors are outdoor resters, Indoor Residual Spray (IRS) is not effective. Malathion is used for outdoor fogging.
- No specific cure exists for the disease.
- JE vaccination is recommended for children between 1 to 15 years of age.
- Emphasis is given on keeping pigs away from human dwellings.

DENGUE AND DENGUE HAEMORRHAGIC FEVER

DIAGNOSIS
- IgM capture ELISA test kits: Detect cases after the 5th day of infection.
- ELISA based NS1 kits: Detect cases from the first day of infection.

STEPS FOR PREVENTION AND CONTROL
1. Surveillance
2. Case management
3. Vector management
4. Outbreak response
5. Capacity building
6. Behaviour Change Communication (BCC)
7. Intersectoral coordination
8. Monitoring and supervision

CHIKUNGUNYA FEVER

STRATEGIES
- Since the same vector (Aedes mosquito) is involved in the transmission of Dengue and Chikungunya, the strategies for prevention and control are also the same.

subsections.title: National Leprosy Eradication Programme (NLEP)
subsections.content:
INTRODUCTION AND KEY MILESTONES

- 1955: National Leprosy Control Programme (NLCP) launched focusing on monotherapy.
- 1982: Introduction of Multi Drug Therapy (MDT).
- 1983: Upgraded and renamed as National Leprosy Eradication Programme (NLEP).
- December 2005: Case load came down to less than 1 per 10,000 population at the national level, achieving the goal of leprosy elimination.
- 34 states / UTs achieved the status of leprosy elimination.
- Historically, only 2 states / UTs viz Chhattisgarh and Dadra & Nagar Haveli were yet to achieve elimination during that evaluation phase.

PROGRAMME COMPONENTS

- Decentralized services.
- Capacity building.
- Education and communication.
- Prevention of disability and medical rehabilitation.
- Monitoring and supervision.

MAJOR INITIATIVES

- More focus is given to new case detection as the New Case Detection Rate is the main indicator for programme monitoring.
- Treatment completion rate is an important indicator which should be calculated on a yearly basis.
- Support and Aid provided:
  - Dressing material, medicine and ulcer kits.
  - Microcellular rubber (MCR) footwear.
  - Reconstructive surgery (RCS).
  - Rs 12,000 as welfare allowance to each patient undergoing reconstructive surgery (updated from earlier Rs 8,000).
  - Rs 5,000 to government institutions/PMR centers doing reconstructive surgery per patient.
- There are 612 self-settled colonies in the country where around 50,000 leprosy-affected persons reside.
- IEC campaign with the theme "Towards Leprosy Free India" and the "Sparsh Leprosy Awareness Campaign" launched in 2017.

ROLE AND INCENTIVES OF ASHA

ASHA workers are heavily involved in bringing out suspected leprosy cases for diagnosis, treatment at PHC, and follow-up for treatment completion. They are paid incentives as follows:
- For each confirmed case brought by them: Rs 250.
- On completion of the full course of treatment of the case within the specified time:
  - Paucibacillary (PB) case: Rs 400.
  - Multibacillary (MB) case: Rs 600.
- ASHA incentive for early case detection (before onset of any visible deformity): Rs 250.
- ASHA incentive for new case detection with visible deformity in hands, feet or eye: Rs 200.
- ASHA Based Surveillance for Leprosy Suspects (ABSULS) was introduced in 2017 to improve early detection.

DISABILITY PREVENTION AND MEDICAL REHABILITATION (DPMR)

Main activities include the implementation of DPMR (Treatment, reconstructive surgery, footwear), integrating services by utilizing services of various different ministries, and maintaining a robust referral system.

3 Tier System for DPMR Activities:
- Primary level: PHC, CHC.
- Secondary level: District Hospital, District Nucleus Unit.
- Tertiary level: ICMR institute JALMA, all PMR institutes and departments of medical colleges.

Referral Pathway for DPMR:
- Sub-Centre: Provides self-care advice, advises RCS cases, and monitors. Refers complicated reactions or disabilities to higher centers.
- PHC: Manages reactions if possible or refers, identifies/refers patients needing RCS, identifies patients needing footwear, advises on self-care. Refers lepra reactions difficult to manage, complicated ulcers, eye problems, and follow-up of RCS to the district level.
- District Hospital: Manages complicated ulcers and lepra reactions. Refers difficult ulcer cases to reconstructive surgery centres.
- District Nucleus (DLC): Manages lepra reactions and supplies footwear. Refers patients for reconstructive surgeries and manages the follow-up of RCS.
- Reconstructive Surgery Centre: Implements reconstructive surgery, handles post-surgery follow-up, and supplies footwear to the district nucleus.

URBAN LEPROSY CONTROL PROGRAMME

- Assistance is provided to urban areas having a population size of more than 1 lakh.
- Urban areas are grouped into 4 categories:
  1. Township
  2. Medium cities I
  3. Medium cities II
  4. Mega cities
- Special action plan targets about 524 urban localities identified with a population of more than 100,000.
- Divisions based on population scale:
  - Town and cities (432): 1 to 5 lacs.
  - Medium cities (53): 5 lacs to 1 million.
  - Mega city (34): 1 to 4.5 million.
  - Areas with population > 4.5 million (5).
- Key Activities in Urban Areas:
  - Identify human resources available with government, NGOs, and private medical practitioners.
  - Examination of all household contacts.
  - Identify one referral centre in each urban location for diagnosis and management.
  - Mobile health clinics to visit slums, peri-urban villages, and migrant populations.
  - Develop a system of record keeping and reporting.
  - Organize IEC activities, exhibitions, and quiz competitions for awareness to reduce stigma.

PROGRAMME IMPLEMENTATION PLAN (12TH PLAN PERIOD 2013-2017)

Objectives:
- Elimination of leprosy i.e., prevalence of less than 1 case per 10,000 population in all districts of the country.
- Strengthen disability prevention and medical rehabilitation of persons affected by leprosy.
- Reduction in the level of stigma associated with leprosy.

Targets Achieved:
- Prevalence rate < 1 / 10,000: 100 percent.
- Annual new case detection rate: 100 percent.
- Cure rate Multibacillary leprosy case: Observed 83.9 percent (2019).
- Cure rate Paucibacillary case: > 97 percent.
- Grade II disability rate in percentage of new cases: 35 percent reduction.
- Stigma reduction: 50 percent reduction.

Strategies:
- Integrated leprosy services through general health care system.
- Early detection and complete treatment of new leprosy cases.
- Carrying out new household contact survey.
- Involvement of ASHA in case detection and treatment.
- Strengthening of DPMR services.
- IEC activities to improve self-reporting and reduction of stigma.
- Intensive monitoring and supervision at CHC.

CASE DETECTION AND MANAGEMENT

- Detection of new cases at an early stage is the only solution to cut down transmission.
- Improve access to services.
- Organize skin camps for detecting leprosy.
- Contact survey to identify the source in the neighborhood.
- Increase awareness through ANM, AWW, ASHA, and other health workers.
- District Leprosy Cell: A team of dedicated workers including a medical officer and para-medical officers at the district level to provide technical support to the health care system and to strengthen the quality of health services.

LEPROSY CASE DETECTION CAMPAIGN (LCDC)

- Introduced for high endemic districts by the Central Leprosy Division.
- Training to all health functionaries is given to suspect leprosy patients through physical examination.
- Components include campaign-specific IEC and house-to-house case search activities.
- Publicity of LCDC is done for 5 days (2 days prior to starting the activity and for 3 days after starting the house-to-house case search).
- Key IEC Messages:
  - If you have a light-coloured patch, redness, loss of sensation, swelling, or nodule over the skin, it can be leprosy.
  - Leprosy can happen to anyone; however, it is completely curable.
  - Disability can be prevented if reported and treated early.
  - Treatment is available free of cost.

SURVEY EDUCATION AND TREATMENT (SET) SCHEME AND NGOs

- NGOs are involved in disability prevention and ulcer care, IEC, referral of suspected cases, referral for reconstructive surgeries (RCS), research, and rehabilitation.
- Information, Education, and Communication (IEC) efforts focus on making the public aware of the availability of MDT, correction of deformity through surgery, and communicating that leprosy-affected persons can live a normal life.
- ILEP Agencies: Include organizations like Leprosy Mission and Lepra India.
- Presently, 54 NGOs (updated data) are getting grant-in-aid from the Government of India under the SET scheme to serve in remote, inaccessible areas, urban slums, and industrial/labour areas.

NATIONAL STRATEGIC PLAN AND ROADMAP FOR LEPROSY (2023-2027)

To accelerate the decline of TB and eliminate Leprosy, a strategic plan was formulated aiming at zero child cases for consecutive years. The strategic pillars are:
- Pillar 1: Strengthen leadership, commitment, and partnerships.
- Pillar 2: Accelerate Case Detection (including mobile apps for self-examination and high-intensity awareness campaigns).
- Pillar 3: Provide Quality Services.
- Pillar 4: Prevention of Disease, Disabilities, Stigma, Discrimination, and Violation of Human Rights (including post-exposure prophylaxis - PEP).
- Pillar 5: Develop Digital Surveillance Systems for NLEP (like Nikusth 2.0).
updatedSegments: - Cure rate Multibacillary leprosy case: Observed 83.9 percent (2019).

subsections.title: National Tuberculosis Elimination Programme (NTEP / formerly RNTCP)
subsections.content:
INTRODUCTION AND HISTORY

The National Tuberculosis Programme (NTP) has been in operation since 1962. However, due to unacceptably low treatment success rates and high default rates, the Government of India revitalized the programme with the assistance of international agencies. The Revised National Tuberculosis Control Programme (RNTCP) was formulated in 1993, adopting the internationally recommended Directly Observed Treatment Short-course (DOTS) strategy. RNTCP was renamed as the National Tuberculosis Elimination Programme (NTEP) in 2020.

In view of the World Health Assembly's "End TB Strategy" (2014) which aims for zero deaths, disease, and suffering due to TB, the programme was renamed from RNTCP to the National Tuberculosis Elimination Programme (NTEP) on January 1, 2020.

GOALS AND OBJECTIVES

Initial RNTCP Objectives:
- Achievement of at least 85 percent cure rate of infectious cases of tuberculosis through DOTS involving peripheral health functionaries.
- Augmentation of case finding activities through quality sputum microscopy to detect at least 70 percent of estimated cases.

National Strategic Plan (NSP) 2012-2017 Objectives:
- Early detection of at least 90 percent of estimated TB cases.
- Target successful treatment of at least 85 percent of new TB patients and at least 85 percent of previously treated patients.
- Reduction in default rate to less than 5 percent.

National Strategic Plan (NSP) 2017-2025 (Concluded):
- 80 percent reduction in TB incidence (i.e., reduction from 217 per 100,000 population in 2015 to 44 per 100,000 population) by 2025.
- 90 percent reduction in TB mortality (i.e., reduction from 32 per lakh to 3 per lakh) by 2025.
- 0 percent of patients having catastrophic expenditure due to TB.

STRATEGIES AND INTERVENTIONS

DOTS Strategy Components:
- Political will and administrative commitment.
- Quality assured sputum smear microscopy for early and reliable diagnosis.
- Quality assured short course chemotherapy drugs.
- Direct observed treatment (DOT).
- Systematic monitoring and accountability.

End TB Strategy (2014):
- Pursuing quality DOTS (expansion and enhancement).
- Addressing TB/HIV and MDR-TB.
- Health system strengthening.
- Engaging all care providers.
- Empowering patients and communities.
- Enabling and promoting research (diagnosis, treatment, vaccine).

National Strategic Plan (2017-2025) Pillars - DTPB:
- Detect: Find all drug-sensitive and drug-resistant TB cases with an emphasis on reaching TB patients seeking care from private providers.
- Treat: Initiate and sustain all patients on appropriate anti-TB treatment wherever they seek care, with patient-friendly systems.
- Prevent: Prevent the emergence of TB in susceptible populations.
- Build: Build and strengthen enabling policies, empowered institutions, and additional human resources.

ORGANIZATIONAL STRUCTURE

The NTEP structure comprises five levels:
1. National Level
- Central TB Division (CTD) manages the National TB Control Programme.
- Nodal arm for financial and administrative control under the Ministry of Health and Family Welfare.
2. State Level
- State TB Office / State TB Cell managed by a full-time State Tuberculosis Officer (STO).
- State TB Training and Demonstration Centre (STDC) with training, supervision, and monitoring units.
- State Drug Store (SDS) for each 5 crore of population ensuring uninterrupted supply of 1st and 2nd line anti-TB medicines.
3. District Level
- District TB Centre (DTC) serves as the nodal point for TB control activities.
- Managed by a full-time District Tuberculosis Officer (DTO).
4. Sub-District Level (Tuberculosis Unit - TU)
- 1 TU per 1.5 to 2.5 lakh population (or 0.75 to 1.25 lakh in hilly/tribal/difficult areas).
- Consists of a designated Medical Officer - Tuberculosis Control (MO-TC), Senior Treatment Supervisor (STS), and Senior TB Laboratory Supervisor (STLS).
5. Peripheral Health Institutions (PHIs)
- 3 Designated Microscopy Centres (DMC) per TU, with 1 DMC per 0.5 to 1 lakh population.
- Includes PHCs, CHCs, dispensaries, and DOTS providers (MPWs, Dais, Anganwadi workers, etc.).

TB LABORATORY NETWORK AND DIAGNOSTICS

Laboratory Network:
- National Reference Laboratories (NRLs): 6 designated centers including National Tuberculosis Institute (NTI) Bengaluru, National Institute for Research in Tuberculosis (NIRT) Chennai, National Institute of Tuberculosis and Respiratory Diseases (NITRD) Delhi, JALMA Institute Agra, Regional Medical Research Centre Bhubaneswar, and Bhopal Memorial Hospital & Research Centre (BMHRC).
- Intermediate Reference Laboratories (IRL): State level nodal laboratories for culture and Drug Susceptibility Testing (DST).
- C&DST Laboratories: Located in medical colleges and private sectors.
- CBNAAT Sites: Established at DTCs, TB units, and medical colleges for rapid molecular diagnostics.

Diagnostics Modalities:
1. Smear Microscopy for Acid Fast Bacilli:
  - Ziehl-Neelsen stains.
  - Fluorescence stains.
2. Culture:
  - Solid: Lowenstein Jansen (LJ) Media.
  - Liquid: Middle Brook Media (MGIT).
3. Rapid Diagnostic Molecular Test:
  - Conventional PCR based Line Probe Assay (LPA).
  - Real-time PCR based Nucleic Acid Amplification Test (NAAT / CBNAAT / TrueNat).
4. Radiography (Chest X-Ray).
5. Tuberculin Skin Test.

DIAGNOSTIC ALGORITHM FOR PULMONARY TB IN NTEP

- Step 1: Identify presumptive TB patient.
- Step 2: Conduct Smear Examination and Chest X-Ray (CXR).
- Step 3: Based on results:
  - Smear positive and CXR suggestive of TB: Proceed to NAAT.
  - Smear positive, but CXR not suggestive of TB: Proceed to NAAT.
  - Smear negative, but CXR suggestive of TB: Proceed to NAAT.
  - Smear negative or not available, and CXR not suggestive or not available: High clinical suspicion requires alternative diagnosis or referral.
- Step 4: Conduct CBNAAT (Cartridge Based Nucleic Acid Amplification Test):
  - If MTB detected: Check Rifampicin (Rif) status.
    - Rif sensitive: Microbiologically confirmed TB. Start first-line treatment.
    - Rif indeterminate: Repeat CBNAAT on 2nd sample. If still indeterminate, collect fresh sample for liquid culture/LPA.
    - Rif resistant: Refer for management of Rif resistance.
  - If MTB not detected or CBNAAT result not available: Consider alternate diagnosis and refer to specialist, or classify as Clinically diagnosed TB.

NEWER INITIATIVES AND DRUG RESISTANT TB MANAGEMENT

- NIKSHAY: A case-based web-based IT system for TB surveillance developed by Central TB Division and National Informatics Centre (launched June 2012).
- TB Notification: From May 2012, it is mandatory for all healthcare providers (including private) to notify every TB case to local authorities (DTO/DHO/CMHO).
- Ban on TB Serology: Currently available serological tests have poor specificity and should not be used for diagnosis. Their import, manufacturing, sale, and distribution are banned by the Government of India since June 7, 2012.
- Daily Regimen and FDCs: Transitioned to daily dosing regimens using child-friendly fixed dosage combinations (FDCs) across weight bands.
- Universal Drug Susceptibility Testing (DST): Expansion of rapid molecular diagnostics to cover all districts for decentralized diagnosis of drug-resistant TB.
- Shorter Regimens and Newer Drugs: Introduction of shorter MDR-TB regimens and newer drugs like Bedaquiline and Delamanid.
- Active Case Finding: Campaign mode screening among high-risk populations (slums, tribal populations, prisons, etc.).
- Nikshay Poshan Yojana: Direct Benefit Transfer (DBT) scheme providing financial support for nutritional needs of TB patients.
- Drug-Resistant Tuberculosis Centres (DRTBC): Decentralized to Nodal DRTB Centres (NDRTBC) and District DRTB Centres (DDRTBC) to manage RR-TB and MDR-TB cases through a "test and treat" approach.

TB-HIV COORDINATION

- Intensified TB case finding implemented nationwide at all HIV testing centres (ICTC).
- HIV testing of TB patients is now routine through Provider Initiated Testing and Counselling (PITC).
- Policy decisions taken by the National Technical Working Group (NTWG) on TB/HIV collaborative activities.
- Intensified case finding activities among HIV infected pregnant women and children living with HIV.
- NACP and RNTCP have taken the policy decision to adopt Isoniazid Prophylaxis Therapy (IPT) as a strategy for the prevention of TB among People Living with HIV (PLHIV).
- Free provision of Antiretroviral Therapy (ART) for all TB-HIV co-infected patients regardless of CD4 count.

FINANCIAL RESOURCES AND ACHIEVEMENTS

Financial Resources:
- World Bank.
- Global Fund to Fight AIDS, Tuberculosis and Malaria (GFATM).
- United States Agency for International Development (USAID).

Key Achievements:
- Treatment success rate increased from 25 percent (in 1998) to 89 percent (in 2022/2023).
- Death rate brought down from 29 percent to 4 percent.
- Widespread infrastructure setup: 731 DTCs, 4,888 TB Units, and 13,886 DMCs functional in the country.
- Significant reduction in delays for diagnosis and treatment of all types of TB cases.
- Increased access to services for marginalized and hard-to-reach populations.
updatedSegments: End TB Strategy (2014):

subsections.title: National AIDS Control Programme (NACP)
subsections.content:
INTRODUCTION AND HISTORY

The National AIDS Control Programme (NACP) was launched in India in the year 1992. The Ministry of Health and Family Welfare set up the National AIDS Control Organization (NACO) in 1992 as a separate wing to implement and closely monitor the various components of the programme. The primary aim of the programme is to prevent further transmission of HIV, to decrease morbidity and mortality associated with HIV infection, and to minimize the socio-economic impact resulting from HIV infection, with NACP Phase-V (2021-2026) specifically targeting an 80% reduction in annual new HIV infections and AIDS-related mortalities by 2025-26 from the 2010 baseline.

KEY MILESTONES
- 1986: First case of HIV detected in India. National AIDS Control Committee set up by the Government of India.
- 1990: Medium Term Plan (MTP) launched for four states and the four metros.
- 1992: NACP-I launched to slow down the spread of HIV infection. NACO set-up. National AIDS Control Board constituted.
- 1998: Professional blood donation prohibited since 1 Jan 1998.
- 1999: NACP-II begins, focusing on behaviour change, increased decentralization, and NGO involvement. State AIDS Control Societies established.
- 2002: National AIDS Control Policy adopted. National Blood Policy adopted.
- 2004: Anti-retroviral treatment (ART) initiated.
- 2006: National Council on AIDS constituted under the chairmanship of the Prime Minister.
- 2007: NACP-III launched for 5 years (2007-2012).
- 2012: NACP-IV launched for 5 years (2012-2017).
- 2017: National Strategic Plan for HIV/AIDS and STIs 2017-2024.
- 2021: NACP Phase-V (2021-2026) launched.

GOALS AND OBJECTIVES

- NACP-III (2007-2012) aimed to halt and reverse the epidemic in India over its five-year period by integrating programmes for prevention, care, support, and treatment.
- NACP Phase-V Goals: Reduce annual new HIV infections and AIDS-related mortalities by 80 percent by 2025-26 from the baseline value of 2010. Attain dual elimination of vertical transmission of HIV and Syphilis. Eliminate HIV/AIDS related stigma and promote universal access to quality STI/RTI services.
- Fast Track Targets (95-95-95): 
  - NACP Phase-V (2021-26) aims for 95 percent of people who are most at risk of acquiring HIV infection to use comprehensive prevention.
  - 95 percent of HIV positive know their status.
  - NACP Phase-V aims for 95 percent of those who know their status to be on treatment.
  - NACP Phase-V aims for 95 percent of those who are on treatment to have suppressed viral load.
  - Less than 10 percent of people living with HIV and key populations experience stigma and discrimination.

ORGANIZATIONAL STRUCTURE

- National level: National AIDS Control Organization (NACO).
- State level: State AIDS Control Societies (SACS).
- District level: District HIV/AIDS Prevention and Control Units (DAPCUs) established during NACP-III to implement AIDS control and prevention strategies at the district level.
- DISHA: District Integrated Strategy for HIV/AIDS (DISHA) clusters created across the country with an expanded mandate to work in a dynamic approach to tackle the emerging pattern of prevalence of the epidemic at the district level.

EPIDEMIOLOGY AND COUNTRY SCENARIO

Based on sentinel surveillance data, the HIV prevalence in adult population can be broadly classified into three groups of States/UTs:
- Group 1 (High prevalence states): Maharashtra, Manipur, Andhra Pradesh, Tamil Nadu, Karnataka, Nagaland. HIV infection > 5 percent in High Risk Groups (HRGs) and >= 1 percent in ANC.
- Group 2 (Moderate prevalence states): Gujarat, Goa, Puducherry. HIV infection > 5 percent in HRGs and < 1 percent in ANC.
- Group 3 (Low prevalence states): Remaining states where HIV infection is < 5 percent in HRGs and < 1 percent in ANC.

CATEGORIES OF DISTRICTS

- Category A (156 districts): More than 1 percent ANC/PTCT prevalence in the district at any time in any of the sites in the last 3 years.
- Category B (39 districts): Less than 1 percent ANC/PTCT prevalence in all the sites during the last 3 years associated with more than 5 percent prevalence in any HRG group (STD/CSW/MSM/IDU).
- Category C (296 districts): Less than 1 percent in ANC prevalence in all sites during the last 3 years with less than 5 percent in all STD clinic attendees or any HRG with known hot spots (Migrants, truckers, large aggregation of factory workers, tourist etc.).
- Category D (118 districts): Less than 1 percent in ANC prevalence in all sites during the last 3 years with less than 5 percent in all STD clinic attendees or any HRG OR, poor HIV data with no known hot spots.

HIV SURVEILLANCE

Types of Surveillance:
1. HIV sentinel surveillance
2. HIV sero surveillance
3. AIDS case surveillance
4. STD surveillance
5. Behavioral surveillance

HIV Sentinel Surveillance Objectives:
- To determine the level of HIV infection among general population as well as high-risk groups in different states.
- To understand the trends of the HIV epidemic.
- To understand the geographical spread of HIV infection and identify emerging pockets.
- To estimate HIV prevalence and HIV burden.

HIV Sentinel Surveillance Framework (Round 2020-2021):
- High Risk Groups (IDU/MSM/FSW/TG): Sentinel sites include Targeted Intervention (TI) projects. HIV Sentinel Surveillance (HSS) is conducted annually, with HIV estimations undertaken biennially. Blood specimen is Dried Blood Spot (DBS). Testing strategy is unlinked anonymous with informed consent.
- Bridge Population (SMM/LDT): Sentinel site is STD clinic, TI projects. Frequency is once in 2 years. Blood specimen is Serum at STD clinics, DBS at TI sites. Testing strategy is unlinked anonymous with informed consent at TI sites.
- General Population (Pregnant women): Sentinel site is Antenatal clinics. Frequency is once in 2 years. Blood specimen is Serum. Testing strategy is unlinked anonymous.

STRATEGIES AND INTERVENTIONS

1. COUNSELING AND HIV TESTING SERVICES
- Goal: To identify as many people living with HIV as early as possible and link them to services.
- Components: Integrated Counselling and Testing Centres (ICTC), PPTCT, HIV/TB collaborative activities.
- CTC (Counseling and testing centres): Include early detection of HIV, provision of basic information on modes of transmission, and prevention of HIV/AIDS for promoting behavioral change.
- Types of ICTC:
  1. Fixed facility ICTC: Located within health care facility. It has two subtypes:
    - Standalone ICTC (SA-ICTC): Full-time counselor and lab technician, providing counseling and testing services. Located in medical colleges, District Hospitals (DH), Community Health Centres (CHC).
    - Facility-integrated ICTC (F-ICTC): Set up below the block level at Primary Health Centres (PHC). PPP-ICTC are similarly established in private facilities.
  2. Mobile ICTC: A van with a room to conduct general examination, counseling, and collection of blood samples. Staffed by an ANM, counselor, and lab technician.

2. PREVENTION OF PARENT TO CHILD TRANSMISSION (PPTCT)
- Aim: To offer testing to every pregnant woman and eliminate transmission of HIV from mother to child.
- Services provided:
  - HIV counseling and testing to all pregnant women enrolled into ANC with an "opt-out" option.
  - Involvement of spouse and other family members to move from an ANC-centric to a family-centric approach.
  - Lifelong ART (Option B of WHO: TDF + 3TC + EFV) to all pregnant and breast-feeding HIV infected women, regardless of CD4 count or clinical stage.
  - Promotion of institutional deliveries for all HIV infected pregnant women.
  - Care of associated conditions (STI/RTI/TB and other opportunistic infections).
  - Nutrition, counseling, and psychological support.
  - Support for initiation of exclusive breast feeds within an hour of delivery and continued for 6 months.
  - ARV prophylaxis (Nevirapine/NVP) to infants from birth up to a minimum of 6 months.
  - Initiation of Cotrimoxazole Prophylactic Therapy (CPT) and Early Infant Diagnosis (EID) using HIV-DNA PCR at 6 weeks of age onwards.

3. CARE, SUPPORT AND TREATMENT (CST)
- Aims to provide comprehensive services to People Living with HIV (PLHIV) regarding free antiretroviral therapy, psychosocial support, prevention, and treatment of opportunistic infections including tuberculosis.
- "Test and Treat" policy: Universal access to free life-long ART for all PLHIV.
- Services provided through: ART centres, Centres of Excellence (CoE), ART plus centres, Link ART centres, ICTCs, STI clinics, and PPTCT services.
- Regimens:
  1. First-line ART: Provided free of cost through ART centres. Eligibility is determined through clinical examination and CD4 count. Includes counseling on treatment adherence, nutrition, and positive living. Follow-up assesses drug adherence. Periodic examination and CD4 count (every 6 months). Treatment is provided for opportunistic infections.
  2. Alternative first-line ART: For patients on first-line ART experiencing acute/chronic toxicity or intolerance, leading to a change to an alternative first-line drug. Handled through Centres of Excellence and ART-Plus centres.
  3. Second-line ART: Available at ART plus centres and CoEs.
  4. Third-line ART: Introduced in 2015 for patients experiencing treatment failure on second-line ART. Uses Raltegravir and Darunavir.
- National pediatric HIV/AIDS initiative: Children living with HIV/AIDS (CLHIV) are registered in ART centres and receive free ART drugs. Pediatric second-line ART is available for children who fail first-line drugs.

4. TARGETED INTERVENTIONS (TI) FOR HIGH RISK GROUPS
- Objective is to improve health-seeking behaviour of High Risk Groups (HRG) and vulnerable populations, and reduce their risk of acquiring STI and HIV infections.
- HRGs include: Female Sex Workers (FSW), Men who have Sex with Men (MSM), Transgenders (TG), Injecting Drug Users (IDU), and Bridge Populations (Migrants and long-distance truckers).
- Services offered: Detection and treatment of STI, condom promotion through social marketing, free condom distribution, and Behaviour Change Communication (BCC).
- Specific intervention for IDU: Opioid substitution therapy, linkage with detoxification/rehabilitation centres, distribution of clean needles and syringes, abscess prevention and management.
- Specific intervention for MSM/TGs: Provision of lubricants, provision of project-based STI clinics.

5. LINK WORKER SCHEME
- Community-based outreach strategy to address HIV prevention and care needs of HRG and vulnerable populations in rural areas.
- Includes counseling, treatment of opportunistic infections, condom promotion, and distribution.
- Objectives: Reaching out to these groups with information and knowledge on prevention and risk reduction of HIV and STI, and reducing stigma and discrimination against PLHIV.

6. BLOOD TRANSFUSION SERVICES
- Professional blood donation has been prohibited since 1 Jan 1998.
- Only licensed blood banks are permitted to operate.
- Voluntary blood donation is encouraged.
- Ensure safe collection, processing, storage, and distribution of blood and blood products.
- Zonal blood testing centres are established to provide linkage with other blood banks.
- Testing of every unit of blood is mandatory for detecting infections like HIV, Hepatitis B, Hepatitis C, Malaria, and Syphilis.
- Blood storage centres are established at First Referral Units (FRUs).

7. CONDOM PROMOTION
- Achieved through social marketing channels, free distribution, and non-traditional outlets.
- The coverage of condom promotion was spread to 395 districts, mapped and classified into 4 categories based on HIV prevalence and family planning needs:
  - HPHF: High Prevalence and High Fertility (141 districts)
  - HPLF: High Prevalence and Low Fertility (84 districts)
  - LPLF: Low Prevalence and Low Fertility (0 districts)
  - LPHF: Low Prevalence and High Fertility (170 districts)

8. STD CONTROL PROGRAMME AND SURAKSHA CLINICS
- Approach adopted: Management of STD through the Syndromic approach. Services for treatment of RTI and STI are integrated at all levels of health care.
- STD clinics at district/block/FRU level function as referral centres.
- NACO branded the STI/RTI services as "Suraksha Clinic".
- Pre-packed STI/RTI colour-coded kits are provided for free supply at all designated STI/RTI clinics.
- Colour codes for kits:
  - Kit 1: Grey, for urethral discharge, ano-rectal discharge, and cervicitis.
  - Kit 2: Green, for vaginitis.
  - Kit 3: White, for genital ulcers.
  - Kit 4: Blue, for genital ulcers.
  - Kit 5: Red, for genital ulcers.
  - Kit 6: Yellow, for lower abdominal pain.
  - Kit 7: Black, for inguinal bubo.

9. INFORMATION, EDUCATION & COMMUNICATION (IEC)
- Directed at: Increasing knowledge among the general population on safe sexual behaviour, sustaining behaviour change in high-risk groups, generating demand for care, support, and treatment services, and making appropriate changes in societal norms to reinforce positive attitudes and reduce stigma and discrimination.
- Adolescence Education Programme: Runs in secondary and senior secondary schools to build life skills of adolescents to cope with physical and psychological changes. Comprises 16-hour sessions during academic terms of Class IX and XI.
- Red Ribbon Clubs: Formed in colleges to encourage peer-to-peer messaging on HIV prevention, provide a safe space for young people to seek clarification of doubts and myths, and promote voluntary blood donation among youth.

10. TB-HIV COORDINATION
- Intensified TB case finding has been implemented nationwide at all HIV testing centres (ICTCs) and ART centres.
- HIV testing of TB patients is now routine through Provider Initiated Testing and Counselling (PITC), implemented in all states.
- Intensified case finding activities are specifically monitored among HIV infected pregnant women and children living with HIV.
- NACP and RNTCP have taken a policy decision to adopt Isoniazid Prophylaxis Therapy (IPT) as a strategy for the prevention of TB among PLHIV.
- The RNTCP has prioritized presumptive TB cases among people living with HIV for the diagnosis of TB and Rifampicin resistance with rapid diagnostic tools having high sensitivity (e.g., Xpert MTB/RIF).
updatedSegments: - NACP-III (2007-2012) aimed to halt and reverse the epidemic in India over its five-year period by integrating programmes for prevention, care, support, and treatment.
- NACP Phase-V aims for 95 percent of those who know their status to be on treatment.
- NACP Phase-V aims for 95 percent of those who are on treatment to have suppressed viral load.

subsections.title: National Programme for Control of Blindness and Visual Impairment (NPCB&VI)
subsections.content:
INTRODUCTION AND HISTORY

The National Programme for Control of Blindness was launched in the year 1976 as a 100 per cent centrally sponsored programme (now 60:40 in all states and 90:10 in North-Eastern States). It incorporates the earlier National Trachoma Control Programme which was started in the year 1963. The programme was launched with the goal to reduce the prevalence of blindness from 1.4 per cent (1974) to 0.3 per cent.

GOALS AND OBJECTIVES

- To reduce the prevalence of blindness to 0.3 per cent. (Recently, the goal is set to reduce the prevalence to 0.25 per cent by the year 2025. As per the 2015-19 survey, the prevalence of blindness was 0.36 per cent).
- To continue signature activities i.e., performance of cataract operations, school eye screening and distribution of free spectacles, and collection of donated eyes for keratoplasty.
- Identification and treatment of curable blindness at primary, secondary, and tertiary levels based on assessment of the overall burden of visual impairment.
- Provision of comprehensive universal eye care services and quality service delivery.
- Strengthening and up-gradation of Regional Institutes of Ophthalmology (RIOs) to become centres of excellence in various sub-specialities.
- To enhance community awareness on eye care and lay stress on preventive measures.
- Increased and expand research for prevention of blindness and visual impairment.
- To secure participation of voluntary organizations and private practitioners in delivering eye care.

STRATEGIES AND INTERVENTIONS

- Free cataract surgery through health care delivery system as well as by NGO and private practitioner.
- Active screening of population above 50 years of age.
- Screening of children for identification and treatment of refractive errors.
- Coverage of underserved areas for eye care services through public private partnership.
- Information Education Communication (IEC) activities for creating awareness on eye care and promotion of preventive eye care.
- District hospital strengthened by upgrading manpower like ophthalmologists and paramedical ophthalmic assistants (PMOA) on contractual basis.
- Establishing vision centers in all PHCs with a PMOA.
- Multipurpose District Mobile Ophthalmic Units: At present, there are 80 central mobile units attached to medical colleges and 341 district mobile units to provide eye care in mobile eye camps, particularly in rural areas.
- Use of mobile ophthalmic units and tele-ophthalmology network to expand coverage and reach of the programme in disadvantaged and hard to reach areas.
- Clearing cataract backlog in a campaign mode to take care of pending cases, especially following the Covid-19 pandemic. District Collectors lead the campaign in their respective districts.
- Cataract surgery packages are included in AB-PMJAY and State Insurance Schemes.
- Involving voluntary organizations such as Lions International, Rotary International, and NGOs to organize eye camps in remote rural and urban areas.

ORGANIZATIONAL STRUCTURE AND ADMINISTRATION

- Central Level: Ophthalmology Section, Directorate General of Health Services, Ministry of Health & FW, New Delhi (National Institute of Ophthalmology AIIMS Delhi).
- State Level: State Ophthalmic Cell, Directorate of Health Services. (Merged with State Health Society under NRHM).
- District Level: District Blindness Control Society. (Merged with District Health Societies).
- Tertiary Level: Medical Colleges, Regional Institutes of Ophthalmology, and Centres of Excellence in Eye Care.
- Secondary Level: District Hospital and NGO Eye Hospital.
- Primary Level: Sub-district level hospitals / CHCs, Mobile Ophthalmic Units, Upgraded PHCs, Ophthalmic Assistants, Link Workers / Panchayats.

CARE DELIVERY LEVELS

1. Primary eye care services
- Preventive and promotive eye care services: Under comprehensive primary health care, Health and Wellness Centres (HWC) are providing preventive and promotive eye care services.
- IEC activities for promotion and preventive eye care and eye donation.

2. Secondary eye care services
- Cataract surgeries: Reduction in the backlog of cataract by performing cataract surgeries in Governmental/Non-Governmental Eye Hospitals and private practitioners. Facilities for intra-ocular lens implantation have been expanded to the taluka level.
- Screening for refractive errors and distribution of spectacles: Screening of children for identification and treatment of refractive errors, and distribution of free spectacles to old persons suffering from presbyopia to enable them for undertaking near work.
- Management of visual impairment: The programme is now geared to take care of all categories of visual impairment including low vision cases. Focus is on treatment and management of diabetic retinopathy, vitreo-retinal diseases, glaucoma, corneal blindness, childhood blindness, retinopathy of prematurity (ROP), and age-related macular degeneration (AMD).
- Eye banking services: Strengthening of eye banking services and collection of donated eyes.

3. Tertiary eye care services
- Grant in aid for strengthening of Regional Institutes of Ophthalmology (RIOs) and medical colleges.
- Superspeciality eye care services.
- Training for up-gradation of skills of eye care personnel.

4. Infrastructure development and capacity building
- Grant in aid for strengthening of eye care units at primary and secondary level.
- Training of eye surgeons and paramedical ophthalmic assistants.

SCHOOL EYE SCREENING PROGRAMME

- 6 to 7 per cent of children aged 10-14 years have a problem with their eye sight affecting their learning at school.
- Children are screened by trained teachers for refractive errors.
- Suspected children are seen by ophthalmic assistants.
- Corrective spectacles are prescribed or given free of cost for persons below the poverty line (BPL).

COLLECTION AND UTILIZATION OF DONATED EYES

- Hospital retrieval programme: Motivation of relatives of terminally ill patients with grave disease, or accident victims, to donate eyes.
- Eye donation fortnight: Organized from 25th August to 8th September every year to promote eye donation.
- During 2022-23, approximately 32,414 donated eyes were collected nationally.
- Gujarat, Tamil Nadu, Maharashtra, and Andhra Pradesh are leading states in this activity.

EXTERNALLY AIDED PROJECTS

- World Bank assisted cataract blindness control project.
- Danish assistance to NPCB.
- WHO assistance for prevention of blindness: Includes intra-country fellowships in corneal transplantation, vitreo-retinal surgery, lasers in ophthalmology, and paediatric ophthalmology; pilot survey on childhood blindness in Delhi.

VISION 2020: THE RIGHT TO SIGHT

- It is a global initiative to reduce avoidable (preventable and curable) blindness by the year 2020. India is also committed to this initiative.
- Objective: To eliminate avoidable causes of blindness by the year 2020.
- Main features:
  - Target diseases are: cataract, xerophthalmia, refractive errors, trachoma, glaucoma, diabetic retinopathy.
  - Human resource development as well as infrastructure and technology development at various levels of the health system.
- Proposed 4 tier system structure:
  1. Centers of excellence (Tertiary): Focus on Research (20 Centres).
  2. Training centers (Tertiary): Focus on Retinal surgery, Corneal transplantation, Glaucoma surgery (200 Centres).
  3. Service centers (Secondary): Focus on Cataract surgery (2,000 Centres).
  4. Vision centers (Primary): Focus on Refraction and Prescription of glasses (20,000 Centres).
updatedSegments: - To reduce the prevalence of blindness to 0.3 per cent. (Recently, the goal is set to reduce the prevalence to 0.25 per cent by the year 2025. As per the 2015-19 survey, the prevalence of blindness was 0.36 per cent).

subsections.title: National Iodine Deficiency Disorders Control Programme (NIDDCP)
subsections.content:
INTRODUCTION AND HISTORY

India commenced a Goitre Control Programme in 1962, which was initially based on the provision of iodized salt to endemic regions. At the end of three decades, the prevalence of the disease still remained high.

As a result, a major national programme, renamed as the "National Iodine Deficiency Disorders (IDD) Control Programme", was initiated. The new approach shifted the focus from area-specific use to nation-wide promotion of iodized salt.

It was decided as a national policy in 1984, included in the Seventh Five Year Plan, to fortify all edible salt in a phased manner by 1992, which coincided with the beginning of the 8th Five Year Plan (and was supported by policies initiated since the 3rd Five Year Plan).

GOALS AND OBJECTIVES

- To assess the magnitude of Iodine Deficiency Disorders (IDD) through surveys in districts.
- Supply of iodized salt in place of common salt.
- Resurveys to assess iodine deficiency disorders and the impact of iodized salt after every 5 years in districts.
- Laboratory monitoring of iodized salt and urinary iodine excretion.
- Health education and publicity.

COMPONENTS AND STRATEGIES

The essential components of the national IDD programme are:
- Use of iodized salt in place of common salt.
- Monitoring and surveillance.
- Manpower training.
- Mass communication.

CURRENT STATUS AND ACHIEVEMENTS

- Notification banning the sale of non-iodized common salt for direct human consumption in the entire country has been issued under the "Food Safety and Standards Act 2006 and Regulations 2011", with clarifications issued in 2019.
- For effective implementation of the National Iodine Deficiency Disorders Control Programme, 35 States/UTs have established Iodine Deficiency Disorders Control Cells in their State Health Directorate.
- Consequent upon the liberalization of iodized salt production, the Salt Commissioner has issued licenses to 824 salt manufacturers, out of which 777 units have commenced production.
- These manufacturing units have an annual production capacity of 222 lakh metric tonnes of iodized salt.
- The production and supply of iodized salt from April 2015 to August 2015 stood at 26.44 lakh tonnes and 25.12 lakh tonnes, respectively.
updatedSegments: It was decided as a national policy in 1984, included in the Seventh Five Year Plan, to fortify all edible salt in a phased manner by 1992, which coincided with the beginning of the 8th Five Year Plan (and was supported by policies initiated since the 3rd Five Year Plan).

- Notification banning the sale of non-iodized common salt for direct human consumption in the entire country has been issued under the "Food Safety and Standards Act 2006 and Regulations 2011", with clarifications issued in 2019.

subsections.title: Universal Immunization Programme (UIP)
subsections.content:
INTRODUCTION AND HISTORY

The Expanded Programme on Immunization (EPI) was launched by the WHO in 1974 globally and introduced in India in 1978. The primary objective was to reduce mortality and morbidity from vaccine-preventable diseases of childhood. Achieving self-sufficiency in vaccine production became an objective under India's Universal Immunization Programme (UIP), which evolved from EPI in 1985.

In 1985, the programme was renamed as the Universal Immunization Programme (UIP) in India. The aim was to achieve 100 percent coverage of pregnant women with 2 doses of tetanus toxoid (or a booster dose) and at least 85 percent coverage of infants with 3 doses each of DPT and OPV, one dose of BCG, and one dose of measles vaccine by 1990.

UIP targets 12 vaccine-preventable diseases: Diphtheria, Pertussis, Tetanus, Polio, Measles, Rubella, severe form of Childhood Tuberculosis, Hepatitis B, and Meningitis & Pneumonia caused by Haemophilus Influenzae type B, Japanese Encephalitis, Pneumococcal Pneumonia, and Rotavirus diarrhea.

PULSE POLIO IMMUNIZATION PROGRAMME

The Pulse Polio Immunization Programme was launched in the country in the year 1995. 

KEY MILESTONES AND TARGETS
- 1st round: Conducted over 2 immunization days 6 weeks apart on 9th December 1995 and 20th January 1996, targeting children aged 0-5 years.
- Pulse Polio Immunization Programme target age: Initially targeted children under 3 years of age. Later on, as per WHO guidelines, it was expanded to children under 5 years of age.
- Pulse: Refers to the sudden simultaneous mass administration of OPV on a single day to all children 0-5 years of age, regardless of their previous immunization status.
- PPI rounds occur in 2 rounds about 4 to 6 weeks apart during the low transmission season of polio, i.e., between November to February.

STEPS TAKEN TO MAINTAIN POLIO FREE STATUS
- All states and UTs to develop Rapid Response Teams (RRT) and Emergency Preparedness and Response Plans (EPRP).
- In UP and Bihar, every newborn is vaccinated and tracked for 8 subsequent rounds.
- Apart from vaccinating at fixed booths and through house-to-house visits, vaccination is also done at railway stations, bus stops, and religious congregations.
- Special booths are established in areas bordering neighboring countries like Wagah border, Attari station, and Munabo train station.
- High level of vigilance is maintained through surveillance across the country for Vaccine-Derived Poliovirus (VDPV).
- Multi-pronged strategy implemented to ensure sanitation, hygiene, and clean drinking water in 107 high-risk blocks.
- Social mobilizing activities are conducted by involving local influencers, community, and religious leaders.
- Emergency stock of OPV is maintained to respond to Wild Poliovirus (WPV) and circulating Vaccine-Derived Poliovirus (cVDPV).

POLIO ENDGAME STRATEGIC PLAN
- Inactivated Poliovirus Vaccine (IPV) was introduced into the national immunization programme from November 2015, initially in 6 states, and expanded across the country by April 2016.
- Trivalent OPV (tOPV) switched to Bivalent OPV (bOPV) from April 2016 (The Big Switch).

MISSION INDRADHANUSH

Mission Indradhanush was launched by the Ministry of Health and Family Welfare in December 2014.

GOALS AND STRATEGY
- Indradhanush depicts 7 colours of the rainbow for prevention against 7 vaccine-preventable diseases.
- Diseases included: Diphtheria, Whooping cough, Tetanus, Polio, Tubercular meningitis, Measles, and Hepatitis B. It also includes Japanese Encephalitis (JE) and Haemophilus influenzae type B in selected districts.
- Pregnant women are also immunized against tetanus.
- Objective: To fully immunize children who are either unvaccinated or partially vaccinated and those who have not been covered during routine vaccination sessions.

PHASES OF MISSION INDRADHANUSH
- 1st Phase: 
  - 1st round started from 7th April 2015.
  - Covered 201 high-focus districts.
  - Carried out for more than a week.
  - Followed by 3 rounds in the months of May, June, and July 2015, starting from the 7th of each month.
- 2nd Phase: 
  - Started from 7th October 2015.
  - Covered 352 districts.
  - Consisted of 4 rounds.
  - Covering all eligible children and pregnant women.

MEASLES-RUBELLA (MR) CAMPAIGN

The Government of India expanded the measles vaccine across the country in 1985 under the UIP. In 2010, a second dose of measles was introduced at 16-24 months of age, which led to a significant reduction in the disease burden. In 2017, the Measles-Rubella (MR) Vaccine was introduced through a phased campaign.

GOAL
- Elimination of measles and rubella and Congenital Rubella Syndrome (CRS) by 2026.
- Achieve and maintain at least 95 percent population immunity.

PHASING AND TARGETS
- Targets over 400 million children in 36 states and UTs in a span of two years. Included under the National Health Mission (NHM).
- 1st Phase: February 2017 in 5 states (Tamil Nadu, Karnataka, Goa, Lakshadweep, and Puducherry).
- Next Round: August 2017 in 8 states/UTs (Andhra Pradesh, Chandigarh, Dadra & Nagar Haveli, Daman & Diu, Himachal Pradesh, Kerala, Telangana, and Uttarakhand).

STRATEGY FOR MR CAMPAIGNS
- MR vaccine introduced through a campaign in a phased manner across the country without sub-phasing within the states.
- Target group: All children in the target age group are vaccinated irrespective of previous immunization status or history of measles/rubella disease.
- MR vaccination is given at fixed and outreach session sites (schools, CHC/PHC/AWC, community halls, village headmen houses, or private doctor clinics).
- Mobile teams are deployed to cover high-risk areas and populations during the campaign.
- Introduction into the routine program by replacing the two doses of measles vaccine currently administered. The first dose is given between 9 and 12 months of age, and the second dose is given at 16-24 months of age.

COMMUNICATION OBJECTIVES AND STRATEGY
- Positive positioning of the MR campaign among stakeholders, communities, and media.
- Facilitate community acceptance of MR vaccination through social mobilization interventions.
- Build an enabling environment through positive media reporting.
- Combat and counter negative responses and myths.
- Components of communication strategy: Pre-campaign building of an enabling environment, social media monitoring and reporting, AEFI crisis communication, advocacy with stakeholders and medical practitioners, social mobilization to create demand, and maximizing reach through mass media and IEC materials.
- Steps for community mobilization: Training of frontline workers (ANM, AWW, ASHA, and community volunteers), name-based enlisting of educational institutions and beneficiaries by ASHA and AWW, preparation of due lists, and making plans to mobilize schools and communities.
- IEC Activities: Auto miking (loudspeakers), posters, banners, hoardings, leaflets to parents and community leaders, and organizing rallies at the community level.

ROLES AND RESPONSIBILITIES
- State Level: High-level political commitment, use of social media, printing and distribution of IEC materials, media sensitization, AEFI crisis communication, and campaign monitoring.
- District Level: CMO has overall accountability and District Immunization Officer (DIO) is the programme officer. Responsible for ensuring intersectoral coordination, monitoring implementation, and reviewing planned activities daily.
- Block Level: BMO has overall accountability. Responsible for building a team, supervising activities, preparing plans for sub-centres, IPC training of ANM, ASHA, and AWW, meeting local leaders, and ensuring IEC materials are displayed.
- Village Level:
  - ANM: Visit all schools for seeking support, organize MR vaccination session sites with the help of ASHA and AWW.
  - ASHA / AWW: Identify children and pregnant women in their area who are not immunized, mobilize the community and beneficiaries to the session site, and conduct IPC with families of the target age groups.

MR VACCINE CHARACTERISTICS
- Live attenuated vaccine.
- Contains Measles vaccine (Edmonston strain) and Rubella vaccine (RA 27/3 strain).
- For certain vaccines in the UIP, such as Measles and Rubella, vaccination results in high (> 95 percent) seroconversion rates, and protection is generally long-term, though measles immunity may decline after 15-20 years.
- Before reconstitution, it is stable when stored between 2 to 8 degrees Celsius.
- Very sensitive to sunlight, hence it comes in colored glass vials.
- Open vial policy is NOT applicable to reconstituted MR vaccine.
- Must be used within 4 hours after reconstitution. At the session site, the reconstituted vaccine should be kept inside the well of the icepack.
- Induces both humoral and cellular immune responses.

DOSAGE AND ADMINISTRATION
- Lyophilized and reconstituted with diluent (provided by the manufacturer) immediately prior to administration.
- MR Vaccine Dose: 0.5 ml.
- Administered subcutaneously in the right upper arm.
- Other live and inactivated bacterial and viral vaccines can be administered simultaneously on the same day along with MR vaccine, at different sites.

ADVERSE REACTIONS AND CONTRAINDICATIONS
- Adverse reactions: Slight pain and tenderness at the site of injection, mild fever, febrile seizures, transient rash, thrombocytopenic purpura, arthralgia/joint pain, and anaphylaxis (very rare).
- Contraindications: 
  - High fever (> 102 degrees F / 38-39 degrees C).
  - Serious disease.
  - Pregnancy.
  - Persons with a history of an anaphylactic reaction to neomycin or gelatin.
  - Severely immune-compromised individuals.
  - Individuals receiving immunosuppressive therapeutic radiation.
  - Vaccination should be delayed for 3 to 11 months after the administration of blood or blood products.
  - Following MR vaccination, the administration of blood products should be avoided for 2 weeks.

LESSONS LEARNT AND RECOMMENDATIONS FROM CATCH-UP CAMPAIGNS
- Phasing of campaigns should consider geographical contiguity (advisable to take a state as a whole for the MR campaign).
- Interdepartmental coordination between Health, Education, and Women and Child Development (WCD) is crucial.
- Sensitization of all key stakeholders and partners, including leading pediatricians, private school principals, IMA, and IAP.
- Social mobilization through IPC, developing special strategies for high-risk areas.
- Focus on injection safety, AEFI surveillance, cold chain management, safe injection practices, and waste management.
- Use of standardized AEFI management kits.
- Establishing an alternate pool of vaccinators identified from medical colleges, nursing colleges, and pharmacy colleges trained in advance.
- Ensure quality monitoring and supervision by identifying supportive supervisors and independent external monitors at all levels.
updatedSegments: - 1st round: Conducted over 2 immunization days 6 weeks apart on 9th December 1995 and 20th January 1996, targeting children aged 0-5 years.

- For certain vaccines in the UIP, such as Measles and Rubella, vaccination results in high (> 95 percent) seroconversion rates, and protection is generally long-term, though measles immunity may decline after 15-20 years.
- Inactivated Poliovirus Vaccine (IPV) was introduced into the national immunization programme from November 2015, initially in 6 states, and expanded across the country by April 2016.

subsections.title: National Health Mission (NHM)
subsections.content:
INTRODUCTION AND KEY MILESTONES

The National Health Mission (NHM) aims to increase access to the decentralized health system by establishing new infrastructure in deficient areas and upgrading the infrastructure in existing institutions. The overarching umbrella of NHM integrates various schemes and programmes to holistically address health (covering prevention, promotion, and ambulatory care) at primary, secondary, and tertiary levels.

Key Components of NHM:
- Health system strengthening in rural and urban areas.
- Reproductive, Maternal, Newborn, Child and Adolescent Health (RMNCH+A).
- Control of communicable and non-communicable diseases.

Key Milestones:
- 1992: Child Survival and Safe Motherhood Programme (CSSM).
- 1997: Reproductive and Child Health (RCH) Phase I.
- 2005: RCH Phase II and launch of National Rural Health Mission (NRHM).
- 2011: Janani Shishu Suraksha Karyakram (JSSK).
- 2013: RMNCH+A strategy and launch of National Health Mission (NHM).
- 2014: India Newborn Action Plan (INAP).

TARGETS AND ACHIEVEMENTS OF NHM

Targets:
- Reduce Infant Mortality Rate (IMR) to 25 / 1000 live births.
- Reduce Maternal Mortality Ratio (MMR) to 1 / 1000 live births (or 100 / 100,000 live births).
- Reduce Total Fertility Rate (TFR) to 2.1.
- Reduce prevalence of leprosy to < 1 / 10000 population.
- Annual malaria incidence to be < 1 / 1000.

Achievements:
- IMR reduced to 39 / 1000 live births.
- MMR reduced to 1.67 / 1000 live births.
- TFR reduced to 2.3.
- Leprosy prevalence rate achieved at < 1 / 10000.
- Annual malaria incidence achieved at < 1 / 1000.

NATIONAL URBAN HEALTH MISSION (NUHM)
