NUHM seeks to improve the health status of the urban population, particularly slum dwellers and other vulnerable sections (homeless, rag pickers, street children, rickshaw pullers, brick / lime kiln workers, sex workers, and migrants) by facilitating their access to quality health care. It focuses on sanitation, clean drinking water, vector control, and strengthening of public health capacity.

Urban Health Care Services and Infrastructure:
- Mahila Arogya Samiti (MAS): Covers 50-100 households (250-500 beneficiaries). NUHM provides an annual untied grant of Rs. 5000 to MAS every year.
- ASHA / Link Worker (LW): Covers 1000-2500 population. Ensures participation by creation of MAS and Rogi Kalyan Samitis (RKS).
- ANM: Covers 10,000 population.
- Urban Primary Health Centre (U-PHC): Covers 50,000 population. Located within or near slums. Functional from 12 pm to 8 pm. No IPD services. Services include OPD, Lab, Drugs, RCH services, and Counseling for diseases.
- Urban Community Health Centre (U-CHC): Serves as a satellite hospital for 4-5 U-PHCs. Covers 2,50,000 population (5,00,000 and 100 bedded in metro cities). Has 30-50 beds with IPD services. Services include medical care, minor surgery, and institutional delivery.

Services rendered by ASHA in NUHM:
- Promoter of good health practices.
- Facilitate awareness on RCH services.
- Facilitate access to health-related services at AWC / PHC.
- Accompany pregnant woman and children requiring treatment.
- Community action for prevention of diseases.
- Formation and promotion of MAS.
- Maintain records about births and deaths, immunization, and ANC services.

Indicators in NUHM:
- Community process: Number of MAS formed, Number of ASHA trained.
- Health system: Number of ANM recruited, Number of U-PHCs and U-CHCs made operational, Number of RKS created.
- RCH services: ANCs registered in 1st trimester, TT 2nd dose coverage in pregnant women, Number of children fully immunized.
- Communicable diseases: Number of malaria cases detected, Number of TB cases identified.
- Non-communicable diseases: Number of diabetes, cancer, and hypertension cases screened.

Targets of NUHM:
- 40 percent reduction in Under-5 Mortality Rate (U5MR).
- 50 percent reduction in MMR.
- 100 percent ANC coverage.
- 100 percent institutional delivery.
- TFR 2.1.

NATIONAL RURAL HEALTH MISSION (NRHM)

The aim of NRHM is to provide accessible, affordable, accountable, effective, and reliable primary health care, and bridge the gap in rural health care by creating a cadre of Accredited Social Health Activists (ASHA). It brings the Indian system of medicine (AYUSH) to the mainstream of health care.

Programmes integrated into NRHM:
- RCH II
- NVBDCP
- NLEP
- RNTCP
- NPCB
- IDDP
- IDSP

Plan of Action and Infrastructure Pyramid:
1. Creation of ASHA: Must be a female resident of the village, preferably married/widow/divorced, aged 25-45 years, with communication skills and leadership qualities. General norm is one ASHA per 1000 population (relaxed to one per habitation in tribal, hilly, and desert areas).
2. Village Level (1000 Population): ASHA, Anganwadi Worker (AWW), Village Health Sanitation and Nutrition Committee (VHSNC). Focus on Village Health and Nutrition Days (VHND), drug kits, and referral chains.
3. Gram Panchayat / Sub Health Centre Level (5-6 villages): Strengthening of SCs with additional ANM. Skill up-gradation of educated RMPs. Staffing of 2 ANMs and 1 male MPW. Services include telephone links, MCH/Immunization days, drugs, and MCH clinics.
4. Cluster of GPs / PHC Level (30-40 villages): Strengthening of PHCs with AYUSH practitioner and a second doctor (1 male and 1 female). Staffing includes 3 Staff Nurses and 1 LHV for 4-5 SCs. Services include ambulance/hired vehicle, fixed day MCH/Immunization clinics, telephone, MO i/c, emergencies handled by nurses 24x7, round the clock services, drugs, and TB/Malaria tests.
5. Block Level Hospital (100,000 population / 100 villages): Strengthening of CHCs with posting of anesthetist and promotion of RKS for hospital management. Services include ambulance, telephone, obstetric/surgical medical emergencies 24x7, and round the clock services.
6. District Level: All vertical health and family welfare programmes merged into a common District Health Mission. Provision of Mobile Medical Unit (MMU) for improved outreach services.

Major Initiatives under NRHM:
- Selection of ASHA.
- Rogi Kalyan Samiti (RKS): Patient welfare committee / hospital management society. Members act as trustees to manage the affairs of the hospital and upkeep of facilities.
- United grants to the sub-centres: SCs are better equipped with blood pressure measuring equipments, haemoglobin (Hb) measuring equipment, stethoscope, and weighing machine.
- Village Health Sanitation and Nutrition Committee (VHSNC): Important tool for community empowerment and participation at the grass-root level.
- Janani Suraksha Yojna (JSY).
- Janani Shishu Suraksha Karyakram (JSSK).
- National Mobile Medical Units (NMMUs): Repositioned with universal colour and design.
- National Ambulance Services: Free ambulance services for patient transport (e.g., Dial 108 and 102).
- Web enabled Mother and Child Tracking System (MCTS): Name-based tracking of pregnant women, infants, and children up to the age of three years to ensure delivery of services like ANC, PNC, and immunization.

Newer Initiatives:
- Home delivery of contraceptives (condoms, oral contraceptive pills, emergency contraceptive pills) by ASHA.
- Conducting District Level Household Survey (DLHS) 4.
- Promotion of menstrual hygiene.
- Allocation of funds for RKS grants.
- Involving ASHA in Home Based Newborn Care.
- Modification of centrally sponsored schemes for the development of AYUSH hospitals and clinics.
- Rashtriya Bal Swasthya Karyakram (RBSK).
- Rashtriya Kishor Swasthya Karyakram (RKSK).
- MCH wings: 30 bedded MCH wings sanctioned in public health facilities with high bed occupancy.
- National Iron+ Initiative: IFA supplementation for children, adolescents, and women of reproductive age group.
- Delivery points (DPs): Objective of providing comprehensive RMNCH+A services at these facilities.
- Universal Health Coverage: Key goal of the 12th 5-year plan.
- Comprehensive Primary Health Care: Strengthening institutional structures, improving access to technologies, drugs, and diagnostics.
- Kilkari: Interactive voice response based mobile service that delivers time-sensitive audio messages about pregnancy and child health directly to mobile phones.
- Anti TB resistance survey to estimate the burden of MDR-TB.
- Kala-azar elimination plan including active search, new drug regimen, and coordinated indoor residual spray.

REPRODUCTIVE AND CHILD HEALTH (RCH) PROGRAMME

RCH - Phase I Major Interventions:
1. Essential obstetric care: Early registration of pregnancy, minimum 3 antenatal checkups, safe delivery at home or institution, and 3 post-natal checkups.
2. Emergency obstetric care: FRUs strengthened through the supply of emergency obstetric kits.
3. 24 hour delivery services at PHCs/CHCs: Additional honorarium to staff to encourage round-the-clock delivery facilities.
4. Medical Termination of Pregnancy (MTP): Engaging doctors trained in MTP to visit PHCs on fixed dates.
5. Control of RTI and STD: Setting up RTI/STD clinics up to the district level.
6. Immunization: Universal Immunization Programme (UIP) became a part of RCH.
7. Essential newborn care: Resuscitation of newborn with asphyxia, prevention of infection, exclusive breast feeding, and referral of sick newborn.
8. Diarrheal disease control: Low osmolarity oral rehydration solution and addition of zinc.
9. Acute respiratory disease control: Treat pneumonia with cotrimoxazole.
10. Prevention and control of vitamin A deficiency in children: Vitamin A to all children under 5 years of age. 1st dose: 1 lac IU at 9 months. 2nd dose: 2 lac IU after 9 months. Subsequent doses: 2 lac IU at 6-month intervals up to 5 years of age.
11. Prevention and control of anaemia in children:
  - Infants 6 months to 5 years: Iron supplement in liquid formulation with 20 mg of elemental iron and 100 mcg folic acid per day for 100 days in a year.
  - Children 6 to 10 years: 30 mg elemental iron and 250 mcg folic acid for 100 days in a year.
  - Children above 10 years: Adult dose.
12. Training of dais: To train at least one Dai in every village.
13. Empowered Action Group (EAG): Constituted in the Ministry of Health and Family Welfare under the Union Minister to get focused attention for different health programmes in socio-demographically deficient states.
14. District surveys: Rapid household survey to assess the reproductive health status of women. Key indicators: Percentage of pregnant women with full ANC, Percentage of institutional deliveries and home deliveries, Percentage of home deliveries by trained birth attendant, Current contraceptive prevalence rate, and Percentage of children fully immunized.

RCH - Phase II Strategies:
1. Essential obstetric care:
  - Institutional delivery: Fifty percent of the PHCs and all the CHCs would be made operational as 24-hour delivery centres.
  - Skilled attendance at delivery: Guidelines for normal delivery and management of obstetric care complications.
  - Policy decision: ANMs/LHVs/SNs permitted to use drugs in specific emergency situations to reduce maternal mortality.
2. Emergency obstetric care: Minimum services provided by FRU include 24-hour delivery services, surgical interventions like caesarian sections, newborn care, emergency care of sick children, family planning services including laparoscopic services, safe abortion services, treatment of STD/RTI, blood storage facility, laboratory services, and referral transport services.
3. Strengthening referral system: Involve local self-help groups, NGOs, and women groups.
4. Safe abortion services:
  - Medical method of abortion: Mifepristone (RU 486) followed by Misoprostol. Recommended up to 7 weeks (49 days).
  - Manual vacuum aspiration (MVA): Feasible to be used in primary health centres.
5. Village health and nutrition day: Once a month at Anganwadi centre to provide antenatal/postpartum care, promote institutional delivery, health education, immunization, family planning, and nutrition services.
6. Maternal death review: To improve quality of obstetric care and reduce maternal mortality and morbidity.
7. Pregnancy tracking: Screening of pregnant women and providing essential and emergency obstetric care.

Vandemataram Scheme:
- Any obstetric and gynaec specialist, maternity home, nursing home, lady doctor, or MBBS doctor can volunteer.
- Enrolled doctors display the Vandemataram logo at their clinic.
- Iron and folic acid tablets, oral pills, TT injections, etc., are provided by the medical officer for free distribution to beneficiaries.

JANANI SURAKSHA YOJANA (JSY)

- Launched on 12th April 2005, modifying the National Maternity Benefit Scheme.
- Objectives: Reducing maternal mortality and infant mortality through encouraging delivery at health institutions.
- Salient features: 100 percent centrally sponsored scheme. Integrates the benefit of cash assistance with institutional care during antenatal, delivery, and immediate post-partum care.
- Yojna subsidizes the cost of caesarean section and management of obstetric complications up to Rs 1500 per delivery.
- ASHA receives her incentive after her post-natal visit to the beneficiary and when the child has been immunized for BCG.

Cash Assistance Structure:
1. Rural Area:
  - Low Performing States (LPS) (Rural): Mother's package Rs 1400, ASHA's package Rs 600. Total = Rs 2000.
  - High Performing States (HPS) (Rural): Mother's package Rs 700, ASHA's package Rs 600. Total = Rs 1300.
2. Urban Area:
  - Low Performing States (LPS) (Urban): Mother's package Rs 1000, ASHA's package Rs 400. Total = Rs 1400.
  - High Performing States (HPS) (Urban): Mother's package Rs 600, ASHA's package Rs 400. Total = Rs 1000.

Eligibility for Cash Assistance:
- LPS: All women delivering in government health centres.
- HPS: Below poverty line women and women from SC and ST categories.
- Limitation of cash assistance: In LPS, it applies to all births. In HPS, it is limited up to 2 live births.

JANANI SHISHU SURAKSHA KARYAKRAM (JSSK)

- Launched on 1st June 2011.
- Entitles all pregnant women delivering in public health institutions to absolutely free and no-expense delivery, including caesarian section.
- Facilities provided for pregnant women:
  - Free delivery and caesarian section.
  - Free drugs and consumables.
  - Free diet up to 3 days during normal delivery and 7 days in caesarian section.
  - Free diagnostics and free blood.
  - Free transport from home to institution, between facilities in case of referral, and drop back home.
- Similar facilities are provided to all sick newborns for treatment till 30 days after birth.

CHILD HEALTH COMPONENTS

1. Nutritional Rehabilitation Centres (NRCs):
- Facility-based units providing medical and nutritional care to Severe Acute Malnutrition (SAM) children under 5 years of age with medical conditions.
- Services provided: 24-hour care and monitoring, treatment of medical complications, therapeutic feeding, sensory stimulation and emotional care, counseling on appropriate feed and hygiene, demonstration on preparation of energy-dense food, social assessment of the family, and follow up of discharged children.

2. Integrated Management of Neonatal and Childhood Illness (IMNCI):
- Indian version of IMCI developed by WHO and UNICEF. Encompasses a range of interventions to prevent and manage the commonest major childhood diseases, including early neonates of 0-7 days of age. Currently implemented in 433 districts.
- Core strategy: Integrated case management of most common childhood problems i.e., diarrhoea, ARI, malaria, measles, and malnutrition.
- Objectives: To reduce death, illness, and disability; to improve growth and development.
- Components: Improvement of case management skills of health staff, improvements in health system, and improvements in family and community practices.
- Integrated Case Management Process (OPD):
  1. Check for danger signs (Convulsions, Unconsciousness, Inability to breastfeed).
  2. Assess main symptoms (Difficulty in breathing, Diarrhoea, fever).
  3. Assess nutrition and immunization status.
  4. Classify condition using colour-coded treatment:
    - Pink: Urgent referral, Emergency treatment in IPD.
    - Yellow: Treatment at OPD, Give oral drugs.
    - Green: Home management, Treat local infection at home.

3. Facility Based Newborn Care:
- Primary Health Centre / Sub-Centre (PHC/SC): Newborn Care Corner (NBCC).
- Community Health Centre (CHC): NBCC and OT leading to Newborn Stabilization Unit (NBSU).
- District Hospital (DH): NBCC and OT leading to Special Newborn Care Unit (SNCU).
- Newborn Care Corner (NBCC): Space within a delivery room where immediate care is provided to all newborns. Mandatory for all health facilities where deliveries are conducted.
- Newborn Stabilization Unit (NBSU): Facility within the maternity ward where sick and low birth weight newborns can be cared for. All FRUs/CHCs should have NBSU. Requires a space of a 4-bedded unit, with 2 beds in the post-natal ward for rooming-in.
- Special Newborn Care Unit (SNCU): Neonatal unit in the vicinity of the labor room to provide special care for sick newborns. Any facility with > 3000 deliveries/year should have an SNCU. Minimum recommended number of beds is 12.

4. Triage for Sick Newborn:
- Emergency Signs (Require immediate action - Assess & Counsel): Hypothermia, Severe Respiratory Distress, Shock, Coma/Convulsions.
- Priority Signs (Assess & Act Rapidly): Cold Stress, Respiratory Distress, Severe Jaundice, Bleeding, Major Congenital Malformation.
- Non Urgent Signs (Assess & Counsel): Minor Birth Trauma, Minor Malformation, Jaundice.

5. Home Based Newborn Care (HBNC):
- Aimed at improving newborn survival. ASHA is the main person involved.
- Objective: Decrease neonatal mortality and morbidity through prevention of complication, early detection of LBW and preterm, and early identification of illness in newborn.
- Responsibilities of ASHA: Mobilize all pregnant mothers to ensure ANC, undertake birth planning and preparedness, assess if the baby is at risk, detect signs and symptoms of sepsis, and provide newborn care through home visits.
- Visit Schedule:
  - Institutional delivery (6 visits): Days 3, 7, 14, 21, 28, and 42.
  - Home delivery (7 visits): Days 1, 3, 7, 14, 21, 28, and 42.
- Incentives to ASHA: Rs 250 for completing visits, plus Rs 50 for monthly follow-up of LBW babies and newborns from SNCU. Twins carry an incentive of Rs 500. Incentives are paid on the 45th day of delivery.

6. Navjat Shishu Suraksha Karyakram (NSSK):
- Launched for the prevention of hypothermia and infection.
- Aim: To train health personnel in basic newborn care and resuscitation.
- Objective: To have a trained health person in basic newborn care and resuscitation unit at every delivery point.

7. Rashtriya Bal Swasthya Karyakram (RBSK):
- Launched in 2013. Provision for child health screening and early intervention services through early detection and management of 4Ds. Covers 30 identified health conditions.
- 4Ds include:
  1. Defects at birth: Neural tube defects, Cleft lip/palate, Club foot.
  2. Deficiencies: Anaemia, Vitamin A deficiency, Severe Acute Malnutrition (SAM).
  3. Disease of childhood: Rheumatic Heart Disease (RHD), Reactive airway disease, Dental caries.
  4. Developmental delays: Motor delay, Cognitive delay, Language delay.
- Programme Implementation:
  - For newborns: Facility-based screening at public health facilities (by ANM, MO, Gynecologist). Community-based screening through ASHA during home visits (priority to LBW/chronic illness).
  - For children 6 weeks to 6 years: Anganwadi centre-based screening conducted twice a year by dedicated Mobile Health Teams.
  - For children 6 years to 18 years: Government and Government-aided school-based screening conducted once a year by dedicated Mobile Health Teams deployed at the block level.

RMNCH+A STRATEGY

Launched by the Government of India in February 2013 as "A strategic approach to reproductive, maternal, newborn, child and adolescent health (RMNCH+A) in India".
- Strategy: Linking maternal and child health to reproductive health, family planning, adolescent health, HIV, gender, preconception, and prenatal diagnostic techniques. Linking home and community-based services to facility-based care. Ensuring linkages and referrals among health facilities at primary (PHC), secondary (CHC), and tertiary levels (DH).
- Aim: To reach the maximum number of people in the remotest corners of the country through a continuum of services, constant innovation, and routine monitoring of interventions.
- Management Tools: 5x5 matrix identifying 5 high impact interventions across each of the 5 thematic areas, 5 cross-cutting, and health system strengthening interventions.

5 Thematic Interventions:
1. Reproductive health: PPIUCD, IUCD at all facilities, Home Delivery of Contraceptives (HDC) and Ensuring Birth Spacing (EBS), Pregnancy test kits (PTK Nishchay kits), strengthening abortion services, and sterilization services.
2. Maternal health: Mother and Child Tracking System (MCTS), detect high-risk pregnancy, EmOC services, review of maternal, infant and child deaths, distribute Misoprostol to selected women in the 8th month of pregnancy for consumption during the 3rd stage of labour.
3. Newborn health: Early initiation and exclusive breastfeeding, Home based newborn care, Essential newborn care, Special newborn care services, and community-level use of Gentamycin.
4. Child health: Complimentary feeding and IFA supplementation, Diarrhoea management using ORS and zinc, Management of pneumonia, Full immunization coverage, and RBSK.
5. Adolescent health: Increase contraceptive prevalence, community-based services through peer educators, strengthen ARSH clinics, National Iron Plus initiatives, and promote menstrual hygiene.

Adolescent Health Programmes:
- Adolescent Reproductive and Sexual Health Programme (ARSH): Includes Adolescent Friendly Health Clinics (AFHC) providing routine checkups, contraceptives, menstrual problem management, and RTI/STI management. Also includes facility-based counseling and outreach activities in schools, teen clubs, and VHNDs.
- Weekly Iron and Folic Acid Supplementation Programme (WIFS): Weekly supplements of 60 mg elemental iron and 500 ug folic acid using a fixed-day approach, screening of target groups, biannual de-worming (albendazole 400mg) six months apart, and counseling for improving dietary intake.
- Menstrual Hygiene Scheme: Community-based health education, training of ASHA and nodal teachers, ensuring regular availability and distribution of sanitary napkins, and safe disposal mechanisms.

Delivery Points (DPs):
- Objective is providing comprehensive RMNCH+A services at these facilities.
- L1: Minimum 3 normal deliveries per month.
- L2: Minimum 10 deliveries per month, including management of complications.
- L3: Minimum 20 to 50 deliveries per month, including C-section.

Maternal and Child Health (MCH) Wing:
- Established in high case load facilities with adequate provisions of beds (30, 50, or 100 bedded units).
- Includes antenatal waiting rooms, labour wing, essential newborn care room, SNCU, OT, blood storage units, post-natal ward, and an academic wing.
- Ensures emergency maternal and newborn care as well as a 48-hour stay.

INDIA NEWBORN ACTION PLAN (INAP)

Developed in response to the Global Every Newborn Action Plan (ENAP), INAP was launched in September 2014.
- Goal: To attain "Single Digit Neonatal Mortality Rate by 2030" and "Single Digit Stillbirth Rate by 2030".
- The plan is built on six pillars of intervention packages:
  1. Pre-conception and antenatal care.
  2. Care during labour and childbirth.
  3. Immediate newborn care.
  4. Care of healthy newborn.
  5. Care of small and sick newborn.
  6. Care beyond newborn survival.

- High Performing States (HPS) (Rural): Mother's package Rs 700, ASHA's package Rs 600. Total = Rs 1300.
- Low Performing States (LPS) (Urban): Mother's package Rs 1000, ASHA's package Rs 400. Total = Rs 1400.
- High Performing States (HPS) (Urban): Mother's package Rs 600, ASHA's package Rs 400. Total = Rs 1000.

subsections.title: National Programme for Prevention and Control of Cancer, Diabetes, Cardiovascular Diseases and Stroke (NPCDCS)
subsections.content:
INTRODUCTION AND BACKGROUND

India is experiencing a rapid health transition with a rising burden of chronic non-communicable diseases (NCDs), especially cardiovascular disease, diabetes mellitus, cancer, stroke, and chronic lung diseases. In 2016, NCDs accounted for 60 percent of deaths. Considering this burden, the National Programme on Prevention and Control of Diabetes, Cardiovascular Diseases and Stroke was integrated with the National Cancer Control Programme to form the National Programme for Prevention and Control of Cancer, Diabetes, Cardiovascular Diseases and Stroke (NPCDCS) in 2010. In 2023, NPCDCS was renamed as the National Programme for Prevention & Control of Non-Communicable Diseases (NP-NCD).

GOALS AND OBJECTIVES

- Prevent and control common NCDs through behaviour and lifestyle changes.
- Provide early diagnosis and management of common NCDs.
- Build capacity at various levels of health care for prevention, diagnosis, and treatment of common NCDs.
- Train human resources within the public health setup, including doctors, paramedics, and nursing staff, to cope with the increasing burden of NCDs.
- Establish and develop capacity for palliative and rehabilitative care.

PROGRAMME COMPONENTS AND STRATEGIES

The programme comprises two major components:
A. Diabetes, Cardiovascular Disease and Stroke (DCS) Component.
B. Cancer Component.

The key strategies include:
- Health education and promotion for healthy lifestyles.
- Opportunistic screening of persons above the age of 30 years.
- Establishment of Non-Communicable Disease (NCD) clinics at Community Health Centres (CHC) and District levels.

A. DIABETES, CARDIOVASCULAR DISEASE AND STROKE (DCS) COMPONENT

1. Activities at Sub-Centre
- Health promotion for behaviour and lifestyle change through organizing camps, interpersonal communications, posters, banners, etc.
- Opportunistic screening and Population Based Screening (launched in 2016) of the population above 30 years of age for common NCDs, including BP measurement and blood glucose by strip method.
- Suspected cases of diabetes and hypertension are referred to CHCs of higher health facilities for further diagnosis and management.
- Provision of glucometer optium xceed, optium test strips, and auto disabled lancets procured at the central level.
- Regular home visits by ANM/Male Health Workers for domiciliary visits to elderly and disabled persons, providing support and rehabilitative equipment.

2. Activities at Community Health Centre (CHC)
- Establishment of NCD Clinic at CHC.
- Diagnosis and required investigations/tests like blood sugar measurement, lipid profile, ultrasound, X-ray, and ECG.
- Management and stabilization of common CVD, diabetes, and stroke cases (out-patient as well as in-patients).
- Health workers supervise the work and attend monthly clinics held in villages on a random basis.
- Complicated cases of diabetes, high blood pressure, etc., are referred to the district hospital.

3. Activities at District Hospital
- Establishment of NCD Clinic at the District Hospital.
- Population-based screening of persons above the age of 30 years to identify individuals at high-risk of developing diabetes, hypertension, and common cancers.
- Management and annual assessment of persons suffering from cancer, diabetes, and hypertension.
- Provision of home-based palliative care for chronic, debilitating, and progressive patients.
- Involvement in the promotion of healthy lifestyle through health education and counselling.

URBAN HEALTH CHECK-UP SCHEME FOR DIABETES AND HIGH BLOOD PRESSURE

- Objectives: To screen the urban slum population for diabetes and high blood pressure, and to sensitize the urban slum population about a healthy lifestyle.
- The blood sugar and blood pressure are checked for all individuals aged 30 years and above as part of population-based screening for common NCDs.
- Creates a database for the prevalence of diabetes and high blood pressure in urban slums.

GUIDELINES FOR REFERRAL AND TREATMENT

- Individuals with a systolic blood pressure > 140 mm of Hg and diastolic blood pressure > 90 mm of Hg, or random blood sugar >= 140 mg/dl, are referred to a medical officer at the nearest facility for confirmation, laboratory investigations, and treatment initiation.
- Those found positive for cancer or precancerous lesions are referred to the PHC / CHC / District Hospital for confirmation and treatment by trained specialists.
- Once the diagnosis of hypertension or diabetes is established, the patient must receive at least a month's supply of drugs from the PHC. A three-month supply can also be provided to the patient each month with the ANM/ASHA to ensure compliance.
- Individuals who are already on treatment under the care of a private practitioner could be offered the choice of taking drugs from the public health system.
- Follow up of individuals by ASHA making visits to enable behavioral modification, treatment compliance, and encouraging regular checkups of BP and blood glucose.

B. CANCER COMPONENT UNDER NPCDCS

Cancer Objectives:
1. Primary prevention: Health education.
2. Secondary prevention: Early diagnosis and screening (cervix, mouth, breast, and tobacco-related cancers).
3. Tertiary prevention: Palliative care and strengthening of existing institutions for comprehensive therapy.

Cancer Schemes and Services:
1. Regional Cancer Centre Scheme: Existing regional cancer centres are strengthened to act as referral centres for complicated cases at the tertiary level.
2. Oncology Wing Development Department: Initiated to fill up geographic gaps in the availability of cancer treatment facilities. Central assistance is provided for the purchase of equipment, including a cobalt unit.
3. Decentralized NGO Scheme: NGOs are given financial assistance for undertaking health education and early detection activities of cancer.
4. IEC Activities: Giving wider publicity about anti-tobacco legislation and ill effects of tobacco. November 7th is observed as National Cancer Awareness Day.
5. Research and Training: Manuals developed for health professionals, cytology, palliative care, and tobacco cessation.

Cancer Services Provided:
- Diagnostic services.
- Surgery.
- Chemotherapy (Chemotherapy drugs are provided for 100 patients at each district hospital; Day care chemotherapy facilities established at 100 district hospitals).
- Palliative care (Home based palliative care is being provided for chronic, debilitating, and progressive cancer patients at 100 districts).

TOBACCO CONTROL LEGISLATION AND PROGRAMME

Cigarette and Other Tobacco Products Act (COTPA) 2003:
- Prohibition of smoking in public places.
- Prohibition of direct and indirect advertisement of cigarettes and other tobacco products.
- Prohibition of sale of cigarettes and tobacco products to a person below the age of 18 years.
- Prohibition of sale of tobacco products near educational institutions.
- Statutory warnings (pictorial warnings) on tobacco packs.
- Mandatory depiction of tar and nicotine contents along with maximum permissible limits.

National Tobacco Control Programme Components:
- Mass media campaigns for awareness building and behavioural change.
- Establishment of tobacco product testing laboratories to build regulatory capacity.
- Adult tobacco surveys.
- Dedicated tobacco control cells for effective implementation and monitoring.
- Provision of tobacco cessation facilities.
- School programmes and training of health and social workers, NGOs, school teachers, and enforcement officers.

NEW INITIATIVES UNDER NPCDCS

1. Intervention for prevention and control of Rheumatic Heart Disease under NPCDCS.
2. Integration with Rashtriya Bal Swasthya Karyakram (RBSK).
3. Integration of AYUSH with NPCDCS.
4. Integration of RNTCP (NTEP) with NPCDCS for the management of TB and Diabetes comorbidities.

In 2016, NCDs accounted for 60 percent of deaths. Considering this burden, the National Programme on Prevention and Control of Diabetes, Cardiovascular Diseases and Stroke was integrated with the National Cancer Control Programme to form the National Programme for Prevention and Control of Cancer, Diabetes, Cardiovascular Diseases and Stroke (NPCDCS) in 2010. In 2023, NPCDCS was renamed as the National Programme for Prevention & Control of Non-Communicable Diseases (NP-NCD).
- Opportunistic screening and Population Based Screening (launched in 2016) of the population above 30 years of age for common NCDs, including BP measurement and blood glucose by strip method.

subsections.title: National Mental Health Programme (NMHP)
subsections.content:
INTRODUCTION AND HISTORY

The National Mental Health Programme (NMHP) was launched during 1982 with a view to ensure the availability of mental health care services for all, especially the community at risk and underprivileged sections of the population.

The programme covers 767 districts and has expanded its reach across the country to encourage the application of mental health knowledge in general healthcare and social development.

AIMS AND OBJECTIVES

Aims:
1. Prevention and treatment of mental and neurological disorders and their associated disabilities.
2. Improve quality of life of the population.
3. Application of mental health technology to improve general health services.

Objectives:
1. To ensure availability and accessibility of minimum mental health care for all in the foreseeable future, particularly to the most vulnerable and underprivileged sections of the population.
2. To encourage application of mental health knowledge in general health care and in social development.
3. To promote community participation in mental health services development, and to stimulate efforts towards self-help in the community.

STRATEGIES

The programme strategies are:
1. Integration of mental health with primary health care through the NMHP.
2. Provision of tertiary care institutions for treatment of mental disorders.
3. Eradicating stigmatization of mentally ill patients and protecting their rights through regulatory institutions like the Central Mental Health Authority and State Mental Health Authority.

DISTRICT MENTAL HEALTH PROGRAMME

Components:
1. Training of all workers in the mental health team at the identified nodal institute in the state.
2. Public education in mental health to increase awareness and to reduce stigma.
3. For early detection and treatment, OPD and indoor services are provided.
4. Providing valuable data and experience at the level of community to the state and centre for future planning and improvement in service and research.

Activities:
The District Mental Health Programme has incorporated promotive and preventive activities for positive mental health which includes:
1. School mental health activities:
  - Life skills education in schools.
  - Counseling services.
2. College counseling services:
  - Provided through trained teachers and counselors.
3. Workplace stress management:
  - Covers formal and informal sectors, including farmers, women, etc.
4. Suicide prevention services:
  - Counseling centre at the district level.
  - Sensitization workshops and IEC.
  - Help lines.

THRUST AREAS

1. District mental health programme in an enlarged and more effective form covering the entire country.
2. Streamlining and modernization of mental hospitals in order to modify their present custodial role.
3. Upgrading departments of psychiatry in medical colleges and enhancing the psychiatric content of the medical curriculum at the undergraduate as well as postgraduate level.
4. Strengthening the central and state mental health authorities with a permanent secretariat. Appointment of medical officers at state headquarters in order to make the monitoring role more effective.
5. Research and training in the field of community mental health, substance abuse, and child adolescent psychiatric clinics.

THE MENTAL HEALTHCARE ACT, 2017

The United Nations convention on the rights of persons with disabilities was ratified by the Government of India, thus making it obligatory to align the policies and laws of the country with the convention.

Key provisions and milestones:
- The Mental Healthcare Bill, 2013 was introduced in Parliament and subsequently enacted as The Mental Healthcare Act, 2017, in order to protect, promote and fulfill the rights of persons with mental illness during the delivery of healthcare in institutions and in the community.
- It ensures healthcare, treatment, and rehabilitation of persons with mental illness is provided in the least restrictive environment possible.
- Aims to regulate the public and private mental health sectors within a rights framework, to achieve the greatest public health good, and to promote principles of equity, efficiency, and active participation of all stakeholders in decision making.
- Suicide has been decriminalized under the Act.
- The Mental Healthcare Act, 2017 received the assent of the President of India on 07.04.2017.

subsections.title: Integrated Disease Surveillance Programme (IDSP)
subsections.content:
INTRODUCTION AND HISTORY

The Integrated Disease Surveillance Project (IDSP) is a decentralized state-based surveillance system in the country. It was launched in November 2004 as a 5-year project up to March 2010. The project was restructured and extended up to March 2012, and continues as the Integrated Disease Surveillance Programme under the National Health Mission (NHM) for all states, with its digital platform now known as the Integrated Health Information Platform (IHIP).

GOALS AND OBJECTIVES

- Intended to detect early warning signals of impending outbreaks and help initiate an effective response in a timely manner in urban and rural areas.
- To provide essential data to monitor the progress of ongoing disease control programmes and help allocate health resources more efficiently.
- The important information in disease surveillance involves identifying who gets the disease, how many get the disease, where they get the disease, why they get the disease, and what needs to be done as a public health response.

ORGANIZATIONAL STRUCTURE AND ADMINISTRATION

- Central Surveillance Unit (CSU): Established and integrated in the National Centre for Disease Control (NCDC), Delhi.
- State Surveillance Unit (SSU): Established at all State / UT headquarters.
- District Surveillance Unit (DSU): Established at all districts in the country.
- An IT network connecting 776 sites in states / district headquarters and premier institutes has been established with the help of the National Information Centre (NIC) and ISRO (Indian Space Research Organization) for data entry, training, video conferencing, and outbreak discussions.

TYPES OF INTEGRATIONS IN IDSP

Different types of integration are proposed and implemented in the project:
1. Health and non-health sectors (Developing effective partnership).
2. Non-communicable and communicable diseases surveillance.
3. Private sector and NGOs.
4. Academic institutions and medical colleges (Bringing them into the primary public health activity of disease surveillance).
5. Sharing of surveillance information of disease control programmes.

COMPONENTS OF SURVEILLANCE

The prerequisite of effective surveillance is the use of standard case definitions, ensuring regularity of reports, and action on reports. The components are:
1. Collection of data
2. Compilation of data
3. Analysis and interpretation of data
4. Follow up action
5. Feedback

CLASSIFICATION OF SURVEILLANCE

Weekly disease surveillance data on epidemic-prone diseases are collected on 'S' (syndromic), 'P' (probable/presumptive), and 'L' (laboratory) formats. Districts report such weekly data, primarily through the Integrated Health Information Platform (IHIP) portal, which enables real-time data entry and analysis.

1. Syndromic diagnosis: Diagnosis is made on the basis of the clinical pattern by paramedical personnel and members of the community (e.g., Sub-centre health worker / ANM).
2. Presumptive (Probable) diagnosis: Diagnosis made on typical history and clinical examination by a Medical Officer (e.g., PHC / CHC MO).
3. Confirmed diagnosis: Clinical diagnosis confirmed by a medical officer and positive laboratory identification.

SYNDROMES LISTED UNDER SURVEILLANCE

The paramedical health staff undertake disease surveillance based on broad categories of presentation. These syndromes are intended to pick up all priority diseases listed under regular surveillance.
- Fever:
  - Less than 7 days duration without any localizing signs (Malaria, Typhoid, JE, Dengue, Measles).
  - With rash (Measles, Dengue).
  - With altered sensorium or convulsions (JE, Meningitis).
  - Bleeding from skin or mucus membrane (Dengue, Haemorrhagic fevers).
  - More than 7 days with or without localizing signs (Typhoid, Malaria).
- Cough: More than 2 weeks duration (Tuberculosis).
- Acute flaccid paralysis: Polio.
- Diarrhoea: Cholera, Acute diarrhoeal disease.
- Jaundice: Hepatitis, Leptospirosis, Dengue, Malaria, Yellow fever.
- Unusual events causing death or hospitalization: Anthrax, Plague, Emerging epidemics.

CONDITIONS UNDER SURVEILLANCE

1. Regular Surveillance:
  - Vector borne diseases: Malaria.
  - Water borne diseases: Acute diarrhoeal disease (Cholera), Typhoid.
  - Respiratory diseases: Tuberculosis.
  - Vaccine preventable diseases: Measles.
  - Diseases under eradication: Polio.
  - Other conditions: Road traffic accidents (Link-up with police computers).
  - International commitments: Plague.
  - Unusual clinical syndromes: Meningoencephalitis / Respiratory distress, Haemorrhagic fevers, other undiagnosed conditions.
2. Sentinel Surveillance:
  - Sexually transmitted diseases / blood borne: HIV / HBV / HCV.
  - Other conditions: Water quality monitoring, Outdoor air quality (Large urban centres).
3. Regular periodic surveys:
  - NCD risk factors: Anthropometry, Physical activity, Blood pressure, Tobacco, Nutrition, etc.
4. Additional state priorities:
  - Each state may identify up to five additional conditions for surveillance.

OUTBREAK AND RAPID RESPONSE

In epidemiology, an outbreak is a sudden increase in the occurrence of a disease in a particular time and place. Whenever there is a rising trend of illness, it is investigated by the Rapid Response Team (RRT) to diagnose and control the outbreak. It is a multi-specialty team typically comprising an epidemiologist, a microbiologist, a medical officer, laboratory technicians, data managers, and other specialists, including veterinary officers for zoonotic diseases, as per requirement.

Warning signs of an impending outbreak:
- Clustering of cases or deaths in time and/or space.
- Unusual increase in the number of cases or deaths.
- Even a single case of measles, AFP, cholera, plague, dengue, or JE.
- Acute febrile illness of unknown aetiology.
- Occurrence of two or more epidemiologically linked cases of meningitis or measles.
- Unusual isolate.
- Shifting in age distribution of cases.
- Sudden increase or high vector density.
- Natural disasters.

Trigger events for investigation:
- Acute watery stools: A single case of severe dehydration or death in a patient > 5 years of age with diarrhoea, OR more than 10 houses having at least one case of loose stools irrespective of age, per village or urban ward.
- Fever < 7 days duration: (Syndromic surveillance category, further classified as 'Only Fever', 'With Rash', 'With Altered Sensorium / Convulsions', or 'With Bleeding').
  - Only fever: 5 cases in 1000 population.
  - With rash (Measles / Dengue): 2 similar cases in a village (1000 population).
  - Altered consciousness: 2 cases of fever with altered consciousness in the village / 1000 population.
  - Fever with bleeding: 2 cases of fever with bleeding in a village / 1000 population.
  - Fever with convulsions: 2 cases of fever with convulsions in a village / 1000 population.
- Fever more than 7 days: More than 2 cases in a village / 1000 population.
- Jaundice: More than 2 cases in a village / 1000 population.
- Unusual event: More than 2 deaths or hospitalizations.

REPORTING UNITS IN IDSP

1. Public Health Sector:
  - Rural: CHCs, District hospitals.
  - Urban: Urban hospitals, ESI / Railway / Medical college hospitals.
2. Private Health Sector:
  - Rural: Sentinel private practitioners and Sentinel hospitals.
  - Urban: Sentinel private nursing homes, Sentinel hospitals, Medical colleges, Private and NGO laboratories.
updatedSegments: - Cough: More than 2 weeks duration (Tuberculosis).

subsections.title: Ayushman Bharat Programme (AB-PMJAY & Health and Wellness Centres)
subsections.content:
INTRODUCTION AND HISTORY

In February 2018, the Government of India announced two major initiatives in the health sector under the Ayushman Bharat Programme. The aim is to cover preventive and health promotive interventions at the primary, secondary, and tertiary care systems. 

Ayushman Bharat is a National Health Protection Scheme which will cover over 10 crore poor and vulnerable families (approximately 50 crore beneficiaries), providing coverage up to 5 lakh rupees per family per year for secondary and tertiary care hospitalization. It subsumes the on-going centrally sponsored schemes like the Rashtriya Swasthya Bima Yojana (RSBY) and the Senior Citizen Health Insurance Scheme (SCHIS).

GOALS AND OBJECTIVES (2018-2022)

- Focus on the wellness of poor families.
- Providing medical benefits to poor families.
- Establishing Health and Wellness Centers at a nearer distance, so that patients will not have to travel a long distance.
- Reduction of Out of Pocket (OOP) expenditure.

COMPONENTS OF AYUSHMAN BHARAT

The Ayushman Bharat programme consists of four main pillars:
1. Ayushman Arogya Mandir (AAM) (Previously known as Health and Wellness Centre).
2. Ayushman Bharat - Pradhan Mantri Jan Arogya Yojana (AB-PMJAY).

AYUSHMAN AROGYA MANDIR (HEALTH AND WELLNESS CENTRES)

The National Health Policy 2017 envisioned Health and Wellness Centres as the foundation of India's health system. 

Key features and achievements:
- The target was to establish 1.5 lakh centres to bring healthcare closer to the homes of people.
- The first Health and Wellness Centre was inaugurated on 14th April 2018 in Bijapur district of Chhattisgarh.
- As of 31st March 2024, 1,72,148 centres are operational in the country.
- The centres provide comprehensive health care, including maternal and child health services, communicable diseases services, and services related to Non-Communicable Diseases (NCDs) such as hypertension, diabetes, and 3 common cancers of the oral cavity, breast, and cervix.
- Incremental addition of primary healthcare services for mental health, ENT, ophthalmology, oral health, geriatric and palliative health care, and trauma care.
- Wellness activities like Yoga, cyclathons, marathons, EAT RIGHT, SAFE, cessation of tobacco and drugs, meditation, laughter clubs, and open gyms are promoted. An annual health calendar guides planned activities.
- The centres also provide free essential drugs and diagnostic services.

Primary Healthcare Team at Sub Health Centre level AAM:
- Headed by a Community Health Officer (CHO) who is a BSc/GNM Nurse or an Ayurveda Practitioner trained in primary care and public health skills, and certified in a six months certificate programme in community health.
- Other members of the team include multi-purpose workers (Male and Female) and Accredited Social Health Activists (ASHAs).
- Training is carried out with support from IGNOU and state specific Public/Health Universities.

Expanded service packages planned to be provided at functional AAMs:
1. Care in pregnancy and child birth.
2. Neonatal and infant health care services.
3. Childhood and adolescent health care services.
4. Family planning, contraceptive services, and other reproductive health care services.
5. Management of communicable diseases: National Health programmes.
6. General out-patient care for acute simple illnesses and minor ailments.
7. Screening, prevention, control, and management of non-communicable diseases and chronic communicable diseases like tuberculosis and leprosy.
8. Basic oral health care.
9. Screening and basic management of mental health ailments.
10. Care for common ophthalmic and ENT problems.
11. Elderly and palliative health care services.
12. Emergency medical services including burns and trauma.

PRADHAN MANTRI JAN AROGYA YOJANA (AB-PMJAY)

AB-PMJAY provides health coverage up to Rs. 5.00 lakh per family per year to poor and vulnerable families identified on the basis of Socio Economic Caste Census (SECC) data.

Salient Features:
- Benefit cover of Rs. 5 lakh per family per year.
- Entire family covered with no limit on family size, age, or number of members.
- All pre-existing illnesses are covered from day one.
- Fully portable across the country.
- Covers poor, deprived rural families and identified occupational urban workers.
- Beneficiaries covered under the scheme are allowed to take cashless benefits from any public or private empanelled hospitals across the country.
- Release of Grant-in-Aid through an Escrow Account.
- IT platform is made operational which entails a paperless, cashless transaction.

Expansion to Senior Citizens (September 11, 2024): All senior citizens aged 70 and above are eligible for health coverage of up to ₹5 lakh per family per year under AB PM-JAY, irrespective of income.
- The Union Cabinet approved a major expansion of AB-PMJAY on September 11, 2024, to cover all senior citizens aged 70 and above, regardless of their socio-economic status or income.
- Benefits around 4.5 crore families, including 6 crore senior citizens.
- New distinct card: Eligible senior citizens aged 70 and above receive a new, distinct card under the AB PM-JAY scheme.
- Top-up coverage: Senior citizens within families already covered by AB PM-JAY receive an additional top-up cover of up to Rs 5 lakh per year, exclusively for family members aged 70 and above.
- Family coverage: Senior citizens who are not part of an existing AB PM-JAY family receive a cover of up to Rs 5 lakh per year on a family basis.
- Choice of schemes: Senior citizens already benefiting from CGHS, ECHS, or CAPF have the option to either continue with their current scheme or opt for AB PM-JAY.
- Eligibility with private insurance: Senior citizens aged 70 years and above, even if covered by private health insurance policies or the Employees' State Insurance (ESI) scheme, are also eligible for benefits under Ayushman Bharat PM-JAY, as approved on September 11, 2024.

TARGET BENEFICIARIES

Target Group for Rural (based on SECC deprivation criteria): Households with only one room with kucha walls and kucha roof (D1); households with no adult member aged 16-59 years (D2); female-headed households with no adult male member aged 16-59 years (D3); households with a disabled member and no able-bodied adult member (D4); SC/ST households (D5); and landless households deriving a major part of their income from manual casual labour (D7).
- D1: Only one room with kachha walls and kachha roof.
- D2: No adult member between age 16 to 59.
- D3: Female headed households with no adult male member between age 16 to 59.
- D4: Disabled member and no able-bodied adult member.
- D5: SC/ST households.
- D7: Landless households deriving major part of their income from manual casual labour.
- Automatically included: Households without shelter, Destitute/living on alms, Manual scavenger families, Primitive tribal groups, Legally released bonded labour.

Target Group for Urban (based on occupational categories):
- Rag picker / Beggar.
- Domestic worker / Street vendor.
- Construction worker / Plumber / Labour.
- Sweeper / Sanitation worker.
- Transport worker / Driver / Conductor / Helper.
- Cart puller / Rickshaw puller / Coolie.
- Shop worker / Peon / Waiter.
- Electrician / Mechanic / Washer-man (dhobi) / Gardener (Mali) / Tailor / Welder / Security guard.

IMPLEMENTATION STRATEGY

- National level: An Ayushman Bharat National Health Protection Mission Agency (AB-NHPMA) has been established.
- State level: State Health Agency (SHA) implements the scheme.
- Funding: The expenditure incurred in premium payments is shared between Central and State Governments in a specified ratio.
- Modes of Implementation: States/UTs can decide to implement the scheme through an insurance company, directly through a Trust/Society, or use an integrated model.
  - Insurance Mode: States/UTs select an Insurance Company.
  - Trust / Assurance Mode: Implemented through Society/Trust of State Health Department.
  - Mixed Model: Combination of Insurance and Assurance.

MAJOR IMPACT

- Reduction of Out of Pocket (OOP) expenditure.
- Increased benefit cover to nearly 40 percent of the population.
- Covering almost all secondary and many tertiary hospitalizations.
- Coverage of 5 lakh for each family with no restriction of family size.
- Increased access to quality health and medication.
- Unmet needs of the population which remained hidden due to lack of financial resources are catered to.
- Timely treatments, improvements in health outcomes, patient satisfaction, improvement in productivity and efficiency, and job creation thus leading to improvement in quality of life.
updatedSegments: - Eligibility with private insurance: Senior citizens aged 70 years and above, even if covered by private health insurance policies or the Employees' State Insurance (ESI) scheme, are also eligible for benefits under Ayushman Bharat PM-JAY, as approved on September 11, 2024.
Expansion to Senior Citizens (September 11, 2024): All senior citizens aged 70 and above are eligible for health coverage of up to ₹5 lakh per family per year under AB PM-JAY, irrespective of income.
The Ayushman Bharat programme consists of four main pillars:

subsections.title: National Tobacco Control Programme (NTCP)
subsections.content:
INTRODUCTION AND HISTORY

In order to facilitate the implementation of the Tobacco Control Laws, to bring about greater awareness about the harmful effects of tobacco, and to fulfill the obligations under the WHO-Framework convention on tobacco control, the Government of India launched a new National Tobacco Control Programme in the 11th Five Year Plan.

KEY MILESTONES
- 2007-08: Pilot phase was launched in 16 districts covering 9 states.
- The programme has since expanded and now covers 108 districts in 31 states in the country.

CIGARETTE AND OTHER TOBACCO PRODUCTS ACT (COTPA) 2003

A comprehensive tobacco control legislation titled "The Cigarettes and other Tobacco Products (Prohibition of Advertisement and Regulation of Trade and Commerce, Production, Supply and Distribution) Act, 2003" was assented to on 18th May 2003 and notified in the Gazette of India on 25th Feb 2004.

The important provisions of the Act are:
- Prohibition of smoking in public places.
- Prohibition of direct and indirect advertisement of cigarette and other products.
- Prohibition of sale of cigarette and other tobacco products to a person below the age of 18 years.
- Prohibition of sale of tobacco products near educational institutes.
- Mandatory depiction of statutory warnings (including pictorial warnings) on tobacco packs.
- Mandatory depiction of tar and nicotine contents along with maximum permissible limits on tobacco packs.

KEY RULES AND REGULATIONS

- The rules related to the prohibition of smoking in public places came into force from 2nd October 2008. As per rules, it is mandatory to display smoke-free signages at all public places.
- Labelling and packaging rules mandating the depiction of specified health warnings on all tobacco product packs came into force from 31st May 2009. Currently, these warnings cover 85% of the principal display area of the package and are rotated periodically, with new rules effective from June 1, 2025.
- On account of sustained efforts on the part of the Ministry of Health and Family Welfare, all States/UTs issued orders for the implementation of the Food Safety Regulations banning the manufacture, sale, and storage of gutka and pan masala containing tobacco or nicotine by the year 2014-15.
- Several States/UTs have banned all forms of smokeless tobacco products such as chewing tobacco, zarda, khaini, and other flavoured and processed tobacco irrespective of name and form.
- On 24th September 2015, the Government of India notified that the rules on "tobacco pack pictorial warnings" would come into effect from 1st April 2016. These rules mandate the display of pictorial health warnings on 85 per cent of the principle display area of the tobacco product pack on both sides (60 per cent of the picture and 25 per cent of the text).

PROGRAMME COMPONENTS AND STRATEGIES

The main components of the programme are:
1. Public awareness and mass media campaigns for awareness building and for behavioural change.
2. Establishment of tobacco product testing laboratories, to build regulatory capacity, as required under COTPA 2003.
3. Mainstreaming the programme components as a part of the health delivery mechanism under the National Health Mission (NHM) framework.
4. Mainstream research and training on alternate crops and livelihood, with other nodal ministries.
5. Monitoring and evaluation, including surveillance, e.g., adult tobacco surveys.
6. Dedicated tobacco control cells at National, State, and District levels for effective implementation and monitoring of anti-tobacco initiatives.
7. Training of health and social workers, NGOs, school teachers, etc.
8. School programme.
9. Provision of tobacco cessation facilities.
updatedSegments: - Labelling and packaging rules mandating the depiction of specified health warnings on all tobacco product packs came into force from 31st May 2009. Currently, these warnings cover 85% of the principal display area of the package and are rotated periodically, with new rules effective from June 1, 2025.

subsections.title: National Programme for Healthcare of the Elderly (NPHCE)
subsections.content:
INTRODUCTION AND HISTORY

The Government of India launched the National Programme for Health Care of the Elderly (NPHCE) to address health-related problems of elderly people. The programme initially identified districts in 21 states during the 11th Five Year Plan period to implement its strategies and interventions.

AIMS AND OBJECTIVES

- To provide separate, specialized, and comprehensive healthcare to senior citizens at various levels of the state healthcare delivery system, including outreach services.
- To ensure preventive and promotive care, and management of illness.
- To focus on health manpower development for geriatric services.
- To provide medical rehabilitation and therapeutic intervention.
- To carry out Information, Education, and Communication (IEC) activities.

PROGRAMME INFRASTRUCTURE AND HEALTHCARE LEVELS

The details of the geriatric setup and activities undertaken at various healthcare levels are as follows:

1. National Centres of Ageing (NCA)
- 2 National Centres of Ageing are being established at AIIMS, New Delhi and Madras Medical College, Chennai.
- Core functions include training of health professionals, research activity, and healthcare delivery in the field of geriatrics.

2. Regional Geriatric Centres
- 8 regional geriatric centres have been developed as referral units in different regions of the country.
- It is expected to cover other districts in a phased manner; 19 Regional Geriatric Centres in medical colleges and two National Centres of Aging have been established under the programme.

3. Department of Geriatric at 20 Super Specialized Institutions
- Geriatric Departments are being developed at 8 identified medical institutions located in various regions of the country, with a 30-bedded in-patient facility.
- Apart from providing referral treatment, research, and manpower development, these institutions are involved in developing and updating training materials for various levels of health functionaries, and developing IEC material and guidelines.
- Funds have been provided for manpower, equipments, medicines, and construction of buildings.

4. Geriatric Unit at District Hospitals
- The programme was approved for implementation in 2017-2018 in 520 districts, covering 35 states.
- Provision exists for establishing 10-bedded geriatric wards and dedicated OPD services exclusively for geriatric patients.
- Grant-in-aid is provided for contractual manpower, equipments, medicines, construction of buildings, and training.

5. Rehabilitation Units at Community Health Centres (CHCs)
- In identified districts, there is a provision for dedicated health clinics for elderly persons twice a week.
- A rehabilitation unit is being set up at all the CHCs falling under identified districts.
- A dedicated rehabilitation worker is supposed to provide physiotherapy to needy elderly persons.

6. Activity at Primary Health Centres (PHCs)
- Weekly geriatric clinics are arranged at the identified PHCs by a trained Medical Officer.
- For diseases needing further investigation and treatment, persons are referred to the first referral unit (CHC or District Hospital) as per need.
- A one-time grant is given to PHCs for the procurement of equipment.

7. Activity at Sub-Centre Level
- The ANMs and Male Health Workers posted in sub-centres make domiciliary visits to elderly persons in their jurisdiction.
- They arrange suitable calipers and supportive devices from the PHC and provide them to elderly disabled persons to make them ambulatory.
- There is a provision for the treatment of minor ailments and rehabilitation equipment at the identified sub-centres.

RECENT UPDATES

- The tertiary component of the programme has been renamed as Rashtriya Varishth Jan Swasthya Yojana (RVJSY).
updatedSegments: - It is expected to cover other districts in a phased manner; 19 Regional Geriatric Centres in medical colleges and two National Centres of Aging have been established under the programme.

subsections.title: National Programme for Prevention and Management of Trauma and Burn Injuries (NPPMTBI)
subsections.content:
INTRODUCTION

The National Programme for Prevention and Management of Trauma and Burn Injuries (NPPMTBI) addresses two major causes of mortality, morbidity, and disability in India: road traffic crashes and burn injuries. The programme operates through two distinct components.

A. TRAUMA CARE COMPONENT

BACKGROUND AND MILESTONES
- Road traffic crashes are one of the major causes of disability, morbidity, and mortality. As per the Road Accident Report for 2019, a total of 4,49,002 accidents took place in the country leading to 1,51,113 deaths and 4,51,361 injuries.
- 9th and 10th Five Year Plans: The programme for trauma care started as a "Pilot Project for Strengthening Emergency Facilities along the Highways".
- 11th Five Year Plan: The programme was approved as "Assistance for Capacity Building for Developing Trauma Care Facilities in Government Hospitals on National Highways" for developing a network of trauma care facilities (TCFs) along the Golden Quadrilateral highway corridor as well as North-South & East-West Corridors. 116 TCFs were approved in 17 States on these corridors with 100 percent central assistance.
- 12th Five Year Plan: The scheme was further extended as "Capacity Building for Developing Trauma Care Facilities in Govt. Hospitals on National Highways" wherein 85 Trauma Care Facilities were identified and approved for financial assistance.

OBJECTIVES
- To establish a network of Trauma Care Facilities on National/State Highways in order to reduce the incidence of preventable deaths and disabilities due to road traffic accidents by observing the golden hour principle.
- To develop Surveillance, Trauma Registry, and Capacity Building Centre for collection, compilation, analysis, and dissemination of information for policy formulation and preventive interventions.
- To improve the awareness about trauma care among the general masses and vulnerable groups by developing and disseminating IEC material.

B. BURN INJURIES COMPONENT

BACKGROUND AND EPIDEMIOLOGY
- India has one of the largest burdens of burns with an estimated 7,000,000 burn injuries per year, an estimated 1.4 lakh deaths, and 2.4 lakh disabilities.
- Burn injuries are the second largest group of injuries after road accidents. This translates to a mortality rate of over 8.3 / 100,000 population, and a loss of 5 million disability-adjusted life years (DALYs).
- This high incidence has been attributed to illiteracy, poverty, and low safety consciousness in the population.
- The epidemiology of mortality after burns is unique in India; most burn injuries are in young adults and deaths are more common in women than men, contrasting trends observed in other low-and middle-income countries.

MILESTONES AND OBJECTIVES
- 11th Five Year Plan (2010): A pilot programme on burn care was initiated by the Ministry of Health & Family Welfare in the name of "Pilot Programme for Prevention of Burn Injuries" (PPPBI) with a total budget of Rs. 29 crore.
- It was initially implemented in 3 Medical Colleges and six District Hospitals.
- The goal of PPPBI was to ensure the prevention of burn injuries, provide timely and adequate treatment in case of burn injuries to reduce mortality, complications, and ensuing disabilities, and to provide effective rehabilitative interventions if disability has set in.
- 12th Five Year Plan: The pilot project continued as a full-fledged programme for the tertiary care level.
- Financial assistance towards the District Hospital component was undertaken under the National Health Mission (NHM). The scheme is being extended since then.
updatedSegments: - 11th Five Year Plan (2010): A pilot programme on burn care was initiated by the Ministry of Health & Family Welfare in the name of "Pilot Programme for Prevention of Burn Injuries" (PPPBI) with a total budget of Rs. 29 crore.

- 12th Five Year Plan: The scheme was further extended as "Capacity Building for Developing Trauma Care Facilities in Govt. Hospitals on National Highways" wherein 85 Trauma Care Facilities were identified and approved for financial assistance.

subsections.title: National Water Supply and Sanitation Programme & Swachh Bharat Mission
subsections.content:
INTRODUCTION AND HISTORY

The National Water Supply and Sanitation Programme was initiated in 1954 with the objective of providing safe water supply and adequate drainage facilities for the entire urban and rural population of the country.

In 1972, a supplement to the national water supply and sanitation programme known as the Accelerated Rural Water Supply Programme was started. During the Fifth Five Year Plan, rural water supply was included in the Minimum Needs Programme of the State Plans. The Central Government supported the efforts of the States in identifying problem villages.

ACCELERATED RURAL WATER SUPPLY PROGRAMME (ARWSP)

A "problem village" was defined as one where no source of safe water is available within a distance of 1.6 km, or where water is available at a depth of more than 15 metres, or where the water source has excess salinity, iron, fluorides and other toxic elements, or where water is exposed to the risk of cholera.

The stipulated norm of water supply was 40 litres per capita per day (lpcd), and at least one hand pump/spot-source for every 250 persons. The programme was subsequently renamed as the Rajiv Gandhi National Drinking Water Mission in 1991.

In 1999-2000, the Sector Reform Project was started to involve the community in planning, implementation and management of drinking water schemes, which was in 2002 scaled up as the Swajaldhara Programme.

SWAJALDHARA AND BHARAT NIRMAN

Swajaldhara was launched on 25th December 2002 as a community-led participatory programme aiming at providing safe drinking water in rural areas with full community ownership. It had two streams: Swajaldhara-I and Swajaldhara-II.
- Swajaldhara I (First Dhara): For a gram panchayat or a group of panchayats at the block/tahsil level.
- Swajaldhara II (Second Dhara): With the district as the project area.

The programme was revised from 1st April 2009 and named as the National Rural Drinking Water Programme (NRDWP).

Bharat Nirman was launched in 2005 to build rural infrastructure. Phase-I was implemented from 2005-06 to 2008-09, and Phase-II from 2009-10 to 2011-12. Priority was given to cover water quality problems and habitations affected by arsenic, fluoride, iron, salinity, and nitrate.

New initiatives in the 12th Five Year Plan included the enhancement of service levels for rural water supply from the norm of 40 lpcd to 55 lpcd for designing systems. The target was at least 50 per cent of the rural population having access to water within their household premises or within 100 metres radius, and at least 30 per cent having individual household connections (up from 13 per cent).

THE NAL JAL YOJANA (JAL JEEVAN MISSION)

The Jal Jeevan Mission (JJM) is a programme that aims to provide every rural household with access to safe and reliable tap water, initially by 2024, and subsequently extended till 2028.

- Goal: To provide every rural household with a Functional Household Tap Connection (FHTC) that delivers at least 55 liters of tap water per person per day, meeting prescribed quality standards, with an extended target of 2028.

RURAL SANITATION PROGRAMME AND NIRMAL BHARAT ABHIYAN

In 2012, a paradigm shift was made in the Total Sanitation Campaign by launching the Nirmal Bharat Abhiyan (NBA) in the 12th Five Year Plan. 

- Objective: To achieve sustainable behavioural change with the provision of sanitary facilities in entire communities in a phased manner, adopting a saturation mode with "Nirmal Grams" as outcomes.

SWACHH BHARAT MISSION (SBM)

Swachh Bharat Abhiyan or Swachh Bharat Mission is a national campaign by the Government of India to clean streets, roads, and infrastructure of the country. It was officially launched by the Prime Minister of India on 2nd October 2014 at Rajghat, New Delhi.

- Aim: To eradicate open defecation by year 2019 by constructing over 100 million toilets in rural India.
- Sub-missions: Swachh Bharat Mission Gramin and Swachh Bharat Mission Urban.

SWACHH BHARAT MISSION GRAMIN (SBM-G)

- Objectives: Bring about an improvement in the general quality of life in rural areas, accelerate sanitation coverage to achieve the vision of Swachh Bharat by 2nd October 2019, motivate communities and Panchayati Raj Institutions, encourage cost-effective technologies, and develop community-managed sanitation systems focusing on solid and liquid waste management.
- Components: Start-up activities, IEC and capacity building, construction of household toilets, and construction of community sanitary complexes.
- School Sanitation: Construction of toilets in government schools and anganwadi centres (focusing on separate toilets for girls and boys) implemented by the Ministry of Education and Ministry of Women and Child Development.
- Incentives: Provided for the construction of household toilets for Below Poverty Line (BPL) households, and restricted Above Poverty Line (APL) households including SCs/STs, small and marginal farmers, landless labourers, physically handicapped, and women-headed families.

SWACHH BHARAT MISSION URBAN (SBM-U)

Implemented by the Ministry of Urban Development. 

- Objectives: Elimination of open defecation, eradication of manual scavenging, modern and scientific municipal solid waste management, effecting behavioural change, generating awareness, capacity augmentation for Urban Local Bodies (ULBs), and creating an enabling environment for private sector participation in Capital Expenditure (Capex) and Operation and Maintenance (Opex).
- Mission Components: Household toilets (including conversion of insanitary latrines into pour-flush latrines), Community toilets, Public toilets, Solid waste management, IEC and public awareness, and Capacity building and administrative office expenses.
- Public vs. Community Toilets: 
  - Public Toilets are for the floating population/general public in places such as markets, train stations, and tourist places.
  - Community Toilets are shared facilities provided for a group of residents or an entire settlement, used primarily by low-income/informal settlements or slums where household space constraints exist.
- Special Focus Groups: Manual scavengers adequately rehabilitated; informal waste management workers (rag pickers) integrated into formal systems; temporary accommodation provided with toilets for migrants and homeless; mandatory temporary toilets at construction sites; priority accorded to vulnerable sections (pensioners, girl children, pregnant and lactating mothers).
- Target Group for Household Toilets: 80 per cent of urban households engaging in open defecation, all households with insanitary latrines, and all households with single-pit latrines. The remaining 20 per cent practicing open defecation are targeted by community toilets due to space constraints.

CLEAN TOILETS CAMPAIGN 2024

- Over the last 10 years, SBM has brought toilets and sanitation to the centre stage.
- Launched to nudge cleanliness drives across all Community Toilets (CTs) and Public Toilets (PTs).
- Achievements: Over 800 Toilet Blocks retrofitted/upgraded and 400+ new toilet blocks constructed. Over 10,000+ toilets nominated adhering to FACES (Functional, Accessible, Clean, Eco-friendly, Safe) parameters. 1,500+ women-led SHGs completed basic maintenance.
- Clean Toilets Campaign: A 5-week 'Clean Toilets Campaign' was conducted from 19th November (World Toilet Day 2024) to 25th December (Good Governance Day 2024) to transform all 70,000+ Community and Public Toilets across the country in terms of cleaning and maintenance.

SOLID WASTE MANAGEMENT COMPONENT

Municipal Solid Waste Management (MSWM) refers to a systematic process that comprises waste segregation and storage at source, primary collection, secondary storage, transportation, secondary segregation, resource recovery, processing, treatment, and final disposal of solid waste.
updatedSegments: Swajaldhara was launched on 25th December 2002 as a community-led participatory programme aiming at providing safe drinking water in rural areas with full community ownership. It had two streams: Swajaldhara-I and Swajaldhara-II.

subsections.title: Miscellaneous Health and Social Programmes
subsections.content:
NATIONAL GUINEAWORM ERADICATION PROGRAMME

INTRODUCTION AND MILESTONES
- India launched its National Guineaworm Eradication Programme in 1983-84 with technical assistance from the World Health Organization (WHO).
- The programme was integrated into the national health system at the village level, utilizing well-defined strategies, an efficient information and evaluation system, and intersectoral coordination.
- India significantly reduced Guinea Worm Disease (Dracunculiasis) in affected areas and reported its last indigenous case in July 1996.
- In February 2000, the International Commission for the Certification of Dracunculiasis Eradication recommended that India be certified free of dracunculiasis transmission.

CURRENT ONGOING ACTIVITIES
As per the recommendations of the International Certification Team, the following activities are continuing:
- Health education activities with special emphasis on school children and women in rural areas.
- Rumour registration and rumour investigation.
- Maintenance of guinea-worm disease on the list of notifiable diseases and continuation of surveillance in previously infected areas.
- Careful supervision of the functioning of hand pumps and other sources of safe drinking water, and provision of additional units wherever necessary.

YAWS ERADICATION PROGRAMME

INTRODUCTION AND STATUS
- The disease has been reported in India from tribal communities living in hilly forest and difficult-to-reach areas in 49 districts across 10 states (Andhra Pradesh, Assam, Chhattisgarh, Gujarat, Jharkhand, Madhya Pradesh, Maharashtra, Orissa, Tamil Nadu, and Uttar Pradesh).
- The National Institute of Communicable Diseases (NICD) is the nodal agency for planning, guidance, coordination, monitoring, and evaluation of the programme.
- The programme is implemented by the State Health Directorates of Yaws endemic states utilizing the existing health care delivery system, with the coordination of the department of tribal welfare and other related institutions.
- The number of reported cases came down from more than 3,500 to Nil during the period from 1996 to 2004.
- Since then, no new case has been reported.

NATIONAL PROGRAMME FOR CONTROL AND TREATMENT OF OCCUPATIONAL DISEASES

INTRODUCTION
- The Government of India launched a scheme called the National Programme for Control and Treatment of Occupational Diseases in 1998-1999.
- The National Institute of Occupational Health (NIOH), Ahmedabad (ICMR) has been identified as the nodal agency for this programme.

PROPOSED RESEARCH PROJECTS
- Prevention, control, and treatment of silicosis and silico-tuberculosis in the agate industry.
- Occupational health problems of tobacco harvesters and their prevention.
- Hazardous process and chemicals, database generation, documentation, and information dissemination.
- Capacity building to promote research, education, and training at the National Institute of Occupational Disease.
- Health Risk Assessment and development of intervention programmes in cottage industries with high risk of silicosis.
- Prevention and control of occupational health hazards among salt workers in the remote desert areas of Gujarat and Western Rajasthan.

PRADHAN MANTRI AWAS YOJNA (URBAN) - PMAY-U

INTRODUCTION
- The Ministry of Housing and Urban Affairs (MOHUA) launched the Pradhan Mantri Awas Yojna-Urban (PMAY-U) on 25th June 2015 as a flagship mission of the Government of India.
- It addresses urban housing shortages among the Economically Weaker Section (EWS) and Low Income Group (LIG) categories, including slum dwellers, by ensuring a pucca house to eligible urban households.

ELIGIBILITY AND FEATURES
- An eligible beneficiary family comprises a husband, wife, unmarried sons, and unmarried daughters.
- The beneficiary family should not own a Pakka House (an all-weather dwelling unit) either in his/her name or in the name of any member of his/her family in any part of India.
- An adult earning member (irrespective of marital status) can be treated as a separate household.
- The programme adopts a demand-driven approach wherein the housing shortage is decided based on demand assessment by States/Union Territories.
- All houses built or acquired or purchased under the mission have basic amenities like kitchen, water supply, electricity, and toilet.
- Promotes women empowerment by providing the ownership of houses in the name of female members or in joint name.
- Preference is given to women (with overriding preference to widows and single women), Scheduled Caste/Scheduled Tribes/Other Backward Classes, Minorities, Persons with disability, and Transgender individuals.

MINIMUM NEEDS PROGRAMME (MNP)

INTRODUCTION
- Introduced in the first year of the Fifth Five Year Plan (1974-78).
- Objective: To provide certain basic minimum needs and thereby improve the living standards of the people.
- It is the expression of the commitment of the government for the social and economic development of the community, particularly the underprivileged and underserved populations.

COMPONENTS OF THE PROGRAMME
- Rural Health
- Rural Water Supply
- Rural Electrification
- Elementary Education
- Adult Education
- Nutrition
- Environmental improvement of Urban Slums
- Houses for landless labourers

BASIC PRINCIPLES
- Facilities under MNP are to be provided first to those areas which are at present underserved so as to remove disparities between different areas.
- Facilities under MNP should be provided as a package to an area through intersectoral area projects to have a greater impact.

HEALTH AND NUTRITION OBJECTIVES
- Rural Health (as per National Health Mission norms): One PHC for 30,000 population in plains and 20,000 population in tribal/hilly areas. One sub-centre for a population of 5,000 people in the plains and for 3,000 in tribal/hilly areas. One community health centre (rural hospital) for a population of 1,20,000 in plains and 80,000 in tribal/hilly areas.
- Nutrition: Extend nutrition support to 11 million eligible persons, expand the special nutrition programme to all ICDS projects, and consolidate the mid-day meal programme and link it to health, potable water, and sanitation.

20-POINT PROGRAMME

INTRODUCTION
- In addition to the Five Year Plans and Programmes, in 1975, the Government of India initiated a special activity called the 20-Point programme.
- It is described as an agenda for national action to promote social justice and economic growth.
- Restructured on August 20, 1986, and again in 2006.
- Objectives: Eradication of poverty, raising productivity, reducing inequalities, removing social and economic disparities, and improving the quality of life.

THE 20 POINTS (2006)
1. Poverty eradication
2. Power to people
3. Support to farmers
4. Labour welfare
5. Food security
6. Housing for all
7. Clean drinking water
8. Health for all
9. Education for all
