- Nutrition program for Adolescent girls: Targets undernourished adolescent girls in age group 11 to 19 years. 6 kg of food grains are provided to each beneficiary per month.
- Indra Gandhi Matritva Yojna: Cash transfer is made to pregnant and lactating women in order to improve their nutritional and health status.
- Sneha Shivir: Targeted for the Malnourished child.
- Anganwadi Karyakarta Bima Yojna: Health insurance scheme for Anganwadi workers.

INTEGRATED MANAGEMENT OF NEONATAL AND CHILDHOOD ILLNESS (IMNCI)
- IMNCI is the Indian version of IMCI (Integrated Management of Childhood Illness).
- IMCI was developed by WHO and UNICEF targeting children less than 5 years.
- Major IMNCI adaptations in India:
  - Includes early neonates of 0 to 7 days of age.
  - Emphasis is on sick neonates, as compared to sick older children.
  - Incorporating national guidelines on Malaria, Anemia, Vitamin A supplementation and Immunization schedule.
  - Training of health workers.
- According to IMNCI, the term Young Infants includes children below 2 months of age.
- 3 Strategies:
  - Improvement of Case management skills of health staff.
  - Improvements in the Health system.
  - Improvements in family and community Health practices.
- Preventive component: Breast feeding and Nutritional counseling, Vitamin A and Iron supplementation, Immunization and Treatment of Helminthic infestation.
- Curative component: Integrated case management of most common childhood problems. (Mnemonic DM - MAM: Diarrhea, Malnutrition, Measles, ARI/pneumonia, Malaria).

CASE MANAGEMENT STEPS IN IMNCI
- i. Assess the child: Check for danger signs, possible bacterial infection, main symptoms, and examine nutrition and immunization status.
- ii. Classify illness: Uses a color-coded triage system.
- iii. Identify treatment.
- iv. Treat the child: Administer oral drugs, feed, and give fluids.
- v. Counsel mother.
- vi. Give follow up care.

INTEGRATED CASE MANAGEMENT PROCESS
1. Check for danger Signs: Inability to breast feed / drink, Persistent vomiting, Severe malnutrition, Unconsciousness / lethargy, Convulsions.
2. Assess main Symptoms: Fever, Ear problems (Otitis media), Difficulty in breathing, Diarrhea.
3. Classify condition using Color-Coded Triage:
  - Pink (NOT RED): Urgent referral and Emergency treatment in IPD.
  - Yellow: Treatment at OPD by giving oral drugs.
  - Green: Home management can be done by Treating local infections at home.

F-IMNCI (FACILITY BASED IMNCI)
- Integration of IMNCI package and Facility-based care package into one.
- Added component: Asphyxia management and Care of sick newborn at the facility level.
- Helps in Skill building of Medical officers and Staff nurses posted in facilities to manage newborn and childhood illness.

JANANI SURAKSHA YOJNA (JSY)
- The National Maternity Benefit Scheme was modified into Janani Suraksha Yojna (JSY) in 2005.
- Objectives: Reducing Maternal mortality and Infant mortality BY encouraging Institutional Delivery.
- Salient features: 
  - 100 percent Centrally Sponsored scheme.
  - Under NRHM, it integrates the benefit of Cash Assistance (to Mother and ASHA) with institutional care during antenatal care, delivery care and immediate post-partum care.
- Cash Assistance structure:
  - Low Performing State (Rural): Mother 1400, ASHA 600.
  - Low Performing State (Urban): Mother 1000, ASHA 400.
  - High Performing State (Rural): Mother 700, ASHA 600.
  - High Performing State (Urban): Mother 600, ASHA 400.
- Eligibility criteria:
  - Age greater than 20 years.
  - NOT more than 2 live children.
  - Low performing states (LPS): Any woman with Institutional Delivery (either BPL or not BPL).
  - High performing states (HPS): Only Below Poverty Line (BPL) women with institutional delivery.
- ASHA should get her money after her post-natal visit to the beneficiary and when the child has been immunized for BCG.
- Yojna subsidizes the cost of caesarean section and for management of obstetric complications upto 1500 per delivery.

JANANI SHISHU SURAKSHA KARYAKRAM (JSSK)
- Objectives: To promote Public Health Facility utilization.
- Beneficiaries: Pregnant Women and Infants.
- Facilities provided for pregnant women:
  - Free Delivery and Caesarian section.
  - Free Drugs and Diet upto 3 days during Normal delivery and 7 days in Caesarian section.
  - Free Diagnostics and Blood transfusion.
  - Free Transport from home to institution and back to home.
- Similar facilities are provided to all sick Newborns for treatment till 30 days after birth.

RASHTRIYA KISHOR SWASTHYA KARYAKRAM (RKSK)
- India's first comprehensive Adolescent Health Programme.
- Target group: Adolescent 10 to 19 years age in urban and rural areas (includes Girls and Boys, Married or Unmarried, Poor or Affluent, School or Out of school).
- Strategy: RMNCH+A.
- Strategic priorities: Nutrition, Mental health, Substance misuse, Sexual and Reproductive health, NCD's, Injuries and Violence.
- Approach: Peer to Peer approach / Sathiya approach.
- 7 components: Clinics, Content, Communication, Counseling, Communities, Convergence and Coverage.

NAVJAAT SHISHU SURAKSHA KARYAKRAM (NSSK)
- Basic Newborn Care and Resuscitation Program.
- Administered by Ministry of Health and Family Welfare (MoHFW) under NRHM.
- Launched for prevention of Hypothermia and Infection.
- Aim: To Train health personnel in basic newborn care and resuscitation.
- Objective: To have a trained health person in basic Newborn care and Resuscitation unit at every delivery point.

RASHTRIYA BAL SWASTHYA KARYAKRAM (RBSK)
- Launched in 2013.
- Target group: 
  - 0 to 6 years children (Rural areas and Urban slums).
  - 6 to 18 years children (enrolled in Government hospitals and schools).
- Provision for Child health screening and Early intervention services programme (under NRHM) through early detection and management of 4 D's.
- The 4 D's cover 34 conditions:
  - I. Defects at birth: NTD, Cleft Lip, Cleft Palate, Club foot, Congenital heart disease, Congenital deafness, Congenital cataract, Developmental dysplasia of hip, Retinopathy of prematurity.
  - II. Diseases of childhood: Dental caries, Otitis media, Rheumatic heart disease, Reactive airway disease, Scabies, Eczema, Fungal infections, Convulsive disorders.
  - III. Deficiencies: Anemia, Vitamin A deficiency (Bitot's spot), Vitamin D deficiency (Rickets), Severe Acute Malnutrition (SAM), Goiter.
  - IV. Disabilities and Developmental delays: Vision impairment, Hearing impairment, Neuro-motor impairment, Motor delay, Language delay, Cognitive delay, Behavior disorder (Autism), Learning disorder, Attention Deficit Hyperactivity Disorder (ADHD), Congenital hyperthyroidism, Sickle cell anemia, Thalassemia.

PROGRAMME IMPLEMENTATION UNDER RBSK
1. For Newborns:
  - Facility based newborn screening at public health facilities (Screening of birth defects in institutional deliveries by ANM, MO, Gynecologist).
  - Community based newborn screening through ASHA during Home visits (ASHA mobilizes caregivers to attend the local Anganwadi centres for screening, giving priority to children with LBW and children from households with known chronic illness).
2. For Children 6 weeks to 6 years:
  - Anganwadi centre-based screening.
3. For Children 6 years to 18 years:
  - Government aided school-based screening.
  - Screening is conducted at Anganwadi centre (Twice a year) and at School (Once a year).
  - At least 2 dedicated Mobile health teams are assigned in each block.
  - Suggested Mobile health team composition: 2 AYUSH Medical Officers (1 male, 1 female), 1 ANM / staff nurse, and 1 Pharmacist.

title: Health Care of the Community
content:
OVERVIEW OF THE CHAPTER
This chapter outlines the sprawling architecture of the healthcare delivery system in India. It highlights the historical committees that shaped the system, the population norms for health centers, and the vital roles played by grassroots workers in achieving universal health coverage.

DEFINITIONS
PRIMARY HEALTH CARE: Essential health care made universally accessible to individuals and acceptable to them, through their full participation and at a cost the community and country can afford.
FIRST REFERRAL UNIT (FRU): A facility equipped to provide round-the-clock services for emergency obstetric care, newborn care, and blood storage. Usually a Community Health Centre (CHC).

CORE CONCEPTS
MAJOR HEALTH COMMITTEES:
- BHORE COMMITTEE (1946): The Health Survey and Development Committee. Proposed the 3 million plan and heavily emphasized the integration of preventive and curative services at all administrative levels.
- MUDALIAR COMMITTEE (1962): The Health Survey and Planning Committee. Focused on consolidating the gains of the early five-year plans rather than massive expansion.
- CHADHA COMMITTEE (1963): Recommended that basic health workers should visit 10,000 population to conduct malaria vigilance and family planning duties.
- KARTAR SINGH COMMITTEE (1973): The Committee on Multipurpose Workers. Replaced separate unipurpose workers (like malaria or family planning workers) with unified Multipurpose Health Workers.
- SHRIVASTAV COMMITTEE (1975): Group on Medical Education and Support Manpower. Recommended the creation of the Village Health Guide scheme to bring healthcare to the doorstep.
RURAL HEALTH INFRASTRUCTURE NORMS:
- SUB-CENTRE: Serves a population of 5000 in plains and 3000 in hilly/tribal/difficult areas. Manned by a Health Worker Female (ANM) and Health Worker Male.
- PRIMARY HEALTH CENTRE (PHC): Serves a population of 30,000 in plains and 20,000 in hilly/tribal areas. It is the first port of call to a qualified medical officer.
- COMMUNITY HEALTH CENTRE (CHC): Serves a population of 1,20,000 in plains and 80,000 in hilly/tribal areas. Requires four core specialists: Surgeon, Physician, Obstetrician/Gynecologist, and Pediatrician.

GRASSROOTS WORKERS:
- ASHA (ACCREDITED SOCIAL HEALTH ACTIVIST): A female resident of the village, married/widowed/divorced, aged 25 to 45 years. Norm is generally 1 ASHA per 1000 population. Acts as the primary bridge between the community and the health system.
- ANGANWADI WORKER: Operates under the ICDS scheme, providing supplementary nutrition, non-formal preschool education, and health education to a population of 1000.

KEY POINTS
The Bhore Committee laid the foundational blueprint for India's post-independence health system.
Ayushman Arogya Mandirs (formerly Health and Wellness Centres) represent the upgrading of Sub-Centres and PHCs to deliver Comprehensive Primary Health Care (CPHC) beyond just maternal and child health.

FORMULAS AND CALCULATIONS
NOT APPLICABLE FOR THIS CHAPTER.

MNEMONICS
POPULATION NORMS FOR PLAINS (5, 30, 120):
SUB-CENTRE: 5 THOUSAND
PHC: 30 THOUSAND
CHC: 120 THOUSAND

subsections.title: General Concepts and Classification of Foods
subsections.content:
OVERVIEW OF THE CHAPTER

Nutrition may be defined as the science of food and its relationship to health. It is concerned primarily with the part played by nutrients in body growth, development, and maintenance. Dietetics is the practical application of the principles of nutrition, which includes the planning of meals for the well and the sick.

DEFINITIONS

- Nutrient: A "food factor" used for specific dietary constituents such as proteins, vitamins, and minerals. Nutrients are organic and inorganic complexes contained in food.
- Good Nutrition: Maintaining a nutritional status that enables us to grow well and enjoy good health.

CLASSIFICATION OF FOODS

Foods can be classified using four major parameters:
1. By Origin:
  - Foods of animal origin.
  - Foods of vegetable origin.
2. By Chemical Composition:
  - Proteins.
  - Fats.
  - Carbohydrates.
  - Vitamins.
  - Minerals.
3. By Predominant Function:
  - Body-building foods: e.g., milk, meat, poultry, fish, eggs, pulses, nuts, and oil seeds.
  - Energy-giving foods: e.g., cereals, sugars, roots and tubers, fats, and oils.
  - Protective foods: e.g., vegetables, fruits, milk.
4. By Nutritive Value:
  - Cereals and millets.
  - Pulses (legumes).
  - Vegetables.
  - Nuts and oilseeds.
  - Fruits.
  - Animal foods.
  - Fats and oils.
  - Sugar and jaggery.
  - Condiments and spices.
  - Miscellaneous foods.

MACRONUTRIENTS AND MICRONUTRIENTS

There are about 50 different nutrients normally supplied through the foods we eat. They are broadly divided into:

Macronutrients
- These form the main bulk of food and are often called "Proximate Principles".
- They include Proteins, Fats, and Carbohydrates.
- In the Indian dietary context, they contribute to the total energy intake in the following recommended proportions for a balanced diet:
  - Proteins: 10 to 15 percent.
  - Fats: 15 to 30 percent.
  - Carbohydrates: 50 to 70 percent.

Micronutrients
- These are nutrients required in very small amounts (from fractions of a milligram to several grams).
- They include Vitamins and Minerals.
- Key minerals and trace elements include Sodium, Potassium, Magnesium, Calcium, Phosphorus, Iron, Iodine, Fluorine, Zinc, Copper, Cobalt, Selenium, Chromium, Manganese, Molybdenum, Nickel, Tin, Silicon, and Vanadium.

TRACE CONTAMINANTS

- These elements have NO functional role in the human body and are considered contaminants.
- Examples include Lead, Mercury, Barium, Boron, and Aluminum.

CALORIC YIELDS OF NUTRIENTS

- Proteins: Yields 4.2 Kcal per gram (or 17 Kj per gram).
- Carbohydrates: Yields 4.1 Kcal per gram (or 17 Kj per gram).
- Fats: Yields 9 Kcal per gram (or 37 Kj per gram).
- Alcohol: Yields 7 Kcal per gram (or ml).

RICH SOURCES OF NUTRIENTS

- Halibut liver oil: Richest source of Vitamin A and Vitamin D.
- Cod liver oil: Rich in Vitamin D, but the absolute richest source is Halibut liver oil.
- Gingelly seeds: Richest source of Vitamin B1 (Thiamine).
- Sheep liver: Richest source of Vitamin B2 (Riboflavin).
- Indian Gooseberry (Amla): Richest source of Vitamin C.
- Ragi (Millet): Richest source of Calcium.
- Dried Pumpkin seeds: Richest source of Iron (greater than Pistachio).

POOR SOURCES OF NUTRIENTS

- Meat: Poor source of Calcium.
- Fish: Poor source of Carbohydrate and Iodine (except some sea fish).
- Egg: Poor source of Carbohydrate and Vitamin C.
- Milk: Poor source of Vitamin C and Iron.
- Butter: Poor source of Iron.
- Rice: Poor source of Calcium, Iron, Vitamin A, Vitamin C, Vitamin D, and Vitamin B1 (Thiamine).

SPECIFIC FOOD FACTS AND PRINCIPLES

- Rice protein is better than other cereals because rice is rich in Lysine.
- A 60 gm Egg contains approximately 6 gm of Protein, 6 gm of Fat, and 30 mg of Calcium.
- Dates are rich in Iron and Calcium.
- Tomatoes are rich in Citric Acid.
- Banana is a good source of Vitamin A, Vitamin B6, Potassium, Phosphorus, Fiber, Carbohydrate, and Energy.
- Fermentation of Pulses increases the content of Vitamin B1, B2, B3, B9, and B12 (Thiamine, Riboflavin, Niacin, Biotin, and Cyanocobalamin).
- White Polished Rice causes a deficiency of Vitamin B1 (Thiamine), which can lead to Beriberi.
- Regular drinking of Cranberry juice prevents Urinary Tract Infection (UTI).
- Vegans are individuals who NEITHER eat Dairy products NOR Eggs.
- Iron (Fe) is the trace element which cannot be adequately supplemented solely by diet during pregnancy.
- Biologically complete protein contains all essential amino acids in amounts corresponding to human needs (e.g., milk and egg proteins). Vegetable proteins are often rated lower as they may lack one or more essential amino acids.

subsections.title: Proteins and Protein Assessment
subsections.content:
OVERVIEW OF PROTEINS

Proteins are of the greatest importance in human nutrition. The word "protein" implies "that which is of first importance."
- They are complex organic nitrogenous compounds.
- They are composed of carbon, hydrogen, oxygen, nitrogen, and sulphur in varying amounts. Some also contain phosphorus and iron, and occasionally other elements.
- The key differentiating factor of proteins from carbohydrates and fats is that they contain nitrogen (usually amounting to about 16 percent).
- Proteins constitute about 20 percent of the body weight in an adult.
- Functions: Proteins are needed for body building, repair and maintenance of body tissues, maintenance of osmotic pressure, and the synthesis of substances like antibodies, plasma proteins, haemoglobin, enzymes, hormones, and coagulation factors.
- Cell-mediated immune response and bactericidal activity of leukocytes are severely lowered in protein energy malnutrition.
- Proteins can supply energy (4 Kcal/g) when caloric intake is inadequate, but this is a wasteful use of proteins.

AMINO ACIDS

Proteins are made up of smaller units called amino acids. There are about 20 amino acids stated to be needed by the human body.

ESSENTIAL AMINO ACIDS (EAA)
- EAA are amino acids which are not synthesized in the human body in adequate amounts and have to be supplemented from outside (diet) to prevent deficiency.
- New tissues cannot be formed unless all essential amino acids are present in the diet.
- There are 10 Essential Amino Acids:
  1. Leucine
  2. Threonine
  3. Lysine
  4. Histidine (Semi-essential, though recent evidence shows it is essential even for adults)
  5. Arginine (Semi-essential)
  6. Isoleucine
  7. Tryptophan
  8. Phenylalanine
  9. Valine
  10. Methionine
- Specific physiological roles: Methionine acts as a donor of methyl groups for the synthesis of choline, folates, and nucleic acids. Tryptophan forms niacin. Cystine and Tyrosine are particularly essential for premature babies.

NON-ESSENTIAL AMINO ACIDS
- These can be synthesized by the body using other building blocks.
- They include asparagine, serine, glutamic acid, proline, and glycine.

CONDITIONALLY ESSENTIAL AMINO ACIDS (CEAA)
- This occurs when Non-Essential Amino Acids turn into Essential Amino Acids because their precursors are limited in the body.
- Examples: Tyrosine, Cystine.

LIMITING AMINO ACIDS (LAA)
- LAA are amino acids which are deficient in a specific food item's protein.
- Cereals: Deficient in Lysine and Threonine (Wheat) and Lysine and Tryptophan (Maize).
- Pulses: Deficient in Methionine and Cysteine.

SUPPLEMENTARY ACTION OF PROTEINS
- Man derives protein from a variety of sources. Cereal proteins are deficient in lysine and threonine, while pulse proteins are deficient in methionine.
- Due to consumption of a particular type of food (e.g., Cereals only), a deficiency of its Limiting Amino Acid develops.
- When two or more food items are Eaten Together (e.g., Cereals + Pulses in a rice-dal combination), their proteins supplement one another to provide a protein comparable to animal protein in respect to EAA. This is known as the supplementary action of proteins.

ASSESSMENT OF PROTEIN NUTRITION STATUS

- The best current measure of the state of protein nutrition is serum albumin concentration.
- Normal level: Should be more than 3.5 g/dl.
- Mild degree of malnutrition: Level of 3.5 g/dl.
- Severe malnutrition: Level of 3.0 g/dl.
- Other tests include: Arm muscle circumference, creatinine-height index, total body nitrogen, and serum transferrin (assesses the ability of the liver to synthesize proteins).

PROTEIN REQUIREMENTS

- Expressed in terms of body weight.
- The ICMR Expert Group (2020) recommended 0.83 g protein/kg body weight per day for an adult (assuming a median obligatory nitrogen loss of 48 mg/kg body weight).

METHODS FOR ASSESSMENT OF PROTEIN QUANTITY

1. Protein Energy Ratio (PE Ratio):
- It evaluates what percent of the energy value in a food is supplied by its protein content.
- If the PE is less than 4 percent, the subject will be unable to eat enough to satisfy protein requirements.
- The ICMR recommends that protein should account for approximately 10 to 12 percent of the total daily energy intake.

METHODS FOR ASSESSMENT OF PROTEIN QUALITY

1. Amino Acid Score (AAS):
- Measures indispensable amino acids in a test protein and compares its values with a reference protein.
- The score is somewhere between 50 and 60 for starches, and 70 and 80 for animal foods.

2. Protein Digestibility Corrected Amino Acid Score (PDCAAS):
- It is the Amino Acid Score with an added Digestibility Component.

3. Digestible Indispensable Amino Acid Score (DIAAS):
- This is the best and most refined indicator for Protein Quality.
- The FAO recommended DIAAS to replace PDCAAS as the preferred method of measuring Protein Quality.

4. Protein Efficiency Ratio (PER):
- Ratio of Weight Gain to the amount of Protein Consumed.

5. Biological Value (BV):
- Amount of Nitrogen Retained in comparison to the amount of Nitrogen Absorbed.

6. Net Protein Utilization (NPU):
- The proportion of ingested Protein that is retained in the body under specified conditions for the growth and maintenance of tissue.
- It is a better assessment of protein quality than biological value (NPU > BV) because it incorporates both digestibility and biological value.
- BV and NPU methods reflect both Availability and Digestibility.
- NPU is considered the best indicator of Protein Quality (by ICMR) for recommending Dietary Protein Requirements.
- Value ranges from 0 to 1. A value of 0 indicates that none of the Nitrogen supplied was converted to Protein. A value of 1 indicates 100% utilization of dietary Nitrogen as Protein.
- The protein requirement varies inversely with the NPU of the dietary protein. The NPU of Indian diets varies between 50 and 80.
- NPU Values of specific foods:
  - Soyabean: 61%
  - Rice: 65%
  - Milk: 75%
  - Meat: 80%
  - Egg: 100% (highest). The NPU of Egg is actually 96 (its maximum Biological Value), but since it's used as a Reference Protein, its NPU is taken as 100 for comparison.

FORMULAS AND CALCULATIONS

- Protein Energy Ratio (PE) = (Energy from Protein / Total Energy In Diet) x 100
- Amino Acid Score = (mg of amino acid per g of test protein / mg of the same amino acid per g of reference protein) x 100
- DIAAS = (Digestible Dietary Indispensable Amino Acid (mg) in 1 gm of Dietary Protein / Same Dietary Indispensable Amino Acid (mg) in 1 gm of Reference Protein) x 100
- NPU = (Nitrogen Retained / Nitrogen Intake) x 100
- NPU = (Biological Value x Digestibility) / 100
- Nitrogen-to-Protein Conversion: 1 gm of Nitrogen is assumed to be equivalent to 6.25 gm of Protein.

MNEMONICS

- Essential Amino Acids: "Lucifer Three Lies......His (Semi-Essential) Urge Is...........Try Phenyl Wali Methi"
  - Lucifer: Leucine
  - Three: Threonine
  - Lies: Lysine
  - His: Histidine (Semi-essential)
  - Urge: Arginine (Semi-essential)
  - Is: Isoleucine
  - Try: Tryptophan
  - Phenyl: Phenylalanine
  - Wali: Valine
  - Methi: Methionine

- Limiting Amino Acids (Cereals & Pulses):
  - Wheat (Lysine, Threonine): "She Lies", "We Three Lies"
  - Maize (Lysine, Tryptophan): "Main Try-To Lie"
  - Pulses (Methionine, Cysteine): "Pulse Me Cyst"

- Conditionally Essential Amino Acids:
  - Tyrosine, Cystine: "Conditionally Tired Siss"

subsections.title: Fats and Essential Fatty Acids
subsections.content:
OVERVIEW OF FATS AND LIPIDS

Fats are solid at 20 degrees Celsius. They are called "oils" if they are liquid at that temperature. Fats and oils are concentrated sources of energy.
- They yield fatty acids and glycerol on hydrolysis.
- The human body can synthesize triglycerides and cholesterol endogenously.
- Most of the body fat (99 percent) in adipose tissue is in the form of triglycerides.
- In normal human subjects, adipose tissue constitutes between 10 to 15 percent of body weight.
- Accumulation of one kilogram of adipose tissue corresponds to 7,700 kcal of energy.

CLASSIFICATION OF LIPIDS

- Simple lipids: e.g., triglycerides.
- Compound lipids: e.g., phospholipids.
- Derived lipids: e.g., cholesterol.

TYPES OF FATTY ACIDS

- Saturated fatty acids (SFA): Examples include lauric, palmitic, and stearic acids. Mostly found in animal fats, with exceptions like coconut and palm oils which are highly saturated vegetable oils.
- Monounsaturated fatty acids (MUFA): Examples include oleic acid.
- Polyunsaturated fatty acids (PUFA): Examples include linoleic acid and alpha-linolenic acid. Mostly found in vegetable oils.

VISIBLE AND INVISIBLE FATS

- Visible fats: Fats separated from their natural source, e.g., ghee (butter) from milk, cooking oils from oil-bearing seeds and nuts. Easy to estimate in the daily diet.
- Invisible fats: Fats present in almost every article of food, e.g., cereals, pulses, nuts, milk, eggs, etc. Not visible to the naked eye and difficult to estimate. They contribute significantly to the total fat intake in the Indian diet.

ESSENTIAL FATTY ACIDS (EFA)

Fatty Acids that CANNOT be synthesized completely in the human body and can only be supplemented from food. They were earlier known as Vitamin F.
- Linoleic Acid: The most important EFA, which serves as a basis for the production of other essential fatty acids (like linolenic and arachidonic acids).

TYPES OF EFA

- Omega-3 (w-3) fatty acid: Derived from alpha-Linolenic acid. Reduces incidence of Coronary Heart Disease (CHD).
- Omega-6 (w-6) fatty acid: Derived from Linoleic acid.
- Omega-9 (w-9) fatty acid: Derived from Oleic acid. Non-essential in human beings.

SOURCES OF LINOLEIC ACID (EFA)

- Safflower oil (Richest source): Contains 73% (10% SFA + 15% MUFA + 75% PUFA).
- Corn oil: 57%
- Sunflower oil: 56%
- Soyabean oil: 51%
- Sesame oil: 40%
- Groundnut oil: 39%
- Mustard oil: 15%
- Palm oil: 9%
- Coconut oil: 2%
- Order of EFA sources: Safflower oil > Corn oil > Sunflower oil > Soyabean oil > Sesame oil > Groundnut oil > Mustard oil > Palm oil > Coconut oil.
- Order of SFA sources: Coconut oil > Butter > Palm oil > Groundnut oil > Margarine > Soyabean oil > Sunflower oil > Safflower oil.
- Fat content in grains: Bajra > Maize > Jowar > Wheat > Ragi > Rice.

DEFICIENCY OF EFA

- Phrynoderma (Toad Skin): Characterized by horny papular eruptions on the posterior and lateral aspects of limbs and on the back and buttocks. Can be cured rapidly by administration of linseed or safflower oil (rich in EFA) along with Vitamin B-complex.

TRANS-FATTY ACIDS (TFAs)

- Formed during the partial hydrogenation of polyunsaturated fatty acids (a process used to increase shelf-life of commercial partially hydrogenated vegetable oils or PHVO like vanaspati).
- Adverse effects: Render the plasma lipid profile even more atherogenic than saturated fatty acids. They elevate LDL cholesterol and decrease HDL cholesterol, increasing the risk of CHD.

DIETARY RECOMMENDATIONS FOR FATS

- Minimum intake of visible fat for Indian adults: 25, 30, and 40 g/day for sedentary, moderate, and heavy physical activity in men respectively; 20, 25, and 30 g/day for women.
- Pregnant and lactating women should contain at least 30 grams of visible fat.
- Replacing SFAs with PUFAs and MUFAs is recommended to lower cardiovascular risk.

subsections.title: Carbohydrates and Glycemic Index
subsections.content:
OVERVIEW OF CARBOHYDRATES

- Main source of energy, providing 4 kcals per gram.
- Essential for the oxidation of fats and synthesis of certain non-essential amino acids.
- Three main sources: Starches, Sugar, and Cellulose.
- The carbohydrate reserve (glycogen) of a human adult is about 500 g. This reserve is rapidly exhausted when fasting.

GLYCEMIC INDEX (GI)

- Measure of how quickly 50 gm of a food's carbohydrate is converted to Sugar and affects blood sugar levels in 2 hours.
- It is defined by the area under the two-hour blood glucose response curve following the ingestion of a fixed portion of test carbohydrate, expressed as a proportion of the AUC of the standard (glucose or white bread).

CLASSIFICATION OF FOODS BASED ON GLYCEMIC INDEX

1. LOW Glycemic Index (GI score < 55)
- Mechanism: Favors slow release of sugar into the small intestine and its absorption into the blood.
- Examples: Most Fruits and Vegetables, Whole Grains, Lentils (Pulses), Beans.

2. MEDIUM Glycemic Index (GI score 56 - 69)
- Examples: Brown Rice, Brown Bread, Sweet Potato, Banana.

3. HIGH Glycemic Index (GI score > 70)
- Mechanism: Foods with readily digestible and absorbable sugar.
- Examples: White Bread, White Rice, Potato, Corn.

GLYCEMIC CONCEPTS

- Glycemic Response: A food's ability to raise the Blood Sugar.
- Glycemic Load: The amount of Carbohydrate, per serving of food.

subsections.title: Dietary Fibre
subsections.content:
OVERVIEW OF DIETARY FIBRE

- Dietary Fibre comprises the remnants of the edible part of plants and analogous carbohydrates that are RESISTANT TO DIGESTION AND ABSORPTION in the human Small Intestine.
- Also known as Non-Starch Polysaccharide.
- Dietary fibre yields some energy from fermentable fibre (short chain fatty acids) utilized by colon cells, generally yielding about 2 kcal/g, though earlier considered indigestible without energy contribution.

TYPES OF FIBRE

1. Insoluble fibers:
- Cellulose, Hemicelluloses, Lignin.
2. Soluble fibers:
- Pectin, Gum, Mucilage.

SOURCES OF FIBRE

- ANIMAL FOOD DO NOT CONTAIN FIBERS.
- Miller's Bran is the richest source.
- Order of fibre content in grains: Ragi > Cluster beans > Maize > Jowar (Sorghum) > Bajra (Pearl Millet).
- Oats are also a significant source.

FUNCTIONS OF FIBRE

- Adds to the bulk of food and favors Satiety.
- Laxative action (increases faecal bulk and frequency).
- Reduces Blood Cholesterol and Blood Glucose (e.g., Gum in Fenugreek seeds reduces Post prandial Glucose level in blood).
- Reduces the incidence of Coronary Artery Disease, Colonic Polyps, and Breast/Stomach/Prostate/Colon Cancers.
- Used in the management of Irritable Bowel Syndrome and Recurrent Diverticulitis.

FIBRE REQUIREMENTS

- Daily intake of 40 gm is desirable.
- The average Indian diet provides 50 - 100 gm fibers per day.
- Intake in excess of 60 gm of fiber over a day can cause Bowel irritation and decrease the absorption of valuable micronutrients like calcium, magnesium, iron, and zinc.

subsections.title: Balanced Diet and Dietary Goals
subsections.content:
OVERVIEW OF BALANCED DIET

- A Balanced Diet is defined as a diet which contains a variety of food in such a Quantity and Proportion that the need for Energy, Amino Acid, Vitamins, Minerals, Fats, Carbohydrate, and other nutrients is adequately met for maintaining Health, Vitality, and Wellbeing, and also makes a small provision for Extra Nutrients to withstand a short duration of Leanness.

PRINCIPLES FOR A BALANCED DIET

- Protein's daily requirement should be met (10-15 percent of total energy intake).
- Fats should be limited to 15 - 30 percent of daily energy intake.
- Carbohydrates rich in Natural Fibers should constitute the remaining food energy (50-70 percent).
- Micronutrient's requirements should be met.

BALANCED DIET QUANTITIES (Moderately Active Adults in grams)

- Cereals: Man 520, Woman 440
- Pulses: Man 50 (40 gm in sedentary), Woman 45
- Leafy Vegetables: Man 40, Woman 100
- Other Vegetables: Man 70, Woman 40
- Roots & Tubers: Man 60, Woman 50
- Milk: Man 200, Woman 150
- Oil & Fat: Man 45, Woman 25
- Sugar & Jaggery: Man 35, Woman 20

DIETARY GOALS (PRUDENT DIET)

- Recommended by the W.H.O.
- It is a diet taken for the achievement of optimal Dietary Goals to prevent non-communicable diseases.
1. Protein should account for 10 percent of daily intake (WHO recommends 10-15 percent).
2. Dietary Fat should be limited to 20 percent of total energy intake (WHO recommends 15-30 percent).
  - Saturated Fat should contribute no more than 10 percent of total energy intake.
3. Carbohydrates rich in Natural Fiber should be taken.
  - Excessive consumption of Refined Carbohydrate should be avoided.
4. Reduction of Cholesterol to below 100 mg / 1000 Kcal per day (or less than 300 mg per day total).
5. Fats and Alcohol should be RESTRICTED.
6. Salt intake should not be more than 5 gm/day.
7. Junk Foods should be AVOIDED.

subsections.title: Vitamin A and Xerophthalmia
subsections.content:
OVERVIEW OF VITAMIN A

- Vitamin A covers both a Preformed Vitamin (Retinol) and a Pro Vitamin (Beta Carotene).
- Preformed Vitamin (Retinol): Found in animal foods. Halibut Fish liver oil is the richest source. Other sources include Liver, Egg, Meat, Milk, Cheese, and Butter.
- Pro Vitamin (Beta Carotene): Found in plant foods. Sources include Green Leafy Vegetables (Spinach), Yellow Fruits (Papaya, Mango, Pumpkin), and Roots (Carrot).
- Dehydration contributes to a significant loss of Beta Carotene in foods.

FUNCTIONS OF VITAMIN A

- Production of Retinal Pigment needed for vision in dim light.
- Maintaining integrity and functioning of Epithelial lining of Intestines, Respiratory and Urinary tract, and at Skin and Eyes.

DAILY INTAKE RECOMMENDED (RDA)

- Retinol: 600 mcg (Pregnancy - 800 mcg).
- Beta Carotene: 4800 mcg (Pregnancy - 6400 mcg).
- RDA in Infant: 350 mcg.
- RDA for 1 to 9 years: 390 to 630 mcg.

VITAMIN A DEFICIENCY (XEROPHTHALMIA / DRY EYE)

- Assessment of Vitamin A Deficiency: Serum Retinol < 10 mcg / dl.
- Xerophthalmia refers to all the ocular manifestations of Vitamin A deficiency in humans.
- Most common in children aged 1- 3 years and is often related to Weaning.
- Also associated with Protein Energy Malnutrition (PEM) and Measles.
- Occurs in Food Donation Programs involving Skimmed Milk, which is totally devoid of Vitamin A.
- States badly affected are predominantly Rice Eating States (Andhra Pradesh, Tamil Nadu, Karnataka, West Bengal, and Bihar), as Rice is devoid of Carotene.

OCULAR MANIFESTATIONS OF VITAMIN A DEFICIENCY

- Earliest manifestation: Loss of vision to Green Light.
- Night Blindness: Due to impairment in Dark adaptation (First Clinical Symptom).
- Conjunctival Xerosis: Conjunctiva become Dry and Wrinkled (First Clinical Sign). Described as emerging like a Sand band at receding tide (Characteristic appearance).
- Bitot's Spots: Triangular Pearly White / Yellowish Foamy spots on bulbar conjunctiva on either side of Cornea. Indicates Vitamin A deficiency in young Children, and inactive sequelae of earlier disease in Adults.
- Corneal Xerosis: Cornea appears Dry and Opaque. Severe deficiency may lead to Corneal Ulcers.
- Keratomalacia: Liquefaction of Cornea. Cornea may become soft and may Burst Open.

EXTRA OCULAR MANIFESTATIONS

- Follicular Hyperkeratosis (Toad-Skin / Phrynoderma).
- Anorexia.
- Growth Retardation.

WHO CLASSIFICATION OF XEROPHTHALMIA

Primary Signs
- X1A: Conjunctival Xerosis
- X1B: Bitot's Spots
- X2: Corneal Xerosis
- X3A: Corneal Ulceration
- X3B: Keratomalacia

Secondary Signs
- XN: Night Blindness
- XF: Xerophthalmic Fundus
- XS: Xerophthalmic Scarring

PREVALENCE (%) CRITERIA FOR DETERMINING THE PROBLEM IN A COMMUNITY

- Serum Retinol: > 5 percent of the population at risk has < 10 mcg/dl.
- Night Blindness: > 1 percent.
- Bitot's Spot: > 0.5 percent.
- Corneal Scar: > 0.05 percent.
- Corneal Xerosis / Corneal Ulceration / Keratomalacia: > 0.01 percent.
- Prevalence is measured in the at-risk population i.e., Preschool children of 6 months to 6 years.

TREATMENT OF VITAMIN A DEFICIENCY

- Oral doses of Vitamin A: 3 oral doses given on Day 1 (immediately), Day 2 (after 24 hours), and Day 14 (after 2 weeks).
  - > 12 months: 2 lakh IU.
  - 6-12 months: 1 lakh IU. (A child < 8 kg despite being > 12 months is given 1 lakh IU).
  - 0-6 months: 50 thousand IU.
- I/M doses of Vitamin A: Water-based formulation (1/2 of oral dose). Given to Very Sick Child, Severe Anorexia, Edema with Malnutrition, Septic Shock etc.
- Therapeutic doses of Vitamin A in Pregnancy are TERATOGENIC.
- Mass Drug Administration is NOT DONE for TREATMENT of Vitamin A deficiency.

VITAMIN A TOXICITY

- Excess intake of Retinol: Causes Skin Desquamation (Exfoliating Dermatitis).
- Excess intake of Carotene: May Color Plasma and Skin but not dangerous. Condition is called Carotenoderma (Yellow / Orange Pigmentation of Face, Palm, Sole and Feet). It SPARES Mucous Membrane.
- Chronic Hypervitaminosis A: Pathological Bone Fracture is seen.
- Acute toxicity of Vitamin A: Pseudotumor Cerebri.

PREVENTION AND CONTROL STRATEGIES

- Adequate intake of Vitamin A rich food and reducing Contributory factors e.g., PEM, Measles.
- WHO Recommended Strategy for prevention:
  - Short Term Action (Vit A prophylaxis to vulnerable): Lactating Woman gets 5 thousand/day or 20 thousand/week. Women (Postnatal) get 3 lakh within 1 month of delivery. Newborns get 50 thousand at birth. Children < 1 year get 1 lakh once every 6 months. Children > 1 year get 2 lakh once every 6 months.
  - Medium Term Action: Fortification of certain foods with Vit A (2500 IU), e.g., Dalda / Vanaspati Ghee.
  - Long Term Action: Health Education and Environmental Modification. Immunization against Measles and Treatment of Diarrheal Infections. Promotion of Breast Feeding for as long as possible. Consumption of Green Leafy Vegetables (Spinach) and Yellow Fruits (Papaya, Mango).

subsections.title: National Program for Prevention of Nutritional Blindness
subsections.content:
OVERVIEW

- Launched in 1970.
- Also known as the National Vitamin A Prophylaxis Program.

BENEFICIARIES

- 1970: 1 year to 5 years Children (Preschool Children).
- 1992: 9 months to 3 years Children.
- 2006: Children upto 5 years.

PROGRAM FOCUS

- Promoting consumption of Vitamin A rich Foods in Pregnant and Lactating Women and in Children upto 5 years.
- Administration of massive doses of Vitamin A in Children upto 5 years of age.

DOSAGE AND ADMINISTRATION

- First Dose: 1 lakh IU given with MR (Measles-Rubella) vaccination at 9 months.
- Subsequent Doses: 2 lakh IU each, given every 6 months upto the age of 5 years.
- Total doses: A total of 9 doses of 17 lakh IU are given under the National Immunization Schedule (NIS).
- Administration method: Administered by a 2 ml Spoon with a rim marking inside at 1 ml (concentration is 1 lakh IU / ml).

FOR SICK CHILDREN

- Children with Xerophthalmia: To be treated at Health Facilities.
- Children with Measles: 1 dose of Vitamin A (if not received in the last 1 month).
- Children with Severe Malnutrition: 1 additional dose of Vitamin A.

subsections.title: Vitamin B Complex and Pellagra
subsections.content:
VITAMIN B1 (THIAMINE)

- Deficiency causes: Beri-Beri, Wernicke's Korsakoff Psychosis, and Lactic acidosis.
- Richest source: Whole grain. Groundnut is also rich in vitamin B1.
- Daily Requirement (RDA): 1 to 1.5 mg / day.

VITAMIN B2 (RIBOFLAVIN)

- Deficiency causes: Ariboflavinosis, Angular cheilitis, Glossitis, and Corneal vascularization.
- Also known as Warburg Yellow Enzyme.

VITAMIN B3 (NIACIN) AND PELLAGRA

- Vitamin B3 (Niacin) deficiency causes Pellagra.
- Characterized by the 4 D's:
  1. Diarrhea
  2. Dementia
  3. Dermatitis
  4. Death
- Clinical Sign: Skin Rashes appear in areas exposed to Sunlight (especially in the Neck, known as Casal's Necklace).
- Endemic occurrence: Vitamin B3 Deficiency is common in Maize / Sorghum (Jowar) eating populations.
- Mechanism in Sorghum eaters: Excess of Leucine in Sorghum (Jowar) interferes in the conversion of Tryptophan to Niacin.
- Conversion: 60 mg Tryptophan converts into 1 mg Niacin.
- Mechanism in Maize eaters: Tryptophan is the Limiting Amino Acid in Maize.
- Contraindication: Vitamin B3 / Niacin is CONTRAINDICATED in Diabetes because it causes Hyperglycemia.

VITAMIN B5 (PANTOTHENIC ACID)

- Deficiency causes: Burning feet (Sole syndrome).
- It is known as the Antistress Vitamin.

VITAMIN B6 (PYRIDOXINE)

- Deficiency causes: Anemia, Peripheral neuropathy.
- Decreased levels: Seen in patients on Isoniazid therapy.
- Toxicity: Toxicity of Vitamin B6 causes Sensory neuropathy.
- Daily requirement is directly proportional to protein intake.

VITAMIN B7 (BIOTIN)

- Deficiency causes: Dermatitis.
- Raw egg consumption: Raw egg contains Avidin, which causes biotin deficiency. Heat inhibits avidin, so cooked eggs do not cause this.

VITAMIN B9 (FOLIC ACID)

- Deficiency causes: Megaloblastic Anemia, Neural Tube Defects.
- Sources: Present in Vegetables, Spinach, Paneer etc.
- Daily requirement (RDA):
  - Pregnancy: 500 mcg
  - Lactation: 300 mcg
  - Adult: 200 mcg
  - Children > 5 years: 150 mcg
  - Children < 5 years: 100 mcg

VITAMIN B12 (COBALAMIN)

- Deficiency causes: Megaloblastic anemia (particularly seen in strict Vegetarians).
- Richest source: Liver.

subsections.title: Vitamins C, D, E, and K
subsections.content:
VITAMIN C (ASCORBIC ACID)

- Deficiency causes: Scurvy.
- Sources: High concentrations are found in Sitaphal (> 5mg / 100gm).

VITAMIN D

- Forms: D2 is Ergocalciferol, D3 is Cholecalciferol.
- Deficiency causes: Rickets in Children, Osteomalacia in Adults.
- Sunlight requirement: 5 min / day of sunlight (UV - B) is sufficient for synthesis of an adequate amount of Vitamin D.
- Active form: Calcitriol is the most active (physiologically) form of Vitamin D.
- Treatment note: Stoss therapy is given in rickets.

VITAMIN E (TOCOPHEROL)

- Deficiency causes: Hemolytic anemia in Newborns.
- Rich sources: Sunflower oil, Wheat, Germ oil, Soyabean.

VITAMIN K

- Forms: Phylloquinone, Menaquinone, Menadione.
- Deficiency causes: Hemorrhagic disease of Newborn (that's why Vitamin K is given to newborns just after birth).
- Daily requirement: 0.03 mg per kg.
- Induced deficiency: Prolonged use of antibiotics causes Vitamin K deficiency by suppressing normal intestinal flora.

subsections.title: Minerals, Trace Elements and Dietary Antioxidants
subsections.content:
OVERVIEW OF TRACE ELEMENTS

- Called Trace Elements because they are required by the body in very less amount (less than a few milligrams per day).
- Major trace elements include: Iron, Iodine, Fluorine, Zinc, Copper, Cobalt, Chromium, Manganese, Selenium, Molybdenum, and Dietary Antioxidants.
- Iron (Fe) is the trace element which cannot be adequately supplemented by diet alone during pregnancy.

COPPER

- Hypocupremia (Deficiency of Copper):
  - Occurs in patients with Wilson's disease, Protein Energy Malnutrition (PEM), and infants fed for long periods exclusively on Cow's Milk.
  - Abnormalities seen: Neutropenia (the best documented abnormality), Myelopathy, Anemia, Growth retardation, Osteoporosis, defective Elastin, and Hair defect.
- Hypercupremia (Excessive intake of Copper):
  - Result from eating food prepared in Copper Vessels.
  - Associated with Leukemia, Anemia, Hyperthyroidism, Hodgkin's Disease, Myocardial Infarction (MI), and Hepatotoxicity.

COBALT

- Part of the Vitamin B12 molecule (cyanocobalamin).
- Cobalt deficiency in soil results in Goiter.
- Necessary for the first stage of Hormone production.

CHROMIUM

- Plays a role in Carbohydrate and Insulin function.

SELENIUM

- Deficiency occurs in Protein Energy Malnutrition (PEM).
- Its deficiency along with Vitamin E deficiency reduces Antibody production.
- Deficiency causes Keshon Cardiomegaly and Hypothyroidism (particularly in the Sub-Himalayan zone).
- Its administration in Kwashiorkor results in a significant Weight increase.

MOLYBDENUM

- Deficiency is associated with Mouth and Esophageal Cancer.
- Excess absorption causes Bone deformity.

ZINC

- Acts as an Antioxidant agent.
- Required for the synthesis of Insulin by the Pancreas.
- Sources: Animal Food (Fish, Meat, Milk). Vegetables have Low Bioavailability.
- Zinc Deficiency causes:
  - Loss of Taste (Ageusia).
  - Acrodermatitis Enteropathica (Inherited disorder of impaired uptake of zinc from the body).
  - Decreased Insulin synthesis.
  - Growth Failure, Dwarfism and Hypogonadism, Sexual Infantilism in Adolescents.
  - Impaired Cell Mediated Immunity, Delayed Wound Healing.
  - Pernicious Anemia / Megaloblastic Anemia (Macrocytic Anemia).
  - Thalassemia, Myocardial Infarction, Hepatosplenomegaly.
- Zinc Deficiency in Pregnancy causes:
  - Spontaneous Abortion, IUGR, Preterm Delivery, Low Birth Weight (LBW).
  - Congenital Malformation (Anencephaly).
- Zinc Supplementation: In combination with ORS, it reduces the Duration and Severity of Diarrhea.
  - Doses: < 6 Months (10 mg), > 6 Months (20 mg).
  - Note: Zinc supplementation for a 12-month-old baby is 20 mg/day.
- Toxicity: Can cause Pulmonary Fibrosis.

DIETARY ANTIOXIDANTS

- Substances which include both Nutrients, Non-nutrients, and also some Enzymes.
- Nutrients: Vitamin A (Beta-carotene), Vitamin C, Vitamin E, and Selenium (remember mnemonic A,C,E).
- Non-nutrients: Plant Phenols, Flavonoids, Coumarins, Benzyl Isothiocyanates, Caffeic, Ferulic, Gallic, and Ellagic Acid.
- Enzymes: Superoxide Dismutase, Catalase, Superoxide Mutase.
- Sources: Vegetables, Fruits, Spices, Tea, Wine, and Cereals.
- Function: Reduces Free Radicals (produced during tissue damage). Reduces the adverse effects of Reactive Oxygen Species (ROS) and Nitrogen Species (which are generated during physiological and pathological conditions).

IRON AND NUTRITIONAL ANEMIA

- Function: Essential for the formation of Hemoglobin (Each gm Hb contains 3.34 mg of Iron). Its central function is Oxygen transport and Cell respiration. Lack of Iron directly affects the Immune system.
- Sources:
  - Haem Iron (Animal origin): Liver, Meat, Poultry, Fish etc. Bioavailability is 12-16 percent.
  - Non-Haem Iron (Vegetable origin): Green Leafy Vegetables (Spinach), Jaggery (poor man's iron source), Dry Fruits, Dates, Soyabean etc. Bioavailability is 6-9 percent.
  - Specific Food Fact: Dates contain 39 mg Calcium, 6 mcg Beta Carotene, and 280 Kcal.
- Absorption:
  - Iron absorbed from the Indian diet is < 5 percent.
  - Mostly absorbed from the Duodenum and Upper Small Intestine in the Ferrous state.
  - Absorption Promoters: Vitamin C rich foods (Iron Pills / Injections must be taken with high doses of Vitamin C).
  - Absorption Inhibitors: Disorders of Duodenum and Jejunum (Coeliac disease, Tropical Sprue), Phosphates, Phytates, Tannates, Oxalates, Fiber, and Calcium.
- Iron Losses: Hemorrhage, Physiological (Menstruation, Child Birth), Pathological (Hookworm, Hemorrhoids, Peptic Ulcer, Malaria).
- Stages of Iron deficiency:
  1. First stage: Decreased Storage of iron.
  2. Intermediate stage: Iron stores are exhausted. Latent Iron deficiency. Serum Ferritin and Transferrin fall.
  3. Third stage: Decrease in the concentration of Circulating Hemoglobin. The end result is Nutritional Anemia.

EVALUATION OF IRON STATUS AND DIAGNOSIS OF ANEMIA

- Hemoglobin Concentration:
  - 10 to 11 gm/dl: Early Anemia.
  - < 10 gm/dl: Marked Anemia.
  - Cut off points for Diagnosis of Anemia: Adult Females Pregnant (11 gm/dl), Adult Females Non-Pregnant (12 gm/dl), Adult Males (13 gm/dl).
- Serum Iron: < 0.5 mg/L indicates iron deficiency.
- Serum Ferritin: < 10 mcg/L. Indicates absence of stored iron. Most sensitive tool for Iron status. Best indicator to detect Iron deficiency in a community.
- Serum Transferrin Saturation: Should be above 16 percent. Used as a Clinical indicator.
- TIBC (Total Iron Binding Capacity): Most sensitive for Iron deficiency.
- Recommended Daily Iron Intake (RDA):
  - Infant: 5 mg/d.
  - Adult Male: 17 mg/d.
  - Adult Female Non-Pregnant (Menstruating / Lactating): 21 mg/d.
  - Adult Female Pregnant: 35 mg/d (Additional Iron required in Pregnancy is 14 mg per day, i.e., 21 + 14 = 35).

NUTRITIONAL ANEMIA

- Condition in which the Hemoglobin content of Blood is lower than normal as a result of deficiency of one or more essential Nutrients.
- By far the most frequent cause is Iron deficiency, and less frequently Folate and Vitamin B12 deficiency.
- Problem Statement: Women of Reproductive age group (15 to 49 years) and Children (6 to 35 months) are at higher risk. Prevalence is high in Adolescent Girls as many get married at a younger age.
- Causes: Poor Bioavailability, Women lose considerable amounts of iron during Menstruation, Malaria and Hookworm infestation.
- Detrimental effect of Anemia:
  - Pregnancy: Abortion, Premature birth, Postpartum Hemorrhage (PPH), Low Birth Weight (LBW).
  - Infections: Anemia is aggravated by Parasitic diseases e.g., Malaria, Intestinal parasites.
  - Work Capacity: Significant decrease in Work Capacity.
- Interventions:
  - If Anemia is Severe (Hb < 10 g/dl): High doses of Iron, Blood Transfusion.
  - If Hb is between 10 to 12 g/dl: Iron Folic Acid Supplementation under the Intensified Iron Plus Initiative / Anemia Mukt Bharat.

ANEMIA MUKT BHARAT (INTENSIFIED IRON PLUS INITIATIVE)

- Aim: To reduce the prevalence of Anemia by 3 percent per year between 2018-2022.
- Strategy: 6 x 6 x 6 Strategy (6 Beneficiaries, 6 Institutional Mechanisms, 6 Interventions).
- 6 Beneficiaries:
  1. Children (6 months - 5 years)
  2. Children (6 - 9 years)
  3. Adolescent girls and boys (10 - 19 years)
  4. Women in Reproductive age group (20 - 49 years)
  5. Pregnant women
  6. Lactating women
- 6 Institutional Mechanisms:
  1. Convergence with other Ministries
  2. Intra Ministerial coordination
  3. Strengthening Supply chain and Logistics
  4. National centre of Excellence and Advanced Research on Anemia Control
  5. National Anemia Mukt Bharat Unit
  6. Anemia Mukt Bharat Dashboard and Digital Portal (one stop shop)
- 6 Interventions:
  1. Prophylactic Iron Folic Acid Supplementation:
    - 6 months to 5 years: Elemental Iron 20 mg, Folic Acid 100 mcg. Biweekly (Syrup).
    - 6 to 9 years: Elemental Iron 45 mg, Folic Acid 400 mcg. Weekly (Pink tablet).
    - 10 to 19 years (Adolescent): Elemental Iron 60 mg, Folic Acid 500 mcg. Weekly (Blue tablet).
    - Women in Reproductive Age Group: Elemental Iron 60 mg, Folic Acid 500 mcg. Weekly (Red tablet).
    - Pregnant Women: Elemental Iron 60 mg, Folic Acid 500 mcg. Daily (Red tablet). 1 tab daily starting from the 4th month of Pregnancy, for a minimum of 180 days and to be continued for 180 days post-partum.
    - Note: In Iron Deficiency Anemia, when Hemoglobin level has returned to normal, Iron tablets are recommended for 3 months (to replenish Iron Stores).
  2. Deworming (Albendazole 1 tablet = 400 mg):
    - 1-2 years: 200 mg Biannual on National Deworming Day at Anganwadi.
    - 3-4 years: 400 mg Biannual on National Deworming Day at Anganwadi.
    - 5-9 years: 400 mg Biannual on National Deworming Day in Schools.
    - 10-19 years (Adolescent): 400 mg Biannual on National Deworming Day in Schools.
    - Women in Reproductive age group: 400 mg Biannual on National Deworming Day.
    - Pregnant Women: 400 mg Single dose in the 2nd Trimester on Village Health & Nutrition Day (VHND) at ANC Clinic.
    - Biannual Deworming occurs in February and August (It is once a year in M.P. & Rajasthan due to low prevalence).
  3. B.C.C campaign: Ensuring Delayed Cord Clamping.
  4. Testing for Anemia: By Digital methods.
  5. Iron Folic Acid fortified foods: In Public Health Programs.
  6. Addressing Non-nutritional causes of Anemia: In Endemic Pockets (Malaria, Kala-Azar, Thalassemia and Fluorosis).
- Iron Fortification:
  - Double Fortification of Salt with Iodine and Iron.
  - Addition of Ferric Ortho-Phosphate or Ferrous Sulphate with Sodium Biphosphate.

IODINE AND IODINE DEFICIENCY DISORDERS (IDD)

- Function: Required for synthesis of Thyroxine T4 and Triiodothyronine T3. T4 is a more sensitive indicator than T3. Adult Human body contains 50 mg of Iodine.
- Requirement: Adult 150 mcg/day, Pregnancy 250 mcg/day, Lactation 290 mcg/day.
- Source: Richest source is Japanese Sea Weed. Sea Foods (Sea fish, Sea salt, Cod liver oil). Vegetables (Onion, Potato). 90 percent of Iodine comes from Foods eaten (the remainder from drinking Water).
- Deficiency: The term Endemic Goiter has now been replaced by Iodine Deficiency Disorder (I.D.D).
- Goitrogens: Interfere with Iodine utilization by the Thyroid gland leading to Goiter. Seen in the Brassica Group of Vegetables (Cabbage and Cauliflower). Important dietary Goitrogens are Cyanoglycosides and Thiocyanates.
- Prevalence of Iodine deficiency in India is 3 to 10.
- Himalayan Goiter Belt: Stretches from Kashmir to Naga hills (2400 kms). 263 districts are endemic where the prevalence of IDD is more than 10 percent.
- Spectrum of Iodine Deficiency Disorder (in order of increasing severity):
  1. Goiter (Grade I, Grade II, Grade III, Multinodular)
  2. Hypothyroidism
  3. Delayed milestones (delayed development)
  4. Mental deficiency
  5. Hearing defects (deafness)
  6. Speech defects
  7. Strabismus (Squint) - Unilateral or Bilateral
  8. Nystagmus
  9. Neuromuscular weakness - Spastic Diplegia, Spastic Quadriplegia
  10. Endemic Cretinism (occurs when Iodine uptake < 20 mcg/d). Includes Hypothyroid Cretinism and Neurological Cretinism.
  11. Stillbirth

PREVENTION AND CONTROL OF IDD

- Iodized Salt:
  - Level of Iodization in Salt (PFA act 1954): Production level is 30 ppm; Consumer level is 15 ppm. This is the Process indicator to monitor the success of the IDD Control Program.
  - Double Fortified / Twin Fortified Salt: Contains Iodine and Iron. Provides 40 mcg Iodine and 1 mg Iron per gram of salt. Contains Salt, Potassium Iodate, Ferrous Sulphate and Sodium Hexa-Meta Phosphate. Developed by National Institute of Nutrition (Hyderabad).
  - Hazards of Iodization: Thyrotoxicosis, Hashimoto's Disease.
- Iodized Oil:
  - I/M: Iodized Oil (Poppy Seed Oil).
  - Oral: Sodium Iodate tablets.
  - Gives protection for 3 to 4 years.

IODINE MONITORING

- Indicators for Epidemiological assessment of Iodine deficiency:
  1. Serum T4 and TSH.
  2. Urinary Iodine Excretion (UIE): This is the Principal impact indicator to monitor the success of the IDD control program.
  3. Prevalence of Goiter: Cutoff value for Endemicity is 10% prevalence (i.e., called Endemic if prevalence is > 10% in School age Children).
  4. Prevalence of Neonatal Hypothyroidism: Sensitive indicator of Environmental Iodine deficiency. Most sensitive for Evaluation of Program.
  5. Prevalence of Cretinism.
- Criteria for IDD Elimination:
  - Proportion of Houses consuming Iodized Salt: > 90 percent.
  - Proportion of Enlarged Thyroid (Children 6-12 years): < 5 percent.
  - Urine Iodine Excretion (UIE): Proportion < 100 mcg/L should be < 50 percent, and proportion < 50 mcg/L should be < 20 percent.
- District IDD Survey / Goiter Survey Methodology:
  - Targets 6 to 12 years age group using Cluster Sampling Technique.
  - 30 Villages / Wards / Schools are selected from a District.
  - From the list of 30 Schools, 90 Children are selected (45 Boys and 45 Girls).
  - From the 90 Children, a Salt sample is collected from the House of every 5th Child.
  - From the 90 Children, Urinary Iodine Excretion (UIE) is tested from every Alternate Child.
- Quality Control: Rapid testing by M.B.I kit is used for Quality control of Salt. Countries with a Medium level (< 100 mcg / dL) of UIE have insufficient Iodine intake.

NATIONAL IODINE DEFICIENCY DISORDERS CONTROL PROGRAMME (NIDDCP)

- History: Started as Goiter Control Programme in 1962. Renamed to NIDDCP in 1986. At the end of the 8th 5-year plan, it became a National policy to Fortify all Edible Salt.
- Targets: Prevalence in All age groups < 10 percent. Prevalence of IDD in Children (10 - 14 years) < 5 percent.
- Objectives: Assess the Magnitude of IDD, Supply of Iodized Salt, Lab Monitoring of Urinary Iodine Excretion, Assess Impact of Iodized Salt on IDD, Health Education and Publicity.
- Strategies: Use Iodized Salt in place of common salt (Potassium Iodate / Iodide), Manpower Training, Mass Communication, Monitoring and Surveillance.
- Organizations: At the District level, the District Goiter Control Cell conducts the National IDD Survey every year.
- Achievements: Banned the sale of Non-Iodized Salt for human consumption. Issued under "Food Safety and Standards Act (2006) and Regulations (2011)". 34 States / UT have established IDD Control Cells.
- Indicators to Monitor Success of NIDDCP:
  1. Process Indicators: Salt Iodine content at Production site, Point of packing, Retail level, and Households.
  2. Impact Indicators:
    - Urinary Iodine level (principal impact indicator): > 100 mcg/L is Normal. < 100 mcg/L is Public Health Problem. 100 - 50 mcg/L is Mild Public Health Problem. 50 - 20 mcg/L is Moderate Public Health Problem. < 20 mcg/L is Severe Public Health Problem.
    - Goiter assessment (by Palpation or Ultrasound): > 5% is Public Health Problem. 5 - 19% is Mild Public Health Problem. 20 - 30% is Moderate Public Health Problem. > 30% is Severe Public Health Problem.
    - Neonatal TSH.
  3. Sustainability Indicators: Availability of Iodized Salt at the Household Level, Median Urinary Iodine levels in Target Population.

FLUORINE AND ENDEMIC FLUOROSIS

- Function: Essential for Mineralization of Bones and Formation of Dental Enamel (96 percent is found in Bones and Teeth).
- Sources: Major source is Drinking Water. Recommended level in India is 0.5 to 0.8 mg / L (approx. 1 ppm). Maximum Permitted level is 1.5 m EQ / L. Foods containing Fluorine include Sea Fish, Cheese, Tea.
- Fluorine is a Double Edged Sword:
  - Deficiency (Inadequate intake) causes Dental Caries.
  - Excess (Prolonged Ingestion) causes Dental Fluorosis and Skeletal Fluorosis.
  - > 1.5 ppm causes Dental Fluorosis (Mottling).
  - 3 - 6 ppm causes Skeletal Fluorosis.
  - > 10 ppm causes Crippling Fluorosis.
- Endemic Fluorosis:
  - Observed in parts where Fluorine in Drinking Water is 5 mg / L.
  - Important Health Problem in Nagaland, Andhra Pradesh, Tamil Nadu, Karnataka and Kerala.
- Toxic Manifestations:
  1. Dental Fluorosis: Characterized by Mottling of Dental Enamel. Best seen in Central Incisors and 1st Molar. Reported at Fluoride level > 1.5 mg / L of Drinking Water. Occurs when excess of Fluoride is ingested during the First 7 years of life (years of Tooth Calcification).
  2. Skeletal Fluorosis: Heavy Fluoride deposition in the Skeleton. Associated with Daily intake of 3 - 6 mg / L (or ppm). Crippling Fluorosis occurs at 10 mg / L.
  3. Genu Valgum: Genu Valgum and Osteoporosis of the Lower Limb. Observed among people whose Staple food is Sorghum (Jowar).
- Interventions:
  1. Changing the Water Source: Running Surface Water contains lower quantities of Fluoride.
  2. Chemical Treatment (Nalgonda Technique): Used for Defluoridation of Water. Involves addition of Lime, Alum (major role) and Bleaching Powder followed by Flocculation, Sedimentation and Filtration.
  3. Household Defluoridation: Using Nalgonda Technique, Alumina, Phosphates.
  4. Other Measures: Fluoride Toothpaste is not recommended in Endemic areas.

subsections.title: Malnutrition, Nutritional Rehabilitation Centres, Hidden Hunger and GHI
subsections.content:
OVERVIEW OF MALNUTRITION

- Defined as a Pathological state resulting from Deficiency or Excess of Nutrients.
- 4 Forms of Malnutrition:
  1. Undernutrition: When insufficient food is eaten over an extended period of time.
  2. Overnutrition: Consumption of an excessive quantity of food over an extended period of time.
  3. Imbalance: Disproportion among essential nutrients.
  4. Specific deficiency: Lack of an individual nutrient.
- 5 Principal Nutritional Deficiency Diseases (on a global scale):
  1. Kwashiorkor
  2. Marasmus
  3. Xerophthalmia
  4. Nutritional Anemia
  5. Endemic Goiter

ECOLOGICAL FACTORS RELATED TO MALNUTRITION

1. Conditional Influences: Infectious diseases like Intestinal parasites, Measles, Whooping cough, TB and Malaria may contribute to malnutrition.
2. Cultural Influences:
  - Food Habits Customs, Beliefs, Traditions and Attitudes: Papaya is avoided during pregnancy because it is believed to be abortifacient. Widespread belief is that if a pregnant woman eats more, her baby will be big with a difficult delivery.
  - Religion: Some orthodox Hindus do not eat Meat, Fish, Eggs and certain vegetables like Onion and Garlic, these are known as Food Taboos.
  - Food Fads: Selection of foods according to personal likes and dislikes.
  - Cooking: Draining away rice water and peeling of vegetables influences the nutritive value of foods.
  - Child Rearing: Premature curtailment of breast feeding and adaptation of Commercial Refined Foods.
3. Socio-Economic Factors: Poverty, Large family size etc.
4. Food Production: It is a problem of uneven distribution.
5. Health Services: Importance of Nutritional Rehabilitation Centres.

PREVENTIVE AND SOCIAL MEASURES

- Action at the Family level: Promotion of Breast Feeding, Attention focused on nutritional needs of expectant and nursing mothers and children.
- Action at the Community level: ICDS Program (Supplementary nutrition, Immunization, Health checkups, Health education and Nutrition for mothers, Nonformal Education for preschool age children).
- Action at the National level: Rural Development (Necessary to raise living standards and purchasing power), Increasing Agricultural Production (Modern farming, Fertilizers), Stabilization of Population (Birth Spacing and Small Family Norm), Nutrition Intervention Programs (Prevention and Control of Endemic Goiter through Iodized Common Salt, Control of Anemia through Iron Folic Acid, Control of Night Blindness through Vitamin A, Supplementary Feeding Program for preschool children).
- Action at the International level: World Food Program, International agencies (FAO, UNICEF, WHO, UNDP, CARE etc).

PROTEIN ENERGY MALNUTRITION (PEM)

- Occurs due to Inadequate intake of food (Food Gap) in both Quantity and Quality, or as a result of Infections (Measles, Intestinal worms etc).
- Common in Developing Countries.
- Incidence: 2 percent in Preschool Children.
- Clinical Note: Hepatomegaly is an essential feature. Hypothermia may be the cause of Death.
- Classification of PEM:
  1. Gomez' Classification: Weight for Age = (Weight of the Child / Weight of Normal Child of same age group) x 100.
    - Normal: 90 - 110
    - Mild malnutrition: 75 - 89
    - Moderate malnutrition: 60 - 74
    - Severe malnutrition: Under 60
  2. Waterlow's Classification:
    - Weight for Height > Mean - 2 SD: Normal
    - Weight for Height < Mean - 2 SD: Wasted
    - Height for Age > Mean - 2 SD: Normal
    - Height for Age < Mean - 2 SD: Stunted
    - Note: If both Weight/Height and Height/Age are < Mean - 2 SD, it is Wasted and Stunted.
  3. Mid Upper Arm Circumference (M.U.A.C):
    - Measured using Shakir's Tape. Used for the age group of 6 months to 5 years (as MUAC remains constant during this age).
    - > 12.5 cm: Green (Satisfactory nutritional status).
    - 12.5 to 11.5 cm: Yellow (Mild to Moderate malnutrition).
    - < 11.5 cm: Red (Severe malnutrition).

CLINICAL FEATURES: KWASHIORKOR VS MARASMUS

- Kwashiorkor:
  1. Oedema is present in Lower Legs, Face and Forearms.
  2. Child is Irritable.
  3. Poor Appetite.
  4. Flaky Paint Dermatosis.
  5. Flag Sign and Easily Pluckable Hairs.
- Marasmus:
  1. Muscle Wasting.
  2. Child is Quiet.
  3. Diarrhea is common.
  4. Severe loss of Subcutaneous Fat.
  5. Weight for Height is very low.

PREVENTIVE MEASURES FOR PEM

1. Health Promotion: Promotion of Breast Feeding and Development of low-cost Weaning Foods. Nutritional Education to pregnant and lactating mothers and Distribution of Supplements.
2. Specific Protection: Immunization, Food Fortification.
3. Early Diagnosis and Treatment: Early diagnosis and treatment of Infections or any Lag in growth. Deworming and Supplementary Feeding Programs.
4. Rehabilitation: Nutritional Rehabilitation Services (at N.R.C).

NUTRITIONAL REHABILITATION CENTRES (NRC)

- Facility based units providing Medical and Nutritional Care to Severe Acute Malnutrition (SAM) Children < 5 years of age who have medical conditions.
- Services provided: Treatment of medical complications, 24-hour Care and Monitoring of child, Therapeutic Feeding, Demonstration on preparation of Energy Dense Food using locally available affordable food, Counseling on appropriate feed and hygiene, Sensory Stimulation and Emotional Care, Social Assessment of the family, Follow Up of discharged children.
- Admission criteria for NRC:
  - MUAC < 11.5 cm with or without any grade of Edema.
  - Weight for Height < - 3 SD with or without any grade of Edema.
  - Bilateral Pitting Edema with Loss of Appetite, Fever and Hypothermia.
  - Persistent Vomiting, Hypoglycemia, Severe Dehydration, Severe Anemia and Severe Pneumonia.
  - Weak, Apathic and Unconscious.
- SAM Management (Therapeutic Feeding):
  1. Stabilization Phase (1 - 2 days): Starter Diet (F - 75) for Nutritional and Electrolyte Balance. F - 75 composition per 1000 ml is 300 ml Cow's milk or Toned milk + 100 gm Sugar + 25 gm Vegetable Oil + Water.
  2. Transition Phase (2 - 3 days): Catch Up Diet (F - 100) when there is beginning of loss of Edema and return of Appetite (Oral Feeding). F - 100 composition per 1000 ml is 900 ml Cow's milk or Toned milk + 75 gm Sugar + 20 gm Vegetable oil + Water.
  3. Rehabilitation Phase: Initiated when there is major loss of Edema and reasonable Appetite (child finishes > 90% of given food).
- Micronutrient Supplementation at NRC:
  - Vitamin A: Given on day 1, 2, and 14.
  - Vitamins B12, C, D, E.
  - Folic Acid: 5 mg on day 1 and then 1 mg/d.
  - Iron: Given only in the rehabilitation phase.
  - Zinc: 2 mg/d.
  - Copper.
- Discharge criteria for NRC:
  - Edema has resolved.
  - > 15 percent Weight Gain.
  - Child is Eating adequate amount of food.
  - All Medical conditions treated.
- Follow Up:
  - NRC visits.
  - Home visits (by AWW / ASHA): Weekly for 1 month in the Rehabilitation Phase. Then every 15 days until the child is released from the program.

HIDDEN HUNGER

- Micronutrient Deficiency (most common cause is Iron).
- It is a form of Undernutrition that occurs when intake or absorption of Vitamin and Minerals (Zinc, Iodine, Iron etc.) are too low to sustain good health and development.
- Factors contributing: Poor Diet, Increased Micronutrient needs during Pregnancy, Lactation, Infectious diseases or Parasitic Infestations.
- Visible Effects: Clinical signs of Hidden Hunger such as Night Blindness (Vitamin A Deficiency) and Goiter (Iodine Deficiency).
- Invisible Effects: Health and Development of a population are affected by these less obvious invisible effects (that's why micronutrient deficiency is known as Hidden Hunger).
- Affects 2 Billion individuals globally.
- Can lead to: Poor Health, Low Productivity and even Death. Adversely affects Health and Survival of First 1000 days of Child's Life. It can curtail Socio Economic Development in Low and Middle-Income Countries.

TRIPLE BURDEN

- Developing countries are moving from Traditional Diets based on minimally processed food to Highly processed, Energy dense, Micronutrient poor Foods and Drinks which are leading to Obesity and diet related Chronic Diseases.
- This Nutrient transition may lead to 1. Malnutrition, 2. Micronutrients Deficiency, 3. Obesity. Hence collectively known as Triple Burden.

GLOBAL HUNGER INDEX (G.H.I)

- G.H.I measures and tracks Hunger globally.
- Agency involved: International Food Policy Research Institute.
- G.H.I calculation is a combination of 3 indicators:
  1. Under Nourishment
  2. Underweight Child (< 5 years): Includes Child Wasting and Child Stunting.
  3. Child Mortality (< 5 years)
- Value ranges from 0 to 100.
- India's status: G.H.I is 27.2. Rank is 94.

FORMULAS AND CALCULATIONS

- G.H.I = (PUN + CUW + CM) / 3
  - PUN: Proportion of Undernourished
  - CUW: Underweight Children (< 5 years)
  - CM: Children Dying (< 5 years)

LOW BIRTH WEIGHT (LBW)

- Defined as Birth weight of less than 2.5 kg irrespective of gestational age.
- Categories of LBW:
  - < 2.5 kg: Low birth weight (LBW)
  - 2.5 to 1.5 kg: Moderately Low birth weight (MLBW)
  - 1.5 to 1 kg: Very Low birth weight (VLBW)
  - < 1kg: Extremely Low birth weight (ELBW)
- Prevalence: 18 percent in India and 15 percent in the world.
- Most common cause of LBW: Prematurity.
- Leading cause of death in LBW babies: Pneumonia, Atelectasis, Pulmonary hemorrhage, Intra cranial bleeding.
- LBW babies include: 1. Preterm babies. 2. Small for date babies.

subsections.title: Nutritional Assessment Methods and Nutritional Surveillance
subsections.content:
OVERVIEW OF NUTRITIONAL ASSESSMENT

- Assessment methods for Nutritional Status is generally an assessment of dietary intake (Diet Survey).
- The components can be remembered via the A.B.C.D.E.F - V classification.

NUTRITIONAL ASSESSMENT METHODS (A.B.C.D.E.F - V)

1. Anthropometry:
  - Height / Length: Measured using Stadiometer / Infantometer.
  - Weight: Measured using Salter Scale.
  - Skinfold Thickness: Measured using Harpenden Caliper.
  - MUAC (Mid-Upper Arm Circumference): Measured using Shakir's Tape.
2. Biochemical and Laboratory Assessment:
  - Biochemical tests: Vitamin A (Serum Retinol), Vitamin K (Prothrombin Time), Protein (Serum Albumin).
  - Laboratory tests: Hemoglobin estimation, Stool examination (for Intestinal Parasite), Urine Albumin and Urine Sugar.
3. Clinical Examination:
  - Bitot's spot (Xerophthalmia).
  - Absence of Knee or Ankle jerks (Beriberi).
  - Enlargement of Thyroid gland (Endemic Goiter).
4. Dietary Intake Assessment:
  - Weighment of Raw Foods: Weighment of all food that is going to be cooked. Dietary Cycle - Weighment of raw food done over a period of 7 days.
  - Weighment of Cooked Foods: Foods analyzed in the state in which they are normally consumed.
  - Oral Questionnaire Method: Quantity of foods eaten during the previous 24 hours (24 Hour recall method).
5. Ecological Factors Assessment:
  - Socioeconomic Factors: Family size, Income, Cultural patterns.
  - Health Services: Feeding and Immunization programs.
  - Conditioning Influences: Parasitic, Bacterial and Viral infections precipitates malnutrition.
  - Food Balance Sheet.
6. Functional Indicator:
  - Capillary Fragility: Indicates Vitamin C status.
  - Prothrombin Time: Indicates Vitamin K status.
  - Sperm Count: Indicates Zinc status.
  - Nerve Conduction: Indicates Vitamin B12 status.
  - Dark Adaptation: Indicates Vitamin A status.
7. Vital Statistics:
  - Mortality.
  - Morbidity: particularly in relation to PEM, Anemia, Xerophthalmia, Endemic Goiter.

NUTRITIONAL SURVEILLANCE

- Keeping watch over Nutrition, to make decisions which will improve the Community's Nutrition Status.
- Nutritional Survey (50 - 100 group size) is specifically for the detection of Malnutrition.
- It uses a Diagnostic Interventional Approach.
- Objective: To provide Input for Program Management (Policy Makers) and to give Timely Warnings.

INDICATORS OF NUTRITIONAL STATUS

1. Maternal Nutrition:
  - Birth Weight.
2. Infant and Preschool Children Nutrition:
  - Proportion being Breast Fed.
  - Proportion on Weaning Food.
  - If Age is known: Height for Age, Weight for Age.
  - If Age is unknown: Weight for Height, Mid Upper Arm Circumference (MUAC).
3. School Child Nutrition:
  - Height for Age.
  - Weight for Height.
- Note: For the assessment of the Nutritional Status of a Community, the key indicators used are LBW, Weight : Height in Preschool, and Mortality in 1 - 4 years.

DIFFERENCE BETWEEN GROWTH MONITORING AND NUTRITIONAL SURVEILLANCE

- Approach:
  - Growth Monitoring: Educational.
  - Nutritional Surveillance: Diagnostic.
- Weight Recorder:
  - Growth Monitoring: Mothers.
  - Nutritional Surveillance: Trained Workers.
- Weight Card:
  - Growth Monitoring: Emphasizes Growth.
  - Nutritional Surveillance: Emphasizes Nutritional Status.
- Strategy:
  - Growth Monitoring: Preservation of Growth.
  - Nutritional Surveillance: Detection of Under Nutrition.
- Response:
  - Growth Monitoring: Intervention.
  - Nutritional Surveillance: Rehabilitation.
- Referral:
  - Growth Monitoring: Health system.
  - Nutritional Surveillance: Nutritional Rehabilitation Centre (N.R.C).
- Intervention:
  - Growth Monitoring: ORS, Vaccines, Vitamin A, Deworming.
  - Nutritional Surveillance: Food Subsidy.
- Enrollment:
  - Growth Monitoring: All Infants.
  - Nutritional Surveillance: Representative Sample.
- Age:
  - Growth Monitoring: Starts before 6 months.
  - Nutritional Surveillance: Representative Ages.
- Number:
  - Growth Monitoring: 10 to 20.
  - Nutritional Surveillance: 50 to 100.

subsections.title: Milk Borne Diseases and Pasteurization
subsections.content:
OVERVIEW OF MILK HYGIENE

Milk is an efficient vehicle for a great variety of disease agents. The sources of infection or contamination of milk may be the dairy animal, human handler, or the environment (e.g., contaminated vessels, polluted water, flies, dust).

MILK BORNE DISEASES

1. Infection of ANIMALS that can be transmitted to man:
- Can be remembered by the mnemonic [A.B.C.D.E.F - L]
- Anthrax
- Brucellosis and Salmonellosis
- Cow Pox and Q Fever
- Foot and Mouth Diseases
- Tick Borne Encephalitis
- Streptococcal Infections and Staphylococcal Food Poisoning
- Leptospirosis and Listeriosis

2. Infections (primary to MAN) that can be transmitted through Milk:
- Typhoid Fever and Para Typhoid Fever
- Cholera, Shigellosis, Enteropathic E. coli
- Streptococcal Infections and Staphylococcal Food Poisoning
- Non-Diarrheal Diseases: TB, Diphtheria, Hepatitis

METHYLENE BLUE REDUCTION TEST

- It is an Indirect method for detection of Microorganism in milk.
- Methylene blue is added to 10 ml of milk, the sample is held at 37 deg C until the color has disappeared.
- Milk which remains blue the longest is considered to be the best quality.

PASTEURIZATION

- Definition: Heating of milk to a specific temperature and for a period of time, in order to destroy its pathogens, with minimum changes in its composition, flavor, and nutritive value.
- Pasteurization kills nearly 90 percent of bacteria in milk EXCEPT Thermoduric Bacteria and Bacterial Spores.
- Pasteurized milk can be kept at 18 deg C for not more than 12 hours.

METHODS OF PASTEURIZATION

1. Holder method / VAT method / Batch pasteurization:
- For Small scale and rural communities.
- Milk is kept at 63 deg C for 30 min and then cooled to 5 deg C.
- Note: Coxiella Burnetii can survive the Holder method of pasteurization.

2. High Temperature Short Time method (H.T.S.T method) / Flash pasteurization:
- For large quantities of milk.
- Milk is rapidly heated to 72 deg C and is held at that temperature for 15 sec then rapidly cooled to 13 deg C.

3. Ultra High Temperature method (U.H.T method):
- Milk is heated to 125 deg C in 2 stages (second stage being under high pressure) for few seconds and then rapidly cooled and bottled.
- UHT milk is packed in a sterile container (Tetra Pack) and has an unrefrigerated shelf life of 6 - 9 months.

4. Higher Heat Shorter Time method (H.H.S.T method):
- Process similar to HTST pasteurization, but uses slightly different equipment and higher temperatures for a shorter time.
- Milk is heated from 80 deg C to 100 deg C for specified time and then cooled down.
- Example parameters: 88 deg C for 1 sec, 90 deg C for 0.5 sec, 94 deg C for 0.1 sec, 96 deg C for 0.05 sec, 100 deg C for 0.01 sec.

TESTS OF PASTEURIZED MILK

1. Phosphatase Test:
- Widely used test to test the Quality of Pasteurization.
- Raw milk contains an enzyme called Alkaline Phosphatase.
- Alkaline phosphatase enzyme in raw milk liberates phenol from a disodium para-nitro phenyl phosphate and forms a Yellow-colored complex.
- At 60 deg C for 30 min Phosphatase is completely destroyed.
- Used to detect inadequate pasteurization or addition of raw milk.

2. Standard Plate Count:
- To determine bacteriological quality of pasteurized milk.
- Limit is < 50000 CFU / ml of pasteurized milk.

3. Coliform Count:
- Coliform organisms should be absent in 1 ml of milk.
- Coliform organisms are completely destroyed by pasteurization, so their presence in pasteurized milk indicates Improper Pasteurization or Post Pasteurization Contamination.

QUALITY ASSESSMENT OF MILK

- Density: Lactometer.
- Q2 Chemical: SNF test (Solid Non-Fat).
- Gerber's test: Fat content.

subsections.title: Food Borne Diseases and Food Toxicants
subsections.content:
OVERVIEW OF FOOD BORNE DISEASES

- Disease either Toxic or Infectious in nature, whose infectious agent enters the body through ingestion of Food.

FOOD BORNE INTOXICATIONS

1. Naturally Occurring Toxins:
- Lathyrism (Beta Oxalyl Amino Alanine - B.O.A.A)
- Endemic Ascites (Pyrrolizidine Alkaloids)
2. Toxins Produced by Bacteria:
- Botulism (Clostridium botulinum)
- Staphylococcus Poisoning (Staphylococcus aureus)
3. Toxins Produced by Fungi:
- Aflatoxin (Aspergillus flavus)
- Ergot toxin (Claviceps purpurea)
- Fusarium toxin (Fusarium incarnatum)
4. Food Borne Chemical Poisoning:
- Heavy Metals: Mercury (Fish), Cadmium (Shellfish), Lead (Canned food)

FOOD BORNE INFECTIONS

1. Bacterial:
- Typhoid and Paratyphoid Fever
- Salmonellosis
- Shigellosis
2. Viral:
- Hepatitis
- Gastroenteritis
3. Parasites:
- Ascariasis
- Amoebiasis
- Taeniasis

FOOD TOXICANTS IN DETAIL

1. LATHYRISM
- Paralyzing disease (Flaccid Paralysis).
- In Humans, it is referred to as Neurolathyrism (Spastic Paraplegia).
- In Animals, it is referred to as Osteolathyrism / Odoratism.
- Endemic areas: Once heavily prevalent in Rewa and Satna districts of Madhya Pradesh (25000 and 32000 cases respectively).
- Occurring mostly in Adults (15 - 45 years).
- Pulse: Lathyrus Sativus commonly known as Khesari Dhal (Teora Dal / Lak Dhal / Batra / Gharas / Matra). Looks similar to Red Gram Dhal or Bengal Gram Dhal.
- Diets containing 30 percent of Khesari Dhal for 2 - 6 months results in Neurolathyrism.
- Toxin: Beta Oxalyl Amino Alanine (B.O.A.A).
- 5 Stages of Disease:
  1. Latent Stage: Individuals are apparently healthy but exhibit Ungainly Gait on physical stress.
  2. No Stick Stage: Patient walks with Short Jerky Steps without the aid of a stick.
  3. One Stick Stage: Patient walks with Crossed Gait with a tendency to Walk on Toes and uses 1 Stick to maintain balance.
  4. Two Stick Stage: Due to excessive Bending of Knees and Crossed Legs, patient needs 2 Crutches for support.
  5. Crawler Stage: Knee joints cannot support the weight of the body and there is Atrophy of Thigh and Leg Muscles. Patient is reduced to Crawling by throwing his weight on his hands.
- Interventions:
  - Vitamin C Prophylaxis: Daily administration of 1000 mg of Ascorbic Acid for a week.
  - Banning the Crop: Prevention of food adulteration act has banned lathyrus in all forms.
  - Removal of Toxin (Steeping method): Water is boiled and pulse is soaked in hot water for 2 hours, then water is drained off and pulse is washed again with clean water, then water is drained off and pulses are dried under sunlight.
  - Removal of Toxin (Parboiling method / Hot Soaking Process): Simple soaking in Lime Water (overnight), followed by boiling (Partial cooking in steam). Vitamins and minerals in the outer layer are driven into the Endosperm, preserving nutritive value even during milling.
  - Genetic approach: Certain strains of lathyrus contain low levels of toxin. Selective Cultivation of such strains.
  - Education: Education on dangers of consuming this pulse.
  - Socioeconomic changes.

2. AFLATOXINS
- Mycotoxin produced by Fungi: Aspergillus Flavus.
- Fungus Infests: Groundnut, Maize, Sorghum, Wheat, Rice etc.
- Aflatoxins B1 and G1 are Hepatotoxic and Carcinogenic.
- Conditions: Moisture level above 16 percent and Temperature between 11 to 37 deg C favors toxin formation.
- Historical Outbreaks: In 1975, 400 cases of Aflatoxin poisoning including 100 deaths were reported in Banswada (Rajasthan) and Panchmahal (Gujarat) districts.
- Prevention: Proper storage of food grains after drying. Moisture content should be kept below 10 percent. Health Education on hazards of consuming contaminated food grains.

3. ERGOTISM
- Also known as Saint Anthony Fire.
- The frequent epidemics of Ergotism in the Middle Ages were called Holy Fire or Saint-Antony's Fire, because of the burning sensations resulting in Gangrene of limbs.
