It was caused by eating rye bread contaminated with the fungus. Named after Monks of the Order of St. Anthony, who were particularly successful at treating this ailment.
- Cereals affected: Bajra, Rye, Sorghum and Wheat can get infested during the flowering stage by Ergot Fungus (Claviceps Purpurea).
- Toxin: Clavine Alkaloids.
- Appearance: Grows as Blackish Mass and seeds become black and irregular.
- Outbreaks: Reported from areas where Bajra is consumed as a staple.
- Symptoms: Nausea, Vomiting, Giddiness, Drowsiness, Painful Cramps in Limb and Gangrene.
- Upper safe limit: 0.05 mg / 100 gm of food material.
- Removal of Ergot: Ergot infested grains can be easily removed by floating them in 20 percent Salt Water, Hand Picking, or Air Floatation.

4. EPIDEMIC DROPSY
- Caused by toxic alkaloids Sanguinarine from Argemone Oil.
- Contamination of Mustard Seeds / Oil occurs with argemone oil / seeds, as seeds of Argemone Mexicana (Prickly Poppy) and mustard seeds closely resemble each other.
- Symptoms: Non-Inflammatory Bilateral Edema of Lower Limb, Diarrhea, Dyspnea, Cardiac Failure, Glaucoma, GI Bleed, Telangiectasia, and even Death.
- Prevention: Contamination can be prevented by removing Argemone Weeds growing among mustard seeds crops.
- Tests for detection of Argemone Oil contamination in Mustard Oil: Nitric Acid Test, Paper Chromatography (most sensitive for Sanguinarine).
- Target Population: Occur in all Ages EXCEPT Breast Feeding Infants.

5. ENDEMIC ASCITES
- Outbreak occurred in Sarguja district of Madhya Pradesh in 1973 among Nagesia tribe with 40 percent mortality.
- Local population subsisted on millet Pannicum Milliare (locally known as Gondhi) which gets contaminated by seeds of Crotalaria (locally known as Jhunjhunia).
- Jhunjhunia seeds contain a Toxin: Pyrrolizidine Alkaloids (Hepatotoxic).
- Preventive measure: De-weeding of Jhunjhunia plants, Educating people in affected areas.

6. FUSARIUM TOXINS
- Field Fungi.
- Contamination of Sorghum or Rice.
- Toxin produced: Fusarium Incarnatum.

subsections.title: Food Adulteration, Food Fortification and Food Preservatives
subsections.content:
FOOD ADULTERATION

- Definition: Adulteration consists of Mixing, Substituting or Misbranding the food products.
- Disadvantages to the Consumer: Paying more Money for lower Quality Food Stuff, Can be Injurious to health.
- Examples of Food Material and their Adulterants:
  - Dals: Keshari Dal
  - Dhania Powder: Cow Dung Powder
  - Black Pepper: Dried Seeds of Papaya
  - Chili Powder: Brick Powder
  - Mustard Seeds: Argemone Seeds
  - Edible Oil: Argemone Oil
  - Milk: Water
  - Ghee: Vanaspati

FOOD ADULTERATION ACT (1954)

- Minimum imprisonment of 6 months and Minimum Fine of Rs 1000.
- Any Adulteration causing Grievous Hurt or Death comes under Section 320 IPC resulting in life imprisonment and fine of Rs 5000 or more.
- Under 1986 Amendment: Consumer / Voluntary Organization are empowered to take samples of any suspicious Food and send them to Food Laboratories (Kolkata, Mysore, Ghaziabad, Pune).

FOOD STANDARDS

1. Codex Alimentarius: Joint FAO/ WHO standards for International Markets. Food standards in India are based on Codex Alimentarius.
2. P.F.A Standards (Prevention of Food Adulteration): Laid under prevention of Food Adulteration Act 1954. Food standards in India have to achieve the minimum level of quality under it.
3. Agmark (Agriculture Mark Raw): Purely Voluntary. Express degree of excellence above PFA standards. Food Standardization is done by the Directorate of Marketing and Inspection (Government Of India).
4. Bureau of Indian standards: Purely voluntary. Express degree of excellence above PFA standards. Gives the I.S.I mark.
5. F.S.S.A.I (Food Safety and Standards Authority of India): Follow through PFA. For quality of food products that are Directly Consumed.

FOOD FORTIFICATION

- Definition: Process where Nutrients are added to Food to improve the Quality of diet.
- It is an example of Primary Level of Prevention (Specific Protection).
- Examples:
  - Salt: Iodization of Salt.
  - Twin Fortification of Salt with Iodine and Iron.
  - Water: Fluoridation of Water.
  - Vanaspati Ghee: Vitamin A (2500 IU) and Vitamin D (175 IU) in per 100 gm Vanaspati Ghee.
- Criteria for Fortification:
  - Vehicle Fortified must be consumed as a part of the regular Daily diet.
  - Addition of Nutrient SHOULD NOT cause any noticeable change in Taste, Smell, Appearance or Consistency.
  - Amount of Nutrient added must NOT cause Toxicity in consumers.
  - Cost of Fortification must NOT raise the Price of the food.

FOOD PRESERVATIVES

- Substance which if added to food, are capable of Inhibiting, Arresting or Retarding the process of Fermentation, Acidification or other Decomposition of food.
- 2 Types of Preservatives:
  - Class I Preservatives: Salt, Sugar (Dextrose, Glucose), Spices, Honey, Vinegar, Acetic Acid.
  - Class II Preservatives: Benzoic Acid, Sulphuric Acid, Sorbic Acid, Lactic Acid, Niacin, Propionic Acid.
- Irradiation and Ionizing Rays are also used in Food Preservation.

subsections.title: Nutritional Factors in Cardiovascular Diseases
subsections.content:
OVERVIEW

1. Cholesterol: Mostly seen with foods of Animal Origin.
2. Lipoprotein:
  - Low Density Lipoprotein (LDL): Dominated by its Cholesterol content. Involved in Atherosclerotic process.
  - Very Low-Density Lipoprotein (VLDL): Dominated by its Triglyceride content. Risk factor for CHD.
  - High Density Lipoprotein (HDL): Functions in the removal of Cholesterol from cells. Protective effect in CHD.
3. Fatty Acids:
  - Saturated Fatty Acids: Increases Plasma Cholesterol.
  - Unsaturated Fatty Acids: Lowers Plasma Cholesterol.
  - Poly-Unsaturated Fatty Acids (PUFA): Inhibits Platelet Aggregation (prevents Thrombus).
4. Triglycerides: Its concentration in blood is markedly increased in Individuals with raised Cholesterol level (risk factor for CHD).
5. Carbohydrate: Fiber's intake decreases the risk of CHD.
6. Salt:
  - Highest incidence of Hypertension is seen in Japan (Sodium intake > 400 mmol/day).
  - Hypertension can be treated with a Low Sodium Diet (< 10 mmol/day).

subsections.title: Nutritional Programs in India
subsections.content:
OVERVIEW OF NUTRITIONAL PROGRAMS

1. Vitamin A Prophylaxis Program (NPCBVI)
2. Prophylaxis against Nutritional Anemia (Anemia Mukt Bharat)
3. Control of Iodine Deficiency Disorder (NIDDCP)
4. Special Nutrition Program
5. Balwadi Nutrition Program
6. Mid-Day Meal Program 1961
7. Integrated Child Development Services 1975 (ICDS)
8. Mid-Day Meal Scheme 1995
9. National Nutrition Mission / POSHAN Abhiyan

VITAMIN A PROPHYLAXIS PROGRAM

- National Program for Control of Blindness and Visual Impairment (NPCBVI).
- Single massive dose of oily preparation of Vitamin A (2 lakh IU) orally to all Preschool Children in the community every 6 months through Peripheral Health Workers.

SPECIAL NUTRITION PROGRAM

- In the 5th 5yr plan it was a part of the Minimum Need Program.
- Gradually merged into ICDS.

BALWADI NUTRITION PROGRAM

- Balwadi are being phased out because of universalization of ICDS.

MID-DAY MEAL PROGRAM 1961

- School Lunch Program.
- Ministry of Education.
- Objective: To attract more children to schools and retain them.
- Minimum number of feeding days in a year should be 250 (by National Institute of Nutrition, Hyderabad).
- Became a part of Minimum Need Program in 5th 5yr plan.

INTEGRATED CHILD DEVELOPMENT SERVICES 1975 (ICDS)

- Staff:
  - 1 Child Development Project Officer (CDPO)
  - 4 Supervisor (Mukhya Sevika)
  - 100 AWW's (Anganwadi Workers)
  - Several Mahila Mandals
- Beneficiaries:
  1. Children 0 - 3 years
  2. Children 3 - 6 years
  3. Adolescent Girls 11 - 19 years
  4. Women of Reproductive Age Group 15 - 45/49 years
  5. Pregnant women
  6. Nursing Mothers
- Services:
  1. Supplementary Nutrition
  2. Nutrition and Health Education
  3. Immunization
  4. Health Checkups
  5. Nonformal and Preschool Education
  6. Referral Services
- Supplementary Nutrition in ICDS:
  - For Children below 6 years, Pregnant Woman and Nursing Mothers.
  - Given 300 days/ year.
  - Children: 500 k.cal, 12 - 15 gm Protein (> 1 meal at Anganwadi) Cost Rs 8.
  - Pregnant and Nursing woman: 600 k.cal, 15 - 20 gm Protein (Take home rashan) Cost Rs 9.5.
  - Malnourished child: 800 K.cal, 20 - 25 gm Protein (> 1 meal at Anganwadi) Cost Rs 12.5.

MID-DAY MEAL SCHEME 1995

- National Program for Nutritional Support to Primary Education.
- Objective: Universalization of primary education by increasing Enrolment, Retention and impacting on Student Nutrition.
- Covers: Children of classes 1st to 8th in Government Schools.
- Free supply of food grains from nearest Food Corporation of India's godown (100 gm/ Student/ Day).
- Meal should be a minimum of 300 calories and 8 to 12 gm Protein.
- Principles of formulating Mid-Day Meals:
  1. Meal should be a Supplement and NOT a substitute to Home Diet.
  2. Meal should supply at least 1/3 of total Energy requirement and 1/2 of Protein need.
  3. Meal Cost should be low.
  4. Meals should be such that it can be prepared in Schools.
  5. Locally available foods should be used for its preparation.
  6. Complicated cooking process should be avoided.
  7. Menu should be changed frequently.
- Mid-Day School Meal Formulation (gm/ Day/ Child):
  - Cereals: 75 gm
  - Pulses: 30 gm
  - Oils: 8 gm
  - Leafy vegetables: 30 gm
  - Non leafy vegetables: 30 gm

POSHAN ABHIYAAN (NATIONAL NUTRITION MISSION)

- POSHaN: PM's Overreaching Scheme for Holistic Nourishment.
- Launched on International Women's Day (2018).
- Vision: To attain Malnutrition Free India.
- Ministry: Ministry of Women and Child Development (MOWCD).
- Target - To reduce (per annum):
  - Under-Nutrition (by 2 percent)
  - LBW (by 2 percent)
  - Stunting (by 2 percent)
  - Anemia (by 3 percent)
- Components:
  - Mapping of various schemes addressing malnutrition.
  - ICT based real time Monitoring System.
  - Incentivizing States and UTs for meeting the targets.
  - Incentivizing AWWs for using IT based tools (thus eliminating use of registers).
  - Introducing Height Measurement at AWCs.
  - Setting up Nutrition Resource Centres.

subsections.title: Reference Indian Adult, RDA, Consumption Units and Soyabean
subsections.content:
INDIAN REFERENCE MAN AND WOMAN

- Indian Reference Man:
  - Weight: 65 kg
  - Height: 1.77 meters (5 ft 8 inch)
  - Age: 19 - 39 years
  - BMI: 18.5 - 22.9
  - Free from Disease.
  - Fit for active work.
  - Engaged in 8 hours of Occupation (moderate activity).
  - 8 hours in Bed.
  - 4-6 hours in Sitting and Moving.
  - 2 hours in Moving (Active Recreational or Household Duties).
- Indian Reference Woman:
  - Weight: 55 kg
  - Height: 1.62 meters (5 ft 3 inch)
  - Age: 19 - 39 years
  - BMI: 18.5 - 22.9
  - Free from Disease.
  - Fit for active work.
  - Engaged in 8 hours of Occupation (moderate activity).
  - 8 hours in Bed.
  - 4-6 hours in Sitting and Moving.
  - 2 hours in Moving (Active Recreational or Household Duties).

RECOMMENDED DIETARY ALLOWANCE (RDA)

- Decided by: Panel of Experts.
- Based on: Scientific Research and Estimated Average Requirement (EAR).
- Higher than minimum requirement as it includes both Daily Requirement and some Additional Requirement for periods of growth and illness.
- RDA is the level of intake corresponding to mean + 2 SD, which covers 97.5 percent of the population.
- RDA safe level approach (i.e., +2SD) is NOT used for energy since excess energy intake is undesirable. For Energy, Mean or Average energy requirement is defined as RDA.
- RDA for Calcium:
  - Adult male or Adult Female or Pregnancy: 1000 mg/ day.
  - Elderly or Lactating: 1200 mg/ day.

RECOMMENDED DAILY ENERGY AND PROTEIN INTAKE

- Adult Female:
  - Sedentary worker: Energy 1660 Kcal/d, Protein 46 gm/d
  - Moderate worker: Energy 2130 Kcal/d, Protein 46 gm/d
  - Heavy worker: Energy 2710 Kcal/d, Protein 46 gm/d
- Adult Male:
  - Sedentary worker: Energy 2170 Kcal/d, Protein 54 gm/d
  - Moderate worker: Energy 2720 Kcal/d, Protein 54 gm/d
  - Heavy worker: Energy 3490 Kcal/d, Protein 54 gm/d
- Pregnancy:
  - Energy: + 350 Kcal (1st Trimester - no additional calories required)
  - Protein: + 23 gm
- Lactation:
  - 0 - 6 months: Energy + 600 Kcal, Protein + 19 gm
  - 6 - 12 months: Energy + 520 Kcal, Protein + 19 gm
- Infant:
  - 0 - 6 months: Energy 92 Kcal / kg / d, Protein 1.16 gm
  - 6 - 12 months: Energy 80 Kcal / kg / d, Protein 1.69 gm
- Additional Notes on Protein:
  - Protein requirement of an Adult: Egg Protein is 0.7 gm/kg/d. Mixed Vegetable Protein is 1 gm/kg/d. New Guidelines say 0.83 gm/kg/d.
  - Recommended protein intake (High Quality) of an Indian Adult Male according to 2010 guidelines is 0.83 gm/kg/d.
  - ICMR recommend 1 gm protein per kg body weight for Indian Adult (assuming NPU of 65 for dietary protein).
  - Protein Energy % of less than 4% is referred as limit value for inadequate protein intake.
  - Sedentary Strict Vegetarian Male requires 460 gm Cereals and 40 gm Pulses (ICMR).
  - Estimated Average Requirement (EAR) of Protein (gm/d): Female is 36, Male is 43.
  - Energy requirement by Adult for basal metabolism is 1 Kcal/ hour/kg body weight.
- Suggested Dietary Fat intake (gm/d):
  - Young Children: 25
  - Older Children: 22
  - Adult Man / Woman: 20
  - Pregnant Woman: 30
  - Lactating Mother: 45
- Daily Calcium Requirement (mg/d):
  - Adult Male / Female: 1000 (Notes mention 600 under old guidelines, new is 1000)
  - Infant: 500
  - Pregnancy / Lactation: 1200 (Notes mention 1200)
  - Postmenopausal: 800

CONSUMPTION UNIT (CU)

- Consumption Unit is used to measure the Food intake / Energy Consumption in a Community / Group of people.
- It's a Coefficient of Dietary Intake, which varies between Individuals on the basis of Age, Sex and Physical Activity.
- Energy Consumption of an average Man doing Sedentary work is taken as 1 Unit.
- 1 Unit corresponds to an energy requirement of 2400 Kcal / day.
- Consumption Unit Coefficient (CUC):
  - Adult Male: Sedentary worker (1), Moderate worker (1.2), Heavy worker (1.6).
  - Adult Female: Sedentary worker (0.8), Moderate worker (0.9), Heavy worker (1.2).
  - Adolescents: 1.
  - Children 9 - 12 years: 0.8.
  - Children 7 - 9 years: 0.7.
  - Children 5 - 7 years: 0.6.
  - Children 3 - 5 years: 0.5.
  - Children 1 - 3 years: 0.4.

SOYABEAN

- Protein: 43.2 gm / 100 gm (richest among Pulses and more than Egg's 6 gm).
- Fat: 20 gm / 100 gm.
- Minerals: 4 gm / 100 gm.
- Characteristics: As compared to other Pulses, richer in Fat, Calcium, Iron, and Vitamin B1, B2, B3.
- Limiting Amino Acid: Methionine.
- NPU: 61.
- Consumption: Can be cooked as Dhal or Chapattis (its powder is mixed with Atta).
- Processed form: Soya Milk.

title: Behavioral Sciences, Sociology, and Economics in Healthcare

subsections.title: Sociology and Society Concepts
subsections.content:
OVERVIEW OF SOCIAL AND BEHAVIORAL SCIENCES

- The secret of national health lies in the homes of the people.
- The term social sciences refers to a composite of several disciplines committed to the scientific examination of human behavior.
- The basic sciences of medicine are anatomy and physiology.
- The social sciences relevant to medicine are psychology and sociology.
- Health cannot be isolated from its social context. There has been a shift from the earlier concept of visualizing disease in terms of a specific germ to the involvement of multiple factors in the causation of disease.
- Social Scientists are the workers stepping up into the field of community health, working on problems of mental health, hospital organization, and social class differences in diseases.

THE DISCIPLINES OF SOCIAL SCIENCE

- Economics: Deals with human relationships in the specific context of production, distribution, consumption, and ownership of scarce resources, goods, and services.
- Political science: Concerned with the study of the system of laws and institutions which constitute the government of whole societies.
- Behavioral sciences: A subset of social sciences applied to the last three disciplines (Sociology, Social psychology, and Social anthropology) because they directly deal with human behavior.
- Sociology: Deals with the study of human relationships and of human behavior for a better understanding of the pattern of human life.
- Social psychology: Sprang from psychology. Concerned with the psychology of individuals living in human society or groups, emphasizing the basis of perception, thought, opinion, attitudes, general motivation, and learning. It studies human behavior, thoughts, and feelings and how they are influenced by the perception of a society.
- Anthropology: Derived from anthropos (meaning man) and logos (meaning science). It is the study of the physical, social, and cultural history of man.
 - Physical anthropology: Study of human evolution, racial differences, inheritance of bodily traits, growth, and decay of the human organism.
 - Social anthropology: Study of development in various types of social life. The behavioral science used extensively in Preventive and Social Medicine (PSM) is anthropology.
 - Cultural anthropology: Study of the total way of life of contemporary primitive man, his ways of thinking, feeling, and action.
 - Medical anthropology: Deals with the cultural component in the ecology of health and disease.

DEFINITIONS IN COMMUNITY HEALTH

- Community Health: Environmental, social, and economic resources to sustain emotional and physical well-being among people, that satisfy their needs in their unique environment.
 - It includes all the Health and Environmental services in a community, irrespective of whether it is public or private.
 - Community health has replaced the terms Public Health, Environmental Health, Preventive Medicine, and Social Medicine.
 - The theme common to community health and social sciences is human behavior.
- Resistance faced by the Community Health Worker in fieldwork:
 - Immunization against communicable diseases.
 - Family planning programs in India.
 - Installation of sanitary latrines.
 - Infant rearing practices.
- Community: A social group determined by geographical boundaries and/or common values and interests. Members know and interact with each other, functioning within a particular social structure.

MEDICAL SOCIOLOGY AND SOCIOLOGICAL THEORIES

- Medical sociology is a specialization within the field of sociology. Its main interest is the study of health, health behavior, and medical institutions.
- It studies the relationship between medicine and the public, along with social factors related to diseases.
- It was first proposed by Charles McIntire in 1894.
- Defined as professional endeavor devoted to social epidemiology, the study of cultural factors and social relations in connection with illness, and the social principles in medical organization and treatment.
- Specializations within sociology include medical, urban, rural, industrial, religion, education, criminology, hospital, and demography.
- Sociological theories of disease:
 - Feminist theory: Disease is due to the social role of women enforced by men.
 - Parsonian theory: Disease is due to social strain caused by social demands. Example: rehabilitated individuals and their social roles.
 - Marxist theory: The cause of disease is putting profit ahead of health.
 - Foucauldian theory: Disease is a label to segregate the population to make it easier to control.

CONCEPTS IN SOCIOLOGY

- Society: A group of individuals with social relationships, who have organized themselves to follow a way of life. It is an organization of member agents characterized by a system of social relationships. Society controls and regulates behavior by law and customs. Its character is dynamic.
- Social structure: Refers to the pattern of interrelationships between persons in a society. Every society has a complex of major institutions, groups, power structure, and status hierarchy. The study of social structure is comparable to anatomy, while social organization is comparable to physiology.
- Social institutions: An organized complex pattern of behavior in which persons participate to further group interest. Examples include the family, school, church, club, hospital, political parties, and panchayats.
- Role: Individuals are allocated roles classified into ascribed (given by virtue of sex, age, birth) and achieved (acquired by education).
 - Sick role: When a person falls ill, they assume this role. They are expected to decrease or relinquish normal duties, seek medical aid, and carry out physician orders.
- Socialism: An economic doctrine favoring the use of property and resources of the country for the public welfare. Based on social ownership to raise the living standard of the working class. Motto is all for all and each for all, as opposed to capitalism (all for each). The Louis Blanc socialist principle is from each according to his abilities, to each according to his needs.
- Socialization: The process by which an individual gradually acquires culture and becomes a member of a social group. Example: the internship training program of doctors gives them an opportunity to learn how to become acceptable to the public.
- Social control mechanisms: Rules (formal and informal) for maintaining relationships of authority and subordination.
 - Formal: Laws and enactments of Parliament.
 - Informal: Social pressures brought by powerful groups, individuals, or friends. Works largely through reward and punishment (e.g., small financial rewards for sterilization operations).
- Customs: Established patterns of behavior within a particular social setting. They are technically divided into:
 - Folkways: The right ways of doing things in less vital areas of human conduct.
 - Mores: More stringent customs. The public takes an active part in their enforcement. Laws are generally customs-inspired.
- Sociometry: The tendency of some group members to interact with selected members only, which leads to the formation of a subgroup.
- Social defense: Preventive, therapeutic, and rehabilitative services for the protection of society from antisocial and criminal conduct of man. Includes control of juvenile delinquency, beggary, prostitution, alcoholism, drug addiction, and suicides. The National Institute of Social Defence was renamed in 1975 under the Department of Social Welfare.
- Socialized medicine: Provision of medical services and professional education by the state. The program is NOT managed by the government but by professional groups. This ensures social equity and universal coverage, decreases competition among physicians, and uses state funds for free medicine.
- Social medicine: Study of social, cultural, economic, environmental, psychological, and genetic factors which have effects on health.
- Social epidemiology: Study of the role of social factors in the etiology of disease. Epidemiological survey and social survey are merged together.

CULTURE AND ACCULTURATION

- Culture: Learned behavior which has been socially acquired.
 - It is transmitted from one generation to another through formal and informal learning processes.
 - Culture stands for customs, beliefs, laws, religion, and moral precepts acquired by a man as a member of the society.
 - Every culture has customs that profoundly influence the incidence of disease (e.g., chewing pan associated with oral cancer in India).
- Acculturation: Cultural contact or mixing of 2 cultures.
 - When there is contact between two people with different cultures, then there is a diffusion of culture in both ways.
 - Cultural change occurs due to socialization.
 - It occurs through:
 1. Socialization
 2. Conquest
 3. Propagation of religion
 4. Trade and commerce
 5. Industrialization
 6. Education
 - Examples: Propagation of religion, introduction of scientific medicine (brought by the British to India), changes in food habits (orthodox brahmins eating meat), widespread use of tobacco.

STANDARD OF LIVING AND SOCIAL STRESS

- Standard of living: Refers to the usual scale of expenditure, the goods consumed, and the services enjoyed (food, dress, house, amusements).
- It depends upon:
 1. Level of national income
 2. Total amount of goods and services a country produces
 3. Size of the population
 4. Level of education
 5. General price level
 6. Distribution of national income
- Comparison of the level of living globally is primarily known through per capita income.
- Dynamics of social change: Interaction between social factors and health is complex. In Western Europe during the Industrial Revolution, increased income decreased birth and death rates (demographic transition). In many developing regions, infant death rates and birth rates declined without massive income increases, while in Africa birth rates remain high.
- Social stress: A major source of stress in transitional societies is the conflict generated by new opportunities and frustrations from societal changes.
 - Inducing conditions include migration from rural to urban areas, diminution of traditional family support, mass media exposure to alien ideas, tourism, and changes in technological needs.
 - Psychological stress and inadequate coping ability are implicated as contributory factors in virtually all diseases. Direct links exist between stress and hypertension, as well as coronary heart disease.

MNEMONICS

- EP - SSS: To remember the Social Science disciplines: Economics, Political science, Sociology, Social psychology, Social anthropology.
- SSS: To remember Behavioral sciences applied to the last three: Sociology, Social psychology, Social anthropology.

subsections.title: Psychology, Behavior, and Learning
subsections.content:
SOCIAL PATHOLOGY AND SOCIAL SURVEYS

- Social Pathology: The study of the relationship between disease and social condition or social disorganization. It also refers to the study of social problems which cause disease in a population.
- Social pathology is uncovered by Social Surveys.
- Social problems studied under social pathology are divided into:
 - Social constraints: Illiteracy and Poverty, Migration, Urbanization and Industrialization, Environmental crises.
 - Social evils: Smoking and Drinking, Gender bias and Gender discrimination, Child neglect, Child abuse and Child labour, Prostitution and Crime.
 - Social deviance: Drug abuse, Juvenile delinquency, Suicide.
- Social Surveys: Disclose social pathology. Have played an important part in the development of public health.
 - Example: Survey by Chadwick that led to the foundation of the General Board of Health in 1848 in Great Britain.
- Social Epidemiology: When the objective of the research is to study the role of social factors in the etiology of diseases, the relationship between Social Survey and Epidemiological Survey merges into what is known as Social Epidemiology.
 - Used in investigating the relationship between social factors and Heart diseases, Cancer, and Arthritis.

SOCIAL PROBLEMS

- When an individual problem affects a large number of people, they become a social problem. Social problems are solved by social welfare programs and social legislation.
- 1. Prostitution: An age-old social evil. Covered under the Immoral traffic prevention act (which covers males or females who are exploited sexually or commercially).
- 2. Delinquency: Committing an offence (e.g., theft, sexual offence, murder, burglary).
- 3. Dowry system: Covered by the Dowry prohibition act 1986. Minimum punishment is 5 years imprisonment and a fine of Rs 15000.
- 4. Alcohol abuse: Causes crime, neglecting family, indebtedness, and road accidents.
- 5. Unmarried mothers: Covered under the Medical Termination of Pregnancy (MTP) act 1971.
- 6. Handicapped: Divided into 7 main categories:
 - i. Blind
 - ii. Hearing disability
 - iii. Orthopedically handicapped
 - iv. Leprosy affected
 - v. Mentally retarded
 - vi. Mental illness
 - vii. Speech disability
- 7. Drug addiction: Chronic intoxication to the individual, which is produced by the repeated intake of habit-forming drugs. Reasons for drug addiction include curiosity, disturbed home, and escape phenomenon.
- Legislative and Government Initiatives:
 - Food adulteration act and MTP act are examples of social legislation solving social problems.
 - Nirbhaya fund: Setup by the government of India for ensuring the safety of women.
 - For Women Empowerment, involvement in political and economic decision making is important.

PSYCHOLOGY AND DYNAMICS OF BEHAVIOR

- Psychology: The study of human behavior, of how people behave and why they behave like that. It includes every aspect of human life and every type of human relation.
- Social psychology: Deals with a man's feeling and thinking. It studies changes in human behavior, thoughts, and feelings and how they are influenced by the perception of society.
- The theme common to community medicine and psychology is human behavior.
- Goal of psychology: To find relations between Causes and Responses.
- Causes of behavior:
 - 1. Emotions
 - 2. Motivation
 - 3. Perception
 - 4. Environmental stimuli
 - 5. Needs
- Responses to stimuli:
 - Physical responses: Habits, skills.
 - Organic responses: Feelings, tension, emotions.
 - Intellectual responses: Thinking and reasoning, perceptions.
- Behavior: The total reaction of an individual accessible to external observation. Human behavior is the result of physical and mental factors (body and mind) interacting in complicated ways.
- Factors that influence behavior: Knowledge, beliefs, values, attitudes, skills, and the influence of family, friends, and coworkers.
- Types of Behavior:
 - I. Health behavior: Refers to those activities which people undertake to avoid disease (e.g., sexually transmitted diseases can be prevented by use of condoms).
 - II. Illness behavior: Refers to how people react to symptoms. Generally, people who detect symptoms will want to see if the symptom persists or gets worse. If symptoms continue, then only the affected person asks for medical help.
 - III. Treatment behavior: Refers to activities used to cure disease and restore health, taking medication as directed, and return for tests.
- Environmental stimuli: Environmental stimuli (e.g., sight, smell, touch) reach the cerebral cortex through the nerve impulse. By another set of impulses, the cerebral cortex orders the behavior of the individual. This is known as Conscious Behavior.

EMOTIONS

- Emotions are a strong feeling of the organism, which motivate human behavior.
- An emotional experience is characterized by both External and Internal changes in the human being.
 - External changes: Apparent and easily seen by others (e.g., changes in facial expression, change in posture).
 - Internal changes: These are psychological such as rapid pulse, respiration, increased blood pressure.
- Some specific emotions:
 - Fear: Most common emotion of man. May produce excitement or depression. Common fears are fear of ghosts, fear of sickness, fear of death. When the fear becomes exaggerated or unnecessary it is called Phobia.
 - Anger or Rage: Offensive reaction with a destructive nature.
 - Anxiety: Manifests as rapid pulse and breathing, flushing, tremors, sweating, raised blood pressure.
 - Love: Feeling of attachment to some person.
- Role of emotions in health and disease:
 - Emotion interferes with efficiency, causing lack of concentration, lack of appetite, lack of sleep.
 - Psychosocial Illness: Mind acting on body (e.g., Essential hypertension, peptic ulcer, asthma, ulcerative colitis).
- Tips for control of emotions:
 - Hobbies
 - Reading and Recreation
 - Try to understand your own limitations and Philosophy of life
 - Develop a sense of humor

MOTIVATION AND MASLOW'S HIERARCHY OF NEEDS

- Motivation: An inner force which drives an individual to a certain action; may be positive or negative. Motivation is contagious; it spreads from one motivated person to another. 
- Motivation of eligible couples for a small family is an important activity in the national family welfare program.
- Factors that stimulate motivation (Incentives):
 - i. Intrinsic incentive: Benefits that come from solving one's own problems.
 - ii. Extrinsic incentive: Rewards that do not relate directly to the goal towards which the desired behavior is aimed (e.g., financial compensation of individuals undergoing sterilization operation for family planning).
- Maslow's hierarchy of needs: A theory of psychology proposed by Abraham Maslow. It determines the priority of human needs which determines human behavior. It describes the pattern that human motivation generally moves through.
- The structure of his pyramid starts from the bottom and proceeds upward:
 - v. Physiological needs: Food, water, sleep.
 - iv. Safety: Security of health, property, and family.
 - iii. Love and Belongingness: Friends, family.
 - ii. Self-esteem: Self-respect, self-esteem.
 - i. Self-actualization: Creativity, morality, acceptance of facts.
- Dynamics of the pyramid:
 - Deficiency Needs: Motivation decreases as needs are met (comprises Physiological, Safety, Love/belonging, and Esteem).
 - Being (growth) Needs: Motivation increases as needs are met (comprises Self-actualization).
- Ego integrative needs of man: Power, Prestige, and Self-respect.

PERCEPTION

- Perception implies observation, recognition, and discrimination. We have visual perception, auditory perception, olfactory perception, etc.
- Disorders of perception:
 - i. Imperceptions: Inability to recognize. May be due to damage to the sense organ (e.g., anesthesia).
 - ii. Illusion: False perception. Mistaking a rope for a snake.
 - iii. Hallucination: Imaginary perception. Seeing objects that do not exist, hearing sounds that are false.

LEARNING

- Learning includes acquiring knowledge, skills, and formation of habits. It is a continuous process. Learning is both conscious and unconscious.
- Conditions affecting learning:
 - i. Intelligence: Children with low IQ are poor learners.
 - ii. Age: The curve for learning reaches its peak between 22-25 years and declines after 30.
 - iii. Learning situation: Facilities for learning such as institutions, teachers, and textbooks promote learning.
 - iv. Motivation: Encouragement, praise, reward, and success encourage learning.
 - v. Physical health: It is difficult for a handicapped person (e.g., deaf).
 - vi. Mental health: Worries, anxieties, and fear interfere with learning.
- Types of learning:
 - I. Cognitive learning (knowledge)
 - II. Affective learning (attitudes)
 - III. Psychomotor learning (skills)
- Theories of learning:
 - 1. Learning by conditioned reflex: Pavlov the Russian physiologist discovered that if a bell was rung when the dogs were fed eventually, salivation could be induced by just the ringing of bell alone. This is called Conditioned Reflex.
 - 2. Demonstration: Here a procedure is carried out by step by step before an audience.
 - 3. Learning by remembering: Learning by memorizing.
 - 4. Learning by observation and imitation: A child learns language by observation and imitation.
 - 5. Learning by doing: Coordination of muscular responses with sensory impulses. Learning to type or a musical instrument. Remembering dates, events, or faces.
 - 6. Field experience: Involves a series of activities for diagnosing, planning, and solving the problem.
 - 7. Learning by insight: When we face a problem, we solve it by insight or mental exploration.
 - 8. Trial and error: A child tries again and again using a number of approaches until accidentally the ideal approaches become obvious.
- Methods of learning: Reading, writing, group discussion, etc.

HABITS

- Habits: Accustomed way of doing things. Example: washing hands before handling food.
- Characteristics of habits:
 - Acquired through repetition.
 - It is automatic.
 - Can be performed only under similar circumstances.
- Principles involved in habit formation:
 - Should begin early in childhood.
 - Formed by frequent repetition.
 - Takes time to form habits.
 - There should be a strong emotional stimulus to form habits.
 - Good habits kill bad habits. The best way to break bad habits is to cultivate good habits.

MNEMONICS

- Maslow's Hierarchy of Needs story approach:
 - Khane Ke Liye Bheek Mangta tha: Physiological Need.
 - Bheek Se thode Paise aa gaye to Safety ke liye Jhopdi Banali: Safety Need.
 - Love Karne ke liye Sathi mil gayi: Love / Belongingness.
 - Sathi mil gayi to Self Esteem aagayi (Bheek nahi mangenge Kaam Karenge): Self Esteem.
 - Jab kaam karna to samajh me aaya ki actual me yahi karne ke liye paida hue the: Self-actualization.

subsections.title: Personality, Intelligence, Coping Mechanisms, and Attitudes
subsections.content:
ATTITUDES, OPINIONS, BELIEFS, AND VALUES

- Attitude: Organization of beliefs around an object, subject, or concept which predisposes someone to respond in a preferential manner.
  - Objective in nature.
  - Includes feelings or an affective element (feeling element).
  - Includes a cognitive or knowledge element and a tendency to action.
  - Acquired characteristics of an individual viz. gained by social interaction.
  - Its Caught NOT TAUGHT.
  - Once formed, attitudes are difficult to change.
  - Permanent way of behaving.
  - Example: Attitude towards Person, Things, Situations, and Issues (government policies and programs).
  - Example of change in attitude: Change in affective level after communication and health education.
  - Social Psychology is largely a study of attitudes.
- Opinions: Temporary or Provisional views, held by people on a point of dispute or debate based on evidence.
  - Subjective in nature.
  - Easier to give up when faced with facts.
- Beliefs: Inner subjective thought of a person towards an individual or a situation.
  - Subjective in nature.
  - Beliefs are permanent, stable, and almost unchanging.
  - Usually derived from our parents, grandparents, and other people we respect.
  - Cultural Belief: Learned behavior viz, permanent and consistent, but liable to change.
- Values: Ideals and Customs of a society towards which they have an affective regard.
  - May be Positive: Cleanliness, freedom, or education.
  - May be Negative: Cruelty or crime.

FRUSTRATIONS, CONFLICTS, AND DEFENSE MECHANISMS

- Frustrations: All people have needs (biological, social, economic) which they try to satisfy. When they are unable to meet their needs and desires, they feel frustrated.
  - Sources of frustration may be external (e.g., unemployment, failures, defeats) or internal (e.g., lack of health, lack of intellectual ability).
  - Frustration, if allowed to continue, may damage one's personality. It may generate feelings of anger, dejection, hostility, withdrawal, or even attempts at suicide.
  - Some people take to drugs and alcohol to escape frustration.
- Conflicts: A conflict is like a tug of war between two or more courses of action or between opposing ideas. Generates painful emotions.
- Defense Mechanisms: When an individual is faced with problems, difficulties, or failures, he employs certain ways or devices to achieve health, happiness, or success. These are called defense mechanisms.
- Types of Defense Mechanisms:
  - 1. Rationalization: Instead of accepting failure and correcting himself, the individual tries to make excuses and justifies his behavior. It is a face-saving device (e.g., the proverbial fox declaring that the grapes were sour when it could not reach them).
  - 2. Projection: Sometimes the individual blames others for his mistakes or failures (e.g., a student saying that he could not score good marks in the examination because his teacher did not like him).
  - 3. Compensation: Many people make use of compensation to enhance their self-esteem and prestige (e.g., a student who is not good in his studies may distinguish himself in sports or dramatics).
  - 4. Escape mechanism: Adopting a mechanism to overcome failure or defeat. This is an escape phenomenon (e.g., pretending illness to skip examinations, or taking to alcohol or drugs trying to solve problems).
  - 5. Displacement: A rebound phenomenon. Trying to escape from one situation and fixing blame on another situation (e.g., an office clerk badly snubbed by his superior takes it out on his wife and children on reaching home).
  - 6. Regression: Some people resort to childhood practices as a mode of adjustment (e.g., weeping when something goes wrong).

PERSONALITY AND ITS DEVELOPMENT

- Personality: A key word in psychology. It implies certain physical and mental traits which are characteristics of an individual; these traits determine the individual's behavior or adjustments to his surroundings.
  - The personality of the doctor affects very much the well-being of the patient.
- Components of personality:
  - 1. Physical: Height, weight, color, facial expression, physical health.
  - 2. Emotional: Fear, anger, love, jealousy, guilt, worries.
  - 3. Intelligence: Intelligent person has forceful personality whereas a person with subnormal intelligence is a dull person.
  - 4. Behavior: Partly dependent on our feeling and partly on the expectation of the society. Termed as Gentle, Kind, Affectionate, Balanced, Aggressive, or Submissive.
- Personality traits: A trait is a tendency to behave in a consistent manner. Basic personality traits are established by the age of 6 years.
  - Traits include: Good manners, Sportsmanship, Honesty, Kindness, Loyalty, Reliability, Sense of humor, Tactfulness.
  - Carl Jung divided personalities into 2 types: Extrovert (dashing, practical, active) and Introvert (reserved, shy).
- Character and Will: Personality also involves Character and Will of a person.
  - Character implies morals.
  - Will indicates determination.
- Stages of Personality Development:
  - Infancy: First year of life. Rapid physical and mental growth. Totally dependent on the mother.
  - Pre-school child: Marked by considerable growth of the brain. Wants to assume responsibility.
  - School-age: Ranges from 5 to 15 years. By the age of 8, mental powers are fully developed and brain size is almost an adult's.
  - Adolescence: Turbulent period. Onset of puberty (about 11 years in girls and 13 in boys). Teenager strives for independence.
  - Adults: Mature and more balanced.
  - Old age: Decline in physical powers. Impaired memory, rigidity of outlook.

THINKING AND PROBLEM SOLVING

- Thinking: An active mental process including perception, memory, imagination, and reasoning. The anatomical basis of thinking is the cerebral cortex.
- Problem solving: Regarded as the highest stage in human learning. It is an aspect of thinking that involves reasoning (collecting information, drawing conclusions, and testing them).

INTELLIGENCE AND IQ CLASSIFICATION

- Intelligence: Ability to see meaningful relationships between things. Includes perceiving, knowing, reasoning, and remembering. Results from hereditary and environmental factors.
- Components of adult intelligence (Thurstone): Space, Number, Verbal comprehension, Facility of expression, Memory.
- Intelligence Quotient (IQ): Mental age indicates the level of intelligence achieved.
  - Benet and Simmon (1896) developed the First IQ test.
  - Stern's IQ test was originally calculated for children.
- Wechsler Intelligence Scale for Children (WISC): Most widely used test of intelligence for school aged children and adolescents (6 - 17 years).
  - i. Verbal IQ
  - ii. Performance IQ
  - iii. Combined full scale IQ
- Wechsler Adult Intelligence Scale (WAIS): Developed by David Wechsler. 1st IQ test designed for adults. It was the 1st IQ test based on Normal / Gaussian distribution.
- Intelligence test: Test of intelligence is a measure of performance.
  - i. Group test: All subjects must start at the same time (Example: Theory examination).
  - ii. Individual test: Need not depend on constant time (Example: Viva examination).
- 80% of people have an IQ of near 100.

FORMULAS AND CALCULATIONS

- IQ = (Mental age / Chronological age) x 100

LEVINE AND MARKS SCALE OF INTELLIGENCE BASED ON IQ

- Near genius: 140 & over
- Very superior: 140 - 120
- Superior: 120 - 110
- Normal (average mental IQ): 110 - 90
- Low normal: 90 - 80
- Borderline: 80 - 70
- Moron: 70 - 50
- Imbecile: 50 - 25
- Idiot: 25 - 0

CATEGORIES OF MENTAL RETARDATION

- Normal: 70 & over
- Mild mental retardation: 50 - 69
- Moderate mental retardation: 35 - 49
- Severe mental retardation: 21 - 34
- Profound mental retardation: 20 or below
- Reading and Writing skills of a moderate mentally retarded child is Basic.
- The term "Mental retardation" has been replaced by "Intellectual Disability".

MEASUREMENT OF DISABILITY

- Wings Handicap, Behavior, Skills (HBS) schedule: Used in epidemiological studies to assess the total child population. It is the most important epidemiological tool used for assessing disability in children.
- Disability Assessment Schedule (DAS): Developed by Wing from the HBS as a small practical schedule for use in service contexts.

subsections.title: Social Organization, Communities, and Economics
subsections.content:
SOCIAL ORGANIZATION

- Society is a group of individuals drawn together by a common bond, who act together for achievement of certain common goals. Different groups are needed for different purposes, these groups comprise Social Organization.
- Social Organizations are divided into Family, Religion & Caste, Temporary social groups, Permanent social groups, and Government & Political organization.
- 1. Family: Group of biologically related individuals, living together and eating from a common kitchen. It is the basic or primary unit of all societies. It is the most powerful example of Social Cohesion. It acts as a Bridge between generations and a Transfer point of civilization.
- 2. Religion and Caste: In India, the caste system is a closed type.
- 3. Temporary social groups:
  - i. Crowd: When a group of people come together temporarily, for a short period due to a common interest or curiosity. Has NO internal organization or leadership.
  - ii. Mob: Group of people coming together temporarily for a short period, having a Leader who forces them into action. Mob is more emotional and unstable. Has NO internal organization.
  - iii. Herd: It is a crowd with a Leader where members have to blindly follow the orders of the leader without questions. Example: Tourist group under a guide.
- 4. Permanent spatial groups:
  - i. Band: Most elementary community of few families living together. The group has organized itself and follows a pattern of life. Examples: gypsies in India.
  - ii. Village: A small collection of people permanently settled down in a locality with their homes and cultural equipment.
  - iii. Towns and cities: Large and permanent settlement of socially heterogeneous individuals. Subdivided into smaller groups on the basis of wealth and social class. When the population exceeds 1 lakh it is called a City.
  - iv. State: An ecological social group based on territory. It is heterogeneous in nature. Example: Indian union is a large state.
- 5. Government and Political organization:
  - Government is the supreme agent authorized to regulate social life in the interest of the public.
  - Types of government:
  - i. Democracy: Government of the people, by the people, and for the people (India, USA).
  - ii. Autocracy: Ruler has the absolute power (Jordan).
  - iii. Oligarchy: Country is ruled by a family group (Saudi Arabia).
  - iv. Monarchy: Head of the state is a Monarch i.e., King or Queen (UK).
  - v. Socialistic: Production and wealth are owned and controlled by the state (China).

RURAL AND URBAN SOCIETIES AND SOCIAL MOBILITY

- Rural societies: Depend primarily upon agriculture. The villages are self-sufficient units for most routine requirements.
- Urban societies: Feature occupational diversity. Social life is impersonal and less intimate. Cities are melting pots of races, people, and cultures.
- Social mobility:
  - Closed-class system: Rigidly based on caste or religion. There is little social mobility (people do not change their caste or religion).
  - Open class system: Movement of the social ladder is unrestricted, based on achievement or gaining wealth. People according to their ability can go up the social ladder.

SOCIAL CLASS AND OCCUPATIONAL CLASSIFICATIONS

- Social class is closely bound up with economic status, level of education, way of life, attitudes, and expectations.
- Occupation is widely used as a means of determining social standing. It determines economic rewards, extent of authority, extent of obligations, and degree of status.
- The International Standard Classification of Occupation 2008 (ISCO-08): Provides a system for classifying and aggregating occupational information. It is a four-level hierarchically structured classification that allows all jobs in the world to be classified into 436 unit groups, which aggregate into 130 minor groups, 43 sub-major groups, and 10 major groups.
- The 10 major groups based on skill level and specialization are:
  - 1. Managers
  - 2. Professionals
  - 3. Technicians and associate professionals
  - 4. Clerical support workers
  - 5. Service and sales workers
  - 6. Skilled agricultural, forestry and fishery workers
  - 7. Craft and related trade workers
  - 8. Plant and machine operators and assemblers
  - 9. Elementary occupations
  - 10. Armed forces occupations
- Limitations of occupational classification: Heterogeneous grouping, Occupational mobility, and differences in classifying Women.

SOCIO-ECONOMIC STATUS (S.E.S) SCALES

- Socio-Economic Status (S.E.S) has been defined as the position that an individual or family occupies with reference to the prevailing average standards of cultural and material possessions, income, and participation in group activity.
- Urban scales: B. G. Prasad scale, Shrivastava scale, modified Kuppuswami scale, Kulshreshtra scale, Jalala scale.
- Rural scales: B.G. Prasad scale, Udai Pareek scale, Radhukar scale, Shirpurkar scale.
- Student scale: Bharadwaj scale.
- Non-Indian scale: Hollingshead scale (occupation based), Henderson scale.
- Kuppuswami scale:
  - For Urban families.
  - 3 parameters used: Educational status of head of the family (score 1-7), Occupation of head of the family (score 1-10), Total per capita family income per month (score 1-12). Total maximum score is 29.
- B.G. Prasad scale:
  - For both Urban and Rural families.
  - Based on Per capita monthly income.
- Udai Pareek scale:
  - For Rural families.
  - 9 parameters (NOT dependent on income): Occupation, Education, Caste, Family members, Social participation, Land, House, Material possession, Form power.
- Likert scale:
  - Psychometric scale (NOT a S.E.S scale) used to understand people's attitude from their responses.
  - Example: Strongly agree / Agree / Neutral / Disagree / Strongly disagree, OR Very Satisfied / Satisfied / Neutral / Unsatisfied / Very unsatisfied.

POVERTY AND BELOW POVERTY LINE (BPL)

- BPL population in India: 29.5% (Rangarajan committee - 2014).
- Criteria for Rural areas:
  - Per capita calorie intake: < 2400 K Cal/ day
  - Per capita income (RC): < 32 INR / day
- Criteria for Urban areas:
  - Per capita calorie intake: < 2100 K Cal/ day
  - Per capita income (RC): < 47 INR / day

ECONOMICS AND HEALTH

- National Income: The size and strength of a country's economy, usually expressed in national aggregates.
- GNI (Gross National Income) or GNP (Gross National product): Gross income generated from within the country and it also includes the net income received from abroad.
- GDP (Gross Domestic Product): Gross income generated from within the country (Does NOT include net income received from abroad).
  - Total health expenditure in India (% of GDP): 4.7%
  - Public health expenditure in India (% of GDP): 1.15%
- NNP (Net National Product): It is the GNP minus the capital we consume (e.g., equipment, machinery) in the production process. NNP is the market value of all final goods and services after providing for depreciation.
- Net Domestic Product (NDP): Gross domestic product minus the value of depreciation on fixed assets.
- Market Price vs Factor Cost:
  - GDP at Market Price: It is GDP at factor cost plus indirect taxes minus subsidies.
  - NNP at Market Price: It is NNP at factor cost plus indirect taxes minus subsidies.
  - GNP at Market Price: It is GNP at factor cost plus indirect taxes minus subsidies.
- PPP (Purchasing Power Parity): Number of currencies required to buy some goods in domestic market as 1 $ would buy in USA.
- Gross domestic savings: It is the excess of current income over current expenditure.

GLOBAL HUNGER INDEX AND HIDDEN HUNGER

- Global Hunger Index (GHI): A tool designed to comprehensively measure and track hunger globally by region and country. Combines three equally weighted indicators:
  - 1. Undernourishment: the proportion of undernourished people as a percentage of the population.
  - 2. Child underweight: the proportion of children under the age of five who are underweight.
  - 3. Child mortality: the mortality rate of children under the age of five.
- Hidden hunger (Micronutrient deficiency): A form of undernutrition that occurs when intake and absorption of vitamins and minerals (such as zinc, iodine, and iron) are too low to sustain good health and development. The health and development of a much larger share of the population is affected by less obvious invisible effects, referred to as hidden hunger.

MNEMONICS

- Urban SES scales: Shehri Shrivastava ki-KUPpi ka-KUL JAL Prasad (Shrivastava, Kuppuswami, Kulshreshtra, Jalala, Prasad).
- Rural SES scales: Rural- pRa paR kaR kaR (Prasad, Pareek, Radhukar, Shirpurkar).
- Prasad scale coverage: Prasad sabko milta hai - Urban / Rural.
- BPL criteria for rural: Gaanv me 32 dant se 24 ghante khayega (Represents <32 INR/day and <2400 K Cal/day).
- BPL criteria for urban: Shaher me Ek-kiss (21) har 4 - 7 din me (Represents <2100 K Cal/day and <47 INR/day).

subsections.title: Family and Cultural Factors
subsections.content:
THE FAMILY AND ITS TYPES

- Family: It is a group of biologically related individuals, living together and eating from a common kitchen. It is the basic or primary unit of all societies.
  - Family is a most powerful example of social cohesion.
  - It is a Bridge between generations and a Transfer point of civilization.
  - Family acts as a:
    - i. Biological unit: Shares a same pool of genes.
    - ii. Social unit: Shares common physical and social environment.
    - iii. Cultural unit: Shares a common culture and beliefs.
  - The primary social relationship is of Husband and Wife.
  - Family of origin refers to the family into which one is born, whereas Family of procreation refers to the family which one sets up after marriage.
- Types of families:
  - 1. Nuclear family (Elementary family / Unitary family): Consists of Married couples (husband and wife) and their dependent children. They tend to occupy the same dwelling space.
    - New families: Nuclear family under 10 years duration. It is important in studies related to family planning.
  - 2. Joint family (Extended family): Number of Married couple and their Children living under same Household, sharing the same Kitchen and having a common family Purse.
    - All Males are blood relatives while females are wives, daughter, sisters, and widow.
    - Property is held common.
    - All authority is vested in senior male member of the family.
    - Provides a greater economic and social security to the old, the helpless, and the unemployed.
  - 3. 3 Generation family: It is an extension of joint family. Young couples living with their parents and having their own children. Consists of a household with representatives of 3 generations related to each other by direct descent. Occurs usually when young couples are unable to find separate housing accommodation.
  - 4. Complex family: Any family involving more than 2 adults.
  - 5. Communal family: Family where all of its members are playing their part in its management (division of labour).
  - 6. Conjugal family: Nuclear family of adult partners and their children, where relationship is focused on emotional bonds rather than strict duties and obligations.
  - 7. Matrifocal family: Consists of mother and her children.
  - 8. Consanguineal family: Consists of mother and her children and other people usually the family of mothers.
  - 9. Dysfunctional family: Family in which conflict, misbehavior, and even abuse on a member occur continuously.

FAMILY LIFE CYCLE

- The normal family cycle is generally conceived as having six phases:
  - 1. Formation: Begins with Marriage, ends with Birth of 1st child.
  - 2. Extension: Begins with Birth of 1st child, ends with Birth of last child.
  - 3. Complete extension: Begins with Birth of last child, ends when 1st child leaves home.
  - 4. Contraction: Begins when 1st child leaves home, ends when Last child leaves home.
  - 5. Complete contraction: Begins when Last child leaves home, ends when 1st spouse dies.
  - 6. Dissolution: Begins when 1st spouse dies, ends with Death of survivor (extinction).

FUNCTIONS OF THE FAMILY

- i. Residence: Providing a clean and decent home.
  - Patrilocal residence: Wife goes to husband's house.
  - Matrilocal residence: Husband goes to wife's house.
- ii. Division of labour: In primitive societies, the male had the sole duty to earn a living, and the female had the total responsibility for care of children. Modern trends involve sharing. Communal family implies all members of the family play a part in the household management.
- iii. Reproduction and bringing up of children: Absolute care of infant and children.
- iv. Socialization: Bridge between generations and transfer point of civilization. Transmission of cultural patterns, traditions, customs, language, dressing sense, eating habits.
- v. Economic functions: Economic partnership for the family and progeny, including inheritance of property.
- vi. Social care: Giving status in the society i.e., use of family names, protecting members from insult, regulating marital activities.
- vii. Care of dependents: Sick, pregnant, aged, and handicapped. Increase drug compliance (example DOTS) is seen when family members are involved in observation.
- viii. Personality formation and stabilization: Act as a shock absorber to the stress and strain of life in order to maintain mental equilibrium and stable relationships.

ROLE OF FAMILY IN HEALTH AND DISEASES

- 1. Child rearing: The physical care of the dependent young (feeding, nutrition, hygiene, sleep, clothing, discipline, habit training). Much depends on factors like capital resources and cultural traditions.
- 2. Socialization: Teaching the young the values of society and transmitting information, culture, and beliefs.
- 3. Personality formation: The capacity of an individual to withstand stress and strain is determined largely by early experience in the family.
- 4. Care of dependant adults: Front-line care for the sick and injured, pregnant women, the aged, and the handicapped. The family does more nursing than the hospital.
- 5. Stabilization of adult personality: Family acts as a shock absorber. Prevents stress diseases like peptic ulcer, colitis, hypertension, and mental illness.
- 6. Familial susceptibility to disease: Family members share a pool of genes and a common environment. 
  - Hemophilia, color blindness, diabetes, schizophrenia etc. are known to have familial inheritance.
  - Congenital malformation is higher among offspring's of consanguineous marriages.
  - Communicable diseases (tuberculosis, common cold, scabies) spread rapidly in families due to common environment.
- 7. Broken family: Family where parents have separated or where one or both parents are dead.
  - Children may show psychopathic behavior, immature personality, growth retardation etc.
  - Children may drift towards crime, addiction, prostitution etc.
  - Dr. John Bowlby brought out clearly the concept of mental deprivation as a dangerous pathogenic factor in child development due to separation.
- 8. Problem family: Family which lags behind with the rest of the community.
  - Parents are unable to meet the physical and emotional needs of their children.
  - Underlying causes include backwardness, poverty, mental and social instability etc.
  - More common in low SES (Socio-Economic Status).
  - Children may drift towards crime, addiction, prostitution etc.

CULTURAL FACTORS IN HEALTH AND DISEASE (AETIOLOGY AND SANITATION)

- 1. Concept of aetiology and cure:
  - I. Supernatural causes:
    - i. Wrath of God: Chickenpox (chotimata) - instead of giving drugs, pujas are done to appease the gods.
    - ii. Breach of taboo: Venereal diseases are supposed to be due to intercourse with a low caste woman or a woman during menstruation.
    - iii. Past sins: Leprosy / TB are believed to be due to past sins.
    - iv. Evil eye: Children are considered to be most susceptible. Charms and amulets are used to ward it off.
    - v. Spirit intrusion: Hysteria and Epilepsy are regarded as spirit intrusion, hence services of an exorcist are taken.
  - II. Physical causes:
    - i. Effects of weather: Heat stroke (attack of Loo) treated with oil / ghee on soles and Aam-pana (mango pulp) with a pinch of salt.
    - ii. Impure blood: Skin diseases (scabies) considered to be due to impure blood, so Neem leaves and flowers are eaten to purify blood.
- 2. Environmental sanitation:
  - I. Disposal of human excreta: Open field defecation in rural areas considered harmless and building toilets in home are not accepted.
  - II. Disposal of wastes: Solid waste (refuse) is thrown in front of houses and is allowed to decompose, in order to be used as a manure. Cow dung is dried and used as a fuel.
  - III. Water supply: Wells in villages are used for all purposes i.e., drinking, bathing, washing clothes / animals etc. Rivers are considered holy and dipping / drinking during pilgrims is considered sacred. Step wells were associated with Guinea worm disease.
  - IV. Housing: Rural houses are mostly kachha and damped as they are plastered with mud and cow dung and have no separate kitchen, toilets, or animal keeping areas.

CULTURAL FACTORS IN FOOD, MCH, HYGIENE, SEX AND MARRIAGE

- 3. Food habits:
  - Diets of the people are influenced by religious customs, beliefs, and local conditions (soil, climate).
  - Vegetarianism is given a place of honor in Hindu society. Muslims abhor pork, Hindus abhor beef.
  - Foods considered to generate heat in the body: eggs, jaggery, meat, fish.
  - Foods considered to be cold for the body: curd, lemon, milk, vegetables.
  - Adulteration of milk is tied to a belief that boiling pure milk will dry up the donor animal's secretion.
  - Fasts (Ramzan, Hindu occasions) are important adjuncts to religion.
  - Eating and drinking from common utensils is a sign of brotherhood among Muslims.
  - In some societies, men eat first and women last and poorly.
- 4. Mother and Child health (MCH):
  - Good practices: Customs such as prolonged breast-feeding, oil bath, massage, and exposure to sun etc.
  - Bad practices: Foods like egg, fish, meat are forbidden during pregnancy in some parts. In rural areas, home delivery is preferred than institutional delivery and conducted by a traditional untrained dai. Colostrum is considered harmful and is drained away. Branding and drastic purgatives are also bad customs.
  - Uncertain practices: Applying kajal or black soot for beautification and warding off the evil eye. Often blamed for transmitting trachoma.
- 5. Personal hygiene:
  - Twigs of Neem tree (Datoon) used as a toothbrush.
  - Ashes and charcoal used for teeth.
  - Traditional barbers (Nai) in the countryside don't sterilize their instruments too often.
  - Smoking Hubble bubble (Hukka) spreads TB.
  - Burning end of cigar in mouth is a common custom in Andhra Pradesh and is associated with oral cancer.
  - Purdah system deprives women of beneficial sunrays and has high incidence of TB.
  - Sleeping on the ground exposes people to insect bites.
  - Walking bare feet (not wearing shoes) is associated with transmission of hook-worm disease.
  - Circumcision is a prevalent custom among Muslims with religious sanction.
- 6. Sex and Marriage:
  - Woman on menstruation is forbidden to pray in temples or have intercourse.
  - Orthodox Jews are forbidden to have intercourse for seven days after the menstruation ceases.
  - Early marriages are a social custom in India.
  - Polygamy: Marriage of one man with several women.
  - Polyandry: Marriage of several men with one woman.

subsections.title: Medical Sociology in Practice and Welfare Systems
subsections.content:
HOSPITAL SOCIOLOGY AND THE SICK ROLE

- Hospitals are among the most complex organizations in modern society.
- The modern hospital is a social universe with a multiplicity of goals, profusion of personnel, and an extremely fine division of labor.
- Historically, the hospital was a place of refuge for the sick and homeless, a charitable institution where one went to die rather than to be cured. Today, hospitals are concerned with active medical treatment, mobilizing the latest in medical sciences to produce a cure.
- The hospital today is a system of increasing complexity: it is a hotel, a school, a laboratory, and a stage set for treatment.
- It is said that each hospital has a personality of its own, including a tempo of work, an emotional atmosphere, traditions, and a community of staff and patients.
- Specialization in medicine has created problems for the traditional doctor-patient relationship, dividing the doctor and patient and placing strain on their relationship. Over-specialization contributes to depersonalization.
- The sick role: To be ill is more than a medical condition. The sick person has to behave in certain prescribed ways and becomes passive for the most part. Four aspects of the sick role have been described:
  - 1. The sick person is exempted from normal social responsibilities depending upon the severity of the illness.
  - 2. The sick person needs to be cared for.
  - 3. The sick role is regarded as a misfortune.
  - 4. The sick person is obliged to seek competent medical care and to cooperate with the doctor in the process of getting well.

INTERVIEWING TECHNIQUES

- Interview is a device for investigation and an instrument of research used by social scientists.
- Kinds of interview:
  - Direct or structured interview: A schedule containing a set of predetermined questions is prepared. The researcher gets answers to these questions only.
  - Non-directive or Unstructured interview: No predetermined questions are asked. Information is collected by free discussion.
  - Focussed interview: Generally used to study the social and psychological effects of mass communication (e.g., reaction to a film show or radio program). Focuses on specific experiences, attitudes, and emotional responses.
  - Repetitive interview: Used when it is desired to note the gradual influence of some social or psychological process.
- Steps for conducting an interview:
  - 1. Establishing contact (Prior appointment).
  - 2. Starting an interview (From general to specific).
  - 3. Securing rapport (Establishing trust).
  - 4. Recall (Giving time for the interviewee to recollect).
  - 5. Probe questions (To catch side-tracks carefully).
  - 6. Encouragement (Interpolating complimentary expressions).
  - 7. Guiding the interview (Keeping on track without offending).
  - 8. Recording (Reduced to a minimum during the interview).
  - 9. Closing the interview (Should not be ended abruptly).
  - 10. Report (Compiled when the mind is still fresh).

DOCTOR-PATIENT AND DOCTOR-NURSE RELATIONSHIP

- Doctor-Patient Relationship: A successful doctor is one who knows how to communicate with their patient. Patients who do not behave according to expectations are often labeled as un-cooperative.
- 3 levels of communication (Communication - I.E.C):
  - 1. Communication on an Intellectual plane: Involves easy to understand explanation of the patient's condition in simple terms. Practitioners of modern medicine tend to be sophisticated, leaving a wide gap between the intellectual level of the practitioner and the illiterate masses. This is known as Social distance.
  - 2. Communication on an Emotional plane: The doctor must have a sympathetic ear towards the complaints of patients and their relatives.
  - 3. Communication on a Cultural plane: The doctor should be aware of the culture and customs of the community with which he is dealing. This is why Indigenous and Folk systems of medicine are successful in rural areas as they are part of that community.
- Medical Ethics: The doctor-patient relationship is governed by medical ethics drawn from Hippocratic, Chinese, and Indian traditions. Transformation of medical ethics has been stimulated by medical biotechnology and social changes in human rights.
- Doctor-Nurse Relationship:
  - Medicine and Nursing have common goals: the preservation and restoration of health.
  - The primary role of Medicine comprises diagnosis and treatment (i.e., the Cure process).
  - The primary role of Nursing lies in the Care process (caring, helping, comforting, and guiding).

MEDICAL SOCIAL WORKER (M.S.W)

- Medical Social Worker (MSW): A paramedical worker, trained in Social case work, the Art of interviewing people, and Coping strategies.
- Forms a link between the Institution and the Community.
- Principle work: To visit the family and probe to collect the social history of the patient to supplement the medical history.
- Medical social work uses case work to find out the social background of illness. This information helps the doctor in social diagnosis, treating illness, and estimating prognosis.

OPERATIONAL RESEARCH

- Operational Research: Application of scientific methods to the study of human organization and services.
- Objective: To develop knowledge about institutions and people working in communities served by them.
- Phases in operational research:
  - 1. Formulation of problem.
  - 2. Collection of data.
  - 3. Analysis of data.
  - 4. Deriving solutions.
  - 5. Forecasting results.
  - 6. Testing solutions in pilot projects.
  - 7. Implementing the solution.
- Operational Research Team includes: Public health administrator, Social scientist, Epidemiologist, Statistician, and Field workers.

CONSUMER PROTECTION ACT (COPRA) AND PATIENT RIGHTS

- Consumer Protection Act (1986) - COPRA: Provides consumers a forum for speedy redressal of their grievances against medical services.
- If a patient or his relatives feel that suffering or death of the patient is because of negligence by the concerned doctor or health facility, they can complain to the Medical Council of India or to the consumer court.
- The Council can only cancel the registration of the doctor temporarily or permanently but cannot punish a doctor. The consumer courts can only give compensation.
- Decision should be taken within 3 to 6 months.
- There is no court fee payment and the person can plead his own case.
- Limits of compensation by Consumer Courts:
  - District consumer court: Up to Rs 20 lacs.
  - State commission: Rs 20 lacs to Rs 1 crore.
  - National commission: Above 1 crore.
- Rights of the Patients (Recognized under the act):
  - Right to be informed.
  - Right to choose Treatment and Health care system.
  - Right of Safety.
  - Right to be Heard and seek Redressal.
  - Right for Consent.
  - Right to Confidentiality.
  - Right to choose second opinion.
  - From iatrogenic infections (Safety aspect).
  - In case of being victim, Right to complain and have compensation.
  - Right to refuse to participate in research.
  - Right to get copies of medical records.

SOCIAL SECURITY, ASSISTANCE, AND INSURANCE

- Social Security: Security that society furnishes against certain risks to which its members are exposed (sickness, maternity, old age, death, disability, unemployment etc.). Refers to Social Insurance Program providing social protection against poverty.
- Bismarck introduced social insurance in Germany which became a model for European countries.
- Approaches to the social security system:
  - Social assistance: Noncontributory benefit extended to vulnerable groups.
  - Social insurance: Contributory benefit extended to individuals as a matter of right.
- Social security for Industrial Worker:
  - Workman's compensation act 1923
  - Central maternity benefit act 1961
  - Factory act 1948
  - ESI act 1948 (Including disablement benefit)
  - Family pension scheme 1971
- Social security for Civil Servants: For employee of central and state government (Pension schemes, provident fund, medical care etc.).
- Social security for General Public: LIC India, ESI scheme, Public provident fund etc.
- Social Safety Net: Collection of services provided by the state, which prevent individuals from falling into poverty beyond a certain level (unemployment benefit, universal health care, homeless shelters, minimum wage, subsidizes services such as public transport).
  - High Social Safety Net includes high Birth Rate, high MMR, high IMR.
- Difference between Social Assistance and Social Insurance:
  - 1. Contribution: Social Assistance is Non contributory. Social Insurance is Contributory.
  - 2. Feeling involved: Social Assistance involves feeling of charity or sympathy. Social Insurance involves a sense of responsibility for future planning.
  - 3. Participation: Social Assistance is generally compulsory. Social Insurance is Generally voluntary.
  - 4. Motive: Social Assistance motive is maintenance of minimum level of living. Social Insurance motive is future planning.
  - 5. Benefits: In Social Assistance, benefits are in form of cash or in kind of food aid (if paid, it is much larger than the contribution). In Social Insurance, benefits are in proportion to the contribution.
  - 6. Design: Social Assistance is designed to supplement incomes of vulnerable groups (elderly/disabled). Social Insurance is designed to supplement income of individual as a part of future planning.
  - 7. Examples: Social Assistance examples include National old age pension scheme, National family benefit, National maternity benefit scheme. Social Insurance examples include ESI scheme, LIC scheme, PF scheme.

AYUSHMAN BHARAT SCHEME (AB-PMJAY)

- Ayushman Bharat Scheme / Pradhan Mantri Jan Arogya Yojna (AB-PMJAY):
  - National Health Protection Scheme / Mission.
  - Entitlement based scheme.
  - Largest health protection scheme globally.
  - Cover over 10 crore poor and vulnerable families.
- OBJECTIVES (2018-22):
  - Focus on wellness of poor families (BPL).
  - Providing medical benefits to poor families (BPL).
  - Establishing Health and wellness centers.
- Strategies:
  - 1. National Health Protection Scheme.
  - 2. Cashless benefits from any public/private empaneled hospitals across the country. Full health care is provided to the family by Empaneled Health Care Facilities (EHCF).
  - 3. Health and Wellness centers: Provide Comprehensive health care, free essential medicine and diagnostics Services.
  - 4. Cover for poor, deprived rural families and identified occupational urban workers.
  - 5. Providing coverage up to 5 lakh / family / year in secondary and tertiary care hospitals. Entire family covered with NO limit on age / gender / family size / type of disease.
  - 6. All Pre-Existing illnesses are covered.
  - 7. Release of Grant-in-Aid through Escrow Account.
  - 8. IT platform for paperless and cashless transaction.
  - 9. Implementation through Insurance and/or Trust and/or Mixed Mode.
  - 10. Convergence with Central as well as State Health Insurance Schemes (Rashtriya Swasthya Bima Yojana (RSBY) and Senior Citizen Health Insurance Scheme (SCHIS)).
- Organization:
  - National level: Ayushman Bharat National Health Protection Mission Agency (AB-NHPMA).
  - States/ UTs: State Health Agency (SHA).
- Modes of implementation: States are given flexibility to decide the mode of implementation.
  - i. Insurance Mode: States / UTs will select Insurance Company.
  - ii. Trust / Assurance Mode: Through Society / Trust of State Health Department.
  - iii. Mixed Model: (Insurance + Assurance).
- Target Group for Urban: Under 11 occupational criteria's.
- Target Group for Rural:
  - D1 - Only one room with kachha walls and kachha roof.
  - D2 - No adult member between age 16 to 59.
  - D3 - Female headed households with no adult male member between age 16 to 59.
  - D4 - Disabled member and no able-bodied adult member.
  - D5 - SC/ST households.
  - D7 - Landless households deriving major part of their income from manual casual labour.
- Automatically includes: People without shelter, People living on alms, Manual scavenger families, Primitive tribal groups, Bonded labour.
- Major Impact:
  - Reduction of Out of Pocket (OOP) expenditure.
  - Increased benefit cover to nearly 40% of the population.
  - Covering almost all secondary and many tertiary hospitalizations.
  - Increased access to quality health services and medication.
  - Unmet needs of the population which remained hidden due to lack of financial resources will be catered.
  - Timely treatments, patient satisfaction and improvements in health outcomes.

title: Tribal Health
content:
OVERVIEW OF THE CHAPTER
Tribal people in India have historically remained marginalized geographically, socio-economically, and politically. As a result, health and healthcare in tribal areas have remained an unsolved challenge. This section outlines the demographic profile, health indicators, burden of disease, healthcare infrastructure shortfalls, and expert recommendations for improving tribal health.

DEFINITIONS AND DEMOGRAPHIC PROFILE

LEGAL DEFINITION
- Scheduled Tribes (STs): The term first appeared in the Constitution of India. Article 366 (25) defines scheduled tribes as "such tribes or tribal communities or parts of or groups within such tribes or tribal communities as are deemed under Article 342 to be Scheduled Tribes for the purpose of this constitution".
- The list of scheduled tribes is state/UT specific. A community declared as a scheduled tribe in one state need not be so in another state.

POPULATION AND DISTRIBUTION
- Total Tribal Population: Over 104 million tribal people live in India.
- Proportion: They account for 8.6 percent of the country's total population.
- Number of Tribes: Spread across 705 recognized tribes.
- Geography: Largely concentrated in 10 states and 8 North-East states. They mostly live in hilly and forested areas.
- Rural Concentration: Nearly 90 percent of the tribal population lives in rural areas.
- Highest Absolute Population: Madhya Pradesh has the largest tribal population (15 million), followed by Maharashtra (10 million), Odisha (9 million), and Rajasthan (9 million).
- Highest Concentration: The concentration (percentage) of tribal population is highest amongst the North-East states.
- Gender Breakdown: 52.5 million males and 52.0 million females.

KEY DEMOGRAPHIC INDICATORS
- Total Fertility Rate (TFR): 2.48 (within reasonable limits, and the decline is comparable to other populations).
- Sex Ratio (2011): 990 females per 1000 males (higher than the national average of 943).
- Child Sex Ratio (0-6 years, 2011): 957 girls to 1000 boys (declined from 972 in 2001, but still higher than the general population average of 914).
- Literacy Rate (2011): 59.0 percent (improved from 47.1 percent in 2001).
  - Male literacy: Increased from 59.2 percent to 68.5 percent.
  - Female literacy: Increased from 34.8 percent to 49.4 percent.
- Life Expectancy at Birth: 63.9 years for ST population (significantly lower than the 67 years for the general population).
