REPRODUCTIVE, MATERNAL, NEWBORN, CHILD HEALTH AND ADOLESCENT (RMNCH+A) STRATEGY

MATERNAL AND REPRODUCTIVE HEALTH
- Total Fertility Rate: 2.09 (vs 1.99 in all population, 2019-2021 data).
- Couple using any contraceptive: 64.4 percent (vs 66.7 percent in all population).
- Institutional Delivery: 68.0 percent (much lower than the national average of 88.6 percent).
- Skilled Birth Attendance: 71.5 percent deliveries are conducted by skilled health personnel (vs 89.4 percent in all population).
- Postnatal Care: Coverage remains poor. Only about 37 percent of tribal women reported receiving any postnatal care within 48 hours after home delivery.
- Risk Factors: Early marriage, early childbirth, low BMI, and high incidence of anaemia are known factors for high maternal mortality among tribal women.

CHILD MORTALITY INDICATORS (NFHS-5, PER 1000 LIVE BIRTHS)
- Infant Mortality Rate (IMR): 41.6 (vs 35.2 in all population).
- Neonatal Mortality Rate: 28.8 (vs 24.9 in all population).
- Post-neonatal Mortality Rate: 12.9 (vs 10.3 in all population).
- Under-5 Mortality Rate (U5MR): 50.3 (vs 41.9 in all population).
- 1-4 Year Child Mortality Rate: 9.0 (vs 6.9 in all population).
- Trend: Time trends show a major improvement in mortality rates, though they remain higher than national averages.

IMMUNIZATION AND NUTRITIONAL STATUS (2019-2021 DATA)
- Fully Immunized Infants: 76.8 percent (comparable to 76.6 percent in all population).
- No Vaccination: 4.1 percent.
- Nutritional Deficiencies (Under-5 Children):
  - Stunted: 40.9 percent (vs 35.5 percent general).
  - Wasted: 23.2 percent (vs 19.3 percent general).
  - Underweight: 39.5 percent (vs 32.1 percent general).
- Malnutrition (both micro-malnutrition and macro-malnutrition) remains substantially higher in tribal children compared to the general population.

BURDEN OF DISEASE IN TRIBAL COMMUNITIES
Epidemiological transition is occurring in tribal areas, resulting in a triple burden of diseases: communicable diseases, non-communicable diseases, and mental illnesses/addictions.

COMMUNICABLE DISEASES
- Tuberculosis (TB): Estimated prevalence of pulmonary TB in tribal communities is significantly higher at 703 per 100,000 population (against 256 per 100,000 nationally).
  - RNTCP Interventions: Free diagnosis and treatment, active case finding, Additional TB Units, Designated Microscopy Centres (DMC) in tribal areas, compensation for transport, higher salaries/allowances for staff, and TB Health Visitors (TBHVs).
  - Mobile TB Diagnostic Van (MTDV): Equipped with X-ray and Sputum Microscopy. Initially undertaken in 5 States (Madhya Pradesh, Gujarat, Chhattisgarh, Rajasthan, and Jharkhand) across 17 districts with 35 fabricated vans covering 17.65 million people.
- Leprosy: Out of 1,35,485 new cases detected in 2016-17, 18.90 percent (25,474) were from scheduled tribes. Funds are allotted to NGOs to work in tribal areas for IEC, deformity prevention, and follow-up.
- Malaria and Vector Borne Diseases: Tribal communities constitute 8 percent of the national population but account for about 30 percent of all malaria cases, over 60 percent of Plasmodium falciparum cases, and 50 percent of malaria-associated mortality. Additional inputs are provided via the Global Fund (NE states) and World Bank.
- Kala-azar: World Bank support in Bihar, Jharkhand, and West Bengal. North Eastern states receive 100 percent central assistance for elimination.

NON-COMMUNICABLE DISEASES
- Hypertension: Affects 1 out of 4 tribal adults. Prevalence increases with age, tobacco/alcohol consumption, and sedentary lifestyle. Awareness is extremely low (only 5 percent of affected men and 9 percent of affected women knew their status).
- Blindness and Visual Impairment: Goal of NPCBVI is to reduce blindness to 0.3 percent by 2020. Interventions include dedicated eye units, mobile ophthalmic units, and contractual ophthalmic manpower, heavily subsidized (90:10 funding) in North-East states.

GENETIC DISORDERS
- Sickle Cell Disease (Anaemia and Trait): Prevalence varies between 1 to 40 percent in different tribes (mostly heterozygous). Sickle cell anaemia (homozygous) has a prevalence of 1 in 86 births among tribal communities in central India.
- G6PD Deficiency: Prevalence varies from 0.7 to 15.6 percent among 14 primitive tribal populations studied.

MENTAL HEALTH, ADDICTIONS, AND VIOLENCE
- Tobacco Use: Almost 72 percent of tribal men (15-54 years) use tobacco compared to 56 percent of non-tribal men.
- Alcohol Use: About 50 percent of tribal men consume some form of alcohol.
- Animal Attacks: Common due to forest surroundings (snake bites, dog bites, scorpion stings).

HEALTH CARE INFRASTRUCTURE AND TRIBAL DEVELOPMENT

DEVELOPMENT INITIATIVES
- Tribal Sub-Plan (TSP) Strategy: Now known as the Scheduled Tribal Component (STC), it was adopted in the 5th Five Year Plan for the accelerated development of tribal people.

HEALTH CARE INFRASTRUCTURE NORMS (NATIONAL HEALTH MISSION)
- Health Sub-centre (HSC): 1 per 3,000 population in tribal/hilly areas (vs normal norms).
- Primary Health Centre (PHC): 1 per 20,000 population.
- Community Health Centre (CHC): 1 per 80,000 population.

INFRASTRUCTURE SHORTFALLS (Data from 18 states & 3 UTs)
- Sub-centres: 27 percent shortfall (4,996 HSCs) across 11 states.
- PHCs: 40 percent shortfall (1,023 PHCs) across 7 deficient states.
- CHCs: 31 percent shortfall (209 CHCs) across 10 deficient states.
- Dadra and Nagar Haveli reported an 8 percent shortfall in Sub-centres and a shortfall of 1 CHC.

HEALTH HUMAN RESOURCE (HHR)
There is a massive gap in human resources in tribal health centres attributed to limited professional growth, social isolation, weak policies, and poor working conditions.

EXPERT COMMITTEE ON TRIBAL HEALTH (2013)
- Constituted jointly by the Ministry of Health and Family Welfare (MoHFW) and Ministry of Tribal Affairs (MoTA) in October 2013 under the chairmanship of Dr. Abhay Bang.
- Key Recommendations:
  1. The healthcare provider, as far as possible, should be a local tribal.
  2. Ensure that local tribal people are trained and deployed in the health force to effect a vibrant, responsive, and accessible workforce.
  3. Place the centre of gravity of the workforce closer to the communities (not at the top with specialists).
  4. ASHAs in tribal areas should have an expanded role (eight types of functions and total 4 hours of work per day expected).
  5. Mid-level care providers should be created through bridge courses and placed at the sub-centres.
  6. Substantially increase the total salary of Medical Officers (MOs) working in tribal areas.
  7. Create dedicated medical colleges in tribal districts exclusively for tribal students from scheduled areas.

title: Sustainable Development Goals
content:
OVERVIEW OF THE CHAPTER
The transition from the Millennium Development Goals (MDGs) to the Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs) marked a significant shift in global health and development agendas. While the MDGs provided a focused set of goals primarily targeting developing nations, the SDGs present a comprehensive, integrated, and universal agenda. This chapter details the MDG achievements, the framework of the SDGs (especially Goal 3), interactions with other goals, specific strategies for diseases and health systems, and India's adaptation and progress.

MILLENNIUM DEVELOPMENT GOALS (MDGS)

- In September 2000, representatives from 189 countries met at the Millennium Summit in New York to adopt the United Nations Millennium Declaration, now widely known as the Millennium Development Goals (MDGs).
- The government set a target date of 2015 to meet the MDGs.
- The MDGs consisted of 8 Goals, 18 Targets, and 48 Indicators.
- The 8 Goals were:
  1. Eradicate extreme poverty and hunger.
  2. Achieve universal primary education.
  3. Promote gender equality and empower women.
  4. Reduce child mortality.
  5. Improve maternal health.
  6. Combat HIV/AIDS, Malaria and other diseases.
  7. Ensure environmental sustainability.
  8. A global partnership for development.

MDG ACHIEVEMENTS AND HEALTH RELATED GOALS

- Progress towards the MDGs was impressive, though much work remained. The dramatic progress paved the way for more ambitious achievements by 2030.
- There were 6 directly Health Related Goals in the MDGs. Their key achievements by the end of their term include:
  - Goal 1: Eradicate Extreme Poverty and Hunger.
    - Poverty: The number of people living on less than 1.25 US dollars a day declined by more than half.
    - Proportion of Undernourished: Fallen from 23% to 13%.
  - Goal 4: Reduce Child Mortality.
    - Under 5 Mortality: Reduced by 53%.
  - Goal 5: Improve Maternal Health.
    - Adolescent Birth Rate: Reduced from 59 to 51.
    - Antenatal Visit Coverage: Nearly doubled from 31% to 60%.
    - Skilled Birth Attendant (SBA): Also increased.
    - Maternal Mortality Ratio (MMR): Declined by 44%.
  - Goal 6: Combat HIV/AIDS, Malaria and Other Diseases.
    - Number of Measles Cases: Declined by 67%.
    - HIV infection: Reduced by 40%.
    - Global Malaria Incidence Rate: Fallen by 37%, and Mortality Rate by 58%.
    - TB Case Detection Rate: Increased from 38% to 68% while Treatment Success Rate reached 85%.
  - Goal 7: Ensure Environmental Sustainability.
    - Safe Drinking Water: The world met the target relating to access to Safe Drinking Water.
  - Goal 8: A Global Partnership for Development.

SUSTAINABLE DEVELOPMENT GOALS (SDGS) INTRODUCTION

- Successor to the MDG, the SDGs came into force on 1st January 2016 and span the period of 2016 to 2030.
- The agenda comprises 17 Goals with 169 Targets.
- Theme: "Leaving no one behind".
- Sustainable development is defined as development that meets the needs of the present without compromising the ability of future generations to meet their own needs.
- The agenda is built on the 5 P's: People, Planet, Prosperity, Peace, and Partnership.
- The new goals are unique as they are universal (applying to all countries), aiming to promote prosperity while protecting the planet.
- They recognize that ending poverty must go hand-in-hand with strategies that build economic growth and address social needs (education, health, social protection, standard of living, job opportunities) while tackling climate changes.
- Implementation relies on sustainable development policies, requiring resource mobilization, financing strategies, and contribution from all stakeholders (governments, civil societies, private sector).

THE 2030 AGENDA AND HEALTH (SDG 3)

- There is only 1 directly Health Related Goal in the SDGs, which is Goal 3: Ensure Healthy Lives and promote Wellbeing for all at all Ages.
- Paragraph 26 of the 2030 agenda addresses health commitments, including achieving universal health coverage, ensuring access to sexual and reproductive healthcare, and ending preventable maternal and child deaths.
- Goal 3 includes 13 targets covering all major health priorities:
  - Four targets on the unfinished and expanded MDGs.
  - Four targets addressing non-communicable diseases (NCDs), mental health, injuries, and environmental issues.
  - Four "means-of-implementation" targets.
- Universal Health Coverage (UHC) is the key to achieving all targets and developing strong resilient health systems.
- Note: Even though the 2030 Agenda refers multiple times to human rights, it does not specifically mention that health is a human right.

TARGETS AND INDICATORS FOR SDG 3 (WITH GLOBAL AND INDIA DATA)

- Target 3.1: By 2030, reduce the global maternal mortality ratio (MMR) to less than 70 per 100,000 live births.
  - 3.1.1 Maternal deaths per 100,000 live births: Global 223, India 97 (2018-2020).
  - 3.1.2 Proportion of births attended by skilled health personnel: Global 86%, India 89% (2013-2021).
- Target 3.2: By 2030, end preventable deaths of newborns and children under 5 years of age. Reduce Neonatal Mortality Rate (NMR) to at least 12 per 1000 live births and Under 5 Mortality Rate to at least 25 per 1000 live births.
  - 3.2.1 Under-5 mortality rate: Global 38, India 31 (2021).
  - 3.2.2 Neonatal mortality rate: Global 18, India 19 (2021).
- Target 3.3: By 2030, end the epidemics of AIDS, TB, Malaria, and neglected tropical diseases (NTDs), and combat Hepatitis and Water Borne Diseases.
  - 3.3.1 Number of new HIV infections per 1,000 uninfected population: Global 0.19%, India 0.05% (2021).
  - 3.3.2 Tuberculosis incidence per 1,000 persons per year: Global 124, India 210 (2021).
  - 3.3.3 Malaria incident cases per 1,000 persons at risk per year: Global 59.2, India 3.2 (2021).
  - 3.3.4 Hepatitis B infections (infants receiving 3 doses of vaccine %): Global 80, India 85 (2021).
  - 3.3.5 Number of people requiring interventions against NTDs: Global 1653.2 million, India 677.2 million (2021).
- Target 3.4: By 2030, reduce by 33% (one-third) premature mortality from Non-Communicable Diseases through prevention and treatment, and promote mental health and well-being.
  - 3.4.1 Mortality of cardiovascular disease, cancer, diabetes or chronic respiratory disease: Global 17.8%, India 21.9% (2019).
  - 3.4.2 Suicide mortality rate: Global 9.2, India 12.7 (2019).
- Target 3.5: Strengthen the prevention and treatment of substance abuse, including narcotic drug abuse and harmful use of alcohol.
  - 3.5.1 Coverage of treatment interventions for substance use disorders.
  - 3.5.2 Harmful use of alcohol (litres of pure alcohol per capita for 15+ years): Global 5.8, India 4.9 (2019).
- Target 3.6: By 2020, halve (50% reduction) the number of global deaths and injuries from road traffic accidents.
  - 3.6.1 Number of road traffic fatal injury deaths within 30 days per 100,000 population: Global 16.7, India 15.6 (2019).
- Target 3.7: By 2030, ensure universal access to sexual and reproductive health-care services (RTI / STD services), including family planning, information, and education.
  - 3.7.1 Percentage of women of reproductive age (15-49) with family planning needs satisfied with modern methods: Global 77.5, India 72.8 (2013-2021).
  - 3.7.2 Adolescent birth rate (per 1,000 women aged 10-14 and 15-19): Global 41.9, India 10.6 (2013-2021).
- Target 3.8: Achieve Universal Health Coverage (UHC) with quality health care, and affordable medicine and vaccines.
  - 3.8.1 Coverage of tracer interventions (e.g., child immunization, ART, TB treatment, hypertension treatment, skilled attendant at birth).
  - 3.8.2 Fraction of the population protected against catastrophic out-of-pocket health expenditure.
- Target 3.9: By 2030, substantially reduce deaths and illnesses from hazardous chemicals, Air, Water, and Soil Pollution.
  - 3.9.1 Mortality rate attributed to household and ambient air pollution per lac population: Global 114.1, India 184.3 (2016).
  - 3.9.2 Mortality rate attributed to hazardous chemicals, water and soil pollution per lac population: Global 11.7, India 187 (2016).
- Target 3.a: Strengthen the implementation of Tobacco Control (WHO Framework Convention).
  - 3.a.1 Age-standardized prevalence of current tobacco use (15+ years): Global 22.3%, India 27.2% (2020).
- Target 3.b: Support the Research and Development of vaccines and medicines.
  - 3.b.1 Proportion of the population with access to affordable medicines and vaccines.
  - 3.b.2 Total net official development assistance to medical research and basic health sectors.
- Target 3.c: Substantially increase health financing, recruitment, and training of the health workforce.
  - 3.c.1 Health worker density and distribution per 10,000 population: Global 25, India 30.2 (2006-2013).
- Target 3.d: Strengthen the capacity of all countries for Early Warning, Risk Reduction, and Management of National and Global Health Risk.
  - 3.d.1 Percentage of attributes of 13 core capacities attained: Global 71, India 95 (2010-2017).

INTERACTIONS WITH ECONOMIC, OTHER SOCIAL AND ENVIRONMENTAL SDGS

- Health is linked to many non-health goals as it is influenced by economic, social, and environmental determinants.
- UHC (Target 3.8) is the only target that cuts across all targets of the health goal and is linked to health-related targets in other goals.
- Key targets in other goals linked to health (SDG 3):
  - 1.3: Appropriate social protection systems and measures for all.
  - 2.2: End all forms of malnutrition (stunting, wasting) in children under five.
  - 4.2: Access to quality early childhood development and pre-primary education.
  - 4.a: Safe, non-violent, inclusive learning environments.
  - 5.2: Eliminate violence against all women and girls.
  - 5.3: Eliminate harmful practices like child marriage and female genital mutilation.
  - 5.6: Universal access to sexual and reproductive health and rights.
  - 6.1: Universal access to safe and affordable drinking water.
  - 6.2: Adequate sanitation and hygiene, ending open defecation.
  - 6.3: Improve water quality by reducing pollution.
  - 10.4: Adopt policies (fiscal, wage, social protection) for greater equality.
  - 11.5: Reduce deaths and economic losses caused by disasters.
  - 16.1: Reduce all forms of violence and related death rates everywhere.
  - 16.2: End abuse, exploitation, and violence against children.
  - 16.6: Develop effective accountable and transparent institutions.
  - 16.9: Provide legal identity for all, including birth registration.
  - 17.18: Enhance capacity-building for high-quality, disaggregated data.

REPRODUCTIVE, MATERNAL, NEWBORN AND CHILD HEALTH (RMNCH)

- The Global Strategy for Women's, Children's and Adolescent's Health (2016-2030) aligns with SDG targets around the themes of "Survive-Thrive-Transform".
- Adolescents are a vulnerable group deserving concerted attention, as road injuries, HIV/AIDS, and self-harm are the leading causes of adolescent deaths.
- RMNCH is a key category of UHC indicators, assessed via family planning, antenatal care (4 or more visits), skilled attendance at birth, full child immunization, and health-seeking behavior for suspected pneumonia.

INFECTIOUS DISEASES

- SDG targets aim to outstrip achievements made since 2000 and progress towards disease elimination.
- New Global Strategies include:
  - UNAIDS Global Strategy Fast Track: Ending the AIDS epidemic by 2030.
  - The End TB Strategy.
  - Global Technical Strategy for Malaria 2016-2030.
  - NTD roadmap and water/sanitation hygiene strategy 2015-2020.
  - Global Health Sector Strategy on viral hepatitis 2016-2021.
- Specific Plan Targets for 2030 (SDG Target 3.3):
  - AIDS: 90% reduction in new HIV infections and 80% reduction in AIDS-related deaths (compared to 2010).
  - TB: 90% reduction in TB deaths, 80% reduction in incidence (<20 per 100,000), zero catastrophic costs for affected families.
  - Malaria: 90% reduction in global mortality and incidence, elimination in at least 35 countries.
  - NTDs: 90% reduction in the number of people requiring interventions.
  - Hepatitis: 95% decline in new chronic HBV and 80% decline in new chronic HCV; 65% reduction in related deaths.
  - Water-borne diseases: No open defecation (by 2025), universal access to basic drinking water and hygiene.
- Key interventions include preventive chemotherapy, distribution of insecticide-treated nets, harm reduction, child vaccinations (Hep B, rotavirus), and rapid diagnosis and treatment.

NON-COMMUNICABLE DISEASES AND MENTAL HEALTH

- Unlike MDGs, SDGs include specific targets for NCDs. Target 3.4 aims for a one-third reduction in premature NCD mortality (probability of dying between age 30 and 70).
- An integrated "total cardiovascular risk approach" is highly cost-effective and forms a core part of UHC.
- Global Voluntary Indicators for NCDs (by 2025):
  1. 25% relative reduction in premature mortality from CVD, cancer, diabetes, or CRD.
  2. At least 10% relative reduction in the harmful use of alcohol.
  3. 10% relative reduction in insufficient physical activity.
  4. 30% relative reduction in mean population intake of salt/sodium.
  5. 30% relative reduction in current tobacco use.
  6. 25% relative reduction in raised blood pressure.
  7. Halt the rise in diabetes and obesity.
  8. At least 50% of eligible people receive drug therapy and counselling to prevent heart attacks and strokes.
  9. 80% availability of affordable basic technologies and essential medicines.
- Mental Health: Depression and anxiety affect nearly 1 in 10 people globally. Suicide accounts for 8.5% of all deaths in young adults (15-29 years), making it the second leading cause of death in this age group.
- Substance Abuse: Global alcohol consumption in 2015 was 6.3 litres of pure alcohol per person (15+ years). Tobacco was used by over 1.1 billion people.
- Air Pollution: Ambient air pollution caused 3 million deaths in 2012. Target 11.6 monitors PM 2.5 and PM 10 levels in cities.

INJURIES AND VIOLENCE

- Injuries account for 9% of global mortality (over 5 million deaths annually).
- Road Traffic Accidents (Target 3.6): Lead to 1.25 million deaths and 20-50 million injuries annually. They are the leading cause of death for the 15-29 years age group.
- Violence (Target 16.1): Homicide and collective violence account for 10% of injury-related deaths. 80% of homicide victims are men, and 60% are aged 15-44.
- Conflict deaths: Estimated 1,52,000 people killed in wars in 2015 (0.3% of global deaths).

HEALTH SYSTEM STRENGTHENING

- Strengthening health systems is central to the overall health goal and essential to achieve UHC (Target 3.8).
- Transitioning requires adapting to demographic shifts, epidemiological transitions, and new technologies.
- Target 3.b focuses on research and development investments and ensuring affordable access to medicines and vaccines for developing countries.

NEW INDICATORS (ADDED IN 2020)

- SDG 2.2.3: Tracks the global prevalence of anaemia among women of reproductive age. In 2016, the prevalence was 32.8% (615.8 million women), highest in the WHO South-East Asia region (45.8%).
- SDG 3.b.1: Tracks Human Papillomavirus (HPV) vaccine coverage. Targeting 9-14-year-old girls, global coverage increased from 3% in 2010 to 12% in 2018.
- SDG 3.b.3: Based on a 25-country survey, only 22.4% of health facilities provided available and affordable essential medicines.
- SDG 3.d.2: Monitors Antimicrobial Resistance (AMR). The Global Antimicrobial Resistance Surveillance System (GLASS) was launched in 2016 to enroll countries and track resistance, impacting Infection Prevention and Control (IPC) and WASH strategies.
- SDG 6.2.2(b): Proportion of population using a hand-washing facility with soap and water.

GPW13 INDICATORS

- Number of cases of poliomyelitis caused by wild poliovirus.
- Age-standardized prevalence of raised blood pressure (>140 mmHg systolic and/or >90 mmHg diastolic) among persons aged 18+ years.
- Prevalence of obesity.

SUSTAINABLE DEVELOPMENT GOALS AND COVID-19

- Before the pandemic, progress was uneven. Improvements were seen in child school enrollment, communicable disease decline, and water access, but poverty and environmental degradation persisted.
- COVID-19 created massive setbacks: health systems were overwhelmed, 1.6 billion students were left out of school, millions were pushed into extreme poverty, and critical care for NCDs and infectious diseases (TB, HIV, Malaria) was disrupted.
- Existing inequalities were severely exacerbated, heavily impacting marginalized groups, informal workers, and developing nations.

INDIA AND SUSTAINABLE DEVELOPMENT GOALS

- The Government of India adopted the principle of "Sabka Saath Sabka Vikas" (Together with All, Development for All).
- There is a strict pledge that the first claim on development belongs to the poor, prioritizing sanitation, health, education, financial inclusion, and dignity for women.
- To progress towards Sustainable Development in health, India requires:
  - High Political Commitment to keep health high on the National and State Agenda.
  - Investment in public health to complete the unfinished agenda (maternal and child health, eliminating malaria, AIDS, TB, hepatitis, and NTDs).
  - Building a Robust Health System across all aspects, strengthening both Urban and Rural Components with Primary Health Care at the centre.
  - Significant involvement of the Private Health Sector.
  - Development of a Strong System for Monitoring, Evaluation, and Accountability.

subsections.title: Environment and Health: Water Sources & Pollution
subsections.content:
OVERVIEW OF THE CHAPTER
This section covers the definitions of safe water, the classification of habitations based on water availability, different sources of water, construction standards for a sanitary well, causes and indicators of water pollution, and the classification of water-related diseases.

DEFINITIONS

SAFE AND WHOLESOME WATER
- Water is said to be safe and wholesome if it fulfills the following criteria:
  - Pleasant to taste
  - Free from Pathogenic agents
  - Free from harmful Chemical substances
  - Free from Odor & Color
  - Usable for Domestic purpose
- Water is said to be Polluted or Contaminated if it does not fulfill the above criteria.

DAILY REQUIREMENT OF WATER TO MEET NEEDS FOR DOMESTIC PURPOSES
- Rural requirement: 40 - 60 liter per capita / day (55 Liter / capita / day)
- Urban requirement: 150 - 200 liter per capita / day

PHYSIOLOGICAL REQUIREMENT OF WATER
- Adult male: 3.7 liter
- 12-year girl: 2.1 liter

PROBLEM HABITATION CLASSIFICATIONS
- Not covered habitation (No Safe Source habitation): 
  - Source of drinking water is NOT available within 1.6 km in plain areas & 100 m in hilly areas.
  - Availability of safe water is NOT enough to meet drinking & cooking needs.
  - Water source is affected with problems like excess of salinity, iron, fluoride, arsenic etc.
- Partially covered habitation:
  - Source of drinking water is available within 1.6 km in plain & 100 m in hilly areas.
  - Capacity is 10 - 40 litres/ capita/ day.
- Fully covered habitation: Include all the remaining habitat (except the above two).
- Problem Village:
  - No water source within 1.6 km from community.
  - Water is present at depth of > 15 m.
  - Excess of Na+, K+, F- salts, iron, heavy metals.
  - Water exposed to risk of cholera.

SOURCES OF WATER

1. RAIN (GIBRALTAR)
- Pure source of all water.
- Very soft water.
- Symon's rain gauge is widely used in India to measure rainfall.

2. SURFACE WATER (MUMBAI, CHENNAI, NAGPUR)
- Impounding reservoirs:
  - Artificial lakes.
  - Next to rain water in purity.
  - Catchment area: areas from which water is drained to these reservoirs.
- Rivers & Streams (Delhi, Kolkata, Allahabad):
  - Grossly polluted water, so without treatment it is unfit for drinking.
- Tanks, Ponds & Lakes.

3. GROUND WATER
- Shallow well:
  - Taps water from above the 1st impervious layer.
  - Moderately hard & Grossly contaminated.
- Deep well:
  - Taps water from below the 1st impervious layer (some descriptions denote below the 2nd impervious layer).
  - Constant supply of pure & hard water (no treatment required).
- Springs:
  - Occurs where there are cracks in the rocks of valleys or other low places on the land surface viz. below the water tables, hence the water that has been stored up there escapes as spring water.

OTHER TYPES OF WELLS
- Artesian wells: NOT common in India. It is a Deep well, so No pump is required (Water put under pressure between impervious layers).
- Step wells: type of pucca well.
- Deep tube wells: are Bore wells.
- Dug well: Most common type of well in India (viz. of 2 types Kachha & Pucca).

DIFFERENCE BETWEEN DEEP & SHALLOW WELL

SHALLOW WELL
- Water from above the 1st impervious layer in ground.
- Usually, hand dug.
- Few meters deep.
- Liable to pollution.
- Dry in summer.

DEEP WELL
- Water from below the 1st impervious layer in ground.
- Usually, machine dug.
- Several hundred meters deep.
- Satisfactory source of water supply.
- Constant supply.

SANITARY WELL
- Definition: A well that is Properly located, Well-constructed, and Well protected from possible locations of contamination, so as to ensure supply of safe water.

POINTS KEPT INTO CONSIDERATION BEFORE BUILDING SANITARY WELL
- Location:
  - 100 m / 300 ft distance from Human dwelling.
  - 15 m / 50 feet from likely Source of contamination.
  - Should be located at a higher elevation.
- Lining / Stone wall:
  - Built of bricks or stones set in cement upto a Depth of at least 6 m / 20 feet.
  - Lining should be carried 1 m / 3 feet above the ground.
- Parapet wall:
  - Up to height of at least 70 - 75 cm / 28 inches above the ground.
- Platform:
  - At least 1 m / 3 feet in all direction.
  - Gentle slope outwards towards a drain.
- Drain:
  - Pucca drain to carry off spilled water to a public drain.
- Covering:
  - Top of the well should be closed by a cement concrete Cover.
- Hand pump:
  - Well should be equipped with Hand pump for lifting the water in a sanitary manner.

WATER POLLUTION

CAUSED BY DIFFERENT KINDS OF WATER IMPURITIES

1. Natural impurities - Comprises of:
- Dissolved gasses: Nitrogen, Carbon dioxide, Hydrogen sulphide.
- Dissolved minerals: salts of Calcium, Magnesium, Sodium.
- Suspended impurities: Clay, Silt, Sand & Mud.
- Microscopic organisms.

2. Water pollution caused by human activities:
- Urbanization & Industrialization.
- Sewage: Contain Organic matter & Pathogenic agents.
- Industrial waste:
  - Contains toxic agents like Metal salts.
  - Most common cause of pollution of drinking water.
- Agricultural pollutants:
  - Fertilizers & Pesticides.
- Physical pollutants:
  - Heat & Radioactive substances.

INDICATORS OF WATER POLLUTION
- Total Suspended Solids.
- Biochemical Oxygen Demand (BOD).
- Concentration of Chlorides, Nitrogen & Phosphorus.

WATER POLLUTION LAW
- Water (Prevention & Control of pollution) act 1974.

DISEASES OF WATER

1. WATER BORNE DISEASES
- Occur due to drinking contaminated water.
- Transmitted by faeco-oral route.
- Viral: Hepatitis A & E.
- Bacterial: Typhoid & Paratyphoid fever, Cholera.
- Protozoal: Amoebiasis, Giardiasis.
- Helminthic: Roundworm, Threadworm.
- Leptospiral: Weil's disease.

2. WATER WASHED DISEASES
- Infection of outer body surface due to improper hygiene & inadequate use of water.
- Example: Scabies, Trachoma, Typhus, Conjunctivitis, Shigellosis etc.

3. WATER BASED DISEASES
- Infection transmitted through an aquatic invertebrate.
- Example: Shistosomiasis, Dracunculiasis (Guinea worm disease).

4. WATER RELATED DISEASES (WATER BREEDING DISEASE)
- Infections spread by insects that depends on water.
- Example: Malaria, Filariasis, Dengue, Yellow fever, Onchocerciasis etc.

5. OTHERS
- Heavy metals, dyes, Bleaching agents, Solvents etc.
- Fluoride: 1mg/L Protect against dental caries but high-level cause mottling of dental enamel.
- Hardness of water: is beneficial against CVD.

MNEMONICS
- Criteria for Safe & Wholesome Water: P-P.C.O.D (Pleasant to taste, Pathogenic agents free, Chemical substances free, Odor & Color free, Domestic purpose usable).

subsections.title: Environment and Health: Water Purification & Quality
subsections.content:
OVERVIEW OF THE CHAPTER
This section details the methodologies for large-scale and small-scale water purification, including filtration techniques, chlorination phases, and the Horrock's apparatus calculations. It also covers the types and measurement of water hardness, as well as the physical, chemical, and microbiological standards for drinking water quality.

PURIFICATION OF WATER ON LARGE SCALE

1. STORAGE
- Water is drawn from the source & impounded in natural or artificial reservoirs.
- As a result of storage, natural purification takes place through:
  - Physical: 90% of the suspended impurities settle down by gravity.
  - Chemical: Aerobic bacteria oxidizes organic matter, which reduces free Ammonia into Nitrates.
  - Biological: When river water is stored properly, bacteria count drops 90% in the first 5 - 7 days.

2. FILTRATION
- 99% of bacteria are removed, apart from other impurities.
- Two types of filters: Slow Sand filter (Biological Filters) and Rapid Sand filter (Mechanical Filters).

SLOW SAND FILTER / BIOLOGICAL FILTER
- Supernatant (Raw) Water:
  - Depth: 1.5 m.
  - Provides constant head of water to promote downward flow of water.
  - Also provides waiting period of 3 - 12 hours which helps to undergo purification by sedimentation & oxidation.
- Sand Bed:
  - Thickness: 1 m.
  - Sand grains diameter: 0.3 mm.
  - Supported by layer of graded gravel.
  - Rate of filtration: 0.1 - 0.4 m3/hour/per square meter of sand bed surface.
- Vital Layer:
  - Surface of filter gets covered with a slimy growth known as Schmutzdecke / Vital / Zoogleal / Biological Layer.
  - Extends: 3 cm into the top portion of sand bed.
  - Slimy gelatinous layer consists of thread like algae, bacteria, planktons & diatoms.
  - Heart of slow sand filter: Filtered water can be used only after formation of vital layer (known as Ripening of the filter).
  - Removes organic matter & oxidizes ammoniacal nitrogen into nitrates.
- Under Drainage System: Perforated pipes which provides an outlet for filtered water & supporting the filter from below.
- Filter Controls: Equipped with valves & devices, to maintain a constant rate of filtration.
  - Venturi-Meter: measures sand bed Resistance / Loss of head (When exceeds 1.3m, it is uneconomical to run the filter).
- Filter cleaning:
  - Supernatant water is cleaned off.
  - Sand bed is cleaned by scrapping off the top portion to the depth of 2cm.
  - After 20 - 30 scrapping, plant is closed down & new bed is constructed.
- Advantages:
  - Simple & Cheap to construct.
  - Physical, chemical & biological quality of filtered water is very high.
  - Removes total bacteria count by 99.9%.

RAPID SAND FILTER / MECHANICAL FILTER
- 2 types: Gravity type (Paterson's filter) and Pressure type (Candy's filter).
- Steps:
  - Coagulation: Raw water treated with alum (5 mg / Liter).
  - Rapid mixing: Violent agitation in a mixing chamber.
  - Flocculation: Slow & gentle stirring of treated water in flocculation chamber for 30 min. Results in formation of thick, copious, white flocculent precipitate of Aluminum Hydroxide.
  - Sedimentation: 6 hours. Flocculent precipitate along with impurities & bacteria settles down.
  - Filtration: Partly clarified water is now subjected to rapid sand filtration.
  - Filter bed: Sand, Gravel, Perforated pipes.
  - Alum floc not removed by sedimentation is held back on the sand bed & forms a slimy layer which absorbs bacteria & oxidizes ammonia.
- Rate of filtration: 5 m3 / m2 / hour.
  - When the loss of head approaches 7 - 8 feet, filtration is stopped & filters are subjected to backwashing.
- Filter cleaning (Backwashing):
  - Reversing the flow of water.
  - Sometimes air is used.
- Advantages:
  - Occupy less space.
  - Filtration is rapid.
  - Washing is easy.

DIFFERENCE BETWEEN RAPID SAND FILTER & SLOW SAND FILTER
- Space: Rapid Sand Filter occupies little space; Slow Sand Filter occupies large area.
- Rate of filtration: Rapid Sand Filter is 5 m3/hour/per square meter; Slow Sand Filter is 0.1- 0.4 m3/hour/per square meter.
- Size of sand: Rapid Sand Filter is 0.4 - 0.7 mm; Slow Sand Filter is 0.2 - 0.3 mm.
- Preliminary treatment: Rapid Sand Filter requires Chemical Coagulation & Sedimentation; Slow Sand Filter requires Plain sedimentation.
- Washing: Rapid Sand Filter requires Backwashing (frequent washing, requires highly skilled workers); Slow Sand Filter requires Scrapping (does NOT require frequent washing, requires less skilled workers).
- Loss of head allowed: Rapid Sand Filter is 6 - 8 feet; Slow Sand Filter is 4 feet.
- Removal of bacteria: Rapid Sand Filter is 98 - 99 %; Slow Sand Filter is 99.9 - 99.99 %.
- Suitable for: Rapid Sand Filter for Big cities; Slow Sand Filter for Small cities.
- Construction cost: Rapid Sand Filter is Expensive; Slow Sand Filter is Cheap.
- Layer is made of: Rapid Sand Filter is Aluminum Hydroxide; Slow Sand Filter is Algae, Bacteria, Planktons & Diatoms.

3. DISINFECTION

CHLORINATION
- Bactericidal & moderately Virucidal but NOT sporicidal.
- Polio virus NOT sensitive to disinfection by chlorination.
- Used for purification of highly polluted water on large scale.
- Action of Chlorine:
  - H2O + Cl2 -> HCl (Hydrochloric acid) + HOCl (Hypochlorous acid)
  - HOCl -> H+ (Hydrogen ion) + OCl- (Hypochlorite ion)
  - Hydrochloric acid is neutralized by alkalinity of water.
  - Disinfection action of chlorine is mainly due to Hypochlorous acid & to a small extent due to Hypochlorite ion.
  - Chlorine disinfects best when pH of water is < 7.
  - When pH exceeds 8.5, chlorine is unreliable as a disinfectant because 90% of Hypochlorous acid gets ionized to Hydrogen & Hypochlorite ions.
  - Most effective water treatment method in rural areas is chlorination of water.
- Phases of Chlorination:
  - Phase I: Formation of chloramines.
  - Phase II: Destruction of chloramines.
  - Phase III: Appearance of breakpoint.
  - Phase IV: Accumulation of residual chlorine.
- Methods of Chlorination:
  - Chlorine gas.
  - Chloramine.
  - Perchloron / High Test Hypochlorite (HTH).
- Chlorine demand:
  - Amount of chlorine needed to destroy bacteria & to oxidize all organic matter & ammoniacal substances.
  - Chlorine demand is the difference between amount of chlorine added to water & amount of residual chlorine remaining at the end of specific period of contact (usually 60 min) at given temperature & pH of water.
- Break point:
  - Point at which chlorine demand of water is met & if further chlorine is added beyond the break point, free residual chlorine (HOCl) begins to appear in water.
- Contact period:
  - Presence of free residual chlorine for a period of at least 1 hour is essential to kill bacteria & viruses.

FREE RESIDUAL CHLORINE
- Recommended level of Free Residual Chlorine in water (1mg / L = 1ppm):
  - Drinking water: > 0.5mg / L (after 1 hour).
  - Post disaster water bodies: > 0.7 mg / L.
  - Swimming pool: > 1 mg / L.
  - Piped supply: 0.5 mg / L.
  - Wells: 1mg / L.
  - Tankers: 2mg / L.
- Residual germicidal effect of chlorine:
  - Provides margin of safety against microbial contamination, which may occur during storage or distribution.
  - Only chlorine has got the residual germicidal effect whereas UV radiation & Ozone gas DON'T have residual germicidal effect.

BREAKPOINT CHLORINATION & SUPER CHLORINATION
- Breakpoint Chlorination:
  - Addition of chlorine to water produces chloramines.
  - If chlorine dose is increased, a reduction in residual chlorine occurs due to destruction of Chloramine by added chlorine.
  - This causes fall in residual chlorine with further increase in chlorine dose & after a stage, residual chlorine begins to increase in proportion to the added dose of chlorine.
  - This point at which the residual chlorine appears & when combined chlorine is completely destroyed is breakpoint.
- Super Chlorination:
  - Super chlorination is followed up with dechlorination.
  - It is the addition of large doses of chlorine to the water & removal of excess of chlorine after disinfection.
  - This method is applicable to heavily polluted waters on large scale.

INSTRUMENTS & TESTS USED IN CHLORINATION
- Instruments:
  - Horrock's apparatus: chlorine demand estimation.
  - Chlorinator / Chloramine: mixing of chlorine.
  - Chloroscope: measuring residual chlorine.
- Tests for Free Residual Chlorine:
  - Orthotolidine test (OT): Determines both free & combined chlorine in water. 0.1 ml reagent (Orthotolidine in 10% HCl) is added to 1 ml of water containing chlorine. It turns yellow, whose color is matched against a colored disc. OT reacts with free chlorine instantaneously but more slowly with combined chlorine. So, it's essential to take reading within 10 seconds to estimate free chlorine because color produced after 15-20 minutes is due to the action of both free & combined chlorine.
  - Orthotolidine Arsenite test (OTA): For Free & Combined Chlorine separately. Errors caused by presence of Nitrites, Iron & Manganese (as they also give yellow color) are overcome.

OZONATION
- Ozone gas O3 is formed by passing dry air or oxygen through a high voltage electric field.
- Resultant ozone-enriched air is dosed directly into water.
- A residual of about 0.5ml/L after a contact time of upto 20 min is typically used.
- Normally used with subsequent treatments such as Biological Filtration or Granular Activated Carbon (GAC), to remove biodegradable organics.
- Virucidal & Bactericidal.
- Removes Odor, Taste & Color.
- No residual effect.

MEMBRANE PROCESS
- High pressure process:
  - Reverse Osmosis (RO): Force the flow of solvent in opposite direction from the higher to the lower concentration. Pressure is 15 - 50 bar. Rejects monovalent ions & organics of molecular weight > 50 Daltons. Most common application is desalination of sea water.
  - Nanofiltration: Uses membrane with properties between RO & Ultrafiltration. Pressure is 5 bar. Rejects divalent ions (Ca, Mg) & higher molecular weight organics. Effective for removal of color forming organic compounds.
- Low pressure process:
  - Ultrafiltration: Similar to RO but with larger pore size & at low pressure. Pressure is < 5 bar. Rejects organic molecule with molecular weight 800 Daltons.
  - Microfiltration: Direct extension of conventional filtration into sub micrometer range. Pressure is 2 bar (lower pressure membrane process for treatment of water). Rejects colloid, suspended material & particle > 0.5 micrometer. Used for water treatment to remove particulates & organic carbon prior to RO.

PURIFICATION OF WATER AT SMALL SCALE

1. HOUSEHOLD PURIFICATION OF WATER
- Boiling:
  - Water must be brought to a rolling boil for 10 to 20 minutes.
  - Kills all bacteria, spores, cysts & ova.
  - Also removes temporary hardness.
  - Water should be boiled preferable in the same container in which it is to be stored.
- Chemical disinfection:
  - Bleaching powder/ Chlorinated lime/ CaOCl2: Bleaching powder is widely used in disinfection of wells. Contains 33% of available chlorine. When mixed with excess of lime it retains its strength, this is called Stabilized Bleach.
  - Chlorine solution: 4 kg bleaching powder mixed with 20 liters of water Gives a 5% solution of chlorine.
  - High Test Hypochlorite (HTH / Perchloron): Contains 60% of available chlorine.
  - Chlorine tablets: Halazone tablets. Single tablet of 0.5 gm is sufficient to disinfect 20 liters of water.
  - Iodine: 2 drops of 2% ethanol solution of iodine will suffice for 1 liter of clear water. High cost & thyroid activity are its major disadvantages.
  - Potassium permanganate: Powerful oxidizing agent, but not a satisfactory agent for disinfecting water.
- Filtration:
  - Ceramic filters NOT used much in India.
  - Chamberland filter: Candle made up of Porcelain.
  - Berkefeld filter: Candle made up of Infusorial Earth or Kieselguhr.
  - Katadyn filter: Surface of filter is coated with silver catalyst so that bacteria are killed by oligodynamic action of silver ions.
- Ultraviolet filtration:
  - Involves exposure of film of water 120 mm thick to mercury vapor arc lamp, emitting ultraviolet radiation at wavelength of 254 nanometer.
  - NO residual effect.
- Multi stage Reverse Osmosis:
  - Clarity cartridge: removes suspended particles.
  - RO cartridge: reduces TDS, hardness, heavy metals & eliminate microorganisms.

2. DISINFECTION OF WELLS

HORROCK'S APPARATUS FOR FINDING CHLORINE DEMAND
- Steps of disinfection of well:
  - Find volume of water in a well: Volume = Pi x R2 x H (in m3). Note: 1 m3 = 1000 litres.
  - Find the amount of bleaching powder: Estimate the chlorine demand of well water by Horrock's Apparatus & calculate the amount of bleaching powder required to disinfect the well.
- Horrock's Apparatus contents & procedure:
  - Estimates chlorine demand of water.
  - Content: 6 white cups (200ml), 1 black cup, 2 metal spoons, 7 glass stirring rods.
  - Indicator: starch iodide (produce blue color). Here development of blue color indicates the presence of residual chlorine.
  - 1 Spoon (2g) of bleaching powder is added in Black cup and a thin paste is made with water. More water is added to bring it up to the circular mark, this solution is called as "Chlorine Stock Solution".
  - 6 White cups are filled with the water to be tested.
  - Using the Pipette in apparatus, add 1 drop of the stock solution to the 1st cup, 2 drops to the 2nd cup, 3 drops to the 3rd cup, and so on to the 6th cup.
  - Stir water in the cups with separate Rods. Wait for 1/2 hour.
  - Add 3 drops of starch-iodide indicator to each white cup.
  - First cup which develops this color gives an estimate of the amount of bleaching powder needed to disinfect 455 L of the sample water.
- Chlorine demand formula (dose of bleaching powder required) = n x 2 gm to disinfect 455 litres of water. (n = number of the 1st cup which shows blue color change).
- Delivery of chlorine solution into the well: Bucket containing chlorine solution is lowered below the water surface & well water is agitated by moving bucket vertically & horizontally.
- Contact period: 1 hour is allowed before the water is drawn for use.
- Orthotolidine Arsenite test: To test for free residual chlorine at the end of 1 hour contact, which should be 0.5mg/ L.

DOUBLE POT METHOD
- Used for chlorination of wells during emergency.
- 2 cylindrical pots, one placed inside other. 30 cm height & 25 cm diameter for outer pot.
- A hole of 1 cm in diameter is made in each pot.
- Inner pot hole is in the upper portion & outer pot hole is in 4 cm above the bottom.
- Mixture of 1kg bleaching powder & 2 kg of coarse sand is prepared is moistened with water.
- Inner pot is filled with the mixture & is introduced in the outer one.
- Mouth of the later is closed.
- Double pot is lowered in the well.
- Pot should be immersed at least 1m below water level.
- Works satisfactory for 2-3 weeks in small household wells.

HARDNESS OF WATER

DEFINITION & CLASSIFICATION
- Definition: Soap destroying power of water.
- Classification of hardness:
  - Carbonate hardness (Temporary hardness): Due to presence of Calcium & Magnesium Bicarbonates.
  - Non-carbonate hardness (Permanent hardness): Due to presence of Calcium & Magnesium Sulphates, Chlorides & Nitrates.
- Unit: Expressed in terms of m Eq/ L (mili equivalent per litre). 1 mEq / L = 50 mg CaCO3 in 1 litre of water.
- Levels of Hardness:
  - Soft water: < 1 mEq/L (< 50 mg/L).
  - Moderately hard water: 1 - 3 mEq/L (50 - 150 mg/L). Note: Drinking water should be moderately hard. Softening of water is recommended at hardness > 3 mEq / L or 150mg/ L.
  - Hard water: 3 - 6 mEq/L (150 - 300 mg/L).
  - Very hard water: > 6 mEq/L (> 300 mg/L).
- Advantages: There is an inverse association between hardness of drinking water & Cardiovascular disease.
- Disadvantages: Consumes more soap & detergents, Scaling of boilers, Shortens life of Fabrics & supply pipes (by erosion).

METHODS OF REMOVAL OF HARDNESS OF WATER
- Temporary hardness removed by:
  - Boiling.
  - Addition of Lime.
  - Addition of Sodium Bicarbonate.
  - Base exchange process / Permutit process.
- Permanent hardness removed by:
  - Addition of Sodium Bicarbonate.
  - Base exchange process / Permutit process.
- Boiling: Precipitates the insoluble calcium carbonate (Ca(HCO3)2 -> CaCO3 + H2O + CO2).
- Addition of Lime: Lime absorbs carbon dioxide & precipitates the insoluble calcium carbonate (Ca(OH)2 + Ca(HCO3) -> 2CaCO3 + 2H2O).
- Clarke's method: 1 ounce of lime added to 700 gallons of water for each degree of hardness.
- Addition of Sodium Bicarbonate: Sodium bicarbonate (soda ash) removes both temporary & permanent hardness (Na2CO3 + Ca(HCO3)2 -> 2NaHCO3 + CaCO3).
- Base exchange process / Permutit process: Sodium Permutit is a complex compound of Sodium, Aluminum & Silica (Na2 Al2 Si2O H2O). It has the property of exchanging sodium cation for calcium & magnesium ions. By this process water can be softened to zero hardness. Water of zero hardness is corrosive so a part of raw water is mixed to secure desired hardness.

WATER QUALITY STANDARDS & CRITERIA

RECOMMENDED GUIDELINES FOR DRINKING WATER
- Color: < 15 TCU (True Color Unit) / < 5 Hozen units.
- Turbidity: < 1 NTU (Nephelometric Turbidity Unit).
- Hardness: < 100 - 300 mg /L calcium ions (permissible level - 50 to 150 mg/L).
- pH: 6.5 - 8.5.
- TDS (Total Dissolved Solids): < 500 mg/L.
- Fluoride: < 1 ppm (safe value - 0.5 to 0.8).
- Nitrates: < 45 mg/L.
- Nitrites: < 3 mg/L.
- Ross alpha radiological activity: < 0.5 Bq /L.
- Ross beta radiological activity: < 1 Bq /L.
- Zero pathogenic microorganism / infectious viruses.
- Absence of pathogenic protozoa & Helminthes.

MICROBIOLOGICAL ASPECTS
- Bacterial indicators:
  - Coliform organism: Primary & most reliable indicator for water quality. NO coliform organism should be present in drinking water. Most reliable evidence of fecal contamination of water.
  - Presumptive coliform test: MPN multiple tube test - estimating Most Probable Number (MPN) of coliform organism in 100 ml of water.
  - Fecal group: E. coli - Indicator of recent fecal contamination rather than water borne pathogen.
  - Non fecal group: Klebsiella aerogens.
  - Fecal Streptococci: Indicates RECENT fecal contamination of water.
  - Cl. Perfringens: Occurrence of fecal contamination of water at some REMOTE TIME.
  - Indicators of fecal contamination of water: Coliforms, E coli, fecal Streptococci, Cl. Perfringens, Enterococci.
- Virological aspects: Drinking water should be free from any viruses. Disinfection with 0.5 mg/L free residual chlorine is sufficient to inactivate virus. Ozone is also an effective disinfectant.
- Biological aspects:
  - Protozoa: Entamoeba histolytica, Giardia & Balantidium coli introduced in water through Fecal Contamination. Rapid or Slow Sand Filtration are effective in removing pathogenic protozoa.
  - Helminths: Dracunculus medinensis (Guinea worm) & Shistosomiasis are hazards of Unpiped Water Supplies.
  - Free living organisms: Fungi & Algae may interfere in water treatment process.

RADIOLOGICAL ASPECTS
- Somatic effect: Effect of radiation exposure if they become manifest in the exposed individual.
  - Stochastic effects: Carcinogenesis effect occurring as a function of a dose.
  - Non stochastic effects: Severity of the effect varies with the dose.
- Hereditary effect: Effect of radiation exposure which affects the descendants.

ACCEPTABILITY ASPECTS
- Physical parameters:
  - Turbidity: Drinking water should be free from turbidity. Turbidity > 4 Nephelometric Turbidity Units (NTU) is usually noticeable to the naked eye.
  - Color: Drinking water should be free from color.
  - Taste & Odor: Indicative of some form of pollution.
  - Temperature: Most desired temperature range for drinking water is 40 - 50 F (5 - 10 C). Low water temperature decreases the efficiency of treatment process including disinfection. High water temperature enhances the growth of microorganisms & corrosion problem.

CHEMICAL ASPECTS (INORGANIC CONSTITUENTS)
- Aluminum: > 0.2mg/ L leads to deposition of aluminum hydroxide floc in distribution system.
- Ammonia: Natural level is below 0.2 mg/L. Originates from agricultural & industrial process & from disinfection with Chloramines. Can cause failure of filters and taste & odor problems.
- Chlorides: Detectable taste begins to appear in water if it goes above 250 mg/ L. Maximum permissible level is 600 mg/L.
- Copper: Staining of laundry & sanitary ware at concentration > 1mg/L. Corrosion of iron & steel fittings.
- Dissolved oxygen: Microbial reduction of nitrate to nitrite & sulphate to sulphide causes odor problem.
- Hardness: Soft water with Hardness less than 100 mg/L may be more corrosive for water. Hardness of 200 mg/L cause scale deposition in the distribution system.
- Hydrogen sulphide: Taste & odor threshold is 0.1 mg/L (Rotten egg odor).
- Iron: > 0.3 mg/L of iron stains laundry & plumbing fixtures. Ground water contains ferrous iron which on exposure to atmosphere oxidizes to ferric iron, giving reddish brown color to water.
- Manganese: < 0.1 mg/L is usually acceptable. Form coating on pipes which may slough off as a black precipitate.
- pH: Acceptable limit of pH in drinking water is between 6.5 to 8.5. < 7 may cause corrosion of distribution pipes & elevated levels of lead. > 8.5 decrease the efficiency of chlorine disinfection.
- Sodium: Taste threshold is 200 mg/L.
- Sulphate: Taste impairment is minimal at level < 250 mg/L.
- Total Dissolved Solid (TDS): Acceptable level in drinking water is < 500 mg/ L. High level of TDS causes scaling in water pipes, boilers & household appliances.
- Zinc: Taste threshold is 4 mg/L. Water containing zinc in excess may develop greasy film on boiling.
- Arsenic: Guideline value is 0.01 mg/L. Introduced into water through industrial effluents.
- Cadmium: Guideline value is 0.3 ug/L. Pollution caused by contamination from fertilizers. Accumulates primarily in kidney.
- Chromium: Guideline value is 0.05 mg/L.
- Cyanide: Consequences of industrial contamination. Effects nervous system.
- Fluoride: Guideline value is 1.5 mg/L. > 5mg/L leads to skeletal fluorosis. Released during manufacturing & use of phosphate fertilizers. Nalgonda technique (Lime + Alum) is used for defluoridation of water.
- Lead: Most undesirable metal in drinking water. Interferes with calcium & vitamin D metabolism. Toxic to both central & peripheral nervous system.
- Mercury: Guideline value is 0.006 mg/L. Present in ground water. Target organ is kidney.
- Nitrate & Nitrite: Nitrate < 50 mg/L (Test for PAST contamination of water. Nitrate in excess of 45 - 50 mg / L may cause infantile methemoglobinemia). Nitrite < 3 mg/L (Test for RECENT contamination of water is nitrites).
- Selenium: Guideline value is 0.01 mg/L.

ORGANIC CONSTITUENTS
- Chlorinated alkanes: Dichloromethane.
- Chlorinated ethenes: 1.1 dichloroethene, 1.2 dichloroethene.
- Aromatic hydrocarbons: Benzene, Ethylbenzene, Benzolapyrene.
- Polynuclear aromatic hydrocarbons.
- Pesticides: Chlorinated hydrocarbons.

MNEMONICS
- All Backup Plans Die: Algae, bacteria, planktons & diatoms (Constituents of the Schmutzdecke/Vital Layer in a slow sand filter).
- LBW - (W is 3) - LB3: Relates to removal of temporary hardness (Lime, Boiling, Bicarbonate, Base exchange).
- hard clarke - L - 7 - 700: Clarke's method for hardness removal (1 ounce of Lime added to 700 gallons of water for each degree of hardness).

subsections.title: Environment and Health: Air, Climate & Housing
subsections.content:
OVERVIEW OF THE CHAPTER
This section comprehensively covers the environmental health aspects of water conservation, swimming pool sanitation, thermal comfort, air pollution and its global impacts (greenhouse effect), ventilation, noise, radiation hazards, lighting standards, and housing guidelines in India.

WATER HARVESTING & CONSERVATION

PRESERVATION OF WASTAGES
- Domestic consumption of water can be reduced by individuals by cultivating better habits in kitchen and bathroom, to avoid free running water.

WATER HARVESTING
- Important to preserve and build up underground water reserves in urban and semi-urban areas.
- Rainwater collected from Rooftops is brought into the Soaking pit by PVC pipes.
- Soaking pits are filled with layers of Stones, Gravel, and Sand.
- Rainwater is filtered through these layers and then travels by a PVC pipe into the nearby Tube Well.

SWIMMING POOL SANITATION

STANDARDS AND SANITATION
- Recommended area: 2.2 sq m or 24 sq ft per person.
- Filtration:
  - Water to be re-filtered in less than 6 hours (rapid sand filter).
  - 15 percent of water to be replaced by fresh water every day.
  - Residual chlorine level should be > 1 ppm (mg/L).
- pH of water: 7.4 - 7.8.
- Swimming pool water is exposed to:
  - Fecal contamination.
  - Organisms from skin and nasopharynx.

HEALTH HAZARDS ASSOCIATED WITH SWIMMING POOL
- Fungal infection of skin: Epidermophyton and Trichophyton (athletic foot).
- Viral infection of skin: Papilloma virus (plantar warts).
- Infections of eye, ear, nose, throat, and upper respiratory tract.
- Industrial infection.
- Accidents.

COMFORT ZONE AND THERMAL METRICS

DEFINITIONS
- Comfort Zone: Defined as the range of effective temperature over which the majority of adults feel comfortable.
- Comfortable Conditions: Conditions under which a person can maintain the balance between production and loss of heat, at a normal body temperature and without sweating.
- Discomfort: A subjective sensation which people experience in ill ventilated and crowded rooms.

COMFORT ZONE IN INDIA (EFFECTIVE TEMPERATURE)
- 20 C: Pleasant & Cool.
- 20 - 25 C: Comfortable & Cool.
- 25 - 27 C: Comfortable.
- 27 - 28 C: Hot & Uncomfortable.
- 28 - 30 C: Extremely Hot.
- > 30 C: Intolerably Hot.
- Effective Temperature of comfort zone in degrees F: 77 to 80 F.

MCARDLE'S MAXIMUM ALLOWABLE SWEAT RATE (P4SR)
- McArdle and associates took 4.5 litres of sweat excreted in 4 hours as the maximum allowable sweat rate compatible with normal physiological reaction of men on repeated exposure to heat.
- Predicted Four Hour Sweat Rate (P4SR):
  - 1 - 3 litres: Comfort Zone.
  - 3 - 4.5 litres: Just tolerable.
  - > 4.5 litres: Intolerable.
- McArdle's has put P4SR value of 3 as the upper limit of comfort zone.

INDICES OF THERMAL COMFORT & INSTRUMENTS
- Air Temperature: Measured by Dry Bulb Thermometer, Dry & Wet Bulb Thermometer, Maximum & Minimum Thermometer, Six's Maximum Minimum Thermometer, Silvered Thermometer.
- Mean Radiant Heat / Temperature: Measured by Globe Thermometer.
- Environment Heat: Measured by Wet Globe Thermometer.
- Air Humidity (Moisture content of air): Relative Humidity (%) = (Actual water vapours pressure / Saturated water vapours pressure) x 100. 
  - Relative Humidity < 35 % is Dry, 50 % is Pleasant, > 65 % is Sticky.
  - Measured by Hygrometer (Dry & Wet Hygrometer is most widely used to measure humidity. Hydrometer is used in Soil analysis, NOT for humidity). Sling / Whirling Psychrometer measures Relative Humidity (Rotated 4 rotation/second at the speed of 5 m/sec). Assmann Psychrometer.
- Cooling Power of Air:
  - Humidity, Air Movement & Temperature together expressed as cooling power.
  - Measured by Kata Thermometer (Alcohol thermometer that calculates the rate of Heat Loss).
  - Rate of Cooling = Kata factor / Average cooling time.
  - Dry Kata (single bulb): reading of >= 6. 
  - Wet Kata (cloth on bulb): reading of >= 20.
  - Both are warmed by immersing in Hot water (130 F) and then the dry one is wiped. Both are suspended in air. The level of fall in temperature on both are recorded (which depends on the cooling power of air).
  - Cooling Range: 100 to 90 F.
  - Currently, Kata Thermometer is used only to measure Low Air Velocity instead of Anemometer (previously used).
- Effective Temperature: Combines into a single value the effect of Temperature, Humidity & Movement of internal air on sensation of warmth or cold felt by the human body.
- Corrected Effective Temperature: Deals with 4 factors: Temperature, Humidity, Velocity & Mean Radiant Heat.

AIR POLLUTION

OVERVIEW AND SOURCES
- Definition: Signifies the presence of substances in the atmosphere, which are generated by the activities of man that interfere with human health and are injurious to vegetation and animals.
- Sources of Air Pollution:
  - Automobiles: Hydrocarbon, Carbon monoxide, Lead & Nitrogen oxide.
  - Industries: Combustion of fuel generates Sulphur dioxide, Nitrogen oxide. Petrochemical industries generate Hydrogen fluoride & Hydrochloric acid.
  - Domestic sources: Domestic combustion of coal, wood or oil generates Sulphur dioxide & Nitrogen oxide.
  - Tobacco smoke: Even those who do not smoke may inhale by Passive smoking.
  - Miscellaneous: Burning refuse & Spraying pesticides.

INDICATORS AND MONITORING
- Sulphur dioxide (SO2): Best indicator of air pollution (Major contaminant in urban & industrial areas).
- Smoke & Soiling Index: Air is passed through a white filter paper under specified conditions. Smoke concentration is estimated using a Photoelectric Meter.
- Grit & Dust measurement (suspended particles): Deposit gauge collects grit, dust & other solids.
- Coefficient of haze: Assessing the amount of Smoke or Aerosol in air.
- Air Pollution Index (A.P.I): Arbitrary takes into account one or more pollutants as a measure of the severity of pollution.
- Lichens: Best biological indicator of air pollution.
- Air Pollution Monitoring in India: National Air Quality Monitoring Program (Central Pollution Control Board - CPCB, 1990).

METEOROLOGICAL FACTORS AND TYPES OF POLLUTANTS
- Pollution at any time depends on meteorological factors: Topography, Air Movement & Climate.
- Vertical diffusion of pollutants depends on temperature gradient.
- Temperature Inversion: When there is rapid cooling of lower layers of air (temperature inversion), pollutants & water vapors remain trapped at the lower most layer of atmosphere & the results is Smog (more frequent in the winter).
- Primary pollutants: Emitted directly into the atmosphere from a source such as factory chimney or exhaust pipe (Example: Volatile Organic Compounds (VOC's), SO2, NO2, CO, Hydrocarbons, CFC's, Ammonia, Particulate matter).
- Secondary pollutants: Formed within the atmosphere, arise from chemical reaction of primary pollutants (Example: Ozone, Per oxyacetyl nitrate).
- State classification: Gaseous air pollutants (present as Gases or Vapor) and Particulate air pollutants (Solid or Liquid suspended in the atmosphere, can either be primary or secondary).
- Scale classification:
  - Local scale Pollutants: Encountered close to where they are emitted.
  - Urban scale Pollutants: Nitrogen oxide & Carbon monoxide, which are present at high concentration throughout the city.
  - Regional scale Pollutants: Sulphate particles & Ozone can cross national boundaries (Long Range Transport).
  - Global scale Pollutants: Greenhouse effect (CO2, Nitrous Oxide, Methane) distributing throughout a hemisphere.

SPECIFIC AIR POLLUTANTS
- Carbon Monoxide: Automobiles, Industrial process & Heating facilities.
- Sulphur Dioxide: Combustion of Sulphur containing Fossil fuel & smelting of Sulphur containing Ores (SO2 combines with water to form Sulphuric acid viz. The main component of Acid Rain).
- Lead: 90 percent derives from the combustion of Leaded Petrol, also arise from mining & smelting of Lead Ores.
- Carbon Dioxide: Combustion process using Coal, Oil & Gas.
- Hydrocarbons: Combustion of Coal, Wood & Petroleum.
- Cadmium: Zinc industries, Waste incineration (Cigarette contain 0.5 to 3ug of cadmium per gram of tobacco).
- Hydrogen Sulphide: Formed from Coke production, Waste water treatment plants, Oil refining & Tanning industries.
- Ozone: Formed by photochemical reaction of Sunlight with Nitrogen oxide from vehicle & industries emission.
- Nitric Oxide: Where coal is used as a fuel in electricity generation.
- Polycyclic Aromatic Hydrocarbons (PAH): Benzo-pyrene / fluoranthene. Automobile exhaust, Industrial emission & Smoke from charcoal & tobacco.
- Particulate Matter: Coarse particles > 2.5 micrometer (Smaller particles forms aerosols). Fine particles < 2.5 micrometer (Large particles contain earth crustal & dust from roads & industries).

INDOOR AIR POLLUTION
- Fuel/ Tobacco combustion: Respirable / Fine particles, Polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons, Carbon monoxide (combustion equipment's like stove & gas heaters).
- Fuel combustion: Nitrogen oxide.
- Coal combustion: Arsenic, Sulphur dioxide.
- Furnishing: Aldehydes.
- Construction material & Fire proofing: Asbestos.
- Painted surface: Lead.
- Soil under buildings: Radon.
- Organic vapors: Benzene.
- Ozone.
- Effects: Acute Respiratory Infections (Pneumonia) in young children, Chronic Lung Disease & Cancer in adults, Adverse pregnancy outcomes (Stillbirth).

MAJOR AIR POLLUTANTS & THEIR ADVERSE EFFECTS
- Oxides of Nitrogen: Bronchial Hyperactivity.
- Hydrocarbon: Lung Cancer.
- Ozone: Bronchoconstriction.
- Sulphur dioxide: Asthma & COPD.
- Lead: Impaired Neuropsychological development in children.

HAZARDS AND CONTROL OF AIR POLLUTION
- Health aspects: Immediate effect (Acute Bronchitis), Delayed effect (Lung Cancer, Bronchial Asthma, Respiratory allergies).
- Social & Economic aspects: Destruction of plant & animal life, Corrosion of metal cars, Damage to building, Soil & damage clothing.
- Disinfection of air:
  - Mechanical Ventilation: Reduces bacterial density.
  - Ultraviolet Radiation: In operation theaters & infectious wards.
  - Chemical Mists: Triethylene glycol vapours is an effective air bactericide.
  - Dust Control: Application of oil to floors of hospital wards.
  - Electrostatic Precipitation.
- Prevention & Control:
  - Containment: Achieved by enclosure, ventilation & air cleaning and Arresters.
  - Replacement: Increased use of electricity, natural gas & central heating in place of coal and Reduced lead in petrol.
  - Dilution: Establishment of Green Belts between industrial & residual areas.
  - Legislation: height of chimneys, no smoke zones, Air (Prevention & Control of Pollution) act 1981.

GREENHOUSE EFFECT (GLOBAL WARMING)

GREENHOUSE GASES
- Water vapours: 36 - 70 percent (Highest contribution).
- Carbon dioxide: 9 - 26 percent (Second highest contribution, maximum effect on global warming).
- Methane: 4 - 9 percent.
- Ozone: 3 - 7 percent (Ozone layer is beneficial as it cuts down UV transmission, Chlorofluorocarbons (CFCs) a non-natural gas depletes ozone layer).
- These gases absorb the energy reflected from Earth's surface, while being transparent to incoming solar radiation. They trap and preserve heat in the atmosphere.
- Increase in Greenhouse gases results due to increase use of Fossil fuel, Nitrogenous fertilizers, Coal mining, Organic matter, Land filling, expansion of Rice paddies & burning of biomass.

GLOBAL WARMING CONSEQUENCES
- Increase of about 3 C in the average global surface temperature by the year 2030.
- Melting of Glaciers.
- Rise in sea level of 0.3 meters by 2050.
- Submerging the coastal areas.
- Increase in occurrence of extreme climatic events such as cyclones & draughts.
- Alter the distribution of rainfall.
- Warming induces a decline in soil moisture that impairs grain production.

PREVENTION PROTOCOLS
- Kyoto protocol: Sets standards to decrease Greenhouse gases globally (by 18 %). Established 16th Feb. 2005. Includes 187 countries. Target's reduction in 6 gases: Carbon dioxide, Methane, Nitrous oxide, Sulphur-hexafluoride (SF6), Perfluorocarbons (PFC), Chloro-Floro-Carbons (CFC).
- Paris protocol: To decrease CO2 emission or other harmful gases.
- Heat Island: Phenomenon that makes cities warmer than surrounding rural areas.

VENTILATION

STANDARDS OF VENTILATION
- Definition: It is not only the replacement of vitiated air by a supply of fresh outdoor air, but also control of the quality of incoming air with regard to its Temperature, Humidity & Purity.
- Floor space: Requirement per person is 50 to 100 sq. ft.
- Cubic space: 3000 cu. ft per person per hour.
- Air change: Air changes per hour = Total hourly air supply to the room / Cubic capacity of the room.
  - Living room / Drawing room: 2 to 3 air change / hour.
  - Work room: 4 to 6 air change / hour.
- Height in excess of 12 feet are ineffective from point of view of ventilation.

TYPES OF VENTILATION
1. Natural ventilation
- Wind: 
  - Cross ventilation: Doors & windows facing each other.
  - Aspiration: When there is obstruction, air passes by & exerts a Suction at its tail end.
  - Perflation: When wind blows through a room.
- Inequality of Temperature:
  - Air rises when slightly heated & escapes from opening provided high up in the room.
  - Outside air which is cooler & denser will enter the room through inlets placed low.
  - Greater the temperature difference greater the velocity of the incoming air.
- Diffusion: Air passes through the smallest Opening or Spaces by diffusion.

2. Mechanical ventilation
- Exhaust ventilation: Air is extracted to the outside by exhaust fans, a vacuum is created which causes fresh air to enter the room through windows. Exhaust fans are placed high, near roof which facilitates removal of the upper heated (light) air.
- Plenum ventilation: Fresh air is blown into the room by centrifugal fans so as to create a positive pressure & displace the vitiated air. Plenum or Propulsion system is used for supplying air to air-conditioned buildings & factories. Air is derived through Ducts at desired points.
- Balanced ventilation: Combination of Exhaust & Plenum systems of ventilation.
- Air conditioning: Simultaneous control of all factors affecting both the physical & chemical conditions of atmosphere include Temperature, Humidity & Air movement.
- Transition room: Where the temperature difference is large between outside atmosphere & air-conditioned room, transition room is provided to prevent sudden exposure to high or low temperature.

NOISE

PROPERTIES AND MEASUREMENT
- Definition: Wrong Sound, in the wrong Place, at the wrong Time.
- Loudness / Intensity: Measured in Decibels (dB).
  - Whispering: 20 - 30 dB.
  - Hospital Wards: 20 - 35 dB (Recommended).
  - Classroom: 30 - 40 dB (Recommended).
  - Hospital: < 40 - 45 dB (Recommended).
  - Industries: 40 - 50 dB (Recommended).
  - Normal conversation: 60 - 70 dB.
  - Residential area: 60 - 65 dB (Recommended).
  - Maximum/ acceptable noise level: 85 dB.
  - Daily maximum tolerable sound level: 85 - 90 dB.
  - Auditory fatigue: 90 dB (4000 Hz).
  - Permanent hearing loss: 100 dB (continuous exposure).
  - Threshold of pain: 140 dB.
  - Permanent mechanical damage / tympanic membrane rupture: 150 - 160 dB (jet takes off with this intensity).
  - According to W.H.O - Disabling hearing loss in adults occurs at > 40 dB.
- Frequency: Denoted as Hertz (Hz). Human ear can hear (sensitive to) frequency from 20 - 20000 Hz. < 20 Hz is Infra audible. > 20000 Hz is Ultra sonic.
- Instruments to measure Noise: Sound level meter, Octave band frequency analyzer, Audiometer.

EFFECT OF NOISE EXPOSURE
- Auditory effect:
  - Auditory fatigue: Whistling & Buzzing in ear (90 dB & 4000 Hz).
  - Deafness: Temporary hearing loss / Temporary threshold shift (Disappears in 24 hours at 4000 - 6000 Hz). Permanent hearing loss (100 dB). Occupational hearing loss - Tympanic membrane ruptures at 160 dB.
- Non auditory effect:
  - Annoyance: Irritated & Short tempered.
  - Physiological changes: Rise in BP, Increase in Heart rate, Increased Sweating, Interference with Sleep.
  - Interference with speech: 500 Hz.
  - Efficiency: Reduction in Work output.

CONTROL OF NOISE
- Careful planning of cities: Industries & railways away from cities with Green Belts in between.
- Control of vehicles: Prohibition of pressure horns & One way traffic.
- Acoustic insulation of buildings: Detached buildings & Sealed windows.
- Protection of exposed persons: Ear plugs (30 dB) & Ear muffs (40 dB).
- Legislation: No horn zones & No DJ's after 10 pm.

RADIATION HAZARDS

SOURCES AND TYPES
- Natural sources:
  - Cosmic rays: Jet pilot.
  - Environmental: Terrestrial radiation (rock formation containing Uranium, maximum in Kerala). Atmospheric radiation (radioactive gases Radon & Thoron in the atmosphere).
  - Internal radiation: radioactive matter stored in the body (Carbon 14).
- Manmade sources: X rays (Patients, Radiologists & Medical technicians), Radioactive fallout (Nuclear explosions), Miscellaneous (TV sets, Luminous wrist watches).
- Types of radiation:
  - Ionizing radiation: Electromagnetic radiation (X rays, Gamma rays), Corpuscular radiation (Alpha particle, Beta particle / Electrons & Protons).
  - Non-ionizing radiation: Ultraviolet radiation, Infrared radiation, Microwave radiation.

RADIATION UNITS & DOSES
- Roentgen: unit of Exposure. Replaced by Coulomb per Kg (1 Roentgen = 2.58 x 10-4 C /kg).
- Rad: unit of Absorbed Dose. Replaced by Gray (Gy) (1 Rad = 0.01 Gy).
- Dose equivalent (H): As all types of radiation do not produce same biological effects per unit of energy absorbed. H = D x Q. (D is grays, Q is Quality factor viz. 1 for X rays, Y rays & Electron and 2 for Particle).
- Sievert: SI unit of Radiation dose.
- Curie: unit of Radioactivity.
- Biological response of high dose of radiation:
  - < 5 rad: No observable effects (No deterministic effect to fetus). Permissible dose of manmade radiation should not exceed 5 rad / year.
  - 5 - 50 rad: Slight blood changes seen.
  - 50 - 150 rad: Major blood changes along with nausea, vomiting & fatigue.
  - 150 - 1100 rad: 50 percent of exposed will die in 60 days due to destruction of blood forming organs.
  - 1100 - 2000 rad: 100 percent death within 1-2 weeks.
  - > 2000 rad: Death is certainty, as above 5000 rad CNS can no longer control body functions.

EFFECTS AND PROTECTION
- Somatic effects: Immediate (Radiation sickness, Acute radiation syndrome), Delayed (Leukemia, Carcinogenesis, Fetal developmental abnormalities, Shortening of life).
- Genetic effects: Chromosome mutation (associated with sterility), Point mutation (affects the gene).
- Radiation protection:
  - Lead shields.
  - Lead aprons (0.5mm of lead).
  - Dosimeter.
  - TLD badge.
  - 10 Day Rule is related to radiation protection in pregnancy: acceptable dose of radiation during pregnancy is 0.5 rad.
  - ALARA: As Low As Reasonably Achievable.
  - ICRP: International Commission on Radiological Protection.
  - IAEA: International Atomic Energy Agency.

LIGHT

FACTORS AND MEASUREMENT
- Light factor essential for effective Vision: Sufficiency (illumination of 15 - 20 foot candles), Distribution (should be uniform), Absence of glare (Direct glare from light source such as automobile headlights, Indirect glare from polished surfaces), Steadiness (should be constant NOT flickering), Color of light (natural light has soothing effect).
- Measurement units:
  - Brightness of point source: Luminous intensity (Candela / Candle power).
  - Flow of light: Luminous flux (Lumen).
  - Amount of light reaching surface: Illumination / Illuminance (Lux / Foot candle).
  - Amount of light reemitted from surface: Brightness luminance (Lambert / Foot Lambert).

STANDARDS OF ILLUMINATION
- Basic illumination for Satisfactory vision is 15 to 20 foot candles; Regular work is 6 to 12 foot candles.
- Lightening standards (Eye respond to illumination of 0.1 Lux to 100000 Lux):
  - Reading: 100 Lux.
  - Office work: 400 Lux.
  - Fine work: 900 Lux.
  - Minute / Very focused work: 2000 Lux.
  - Very fine work (watch makers): 3000 Lux.

REFLECTION AND DAYLIGHT FACTOR
- Reflection factor for efficient vision: Ceiling & Roof (80 percent), Walls (50 - 60 percent), Floor (15 - 20 percent), Furniture (30 - 40 percent).
- Daylight Factor = (Instantaneous illumination indoors x 100) / Simultaneous illumination outdoors.
- Daylight Factor should be at least: Living room (8 percent), Kitchen (10 percent).
- Natural lighting is derived partly from Visible sky & partly from Reflection.
- Biological effect of daylight: Effects Body temperature, Stimulation of Melanin synthesis, Activation of precursors of Vitamin D, In vitro degradation of Bilirubin (therapeutic measure for premature infant with hyperbilirubinemia).
- Suggestion for improving daylight illumination:
  - Orientation: Buildings are oriented towards North or South for uniform illumination.
  - Windows: window area should not be less than 10 percent of Floor area.
  - Interior of rooms: Ceiling should be White, Upper portion of the walls should be light & Lower portion should be dark.

HOUSING STANDARDS IN INDIA

URBAN HOUSING STANDARDS
- Site: Elevated, Subsoil water - below 10 ft.
- Built-up area: 2/3 of total area.
- Walls: 9 inch brick wall.
- Roof: > 10 ft.
- Floor area: 50 to 100 sq ft / person.
- Cubic space: > 500 cu ft / capita.
- Windows: 20 percent (1/5) of floor area. Height should NOT be more than 3 ft from floor.
- Doors + Windows: 40 percent (2/5) of floor space.
- Day Light Factor: > 1 percent over half of floor area.
- Kitchen: Separate, Impervious floor, Provided with Water supply & Drainage.
- Sanitary Privy: must be present in every house.

RURAL HOUSING STANDARDS
- Rooms: minimum 2 Living rooms.
- Built-up area: 1/3 of total area.
- Kitchen: Separate kitchen with Paved platform.
- Window area: 10 percent of floor area.
- Sanitary well / Tube well: within 1/2 mile.
- Cattle shed: > 25 ft away.
- Indra Awas Yojna: to address Rural Housing in India.
- National Rural Housing Services is NOT an organization related to housing in India.

OVERCROWDING STANDARDS
- Definition: Refers to situation in which more people are living within a single dwelling so that Movements are restricted, Privacy is secluded, Hygiene becomes impossible & Sleep is disturbed.
- Persons per Room:
  - 1 room: 2 persons.
  - 2 room: 3 persons.
  - 3 room: 5 persons.
  - 4 room: 7 persons.
  - 5 or more rooms: 10 persons (Additional 2 person for each further room).
- Floor Space (Baby under 1 year is not counted & children between 1 to 10 years are counted as 1/2 a unit):
  - Under 50 sq.ft: nil.
  - 50 - 70 sq.ft: 1/2 person.
  - 70 - 90 sq.ft: 1 person.
  - 90 - 100 sq.ft: 1.5 person.
  - 110 sq.ft: 2 persons.
- Sex Separation: 2 persons over 9 years of age, not Husband & Wife, of opposite sexes are obliged to sleep in the same room.

MNEMONICS
- Types of Natural Ventilation: W.I.D - Nature (Wind, Inequality of Temperature, Diffusion).
- Wind subtypes: Wind - CAP (Cross ventilation, Aspiration, Perflation).
- Non-auditory effects of noise: Annoying PIE (Physiological changes, Interference with speech, Efficiency reduction).

subsections.title: Environment and Health: Waste & Excreta Disposal
subsections.content:
OVERVIEW OF THE CHAPTER
This section discusses the management of solid and liquid waste, differentiating between refuse, sewage, and sullage. It outlines various disposal methods including composting, sanitary landfills, septic tanks, and oxidation ponds. It also covers the concept of the sanitation barrier for disease prevention and the Kayakalp award initiative for environmental sanitation in healthcare facilities.

TYPES OF WASTE AND SEWAGE

DEFINITIONS OF WASTE
- Refuse: Solid waste generated from Street / Market / Domestic refuse etc. Human excreta or Night soil is NOT a solid waste.
- Domestic refuse:
  - Ash: residue from fire used for cooking or heating.
  - Rubbish: paper, clothes, glass, metal wood, dust etc.
  - Garbage: food waste generated from kitchen.
- Sewage: Liquid waste containing Excreta.
  - Composition: 99.9 percent water + 0.1 percent solid (Organic & Inorganic).
  - Sewage ground water is disposed by Activated Sludge Process.
  - Dry Weather Flow: amount of sewage that flows in sewage system in 24 hours.
- Sullage: Liquid waste WITHOUT Excreta. Generated from domestic process such as kitchen, laundry, bathing etc.
- Litter: Waste disposed in wrong place by unlawful action.

STRENGTH OF SEWAGE (EXPRESSIONS)
- Biological Oxygen Demand (BOD):
  - Amount of oxygen absorbed by sewage for aerobic destruction of organic matter by living organism.
  - Indicator of organic content of sewage.
  - Strong sewage: BOD > 300 gm / L.
  - Weak sewage: BOD < 100 gm / L.
- Chemical Oxygen Demand (COD):
  - Measures the portion of organic matter which is susceptible to oxidation by chemical oxidizer.
  - Potassium Dichromate is best for COD estimation.
  - If waste water contains toxic substances, organic load is measured by COD.
- Suspended Solids (SS):
  - Strong sewage: SS > 500 mg / L.
  - Weak sewage: SS < 100 mg / L.

METHODS OF REFUSE DISPOSAL

INSANITARY METHODS
- Hog feeding: feeding refuse to pigs.
- Stocking: piling up of refuse & cow dung.
