- Salvaging: screening refuse dumps to cover objects that can be reused.
- Dumping: throwing refuse openly.

SANITARY METHODS
- Composting: Integrating disposal of Refuse & Night soil.
  - NOT a safe method of disposal, but it's good for soil building.
  - If land is available, best method of sewage disposal is composting.
- Sanitary landfill (Controlled tipping):
  - Laying Dry & Condensed refuse in layers with intervening earth, followed by mechanical compression.
  - Most satisfactory method of refuse disposal.
  - Types include Ramp method, Area method, and Trench method.
  - Trench method requirement: 1 acre land is required per year for 10000 population to fill its 2 m depth.
- Incineration: high temperature dry oxidation process.

COMPOSTING PROCESS AND MECHANISMS

METHODS OF COMPOSTING
- Definition: Method of Integrated disposal of Refuse & Nightsoil (or Sludge).
- Indore method: Aerobic Process.
- Bangalore method: Anaerobic Hot Fermentation Process.
  - Alternate layer of Refuse & Night soil in 3:1 proportion.
  - Topmost & Lowermost layer should be of refuse.
  - Steps: Trench is dug 3 ft deep, 5 ft broad & 15 ft long. First layer of refuse 6 inch spread at bottom, over which nightsoil of 2 inch is added. Alternate layers are added till the heap rises 1 ft above the ground level. The heap is then covered with Earth.

MECHANISM AND BYPRODUCTS
- Within 7 days heat is generated which decomposes refuse & nightsoil & destroys all pathogenic & parasitic organisms.
- Organic matter breaks down under bacterial action resulting in formation of Humus like material known as Compost.
- At the end of 6 months resulting Manure is ready for application to the land.
- Principle byproducts: Carbon Dioxide, Water & Heat.
- Heat produced (60 deg C): destroys Eggs, Larvae & Pathogens.
- End product: is a good soil builder containing Nitrates & Phosphates.

MECHANICAL COMPOSTING
- Refuse is first cleared of salvageable material such as bones, metals & glass.
- It is then pulverized to reduce the size to < 2 inches.
- Then mixed with Sewage, Sludge or Nightsoil in a rotating machine & then incubated.
- Entire process is completed in 6 weeks.
- Method of composting in Holland, Germany & Switzerland.

SEPTIC TANK SANITATION

DESIGN AND CAPACITY
- Definition: Water tight masonry tank into which household sewage is admitted for treatment.
- Satisfactory method for individual dwelling or houses which have adequate water supply but NO public sewage system.
- Capacity: 500 gallons (Ideal Retention Period is 24 hours).
- Length: twice of breadth.
- Depth: 2 m (Liquid depth is 1.2 m).
- Air space depth: 30 cm.

PROCESS OF PURIFICATION
- Solid settles down in a tank to form Sludge.
- Lighter solid (grease & fat) rise up to form Scum.
- These solids undergo Anaerobic digestion & methane is formed.
- Liquid which passes out of outlet pipe is called Effluent (contain bacteria, cysts, Helminthic ova & organic matter).
- Effluent is allowed to percolate in subsoil where it undergoes Aerobic digestion by bacteria.
- Anaerobic digestion occurs inside the septic tank.
- Aerobic digestion occurs outside of septic tank (i.e. in Subsoil).

KEY CONCEPTS IN SEWAGE TREATMENT
- Heart of activated sludge is aeration tank.
- Most important factor in sewage treatment is solids.

SANITATION BARRIER AND EXCRETA DISPOSAL

SANITATION BARRIER
- Definition: Segregation of the excreta by imposing a barrier. It is a barrier to prevent the spread of Faeco Oral Diseases.
- Disease Cycle can be broken at various levels:
  - Segregation of Faeces.
  - Protection of Water supplies.
  - Maintenance of Personal Hygiene.
  - Control of Flies.
  - Protection of Foods.
- Of these, the most effective step would be to Segregate the Feces & Arrange for its Proper Disposal, so that the disease agent cannot reach the new host, directly or indirectly.
- Barrier can be provided by: Sanitary Latrine, Disposal Pit, Sewage System, Sewage Treatment Plants etc.
- Example: Water Seal / Water Trap in Sanitary Latrine.
  - Bent pipe of 7.5 cm diameter with Depth of 2 cm.
  - Prevent Flies access & suppresses Smell.
  - Most important prerequisite of sanitary latrine.
- 6F's of Sanitation Barrier: Faeces on one side and Finger, Flies, Fomites, Fluids & Food on the other side.

METHODS OF EXCRETA DISPOSAL
- Unsewered areas:
  - Service type latrine (Conservancy System): nightsoil in bucket type latrines.
  - Non service type latrines (Sanitary Latrines): Bore hole latrines, Pit latrines, Water seal type of latrines (P.R.A.I type, R.C.A type, Sulabh Shouchalaya), Septic Tank, Aqua Privy, Chemical Closet.
  - Latrines suitable for temporary use: Shallow trench latrines, Deep trench latrines, Pit latrines, Bore hole latrines.
- Sewered areas:
  - Water carriage system: Households' sanitary fittings (Water closet - Indian squatting type & Western commode type), House sewers or Public sewers, Sewer appurtenances (Man holes, Trap).
  - Sewage treatment: Primary treatment (Screening, Removal of grit in grit chamber by settlement of heavy objects, Plain sedimentation). Secondary treatment (Trickling filter for aerobic digestion, Activated sludge process where sewage ground water is disposed by aerobic digestion).
  - Other methods: Sea outfall or River outfall, Sewage farming, Oxidation pond, Oxidation ditches.

OXIDATION POND

CHARACTERISTICS
- Also known as Stabilization Pond / Redox Pond / Sewage Lagoons / Waste Stabilization Pond.
- It is an open shallow pool 5 ft deep with an Inlet & Outlet.
- Over 50 ponds are working at present in India.
- Established method of purifying sewage for Small Communities.
- 1st large scale installation was in Bhilai for the population of 1 lakh.
- Oxidation Pond requires: Bacteria, Algae, Sunlight.

MECHANISM OF OXIDATION POND
- Organic matter in the Sewage is oxidized by Bacteria to Carbon Dioxide, Ammonia & Water.
- Oxygen needed for oxidation is derived from Algae which liberates oxygen under Sunlight.
- During Sunshine Hours: Oxidation Pond is predominantly Aerobic.
- During Night Hours: Bottom layers are generally Anaerobic.
- Hence, purification in oxidation pond is by Combination of Aerobic & Anaerobic Bacteria's.
- Effluent may be used for growing crops.

KAYAKALP AWARD INITIATIVE

OVERVIEW AND OBJECTIVES
- Kayakalp award is given for environmental sanitation.
- National initiative to reward public health facilities which shows high level of sanitation, hygiene & infection control.
- Parameters used: sanitation & hygiene, waste management, infection control.
- Objectives:
  - To promote cleanliness, hygiene & infection control practices in public health care facilities.
  - To recognize & give incentives to public health care centers which shows exemplary performances.
  - To create sustainable practices related to improved cleanliness.

SCOPE AND REWARDS
- Scope:
  - Best district hospital (DH): Category A- 1, Category B - 2, Category C - 3.
  - Best 2 CHC/ sub district hospital (SDH).
  - Best 1 PHC in every district.
- Reward amount:
  - DH: 50 lakh.
  - CHC: 15 lakh.
  - SDH: 10 lakh.
  - PHC: 2 lakh.
- Certificate of commendation (at least 70 percent score):
  - DH: 3 lakh.
  - CHC / SDH: 1 lakh.
  - PHC: 0.5 lakh.

MNEMONICS
- Sanitary RAT: Methods of Sanitary landfill (Ramp method, Area method, Trench method).
- B.A.S: Requirements for an Oxidation Pond (Bacteria, Algae, Sunlight).

subsections.title: Entomology: Vectors & Disease Transmission
subsections.content:
OVERVIEW OF THE CHAPTER
This section deals with Medical Entomology, specifically studying arthropods of medical importance. It details the specific diseases transmitted by various vectors (including mosquitoes, flies, fleas, ticks, mites, lice, and bugs) and defines the exact modes of disease transmission within these arthropod hosts.

ARTHROPOD BORNE DISEASES BY VECTOR

MOSQUITOES
- Anopheles: Malaria (Definitive host).
- Culex: Bancroftian filariasis (Intermediate host), Japanese Encephalitis (JE), West Nile fever, Western Equine Encephalitis Virus.
- Aedes: Dengue, Chikungunya, Yellow Fever, Rift Valley Fever, Filariasis, Zika.
- Mansonia: Malayan / Brugian filariasis, Chikungunya, St. Louis encephalitis.

FLIES
- Housefly: Typhoid/Paratyphoid Fever, Cholera, Amoebiasis, Poliomyelitis. (Note: Houseflies do NOT bite).
- Tsetse fly: Sleeping Sickness (Intermediate host).
- Blackfly: Onchocerciasis (River Blindness).
- Sandfly (Phlebotomus):
  - Diseases: Oroya fever, Kala Azar, Oriental Sore.
  - Habitat: Holes, Cracks & Cervices in wall & tree.
  - Morphology: Legs twice longer than body & fine hairs on the margin of wings.
  - P. argentipes: Transmits Kala Azar (Visceral leishmaniasis).
  - P. papatasii: Transmits Sand Fly Fever, Oriental Sore.
  - P. sergenti: Transmits Oriental Sore (Cutaneous leishmaniasis).

FLEAS
- Sand flea: Causes Ulcers in foot.
- Rat flea: Transmits Bubonic Plague, Endemic Typhus / Murine Typhus, Chiggerosis. Also acts as host for Hymenolepis diminuta (Rat Tapeworm).

TICKS
- Hard tick: Transmits Tick Typhus, Kyasanur Forest Disease (KFD) (in India), Babesiosis, Tularemia, Viral Encephalitis, Tick Paralysis, Rockey Mountain Fever.
- Soft tick: Transmits Q Fever, KFD (outside India), Relapsing Fever.

MITE
- Diseases: Rickettsial pox, Scrub typhus, Scabies.
- Trombiculid mite: Transmits Scrub Typhus.
- Itch mite: Causes Scabies (Drug of Choice is 5 percent Permethrin).

OTHER VECTORS
- Louse/ Lice: Transmits Epidemic Typhus, Relapsing Fever, Trench Fever, Pediculosis.
- Reduviid bug: Transmits Chagas Disease.
- Cyclops: Transmits Guinea Worm Disease.

DISEASE TRANSMISSION IN ARTHROPODS

DIRECT CONTACT
- Transferred from man-to-man Close contact.
- Example: Scabies by Itch mite.

MECHANICAL TRANSMISSION
- Agent is transferred Mechanically by the arthropod.
- Example: Typhoid / Trachoma by Housefly.

BIOLOGICAL TRANSMISSION
- When disease agent multiplies or undergoes Cyclical / Developmental change with or without multiplication in arthropod host it is called Biological Transmission. It has three types:
  - I. Cyclo - Developmental:
    - Disease agent undergoes Cyclical changes but does NOT multiply in the body of the arthropod.
    - Example: Guinea worm embryo in Cyclops & Filarial parasite in Culex mosquito.
  - II. Propagative:
    - Disease agent Multiplies in the body of vector but does NOT undergo any cyclical changes.
    - Example: Plague bacilli in Rat fleas & Yellow fever virus in Aedes mosquito.
  - III. Cyclo - Propagative:
    - Disease agent undergoes Cyclical changes & Multiplies in the body of arthropod.
    - Example: Malaria parasite in Anopheles mosquito.

VERTICAL AND TRANSSTADIAL TRANSMISSION
- Transovarial Transmission (Vertical Transmission):
  - Female vector passes the infectious agent through her eggs to the next generation.
  - Example: Rickettsia Rickettsii in Ticks.
- Transstadial Transmission:
  - Agent transmitted from Nymph to Adult vector.
  - Example: Borrelia Burgdorferi in Ticks.

MNEMONICS
- Aedes Mosquito Diseases: Donge me (Dengue), Fill (Filariasis), Rift Valley ka (Rift Valley fever), Zaykedar (Zika virus disease), Yellow (Yellow fever), Chicken (Chikungunya).
- Mansonia Diseases: ManSoNia (Malayan filariasis, Saint Louis Encephalitis).
- Tsetse fly: Tse-Tse fly -> Sleep -> Sleeping Sickness.
- Blackfly: Onchocerciasis (River Blindness) -> Black fly.
- Sandfly Diseases: Kala oriental sand Phailke Oori... -> Kala-Azar, Oriental sore, Sandfly, Phlebotomus, Oroya fever. (Surgeon Papa = P. papatasii for Oriental Sore; Oriental Insurance = P. sergenti for Oriental Sore; Kala Argentanian = P. argentipes for Kala Azar).
- Rat flea Diseases: Rats mu ends at Cheese -> Bubonic Plague, Murine typhus, Endemic Typhus, Chiggerosis.
- Hard tick Diseases: Forest Hard Rocking Indian Forest Babes Tolerate Viral ticks -> KFD (in India), Rocky mountain spotted fever, Indian tick typhus, Babesiosis, Tularemia, Viral encephalitis, Tick paralysis.
- Soft tick Diseases: Soft Takiya ghar pe to Q fever -> Relapsing fever, Q fever, KFD (outside India / foreign me).
- Mite Diseases: Rickey might Scrub for Scabies -> Rickettsial pox, Scrub typhus, Scabies.
- Louse Diseases: feeling Lousy, Relaxing on Bench, Typing Epidemic with Pedicure -> Louse, Relapsing fever, Trench fever, Epidemic typhus, Pediculosis.
- Cyclops Disease: Guinea Cycle -> Guinea worm disease via Cyclops.
- Biological Transmission Types:
  - CycLo-developmental: CuLex (FiLarial Parasite) & CycLops (Guinea Cycle).
  - Propagative (Multiply only): Plague bacilli in Rat flea, Yellow fever virus in Aedes mosquito.
  - TranStadial: Stages (Nymph to Adult -> Baccha to Bada) -> Borrelia Burgdorferi.

subsections.title: Entomology: Mosquitoes & Control
subsections.content:
OVERVIEW OF THE CHAPTER
This section provides an in-depth comparison of the four major mosquito vectors: Anopheles, Culex, Aedes, and Mansonia. It details their species, diseases transmitted, physical characteristics across life stages, breeding and biting habits. It also covers the mosquito life cycle and comprehensive Integrated Vector Control measures encompassing anti-larval, anti-adult, and personal protection strategies.

MOSQUITO VECTORS: COMPARATIVE CHARACTERISTICS

ANOPHELES
- Species: An. fluviatilis, An. minimus, An. sundaicus, An. stephensi, An. culicifacies, An. philippinensis.
- Disease transmitted: Malaria.
- Also known as: Sophisticated mosquito.
- Flight range: 5 km.
- Breeding habits: Clean water.
- Eggs: Laid singly, Boat shaped (has floats).
- Larvae: Rest Parallel to undersurface of water (Surface feeder). NO siphon tubes. Palmate hair present.
- Pupae: Broad & Short siphon tube.
- Adults: Rest with an Inclined angle to the surface. Spotted wings.

CULEX
- Species: Culex fatigans.
- Disease transmitted: Bancroftian filariasis, Japanese Encephalitis (JE), West Nile fever, Western Equine Encephalitis.
- Also known as: Nuisance mosquito.
- Flight range: 11 km (greatest).
- Biting habits: Midnight, Below knee (leg).
- Breeding habits: Dirty / Polluted water, Stagnant drains, Septic tank, Burrow pits.
- Eggs: Laid in Clusters.
- Larvae: Rest Perpendicular to undersurface of water. Siphon tube present, Long air tube.
- Pupae: Long & Narrow siphon tube.
- Adults: Rest with Hunch back.

AEDES
- Species: Aedes aegypti, Aedes vittatus, Aedes albopictus.
- Disease transmitted: Dengue, Filariasis, Rift valley fever, Zika, Yellow fever, Chikungunya.
- Also known as: Tiger mosquito.
- Flight range: 100 m.
- Biting habits: Day, Fearless & Recurrent biters.
- Breeding habits: Artificial collection of water. Discarded tins, Broken bottle, Flower pots.
- Eggs: Laid Singly, Cigar shaped (no floats).
- Larvae: Rest Perpendicular to undersurface of water. Siphon tube present, Short air tube.
- Pupae: Long & Narrow siphon tube.
- Adults: Yellow / White stripes on black body & legs.

MANSONIA
- Species: M. annulifera, M. uniformis, M. indiana, M. longipalpis.
- Disease transmitted: Malayan / Brugian filariasis, St. Louis Encephalitis, Chikungunya.
- Breeding habits: Floating Aquatic plants in water, Pistia stratoites, Water hyacinth.
- Eggs: Laid in Clusters, Star shaped.
- Larvae: Rest Perpendicular to rootlets of plants. Siphon tube present.
- Pupae: Long & Narrow siphon tube.
- Adults: Big brown mosquito with speckling on their wings & legs.

IMPORTANT INDICES AND ZONES
- Aedes Aegypti Index: Number of houses in defined area of which Actual breeding of Aedes aegypti were found / Total number of houses Examined in that area.
- Distance of Airport or Seaport which has to be kept free from Aedes mosquito is 400 m.

LIFE CYCLE AND HABITS OF MOSQUITOES

LIFE CYCLE STAGES
- Egg stage: 1 to 2 days.
- Larva stage: 5 to 7 days.
- Pupal stage: 1 to 2 days.
- Adult stage: 2 weeks (14 days).
- Total Life Cycle: 3 weeks.
- From Egg to Adult: 7 to 10 days.
- Life Span: 8 to 34 days (Life span is Influenced by Temperature & Humidity).

HABITS OF MOSQUITOES
- Feeding habits:
  - Male subsist on Plant juices.
  - Females require a Blood meal, once in 3 days (Female can travel upto 3 kms).
  - Anthrophillic: prefer Human blood.
  - Zoophillic: prefer Animal blood.
- Time of biting: Evening or early Night.
- Resting habits:
  - Endophilia: rest indoors (dark corners, upper walls, under furniture).
  - Exophilia: rest Outdoors (vegetation, cattle sheds, wells).
- Breeding habits (Anopheline - Clean water specific locations):
  - An. culicifacies & An. philippinensis: found in Plains.
  - An. fluviatilis & An. minimus: found in Foot hill region.
  - An. sundaicus & An. stephensi: found in Coastal region.
  - Anopheles stephensi: causes Urban malaria & breeds in manmade water containers (overhead water tanks, well).

MOSQUITO CONTROL MEASURES (INTEGRATED VECTOR CONTROL)

ANTI-LARVAL MEASURES
- Environmental control: 
  - Best approach for control of arthropods.
  - Intermittent Irrigation: field is alternately watered & drained i.e., soil surface is allowed to dry prior to next application of water.
  - Changing the salinity of water.
  - Cover Overhead tanks (most efficient).
  - Eliminate breeding places (Source Reduction): Filling, Leveling & Drainage of breeding places. Best way to control House flies. 
  - For Culex: adequate disposal of Polluted dirty water i.e. sewage & waste water.
  - For Aedes: get rid of Artificial collection of water i.e., discarded tin, empty pots & broken bottles.
  - For Anopheles: filling & drainage of Clean water collection.
  - For Mansonia: Aquatic plants should be removed.
- Chemical control:
  - Mineral oil: 80 - 90 litres / hectare - Once a week. Diesel oil, Fuel oil, Kerosene. Oil forms a thin film which cuts off the air supply to the mosquito larvae & pupae.
  - Paris Green: 2 percent dust applied to a dose of 1 kg / hectare. Copper Acetoarsenite (contain 50 percent arsenious oxide). Micro crystalline Powder, insoluble in water. Stomach poison to larvae but harmless to human & animals (Kills mainly Anopheles larvae).
  - Synthetic insecticides: Abate, Malathion, Fenthion, Chlorpyrifos. DDT is NOT used in antilarval measures due to its residual effect.
- Biological control:
  - Fish: Fish feeds readily on mosquito larvae. Gambusia affinis & Lebister reticulatus (Barbados millions) & Poicelia.
  - Bacteria: Bacillus thuringiensis H14. Bacillus thuringiensis serovar israelensis used as larvicide against mosquito larvae (Bt. toxin).
  - Fungi: Coelomomyces.
  - Predator mosquito: Toxorhynchites splendens.

ANTI-ADULT MEASURES
- Residual Sprays: Spraying houses with residual insecticides.
  - DDT: 1 gm/m2 (effective for 6 months).
  - Malathion: 2 gm/m2 (effective for 3 months).
  - Lindane (BHC): 0.5 gm/m2 (effective for 3 months).
  - Other agents: Propoxur, Linithion.
- Space Sprays:
  - Insecticidal formulation sprayed into atmosphere in form of Mist or Fog.
  - Pyrethrum: nerve poison, for which aerosol dispenser is needed & has NO residual effect.
- Genetic Control:
  - Sterile male technique, Cytoplasmic incompatibility, Chromosomal translocation, Sex distortion & Gene replacement.

PROTECTION AGAINST MOSQUITO BITES
- Mosquito Net: Should be White Rectangular net (best pattern). Net opening should not exceed 0.0475 inch. Number of holes in one square inch is usually 150.
- Insecticide Treated Bed Nets (ITBN): Use synthetic Pyrethroid (Deltamethrin / Cyfluthrin). Effective for 6 months.
- Long Lasting Insecticidal Nets (LLIN): Use Pyrethroid insecticides. Allows > 20 wash & use for 3 years.
- Screening: Screening of building with Copper or Bronze gauze. Size - 16 meshes to 1 inch. Aperture should not be larger than 0.0475 inch.
- Repellents: Allethrin, Indolane, Dimethyl carbate, Ethyl hexanediol, Benzyl benzoate, Diethyltoluamide (DEET - N,N, di ethyl m toluamide).

MNEMONICS
- Breeding Habits of Anopheles:
  - Kal Philippines in Plane: An. culicifacies & An. philippinensis are found in Plains.
  - Mini Flowers at Foothill: An. minimus & An. fluviatilis are found in Foot hill region.
  - Coastal Sundays with Stephen: An. sundaicus & An. stephensi are found in Coastal region.

subsections.title: Entomology: Insecticides, Lice & Ticks
subsections.content:
OVERVIEW OF THE CHAPTER
This section classifies the chemical agents used for vector control, detailing the properties, dosages, and mechanisms of action of specific insecticides like Paris Green, DDT, Pyrethrum, Malathion, Fenthion, and HCH. It also explains the color-coding system for pesticide toxicity and provides a detailed anatomical and behavioral differentiation between head and body lice, as well as hard and soft ticks.

CLASSIFICATION OF INSECTICIDES

NATURAL INSECTICIDES
- Pyrethrum.
- Rotenone.

SYNTHETIC INSECTICIDES
- 1. Organophosphates:
  - Malathion (alkyl phosphate).
  - Parathion (aryl phosphate).
  - Chlorthion (aryl phosphate).
  - Chlorpyrifos.
  - Dioxathion.
  - Diazinon (aryl phosphate).
  - Fenthion.
  - Fenitrothion.
- 2. Organo - Chlorines:
  - DDT.
  - BHC (HCH).
  - Lindane.
  - Dieldrin.
- 3. Carbonate Carbonyl:
  - Carbaryl.
  - Propoxur carbamate.

COLOR CODING IN PESTICIDES
- Red: Extremely toxic (Example: Zinc phosphide).
- Yellow: Highly toxic (Example: Endosulfan).
- Blue: Moderately toxic (Example: Malathion).
- Green: Slightly toxic (Example: Mosquito Repellant).

INSECTICIDES USED IN REPELLANTS
- Benzyl benzoate.
- Ethyl hexanediol.
- Indolane.

IMPORTANT PESTICIDE NOTES
- Cyfluthrin: Pyrethroid of Choice.
- Permethrin: Synthetic Pyrethroid.
- Zinc Phosphide: Most hazardous & most common rodenticide (Single dose).

DETAILED INSECTICIDES PROFILES

PARIS GREEN
- 2 percent dust applied at dose of 1 kg / Hectare.
- Copper acetoarsenite (contains 50 percent Arsenious oxide).
- Emerald Green powder.
- Kills mainly or more effective against Anopheles larvae as they are surface feeders.
- Stomach poison for larvae but HARMLESS to Human, Animals & Fish.

DDT (DICHLORO - DIPHENYL - TRICHLORO-ETHANE)
- Synthesized by: Zeilder.
- Properties discovered by: Paul Miller.
- Its most active form: Para-Para isomer (70 - 80 percent DDT).
- Action: Contact Nerve Poison.
- Mechanism: Inhibit Acetyl Cholinesterase Enzyme (ACE).
- Speed of action: Takes several hours to kill.
- Residual effect: 18 months.
- Dose: 100 - 200 mg / sq ft.
- Sand flies are NOT resistant to DDT i.e., DDT can kill Phlebotomus argentipes.
- Used for Adult Mosquitoes.
- Pyrethrum has synergistic action with DDT.
- Least toxic Organochlorine.

PYRETHRUM
- Natural insecticide: vegetable origin (Flower - Chrysanthemum).
- NO residual action (short lived effect).
- Action: Contact nerve poison for which aerosol dispenser is needed.
- Mode of application: Space spray.
- Effect: Knock down effect with paralysis.
- 5 active Pyrethrums: Pyrethrin I & II, Cinerin I & II, Jasmoline II.
- Synergistic action with DDT.

MALATHION
- Used for Ultra - Low Volume fogging.
- Least toxic among organophosphate compound.
- LEAST toxic to man & Most toxic to insects.
- Alternative to DDT.
- Dose: 100 - 200 mg / sq ft (Every 3 months).
- Mechanism: Inhibit Acetyl Cholinesterase.

FENTHION
- Also known as: Baytex.
- Appearance: Brown liquid.
- Odor: Garlic odor.
- Mode of application: Residual Spray.
- Action: Stomach + Contact Poison.
- NOT used in repellants.

HEXA CHLOROCYCLO HEXANE (HCH)
- Synthesized by: Michel Faraday.
- Appearance: White color powder.
- Gamma (y) HCH is: Lindane.
- Short residual action: 3 months.

DIFFERENCE BETWEEN HEAD & BODY LICE

HEAD LICE
- Species: Pediculus humanus capitus.
- Eggs location: Lay its nets (eggs) on the Hairs of the head.
- Habitat: Lives on the Scalp.
- Dependency: CANNOT live away from body of human host.
- Appearance: 2 - 3 mm long, Brownish in color with 6 legs & NO wings.

BODY LICE
- Species: Pediculus humanus corporis.
- Eggs location: Lay their eggs in Bedding or Clothing's.
- Habitat: Found around the base of Neck or Torso.
- Dependency: Live on Bedding & Clothing (come out to feed).
- Appearance: Slightly longer than head lice (2.3 - 3.6 mm), rest is similar to head lice in appearance.

DIFFERENCE BETWEEN HARD & SOFT TICK

HARD TICKS (IXODIDAE)
- Capitulum visibility: Visible Anteriorly Located.
- Scutum: Covers entire Back in Males, but NOT entirely in Females.
- Head/Capitulum: Situated at Anterior end.
- Spiracles: Situated behind 4th Coxa.
- Eggs: Thousands of eggs laid at 1 setting.
- Nymphal stages: 1.
- Habits: CANNOT withstand Starvation, so feeds Night & Day.
- Disease transmitted: Tick Typhus, Tularemia, Human Babesiosis, KFD (in India), Viral Encephalitis, Tick Paralysis.
- Species: Dermacentor Andersoni, Haemophysalis Spinigera.
- Found: Always on their Host.

SOFT TICKS (ARGASIDAE)
- Capitulum visibility: Not Visible, Ventrally Located.
- Scutum: ABSENT.
- Head/Capitulum: Lies Ventrally.
- Spiracles: Situated between 3rd & 4th Coxa.
- Eggs: 100 eggs over a long period.
- Nymphal stages: 5.
- Habits: Can stand Starvation for a year or more.
- Disease transmitted: Relapsing fever, Q fever, KFD (outside India).
- Species: Ornithodorus Moubata.
- Found: In Cracks & Crevices.

ADDITIONAL TICK-BORNE DISEASES
- Rocky Mountain Spotted Fever & Crimean Congo fever are transmitted by ticks.
- Ixodes ticks transmits Lyme's disease.

MNEMONICS
- Insecticides used in Repellants: B.E.I (Benzyl benzoate, Ethyl hexanediol, Indolane).
- Malathion's key features: 'M' for Most toxic to insects, 'L' for Least toxic to man, 'A' for Alternate to DDT.

title: Hospital Waste Management
content:
OVERVIEW OF THE CHAPTER
This chapter covers the generation, health hazards, and comprehensive management of biomedical waste (BMW) produced in healthcare settings. It includes legal guidelines, segregation categories, treatment modalities, and spill management protocols essential for safe disposal.

DEFINITIONS
- Biomedical Waste (BMW): Any waste which is generated during the research, diagnosis, treatment, or immunization of human beings or animals, or in research activities pertaining thereto, or in the production or testing of biologicals.
- Disinfectants: Chemical agents which destroy or inhibit the growth of pathogenic microorganisms in non-sporing or vegetative states. Applied on inanimate objects such as instruments and surfaces to control and prevent infections.
- Antiseptics: Chemical agents that destroy or inhibit the growth of microorganisms on living tissue. Applied to the surface of the body or to exposed tissues.

CORE CONCEPTS

Sources of Healthcare Waste
- Government hospitals
- Private hospitals
- Nursing homes
- Physician's and Dentist's offices or clinics
- Dispensaries and Primary health centres
- Medical research and training establishments
- Mortuaries, Blood banks, and collection centres
- Animal houses and Slaughterhouses
- Laboratories, Research organizations, and Vaccinating centres
- Biotechnology institutions and production units

Composition of Biomedical Waste
- Between 75 to 90 percent of waste is non-risk or general healthcare waste, comparable to domestic waste.
- The remaining 10 to 25 percent is regarded as hazardous and creates health risks.
- Average hospital waste produces per bed per day in government hospitals is 0.5 to 4 kg.
- Private hospitals produce 1/2 to 2 kg per bed per day.
- Nursing homes produce 1/2 to 1 kg per bed per day.
- The total quantity of hospital wastes generated in Bangalore is about 40 tonnes per day.
- Average composition of hospital waste in India (wet-weight basis):
  - General waste (food waste, sweepings from premises): 53.5 percent
  - Paper: 15 percent
  - Rags: 15 percent
  - Plastics: 10 percent
  - Glass: 4.0 percent
  - Infectious waste: 1.5 percent
  - Metal (sharps etc.): 1 percent
- WHO distribution estimates for preliminary planning in developing countries:
  - 80 percent general healthcare waste
  - 15 percent pathological and infectious waste
  - 3 percent chemical and pharmacological waste
  - 1 percent sharps waste
  - Less than 1 percent special waste (radioactive, cytotoxic, pressurized containers, broken thermometers, used batteries)

Health Hazards and At-Risk Groups
- All individuals exposed to hazardous healthcare waste are potentially at risk.
- Groups at risk from BMW: Medical doctors, nurses, healthcare auxiliaries, hospital maintenance personnel, patients, visitors, workers in support services (laundries, waste handling, transportation), and workers in waste disposal facilities (landfills, incinerators, scavengers).
- Hazards from infectious waste and sharps: Pathogens may enter the human body through a puncture, abrasion, cut in the skin, mucous membranes by inhalation, or by ingestion. Particular concern for HIV and Hepatitis B and C. Bacteria resistant to antibiotics also contribute to hazards.
- Hazards from chemical and pharmaceutical waste: May cause intoxication, acute or chronic exposure injuries, or burns. Disinfectants are used in large quantities and can be corrosive or reactive, forming highly toxic secondary compounds.
- Hazards from genotoxic waste: Exposure through inhalation of dust or aerosols, absorption through skin, or ingestion of contaminated food. Outdated cytotoxic drugs are best disposed off by destruction and dumping in a secure landfill (or returned to supplier / incinerated at > 1200 C).
- Hazards from radioactive waste: Causes headache, dizziness, and vomiting. Genotoxic and affects genetic material.
- Public sensitivity: The general public is very sensitive to the visual impact of healthcare waste, particularly anatomical waste.

LEGISLATION AND BMW MANAGEMENT IN INDIA
- Ministry of Environment and Forests is the regulatory body in India.
- Legislation involves Section 6, 8, 25 of the Environmental (Protection) Act 1986.
- BMW Management and Handling Rules 1998 previously categorized waste into 10 categories. The schedules under it were:
  - Schedule I: Categories of BMW
  - Schedule II: Color coding of container
  - Schedule III: Labels for container
  - Schedule IV: Labels for transport of containers
  - Schedule V: Standards for Treatment and disposal of BMW
- The Act is superseded by Bio-Medical Waste Management Rules 2016, which simplified categories into four color codes (Yellow, Red, White, Blue).
- Exemptions: BMW guidelines do NOT apply to: Radioactive waste, Hazardous waste/chemicals, Municipal solid waste, Lead acid batteries, E-waste (best way to dispose is recycling), and Genetically engineered microorganisms.
- Siting rule: No healthcare facility should have a treatment and disposal facility if a common BMW treatment facility is available within 75 kms.

BIOMEDICAL WASTE CATEGORIES AND SEGREGATION (2016 GUIDELINES)

Yellow Category (8 subclasses)
- Bag type: Yellow non-chlorinated plastic bags or containers.
- Types of waste included:
  - Human anatomical waste (tissues, organs, body parts, placenta, fetus, tooth).
  - Animal anatomical waste (animal carcasses, body parts).
  - Soiled waste (cotton, dressings, plaster casts contaminated with blood and body fluids, shoe covers, N95 masks, face masks, bags containing discarded or residual blood).
  - Expired or discarded medicines (outdated, contaminated and discarded medicine, and cytotoxic drugs).
  - Chemical waste (chemicals used in disinfections, discarded chemical containers).
  - Liquid waste (liquid from lab or disinfecting activities, X-ray film liquid, discarded formalin, infected secretions, aspirated body fluids, insecticides).
  - Discarded linen, mattresses, and beddings contaminated with blood or body fluids.
  - Microbiology, Biotechnology, and other clinical laboratory waste (culture plates, specimens of microorganisms, live or attenuated vaccines, glucometer strips, blood bags).
- Treatment and disposal:
  - Pretreatment is required for microbiology/biotech waste and liquid waste (chemical disinfection before drain).
  - Final disposal: Incineration, plasma pyrolysis, or deep burial.

Red Category
- Bag type: Red non-chlorinated plastic bags (greater than 50-micron thickness). NO pretreatment required.
- Types of waste included: Contaminated waste (recyclable) from disposable items such as IV sets, urine bags, syringes (without needles and fixed needle syringes with their needles cut), gloves, vacutainers, catheters, tubing, hazmat suits (PPE kits), oxygen masks.
- Treatment and disposal: Autoclaving, microwaving, or hydroclaving. Followed by shredding or mutilation. Treated waste is sent to authorized recyclers for energy recovery or plastics to diesel, fuel oil, or road making. Plastic waste should not be sent to landfill sites. BMW red bags should NOT be incinerated as they contain cadmium.

White Category
- Bag type: Translucent, puncture-proof, leak-proof, tamper-proof plastic bin.
- Types of waste included: Waste sharps including metals. Needles from hub cutter, suture needles, lumbar puncture needles, blades, scalpels, syringes with fixed needle (e.g., BCG), lancets, and any other contaminated sharp object that may cause puncture and cuts.
- Treatment and disposal: Autoclaving or dry heat sterilization. Followed by shredding or mutilation. Then sent for final disposal to iron foundries, sanitary landfills, or designated concrete waste sharp pits.

Blue Category
- Bag type: Cardboard boxes with blue colored markings or puncture-proof blue bin.
- Types of waste included: Glassware (broken, discarded, and contaminated glass including EMPTY medicine vials and ampoules, except those contaminated with cytotoxic waste) and Metallic body implants (ortho transplants, plates, screws, wires).
- Treatment and disposal: Disinfection (by soaking in sodium hypochlorite), autoclaving, microwaving, or hydroclaving. Then sent for recycling.

Miscellaneous Segregation Rules
- Biodegradable waste products are disposed of in yellow bags.
- Dressings soaked in an HIV patient's blood are first treated with 1 percent hypochlorite and then sent for incineration in a yellow bag.
- Plastic covers are disposed of in black bags.
- Incineration ash is discarded in a black bag. Incineration ash was included in category 9 of the old classification, but falls under NONE in the new classification.
- Cytotoxic drug disposal mechanism in India is incineration.
- Yellow bags can be incinerated.
- Dead foetus below the viability period can be considered human anatomical waste and handed over in a yellow bag with a copy of the official medical termination of pregnancy certificate.

TREATMENT MODALITIES FOR BIOMEDICAL WASTE

1. Mechanical Process
- Compacting: Reducing size and volume of non-hazardous waste.
- Shredding: Breaking into pieces by grinding or cutting (plastics, rubber, soft metals). Subject to frequent breakdowns and poor functioning.
- Landfill: Includes open dumps, sanitary landfills, and deep burial. Open dumps are not a safe method of disposal for infectious pathogens.
- Deep burial: Pit must be greater than 2m deep. Half filled with waste, then alternate layers of 10 cm soil and waste until it reaches 50 cm from the surface, then filled with soil.
- Encapsulation: Filling containers with waste and adding immobilizing material (plastic foam, sand, cement, clay), then sealing the container. Simple, low-cost, and safe, but not recommended for non-sharp infectious waste.
- Inertization: Mixing BMW with cement, lime, and water to form cubes before disposal. Prevents toxic substances from contaminating ground or surface water. Composition: 65 percent wastes, 15 percent lime, 15 percent cement, 5 percent water. NOT useful for infectious waste. Used for pharmaceutical waste and incineration ashes with high metal content.

2. Thermal Process
- Heat disinfection: Boiling for 20 minutes. Used for pretreatment of sharps and plastic wastes.
- Hot air oven: Causes sterilization and mutilation. Parameters: 160 C for 2 hours, 170 C for 1 hour, or 180 C for 30 minutes. Used for glassware, powders, oils (items impermeable to steam).
- Autoclave: Steam sterilization under pressure (Low heat thermal process). Biological indicator: Geobacillus stearothermophilus (at least 1 x 10^6 spores per strip).
  - Gravity flow autoclave: >= 121 C at 15 psi for > 60 min; >= 135 C at 31 psi for > 45 min; >= 149 C at 52 psi for > 30 min.
  - Vacuum flow autoclave: >= 121 C at 15 psi for > 45 min; >= 135 C at 31 psi for > 30 min.
- Hydroclave: Steam sterilization under pressure. Causes fragmentation of wastes. Parameters: 121 C or 132 C. Biological indicator: Bacillus atrophaeus spores (>= 1 x 10^4 spores per strip). NOT used for cytotoxic, hazardous, radioactive waste, body parts, animal carcasses, or large metal items.
- Microwave: Volumetric heating for microbial hazardous waste. Low heat system. Frequency: 2450 MHz. Wavelength: 12.24 nm. Waste destruction occurs by heat conduction from water contained within the waste. Environmentally sound and creates a drastic reduction in waste volume.
- Plasma arc: Ionized gas at high temperature causes gasification and molecular dissociation of organic wastes. Temperature is 2000 C.
- Gamma irradiation: Used for re-usable medical equipment and clothing.
- Wet and dry thermal treatment: Exposure of infectious wastes to high temperature and high-pressure steam. 
  - Screw feed technology: Non-burn, dry thermal disinfection. Waste is shredded and heated in a rotating auger. Waste is reduced to 80 percent in volume and 20 to 30 percent in weight. Suitable for infectious waste and sharps, but inappropriate for anatomical, pharmaceutical, and chemical waste.
- Dry heat sterilization: Used for sharp waste. Parameters: >= 185 C for >= 150 minutes. Validation test: Geobacillus stearothermophilus spores or Bacillus atrophaeus spores (> log 10^6).
- Incineration: High temperature dry oxidation process. Reduces organic and combustible waste to inorganic incombustible matter. Leads to reduction in volume and weight (up to 80 percent). Does NOT require pre-treatment. Generation of smoke is a big disadvantage.
  - Recommended temperature is > 1200 C. Primary chamber > 800 C, Secondary chamber > 1050 C +- 50 C (with a gas residence time >= 2 seconds).
  - Characteristics of wastes suitable for incineration: Low heating volume, combustible matter > 60 percent, moisture content < 30 percent, noncombustible solids < 5 percent, noncombustible fines < 20 percent.
  - Wastes NOT to be incinerated: Pressurized gas containers, silver radiographic or photographic wastes, halogenated plastics (PVC), wastes with high mercury or cadmium (red bags), sealed ampoules with heavy metals, sharp waste, reactive chemical waste, and radioactive waste.
  - Wastes to be incinerated: Human and animal anatomical waste, microbiology and biotechnology waste, expired or discarded medicine and vaccines, soiled waste, chemical waste, linen and beddings.
  - Types of incinerators:
    1. Double chamber pyrolytic: Best for infectious waste. Very high disinfection efficiency. 
    2. Single chamber pyrolytic: Good disinfection efficiency and low cost. Highly skilled manpower is not required. Disadvantage is pollutant emission. Not useful for thermo-resistant chemicals and cytotoxic drugs.
    3. Rotary kilns: Adequate for infectious, chemical, and pharmaceutical waste. Operates at high temperatures causing decomposition of genotoxic substances. Brick incinerators are also used for infectious waste.

3. Chemical Process
- Disinfectants: Chemical agents applied on inanimate objects. Standard for efficacy of chemical disinfection is >= 4 log reduction or more for Bacillus subtilis. Disinfection and mutilation is the treatment of choice for sharp waste. A common regimen is 10 percent Sodium Hypochlorite having 30 percent residual chlorine for twenty minutes.
- Antiseptics: Used on living tissues.

4. Biological Process
- Composting: Land and cow dung (gobar) used. It is NOT a safe method of disposal but is good for the soil.
- Vermicomposting: Earthworms (Eisenia foetida), land, matured cow dung (khad), and coconut husk are used. Not useful for non-biodegradable wastes.
- Bio digestion: Kitchen waste and leftover food are used. Leads to the production of manure and methane. More useful in rural settings.

SPILL MANAGEMENT

Mercury Spill Management Steps
1. Wear Personal Protective Equipment (PPE).
2. Collect mercury using cardboard, x-ray films, duct tape, or a syringe. DO NOT collect by hands.
3. Transfer it to a bottle, half filled with water.
4. Cover the spilled area with 10 percent Sodium Thiosulphate.
5. Mop up the area.
6. Place the bottle separately, by labeling mercury on it, and discard it in a Yellow BMW container (Chemical Waste).
- Mercury disposal rules: It is a hazardous waste. Must be disposed of in recycle units. NEVER drain in the sink. NEVER combined with organic or inorganic wastes.

Blood Spill Management Steps
1. Wear Personal Protective Equipment (PPE).
2. Put old newspaper or blotting paper to absorb the spill over the area.
3. Add disinfectant (Sodium Hypochlorite) and leave for 20 minutes.
4. Put old newspaper or blotting paper again over the spill.
5. Discard everything in a Yellow BMW container.

Hypochlorite in Hospital Waste Management
- It is a chlorine-based compound.
- Used in: Disinfection for floors, counter tops, laundry, and dental appliances. Decontamination of blood spills. Treatment of liquid waste before disposal.

IMPORTANT SYMBOLS IN HOSPITAL WASTE MANAGEMENT
- Biohazard: Three interlaced crescents/circles on a background. Indicates the presence of infectious biological material.
- Cytotoxic: A triangle with a cell-like structure in the center, typically containing the letter "C". Indicates toxicity to cells.
- Radioactive: A trefoil symbol (three blades surrounding a central circle). Indicates radiation hazard.
- Recycle: Three chasing arrows forming a continuous loop.

KEY POINTS AND COVID-19 WASTE
- Workers handling BMW should have 6 Personal Protective Equipments (PPE): Cap, Goggles/face shield, Mask, Apron, Heavy duty rubber gloves, and Gum boots.
- 3-D's in hospital waste management means: Disinfection, Disposal, and Drainage.
- COVID-19 Waste: General solid waste should not be contaminated by patient secretions and body fluids. Wet and dry solid waste bags must be tied securely. As a precaution, double-layered bags (using 2 bags) should be used for the collection of waste from COVID-19 isolation wards to ensure adequate strength and prevent leaks. It should be prominently labelled as "COVID-19 Waste".

MNEMONICS
- Waste Jungle me fek ke sab Environment kharaab: Reminder that the Ministry of Environment and Forests is the key regulatory authority responsible for biomedical waste management rules in India.

title: Disaster Management
content:
OVERVIEW OF THE CHAPTER

Disaster management involves the systematic process of using administrative decisions, operational skills, and coping capacities of society to lessen the impacts of natural hazards and related environmental and technological disasters. It requires a comprehensive approach covering preparedness, response, mitigation, and rehabilitation.

DEFINITIONS

- Disaster (UNISDR): A serious disruption of the functioning of a community or a society involving widespread human, material, economic or environmental losses and impacts, which exceed the ability of the affected community or society to cope using its own resources.
- Disaster (Disaster Management Act 2005, India): A catastrophe, mishap, calamity or grave occurrence in any area, arising from natural or manmade causes, or by accident or negligence which results in substantial loss of life or human suffering or damage to, and destruction of property, or damage to, or degradation of, environment, and is of such a nature or magnitude as to be beyond the coping capacity of the community of the affected area.
- Disaster (WHO): Any occurrence that causes ecological disruption, loss of human life or deterioration of health services on a scale sufficient to warrant response from outside the affected area.
- Disaster (Colin Grant): A catastrophe causing injuries simultaneously to at least 30 people, who require an emergency treatment.
- Hazard: A dangerous phenomenon, substance, human activity, or condition that may cause loss of life, injury or other health impacts, property damage, loss of livelihoods and services, social and economic disruption, or environmental damage.

CORE CONCEPTS

Disaster Risk and Vulnerability
- Disaster Risk = Hazards x Vulnerability factor.
- Hazards have the potential to cause damage or disruption.
- Vulnerability relates to the conditions determined by physical, social, economic, and environmental factors which increase the susceptibility of a community to the impact of hazards.
- World Disaster Reduction Day (International Day for Disaster Risk Reduction) is observed on October 13 (formerly the 2nd Wednesday of October).

LEVELS OF DISASTER

- L0: No Disaster. Represents a normal scenario.
- L1: Manageable Disaster at the District Level.
- L2: Manageable Disaster at the State Level.
- L3: Manageable Disaster at the National Level (requires intervention by the National Disaster Response Force - NDRF).

TYPES OF DISASTERS

Disasters are primarily triggered by natural hazards or are human-induced, often compounded by climate change and urbanization. Secondary disasters may occur after an initial disaster has occurred.

Natural Hazards (DesInventar Classification)
1. Geophysical
  - Earthquakes / Mass movement of earth material.
  - Volcanoes.
  - Tsunamis: A series of waves with long wavelengths generated by underwater earthquakes, volcanic eruptions, or landslides. They slow down and grow steeper as they reach shallow water.
2. Hydrological
  - Floods (including flash floods).
  - Landslides (Avalanche, debris flow, mud flow, rockfall).
  - Wave action (Wind-generated surface waves over oceans, rivers, or lakes).
3. Meteorological
  - Cyclones, storm surges, tornadoes, convective storms, extratropical storms, and high winds.
4. Climatological
  - Droughts.
  - Extreme hot or cold conditions (Cold wave, derecho).
  - Forest wildfires.
  - Glacial lake outbursts.
  - Subsidence.
5. Biological
  - Epidemics: Viral, bacterial, parasitic, fungal, or prion infections.
  - Insect infestations.

Human-Induced Disasters (Man-made Disasters)
- Sudden disasters:
  - Bhopal gas tragedy: Occurred on 3 December 1984 at the Union Carbide pesticide plant due to the release of Methyl Isocyanate. Left about 3,000 dead initially.
  - Chernobyl nuclear power station disaster: Occurred in the Soviet Union on 26 April 1986. Resulted in the massive release of radioactive materials including Iodine 131, Cesium 134 & 137, and Strontium 90.
- Insidious disasters:
  - Global warming (greenhouse effect) and ozone layer depletion.
  - Wars, civil conflicts, and terrorism (e.g., World Trade Center attack).
  - Radiation or chemical exposures in research labs or chemical weapons production factories releasing toxic by-products into underground water sources.

DISASTERS IN INDIA

India is a highly disaster-prone country in the Asia-Pacific region with an average of 8 major natural calamities a year due to its varied topographic and climatic conditions.
- Northern mountain region: Prone to snow storms, landslides, and earthquakes.
- Eastern coastal region: Prone to floods and cyclones (e.g., Andhra Pradesh, West Bengal, Odisha).
- Western desert areas: Prone to draughts.
- Natural disasters causing maximum deaths and damage: Hydrological disasters (Floods).

Notable Catastrophic Events in India
- Super cyclone in Orissa: 1999 (thousands lost lives and became homeless).
- Severe earthquake in Gujarat: 2001 (about 16,480 died).
- Tsunami in the Indian Ocean: 2004 (killed more than 200,000 persons overall).
- Major earthquake in Jammu & Kashmir: 2005 (7.4 RS magnitude, left 2,100 dead).
- Cloudburst in Uttarakhand causing floods in Kedarnath: 2013 (killed about 5,748 people).
- Floods in Kashmir Valley: 2014.

SHORT-TERM EFFECTS OF MAJOR DISASTERS

- Earthquakes: Many deaths and severe injuries requiring extensive treatment. Severe damage to health facilities, water systems, and structures.
- High winds (without flooding): Few deaths and moderate injuries. Severe damage to structures but light damage to water systems.
- Tidal waves / Flash floods: Many deaths, few severe injuries. Severe but localized damage to facilities and common food shortages.
- Slow-onset floods: Few deaths and injuries. Severe damage to equipment, light damage to water systems, common food shortages, and major population movements.
- Landslides: Many deaths, few injuries. Severe but localized damage. Death frequently exceeds injuries due to crushing by falling objects or asphyxiation.
- Volcanoes / Lahars: Many deaths and few injuries. Severe damage to structures and water systems.
- Potential risk of communicable diseases rises following all major disasters due to overcrowding and deteriorating sanitation.

THE DISASTER CYCLE

The disaster management cycle involves continuous phases of action. There are 5 stages in the disaster cycle:

1. Disaster Impact
- The initial phase where the event strikes and causes damage, injuries, and loss of life.

2. Disaster Response
- Search, rescue, and first aid: This is the first thing to be done after a disaster. Most immediate help comes from uninjured survivors.
- Field care: Provision of emergency medical care where injured persons converge spontaneously.
- Triage and Tagging: Sorting of patients to determine treatment priority.
- Identification of dead: Essential for legal, social, and psychological reasons.

Phases of Disaster Containment (Stage of health and medical relief)
- Primary phase (0-6 hours): Focuses on first aid and medical care.
- Secondary phase (6-24 hours): Focuses on transportation, sanitation, and immunization.
- Tertiary clean up (1-60 days): Focuses on food, clothing, shelter, and rehabilitation.

3. Rehabilitation or Recovery
- Water supply: Ensure 1 safe water supply point for every 250 people.
- Sanitation and personal hygiene: Provide 1 latrine for every 20 people.
- Food safety: Prevent food-borne illnesses, especially in shelters where feeding programs are used.
- Vector control: Manage breeding sites for mosquitoes (dengue, malaria), rats (leptospirosis), and fleas (plague).

4. Mitigation
- Done before a disaster strikes.
- Measures designed to prevent hazards or lessen the effect of hazards on vulnerable populations and structures.
- Includes implementing proper building codes, fire safety norms, and flood mitigation works.

5. Disaster Preparedness
- Objective is to ensure appropriate systems, procedures, and resources are in place to provide prompt assistance.
- To know what to do during a disaster through gathering resources and capacity building.
- Goal: To strengthen the overall capacity of a country to manage efficiently all types of emergencies.
- Key tasks: Evaluate the risks, organize warning systems, ensure response mechanisms and availability of resources, develop public education programs, and organize disaster simulation exercises.

TRIAGE

Triage consists of rapidly classifying the injured on the basis of the severity of their injuries and the likelihood of survival with medical intervention.
- The principle of "first come, first treated" is NOT followed in mass emergencies.
- Yields best results when carried out at the Site of disaster.
- It is carried out to determine transportation priority and admission to the hospital.
- Aims: To deliver the right Patient, to the right Place, at the right Time.
- Priorities: Higher priority is granted to victims whose immediate or long-term prognosis can be dramatically affected by simple intensive care. Lowest priority is assigned to moribund patients with questionable benefit. Triage is the only approach that can provide maximum benefit to the greatest number of injured.

Triage Process Steps
- Triage sieve: A quick survey to separate the Dead and Walking wounded from the rest.
- Triage sort: Remaining casualties are assessed and allocated to specific treatment categories.
- Tagging: All patients should be identified with tags stating their name, age, place of origin, triage category, diagnosis, and initial treatment.

COLOR CODING SYSTEM FOR TRIAGE

- Red (Category I - Highest priority): Patients requiring high priority treatment or transfer. Need immediate resuscitation or surgery in 6 hours. Examples: Massive hemorrhage, Tension pneumothorax.
- Yellow (Category II - High to medium priority): Stable patients who need observation. Examples: Isolated simple fracture, degloving injury (requires surgery or resuscitation in the next 24 hours).
- Green (Category III - Low priority): Minor illness or ambulatory patients. Examples: Isolated abrasions, contusions, sprains.
- Black (Category IV - Least priority): Dead, moribund, or unsalvageable patients. Examples: Massive head injuries, 95 percent 3rd-degree burns.
- White: Dismissed or walking around. Patients who have minor injuries (cuts or minor burns only) where first aid and home care are sufficient.

TYPES OF TRIAGE

1. Simple triage: Done before transportation. Used in scenes of mass tragedy to sort patients into those who need critical attention and immediate transportation. Aided by printed triage tags.
2. Rapid triage (S.T.A.R.T. - Simple Triage and Rapid Treatment): Can be performed by lightly trained personnel in emergencies. Used in field operations with mass casualties, such as train wrecks and bus accidents.
3. Reverse triage: A condition where sometimes less wounded are treated in preference to the more severely wounded. Used in situations such as war where the military settings may require soldiers to be returned to combat as quickly as possible.

POST-DISASTER PHASE AND EPIDEMIOLOGIC SURVEILLANCE

Diseases Common in Post-Disaster Phase
- Gastroenteritis (the most commonly reported disease).
- Acute Respiratory Infections (ARI), specifically Pneumonia.
- Vector-borne and zoonotic diseases: Leptospirosis (reported following large floods like the Odisha super cyclone), Rickettsiosis, Rabies, Equine encephalitis, Anthrax.

Vaccination Guidelines (WHO Recommendations)
- Health workers: Recommended to receive vaccines for Typhoid, Cholera, and Tetanus toxoid (Tc - Td).
- General population: Routine mass vaccination for typhoid and cholera is NOT recommended (NONE) as it gives a false sense of security and diverts resources.
- Highest priority vaccine: Measles is the highest priority vaccine for children in disaster-affected areas, as measles can rapidly deplete Vitamin A stores.
- Tetanus: Mass vaccination is usually unnecessary. Tetanus toxoid booster is given as an effective preventive measure only in previously unimmunized patients sustaining open wounds.

Water, Sanitation, and Micronutrients
- The most practical and effective strategy for disease prevention and control is supplying safe drinking water and the proper disposal of excreta.
- The foremost step of disease prevention and control is the chlorination of all water bodies. The level of residual chlorine must be maintained at greater than 0.7 mg/l.
- Micronutrient deficiency: Vitamin A is the most common micronutrient deficiency, as relief diets are inherently deficient in Vitamin A.

MANAGEMENT OF DEAD BODIES

- Care of the dead involves: removal from the disaster scene, shifting to the mortuary, identification, and reception of bereaved relatives.
- Health hazards from cadavers are minimal if death results from trauma. Bodies do not typically cause outbreaks of typhoid, cholera, or plague. If they contaminate water sources, they may transmit gastroenteritis or food poisoning.

DISASTER MANAGEMENT INFRASTRUCTURE AND FRAMEWORKS

National Disaster Management Framework (India)
- Nodal centre for disaster management at the grassroots level: District (control room).
- NDMA (National Disaster Management Authority): The lead agency responsible for the preparation of Disaster Management plans. The Chairman of NDMA is the Prime Minister of India.
- SDMA (State Disaster Management Authority): Functions at the state level. Chief Minister is typically the head.
- NIDM (National Institute of Disaster Management): Established in 1995 (under the Indian Institute of Public Administration). The nodal ministry is the Ministry of Home Affairs (MOHA). The Head of NIDM is the Union Home Minister. Its purpose is policy formulation and reducing the impact of disasters.
- NDRF (National Disaster Response Force): Established in 2006. Comprises battalions (originally 10 battalions drawn from CRPF, BSF, ITBP, CISF). Its purpose is civil defense and operating dedicated Fire cells.
- NCMC (National Crisis Management Committee) and CCS (Cabinet Committee on Security): Key committees for top-level decision making.

Emergency Communication and Support
- ERSS (Emergency Response Support System): Established in 2019. It is a Pan-India single number (112) based emergency response system for citizens (integrates voice call, email, mobile app, etc.) bridging police, fire, health, and disaster management departments.
- Emergency Medical Relief Fund: A special wing under the Ministry of Health & Family Welfare (MOHFW) / DGHS that coordinates all activities related to health and medical relief.

Sendai Framework for Disaster Risk Reduction
A United Nations program outlining 4 major strategies:
1. Understand disaster risk.
2. Improve disaster risk governance.
3. Invest in disaster risk reduction.
4. Enhance disaster preparedness.

PERSONAL PROTECTION IN DIFFERENT EMERGENCIES

Floods
- Before-hand: Know special warning signals (like foghorns from dams). Find out about local risks if living downstream.
- During: Turn off electricity to reduce the risk of electrocution. Move vulnerable people and belongings upstairs or to raised shelters. Evacuate when ordered by authorities.
- After: Do not return until authorities declare it safe. Wait until water is declared safe before drinking. Clean and disinfect flooded rooms; discard any food that has been near the water.

Earthquakes
- Before-hand: Build in accordance with urban planning regulations. Ensure all electrical and gas appliances are firmly fixed. Avoid storing heavy objects in high positions. Prepare a family emergency kit.
- During: Keep calm. Move to the central part of the building if indoors. Keep away from stairs, collapsing walls, and electric cables. If in a vehicle, park it away from bridges and buildings.
- After: Do not go back into damaged buildings due to potential aftershocks. Alert emergency services. Keep a radio nearby for instructions.

Storms, Hurricanes, and Tornadoes
- Before-hand: Choose a shelter in advance (cellar, basement, or alcove). Prune dead tree branches and check roofs.
- During: Listen to radio advice. Do not go out in a car or boat. Tie down objects liable to be blown away. Unplug electrical appliances. If caught outside, seek shelter or lie down flat in a ditch. Keep away from fences and electric cables.

Clouds of Toxic Fumes
- Before-hand: Familiarize with alarm signals and equip doors and windows with the tightest possible fastenings.
- During: Do not use the telephone to keep lines free. Close doors and windows, stop up air intakes, and seal any cracks or gaps with adhesive tape. Listen to messages given by radio.

INTERNATIONAL AGENCIES PROVIDING HEALTH HUMANITARIAN ASSISTANCE

- WHO (World Health Organization)
- UNICEF (United Nations Children's Fund)
- FAO (Food and Agriculture Organization)
- CARE
- IFRC (International Federation of Red Cross and Red Crescent Societies)
- OCHA (United Nations Office for the Coordination of Humanitarian Affairs)

subsections.title: Introduction, Ergonomics, Occupational Environment, and Physical Hazards
subsections.content:
OVERVIEW OF THE CHAPTER
Occupational health is essentially preventive medicine. It is the application of preventive medicine in all places of employment. In the past, occupational health was entirely thought of in relation to factories and mines, using terms like industrial hygiene or industrial health. Modern concepts now embrace all types of employment including mercantile and commercial enterprises, service trades, forestry, and agriculture.

DEFINITIONS
- Occupational health: The Joint ILO/WHO Committee on Occupational Health (first session, 1950) defined it as the promotion and maintenance of the highest degree of physical, mental and social well-being of workers in all occupations; the prevention among workers of departures from health caused by their working conditions; the protection of workers in their employment from risks resulting from factors adverse to health; the placing and maintenance of the worker in an occupational environment adapted to his physiological and psychological equipment; and to summarize, the adaptation of work to man and of each man to his job.
- Ergonomics: Derived from the Greek words ergon (meaning work) and nomos (meaning law). It simply means fitting the job to the worker. It involves designing machines, tools, equipment, manufacturing processes, layout of places of work, methods of work, and environment to achieve greater efficiency of both man and machine.

ERGONOMICS AND ITS TYPES

Objectives of Ergonomics
- To achieve mutual adjustment of man and his work for the improvement of human efficiency and well-being.
- Includes adjusting the worker to his job.
- Includes designing of machines.
- Includes improvement of both efficiency of man and machines.

Types of Ergonomics
- Physical ergonomics: Deals with the human body's response to physical and physiological stress. Includes strain injuries due to repetition or posture.
- Cognitive ergonomics (Engineering psychology): Concerns mental processes such as perception, reasoning, and motor response. Includes workload, decision making, errors, and training.
- Organizational ergonomics (Macro ergonomics): Concerned with organizational structures, policies, and processes. Includes motivation, supervision, ethics, teamwork, and job satisfaction.

OCCUPATIONAL HEALTH INSTITUTES IN INDIA
- For scientific study of the various aspects of occupational health, several institutes have been established.
- Central Labour Institute: Located in Mumbai (set up in 1960). Three Regional Labour Institutes are at Kanpur, Kolkata, and Chennai. They feature a Museum of Industrial Health, Safety and Welfare, Industrial Hygiene Laboratory, Training section, Library cum Information Centre, Industrial psychology, and Occupational Physiology sections.
- Central Mining and Research Station: Located in Dhanbad (under CSIR).
- Industrial Toxicology Research Centre: Located in Lucknow (under CSIR).
- Occupational Health Research Institute: Located in Ahmedabad (under ICMR).
- National Institute of Occupational Health: Located in Ahmedabad.
- National Environmental Engineering Research Institute (NEERI): Located in Nagpur.
- All India Institute of Hygiene and Public Health: Located in Kolkata.
- Indian Institute of Technology: Located in Kanpur.

HEALTH OF THE WORKER AND OCCUPATIONAL ENVIRONMENT

Factors Influencing Health
- Industrial workers constitute a segment of the general population. Factors that influence the health of the population (housing, water, sewage, waste disposal, nutrition, education) apply equally to industrial workers.
- The health of industrial workers is largely influenced by conditions prevailing in their workplace. 
- Occupational environment refers to the sum of external conditions and influences which prevail at the place of work and have a bearing on the health of the working population.
- The occupational environment cannot be considered apart from the domestic environment. Stress at work may disturb sleep, and stress at home may affect work. Occupational health represents a dynamic equilibrium between the industrial worker and his occupational environment.

Types of Interaction in a Working Environment
- Man and physical, chemical, and biological agents: Physical factors (heat, cold, light, noise, vibrations, radiation) can act singly or in combinations. Chemical agents include toxic dusts and gases causing respiratory illness, skin injury, or blood toxicity. Biological agents include viral, rickettsial, bacterial, and parasitic agents from animals, soil, water, or food.
- Man and machine: Industry emphasizes mass production using power-driven machines. Unguarded machines, protruding/moving parts, poor installation, and lack of safety measures are major accident causes. Working long hours in unphysiological postures causes fatigue, backache, and joint/muscle diseases.
- Man and man: Psychosocial factors operating at the workplace. Includes human relationships among workers, and between workers and authority. Examples include rhythm of work, work stability, service conditions, job satisfaction, leadership style, security, communication, system of payment, degree of responsibility, and trade union activities.

OCCUPATIONAL HAZARDS CLASSIFICATION
- Physical hazards
- Chemical hazards
- Biological hazards
- Mechanical hazards
- Psychosocial hazards

PHYSICAL HAZARDS

Heat and Cold
- Heat is a common physical hazard in most industries. 
- Direct effects of heat exposure include burns, heat exhaustion, heat stroke, and heat cramps.
- Indirect effects of heat exposure include decreased efficiency, increased fatigue, and enhanced accident rates.
- Radiant heat is the main problem in foundry, glass, and steel industries.
- Heat stagnation is the principal problem in the jute and cotton textile industry.
- High temperatures are found in mines (e.g., Kolar Gold Mines of Mysore, temperatures as high as 65 degrees Celsius recorded).
- The comfort zone in India is 20 degrees C to 27 degrees C (69 to 80 degrees F). Temperatures above 80 degrees F (27 degrees C) cause discomfort.

Heat Related Diseases (Detailed Clinical Picture)
- Heat Syncope: Due to vasodilatation leading to pooling of blood in lower limbs, causing impaired cardiac filling. Results in brief fainting with self-recovery. Patient may have hypertension too. Treatment is shifting to a cooler area.
- Heat Cramps: Due to loss of electrolytes and salts. Seen in athletes due to increased sweating over exertion. Sweating is present.
- Heat Hyper-pyrexia: Due to impaired heat regulation. Body temperature reaches 39 degrees C. Skin is dry and hot, but sweating is present.
- Heat Exhaustion: Due to salt and water imbalance. Body temperature is less than 40.5 degrees C. Skin is moist, and sweating is present. Treatment requires salt water replacement.
- Heat Stroke: Due to failure of heat regulation. Body temperature is greater than 40.5 degrees C. Skin is dry and hot. NO sweating occurs. Treatment requires rapid cooling.

Cold Related Diseases
- General hypothermia is not unusual in cold work.
- Trench Foot: Wet injury occurring due to immersion of foot in cold water, ice, or snow. Leads to swelling and paleness of the foot.
- Frostbite: Starts as superficial frostbite and progresses to deep frostbite. Involves freezing of tissue and formation of ice crystals in the tissue. Treatment is to re-warm to 42 degrees C for 20 to 30 minutes.
- Chilblains (Pernio): Result of cutaneous vasoconstriction.
- Erythrocyanosis.

Light
- Poor illumination causes acute effects like eye strain, headache, eye pain, lachrymation, congestion around the cornea, and eye fatigue.
- Chronic effects of poor illumination include miner's nystagmus and occupational cataract.
- Excessive brightness or glare causes discomfort, annoyance, visual fatigue, and blurring of vision which can lead to accidents.

Noise
- Auditory effects: Consist of temporary or permanent hearing loss (occupational deafness).
- Non-auditory effects: Consist of nervousness, fatigue, interference with communication by speech, decreased efficiency, and annoyance.
- Degree of injury depends on intensity, frequency range, duration of exposure, and individual susceptibility.

Vibration
- Encountered in frequency range 10 to 500 Hz.
- Associated with pneumatic tools like drills and hammers.
- Affects the hands and arms. Fine blood vessels of fingers become sensitive to spasm, resulting in White Fingers (vibration-induced white finger). 
- May produce injuries to joints of the hands, elbows, and shoulders.

Radiation
- Ultraviolet Radiation: Main occupational exposure is in arc welding. Affects the eyes causing intense conjunctivitis and keratitis known as welder's flash. Symptoms are redness of eyes and pain, disappearing in a few days without permanent vision effect.
- Ionizing Radiation: X-rays and radioactive isotopes (e.g., Cobalt 60, Phosphorus 32). Hazards include genetic changes, malformation, cancer, leukemia, aplastic anemia, depilation, ulceration, sterility, and death. Tissues like bone marrow are highly sensitive, and gonads have special genetic hazards.
- Maximum permissible limit of occupational exposure to ionizing radiation is set at 5 rem per year to the whole body by the International Commission of Radiological Protection.

Radiation Hazards for X-Ray Technicians and Preventive Measures
- Caused by Radium (mining of radioactive ores), X-rays (diagnostic measures), Ultraviolet rays (electric welding process), and Infrared rays (glass blowing).
- Causes malignancies and genetic effects.
- Preventive measures include shielding with lead aprons (0.5 mm thickness).
- Periodic monitoring and replacement of workers should be done every 2 months.
- Employees must be monitored for radiations using Dosimeters or TLD badges.

Dosimeters and TLD Badges
- TLD stands for Thermo Luminescent Dosimeter.
- Chest Badge: Measures whole body dose.
- Wrist Badge: Measures extremity dose.
- Finger Dosimeter: Measures finger dose.
- TLD looks like a film badge but contains a lithium fluoride crystal.
- Responds to radiation similarly to skin.
- Measured by a TLD analyzer. The crystal will luminesce if exposed to radiation, and is then heated to measure the dose.
- More accurate than a film badge.

Decompression Sickness (DCS) / Caisson's Disease
- It is a type of diving hazard and dysbarism.
- Occurs due to low pressure when a diver ascends rapidly to the surface.
- Gases implicated: Nitrogen, Trimix (Nitrogen + Oxygen + Helium), Heliox (Helium + Oxygen).
- Pathophysiology (The Bends): Nitrogen moves from high pressure in the lungs into the blood due to low pressure. If ascent is rapid, swimming up too quickly does not give the nitrogen enough time to leave the blood, instead it forms painful bubbles. A slow return to the surface lets the nitrogen return to the lungs where it is breathed out.

Types of Decompression Sickness
- Type 1: Mild swelling or bends.
- Type 2: Neurological symptoms, cardiovascular symptoms, pulmonary edema.

Effects of Nitrogen Effervescence
- Bends: Steady aching pain in joints.
- Chokes: Rapid shallow, dyspneic breathing.
- Prickles: Irritation of nerve terminals in skin.
- Paralysis: The most serious complication of nitrogen effervescence.
- Aseptic bone necrosis: Affects the hip, knee, and shoulder joints.
- Other manifestations include barodantalgia, barosinusitis, barotitis, abdominal distension, and emphysema.
- Emphysema is the most serious overall complication which may lead to cerebral embolism.
- Treatment: Recompression is the only effective treatment. Rest and 100 percent hyperbaric oxygen applied to lighter cases.

Acute Mountain Sickness
- Occurs at altitudes of 2500 meters and above.
- Partial pressure of oxygen is low, which leads to alkalosis.
- Types include Mild (headache, nausea, vomiting) and Serious.
- Serious types include HAPE (High Altitude Pulmonary Edema) and HACE (High Altitude Cerebral Edema).
- Treatment and prevention relies on Acclimatization. The ascent should NOT be more than 300 meters per minute.

MNEMONICS
- St - Roke: In Heat Stroke, Sweating stops (No sweating).
- PVR CINEMAS: To remember physical agents (Pressure, Vibration, Radiation, Cold, Heat, Light).

subsections.title: Chemical, Biological, Mechanical, Psychosocial Hazards, and Pneumoconioses
subsections.content:
CHEMICAL HAZARDS
- There is hardly any industry which does not make use of chemicals. Chemical hazards are increasing with the introduction of newer and complex chemicals.
- Chemical agents act in three ways: local action, inhalation, and ingestion.
- Ill-effects depend upon the duration of exposure, quantum of exposure, and individual susceptibility.
- Local Action: Some chemicals cause dermatitis, eczema, ulcers, and even cancer by primary irritant action; some cause dermatitis by an allergic action.
- Inhalation of Dusts: Dusts are finely divided solid particles ranging from 0.1 to 150 microns. Dust particles larger than 10 microns settle down rapidly, while smaller ones remain suspended indefinitely. Particles smaller than 5 microns are directly inhaled into the lungs. This fraction is called respirable dust and is mainly responsible for pneumoconiosis.
- Inhalation of Gases: Gases are classified as simple gases (oxygen, hydrogen), asphyxiating gases (carbon monoxide, cyanide gas, sulphur dioxide, chlorine), and anaesthetic gases (chloroform, ether, trichlorethylene). Carbon monoxide hazard is frequently reported in coal-gas manufacturing plants and the steel industry.
- Inhalation of Metals and their Compounds: The industrial physician should be aware of the toxic effects of lead, antimony, arsenic, beryllium, cadmium, cobalt, manganese, mercury, phosphorus, chromium, zinc, and others.
- Ingestion: Occupational diseases may result from the ingestion of chemical substances such as lead, mercury, arsenic, zinc, chromium, cadmium, and phosphorus. These are swallowed in minute amounts through contaminated hands, food, or cigarettes.

BIOLOGICAL HAZARDS
- Workers may be exposed to infective and parasitic agents at the place of work.
- Occupational diseases in this category include brucellosis, leptospirosis, anthrax, hydatidosis, psittacosis, tetanus, encephalitis, fungal infections, schistosomiasis, and actinomycosis.
- Persons working among animal products (hair, wool, hides) and agricultural workers are specially exposed to biological hazards.

MECHANICAL HAZARDS
- The mechanical hazards in industry centre round machinery, protruding and moving parts, and the like.
- About 10 percent of accidents in industry are said to be due to mechanical causes.

PSYCHOSOCIAL HAZARDS
- Psychosocial hazards arise from the workers' failure to adapt to an alien psychosocial environment.
- Causes include frustration, lack of job satisfaction, insecurity, poor human relationships, and emotional tension.
- The capacity to adapt is influenced by education, cultural background, family life, social habits, and what the worker expects from employment.
- Health effects are classified into two main categories: Psychological and behavioural changes, and Psychosomatic illhealth.
- Psychological and behavioural changes: Include hostility, aggressiveness, anxiety, depression, tardiness, alcoholism, drug abuse, and sickness absenteeism.
- Psychosomatic illhealth: Includes fatigue, headache, pain in the shoulders, neck, and back; propensity to peptic ulcer, hypertension, heart disease, and rapid aging.
- Arise in workers who fail to adopt workplace environment resulting in maladjustment and stress.

OCCUPATIONAL DISEASES CLASSIFICATION
- Diseases due to physical agents: Heat (hyperpyrexia, exhaustion, syncope, cramps, burns), Cold (trench foot, frost bite, chilblains), Light (cataract, miner's nystagmus), Pressure (caisson disease, air embolism, blast), Noise (occupational deafness), Radiation (cancer, leukaemia, aplastic anaemia), Mechanical (injuries), Electricity (burns).
- Diseases due to chemical agents: Gases (CO2, CO, HCN, CS2, NH3, N2, H2S, HCl, SO2), Inorganic dusts (Coal dust, Silica, Asbestos, Iron), Organic vegetable dusts (Cane fibre, Cotton dust, Tobacco, Hay or grain dust), Metals and their compounds (lead, mercury, cadmium, manganese, beryllium, arsenic, chromium), Chemicals (acids, alkalies, pesticides), Solvents (carbon bisulphide, benzene, trichloroethylene, chloroform).
- Diseases due to biological agents: Brucellosis, leptospirosis, anthrax, actinomycosis, hydatidosis, psittacosis, tetanus, encephalitis, fungal infections, and bird fancier's lung.
- Occupational cancers: Cancer of skin, lungs, bladder, and leukaemia.
- Occupational dermatosis: Dermatitis, eczema.
- Diseases of psychological origin: Industrial neurosis, hypertension, peptic ulcer.

PNEUMOCONIOSIS OVERVIEW
- Dust within the size range of 0.5 to 3 microns is a health hazard producing pneumoconiosis after a variable period of exposure.
- It is a lung disease which may gradually cripple a man by reducing his working capacity due to lung fibrosis and other complications.
- Hazardous effects depend on: (a) chemical composition (b) fineness (c) concentration of dust in the air (d) period of exposure and (e) health status of the person exposed.
- The most dangerous dust size is 0.5 to 3 microns as they can reach to the internals of lungs.
- Threshold limit values for different dusts are different.
- Super-imposition of infections like tuberculosis may also influence the pattern of pneumoconiosis.
- There is no cure known for pneumoconiosis, making prevention essential.

SILICOSIS
- Also known as Grinder's Disease.
- It is the most common pneumoconiosis in the world and the major cause of permanent disability and mortality among occupational diseases.
- First reported in India from the Kolar Gold Mines (KGF) in Mysore in 1947.
- Caused by inhalation of dust containing free silica or silicon dioxide (SiO2).
- Occupations at risk: mining industries (gold, silver, lead, zinc, manganese, etc.), pottery and ceramic industry, sand blasting, metal grinding, building and construction work, rock mining, iron and steel industry.
- Pathologically characterized by dense nodular fibrosis. Causes alveolitis leading to nodular fibrosis.
- Mainly affects the upper zone of the lung.
- X-ray of the chest shows a Snow Storm appearance in the lung fields.
- Incubation period may vary from a few months up to 6 years of exposure.
- Preventive measures: rigorous dust control measures, complete enclosure, isolation, hydro blasting, good housekeeping, personal protective measures, and regular physical examination of workers.
- Notifiable disease under the Factories Act 1948 and the Mines Act 1952.

SILICOTUBERCULOSIS
- Silicotics are prone to pulmonary tuberculosis, a condition called silico-tuberculosis.
- Patients with silicosis are particularly susceptible to tuberculosis infection.
- Children and women of silico-tuberculotics do not develop tuberculosis.
- It is thought that silica damages pulmonary macrophages, inhibiting their ability to kill mycobacteria.
- Features of Silicotuberculosis: AFB negative in sputum, Autopsy does not show existence of TB disease but shows cases of pure silicosis, X-ray is inconsistent but Clinical features of TB are seen, Impairment of total lung capacity (TLC), and Nodular fibrosis.

ANTHRACOSIS
- Also known as Black Lung or Coal miner's pneumoconiosis.
- Minimum duration to develop is greater than 10 years.
- Caused by inhalation of Coal dust.
- It occurs in two phases:
 - Phase 1: Labelled simple coal worker pneumoconiosis. Associated with little ventilatory impairment and requires about 12 years of work exposure.
 - Phase 2: Complicated coal worker pneumoconiosis. Characterised by progressive massive fibrosis (PMF). This causes severe respiratory disability and frequently results in premature death.
- Caplan syndrome is a complication (Complicated Coal Worker Pneumoconiosis + Rheumatoid Arthritis).
- Risk of death among coal miners has been nearly twice that of the general population.
- Anthracosis is a notifiable disease in the Indian Mines Act of 1952, and compensatable in the Workmen's Compensation Act of 1959.

BYSSINOSIS
- Also known as Monday chest tightness, Monday morning fever, or Mill fever.
