- Caused by inhalation of cotton fiber dust over long periods of time.
- Symptoms are chronic cough and progressive dyspnoea, ending in chronic bronchitis and emphysema.
- Occupation at risk: Textile industries.
- India has a large textile industry employing nearly 35 percent of the factory workers.
- Incidence of byssinosis is reported to be 7 to 8 percent in independent surveys carried out in Mumbai, Ahmedabad, and Delhi.

BAGASSOSIS
- Caused by inhalation of sugarcane dust (bagasse) or molasses.
- Bagasse contains silica, fungal spores, and microorganisms.
- Agent causing the disease is a thermophilic actinomycete named Thermoactinomyces sacchari (or vulgaris).
- Occupation at risk: Paper industries, Cardboard manufacturing industries, and rayon manufacturing.
- Symptoms consist of breathlessness, cough, haemoptysis, and slight fever.
- Skiagram (X-ray) shows Mottling in lungs or shadow.
- Causes acute diffuse bronchiolitis, bronchiectasis, and bronchopneumonia. If left untreated, there is diffuse fibrosis and emphysema.
- It is NOT a notifiable disease.
- Preventive measures include:
 - Dust control: Wet process, enclosed apparatus, exhaust ventilation.
 - Personal protection: Masks or respirators with mechanical filters or oxygen supply.
 - Medical measures: Periodic medical examination of workers.
 - Bagasse control: Keep the Moisture content above 20 percent and spray the bagasse with 2 percent Propionic Acid (a widely used fungicide) to render it safe for manufacturing use.

ASBESTOSIS
- Asbestos is a commercial name given to certain types of fibrous materials. They are silicates of varying composition (silica combined with magnesium, iron, calcium, sodium, and aluminum).
- Types of Asbestos:
 - Serpentine type: Hydrated magnesium silicate. Chrysolite variety.
 - Amphibole type: Contains little magnesium. Includes Crocidolite (blue), Amosite (brown), and Anthophyllite (white). These are the most dangerous types.
- Asbestosis disease appears after 5 to 10 years of exposure.
- Occupation at risk: Asbestos cement, fireproof textiles, roofer, brake lining, shipping industries, roof tiling.
- Dust deposited in the lungs causes pulmonary fibrosis. Fibrosis is due to mechanical irritation, is peri-bronchial, diffuse in character, and basal in location.
- Affects the lower zone of lungs.
- Most common cancer associated is Carcinoma of Bronchus (Lung cancer). Risk is high if combined with cigarette smoking.
- Most specific cancer (strong association) is Mesothelioma of peritoneum or pleura (associated with Crocidolite variety).
- Sputum shows Asbestos bodies, which are asbestos fibers coated with fibrin.
- X-ray chest shows a Ground Glass appearance in the lower two-thirds of the lung fields.
- Preventive measures include:
 - Use of safer types of asbestos (Chrysolite and Amosite).
 - Substitution of insulant by Glass fiber, mineral wool, calcium silicate, plastic foams.
 - Rigorous dust control.
 - Periodic examination of workers (clinical, X-ray, lung function).

FARMER'S LUNG
- Caused by inhalation of mouldy hay dust or grain dust.
- In grain dust or hay with a moisture content over 30 percent, bacteria and fungi grow rapidly causing a rise in temperature, encouraging thermophilic actinomycetes.
- Agent causing the disease is Micropolyspora faeni.
- Repeated attacks cause pulmonary fibrosis, inevitable pulmonary damage, and cor pulmonale.

OTHER PNEUMOCONIOSES AND EXPOSURE SOURCES
- Siderosis: Caused by Iron dust.
- Stannosis: Caused by Tin dust.
- Bird fancier's lung: Caused by Avian or bird droppings.
- Compost lung: Caused by Compost.
- Berylliosis: Caused by Beryllium.
- Wood workers lung: Caused by Oak, cedar, or pine dust.
- Aluminosis: Caused by Aluminum.
- Baritosis: Caused by Barium (seen in the printing industry).
- Lithosis: Caused by Stone dust (seen in the stone industry).
- Tobaccosis: Caused by Tobacco dust (seen in tobacco factories, beedi, cigar, cigarette manufacturing).

PNEUMOCONIOSIS ANTIGENS
- Bagassosis is caused by the antigen Thermoactinomyces sacchari.
- Farmers lung is caused by the antigen Micropolyspora faeni.
- Compost lung is caused by the antigen Aspergillus.
- Chemical workers lung is caused by Isocyanates.

MNEMONICS
- GANNE se SHACCAR: Bagassosis is caused by Sugarcane (Ganne) dust and the agent is Thermoactinomyces sacchari (Shaccar).
- GANNE khaoge MOTE ho jaoge: Bagassosis shows Mottling (Mote) in Lungs on X-ray.
- BAG me band kar do i.e. DON'T NOTIFY: Bagassosis is NOT a notifiable disease.
- SEILING is UP: Silicosis affects the upper zone of the lung.
- SILLY in SNOWSTORM: Silicosis shows a Snow Storm appearance on X-ray.
- SERP is SAFE: Serpentine type of asbestos is safer.
- CROCODILE is DANGEROUS: Crocidolite (Amphibole type) is the most dangerous type of asbestos.
- asBES - BASE of lung: Asbestosis affects the lower zone (base) of lungs.
- asBES - base - GROUND: Asbestosis shows a Ground Glass appearance on X-ray.
- FARMer - FAeni: Farmer's lung is caused by Micropolyspora faeni.

subsections.title: Lead Poisoning, Occupational Cancers, Occupational Dermatitis, and Agricultural Hazards
subsections.content:
LEAD POISONING (PLUMBISM / SATURNISM / PAINTER'S COLIC)

OVERVIEW AND PATHOPHYSIOLOGY
- It is the most common heavy metal poisoning in the world among industrial workers.
- Lead inhibits Amino levulinic acid dehydratase (ALAD) and ferrochelatase in heam synthesis. Protoporphyrin and ALA accumulate in the urine in lead poisoning.
- The body store of lead in the average adult population is about 150 to 400 mg. Normal adults ingest about 0.2 to 0.3 mg of lead per day largely from food and beverages.
- Distribution in the body: 90 percent of ingested lead is excreted in the faeces. Lead absorbed from the gut enters the circulation, and 95 percent enters the erythrocytes. It is then transported to the liver and kidneys, and finally to the bones where it is laid down with other minerals (metabolically inactive, but released under bone resorption).
- Toxic action occurs by combining with essential SH-groups of certain enzymes (e.g., involved in porphyrin synthesis and carbohydrate metabolism). It also causes membrane permeability and potassium leakage from erythrocytes.

SOURCES AND INDUSTRIES
- Gasoline / Petrol / automobile exhaust: Gasoline inhalation from automobiles is the greatest source of environmental (non-occupational) lead.
- Lead pipes for water supply.
- Lead paints on toys and windows in case of children.
- Industries using lead: Over 200 industries, including storage batteries, glass manufacturing, printing, and rubber industries.

TOXIC LEAD COMPOUNDS
- Lead carbonate: Most dangerous.
- Lead arsenate and Lead oxide: Highly dangerous.
- Lead sulphide: Least toxic.

MODE OF ABSORPTION
- Inhalation: Most common mode in industrial lead poisoning. Inhalation of dust and fumes of lead or its compounds.
- Ingestion: Lead contaminated food or drink. Small quantities trapped in the upper respiratory tract may also be swallowed.
- Through Skin: Absorption through skin occurs only in respect of organic compounds of lead, especially tetraethyl lead. Inorganic compounds are not absorbed through the skin.

CLINICAL PICTURE
- CNS effects are mostly due to Organic lead compounds.
- Other effects are mostly due to Inorganic lead compounds.
- Anemia: Microcytic hypochromic.
- Burtonian line: Blue line on gums.
- Lead colic (Painter's colic): Associated with obstinate constipations and abdominal cramps.
- Lead palsy (peripheral neuropathy): Results in Wrist drop and Foot drop.
- Lead Encephalopathy: Insomnia, headache, mental confusion, delirium.
- Facial Pallor: Earliest and most consistent sign of lead poisoning.
- Gout (saturnine).
- Also causes sterility in females.
- Threshold phenomenon is seen: Signs and symptoms occur when blood level is greater than 70 mcg/dL.

DIAGNOSIS AND LABORATORY TESTS
- Urine Coproporphyrin (CPU): Greater than 150 mcg/l indicates exposure to lead. It is a useful screening test.
- Urine Aminolaevulinic acid (ALAU): Greater than 5 mg/l indicates lead absorption.
- Lead in urine: Greater than 0.8 mg/l indicates lead exposure and absorption (normal is 0.2 to 0.8 mg/l).
- Lead in blood: Greater than 70 mcg/100ml indicates clinical symptoms appear.
- Basophilic stippling of RBCs: Also known as punctate basophilia. It is a sensitive parameter of the haematological response.

TREATMENT AND MANAGEMENT
- A saline purge will remove unabsorbed lead from the gut.
- Succimer: Oral chelating agent.
- Dimercaprol: Parenteral chelating agent.
- EDTA (Ca-EDTA): Chelating agent that works by promoting lead excretion in urine.
- d-Penicillamine: Chelating agent.
- Note: Lead poisoning is a notifiable and compensatable disease in India since 1924.

PREVENTIVE MEASURES
- Substitution: Use less toxic materials where possible.
- Isolation: Enclose and segregate processes giving rise to harmful lead dust.
- Local exhaust ventilation: Remove fumes and dust promptly.
- Personal protection: Workers should be protected by approved respirators.
- Good house-keeping: Floors, benches, machines should be kept clean by wet sweeping.
- Working atmosphere: Lead concentration in the working atmosphere should be kept below 2.0 mg per 10 cubic meters of air (this is the permissible limit or threshold value).
- Periodic examination: Urinary lead, blood lead, red cell count, haemoglobin estimation, and coproporphyrin test. 
- Personal hygiene: Hand-washing before eating. Prohibition on taking food in work places.
- Health education.

OCCUPATIONAL CANCERS

CHARACTERISTICS
- Appear after prolonged exposure (period between exposure and development may be 10 to 25 years).
- Disease may develop even after the cessation of exposure.
- Localization of the tumor is constant in any one occupation.
- Occurrence on exposed parts of the body (head, neck, hands, arms) that remain in direct contact with carcinogens.
- Average age incidence is earlier than that for cancer in general.
- Caused by UV light, ionizing radiation, coal products, petroleum products, lubricating oils, and fuel oils.
- Affects skin, lung, bladder, liver, and blood-forming organs.

SKIN CANCER
- First noted as cancer of the scrotum in chimney sweepers by Percival Pott in 1775.
- Caused by coal tar, X-rays, certain oils, and dyes.
- Occupations at risk: Gas/oven workers, road makers, oil refiners, dye-stuff makers.
- 75 percent of occupational cancers are skin cancers, predominantly squamous cell carcinoma.

LUNG CANCER
- Caused by arsenic, beryllium, and isopropyl oil.
- Occupations at risk: Arsenic roasting plant, asbestos industries, nickel and chromium work, and mining of radioactive substances (e.g., uranium).
- Greater than 90 percent of lung cancers are due to smoking, air pollution, and occupational exposure.
- Most common type is peripheral squamous cell carcinoma.

BLADDER CANCER
- First noted in aniline industries in 1895.
- Caused by aromatic amines which are metabolized in the body and excreted in the urine.
- Occupations at risk: Dyeing industries, rubber industries, electric cable industries, and gas industries.
- Specific carcinogens: Beta naphthylamines, benzidine, para-amino-diphenyl, auramine, and magenta. Drugs like cyclophosphamide and phenacetin are also implicated.
- Most common type is transitional cell (urothelial) carcinoma (TCC - 90 percent).
- Most common symptom is hematuria.
- Treatment: Immunotherapy via intravesical BCG instillation is used to treat and prevent reoccurrence of superficial tumors.

LEUKEMIA
- Caused by radioactive substances, roentgen rays, and benzol (benzene, used as a solvent in many industries).

CARCINOGENS AND ASSOCIATED CANCERS
- Asbestos: Mesothelioma
- Arsenic: Skin Cancer, Lung Cancer, Liver Cancer
- Benzene and Ethyl oxide: Leukemia
- Benzidine: Urinary Bladder Cancer
- Chromium and Nickel: Nasal Sinus Cancer, Lung Cancer
- Radon, Silica, Beryllium, and Cadmium: Lung Cancer
- Vinyl Chloride: Liver Cancer
- Wood Dust: Nasal Sinus Cancer
- Ionizing Radiation: Skin Cancer, Thyroid Cancer, Lung Cancer
- Polycyclic Aromatic Hydrocarbons: Skin Cancer, Scrotum Cancer, Lung Cancer

OCCUPATIONAL DERMATITIS

OVERVIEW AND CAUSATIVE AGENTS
- It is a big health problem in many industries.
- Physical agents: Heat, cold, moisture, friction, pressure, X-rays and other rays.
- Chemical agents: Dyes, solvents, grease, tar, acid, alkalis, pitch, chlorinated phenols, lime, rubber.
- Biological agents: Fungi, parasites, viruses, bacteria.
- Plant products: Leaves, fruits, flowers, vegetable dust.

TYPES OF AGENTS
- Primary irritants: Substances that cause inflammation of skin on first contact or exposure in sufficient concentration and for a long enough period. Examples: acids, alkalies, dyes, solvents.
- Sensitizers: Substances that sensitize the skin on first contact and cause inflammation on subsequent exposure (allergic dermatitis). Examples include Poison Ivy (trifoliate with three almond shaped leaflets, presenting as a linear lesion).

PREVENTION (THE 4 P'S)
- Pre-selection: Preplacement examination. Workers known to be predisposed to skin disease or having established dermatitis are kept away from jobs involving skin hazards.
- Protection: Use of protective clothing like gloves, aprons, boots. Barrier creams must be used regularly and correctly, though there is no barrier cream so far invented which will prevent dermatitis in all occupations.
- Personal hygiene: Hand hygiene, available plentiful supply of warm water, soap, and washing facilities (statutory obligation under Factories Act).
- Periodic inspection: Periodic medical examination of all workers for early detection and treatment. The affected worker may have to be transferred to a job not exposing him to risk.

AGRICULTURAL HAZARDS

OVERVIEW
- Agriculture is a big industry. Agricultural workers have a multitude of health problems, often forgotten due to the misconception that occupational health is mainly concerned with industrialized countries.

TYPES OF HAZARDS
- Zoonotic diseases: Due to close contact of the agricultural worker with animals or their products. Examples: Brucellosis, leptospirosis, anthrax, bovine tuberculosis, tetanus, Q fever.
- Accidents: Becoming more frequent due to increasing use of agricultural machinery. Snake bites are an additional health problem in India.
- Toxic hazards: Chemicals are increasingly used as fertilizers, insecticides, and pesticides. Agricultural insecticides are also responsible for sterility in females. Associated factors such as malnutrition and parasitic infestation may increase susceptibility to poisoning at relatively low levels of exposure.
- Physical hazards: Extreme climatic conditions (temperature, humidity, solar radiation, excessive noise and vibration, inadequate ventilation, working in uncomfortable positions for long periods).
- Respiratory diseases: Exposure to dusts of grain, hay, cotton, wood, rice husks, coconut fibres, tea, and tobacco. Resulting diseases include Byssinosis, Bagassosis, Farmer's lung, and occupational asthma.

MNEMONICS
- Clinical Picture of Lead Poisoning (A to G):
 - A: Anemia
 - B: Burtonian line
 - C: Colic
 - D: Drop (Wrist/Foot drop)
 - E: Encephalopathy
 - F: Facial Pallor
 - G: Gout
- Industries causing Bladder Cancer (Red Rubber Cable Gas):
 - Red: Dyeing industry
 - Rubber: Rubber industry
 - Cable: Electric cable industry
 - Gas: Gas industry

subsections.title: Accidents in Industry, Sickness Absenteeism, and Prevention Measures
subsections.content:
ACCIDENTS IN INDUSTRY

OVERVIEW
- Accidents are a common feature in most industries (e.g., coal, mining, quarries, construction).
- It is estimated that nearly 3 million mandays are lost yearly in India because of accidents.
- To the worker, the loss is in terms of wages and human suffering.
- To the industry, it is in terms of compensation costs, provision of medical care, lowered morale, lowered production, and damage to machinery.
- To the nation, it is in terms of lost production.
- 98 percent of accidents are preventable.

CAUSES OF ACCIDENTS

Human Factors
- Most authorities consider human factors much more important than environmental factors in accident causation.
- Human factors are responsible for 85 percent of all accidents.
- Physical factors: Physical capabilities of the worker may not meet the job requirements; visual acuity or hearing may be inadequate.
- Physiological factors: 
  - Sex: Women are known to have less accidents than men doing comparative jobs (a study showed a ratio of 5:24 in Bengal).
  - Age: Younger ages are known to be involved more in accidents than older age groups. The very old again are more prone to accidents.
  - Time: Accidents are minimum at the beginning of the day and increase gradually as fatigue sets in.
  - Experience: Approximately 50 percent of the employees had accidents in their first 6 months of employment, 23 percent in the next 6 months, and only 3 percent subsequently in certain industrial undertakings.
  - Working hours: An increase in occurrence of accidents is found whenever the daily or weekly working hours increase.
- Psychological factors: These are mental factors that might involve a person in accidents (carelessness, inattentiveness, overconfidence, slow cerebration, ignorance, inexperience, emotional stress, and accident proneness).

Environmental Factors
- These factors influence the incidence of accidents.
- Include temperature, poor illumination, humidity, noise, and unsafe machines.
- Causes directly attributable to unsafe machines account for 10 to 20 percent of all accidents.

ACCIDENT PREVENTION
- Adequate preplacement examination.
- Adequate job training and continuing education.
- Ensuring a safe working environment.
- Establishing a safety department in the organization under a competent safety engineer.
- Periodic surveys for finding out hazards.
- Careful reporting, maintenance of records, and publicity.

SICKNESS ABSENTEEISM

OVERVIEW
- Sickness absence is an important health problem in industry that may seriously impede production.
- It is a useful index in industry to assess the health status of workers, along with their physical, mental, and social well-being.
- Incidence: India has a working force of about 30 million in registered factories. The rate of absenteeism was reported to be 8 to 10 days per head per year (or 8 to 10 days per worker per year).

CAUSES OF SICKNESS ABSENTEEISM
- Medical cause: Absent due to any Occupational accidents or illness. About 10 percent of the days lost were found to be due to occupational accidents. Respiratory and alimentary illnesses are also important causes.
- Economic cause: Absent because of remaining Sick leave with pay. Studies have shown that if the worker is entitled to sick leave with pay, he tends to avail of this privilege by reporting sick.
- Non-occupational cause: Absent due to nutritional disorders, Alcoholism, or drug addiction.
- Social cause: Absent due to Weddings, festivals, repair and maintenance of ancestral house, or harvesting and sowing seasons (many workers from rural areas go back to their villages).

FORMULAS AND CALCULATIONS
- Sickness absenteeism = (Number of workers absent x Number of days absent) / Total working days x 100
- Example calculation: If 5 workers are absent for 3 days in a month, and the month has 30 working days, then the numerator is 15 (5 x 3) and the denominator is 30. The calculation is (15 / 30) x 100 = 50 percent.

PREVENTION
- Good factory management and practices.
- Adequate preplacement examination.
- Good human relations.
- Application of ergonomics.

HEALTH PROBLEMS DUE TO INDUSTRIALIZATION

OVERVIEW
- Industrialization implies the transformation of a peasant society into a community dependent upon industries. 
- It means a social and economic revolution in the culture of a nation, bringing associated hazards.

SPECIFIC HEALTH PROBLEMS
- Environmental Sanitation: 
  - Housing: Rise in the number of slums and insanitary dwellings due to migration from the countryside.
  - Water pollution: Discharge of industrial wastes without treatment into water courses (contains acids, alkalies, oils, toxic synthetic detergents, and radioactive substances).
  - Air pollution: Discharge of toxic fumes, gases, smoke, and dusts into the atmosphere.
  - Sewage disposal: Pressure on existing sanitation services leads to lack of facilities for disposal, causing pollution of water supply and soil contamination with parasites and ova.
- Communicable Diseases: Main problems are tuberculosis, venereal diseases, and food and water-borne infections. Industrial areas without proper sewage disposal become hot-beds for filariasis owing to mosquito breeding.
- Food Sanitation: Standards of food sanitation are lowered due to industrialization if proper precautions are not taken. Typhoid fever and viral hepatitis are common.
- Mental Health: Problems arise due to altered living conditions, alien environments, and failure of adjustment leading to mental illness, psychoneurosis, behaviour disorders, and delinquency.
- Accidents: Vehicular traffic and the increased tempo of life cause public health problems in industrial areas, in addition to factory accidents.
- Social Problems: Alcoholism, drug addiction, gambling, prostitution, increased divorces, breaking up of home, and higher incidence of crime.
- Morbidity and Mortality: Industrial areas are characterized by higher incidence of chronic bronchitis and lung cancer. Crude death rate and infant mortality rate tend to be high.

MEASURES FOR PREVENTION OF OCCUPATIONAL DISEASES

MEDICAL MEASURES

Pre-placement Examination
- It is the foundation of an efficient occupational health service, done at the time of employment.
- Includes the worker's medical, family, occupational, and social history.
- Includes a thorough physical examination and a battery of biological, radiological (chest X-ray), electrocardiogram, vision, urine, and blood examinations.
- The main purpose is to place the right man in the right job (ergonomics) so that the worker can perform duties efficiently without detriment to health.
- Example conditions preventing certain employment:
  - Lead exposure: Avoid placing those with anemia, hypertension, nephritis, or peptic ulcer.
  - Dyes exposure: Avoid placing those with asthma, precancerous lesions, skin, bladder, and kidney diseases.
  - Solvents exposure: Avoid placing those with liver/kidney disease, dermatitis, or alcoholism.
  - Silica exposure: Avoid placing those with healed or active pulmonary TB, or chronic lung disease.
  - Radium and X-rays exposure: Avoid placing those with blood disorders or pre-cancerous lesions.
  - Food handlers: Avoid placing those with hepatitis, typhoid, or contagious diseases.

Periodical Examination
- Objective is early diagnosis and prompt treatment (secondary level of prevention).
- The frequency and content of periodical medical examinations depend upon the type of occupational exposure.
- Yearly: Ordinary workers are examined once a year.
- Monthly: Workers with exposure to lead, dyes, radium, arsenic, chromium, and cadmium.
- Daily: Workers handling chemicals or dichromate exposure (Level 4 toxic substances by WHO).
- Involves biological and radiological examinations. Particular care is given to workers returning from medical leave.

Other Medical Measures
- Medical and health care services: Medical care provided not only for the worker but also for his family. Factory first aid services must be available. Immunization is an accepted function.
- Notification: Main purpose is to initiate measures for prevention/protection, and to investigate working conditions. The Factories Act requires notification of 29 diseases; the Mines Act requires 3 diseases; and the Dock regulation requires 8 diseases.
- Supervision of working environment: Frequent visits by the physician to monitor worker's health and the working environment (temperature, lighting, ventilation, humidity, noise, cubic space, sanitation).
- Maintenance and analysis of records: Proper records (worker's health record and occupational disability record) are essential to assess hazards and devise preventive measures.
- Health education and counselling: Should start before the worker enters the factory. Includes use of protective devices like masks and gloves, and simple rules of hygiene (hand-washing, bodily cleanliness).

ENGINEERING MEASURES

- Design of building: Attention to floors, walls, roof, doors, and windows, and cubic space per worker in the blue-print stage.
- Good house-keeping: A fundamental requirement. Walls, ceilings, and passages should be white-washed at least once in 3 years. Dust on the floor should be removed by vacuum cleaners or wetting agents.
- General ventilation: A ventilating opening of 5 sq feet for each worker employed in a room is recommended. The Factories Act prescribes a minimum of 500 cubic feet of air space for each worker.
- Mechanization: Replaces hand-mixing with mechanical devices. Acids can be conveyed from one place to another through pipes.
- Substitution: Replacing a harmful material with a harmless one (or one of lesser toxicity). Examples: Zinc or iron paints in place of lead paints; silver salts in place of mercury salts; acetone in place of benzene; chrysolite or amosite in place of crocidolite.
- Dusts control: Wet drilling of rock (inclusion of moisture). Wet methods should be tried before more elaborate methods.
- Enclosure: Grinding machinery can be completely enclosed to prevent escape of dust and fumes.
- Isolation: Isolate the offensive process in a separate building so workers not directly connected are saved from exposure.
- Local exhaust ventilation: Dusts and fumes are drawn into the hood by suction and conveyed through ducts into collecting units. The breathing zone of workers is kept free of dangerous dust.
- Protective devices: Respirators, gas masks, ear plugs, ear muffs, helmets, safety shoes, aprons, gloves, gum boots, barrier creams, screens, and goggles. Lab coat is NOT considered a personal protective equipment.
- Environmental monitoring: Periodical environmental surveys, especially sampling the factory atmosphere to ensure dusts and gases are within permissible limits.
- Statistical monitoring: Review of collected data on health and environmental exposure to evaluate the adequacy of preventive measures.
- Research: Study of permissible limits of exposure, occupational cancer, accident prevention, industrial fatigue, and vocational psychology.

MNEMONICS
- MEN'S: Causes of Sickness Absenteeism.
  - M: Medical cause
  - E: Economic cause
  - N: Non-occupational cause
  - S: Social cause

subsections.title: Basic Genetics, Cytologic Facts, and Mendelian Inheritance
subsections.content:
OVERVIEW OF THE CHAPTER

Human genetics has emerged as a basic biological science for understanding the endogenous factors in health and disease. Owing to rapid specialization, several branches have emerged, such as cytogenetics, biochemical genetics, and population genetics. Genetically conditioned diseases or diseases with a clear genetic component account for 25 to 40 percent of all cases treated by health services.

HISTORICAL MILESTONES IN GENETICS

- Mendel and Galton: Formulated the basic principles of genetics towards the close of the 19th century.
- Tijo and Levan (1956): Surprised the scientific world by discovering that the normal human karyotype consists of exactly 46 chromosomes.
- Watson, Crick and Wilkins: Discovered the double helical structure of DNA.
- Har Gobind Khurana: Decoded the codes of nucleic acids and developed synthetic genes.

DEFINITIONS AND CORE CONCEPTS

- Genomics: The study of the human genome.
- Genome: The sum total of genetic information of an individual which is encoded in the structure of DNA.
- Base Pairs: Human DNA consists of 3 billion base pairs.
- Genotype: Refers to the total genetic constitution of an individual (e.g., ABO blood group genotypes AA, AO, BO).
- Phenotype: Refers to the outward expression of the genetic constitution (e.g., blood group A, height, weight, musculature). Medicine is often described as the science of management of the human phenotype.
- Penetrance: The extent to which a genetically determined condition is expressed in an individual. Lack of penetrance is a reason for skipped generations and unexpected pedigree patterns.
- Homozygous: The state where the genes comprising a pair are alike (e.g., AA).
- Heterozygous: The state where the genes comprising a pair are different (e.g., Aa).
- Dominant Gene: A gene that manifests its effect both in the heterozygous and the homozygous state.
- Recessive Gene: A gene that manifests its effect only in the homozygous state.
- Polygenes (Multiple Genes): Instances where the same character is controlled by several genes combined (e.g., skin colour, weight, degree of resistance to disease, blood pressure, and intelligence).
- Locus: The specific position occupied by a gene on a chromosome. For example, the ABO locus is in chromosome 9, and the major histocompatibility complex locus is in chromosome 6.
- Mutation: The conversion of normal genes into abnormal ones, representing a regular phenomenon in nature. The natural mutation rate is increased by exposure to mutagens (ultraviolet rays, radiation, or chemicals).
- Founder Effect: Describes the distribution of diseases on the basis of genetics when a population expands from a relatively few founding members, leading to localized clusters of specific genetic abnormalities.

CYTOLOGIC FACTS AND CHROMOSOMES

- The normal human body cell (except sex cells) contains 46 chromosomes, arranged as 22 pairs of autosomes and one pair of sex chromosomes (XX in the female and XY in the male).
- Chromosomes vary in length, with the longest being about 5 times as long as the smallest.
- Barr Body: A dark-staining area at the periphery of the normal female cell nucleus, also called a sex chromatin body, which is not present in normal males.
- Chromosomes are rod-like condensations of chromatin made up of deoxyribonucleic acid (DNA) and are classified into 7 groups based on length and morphological similarities:
  - Group A: Pairs 1 to 3
  - Group B: Pairs 4 and 5
  - Group C: Pairs 6 to 12 (The X-chromosome is included in this group)
  - Group D: Pairs 13 to 15
  - Group E: Pairs 16 to 18
  - Group F: Pairs 19 and 20
  - Group G: Pairs 21 and 22 (The Y-chromosome is included in this group)

CELL DIVISION PROCESSES

- Mitosis: Ordinary cell division where each chromosome divides lengthwise into two sister chromatids joined at a centromere. They separate into daughter cells, preserving the diploid number of chromosomes.
- Meiosis: Occurs in reproductive cells (sperms and ova). Involves two nuclear divisions but only one chromosome division, known as a reduction division. The chromosome number is reduced to half (the haploid number of 23). Fertilization restores the full diploid complement of 46.

LAWS OF INHERITANCE

Formulated by Mendel using crosses of the common pea (Pisum sativum):
- Law of unit characters: All characters are units by themselves, controlled by factors (genes) during organism development.
- Law of dominance: Factors or genes occur in pairs. One factor may mask the expression of the other (dominant masks recessive).
- Law of segregation: When germ cells are formed, the opposed factors are separated or segregated so each germ cell carries only one factor of the pair.

CLASSIFICATION OF GENETIC DISORDERS

1. Chromosomal Abnormalities
2. Unifactorial Disorders (Single gene or Mendelian diseases)
3. Multifactorial Disorders

CHROMOSOMAL ABNORMALITIES

- Overall Incidence: 5.6 per 1000 live births.
- Incidence Breakdown:
  - Sex Aneuploidies: 2 per 1000 live births.
  - Autosomal Aneuploidies: 1.7 per 1000 live births.
  - Chromosomal Translocation: 1.9 per 1000 live births.
- Mechanisms of Chromosomal Abnormalities:
  - Non-disjunction: An error in nuclear division where a pair of chromosomes fails to separate, resulting in aneuploidy (trisomy or monosomy).
  - Translocation: A portion of one chromosome breaks away and attaches to another non-homologous chromosome.
  - Deletion: A piece of a chromosome becomes detached and lost.
  - Duplication: Some genes appear twice in the same chromosome.
  - Inversion: A chromosomal segment becomes inverted, altering the sequence of genes.
  - Isochromosomes: A special class of structural abnormality arising from transverse instead of normal longitudinal misdivision.
  - Mosaicism: The cells of the body are compounded of two or more genetically different types.

DISEASES RELATING TO SEX CHROMOSOMES

- Klinefelter's Syndrome (47, XXY or more X Chromosomes):
  - Incidence is about 1 in 1000 males at birth.
  - Abnormal males having 2 or more X chromosomes with a normal autosomal set of 22.
  - Characterized by non-functional testis (eunuchoid males).
  - Spermatozoa are ABSENT in their ejaculations.
  - Scanty hairs on face, axillae, and pubis.
  - Associated with gynecomastia and mental retardation.
  - Most common cardiac abnormality: Mitral Valve Prolapse.
- XYY Syndrome (Super Male):
  - Incidence is about 1 in 1000 males at birth.
  - Male with an extra Y chromosome.
  - Characterized by exceptional height (6 feet or over).
  - Associated with personality disorders and behavioral disturbances.
  - Tendency toward anti-social, aggressive, and criminal behavior.
- Turner's Syndrome (45, XO):
  - Incidence is 1 in 7500 live born girls, as 98 percent of conceptuses abort spontaneously.
  - They have 45 chromosomes instead of 46 (XO instead of XX). The O represents the missing chromosome due to non-disjunction of sex chromosomes.
  - This is the most common chromosomal disorder in humans.
  - Apparent females with under-developed sex glands (streak ovaries).
  - Infertile and present with primary amenorrhea (most common cause for primary amenorrhea).
  - Characterized by short stature and a webbed neck.
  - Associated with pulmonary stenosis and renal malformations.
  - Most common cardiac anomaly: Bicuspid Aortic Valve.
  - Most common cause of death: Coarctation of Aorta.
  - Patients generally have normal intelligence.
- Super Females:
  - Females with 3 to 5 X chromosomes (XXX, XXXX, XXXXX).
  - Characterized by underdeveloped external genitalia, uterus, and vagina.
  - Associated with mental retardation (greater degree with higher number of X chromosomes).

DISEASES RELATING TO AUTOSOMES

- Mongolism / Down's Syndrome (Trisomy 21):
  - Caused by an extra chromosome which occurs on the 21st pair.
  - Incidence is reported to be 1 in 900 births in European origin communities.
  - Characterized by short stature and a small round head.
  - Present with narrow tilted eye-slits and malformed ears.
  - Short broad hands and lax limbs.
  - Associated with mental retardation and atresia of the alimentary tract.
  - Most common cardiac defect: Endocardial Cushion Defect.
  - The frequency of Mongolism increases with increasing maternal age (unaffected by paternal age).
  - The risk for a woman of 20 years is 1 in 3000, whereas the risk for a woman of 45 years is 1 in 50.

MENDELIAN OR UNIFACTORIAL DISEASES

- These are inherited according to Mendelian Laws and are caused by point mutations confined to a single gene.
- Combined incidence of Mendelian diseases is about 1 percent of all live-born individuals.
- Autosomal Dominant Inheritance: An individual has a 50:50 chance of passing the condition to offspring if the other parent is normal. Sexes are equally affected.
- Autosomal Recessive Inheritance: Occurs when both parents are heterozygous. Each offspring has a 1:4 chance of being affected. Often appears sporadically in children of outwardly normal parents.
- Sex-Linked Inheritance: A mutant gene on the X-chromosome in males expresses readily (no normal allele). If an affected male marries a normal female, the gene is transmitted to all daughters (carriers), but sons escape. If a carrier female marries a normal male, 50 percent of her daughters will be carriers, and 50 percent of her sons will be affected.

LIST OF MENDELIAN DISEASES

Autosomal Dominant Traits (Male = Female):
- Achondroplasia
- Huntington's Chorea
- Neurofibromatosis
- Marfan's Syndrome
- Retinoblastoma
- Hereditary Spherocytosis
- Familial Polyposis Coli
- Hyperlipoproteinemia I, II, III, IV
- Familial Hypercholesterinemia
- Tuberous Sclerosis
- Polycystic Kidney (Adult)
- Brachydactylia

Autosomal Recessive Traits (Male = Female):
- Albinism
- Phenyl Ketonuria
- Alkaptonuria
- Cystic Fibrosis
- Galactosemia
- Haemoglobinopathies (Sickle Cell Anemia)
- Thalassemia
- Wilson's Disease (Hemochromatosis)
- Congenital Adrenal Hyperplasia
- Friedrich Ataxia
- Ataxia Telangiectasia
- Maple Syrup Urine Disease
- Tay Sach's Disease
- Megacolon (Hirschsprung Disease)
- Agammaglobulinaemia (Swiss type)

Sex-Linked Dominant Traits (100 percent Daughters affected, No Sons):
- Vitamin D Resistant Rickets
- Blood Group Xg
- Familial Hypophosphatemia
- Alport Syndrome
- Hypophosphatemic Rickets
- Incontinentia Pigmenti

Sex-Linked Recessive Traits (Male > Female):
- Hemophilia Type A and B
- Duchenne Muscular Dystrophy (DMD)
- Color Blindness
- G6PD Deficiency
- Fragile X
- Retinitis Pigmentosa
- Leschnyhen Syndrome (Lesch-Nyhan)
- Wiskott Aldrich Syndrome
- Agammaglobulinaemia (Bruton type)
- Hydrocephalus

subsections.title: Blood Groups, Specific Genetic Diseases, Multifactorial Disorders, and Molecular Genetics
subsections.content:
BLOOD GROUPS AND DISEASE

ABO SYSTEM
- Early genetic studies centered around blood groups, which are determined by genes.
- There are 14 blood group systems discovered in man, the most well-known being ABO and Rh.
- Genotypes and Phenotypes: The ABO system involves genotypes AA, AB, BB, AO, BO, and OO, corresponding to phenotypes A, B, AB, and O.
- Gene O is recessive; genes A and B are dominant.
- Red blood cells of a person whose blood group is O have no antigens.
- Approximate frequency in the Indian population:
  - O group: 40 percent
  - B group: 33 percent
  - A group: 22 percent
  - AB group: 5 percent
- Disease Associations with ABO Groups:
  - Duodenal ulcer and gastric ulcer are common in 'O' group individuals.
  - Stomach cancer is common in 'A' group individuals.
  - Carcinoma of the uterine cervix, pernicious anaemia, and rheumatic heart disease are associated with group 'A'.
  - Haemolytic tendencies appear to be associated with group 'O'.
  - Thrombosis is associated with group 'A' (An 'A' group woman taking an oral contraceptive is at a greater risk of thrombotic episodes than an 'O' group woman).

RHESUS SYSTEM
- The Rhesus system depends upon three genes designated by the letters C, D, and E, and their alleles c, d, and e.
- The Rh-antigens develop very early and have been demonstrated in a 38-day old foetus, present only on the surface of red cells.
- The most potent antigen is antigen D. 'Rhesus-positive' means possessing this antigen; 'Rhesus-negative' means lacking it.
- In India, about 93 percent of the population is Rhesus-positive, compared to 85 percent in Northern Europe and North America.

ERYTHROBLASTOSIS FOETALIS
- Also known as Rh-haemolytic disease, it exemplifies the importance of the Rhesus system in preventive medicine.
- Occurs when the foetus is Rh-positive and the mother is Rh-negative.
- Foetal red cells cross the placenta, entering the maternal circulation, acting as foreign antigens and causing the production of Rh antibodies.
- Two main types of Rh antibodies:
  - Strong or saline antibodies.
  - Weak or albumin antibodies (small 7S gammaglobulins) which cross the placental barrier and pass back to destroy the foetus's red blood cells.
- Consequences: Miscarriage (if damage is severe), or the infant may be born with jaundice, anaemia, and oedema.

SPECIFIC GENETIC DISEASES

SICKLE CELL ANAEMIA
- An autosomal recessive disorder caused by a point mutation in DNA.
- Leads to chronic haemolytic anaemia with a variety of severe clinical consequences.
- The haemoglobin S gene is carried by 8 percent of American blacks; 1 in 400 American black births produces a child with sickle cell anaemia.
- Highly prevalent in certain parts of Africa and regions in India, often overlapping with areas of high malaria frequency.
- The heterozygote of the sickle cell trait is resistant to falciparum malaria.
- Onset occurs during the first year of life when haemoglobin F levels fall.
- Clinical features: Chronically ill and jaundiced, hepatomegaly (spleen is not palpable in adult life), enlarged heart with hyperdynamic precardium and systolic murmurs, and non-healing leg ulcers.
- Becomes a chronic multisystem disease with death from organ failure commonly occurring between ages 20 and 40 years.

THALASSAEMIAS
- Hereditary disorders characterized by a reduction in the synthesis of the globin chain (alpha or beta).
- Produces hypochromic microcytic anaemia due to defective haemoglobinization of red cells.
- Alpha thalassaemia is primarily due to gene deletion directly causing reduced alpha-globin chain synthesis.
- Beta thalassaemia is usually caused by point mutations rather than large deletions.
- Signs develop after 6 months of age, when haemoglobin synthesis switches from haemoglobin F to haemoglobin A.
- Asian couples (alpha thalassaemia trait) are at risk of producing infants with hydrops foetalis.
- Mediterranean people are highly associated with beta thalassaemia.

HAEMOPHILIA
- A hereditary bleeding disorder affecting 15-20 of every 100,000 males born.
- An estimated 420,000 people have haemophilia worldwide.
- Affects males but is carried by females (who are only occasionally, usually mildly, affected).
- Caused by the absence, decrease, or deficient function of blood coagulating factors.
- Results in excessive, prolonged, or delayed bleeding, most commonly in the large joints of the limbs, leading to progressive joint disease and muscle atrophy.
- Without plasma derivatives, the foremost cause of death was haemorrhage in the skull. In the past decade, mortality heavily stemmed from HIV/AIDS and hepatitis liver disease secondary to blood transfusions.

CYSTIC FIBROSIS
- A genetic disease occurring worldwide that affects the respiratory and gastrointestinal tracts and the sweat glands.
- Incidence ranges from 2.5 to 5 per 10,000 live births in most European populations; rare in Orientals and less common in blacks.
- The gene defect was identified in 1989.
- Life expectancy in developed countries is around 30 to 40 years; in Latin America, up to 95 percent of cases are undiagnosed, and life expectancy is only about 10 years.

PHENYLKETONURIA (PKU)
- An autosomal recessive disorder resulting in a deficiency of the liver enzyme phenylalanine hydroxylase (converts phenylalanine to tyrosine).
- Derives its name from the build-up of phenylpyruvic acid in the urine.
- Frequency is about 1 in 10,000 births.
- Phenylalanine accumulates in the blood and tissues, having a toxic effect on the brain leading to mental retardation.
- Blood testing must be done to detect elevated levels; bottle-fed infants should be tested no sooner than 48 hours after the first successful formula feeding. Breast-fed babies are tested at 7 days due to the low protein content of early breast milk.

MULTIFACTORIAL DISORDERS

- The frequency of multifactorial disorders is high compared to Mendelian and chromosomal disorders.
- A small minority of cancers (e.g., familial polyposis coli, familial non-polyposis colon cancer, thyroid cancers) are clearly inherited.
- Features that arouse suspicion of inherited predisposition: early onset, similarly affected parents/siblings, frequency differences between sexes, and high incidence in specific ethnic groups (e.g., spina bifida is 40 times more common in Caucasians and the Irish than in Asians).
- Environmental factors strongly interact with genetic predispositions (e.g., diet, obesity, smoking, and lack of exercise).
- List of Multifactorial Disorders:
  - Essential Hypertension
  - Schizophrenia
  - Mental Retardation
  - Duodenal Ulcers
  - Ischemic Heart Disease
  - Diabetes Mellitus
  - Familial Polyposis Coli
  - Familial Non-Polyposis Colon Cancer
  - Thyroid Cancer
- Polygenic Inheritance is classically seen in Hypertension and Hypertrophic Obstructive Cardiomyopathy (HOCM).
- Diabetes Mellitus: There is a higher concordance in identical twins (25-30 percent) than in non-identical twins (5-10 percent). About 85 percent of diabetes cases in developed countries are non-insulin-dependent, showing a particularly strong familial tendency.

MOLECULAR GENETICS

DNA TECHNOLOGY
- DNA Probes: Identifies any complimentary DNA sequences.
- DNA Sequencing Methods: Identification of mutations.
- Restriction Enzymes: Cut DNA consistently only at specific sequences.
- Positional Cloning: Strategies using genetic markers.
- Fluorescence In Situ Hybridization (FISH): Direct visualization of the relationship of genes to one another in the nucleus.

GENE THERAPY
- Introduction of a gene sequence into a cell with the aim of modifying the cell's behavior in a clinically relevant fashion.
- Uses: To correct a genetic mutation (e.g., Cystic Fibrosis) or to kill a cell (e.g., Cancer).
- The gene may be introduced using a Virus (usually a Retrovirus or Adenovirus) or by means of Lipid or Receptor Targeting.

THE HUMAN GENOME PROJECT
- An attempt to systematize research to create a single linear map of the human genome, with each coding gene defined and sequenced.
- Agencies included: UNESCO, HUGO, Genome Data Base, National Institute of Health/Department of Energy (USA), Medical Research Council (UK), Genethon (France), and the European Union.
- Primary Goal: To determine the sequence of chemical base pairs which make up human DNA and to identify approximately 25,000 genes of the human genome (later found to be around 20,500).
- Secondary Goal: To understand the human genome and complete a map of all findings.
- Project Timeline: Began in 1990, headed by James D. Watson. In April 2003, there was an announcement of the essential complete genome. Still going on, preliminary counts indicate 19,000 to 20,000 genes in the human genome.

HUMAN GENOME DIVERSITY PROJECT (HGDP)
- Aimed at understanding human evolution and mapping the DNA that varies between human ethnic groups.
- Objective is to define genetic relationships between populations and interpret them in terms of natural selection, genetic drift, and migration.

subsections.title: Population Genetics, Preventive Measures, and Screening
subsections.content:
GENOME-BASED RESEARCH & POPULATION HEALTH (GRaPH)

POPULATION GENETICS
- Study of the genetic composition of population and various factors determining the incidence of inherited traits in them.
- Population genetics is interested in mutation rates, estimating that each gene has its own characteristic mutation rate (anywhere from 10^-4 to 10^-6 per generation).

HARDY-WEINBERG LAW / GENETIC EQUILIBRIUM
- A basic principle of population genetics based on Mendelian genetics.
- States that: The relative frequencies of each gene allele tend to remain constant from generation to generation in the absence of forces that change the gene frequencies and the influences which alter the gene pool.
- Applicable for:
  - Infinitely large population
  - Static population
  - Random mating population
- Not applicable for:
  - Small population
  - Dynamic population or Migratory population
  - Nonrandom mating or Assortative mating
  - Mutations and Natural selection
  - Gene flow and Genetic drift

FACTORS WHICH INFLUENCE THE GENE FREQUENCIES
- Mutation: Implies a change in the genetic material of an organism which results in a new inherited variation. Influences such as ionizing radiation and certain chemicals are capable of producing mutations experimentally. Most mutant genes are harmful, but the heterozygote of the sickle cell trait is resistant to falciparum malaria.
- Natural Selection: Darwin proposed the theory of natural selection (survival of the fittest) to explain evolution. Harmful genes are eliminated from the gene pool and favorable genes are passed to the offsprings. Example: DDT once effective against flies is now resistant (resistant variety of houseflies has become the usual form).
- Population Movements: Intermixing of people makes new genetic combinations possible due to industrialization, urbanization, and migration. This affects both areas of immigration and emigration.
- Breeding Structure: If all marriages were random, a genetic equilibrium would be attained. In practice, matings tend to occur selectively (based on religion, economics, education). This is known as Assortative Mating (e.g., doctors tend to marry doctors, musicians to musicians).
- Public Health Measures: Advances in public health and medical care affect the genetic endowment of the people. Medical sciences are saving individuals with genetically conditioned weaknesses (e.g., retinoblastoma, diabetes). Example: Ramstedt operation has saved many children from congenital pyloric stenosis.

PREVENTIVE MEASURES FOR CONTROL OF HEREDITARY DISEASES

EUGENICS (GENETIC MANIPULATION)
- Term proposed by Sir Francis Galton.
- It is the study of Hereditary Improvement of the Human Race by Controlled Selective Breeding.
- Improvement of human hereditary traits is done through various forms of interventions.
- Negative Eugenics: People who are suffering from serious hereditary diseases are sterilized from producing children. It aims to reduce the frequency of hereditary disease by lowering fertility of genetically disadvantaged through abortions, sterilization, and other methods (Hitler used negative eugenics by killing the weak and defective).
- Positive Eugenics: Encouraging the carriers of Desirable Genotypes for parenthood. Aimed to encourage reproduction among the genetically advantaged. Difficult to apply because socially valuable traits (e.g., intelligence) are multifactorial.

EUTHENICS (ENVIRONMENTAL MANIPULATION)
- Improvement or manipulation in an environment, which enables the genes to express themselves readily.
- Adapting environment to his gene.
- Deals with human improvement through altering external factors such as:
  - Controllable environment
  - Prevention and removal of contagious disease and parasites
  - Education regarding home economics, sanitation, and housing

DYSGENICS
- Environment is changed that lead to deterioration in expression of genes (i.e., growth of population).

GENETIC COUNSELING
- The most immediate and practical service that genetics can render in medicine.
- Genetic counseling is the process of investigating individuals and families affected by or at risk of genetic disorders, to help them understand and adapt to the medical, psychological, and familial implications of genetic contributions to disease.
- Prospective Genetic Counseling: Identifying heterozygous individuals and explaining to them the risk of having affected children if they marry another heterozygote. Done in Sickle Cell Anemia and Thalassemia.
- Retrospective Genetic Counseling: Most of the genetic counseling at present is retrospective (i.e., the hereditary disorder has already occurred within the family). WHO recommends establishing centers where genetic disorders constitute a public health problem.

OTHER GENETIC PREVENTIVE MEASURES

- Prevention of Consanguineous Marriages: When blood relatives marry each other, there is an increased risk in offspring of traits controlled by recessive genes. May cause Albinism, Alkaptonuria, Phenylketonuria, premature death, and congenital malformation. Lowering of consanguineous marriages will improve community health.
- Prevention of Late Marriages: Down's Syndrome / Mongolism is more frequent in children born of elderly mothers. Early marriage of females is better than late marriage for preventing mongolism.
- Specific Protection against Mutagens: Protection against ionizing radiations (X-rays) and chemical mutagens. For example, Rh Hemolytic Disease in the newborn is prevented by immunization with Anti-D Globulin.

EARLY DIAGNOSIS AND SCREENING

DETECTION OF CARRIERS
- Female carriers of Duchenne Muscular Dystrophy are detected by elevated Serum Creatine Kinase (in 80 percent of carriers).

PRENATAL DIAGNOSIS (AMNIOCENTESIS)
- Examination of a sample of amniotic fluid for prenatal diagnosis of chromosomal anomalies and certain metabolic defects.
- Culture and Karyotyping of fetal cells from amniotic fluid for diagnosis of fetal anomalies.
- Biochemical Analysis of amniotic fluid for diagnosis of metabolic disorders.
- Amniocentesis can be done as early as the 14th week of pregnancy (14-16 weeks) when abortion of the affected fetus is still feasible.
- Indicators for prenatal diagnosis:
  - Down's Syndrome
  - Inborn error of metabolism (Alpha Thalassemia, Neural tube defects, Tay Sach's disease, Galactosemia)

METHODS FOR PRENATAL DIAGNOSIS (BASED ON INDICATIONS)
- Advanced Maternal Age (or previous child with chromosome aberration): Cytogenetics via Amniocentesis (14-16 weeks) and Chorionic Villous Sampling (11-13 weeks).
- Biochemical Disorders: Protein Assay, DNA diagnosis.
- Congenital Anomaly: Sonography, Fetoscopy, Targeted Imaging for Fetal Anomalies - TIFFA (20 week).
- Screening of Neural Tube Defects & Trisomy: Maternal Serum Alpha Feto Protein and Chorionic Gonadotropin.

SCREENING OF NEWBORNS
- Neonates should be routinely examined for congenital abnormalities (like dislocation of the hip).
- Biochemical screening uses heel-prick blood samples collected on filter paper (Guthrie card) at 5-10 days after birth.
- Sickle Cell Disease: Detected via Hemoglobin Electrophoresis using Guthrie Blood Spot.
- Cystic Fibrosis: Detected via Immunoreactive Trypsin in Guthrie Blood Spot.

RECOGNIZING PRE-CLINICAL CASES
- Phenylketonuria: Phenylalanine Tolerance Test (detects heterozygotes).
- Diabetes: Urine Examination for Sugar.
- Gout: Serum Uric Acid.

ADDITIONAL CONCEPTS
- Punnett Square Test: Used to find the genotype of offspring.
- Founder Effect: Describes the distribution of disease on the basis of genetics when an isolated population originates from a few founders, leading to a high prevalence of specific genetic traits.

subsections.title: Part 1: Mental Health Concepts and Services
subsections.content:
OVERVIEW OF THE CHAPTER
This chapter covers the foundational concepts of mental health, the burden of mental disorders, characteristics of a mentally healthy individual, and the various classifications of mental illnesses. It also details the preventive aspects, community mental health services, the National Mental Health Program (NMHP), the District Mental Health Program (DMHP), and the legal framework including the Mental Health Care Act of 2017.

DEFINITIONS
- Health: Defined as a state of complete physical, mental, and social well-being and not merely the absence of disease or infirmity. A sound mind in a sound body has been recognized as a social ideal for many centuries.
- Mental Health: A state of well-being in which an individual realizes his or her own abilities, can cope with the normal stresses of life, can work productively, and is able to make a contribution to his or her community. Mental health is fundamental to collective and individual ability as humans to think, emote, interact with each other, earn a living, and enjoy life.

CORE CONCEPTS OF MENTAL HEALTH
- Mental health is Universal. It is NOT specific to any group.
- Mental health is NOT mere absence of Mental illness. Capacity in an individual to form harmonious relations with others and to participate in or contribute constructively to changes in his physical and social environment.
- Hereditary factors contribute to mental health.
- The incidence of Mental illness is increased if causal factors affect the first 5 years of life.
- World Mental Health Day is observed on 10 October (10/10). The theme for 2022 was "Making Mental Health & Wellbeing for all a Global Priority".

PROBLEM STATEMENT AND EPIDEMIOLOGY

Global Burden
- Mental disorders are truly universal and found in all regions and societies.
- They are characterized by a combination of abnormal thoughts, perceptions, emotions, behaviour, and relationship with others.
- Tentative global estimates of major mental disorders:
 - Depression: 300 million
 - Bipolar affective disorders: 60 million
 - Schizophrenia and other psychoses: 23 million
 - Dementia: 50 million
- Economic impact: Cumulative global impact of mental disorders in terms of lost economic output is estimated to amount to US$ 16.3 trillion between 2011 and 2030.

Indian Scenario (National Mental Health Survey - NMHS 2015-16)
- Conducted in 12 states to identify prevalence, pattern, outcome, treatment gap, disabilities, and current status of medical health services.
- Nearly 150 million Indians aged 13 and above are likely to be suffering from one or more mental health problems and are in need of services.
- Mental health problems are comparatively more prevalent in urban areas.
- The proportion of those with a mental health disorder in young adolescents was 7.3 percent.
- Neurosis and stress-related disorders (phobias and anxiety disorders) affected twice as many women compared to men.
- Alcohol use disorder in men was 4.6 percent amongst the 18+ population, while illicit substance use (dependence + abuse) was 0.6 percent.
- Less than 2 percent had a severe mental illness like psychosis or bipolar disorder. Amongst those with the disorder, nearly 50 percent had moderate to severe disability.
- Suicide risk: 0.9 percent are at a high risk of suicide in India.
- Economic impact: Median monthly expenditure ranged between INR 1000 to 2500 and varied across conditions.
- Lifetime prevalence in the surveyed population was 13.7 percent.
- Point prevalence (current morbidity) of individuals above 18 years was 10.6 percent.
- India Mental Morbidity Rate: 18 to 20 per 1000.
- Suicide Rate in India: 10.6 per lakh population per year.
- Most common cause of DALYs lost: Unipolar Depressive Disorder.
- Most common cause of Deaths: Alzheimer's and other Dementias.

CHARACTERISTICS OF A MENTALLY HEALTHY PERSON
A mentally healthy person has three main broad characteristics which can be expanded into nine specific traits:
- He feels comfortable about himself: He feels reasonably secure and adequate. He neither underestimates nor overestimates his own ability.
- He feels right towards others: He is interested in others, loves them, has satisfying and lasting friendships, and takes responsibility for his neighbours and fellow men.
- He is able to meet the demands of life: He does something about problems as they arise, thinks for himself, takes his own decisions, sets reasonable goals, and shoulders his daily responsibilities.

Detailed 9-Point Characteristics
1. Accepts his Shortcomings.
2. He has Self-Respect.
3. Able to feel as a Part of a group.
4. Able to like and Trust others.
5. Able to meet Demands of life.
6. Able to Think for himself and to take his Own decisions.
7. Sets Reasonable goals.
8. Able to shoulder his Daily responsibilities.
9. Not bowled over by his emotions of Fear, Love, or Guilt.

WARNING SIGNALS OF POOR MENTAL HEALTH
William C. Menninger, President of the Menninger Foundation, drew up 13 questions to aid in taking one's own mental health pulse. Help is necessary if the answer to any of these questions is "Definitely Yes".
1. Are you continually Unhappy without justified cause?
2. Are you regularly troubled by Insomnia?
3. Are you upset if your Routine is disturbed?
4. Are you unable to Concentrate because of unrecognized reasons?
5. Do Children consistently get on to your nerves?
6. Do you lose your Temper easily and often?
7. Are you constantly Bitter?
8. Do you think you are Always Right and the other person always wrong?
9. Do you dislike to be with People?
10. Are you always Worrying?
11. Are you always Afraid without any cause?
12. Do you have Aches and Pain without any cause (which no doctor can find a physical cause for)?
13. Do you have wide fluctuation of moods with Elation to Depression which incapacitate you?

CLASSIFICATION AND TYPES OF MENTAL ILLNESS
International Classification of Diseases (ICD-10) classifies mental and behavioural disorders into 9 major groups:
1. Organic disorders: Including symptomatic mental disorders (e.g., dementia in Alzheimer's disease - the Silent Epidemic, delirium).
2. Disorders due to psychoactive substance use: Harmful use of alcohol, opioid dependence syndrome.
3. Delusional disorders: Schizophrenia, schizotypal, acute and transient psychotic disorders.
4. Mood (affective) disorders: Depressive episodes, bipolar affective disorders.
5. Stress related disorders: Neurotic, stress-related and somatoform disorders (e.g., generalized anxiety disorders, Obsessive Compulsive Disorder - OCD).
6. Behavioral syndromes: Associated with physiological disturbances and physical factors (e.g., Eating disorder, non-organic sleep disorders).
7. Disorders of Adult Personality and behaviour: Transsexualism, paranoid personality disorder.
8. Disorders of Psychological Development: Reading disorders, childhood autism.
9. Emotional disorders in Childhood: Behavioural and emotional disorders with onset usually occurring in childhood and adolescence (e.g., Hyperkinetic disorders, conduct disorders, tic disorders).

Broad Categorization into Major and Minor Illnesses

Major Illnesses (Psychosis)
The patient is often considered "insane" and out of touch with reality.
- Schizophrenia (Split Personality): The patient lives in a dream world of his own.
- Manic Depressive Psychosis: Symptoms vary from heights of excitements to depths of depression.
- Paranoia: Associated with undue and extreme suspicion and a progressive tendency to regard the whole world in a framework of delusions.
- Bipolar disorder.

Minor Illnesses
The patient is unable to react normally to life situations but is not considered "insane".
- Neurosis or Psychoneurosis: Symptoms such as Morbid Fears, Compulsions and Obsession (OCD), Dementia.
- Personality and Character disorder: This group of disorders are the legacy of unfortunate childhood experiences and perceptions.

CAUSES OF MENTAL ILL-HEALTH
Mental illness is due to multiple causes involving agent, host, and environmental factors.
- Organic conditions: Cerebral Arteriosclerosis, Neoplasms, Neurological diseases, and other Metabolic / Endocrinal / Chronic diseases (e.g., tuberculosis, leprosy, epilepsy).
- Heredity: May be an important factor. For example, the child of 2 Schizophrenic parents is 40 times more likely to develop schizophrenia than the child of healthy parents.
- Socio Pathological Causes: A combination of genetic and environmental factors including Anxiety, Frustration, Stress, Unhappy Marriages, Broken Homes, Poverty, Rejection, industrialization, urbanization, population mobility, economic insecurity, cruelty, and neglect.
- Environmental causes (Non-psychosocial):
 - Psychotropic Drugs: Barbiturates, griseofulvin, alcohol.
 - Substance Abuse.
 - Toxic substances: Lead, carbon disulfide, mercury, manganese.
 - Infective agents: Measles / Rubella infection (during Prenatal, Perinatal and Postnatal period).
 - Radiation exposure: Especially during the period of Neural development.
 - Nutritional factors: Deficiency of Thiamine / Pyridoxine.
 - Minerals: Deficiency of Iodine.
 - Trauma: Road and occupational Accidents.

CRUCIAL POINTS IN THE LIFECYCLE OF HUMAN BEINGS
Certain key points in human development are critical from a mental health perspective:
- Prenatal period: Pregnancy is a stressful period. Women need help for both physical and emotional needs.
- First 5 years of life: The roots of mental health lie in early childhood. A warm, intimate, and continuous relationship with the mother and father is essential. Broken homes are likely to produce behaviour disorders.
- School child: School programs and practices affect mental health. Proper teacher-pupil relationship and classroom climate are important.
- Adolescence: Transition to manhood is stormy. Basic needs include being needed by others, increasing independence, adjustment to the opposite sex, and rethinking cherished beliefs of elders.
- Old age: Problems stem from organic brain conditions, economic insecurity, lack of a home, and poor status.

PREVENTIVE ASPECTS
- Primary Prevention: Improving the Social Environment. Preventing the occurrence of disease by modifying risk factors.
- Secondary Prevention: Early Diagnosis through:
 - Screening Program in schools, universities, and industry.
 - Provision of Treatment facilities.
 - Case work and Family Counselling.
- Tertiary Prevention: Reducing the duration of mental illness in a patient, so the stress they create on their Family and Community can also be reduced.

DIAGNOSTIC CRITERIA AND DISABILITY ASSESSMENT
- D.S.M (IV - TR): Diagnostic & Statistical Manual of mental disorders (4th Edition - Text Revision).
- I.C.D - 10: International Classification of Diseases.
- I.D.E.A.S: Indian Disability Evaluation & Assessment Score. Used for measuring psychiatric disability.
 - Components of I.D.E.A.S:
 - Illness duration (chronicity)
 - Dysfunctionality
 - Work performance, Communication & Understanding
 - Interpersonal Activities
 - Self-care

MENTAL HEALTH SERVICES AND COMMUNITY HEALTH PROGRAMME
Mental health services comprise:
- Early Diagnosis and Treatment (Use of modern psychoactive drugs).
- Rehabilitation and Aftercare services.
- Mental Health Education.
- Psychotherapy: Group and Individual.

Community Health Programme Elements
The current trend is full integration of psychiatric services with other health services (95 percent of cases can be treated close to home).
- In-patient and Outpatient services.
- Diagnostic services.
- Aftercare services (including foster home placement and home visiting).
- Education services.
- Research and Training.
- Partial hospitalization and Emergency services.

NATIONAL MENTAL HEALTH PROGRAM (NMHP) - 1982
- Logo: Sunflower + Green ribbon (symbol for mental health).
- Objectives:
 - Mental health care (preventive and curative services) at the district level.
 - To ensure availability and accessibility of minimum mental health care for all.
 - To encourage application of mental health knowledge in general health care.
 - To promote community participation.
- Strategies:
 - Integration of mental health with primary health care.
 - Provision of tertiary care institutes for the treatment of mental disorders.
 - Eradication for stigma and protection of rights.
- P.T.R.P Strategy (also known as the Bellary model):
 - Promote mental health.
 - Treat mental health.
 - Rehabilitate.
 - Prevent mental health disorder.

DISTRICT MENTAL HEALTH PROGRAM (DMHP)
Key components of the DMHP include:
- Training: Training program for health workers.
- IEC: Public education to increase public awareness and decrease stigma.
- Provide data: For future planning.
- Monitoring: Record keeping.
- Diagnosis: Early diagnosis and treatment.
- Hospital: Modernization of mental hospitals and Upgrade of the psychiatry department in medical colleges.

MENTAL HEALTH CARE ACT 2017
The recent mental health bill in India that repealed the earlier Mental Health Act of 1987. Key highlights include:
- Notification of mental disease.
- Admission and Discharge: If admission is greater than 30 days, approval is required from the Mental Health Rehabilitation Board (MHRB).
- Rights of persons with mental illnesses: To access health care, live without discrimination, enjoy confidentiality, and have the provision of medical insurance.
- Decriminalizing suicide.
- Prohibiting ECT (Electroconvulsive Therapy) unless Anaesthesia is used (specifically, the use of a muscle relaxant).
- Advance directive (Medical Will): Allowing people to make decisions regarding their own mental health care and treatment in advance.

MENTAL HEALTH GAP PROGRAM (mhGAP)
WHO's action plan to scale up services for mental, neurological and substance use disorders. Key components include:
- Mobilization of resources.
- Social support.
- Interpersonal therapy.

subsections.title: Part 2: Alcoholism and Drug Dependence
subsections.content:
ALCOHOLISM AND DRUG DEPENDENCE

DEFINITIONS
- Drug: Any substance that, when taken into the living organism, may modify one or more of its functions (WHO).
- Drug abuse: Self-administration of a drug for non-medical reasons, in quantities and frequencies which may impair an individual's ability to function effectively, and which may result in social, physical, or emotional harm.
- Drug dependence: A psychic and sometimes physical state, resulting from the interaction between an organism and a drug. It is characterized by behavioural responses that always include a compulsion to take the drug on a continuous or periodic basis in order to experience its psychic effects, and sometimes to avoid the discomfort of its absence.

CLASSIFICATION OF DEPENDENCE-PRODUCING DRUGS
ICD-10 recognizes the following psychoactive drugs or drug classes, the self-administration of which may produce mental and behavioural disorders:
1. Alcohol
2. Opioids
3. Cannabinoids
4. Sedatives or hypnotics
5. Cocaine
6. Other stimulants including caffeine
7. Hallucinogens
8. Tobacco
9. Volatile solvents
10. Other psychoactive substances and combinations

SPECIFIC AGENTS OF ABUSE (AGENT FACTORS)

1. Amphetamines
- Used in medical practice to treat Obesity, mild depression, narcolepsy, and certain behaviour disorders in children.
- The ordinary therapeutic dose is 10 to 30 mg a day.
- Includes brands like Benzedrine, Dexedrine, and Methedrine.
- Produce Mood Elevation, Elation, a feeling of Well Being, Increased Alertness, and heightened Awareness.
- Boost Self Confidence and Energy.
- Also known as the "Superman Drug".
- Results in rapid and strong Psychic Dependence.

2. Cocaine
- Derived from the Leaves of the Coca plant.
- Formerly used as a potent local anaesthetic; acts as a central nervous stimulant.
- Chewing of Coca Leaves is a common practice in Bolivia and Peru.
- Produces a Sense of Excitement, Heightened and Distorted Awareness, and Hallucinations.
- Produces NO tolerance, NO physical dependence, and NO withdrawal symptoms per se.

3. Barbiturates
- Major ingredient in Sleeping Pills.
- Drug users generally prefer short-acting barbiturates (pentobarbital, secobarbital) over long-acting ones (Phenobarbital).
- Barbiturates sedate, unlike amphetamines which stimulate.
- Produce intense Craving.
- Lead to both Physical and Psychic Dependence.

4. Cannabis
- The most common illegal substance of abuse.
- Obtained from Hemp Plants: Cannabis sativa, C. indica, C. americana.
- Includes various forms:
 - Hashish / Charas: Resinous Exudates from the flowering tops.
 - Bhang: Dried leaves and flowering shoots.
 - Ganja: Resinous mass from small leaves and brackets of inflorescence (flower tops).
- All these products are known as Marijuana in the USA (any part of the plant inducing somatic and psychic changes).
- Produce a Dreamy State of altered consciousness, Relaxation, Euphoria, and an increased tendency to laugh.
- Causes Interference with perception of both Time and Space, and paranoia.
- Can lead to Amotivation Syndrome.
- Associated with Run Amok (a destructive progression: do homicide -> Runaway -> Suicide).
- Human death is a rare phenomenon. There is psychic dependence.

5. Heroin and Opioids
- Heroin, Morphine, Codeine, Methadone, and Pethidine are Narcotic Analgesics.
- Addiction to heroin is severe because it produces intense Craving.
- Produces strong Psychic and Physical Dependence which tends to develop early.
- Tolerance occurs rapidly, necessitating increasing doses.

6. LSD (Lysergic acid Diethylamide)
- Synthesized in 1938 by Hoffmann in the Sandoz Laboratories in Switzerland.
- A potent psychotogenic agent. Oral doses of 100 to 250 micrograms cause intense depersonalization; 20 to 25 micrograms cause subjective disturbances.
- Produces Depersonalization, Fantasies, Pseudo Hallucinations, and Visual Illusions (body image distortions).
- Time is deranged (seconds seem to be minutes and minutes pass as slowly as hours).
- Produces Bad Trips and Flash Backs.
- Results in Psychological Dependence only. It does NOT lead to Physical Dependence NOR characteristic Withdrawal Symptoms.

7. Alcohol
- Classified pharmacologically as a Sedative, Tranquillizer, Hypnotic, and Anesthetic.
- The only drug whose self-induced intoxication is Socially Acceptable due to dilution.
- Rapidly absorbed from the Stomach and Small Intestine within 2 to 3 minutes of consumption. Maximum concentration in blood is reached in about one hour.
- Presence of Food in the Stomach inhibits the absorption of Alcohol because of dilution.
- Produces both Psychic and Physical Dependence.
- Anonymous Group (Alcoholics Anonymous) acts as a Support Group for Alcoholics.
- Harmful use of alcohol resulted in 3 million deaths worldwide in 2016 (5.1 percent of all DALYs).

8. Tobacco
- The most commonly used Legal Addictive Substance in India.
- Most common substance of abuse is Nicotine.
- World No Tobacco Day is observed on 31st May.
- Tobacco Related Diseases include Lung Cancer, Stroke, Myocardial Infarction, Aortic Aneurysm, and Peptic Ulcer.
- Causes Intrauterine Growth Retardation (IUGR) and Low Birth Weight (LBW) in pregnancy.
- Adverse effects of Oral Contraceptive Pills (OCP) are markedly increased in women smokers.
- Young People indulge in Smoking due to Peer pressure, Following Siblings and Parents, and Employment outside the home.
- Prevalence of overall Tobacco use in India: Rural areas (38 percent) and Urban areas (25 percent).
- Includes forms like Cigarette, Bidi, Pan-masala, Gutkha, and Khaini.
- Withdrawal Symptoms include Irritability, Anxiety, Craving, Tremors, Lethargy, and Sleep problems.

9. Volatile Solvents
- Sniffing of substances such as Glue, Paint, Thinner, and Petrol.
- These substances are CNS depressants and produce effects comparable to Alcohol.
- Produce Euphoria, Confusion, Disorientation, Ataxia, Grandiosity, Recklessness, Delusions, and Hallucinations.
- Lead to a substantial Loss of Self Control.
- Lead Encephalopathy is associated with sniffing Lead Gasoline.

10. Caffeine
- The most common substance used worldwide.
- Includes Tea, Coffee, Cocoa, and Cola Drinks.
- Caffeinism is associated with the ingestion of over 500 mg per day.
- Produces Anxiety, Agitation, Restlessness, and Insomnia.
- Withdrawal causes Headache, Irritability, Lethargy, and Nausea.

HOST FACTORS: SYMPTOMS OF DRUG ADDICTION
Symptoms pointing to potential drug abuse include:
- Loss of Interest in Sports and Daily Routine.
- Loss of Appetite and Body Weight.
- Unsteady Gait and Tremors.
- Reddening and Puffiness of Eyes, unclear vision.
- Slurring of Speech.
- Drowsiness, Lethargy, and Passivity.
- Acute Anxiety, Depression, and Profuse Sweating.
- Changing mood and Temper Tantrums.
- Depersonalization and Emotional Detachment.
- Impaired Memory and Concentration.
- Injection marks on the body and blood stains on clothes.
- Presence of Needles, Syringes, and strange packets at home.

ENVIRONMENTAL FACTORS
Environmental factors increasing the risk of drug abuse include:
- Unemployment.
- Migration to Cities.
- Relaxed Parental Control.
- Alienation from family.
- Living away from Home.
- Broken Homes (one parent families).
- Early exposure to drugs and leaving school early.
- Large urban environments or areas where drugs are sold, traded, or produced.

MAGNITUDE OF SUBSTANCE ABUSE IN INDIA
Key findings from the National Survey (2017-2018):
- Alcohol is the most common psychoactive substance used. About 14.6 percent (16 crore) of the population uses alcohol. 5.7 crore are problem users, and 2.9 crore are dependent.
- Cannabis is used by about 2.8 percent of the population (3.1 crore individuals). Legal forms (bhang) are used by 2.2 crore, and illegal forms (ganja/charas) by 1.3 crore.
- Opioids are used by 2.1 percent (2.3 crore). Includes opium, poppy husk (doda/phukki), heroin (smack/brown sugar), and pharmaceutical opioids. 77 lakh are problem users.
- Sedatives and Inhalants are also a concern, especially among children and adolescents in states like Uttar Pradesh, Madhya Pradesh, Maharashtra, Delhi, and Haryana.
- Injecting Drug Users (PWID): Estimated at 8.5 lakh nationally. Heroin and pharmaceutical opioids are predominantly injected.

PREVENTION OF DRUG DEPENDENCE
1. Legal Approach
- Prohibition of sales of Tobacco to Minors.
- Prohibition of Smoking and Alcohol in Public places.
- Prohibition of Cigarette and Alcohol Advertisement.
- Placing of Mandatory Warning on packing.
- Imprisonment for Drunken Driving.

2. Educational Approach
- Messages should provide specific advice capable of Provoking Action.
- Educational programmes for School Children.
- Public Information Campaigns on Electronic Media.

3. Community Approach
- Provision of alternative activities.
- Prohibition of Alternate Activities like Sports, Music, and Artistic activities (Notes indicate encouraging alternative activities to prevent drift into subculture, meaning shifting focus to sports/music).

TREATMENT
1. Medical Care
- Identification of Drug Addicts and assessing their motivation.
- Detoxication after Hospitalization.
- Post detoxication Counseling and Follow Up (clinic and home visits).
- Rehabilitation: Vocational Training and After Care.

2. Simultaneous Interventions (Psychosocial)
- Change in Environment (home, school, college).
- Complete Break with his Peer Group (otherwise chances of relapse are 100 percent).
- Psychotherapy.

REHABILITATION
- Vocational Training and provision of sheltered work opportunities.
- After Care to prevent relapse.
- Success necessitates the adoption of a mature and realistic attitude by the local community and avoidance of panic, moral condemnation, and discrimination.

COMPREHENSIVE MENTAL HEALTH ACTION PLAN 2013-2020
- Vision: A world in which mental health is valued, promoted, and protected.
- Objectives:
 1. To strengthen effective leadership and governance for mental health.
 2. To provide comprehensive integrated and responsive mental health and social care services in community-based settings.
 3. To implement strategies for promotion and prevention in mental health.
 4. To strengthen information systems, evidence, and research for mental health.

title: Health Information and Basic Medical Statistics
content:
OVERVIEW OF THE CHAPTER
This chapter introduces the quantitative tools required to analyze public health data. It covers sampling techniques, measures of central tendency, measures of dispersion, normal distribution characteristics, and the tests of significance essential for reading medical literature and conducting PG research.
[Image of standard normal distribution curve

DEFINITIONS
STATISTICS: The science of compiling, classifying, and tabulating numerical data and expressing the results in a mathematical or graphical form.
VITAL STATISTICS: The ongoing collection and analysis of demographic data pertaining to vital events like births, deaths, and marriages.
SAMPLING: The process of selecting a statistically representative portion of a population for a study.
NULL HYPOTHESIS: The assumption that there is no true difference between the samples being compared, and any observed difference is purely due to sampling variation (chance).

CORE CONCEPTS
MEASURES OF CENTRAL TENDENCY:
1. MEAN: The mathematical average. Highly affected by extreme values (outliers).
2. MEDIAN: The middle value when data is arranged in ascending/descending order. Best measure for skewed data.
3. MODE: The most frequently occurring value in a dataset.
MEASURES OF DISPERSION:
